lessons.title: God's Plan for Man
lessons.content:
# LESSON ONE

## 1. General Subjects Concerning God's Plan for Man

Welcome to our very first lesson on "God's Plan for Man" — a journey that stretches from the eternal past all the way to the eternal future. Before we dive in, we need to understand the overall shape of the plan and get familiar with the key terms we'll be using.

These fifty-two lessons are divided into four main parts:

> PART I The Origin of All Things, Lessons 1–8

> PART II God's Historical Dealings with Man, Lessons 9–18

> PART III God's Present Dealings with Man, Lessons 19–36

> PART IV God's Future Dealings with Man, Lessons 37–52

### I. Importance of the Study of "God's Plan for Man"

If you want to truly know the Bible and be saved for eternity, this study is the most important subject you'll ever encounter. Think of it as the backbone of the entire Bible — it covers every age, every dispensation, and every major biblical subject from eternity past to eternity future.

Here's the key: once you learn to distinguish the ages and dispensations, the whole Bible will start to harmonize beautifully. The main reason so many people feel confused about Scripture is simply that they don't understand God's plan for humanity.

If you'll take the time to get a working knowledge of this blueprint, you'll find the Bible isn't nearly as difficult as you might have thought. We really can't overstate how important these lessons are — they're built on a correct understanding of Scripture itself.

Here's what we recommend: memorize the name of each age and dispensation, and pay careful attention to where each one fits in God's overall plan. That way, you'll have a mental picture of exactly which time period each lesson addresses and where various scriptures apply.

Keep the key features of each age in mind as we move from lesson to lesson. As you do, God's ultimate purpose for humanity will begin to dawn on you in a fresh, powerful way.

If you want a clear, balanced, and harmonious understanding of Scripture, get to know God's plan of the ages inside and out. Understand how the history of the past connects to the present, and grasp the prophecies about the future. Then — and only then — can you truly be "a workman that needeth not to be ashamed, rightly dividing the word of truth" (2 Tim. 2:15).

Along with this big-picture knowledge, you'll learn how to apply Scripture to your daily life. You'll discover how to receive healing, health, prosperity, and happiness — right here and now. After all, isn't that what you need most at this moment? You won't just gain knowledge about God; you'll learn how to experience the full blessings of life by putting that knowledge to work.

### II. The Simplicity of the Plan of God for Man

Here's some good news: the Bible — God's plan in Scripture — is actually very simple to understand. No one has any real excuse for misunderstanding it.

Jesus constantly invited and even challenged people to study Scripture. He rebuked those who lacked knowledge of revealed truth. He pointed to ignorance of the Bible as the root cause of every error. When critics questioned Him, He answered, "Ye do err, not knowing the scriptures, nor the power of God" (Mt. 22:29). He commanded people to "search the scriptures: for in them ye think ye have eternal life: and they are they which testify of me" (Jn. 5:39).

Even after more than three years of the simplest teaching by the greatest teacher who ever lived, the disciples had to be corrected. Jesus said, "O fools, and slow of heart to believe all that the prophets have spoken" (Lk. 24:25-27). Notice — this wasn't because they couldn't understand. It was because they didn't believe what Christ said.

Even after Jesus rose from the dead and appeared to them in various ways, they still struggled to believe until He "upbraided them for their unbelief and hardness of heart" (Mk. 16:13-14). His words were always simple enough to grasp — but believing them was another matter entirely.

You might find the same thing true for yourself. At first, it may be hard to accept that the benefits we're promising you from Scripture are really true — and really for you. But we're going to prove every one of them from the plain words of the Bible. Start believing those promises of abundant life right now, and begin to expect these blessings for yourself.

Paul taught the same principle — that simply hearing God's Word is enough to produce faith. He wrote, "How then shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? and how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? and how shall they hear without a preacher? . . . But they have not all obeyed the gospel. For Esaias saith, Lord, who hath believed our report? So then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the word of God" (Rom. 10:11-18).

Nearly ninety times in the New Testament alone, we're pointed back to what "is written" — as if everything is simple enough to understand for anyone who genuinely wants to understand and believe. In fact, nowhere in either Testament does any speaker or writer suggest that God's words are hard to understand — if people would simply believe them.

The only hint of confusion comes from those who didn't want to believe and obey. Jesus taught that the difficulty comes from human willfulness — some people simply don't want to understand. But those who do want to understand can, without exception (Mt. 13:9-17).

Peter mentions "the unlearned and unstable" who twist the Scriptures to their own destruction (2 Pet. 3:16-18). But you don't have to be in that group — not if you make up your mind to believe and obey what's written, instead of rebelling against it.

Pride, stubbornness, and rebellion against what "is written" — these are what make the Bible hard to understand. The challenge isn't grasping the truth with your mind; it's being willing to obey what you'd rather not obey. Think of it like this: if you truly couldn't understand something, you wouldn't be able to reject it either.

There's no excuse for misunderstanding God's Word if you'll approach it like a child — accepting the Bible for what it says and honestly committing yourself to obey it. You must accept the Bible as God's Word. You must believe that God couldn't be honest if He tried to hide the very truths He'll judge you by in the end.

Accept the Bible as the final authority on its own subjects, and set aside human interpretations and distortions. Believe that God knows what He's talking about. Trust that He knows how to express Himself in human language. Accept that He said what He meant and meant what He said — and that what He says on any subject matters more than what any person says about it.

You don't have to look up every scripture reference in these lessons to get the truth. The references are provided as proof that what we're teaching is biblical, and for those who want to investigate for themselves. Looking up all the references would certainly enrich your life and bring great blessing. But since we'll state exactly what the references say, you can still grasp the truth even if you don't have time to check every citation.

That said, if you have any doubts that we're teaching sound doctrine, look up the references and see exactly what the Bible says — and believe it over any human teacher. You can't go wrong with that approach. Just make sure you stick to what's actually written, and don't let preconceived ideas make you biased. Don't try to force the Bible to match your ideas — always adjust your ideas to match the Bible.

Read the plain language of Scripture and understand it the same literal way you'd understand similar statements in any other book.

In Lesson Three, we'll show you how to understand the Bible in more detail. For now, follow these simple principles. Accept that the Bible is straightforward and that God's plan for humanity is clear. Nothing will be hard to understand as we continue our study.

Don't defeat yourself before you start by thinking the Bible is impossibly hard. Let go of that idea right now! We'll show you just how simple it really is. You'll be amazed at its simplicity, lesson after lesson. So-called "mysteries" that have puzzled the wise of this world are revealed to those with childlike hearts.

Jesus once said, "I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes. Even so, Father: for so it seemed good in thy sight" (Mt. 11:25-26). He said of the so-called wise, "This people's heart is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes they have closed; lest at any time they should see with their eyes, and hear with their ears, and should understand with their heart, and should be converted, and I should heal them" (Mt. 13:10-17).

So your attitude matters tremendously. Be simple and humble. Take the place of a learner and accept at face value everything God says, whether or not it fits with your theories or what you've been taught before.

If you truly want truth as it is in the Bible, you've come to the right place. We promise and guarantee that we won't twist any part of the Bible to fit our ideas or anyone else's. We'll give you chapter and verse for everything we say about God's plan for humanity.

Let's move forward with an open Bible, an open heart, and an open mind. Let's be honest enough to set aside old theories for the plain, literal, simple Word of God on every topic we discuss.

### III. Definition of the Terms and Expressions of the Ages

Before you can "rightly divide the word of truth" (2 Tim. 2:15), there are some words and expressions you need to understand. Knowing these terms and how Scripture uses them is essential for grasping God's Word correctly.

Here are the key terms we want you to become familiar with. Come back to this section often as you study future lessons.

**1. AGE.** The Greek word *aion* means an age or period of time, whether long or short. In this sense, there are countless ages:

> (1) PAST AGES. Paul said of the revelation of the Church's mystery "which in other ages was not made known unto the sons of men, as it is now revealed unto his holy apostles and prophets by the Spirit" (Eph. 3:5-6; Col. 1:26). Paul also said that God, through Christ, planned the ages — "made the worlds" (Greek: *ages*, Heb. 1:1-3; 11:3). See also Lk. 1:70; Jn. 9:32; Acts 3:21; 15:18; 1 Cor. 2:7.

> (2) PRESENT AGE. This Age of Grace, between the first and second comings of Jesus Christ, is referred to many times in Scripture as "this world" (Greek: *age*, Mt. 13:22); "end of this world" (*age*, Mt. 13:39-40; Mk. 4:19; Lk. 16:8; 20:34; Rom. 12:2; 1 Cor. 1:20); "end of the world" (*age*, Mt. 13:49; 24:3; 28:20); and "present evil world" (*age*, Gal. 1:4; 2 Tim. 4:10; Titus 2:12). In another sense, "present age" refers to the period from Noah's flood to the Millennium, as we'll see in Lesson Thirteen and point (5), note c.

> (3) FUTURE AGES. One particular age — the Millennium — follows "this age" and is called "the world [*age*] to come" (Mt. 12:31-32; Mk. 10:30; Eph. 1:21; Heb. 6:5) and "that world" (*age*, Lk. 20:35). After the Millennium, which is the last dispensation before the eternal perfect state, there will be ages following ages like links in a continuous chain. Paul speaks of the "ages to come" in which God will forever show "the exceeding riches of his grace in his kindness toward us through Christ Jesus" (Eph. 2:7; 3:21; 1 Tim. 1:17).

> (4) THE CREATIVE AGES (Gen. 1:1–2:25). By this we mean the ages in the dateless past when God created the universe and everything in it — called in Gen. 2:4 "the generations of the heavens and of the earth." During these creative ages, God brought into existence the vast heavens, including the sun, moon, stars, and planets, as well as innumerable things in the three heavens and the earth mentioned in Scripture (2 Cor. 12:2; Col. 1:15-18; Rom. 1:20). God also created various kinds of spirit and human beings to inhabit the planets, planning to reveal Himself to them and show the riches of His glory and kindness forever. In Lessons Five through Eight, we'll explore the most wonderful creation story ever told, straight from Scripture.

> (5) THE FIVE MAIN AGES. There are five main ages connected with God's dealings with free moral agents:

> A. THE ANTECHAOTIC AGE — from the original creation to chaos; from the beginning of the heavens and earth in Gen. 1:1 to the chaotic state in Gen. 1:2. During this period, Lucifer ruled the earth but fell through pride and caused the earth to be cursed, as we'll see in Lesson Seven.

> B. THE ANTEDILUVIAN AGE — from the six days of the re-creation (Gen. 1:3–2:25) to Noah's flood; or from Lucifer's flood (Gen. 1:2) to Noah's flood (Gen. 6–8).

> C. THE PRESENT AGE — from Noah's flood (Gen. 6–8) to the Millennium, which follows Christ's second coming and the Battle of Armageddon (Rev. 19:11–20:7).

> D. THE AGE TO COME — the Millennium, from Christ's second coming and the Battle of Armageddon (Rev. 19:11-21) to the New Heavens and New Earth (Rev. 21–22).

> E. THE AGE OF THE AGES — eternity, time without end. In Lessons Forty-nine through Fifty-two, we'll study the future and eternal conditions on earth and in the heavens in amazing detail — truths most church members have seldom, if ever, heard. Studying these truths will make you rich in knowledge and give you a concrete understanding of the eternal future.

**2. WORLD.** There are ten Hebrew and Greek words translated by our single English word "world." Each has a different shade of meaning, and failing to understand which original word is used can lead to misunderstanding.

The context and subject matter of a passage, along with how the word is used, will help you determine the true meaning. If you want to dig deeper, you can find out which original word was used and study its exact meaning. This method applies not just to "world" but to every English word where there's any doubt about the meaning.

We won't go into a lengthy technical discussion of all ten original words, but here are the important ones:

> (1) The Hebrew word *olam* means unknown time, time out of mind, eternity, always, everlasting, and perpetual. This word should never have been translated "world" — it always relates to time. For example, in Ps. 73:12, it should read "continually" instead of "in the world." In Isa. 45:17; 64:4 it should read "eternal" instead of "world without end." In other words, it refers to time without any defined beginning or end, as we'll see in later lessons when this word is used for things continuing throughout eternity.

> (2) The Greek word *aion* means a period of time, whether long or short. It should always be translated "age," never "world," as we've already seen. Sometimes it's translated "eternal," "forever," or "for ever and ever" — and those are correct, since *aion* can mean a long or eternal period as well as a short one.

> (3) The Greek word *oikoumene* means the inhabited earth, or the world of people. Sometimes it refers only to part of the inhabited earth, such as the Roman Empire (Lk. 2:1; Acts 11:28).

> (4) The Greek word *kosmos* means order, regularity, arrangement, ornament, and social system. It's always translated "world," except in 1 Pet. 3:3 where it's translated "adorning." The true meaning is the social system or order on the earth. When Peter said "the world that then was, being overflowed with water perished," we should understand that only the social order on earth perished — not the ground itself (2 Pet. 3:5-7).

> (5) The Greek word *aionios* means unknown time, time out of mind, eternity, always, perpetual, and forever. It corresponds to the Hebrew *olam*. The three places where it's translated "the world began" should really read "time without defined limits as to beginning or end" (Rom. 16:25; 2 Tim. 1:9; Titus 1:2).

Wherever *olam*, *aion*, or *aionios* are used in connection with God's plan, God Himself, eternal life, eternal punishment, or other eternal things, they literally mean everlasting, eternal, and perpetual — as we'll see in our future studies.

**3. TIMES AND SEASONS.** There are twenty-eight Greek words translated "time" or "times," and eight translated "season" or "seasons." Each has a different shade of meaning, but only two need to be listed here:

> (1) CHRONOS — time unlimited, unless specifically defined (Mt. 2:7, 16; Acts 1:6-7; 3:21; Gal. 4:4; 1 Thess. 5:1; 1 Pet. 1:17, 20; Rev. 10:6).

> (2) KAIROS — a certain limited or definite portion of chronos, or time (Mt. 8:29; 11:25; 12:1; 13:30; Acts 3:19; Eph. 1:10; Rev. 12:12, 14).

God has spoken "at sundry times and in divers manners" about certain times and seasons in history (Heb. 1:1). To rightly divide the Word of Truth, you must understand the times when the different books of the Bible were written and the times to which they apply. There's a time and season for everything under the sun (Eccl. 3).

Beyond natural times and seasons, there are also specific times and seasons for preordained events in God's eternal plan. The children of Issachar "had understanding of the times, to know what Israel ought to do" (1 Chr. 12:32). The wise men knew by a star when Christ was born: "Where is he that is born King of the Jews? for we have seen his star in the east, and are come to worship him . . . the star, which they saw in the east, went before them, till it came and stood over where the young child was" (Mt. 2:1-10).

Jesus understood the signs of His time and rebuked the Pharisees for not discerning them as clearly as they could read the weather. He said, "O ye hypocrites, ye can discern the face of the sky; but can ye not discern the signs of the times?" (Mt. 16:1-3).

After Jesus' resurrection, the disciples asked, "Lord, wilt thou at this time restore again the kingdom to Israel?" He replied, "It is not for you to know the times or the seasons, which the Father hath put in his own power" (Acts 1:6-7). Even Satan and demons know the time of their coming doom (Mt. 8:29; Rev. 12:12).

Nevertheless, there are certain times and seasons we can know about the prophetic events of the last days (1 Thess. 5:1-9). What we can and should know of God's plan is what He has plainly revealed in Scripture. As Moses said, "The secret things belong unto the Lord our God: but those things which are revealed belong unto us and to our children for ever" (Deut. 29:29).

**4. THE ETERNAL PAST.** By this we mean the dateless past before time as we know it — before the six days of Gen. 1:3–2:25.

**5. THE ETERNAL FUTURE.** By this we mean the eternal continuation of time after the Millennium, when God will be all-in-all as He was before rebellion started in the universe through Lucifer and Adam.

**6. THE TIMES OF THE GENTILES.** This refers to the dispensation or administration when Gentiles served as the rod of chastening upon Israel and to further God's purpose concerning them. It began with Israel's oppression in Egypt and continues until Christ's second coming, as we'll see in Lesson Fifteen.

**7. DISPENSATION.** The Greek word *oikonomia* means an administration, a stewardship of a household or estate, a dispensation — you could call it a guardianship. In 1 Cor. 9:17; Eph. 3:2; Col. 1:25, Paul speaks of his responsibility in preaching the gospel as a "dispensation of the gospel" and "the dispensation of the grace of God." The Millennium is called "the dispensation of the fullness of times," when God will gather all things in Christ and put all enemies under His feet (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Eph. 1:10).

The word "dispensation" comes from two Greek words: *oikos* (house) and *nomos* (law). Applied to the various ages, it means a moral or probationary period in human and angelic history. In each period, God tests free moral agents according to a fixed standard of conduct, under which they're supposed to remain true to God and rule for Him on earth or elsewhere in the universe.

Think of it like a series of job assignments from the same employer. Each assignment has its own start and end date, its own expectations, and its own evaluation. Each dispensation begins under favorable circumstances with a particular test — and ends in failure and judgment.

Nothing but confusion can come from reading meanings into Scripture that don't apply to a particular age. In each age, God has a different purpose. What He said during one dispensation may or may not apply to another.

### IV. The Dispensation of Angels

The dispensation or administration of angels came before Adam's time, as we'll see in Lesson Seven. It included rulership of the earth and other planets in the heavens.

Paul speaks of "invisible things" in the heavenly world as being just as real and understandable as visible things on earth (Col. 1:15-18). In fact, he says these "invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead; so that they are without excuse" (Rom. 1:19-23). In other words, there's no excuse for being ignorant of heavenly realities.

Paul also speaks of heavenly bodies as being just as real as earthly bodies: "There are also celestial bodies, and bodies terrestrial . . . There is a natural body, and there is a spiritual body . . . As is the earthy such are they also that are earthy: and as is the heavenly, such are they also that are heavenly" (1 Cor. 15:35-50).

Let's put aside the idea that heaven is some invisible nothing, or just a cloud where the redeemed float around playing harps for all eternity. The heaven where God dwells is a real planet — "a better country" than Earth (Heb. 11:14-16).

As we'll see in Lesson Forty-two, heaven is a real place with cities, mansions, food, water, trees, and other things we know in this visible world. Lucifer is described as having a throne (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17), and other thrones are mentioned in Col. 1:16-18 as existing in the heavens. These are apparently thrones of other angels, so Scripture does indeed teach the Dispensation of Angels.

### V. The Seven Dispensations of Man Between the Two Eternities

There are seven dispensations in human history between the eternal past and the eternal future — between man's creation in the six days of Gen. 1–2 and his final restoration in the New Heavens and New Earth of Rev. 21–22.

Here are the seven dispensations:

> (1) THE DISPENSATION OF INNOCENCE (Gen. 2:15–3:21). This age stretched from man's creation and commission to rule the restored earth to man's fall and expulsion from the Garden of Eden. We'll study this in Lesson Nine.

> (2) THE DISPENSATION OF CONSCIENCE (Gen. 3:22–8:14). This age ran from man's fall and expulsion from Eden to Noah's flood — a period of 1,656 years. We'll study this in Lesson Eleven.

> (3) THE DISPENSATION OF HUMAN GOVERNMENT (Gen. 8:15–11:32). This age spanned from Noah's flood to the call of Abraham — 427 years. We'll study this in Lesson Thirteen.

> (4) THE DISPENSATION OF PROMISE (Gen. 12:1—Exod. 12:37). This age covered the call of Abraham to Israel's Exodus from Egypt under Moses — 430 years. We'll study this in Lesson Fifteen.

> (5) THE DISPENSATION OF LAW (Exod. 12:38; Mt. 2:23; 11:10-13; Lk. 16:16). This age lasted from Israel's Exodus from Egypt to John the Baptist's preaching of the kingdom of heaven — from Moses to Christ's first coming, over 1,718 years. We'll study this in Lesson Seventeen.

> (6) THE DISPENSATION OF GRACE (Mt. 3:1—Rev. 19:10). This age has already lasted nearly 2,000 years since Christ's first coming and will continue until His second coming in the near future. How much longer it will last, no one knows. We'll study this in Lesson Nineteen.

> (7) THE DISPENSATION OF DIVINE GOVERNMENT—THE MILLENNIUM (Rev. 19:11–20:15). This age will cover Christ's second coming to the last rebellion of Satan and man — a period of 1,000 years. We'll study this in Lesson Forty-nine.

### VI. The Dispensation of the Redeemed and Faithful Angels

The final dispensation will come after the Millennium, in the New Heavens and New Earth. God's original purpose will then be fully realized, just as it was before rebellion started through Lucifer and Adam.

At that point, God will have a universe completely free from any possibility of rebellion throughout all eternity — governed solely by Himself along with redeemed human beings and faithful angels (Rev. 21:1–22:5; 1 Cor. 15:24-28). This administration will be eternal, as we'll prove in Lessons Forty-nine through Fifty-two.

Humanity will be restored to original glory. In the eternal future, we'll once again have dominion over all things through Christ, becoming "heirs of God, and joint heirs with Christ" of all creation (Rom. 8:17-18; 1 Cor. 15:24-28; Heb. 2:5-11).

This doesn't mean God will dethrone the faithful angels who have remained true throughout all the ages of rebellion. All free moral agents who have proven loyal to God will share in administering the affairs of the universe forever, as we'll see in future lessons.

That angels are free moral agents — and that many have sinned — is clear from 2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7; Rev. 12:7-12; Mt. 25:41. We'll study the doctrine of angels in Lesson Six.

### VII. Reasons for God's Dispensational Dealings

Why does God deal with free moral agents through various dispensational or probationary tests? Here are several reasons:

**1.** God created free moral agents with genuine choice rather than making them mere machines running forever with no will of their own. If you could create as God does, wouldn't you also choose to create beings with the power of choice instead of mindless robots?

It's similar to how we feel about building things. We're proud when we can make automatic machines that ease our work. How much more proud would we be if we could create machines with the ability to choose and act independently? We'd be free to do other things instead of being tied down to operating them.

But if we could create such beings, we'd naturally have to regulate and limit them, holding them responsible for their moral actions. Otherwise, they might rebel, destroy us, and rule in our place. We couldn't afford to create free agents and then hand over our existence to them without any boundaries.

The same is true with God. He made humanity as a wondrous machine — but a free, moral, self-acting one. So He must have reasonable restrictions and limits for the best interest of all. Otherwise, we might rebel and try to destroy the Creator. When we choose to rebel against God, there's war — just as there is whenever any two forces oppose each other.

Yet God continues to deal with us as free moral agents because that's how He created us. His dealings must be cooperative, for the welfare of all, or His universe would become chaotic and couldn't continue.

**2.** The wills of all free creatures must be tested to see if they're willing to cooperate with God for the greatest good of everyone. They must prove themselves trustworthy before being entrusted with the eternal administration of the universe.

**3.** All free moral agents must have their wills purged of any possibility of falling, so there can be complete mutual confidence. This guarantees no one will mar God's plan at any point in eternity through misuse of free will.

**4.** Free wills must be put through every possible test so that no transgression against any part of the eternal plan is possible at any time. This is the only sure way to guarantee the plan's success forever.

**5.** All free wills must eventually learn these lessons:

> (1) God must be respected and obeyed.

> (2) His laws are final and just.

> (3) Sin doesn't pay and will never be excused.

> (4) God's form of government is the only correct one.

> (5) Loving, free submission to God is the highest and most noble principle of free moral government.

> (6) Justice and righteousness must prevail, or no society can be preserved eternally.

> (7) Consecration to the greatest good of all is the nature and highest glory of any creature.

> (8) God is merciful and forgiving to rebels who repent and learn obedience through their experiences.

> (9) God is the only absolutely just and perfect Being — the only one capable and worthy of unquestionable authority.

> (10) All accusations current rebels make against God are untrue.

> (11) God does only what is for the common good of all creation.

By virtue of His position as Creator, Preserver, Governor, and Lord — and His history of justice and righteousness — God should be recognized by all as the Supreme Moral Governor of the universe. Until free moral agents learn these lessons, they remain ignorant of certain facts and need such training.

**6.** One of the main reasons God deals with humanity is to bring us back to where we were before the fall. Having purged us of all possibility of future falling, God will restore redeemed humanity to our original position of dominion over all things. God has no doubt whatsoever about the future success of His plan.

**7.** God's dealings with free moral agents in holiness and absolute justice give Him a sound basis for punishing rebels who refuse to conform and blessing those who do. God can still be just and holy in judging and confining rebels to everlasting punishment, since they've refused all merciful dealings and hardened themselves against everything good.

There's nothing else for God to do but give justice according to deeds — for the continued good of society forever. He must make examples of both the good and the bad for the sake of all generations to come in eternity.

God cannot excuse rebels and bless them contrary to His revealed plan, or there would be no end to the breaking of His eternal laws. He would have to excuse all wrongdoing forever if He started that pattern — and free wills would have no real restraint. If He excused some but not others, showing partiality, free agents would lose proper respect for Him. If He failed to reward obedience and punish disobedience, there would be no true incentive for free wills to conform to the eternal plan.

Scripture plainly reveals that rebels in everlasting hell will be an eternal monument of God's wrath toward sin, causing future generations to conform to the highest good of their own being and of the universe (Isa. 66:22-24; Rev. 14:9-11).

Why shouldn't we cooperate with God for the best interest of all, if such a place exists? Why shouldn't we, since it's the creative purpose and the only natural, normal life for each creature? Why shouldn't God punish rebels who persist in destroying themselves and the eternal society He planned? Why shouldn't He finally confine all eternal rebels away from that society? Why shouldn't God bless all who voluntarily and wholeheartedly cooperate with His plan?

This plan — rewarding obedience and punishing disobedience — is the only just approach with free moral agents. Everyone can be blessed if they choose, or cursed if that's their choice. Punishment isn't compulsory for anyone. It's the free choice of the individual, and each person alone is responsible for their eternal destiny. God's will is that none should

lessons.title: The Holy Scriptures
lessons.content:
# The Holy Scriptures

In this lesson, we're going to explore the wonderful facts about the Bible—how it came to be, the proofs that it's truly inspired by God, and how you can use it to get what you need in this life and the life to come.

## I. Names of the Bible

### 1. Bible

The word "Bible" refers to the collection of scriptures—both Old and New Testaments—that Christian churches recognize and use as they follow Jesus Christ.

You won't actually find the word "Bible" in the English versions of Scripture. It comes from the Greek word *biblia*, which simply means "books." The phrase *ta biblia* ("the books") appears in Dan. 9:2 in the Septuagint, referring to the prophetic writings. Early Christians adopted this usage for the Old Testament and eventually extended it to include the New Testament as well.

Here's something interesting: around the thirteenth century, common usage shifted the term from plural ("the books") to singular—"the Book." That's how we came to speak of the Bible as one unified volume.

### 2. Other Names of the Bible

Scripture goes by many names throughout its pages: the Scripture (Mk. 15:28; Jn. 7:38; 2 Tim. 3:16-17); the Scriptures (Lk. 24:27, 32; Jn. 5:39; Acts 17:11); the Holy Scriptures (Rom. 1:2; 2 Tim. 3:15); the Promises (Rom. 9:4-5; 15:8); the Oracles of God (Rom. 3:2; Heb. 5:12; 1 Pet. 4:11); the Lively (living) Oracles (Acts 7:38); the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms (Lk. 24:25, 44); the Law of the Lord (Ps. 1:2); the Law and the Prophets (Mt. 5:17; 11:13; Acts 13:15); the Book of the Lord (Isa. 34:16); the Word of God (Mk. 7:13; Rom. 10:17; Heb. 4:12); the Sword of the Spirit (Eph. 6:17); the Old and New Testament (Lk. 22:20; 2 Cor. 3:6-15; Heb. 9:15); the Word of Christ (Col. 3:16); the Word of Life (Phil. 2:16); the Scripture of Truth (Dan. 10:21); the Word of Truth (2 Tim. 2:15); and the Gospel of Christ (Rom. 1:16).

The word "testament" means a document that reveals someone's will, a contract, an agreement, or a covenant between two parties. All these meanings become clearer the more you study the Bible. By the end of the second century, "Old Testament" and "New Testament" had become the permanent names for the Jewish and Christian Scriptures.

The Old Testament is largely a record of God's dealings with the Hebrew people. It reveals His will to them and through them to the whole human race, showing how He binds Himself to take into a new and special relationship all who obey His will.

The New Testament is largely the fulfillment and expansion of the Old Testament. It gives us the record of God's promises, agreements, and covenants with humanity, showing the privileges, blessings, and requirements of the gospel through Jesus Christ, the Redeemer of the world.

## II. What the Bible is Not

> 1. The Bible is not an amulet, a charm, a fetish, or something that works wonders just by being present—without any action on your part. The Bible makes no such claim. What it *does* claim is that if you study and practice its teachings, it will work wonders in your life now and in eternity. Think of it like a spring of cool water in the desert: it won't benefit a thirsty man if he refuses to drink.

> 2. The Bible is not a book of strictly chronological events or one unbroken series of divine utterances. It was given piece by piece—"here a little, and there a little"—to many people across eighteen or more centuries (Isa. 28:9-11). Yet despite this, it forms a perfect unity, as we'll see.

> 3. The Bible is not a book of heavenly utterances in some supernatural language. It's God's revelation in the simplest human language possible.

> 4. The Bible is not a book of mysteries. It explains what it calls "mysteries," and it interprets itself, so that no mystery remains unsolved for those who seek.

> 5. The Bible is not a book that says one thing but means another. It generally has only one simple meaning. If a few passages have a double meaning, that's made clear either in the passage itself or in parallel passages. You really can't get a thousand different meanings from any single verse, despite what some people think.

> 6. The Bible is not a specimen of God's skill as a writer or logician. It's a book written by people whom God used to record His revelation. God gave these writers freedom to use their own language and ways of expressing truth. The writers were God's *penmen*, not God's *pens*. Inspiration guarantees unity of thought, not sameness of words and expressions.

> 7. The Bible is not a book of systematic, topical discourses on any one subject. However, it does provide divine information on practically every subject. You must gather together all of God's information from various writers throughout the Bible to get the whole truth. When you do this, you'll find perfect harmony and clarity on everything that really matters.

> 8. The Bible is not a book that conforms to the tastes, customs, or habits of any one nation, people, or time period. It's a book that all people in all ages can conform to, while still keeping their own customs and habits—as long as those aren't sinful or contrary to God's will.

## III. What the Bible Is

The Bible is God's inspired revelation of the origin and destiny of all things. It's the power of God for eternal salvation and the source of present help for body, soul, and spirit (Rom. 1:16; Jn. 15:7).

It's God's will and testament to people in all ages, revealing His plan for you here and now, as well as in the life to come. It's the record of God's dealings with humanity—past, present, and future. It contains God's message of eternal salvation to everyone who believes in Christ, and eternal damnation for those who rebel against the gospel.

As a literary composition, the Bible is the most remarkable book ever made. It's a divine library of sixty-six books—some quite large, others no bigger than a short pamphlet.

These books include all kinds of literature: history, biography, poetry, proverbs, hymns, letters, detailed instructions for worship, laws, parables, riddles, allegories, prophecy, drama, and every other form of human expression. They embrace every manner of literary style. From any standpoint, the Bible simply cannot be surpassed.

It's the book that contains the mind of God, the state of humanity, the way of salvation, the doom of sinners, and the happiness of believers. Its doctrines are holy, its commands are binding, its histories are true, and its decisions are unchangeable.

Read it to be wise, believe it to be safe, and practice it to be holy. It contains light to direct you, food to support you, and comfort to cheer you. It's the traveler's map, the pilgrim's staff, the pilot's compass, the soldier's sword, and the Christian's charter. Here heaven is opened and the gates of hell disclosed.

Christ is its grand subject, your good is its design, and the glory of God is its end.

Read it slowly, frequently, prayerfully, meditatively, searchingly, and devotionally. Study it constantly, perseveringly, and industriously. Read it through and through until it becomes part of your very being and generates faith that can move mountains.

It's a mine of wealth, a source of health, and a world of pleasure. It's given to you in this life, will be opened at the judgment, and will last forever. It carries the highest responsibility, rewards the smallest to the greatest labor, and condemns all who trifle with its sacred contents.

It is:
- A mirror to reflect (Jas. 1:23)
- A hammer to convict (Jer. 23:29)
- A fire to refine (Jer. 23:29)
- Seed to multiply (1 Pet. 1:23)
- Water to cleanse (Eph. 5:26; Jn. 15:3)
- A lamp to guide (Ps. 119:105)
- Food to nourish—including milk for babes (1 Pet. 2:2), bread for the hungry (Mt. 4:4), meat for the mature (Heb. 5:11-14), and honey for dessert (Ps. 19:10)
- Rain and snow to refresh (Isa. 55:10)
- A sword to cut (Heb. 4:12)
- A bow to avenge (Hab. 3:9)
- Gold to enrich (Ps. 19:7-10)
- Power to create life and faith (1 Pet. 1:23; Rom. 10:17)

## IV. General Facts About the Bible

### 1. How the Bible Was Given to Man

The Bible came to us through the audible voice of God (Mt. 3:16-17; 17:5; Jn. 12:28; Dt. 5:24), through angels (Acts 7:38; Heb. 2:2), through prophets (Acts 3:21; 2 Pet. 1:21), through Jesus Christ (Heb. 1:1-3; Rev. 1:1), and through the apostles (Acts 1:2; Eph. 4:7-11).

It came through visions (Isa. 6; Dan. 7–8; Ezek. 1), dreams (Dan. 2; Mt. 1:20; 2:12), revelation (Gal. 1:15-16; Eph. 3:3), and inspiration (2 Tim. 3:15-17).

### 2. Languages of the Bible

The Old Testament was written in Hebrew and Aramaic. The New Testament was written in Greek.

### 3. Divisions of the Bible (King James Version)

There are two main divisions: the Old Testament (39 books) and the New Testament (27 books). Each Testament is divided into five main sections.

**The Old Testament:**
- The Pentateuch (five books of Moses): Genesis through Deuteronomy
- Twelve historical books: Joshua through Esther
- Five poetical books: Job through Song of Solomon
- Five Major Prophets: Isaiah through Daniel
- Twelve Minor Prophets: Hosea through Malachi

**The New Testament:**
- Four Gospels: Matthew through John
- One historical book: Acts
- Fourteen Pauline Epistles: Romans through Hebrews
- Seven General Epistles: James through Jude
- One prophetic book: Revelation

The Bible as a whole contains 66 books, 1,189 chapters, and 31,214 verses, made up of 773,746 words and 3,566,480 letters.

There are nearly 1,250 promises to people in various ages, but only about 500 that modern Christians can definitely claim. Many promises concerned Israel and certain nations of the past, present, and future that don't specifically apply to Christian life today. However, there are still an abundance of promises for Christians covering every known need in this life and the life to come, as we'll see in the following lessons.

Here are some interesting Bible facts: The two longest words are in Isa. 8:1 and the title of Ps. 56. The longest verse is Esth. 8:9; the shortest verse is Jn. 11:35. The middle book is Micah; the middle verse is Ps. 118:8. The middle and shortest chapter is Ps. 117. The longest book is Psalms; the shortest is 2 John. The longest chapter is Ps. 119.

Chapter divisions were made in A.D. 1236. Four verses in Ps. 107 are alike: v 8, 15, 21, and 31. Also, 2 Ki. 19 and Isa. 37 are alike, as are Ezra 2 and Neh. 7, and 2 Chron. 36:22-23 with Ezra 1:1-3a.

There are 26 unknown books mentioned in the Bible. Since the invention of printing, over 1,330,231,815 Bibles have been printed and sold in England and Europe alone. Over 30 million are now distributed annually.

The first complete English Bible was finished in 1380. The first printed Bible was the Gutenberg Bible in 1450.

The only uninspired parts of Bibles are the chapter and verse divisions, references, marginal notes, chapter and page headings, footnotes, chapter summaries, and everything except the original text.

## V. The Apocryphal Books

In some large family Bibles, you'll find a section of fourteen books called the Apocrypha—a group of spurious books that were rejected from our present canon of Scripture. Here's why they were rejected:

> 1. They didn't pass the tests required of inspired books.

> 2. They weren't written or approved by a prophet.

> 3. They weren't recognized by the Jews as inspired or as part of Scripture.

> 4. They weren't recognized or even quoted by Christ and the Apostles—a striking fact, especially since Paul quotes twice from pagan poets.

> 5. The last Old Testament prophet predicted that the next messenger to Israel from God would be the forerunner of Christ (Mal. 3:1). Most of these books were written during the period between Malachi and Christ.

> 6. Their authors don't claim divine authority, and some virtually disown it (2 Macc. 2:23; 15:38).

> 7. They contain statements that contradict biblical history.

> 8. They are self-contradictory and oppose the doctrines of Scripture.

> 9. Josephus, who lived at the time of the apostles, didn't regard them as Scripture. He stated that the present books of the Old Testament in our version were the only inspired books (see Josephus, Book I, section 8).

> 10. They weren't part of the ancient versions of Scripture.

> 11. They were first added after A.D. 300. The Laodicean Council in A.D. 363 rejected them as uninspired, proving that by then some were claiming inspiration for them. They first appeared in the Vatican Version of the fourth century. At the Council of Trent in A.D. 1546, Catholics accepted six of these books as inspired and added them to their modern versions. These are: Wisdom of Solomon, Ecclesiasticus, Tobit, Judith, and 1 and 2 Maccabees.

> 12. Philo and others didn't regard them as inspired.

> 13. They lack prophetic content and appear to imitate other inspired Old Testament books.

> 14. They make too free a use of imagination, give rise to silly stories, and lack spiritual force and power.

## VI. The Bible: An Inspired Revelation From God

Christianity claims to be a revealed religion, and the record of that revelation is contained in the Sacred Scriptures. Let's look at some key points:

### 1. Reasons Man Can Expect a Revelation From God

Your intellectual and moral nature requires a revelation from God. Such a revelation helps you preserve and ensure the moral and spiritual progress you need—progress you cannot achieve on your own.

Within your moral makeup, there's an intuition, a reasoning, and a hunger for the supernatural and for knowledge of things that haven't been revealed. Since nothing in nature exists without a purpose, these natural cravings must be satisfied. The only way these desires can be met is through a supernatural revelation and manifestation, leading you to the end for which you were created.

Only a revelation of things beyond this life can fully satisfy the natural demands of your nature.

All history shows that in nations where biblical revelation isn't known, there's a very low standard of living. Because of imperfect knowledge, humanity's actual condition has been one of moral depravity and utter helplessness against evil forces and the higher demands of nature.

What we know of God through nature gives us grounds to believe that this lack in human life has been met by supernatural revelation. It's only right to believe that since God made you a moral and spiritual being for spiritual purposes, He also supplied the means to achieve those purposes.

It's not natural or reasonable to think the Creator would leave His creation incomplete—creating within you this longing for the unknown without meeting your needs with divine supply.

At the same time, both conscience and reason tell you that you're a sinner deserving condemnation. But reason alone can't tell you whether God will be merciful and save you. Your doubts can only be removed by God's own word to you. This requires a divine revelation.

### 2. What Man May Expect of a Divine Revelation

You should not only expect a revelation—you may also expect it to be in perfect harmony with nature, supplementing what nature already reveals. Those who are honest and rational about such proof may expect a revelation that is beyond all doubt from a supernatural source.

You may expect this revelation to come through chosen vessels and to be preserved by divine power through the ages. You may further expect it to be confirmed by miraculous works and prophetic utterances fulfilled to the letter.

All of this is true of the Bible, as we'll see in the proofs of inspiration below.

### 3. The Meaning of Revelation

The word "revelation" comes from the Greek *apokalupsis*, meaning to unveil, reveal, and uncover—like lifting a curtain so everyone can see what was previously hidden.

There's no excuse for different interpretations of God's revelation when we're looking at things that can be seen alike by all—if we all look at the same thing in the same way.

God's revelation is simply the unveiling of facts and truths you couldn't know on your own but are divinely revealed by the Spirit of God. About one-fifth of the Bible is prophecy, which required revelation. Then there are many other parts—creation, Lucifer's reign, God's plan, and many other things—that had to be revealed before anyone could know the facts about them.

The historical portions of the Bible didn't have to be revealed, but they're just as inspired as the revealed portions. Inspiration simply guarantees the true recording of facts as they happened—or as they're going to happen.

Both the method of disclosure and the truth disclosed are called revelation (Eph. 3:3; Col. 1:26; Rev. 1:1; Rom. 16:25).

### 4. The Meaning of Inspiration

The Greek word *theopneustos* literally means "God-breathed." Inspiration is that special influence or power of the Holy Spirit upon the minds and lives of holy people, which qualified and enabled them to make an infallible record of divine truth concerning God's will for humanity.

Paul said, "All scripture is given by inspiration of God [i.e., is God-breathed], and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness; that the man of God may be perfect, throughly furnished unto all good works" (2 Tim. 3:15-17).

Peter said, "God hath spoken by the mouth of all his holy prophets since the world began," and that "holy men of God spake as they were moved [borne along] by the Holy Ghost" (Acts 3:21; 2 Pet. 1:21).

### 5. The Purpose of Inspiration

The purpose of inspiration is to secure truth and unity in the record—not conformity or sameness of statement. Inspiration has accomplished this beyond human comprehension, as proven in Point VII below.

### 6. The Difference Between Revelation and Inspiration

Revelation discovers new truth, while inspiration supervises the communicating and recording of that truth.

We have examples of:
- Inspiration without revelation (Lk. 1:1-4)
- Inspiration including revelation (Rev. 1:1, 11)
- Inspiration without illumination (1 Pet. 1:11; 1 Cor. 14:13-19)
- Revelation and inspiration including illumination (1 Cor. 2:12-16; 14:30)

We find lies of Satan and people, false statements, murders, adulteries, and many sins recorded in Scripture. Inspiration simply records the truth of what was said or done. It guarantees that Satan lied and that what is written is actually what he said—but it doesn't change the lie into truth.

Inspiration records the sins of people, but it doesn't sanction those sins as God's will. As you study Scripture, you must keep in mind the difference between what Satan and people say versus what God actually says. God is responsible only for what He says and what He inspires people to say—not for what others say. All He's responsible for is the true record of what people and demons have said and done.

### 7. To What Degree Were the Writers of the Bible Inspired?

Some words were the exact words of God (Exod. 32:16; Deut. 5:4, 24; Mt. 3:17; 17:5; Jn. 12:28). Some words were put into the mouths of speakers who spoke as the Spirit inspired them (Exod. 4:12; Num. 23:5; Ezek. 2:7; 3:10-11; Acts 3:21). Some words were written as the writers were moved by the Spirit (Exod. 34:17; 2 Pet. 1:21; Rev. 1:11).

In other parts of Scripture, the speakers and writers were inspired to choose their own words in relating divine truth, or in saying things that were later recorded by the Spirit through others (Dan. 12:8-9; Jn. 20:30-31; Lk. 1:1-4; Acts 1:1-2).

Regardless of how the various passages were recorded, it was all by full and plenary inspiration—meaning the entire Bible was inspired by God.

In speaking and writing the divine record of things past, present, and future, the Spirit used the writers' attention, reason, investigation, memory, logic, and all their faculties. He guided them to choose material from others—spoken and written—such as imperial decrees, genealogies, letters, historical records, and whatever was necessary for recording the truth.

The Spirit worked in and through their own spirits and preserved their individualities in their writings. The Bible is truly the Word of God in human language, and it should be understood on the same basis we understand other books written in human language.

## VII. Proofs of Revelation and Inspiration

The following reasons are sufficient to prove to an unbiased mind that the Bible is an inspired revelation of God:

### 1. Its Wonderful Unity

Over 40 different authors wrote the 66 books of the Bible during a period of more than 1,800 years, and they all had one theme: the creation and redemption of the human race by God through Jesus Christ and the Holy Spirit.

These books were written by people from all walks of life—kings, priests, judges, lawyers, princes, shepherds, soldiers, courtiers, statesmen, musicians, inventors, singers, poets, preachers, prophets, fishermen, farmers, tentmakers, tax collectors, physicians, rich and poor alike.

They were written in various lands across three continents—Europe, Asia, and Africa. They were written in different ages by people who often never saw each other or knew what the others wrote on the same subjects. Yet when their writings became one book, there's not a single contradiction among them.

Think of it like this: suppose 40 medical experts, each in a different land and time period, each wrote their own book on how to cure a particular disease. What kind of cure would such a collection produce? How much unity would there be among their writings?

Or try this: collect 40 books on the same subject, each written by a different author, and see how many contradictions exist. Some will try to prove how the others are wrong and why their own theory is right. This is obvious to anyone who has read different authors on any subject.

There's no unity of thought between human books on any subject. But there's perfect unity between the books of the Bible, which speak of hundreds of subjects in religion, politics, science, and more.

This proves there's one divine author for all 66 books. Who else but a divine author could produce such a work?

### 2. Its Superiority to Other Books

There's abundant evidence that the Bible is superior to all other books. It's unlike any other book in its claims, its message, its moral tone, its insight into the future, and its words of peace, comfort, and hope for everyone. It's different because of its insight into human nature and into all things of life here and hereafter.

It's the outstanding book among millions of books—in a class by itself. Other books can be classified by subject matter, message, and style, but not the Bible. It doesn't fit into any human classification. Its unique origin, message, and wonderful structure all prove its superiority.

It's also unequaled in its benefits to people, its circulation, and its popularity. Despite all competition and the advertising advantages other books have, the Bible is still the best seller. More than 30 million copies are distributed annually.

Millions of people in all lands proclaim the Bible as the only book that satisfies the soul and gives hope of life beyond the grave. When a modern author's book is translated into a few languages, they're as proud as can be. The Bible has been translated into 1,086 languages and dialects, with new translations being made every year. There are twelve editions solely for the blind.

It's the most modern book of all ages, and all other books are hopelessly outclassed when compared to it.

From the standpoint of literature and truth, the Bible is a recognized authority on daily life as well as things in the next life. It has no equal. Millions of people in all ages have lived and died by its teachings. No such trust can be placed in any other book.

Religions, secret orders, and practically every kind of human institution claims to be based on the Bible. It's the simplest book to understand, yet it requires constant study. Even non-believers feel uneducated without a sound knowledge of its teachings.

The more you read, the more you realize how far you are from mastering its contents. There are always new discoveries of truth in the Bible. It's always new to those who read it most.

This isn't true of any other book. When you read other books once or twice, their contents are mastered, and the subject matter becomes old and uninteresting. All this proves the divine origin of the Bible.

### 3. Its Influence in the World

No book has ever influenced the world like the Bible. Its impact is as high as the heavens above the earth compared to other books. In lands where the Bible is unknown, this difference is plain to see.

While the Bible has shaped modern civilization, if humanity's current trend of rejecting the Bible continues, that civilization will be destroyed. Without the Bible, people would be in dense spiritual and moral darkness, mental ignorance, and pagan superstition.

Millions of lives have been enlightened and changed in every generation by its teachings, proving that its superior influence must come from a divine source.

### 4. The Character of Those Who Accept It

The character of those who accept the Bible proves it to be inspired by God. Those who grow in holiness and dedicate themselves to the highest good of others accept it as from God and cherish it more and more.

Only infidels, skeptics, moral and spiritual rebels, and human wrecks of all kinds refuse to accept its inspiration. Naturally, they reject it because it condemns all their activities and promises punishment in the end.

### 5. The Greatness of Those Who Accept It

The greatness of those who accept the Bible as God's revelation proves it's more than an ordinary human product. Some argue that the Bible and its religion are accepted only by the weak, the young, and the ignorant, and that its champions outside the pulpit are few.

This is one of the greatest errors among skeptics and shows either willful prejudice or superficial knowledge. The greatest thinkers and representative people of all ages have openly confessed their faith in Christianity and the Bible. The wise of the world, best qualified to detect error and imposture, have laid their homage at Christ's feet and confessed that the Bible is God's Word.

We have over a thousand testimonies from the greatest people since Christ—outside the clergy—who have left witness of their unwavering faith in the Bible as God's Word and in Christ as God's Son.

These leaders of human thought throughout the centuries—who weren't biased as preachers are accused of being, and who had no personal interest at stake—have simply been honest in acknowledging facts. When such people use their pen and voice to declare that Christ and the Bible are what they claim to be, it's time for skeptics to listen to facts without bias.

These people have been kings, presidents, senators, congressmen, diplomats, philosophers, scientists, poets, authors, historians, artists, philanthropists, reformers, educators, lawyers, physicians, soldiers, journalists, financiers, governors, and leaders of great movements—acknowledged representatives of the old and new world.

Carlyle said, "No sadder proof can be given a man of his own littleness than disbelief in great men." Webster said, "Great authorities are great arguments."

Here are a few testimonies to prove the point:

> "Above all, the pure light of revelation has had an influence on mankind, and increased the blessings of society. It is impossible to rightly govern the world without God and the Bible."—George Washington

> "I have always said that a studious perusal of the sacred volume will make better citizens, better fathers, and better husbands."—Thomas Jefferson

> "The first and almost the only book deserving of universal attention is the Bible. I speak as a man of the world."—John Quincy Adams

> "It [the Bible] is the rock on which our Republic rests."—Andrew Jackson

> "It was for the love of the truths of this great and good Book that our fathers abandoned their native shores for the wilderness."—Zachary Taylor

> "I am profitably engaged in reading the Bible. Take all of this Book that you can by reason and the balance by faith, and you will live and die a better man. It is the best book which God has given to man."—Abraham Lincoln

> "I have known ninety-five of the world's great men in my time, and of these eighty-seven were followers of the Bible."—W. E. Gladstone

> "Almost every man who has by his lifework added to the sum of human achievement . . . has based his lifework largely upon the teachings of the Bible."—Theodore Roosevelt

> "A man has deprived himself of the best there is in the world who has deprived himself of this" (a knowledge of the Bible).—Woodrow Wilson

> "There is no other book so various as the Bible, nor one so full of concentrated wisdom. Whether it be of law, business, morals, etc. . . . he who seeks for guidance . . . may look inside its covers and find illumination."—Herbert Hoover

> "The Bible contains more philosophy than all the libraries that I have ever seen; and such parts as I cannot reconcile with my little philosophy, I postpone for future investigation."—John Adams

> "To the influence of this Book we are indebted for the progress made in civilization, and to this we must look as our guide in the future."—Ulysses S. Grant

> "The young must be taught, and they must be taught truly if the spring-waters of democracy are to be kept untainted . . . The influence of the Scriptures in the early days of the Republic is plainly revealed in the writing and thinking of the men who made the nation possible . . . They found in the Scriptures that which shaped their course and determined their action."—Franklin D. Roosevelt

> "England has become great and happy by the knowledge of the true God through Jesus Christ . . . This is the secret of England's greatness."—Queen Victoria

> "Do not join those who either ignore the Bible altogether, or, at least, give it a spurious interpretation of their own devising."—Kaiser Wilhelm of Germany

> "What I was as an artist, seemed to me of some importance while I lived; what I really was as a believer in Christ Jesus, is the only thing of importance to me now. What can I do with respect to the next world without my Bible? No philosopher offers anything that meets, and, much less, relieves, my case."—John Bacon

> "The Bible is more than a Book; it is a living being with an action, a power which invades everything that opposes its extension."—Napoleon Bonaparte

> "The sacredness of the Bible awes me."—William Cohen Bryant

> "It is hard to make a choice of the most beautiful passage in a Book which is gemmed with beautiful passages as the Bible."—Mark Twain

> "The only spiritual light in the world comes through Jesus Christ and the inspired Book . . . Without His presence and the teachings of the Bible we would be enshrouded in moral darkness and despair . . . nations without Christ, contrasted with those where Christ is accepted, reveal so marked a difference that no arguments are needed."—Samuel Colgate

> "All other books are of little importance in comparison with the Holy Scriptures, which are a revelation from God, and are given as the only rule of faith and practice."—Alexander Cruden

> "Here is my creed: I believe in one God, the Creator of the Universe, that He governs it by His providence; that He ought to be worshipped; that the most acceptable service we can render Him is doing good to others; that the soul of man is immortal, and will be treated with justice in another world respecting his conduct in this. As to Jesus of Nazareth, I think His system of morals, as He left them

lessons.title: How to Interpret the Bible
lessons.content:
# LESSON THREE

## 3. HOW TO INTERPRET THE BIBLE

There's a widespread belief that the Bible is incredibly hard to understand—that it needs to be changed or decoded, that it's packed with mysteries and hidden meanings wrapped in spiritual language only a few "special" people can grasp. Some think the Bible can mean almost anything, that one person's interpretation is as good as another's, and that you can prove just about any point you want from its pages.

But here's the truth: not one of those ideas is even close to accurate. Let's look at what's actually true about understanding Scripture.

## I. The Bible is the Simplest Book to Understand

This probably sounds ridiculous at first. But if you take a moment to consider a few straightforward facts, I think you'll see how sensible this really is.

### 1. BECAUSE THE BIBLE IS A REVELATION

We saw in Lesson Two, Point VI, that the Bible is an inspired revelation from God. Think of it like this: a revelation is an unveiling—like pulling back a curtain so everyone can see what was previously hidden. The only reason someone wouldn't see what's been uncovered is if they simply refuse to look.

If something has truly been revealed, it's meant to be clear. Otherwise, the whole point of revealing it has failed.

### 2. BECAUSE OF ITS REPEATED TRUTHS

The Bible repeats its truths over and over so that "in the mouth of two or three witnesses every word may be established" (Dt. 17:6-7; 19:15; Mt. 18:16; 2 Cor. 13:1; 1 Tim. 5:19; Heb. 10:28). Any doctrine that isn't plainly stated in Scripture is best left alone.

If God didn't say anything about a particular question, then we have no right to teach anything about it as though it were in the Bible. We should limit our teaching to a "thus saith the Lord" or simply stay quiet. Our personal opinions carry no weight in proving something the Bible doesn't teach.

On the other hand, if God did address a subject, you'll find it mentioned in several places—so you won't be left guessing. All you need to do is collect everything God says on a topic, and it becomes so clear that no special interpretation is necessary.

When you do this honestly, nothing needs to be added to or taken from Scripture to arrive at the truth. Just find out where "it is written" and believe it. Always make your ideas conform to the Bible—never twist Scripture to fit your ideas. Anyone who presumes to know more than what God has said is putting themselves in God's place as the Author of Scripture.

### 3. BECAUSE THE BIBLE IS WRITTEN IN THE SIMPLEST HUMAN LANGUAGE POSSIBLE

Anyone who understands basic human language can understand what the Bible says. Every time any group of people reads a particular passage, they all read the same words. If they read it again, it will still say the same thing.

If you asked them to tell you what the passage says, they could all do it. And if they can tell you what it says—if they can read what it says—then they can all believe what it says. That's really all that's necessary to understand the Bible.

What's so hard about something everyone can read and believe alike without needing special interpretation?

### 4. BECAUSE GOD IS THE AUTHOR OF THE BIBLE

God Himself authored Scripture (2 Tim. 3:15-17; 2 Pet. 1:21). If God is the author, we have every right to expect it to be clear. No human being can make a book simpler than God can.

If God could have made a book as easy to understand as any person could—and chose not to—then we'd have to conclude He didn't want us to understand His Word. But why then would He give it to us? In that case, we'd have to throw out the Bible and accuse God of injustice for judging us based on something we can't comprehend.

Since we can't imagine God acting that way, we're left with one conclusion: the Bible is simple enough for everyone to understand.

A God who couldn't make Himself clear—or who needed interpreters every time He spoke—would be no God at all. Almost any human being can express themselves clearly enough to be understood! And a God who could make Himself clear but deliberately chose to confuse us wouldn't be worth listening to.

Think of it like a parent giving their child instructions for something important, then deliberately making those instructions impossible to follow—and punishing the child anyway. That wouldn't be a loving parent; that would be a tyrant. The same logic applies to God.

Let's reject these slanderous concepts of God! It's the devil's work to get people believing such things about God and His eternal revelation. Satan wants us to twist God's Word into something false so we'll face judgment for not believing what it actually says.

Let's wake up to this deception before it's too late. Satan knows that God means what He says—so he wants to deceive us into being lost.

Let's believe, like sensible people, that God can and did speak to us in the simplest human language. He meant exactly what He said and said exactly what He meant. He expects us to understand His Word using the same common-sense principles we use with any other book.

He will hold us responsible for what He says—not for what others interpret His words to mean. He has every right to judge people who constantly twist His words and listen to satanic theories instead of what He actually said.

Jesus Himself warned us: "He that rejecteth me, and receiveth not my words, hath one that judgeth him: the word that I have spoken, the same shall judge him in the last day" (Jn. 12:48; Rev. 20:11-15).

Shouldn't this be enough warning to stop the foolishness of making God's Word mean whatever we want? It's the height of arrogance to claim we can interpret the Bible better than God Himself. The Bible needs no interpretation apart from its own explanation of its contents. Scripture interprets its own terms, symbols, parables, allegories, and figures of speech.

So in these lessons, we're taking a different approach. We'll let God's own Word be the final authority on every question. We'll let what God says mean exactly what He says—and reject any contradicting human theory.

### 5. THE BIBLE IS SIMPLE BECAUSE IT WAS GIVEN BY GOD TO BE UNDERSTOOD BY THE SIMPLE

Following the logical argument that a perfect God cannot make anything imperfect, we can say scripturally that God did not fail in His purpose of giving mankind a simple revelation (Deut. 29:29; Ps. 119:104, 130; Prov. 1:1-4; 2 Tim. 3:15-17).

Paul speaks of "the simplicity that is in Christ" (2 Cor. 11:3). Jesus thanked God that biblical truths were hidden from the worldly-wise who refused to believe, saying God has "revealed them unto babes" (Mt. 11:25-27).

Why are truths hidden from some people? Because they refuse to humble themselves to believe and conform to the Bible (Mt. 13:10-17). Jesus speaks of the devil taking the Word from people's hearts before it can bear fruit (Mt. 13:19-23). Paul describes Satan blinding people's minds and people willfully handling God's Word dishonestly (2 Cor. 4:1-6).

The simplest beginner can understand the Bible one line at a time—that's how it was given, and it's the best way to understand it (Isa. 28:9-13). No one can grasp the vastness of the Bible all at once. It's this seemingly infinite scope that makes some people think the Bible is difficult.

It's similar to someone claiming they can't understand water because they can't drink the entire ocean in one sitting! Naturally, it takes time to gain a working knowledge of the whole Bible. But here's our point: it cannot be hard to understand if you take it one line at a time, one verse at a time, one truth at a time.

You can't look at any big book and absorb its entire contents at a glance. Anyone who says the Bible is hard to understand before they've actually dug into it is being foolish. Once you do, you'll find truths opening up beyond your wildest dreams.

Those who insist the Bible is hard to understand generally fall into these categories:

> (1) Those who are ignorant of its contents.

> (2) Those who are too lazy to master its contents.

> (3) Those who are biased against its contents (Mt. 15:7-14; Jn. 8:43).

> (4) Those who refuse to believe what it says and get tangled up in hopeless theories and interpretations they've been taught.

> (5) Those who've heard the old theory that it's hard to understand and give up before they even try.

> (6) Those raised in the wrong school of thought about the Bible who wonder if God really means what He says (Mt. 16:6-12; Lk. 24:25; Mk. 16:14).

> (7) Those who are too worldly-wise, thinking they know more than the Bible, discounting its contents through unbelief and pride.

> (8) Those who are unstable and unlearned and twist the scriptures to their own destruction (2 Pet. 3:16).

> (9) Those who willfully handle God's Word deceitfully to cover up their own sins, which the Bible condemns (2 Cor. 4:1-4).

> (10) Those who let Satan steal the Word from their lives, causing them to be blind to truth (Mt. 13:19-23; 2 Cor. 4:1-4).

> (11) Those who make money by fighting the Word of God (Mt. 15:7-14; 2 Pet. 2:1-3).

> (12) Those who refuse to be converted and become as little children (Mt. 18:3; 28:9-15; Isa. 6:9-10; Acts 28:24-29).

### 6. THE BIBLE IS SIMPLE BECAUSE READING AND BELIEVING WITH A SIMPLE HEART IS ALL GOD CONSIDERS NECESSARY TO UNDERSTAND IT

God commands all people to be saved and to understand His Word (Ps. 32:9; Prov. 4:5; 8:4-10). We're not to add to or take from the Bible in any detail—we must take it as written (Deut. 4:2-6; 12:32; Rev. 22:18-19).

We're instructed to search and study the Bible and apply our hearts to it—which means we must be able to understand it (Jn. 5:39; Ps. 1:2-3; 90:12; 2 Tim. 2:15; 3:15-17). God created us with a spirit capable of understanding His Word. He made both us and His Word—and they fit together like a lock and key (Job 32:8; 38:3-6; Jn. 1:4-9).

Even unbelievers can understand if they truly want to (Rom. 1:16-20). So the old theory that you must be saved and spiritual to understand the Bible isn't scriptural. All the references above apply to everyone—saved and unsaved alike.

Anyone can understand the letter of the Bible. And if you want the experiences it teaches, you can understand them through the letter well enough to attain them. Because some don't live up to the light they receive, God has a just basis to judge them.

Some people point to 1 Cor. 2:14 to argue that the Bible is hard to understand. But that passage says the "natural man" cannot receive the things of the Spirit—it doesn't teach that sinners can't understand the written Word. Others use 2 Pet. 3:16-18 similarly, but that passage refers to the "unstable and unlearned" who twist scriptures to their own destruction.

If sinners couldn't understand the Bible, how could they get saved or know what God requires? How could God judge them for things they can't comprehend? The reality is that many sinners understand the Bible better than some believers because they're sensible enough to take it at face value.

If all believers did the same, they'd have a distinct advantage—because they have God's Spirit to illuminate them, while unbelievers don't (Jn. 14:17; Rom. 8:9).

### 7. THE BIBLE IS A SIMPLE BOOK TO UNDERSTAND BECAUSE MOST OF IT IS HISTORY AND SIMPLE INSTRUCTIONS ABOUT HOW TO LIVE

About 25,007 verses—around 80 percent of the Bible—contain straightforward history, commands, warnings, promises, rebukes, and plain instructions for understanding God's will. What could be in any of these verses that a normal person couldn't understand? Anyone with common intelligence should be able to comprehend them.

The other 6,207 of the Bible's 31,214 verses are prophecy, written in the same simple human language used for history. Of these, 3,299 have already been fulfilled—meaning they're now history too. The remaining 2,908 verses are unfulfilled prophecy, but they're just as easy to understand as history.

Picture it this way: prophecy is simply history written beforehand. If you can understand what has happened by reading a clear record of it, you should be able to understand what will happen by reading the same kind of clear record.

What's hard about either history or prophecy? What's hard about any part of the Bible? We conclude that the Bible is remarkably simple to understand—if we believe it as written and let it guide our faith and practice.

## II. Definition of Terms

### 1. INTERPRET

As we use this word, it means to state the true sense of God's message exactly as He expresses it. It means giving readers the exact statements of Scripture without changes to prove any point discussed—stating precisely what God says and where He says it. We consider this all-sufficient.

The modern way of interpreting the Bible often means changing what's written to suit personal preferences and make Scripture fit human theories. We call this "how not to interpret the Bible" because it violates every sensible principle of true interpretation and elevates human authority above God regarding His own Word.

The Bible is clear in itself when traditions, wrong interpretations, changes, and spiritualizing are set aside. The average person is blind to many simple truths of Scripture because they've been covered over with human traditions and interpretations designed to serve a church, a party, or personal whims.

Our purpose in this lesson is to expose this overlay and draw attention to what God actually says—which is always clear in itself.

### 2. HERMENEUTICS

This is the science or art of interpretation and explanation. It comes from the Greek word *ermeneuo*, meaning to explain, expound, and interpret (Jn. 1:38-42; 9:7; Heb. 7:2). It's the science that establishes and classifies the principles, methods, and rules for discovering an author's intended meaning.

How you interpret any piece of literature depends on what kind of writing it is. Poetry, history, fiction—each requires different rules. Rules for interpreting fiction wouldn't work for history. Similarly, the rules for Bible interpretation depend on the character of its different types of writing, just as with other books.

Since the Bible is written in human language like other books, it must be interpreted like other literature. If heavenly, supernatural, and spiritual truths are expressed in human language, we must understand them on that basis. We interpret words and expressions in the Bible the same way we would if we found them outside it.

There's no special Bible logic, rhetoric, or grammar. The laws of grammar apply to Scripture just as they do to other writings.

Christ and His disciples demonstrated this method. In about 400 quotations from the Old Testament in the New Testament, the phrase "It is written" is treated as all that's necessary to prove God's message. This settles every doctrinal point today if we want to be Christ-like.

Not one example exists in Scripture where the plain, literal sense was replaced by allegorical, mystical, speculative, spiritualizing, or symbolizing methods so common today. We must abandon such approaches if we want true knowledge of the Bible.

### 3. BIBLICAL HERMENEUTICS

This is the science that establishes and classifies the principles, methods, and rules by which God's Word is made plain.

### 4. EXEGESIS

This is applying the rules of biblical hermeneutics to unfold the meaning of a Scripture passage. Interpretation expresses exactly another's mind and thoughts—it's purely reproductive, involving no originality from the interpreter.

Exegesis uses the science of interpretation to reproduce God's thoughts as expressed in Scripture. Being a faithful exegete carries great responsibility and great reward. But being an eisegete—someone faulty in explaining Scripture—may cost both the interpreter's soul and those who follow them.

## III. Reasons for Biblical Hermeneutics

> 1. The Bible is a heavenly message conveyed in human language, so the same interpretation principles used with all human language must apply to Scripture.

> 2. The Bible's original languages differ in some ways from English in grammatical structure and idiomatic usage. Understanding these differences is necessary for certain passages.

> 3. The Bible is a composite book of 66 books.

> 4. The Bible is a religious book for this life and the life to come.

> 5. The Bible is varied—an oriental library containing all forms of human expression and literature.

> 6. The Bible comes from many lands and peoples with habits and customs different from ours.

> 7. The changing nature of English and its imperfect translation of every Hebrew and Greek nuance makes certain rules necessary for understanding particular passages.

## IV. Two Seemingly Contradictory Ways to Read the Bible
