> 1. Read it like any other book—from beginning to end—grasping each writer's thought, the meaning of words and unique expressions, the customs of Bible lands and times, God's purpose in each message, and the particular audience addressed.

> 2. Read it differently from any other book because it's inspired. Much of it is direct revelation from God (2 Tim. 3:15-17). Read it slowly, prayerfully, frequently, reverently, meditatively, searchingly, perseveringly, believingly, and obediently.

## V. Historical Sketch of Hermeneutics

A brief understanding of interpretation history has great value. It helps guard against repeating others' errors and shows what influences caused those mistakes. The science of interpretation has gone through many false concepts, just like other sciences. But thankfully, like other sciences, we're starting to demand reasons for particular interpretations.

Past efforts have demonstrated the foolishness of abandoning Scripture's plain literal sense. The belief that the Bible was divine almost completely blinded ancient interpreters to its human elements—its literary and grammatical construction, its history, and its literal, intended meaning.

Both Jews and Christians sought hidden meanings in the smallest jot and tittle of the text. They didn't consider God's original purpose. Like many people today, their main focus seemed to be proving their own speculations, regardless of how much they contradicted the Bible.

Through this abuse, the Bible came to be seen as a mysterious book beyond ordinary understanding. Among Jews, only rabbis were thought capable of understanding it. Among Christians, only select church leaders could supposedly unravel its mysteries. Millions today are still taught that common people can't understand the Bible and should leave interpretation to priests and preachers. This is one of Christianity's greatest fallacies.

### 1. THE JEWISH METHOD OF INTERPRETATION

Jewish interpretation from Ezra to Christ can be traced through the Apocrypha, Philo's works, Josephus, and the Talmud. Interpreters of this period valued each letter and believed each contained great mysteries. They assigned numerical values to letters and imposed fantastic meanings on plain historical statements.

For example, the letters in "Eliezer" have a numerical value of 318 in Hebrew. Gen. 14:14 says Abraham had 318 trained servants—so this was interpreted to mean Eliezer equaled all these servants in value. "Keturah" means "sweet odor" in Hebrew. Abraham married Keturah—which was interpreted to mean he wedded a holy life.

Gen. 25 tells us Abraham had six sons by Keturah. Following Jewish interpretation, we couldn't believe the literal account that Abraham married a woman and had children with her!

The scribes carefully guarded against text errors, but they established oral tradition that eventually was considered equal to Scripture. Christ swept away all these traditions and accepted the plain, literal written Word as the only truth (Mk. 7:1-13). Paul likewise rebuked the Jews for prioritizing tradition over God's Word (Gal. 1:13-14; Col. 2:8; 1 Tim. 1:4; 6:20; 2 Tim. 2:14-16; Titus 1:14; 3:9).

### 2. THE ALLEGORICAL METHOD OF INTERPRETATION

Instead of following Christ and the apostles in taking Scripture's plain literal meaning, the early Church Fathers largely followed Jewish methods. The literal sense was buried under allegorical, moral, and spiritual interpretations.

Origen gave all Scripture a threefold meaning corresponding to body (literal), soul (moral), and spirit (spiritual or mystical). His influence lasted centuries. Clement interpreted Rahab's scarlet cord as Christ's blood. Chrysostom believed Solomon's throne's six steps represented six steps to God. Nearly everything was treated this way—the literal and historical meaning was almost entirely replaced by spiritual and mystical meanings.

This approach continued until the Reformation, when Scripture was increasingly freed from earlier spiritualizing tendencies and studied more literally. Despite this freedom, many ministers today have reverted to the unintelligent methods of the past. We should completely reject such foolishness.

These interpreters disregard the common meaning of words, grammatical construction, and God's literal intention. They force whatever meaning they fancy into Scripture, making themselves equal to God and their interpretations superior to His plain Word.

### 3. THE RATIONALISTIC METHOD OF INTERPRETATION

Several modern interpretation methods are rationalistic in spirit—substituting reason for faith and human speculation for divine revelation. They explain away the supernatural, miracles, eternal judgment, atonement, resurrection, and all biblical essentials.

These methods let people choose their own Scripture meanings—which are never what's actually written. They're lawless and irrational, with no agreement among adherents. Sin, Satan, sickness, and the realities of this life and the next are explained away as errors of mortal mind and unreal things. God's Word is nullified by human theories.

## VI. Summary of the False Methods of Interpretation

> 1. An excessive reverence for Scripture that manifests in searching for hidden meanings in every letter and word. This amounts to idolatry of the letter and leads away from Scripture's real meaning. This is why so many today find more written "between the lines" than in the lines themselves. They can seemingly prove doctrines never once mentioned in Scripture, yet can't seem to prove those stated repeatedly.

> 2. A hostile attitude toward the text, resulting in vain attempts to eliminate the supernatural and redemption through Jesus Christ. This utterly destroys God's Word as far as real benefit in this life and the next.

## VII. The True Method of Bible Interpretation

The chief fundamental principle is gathering from Scripture itself the precise meaning the writers intended. We apply to the Bible the same principles, rules, grammatical processes, common sense, and reason that we use for other books.

In doing this, take the Bible literally whenever possible. When you encounter a statement that can't possibly be literal—like Jesus being a "door," or a woman "clothed with the sun and standing on the moon with a crown of twelve stars," or "land animals coming out of the sea"—you know the language is figurative.

In such cases, find the literal truth conveyed by the figurative language. That truth will be just as real as if expressed in literal terms. After all, figurative language expresses literal truth just as surely as non-figurative language does.

In general, the true method encompasses:

> 1. The primary meaning of words, their common use in the particular era, and the importance of synonyms.

> 2. The grammatical construction and idiomatic peculiarities of the Bible's languages, and the meaning of both immediate and remote context.

> 3. Comparison of parallel passages on the same subject.

> 4. The purpose or object of each writer in each particular book.

> 5. The historical background of each writer and the circumstances of writing.

> 6. The general plan of the entire Bible and its moral and spiritual teachings.

> 7. Scripture's agreement in its several parts, plus prophecies and their fulfillment.

> 8. The manners and customs of each writer's particular age and land.

> 9. Understanding how to interpret prophecy, poetry, allegories, symbols, parables, figures of speech, types, and all other forms of human expression.

> 10. The different classes of people and institutions dealt with in Scripture, and applying the appropriate principles and rules.

When you keep all these facts in mind and interpret Scripture accordingly, there simply can't be any misunderstanding. Remember this: take the Bible literally whenever possible. When language can't be taken literally, it's figurative. Then find the literal truth the figurative language conveys, just as if it were expressed in literal terms.

## VIII. General Rules of Biblical Interpretation

1. The entire Bible came from God and possesses unity of design and teaching. We therefore consider both Testaments equally inspired.

2. We can assume no one speaks or writes without having an idea to express, and they'll use words and expressions familiar to their audience. If they use a word or figure differently from its common meaning, they'll make that clear.

3. The Bible cannot contradict itself. Its teachings in one part must agree with its teachings elsewhere. Any interpretation making the Bible inconsistent with itself rests on false principles.

4. Passages on Christian experience can't be fully understood beyond the letter until we enter into those experiences ourselves. Christian experience should be founded on the Bible—not Scripture interpreted through experience.

5. No meaning should be extracted from the Bible except what fair, honest, grammatical, and historical interpretation yields.

6. Language is an accumulation of words used to exchange thoughts. To understand a speaker or writer, you must know what their words mean. True word meanings yield true understanding. This is as true of the Bible as any other book.

7. Often, fully understanding a passage requires knowing the scope or plan of the entire book. Sometimes the design is clearly stated—Proverbs (1:1-4), Isaiah (1:1-3), John (20:31), Revelation (1:1). When not explicitly stated, determine the purpose from the contents and the Bible's overall design, as clear in Jn. 5:39; 2 Tim. 2:15; 3:16-17. Some apparent contradictions clear up when this rule is followed. The difference between Paul and James is easily understood when you recognize their books' different purposes. In Romans, Paul proves a person isn't saved by works; in James, he shows a person can't remain saved without producing good works.

8. Sometimes the connection is obscured through virtual dialogue between writers and unseen persons, as in Ps. 15; Isa. 52:13; 63:1-6; Rom. 3; etc.

9. One of the most fundamental rules is comparing Scripture with Scripture. By strictly and honestly observing this rule, you can find the true meaning when everything else has failed. Before concluding on any topic, collect all the Scriptures on that subject and read them together. If questions remain, go over everything again until every question is resolved. A major fault among many people is accepting only part of Scripture on a subject while rejecting passages that contradict their theory. This is dishonest with the Bible and leads to darkness rather than light.

10. Not only should all passages be compared until perfectly harmonized, but the words different writers use should be compared too. Words often change meaning from one age to another. The Bible was written in different lands, some parts about 1,800 years apart, so word comparison is essential to see if the same words mean the same thing across different eras. The subject matter always helps determine this.

11. Sometimes a brief, seemingly obscure statement on a subject becomes perfectly clear through a longer passage on the same topic. Always explain the seemingly difficult with the simpler Scriptures. No doctrine founded on a single verse contains the whole subject, so don't be dishonest—don't force a meaning into a passage that isn't clearly there or in parallel passages. Be honest, open-minded, studious, and zealous to arrive at the whole truth and nothing but the truth. Determine whether the language is literal or figurative, whether you understand the right meaning of words and terms, and whether they have only one meaning. Choose the meaning that best harmonizes with the passage itself and all other passages on the same subject.

12. Recognize the progressive character of revelation and gradual development of truth. Some truths found in seed form in the Old Testament are fully developed in the New. For example, blood sacrifices were progressively developed from Abel's time until fully culminating in Christ's sacrifice at Calvary.

13. Don't carry a New Testament word or phrase meaning back into Old Testament doctrine unless both Testaments warrant it. Water baptism, the Lord's Supper, and other New Testament doctrines aren't found in the Old Testament. It's not proper to ask whether David was baptized in water or whether Saul was a Christian—these are New Testament terms.

14. Don't spiritualize obviously literal passages. For example, making Canaan's natural blessings represent Heaven's spiritual blessings, or regarding Noah's ark as salvation through Christ, and countless similar interpretations. You may draw lessons and illustrations from historical passages for sermons, but in interpretation, take them literally—don't spiritualize them. Such lessons from Old Testament events form the basis of many church doctrines in some circles. Some can't discuss Christ and His bride without referencing Adam and Eve, Isaac and Rebekah, etc. If you want to prove a church doctrine, get plain passages on the subject—don't base proof on historical events that literally happened and weren't recorded as types. Always get two or three plain Scriptures to prove a doctrine, or let it go.

15. Note the dispensational character of Scripture so you can place every passage in a definite period of God's plan.

16. Note the three classes of people dealt with in Scripture: Jews, the Church, and Gentiles. Up to Gen. 12, the race as a whole is addressed. From Gen. 12 to the New Testament, Jews and Gentiles are addressed. In the New Testament, these plus the Church of God (made up of Jews and Gentiles) are addressed (1 Cor. 10:32).

17. In all doctrinal study, keep the practical aspect in view (2 Tim. 3:16-17).

18. Emphasize the comparative importance of truth. Study positive truths more than negative ones. It's more important to have faith than unbelief, to know God better than Satan. So learn more about faith and God than about unbelief and Satan.

19. General familiarity with the Bible as a whole is very important. Keep reading through the Bible until its overall contents become familiar. The more you remember of what you've read, the clearer the Bible becomes.

20. Keep some "musts" and "must nots" in mind while studying Scripture. Don't handle God's Word deceitfully (2 Cor. 4:1-4). Don't insist the Bible is hard to understand. Don't misapply Scripture to a subject or age it doesn't address. Don't misinterpret Scripture. Let the Bible be its own interpreter and be satisfied accepting its own authority on any subject's meaning.

In our Lord's time, accepting the mass of Jewish reasonings, traditions, and interpretations piled on Scripture was considered orthodox. Returning to the authority of Scripture itself was heterodox—this was Jesus' most serious offense to the Jews (Mt. 15:1-9; 16:6-12; 23:1-36; Mk. 3:1-6; 7:1-13; Jn. 5-8).

Today, church council decisions, church leader decrees, and fanciful spiritual interpretations have nearly nullified God's Word and Scripture's literal sense. Some groups dismiss scriptures contrary to their theories by saying "that's a parable" or "that's figurative language"—as if such language means nothing.

21. Words of Scripture must agree with the content and subject matter in their passages. No meaning should be given to a word that would be out of harmony with any Scripture. For example, "seen" in Jn. 1:18 should be understood as "comprehended" to harmonize with all scriptures stating that men saw God with their natural eyes.

22. Pay careful attention to connecting words that link events—words like "when" and "then" in Mt. 24:15-16, 21, 23, 40; 25:1.

23. Pay careful attention to prepositions, definite articles, names of different persons and places with the same name, same persons and places with different names, and different spellings by different authors in different books.

24. Ascertain the exact meaning of Scripture's words. How a word is used, the subject matter, and the context often determine the true meaning.

25. Note Hebrew and Greek idioms. Sometimes a person with a peculiar characteristic, or subject to a peculiar evil, or destined to a particular destiny is called the "child" of that evil or destiny (Lk. 10:6; Eph. 2:1-3; 2 Thess. 2:3). "Father" is applied to the originator of any custom or inventor of something (Gen. 4:20-21; Jn. 8:44) and is also used for ancestor (1 Chron. 1:17). "Son" and "daughter" are sometimes used for descendants or in-laws (Gen. 46:22; Lk. 3:23). "Brother" and "cousin" are sometimes used for relatives and countrymen (Gen. 14:16 with 11:31; Lk. 1:36, 58). Names of parents are used for posterity (1 Ki. 18:17-18).

26. Preference is sometimes expressed by the word "hate" (Lk. 14:26; Rom. 9:13).

27. A peculiar idiom concerning numbers must be understood. Sometimes round numbers rather than exact numbers are used (Judg. 20:35, 46). This explains seem

lessons.title: The Truth About God
lessons.content:
# LESSON FOUR

## 4. THE TRUTH ABOUT GOD

Have you ever noticed how most people who believe in God start their conversations about Him with an apology? "Well, we really can't know much about God..." Preachers and authors almost universally make God out to be completely incomprehensible, totally beyond human reason. And yet—here's the irony—they'll preach lengthy sermons and write thick books about a subject they claim nobody can really understand!

It seems like many think the best way to honor God is to turn Him into a big mystery. This is one of the greatest errors in the Christian Church today.

But here's the thing: there's simply no good excuse for this kind of ignorance about God. The Bible contains over 20,000 references about Him, spelling out in remarkable detail what He's like, what He can and cannot do, and what He has done and still plans to do. We'll magnify God best by believing and teaching everything the Bible actually says about Him. We can develop a comprehensive knowledge of God from these many references—if we'll just believe what they say and stop treating everything God says about Himself as somehow untrue.

Over the centuries, people have spiritualized and twisted so many statements about God in Scripture that they've essentially emptied the Bible's true revelation of Him. Think of it like taking a clear photograph and applying so many filters that you can't recognize the original image anymore. That old approach hasn't given us a true, sensible, simple knowledge of God, so why not try something different?

Why not believe what God says about Himself in the same straightforward way we understand similar language when it's used about other people? Why not trust that God means what He says? He should know more about Himself than any human being does!

Wouldn't it be easier to believe what He actually says about Himself than to believe things He never said? If God didn't mean what He said, why would He say such things in the first place? Why should it be some kind of unforgivable error to believe the Bible literally on this subject, just as we believe it literally on other topics? Taking God at His word simply makes better sense—it gives us a clearer, more "common sense" understanding of who He is.

We've all been taught that God cannot be understood, and nearly every theology book makes the subject of God vague and unreachable for ordinary people. Such books are quite good at explaining away what the Bible actually says about God! For once, let's set aside all that confusion and simply stand with what the Bible plainly states. That approach certainly can't make things any more mysterious than they already seem.

We can't go wrong if we simply believe what God reveals about Himself. Since we've already seen how to understand the Bible literally, let's be wise and follow this practice with every subject in Scripture—not just the ones we've chosen to take at face value.

Here's something that strikes me as odd: people can understand all about the Lord's Supper, baptism, or any other topic from just a handful of passages. But when it comes to God Himself, suddenly they claim they can't grasp anything about Him—even though there are thousands of passages on the subject! That attitude just doesn't make sense.

So let's study God from a different angle. Let's believe what the Bible says and see for ourselves that this subject has been greatly misunderstood.

---

## I. Definition of the Terms Used

### 1. GOD
This word simply means deity or divinity. It's a general term used both for false gods and for the true God. You can't determine how many persons are in the true deity just from the word itself—you need clear scriptures on the subject to settle that question.

### 2. GODHEAD
This term simply means "that which is divine." It's used of Jesus in Col. 2:9, showing that He has all the qualities of divinity in His manifestation of God to humanity. It's also used of all three persons in the deity in Rom. 1:20.

### 3. ONE
The Hebrew word for "one" in scriptures like "one Lord" (Dt. 6:4-6) and "one God" (Mal. 2:10) is *achad*, meaning to unify, collect, or be united as one. It's frequently used to describe unity: "they shall be one flesh" (Gen. 2:24); "the people is one" (Gen. 11:6).

The Greek word for "one" in "one Lord" and "one God" in Mk. 12:29, 32 is *heis*, which means to gather together in one (Jn. 11:52) and to be one in unity (Jn. 10:30; 17:11, 21-23; 1 Jn. 5:7-8). Our English word "one" can also mean "one in unity," as these passages show. Whether a particular passage means "one in unity" or "one in number" must be determined by Scripture, not by the word's meaning alone.

### 4. IN
This word means "in union with." When used of persons, it doesn't mean physically entering into another body—except in the case of disembodied spirits or demons.

We read of God being in Christ (2 Cor. 5:19) and Christ being in God (Jn. 14:10-11, 20); of believers being in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17) and Christ being in believers (Rom. 8:10); of believers being in the Spirit and the Spirit being in believers (Rom. 8:9); and of Satan entering into someone (Jn. 13:27). But none of these mean physical entrance into another body, because all these persons have bodies and can't literally get inside each other.

When Paul said, "I have you in my heart" and "ye are in our hearts" (2 Cor. 7:3; Phil. 1:7), he could only mean "in union with," not physically inside. It's similar to how we might say a loved one is "always in my heart"—we don't mean they're literally living inside our chest!

The Bible's teaching about interpenetration means the union of two or more persons together for the same purpose. People can be "one with" each other toward a common goal without literally getting inside each other or becoming a single person. Being united doesn't require physical contact or losing one's individual personality. You can be "in" someone and "one with" them even if you're thousands of miles apart.

People influence each other to the point of being unified in purpose. The same is true of God and Satan, who influence people toward union with their respective purposes. So when God dwelled in Christ and Christ dwelled in God, it didn't mean they were one person or that they lived bodily inside each other. They were one in union—united toward the same purpose, in the same way that believers and Christ, or believers with each other, "dwell in" one another. "He that is joined unto the Lord is one Spirit" (1 Cor. 6:17).

### 5. PERSON
A person is anyone who can act, think, and feel; anyone capable of self-consciousness and self-determination; any individual with legal rights and duties; a rational being with bodily presence, soul passions, and spirit faculties.

In grammar, it refers to one of three separate relations distinguished by certain pronouns: the speaker (first person), the one spoken to (second person), and the one spoken of (third person).

First, second, and third personal pronouns are all used of God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit when they speak to and about each other, as we'll see in Lesson Twenty-seven. Both singular and plural personal pronouns are used as well.

**God is a Real Person:**

> (1) Personal names are given Him (Gen. 1:1; Exod. 3:13-15; 6:3; Ps. 68:4), and His names appear over 19,000 times in Scripture.

> (2) Personal statements are made about God, just as with other persons. See Over 500 Scriptures Proving a Divine Trinity in Lesson Twenty-seven.

> (3) Personal relationships are ascribed to God (2 Cor. 6:1; 13:14; 1 Jn. 1:3; 3:1; Rev. 21:3-7).

> (4) Personal plans are ascribed to Him (Lesson One).

> (5) A personal body, soul, and spirit are ascribed to Him (Point II, 7-9).

> (6) Personal works are ascribed to Him. Many personal acts are recorded in the first two chapters of the Bible.

> (7) Personal pronouns are used of all three persons of the divine Trinity, both singular and plural (Gen. 1:26; 3:22; 11:7; Isa. 6:8; Jn. 17:11-21). See Point I, Lesson Twenty-seven.

### 6. INCARNATION
This means a person taking on a body as his very own, dwelling inside that body and not existing in any sense outside of it (Ps. 40:6-10; Heb. 10:5-10).

### 7. FATHER AND SON
A father is one who has brought a child into existence. A son is the one brought into being by a father. It takes two separate persons to be a father and a son—they could never be one person, but they can certainly be one in unity, just as any two people can be.

### 8. TRINITY
This means the union of three persons—the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit—in one (unified) Godhead or divinity. All three persons are one in unity and eternal substance, yet three separate and distinct persons in their individuality (1 Jn. 5:7-8; Dan. 7:9-14; Mt. 3:16-17; 28:19; Acts 7:56-59).

### 9. BODY, SOUL, AND SPIRIT
God the Father, God the Son, God the Holy Spirit, each angel and human being, and every separate person in the universe has a personal body, soul, and spirit, distinct from all others.

> (1) **The body** of any being is the outward form or dwelling place where the soul and spirit reside (Gen. 2:7, 19; Jn. 5:28-29; Mt. 27:52; 1 Cor. 15:34-58; Jas. 2:26; 1 Thess. 5:23; Heb. 10:5-10). There are spiritual bodies and natural bodies—heavenly and earthly bodies—and both kinds are real (1 Cor. 15:40-49).

> (2) **The soul** is that invisible part of all living beings that feels—the seat of affections, emotions, passions, and desires—that which gives self-consciousness and makes one a sentient being (Lev. 23:43; 1 Sam. 22:2; 30:6; 2 Sam. 13:39; 2 Ki. 4:27; 23:3; Ps. 107:5, 9, 18, 26; Mk. 12:33; Mt. 26:38; Jn. 12:27; Heb. 4:12; 10:38).

> (3) **The spirit** is that invisible part of all living beings that knows—the seat of intellect, mind, and will—that which gives self-determination and makes one a free moral agent and rational being (1 Cor. 2:11; Mt. 26:41; Exod. 35:21; Job 38:8, 18; Prov. 20:27; Phil. 1:27; Heb. 4:12; Jas. 2:26; 1 Thess. 5:23).

The Bible teaches that unsaved people do not have God's Spirit in them (Rom. 8:9-16; 1 Cor. 12:13; 2 Cor. 1:22; 6:16-18; Gal. 3:2-5; 4:6; Eph. 1:13; 2 Tim. 1:7; 1 Jn. 4:4-6); that God's Spirit isn't received until the New Birth (Jn. 3:1-5; 14:17; Rom. 8:9-16); that there's a difference between the natural spirit of humans and animals (Eccl. 3:21); that there's a difference between breath and spirit (Job 34:15; Ps. 19:7; Lk. 12:19; 1 Cor. 5:5; 2 Cor. 7:1); and that each creature has a separate spirit which is not part of God (Num. 16:22; 27:17; Eccl. 3:21; Ezek. 21:7; Dan. 2:1-3; Mal. 2:15-16; Prov. 16:2; 1 Cor. 2:10-12; 14:32; Heb. 12:9, 23; 1 Thess. 5:23).

Try substituting "breath" for "soul" and "spirit" in all those passages—you'll quickly see how ridiculous that meaning becomes!

---

### The Invisibility of God

When we use the word "invisible," we shouldn't think of it only in terms of substance, but mainly as distance—beyond eyesight, beyond natural visibility. God has been seen with natural eyes many times, so His invisibility isn't something beyond spiritual or physical possibility (Point II, 3, below).

In eternity, He will be seen by people as clearly as we see each other now (Rev. 21:27; 22:4-5; Ezek. 43:7; 48:35). He can appear visible or invisible to natural eyes right now; and when our eyesight is adjusted to see spiritual things, spiritual sight will be as natural and normal as physical sight is today. Angels and other spirit-beings have also been seen by natural eyes (Lesson Six, VI).

Think of it like this: our eyes currently aren't equipped to see certain things—even some material things—that we'll be able to see when God removes the veil of darkness spread over all nations since humanity's fall (Isa. 24:21-22; 25:7; 1 Cor. 13:12). At that time, the sun's light will increase sevenfold, and the moon's light will be as bright as our current sunlight (Isa. 24:23; 30:26).

Even the invisible soul and spirit, or inner person, has been and can be seen (Lk. 16:19-31; Rev. 6:9-11). Therefore, it's possible for all spirits and spiritual things to be seen. Sin has dimmed our natural sight so that now we see "through a glass darkly" (1 Cor. 13:12).

No one can claim with scriptural authority that God consists of some kind of invisible substance that can never be seen or touched. In fact, God will live among people in visible form forever (Rev. 21:3-7; 22:4-5).

---

## II. True Interpretation of God as Spirit (Jn. 4:24)

If we can come to understand what spirit-beings are actually like, we can begin to comprehend God as Spirit. Hundreds of clear scriptures help us gain this knowledge.

### 1. Heavenly and Earthly Bodies

The Bible declares that there are heavenly and earthly bodies—"there is a natural body, and there is a spiritual body" (1 Cor. 15:35-58). From this passage we learn that all created things have their own distinct bodies: bodies for grain, fish, birds, animals, humans, and every living thing on earth; bodies for the sun, moon, stars, and all celestial objects; bodies for angels, cherubim, seraphim, and all spirit-beings.

Nowhere in the Bible is there an exception stating that God alone, of all beings in the universe, lacks a body.

When Jesus said, "a spirit hath not flesh and bones, as ye see me have" (Lk. 24:39), He certainly wasn't implying that spirit-bodies aren't real and tangible. He was simply teaching that spirit-bodies aren't made of earthly flesh and bone. He couldn't have meant that God lacks a real spirit-body, because He taught elsewhere that God has a voice and a shape (Jn. 5:37). He also showed John in Rev. 4 and 5 that God has a body and sits on a throne just like anyone else.

The Greek word for "shape" in Jn. 5:37 is *eidos*, meaning form, appearance, shape, fashion, or sight—referring to outward form or what can be seen with the eyes, as is clear from Lk. 3:22; 9:29; 2 Cor. 5:7.

### 2. Made in God's Image

Moses declared that humans were made in God's image and likeness (Gen. 1:26-27; 9:6). The Hebrew word for "image" is *tselem*, meaning shape, shadow, resemblance, figure, or bodily form, as proven throughout Scripture where it's used (Gen. 5:3; 9:6; Exod. 20:4; Lev. 26:1; Ps. 73:20; 106:19; Isa. 40:19-20; 44:9-17; 45:20; 48:5; Jer. 10:14; 51:17).

The Hebrew word for "likeness" is *demooth*, meaning model, shape, fashion, or bodily resemblance, as proven in Gen. 5:1, 3; Isa. 40:18; Ezek. 1:5, 10, 13, 16, 22, 26, 28; 10:1, 10, 21-22.

The Fenton translation of Gen. 1:26-27 reads: "Let us make men under our shadow, as our representatives... So God created men under his own shadow, creating them in the shadow of God." Anything that casts a shadow must be real!

Paul said that man is "the image and glory of God" (1 Cor. 11:7). The Greek word for "image" here is *eikon*, meaning likeness, profile, statue, or bodily resemblance, as proven wherever it's used (Mt. 22:20; Acts 19:35; Rom. 1:23; 8:29; 11:4; 1 Cor. 15:49; 2 Cor. 4:4; Col. 1:15; Heb. 10:1; Rev. 13:14-15; 14:9-11; 15:2; 16:2; 19:20; 20:4).

There's no question that humans were made in God's moral and spiritual likeness, but that's not what these passages are referring to—they're talking about bodily form and shape. If humans were made in God's bodily image and likeness, then God must have a body with outward form and shape.

It would make just as much sense to argue that "image and likeness" when used of idols means moral and spiritual qualities rather than physical form—yet the same Hebrew and Greek words are used in both cases! That these words refer to what can be seen with natural eyes is clear from Gen. 5:3; Isa. 40:19-20; 44:9-17; Ezek. 1:5-28; Acts 19:35; and other passages.

### 3. Eyewitness Accounts

Bible writers not only stated that God has a body—they testified that they've seen it with their natural eyes!

- Abraham prepared dinner for God and two angels, and they actually ate food (Gen. 18).
- Jacob had an all-night physical wrestling match with God (Gen. 32:24-30).
- Moses talked with God face to face (Exod. 33:11-23).
- Seventy-four elders of Israel had a banquet with God on Mount Sinai (Exod. 24:9-11).
- Joshua and all Israel saw God with a sword in His hand (Josh. 5:13-15).
- Gideon (Judg. 6:11-23), Manoah and his wife (Judg. 13:3-23), David (1 Chron. 21:16-17), Job (Job 42:5), Isaiah (Isa. 6:1-13), and Amos (Amos 9:1) all saw God standing on the ground, sitting on thrones, and having bodily parts like humans.
- Ezekiel saw God on a chariot and described Him as having "the appearance of a man" with loins and upper and lower body parts like a human (Ezek. 1:26-28; 10:1, 20; 40:3).
- Daniel saw both God the Father and the Son of Man as two separate beings at the same time and place. God was on a throne wearing white clothes, with white hair. The Son of Man also had a body, wore clothes, and had hair (Dan. 7:9-14; 10:5-6).
- Stephen saw both God and Christ at the same place with the same eyes (Acts 7:56-59).
- John saw God on a throne, Christ symbolized as a lamb, and the Holy Spirit symbolized as lamps of fire and seven horns and eyes—all at the same time (Rev. 4:2-5; 5:1, 5-7).

### 4. The Promise of Seeing God

The Bible predicts that all the redeemed will see God's face (Greek *prosopon*—countenance, outward appearance, person) with their eyes (Rev. 7:9; 14:5; 21:3; 22:4-5). The word "face" is used in Mt. 6:17; 18:10; 26:39, 67; Acts 6:15; 20:25, 38; 1 Cor. 13:12; Rev. 4:7; 6:16; 10:1; 20:11 for both God and humans—so both must have a face.

Are we going to call all these witnesses liars just to match what modern interpreters say—interpreters who have never seen God themselves? Wouldn't it be wiser to trust the testimony of those who actually saw God and knew what they were talking about?

Modern doctrine writers assert that no one can understand God, that no one has ever seen God, and that God has no body with parts and passions to be seen. I've quoted only from prominent leaders who have had wide influence in major denominations. We can personally admire these men for their great work, but that doesn't change the fact that they're wrong on this most important subject.

It shows the modern trend to make God too mystical to understand. One writer says: "It is clearly revealed in Scripture that God is one being constituted by three persons. We give this complex Person the name Trinity... It would be folly to seek to explain this startling revelation... We can only say that we believe it because we do not comprehend it... The doctrine of the Trinity bewilders the most astute and is frankly beyond the comprehension of the most learned."

We quote from widely-used books on great Bible doctrines: "God as a spirit is incorporeal, invisible reality; has no body or parts like human beings; nothing of a material or bodily nature... God cannot be seen with the material eyes; nothing on earth to resemble Him; without parts, without body, without passions... God cannot be comprehended by the senses, but by the soul; and is above sensuous perceptions... The image of God consists only in intellectual and moral likeness; when God is spoken of as having hands, feet, eyes, hair, and other bodily parts, these are figures of speech and mere human expressions trying to convey some idea of God."

Such statements are, frankly, foolish and unscriptural. No wonder these writers can't comprehend the Trinity the way they describe it! They make such impossible claims about God that understanding becomes impossible.

If we take the Bible instead of these statements, we *can* comprehend God. The Bible does not say that God is one person made up of three persons—that could never be true. But God *can* be three distinct persons, as separate and distinct as any three people we know. This is understandable! The other idea isn't, because there's simply no such thing as three persons inside one person.

God Himself couldn't explain it that way because that's not how He explained it, nor is it how He asks us to understand it.

The Bible also doesn't say that God's bodily parts are figures of speech or mere human attempts to convey ideas about God, or that these references do away with the reality of God's body. All figures of speech actually emphasize and make the ideas they express as real—or more real—than literal language would. There can be no true figure of speech unless the idea it conveys is real. So if God's bodily parts are figures of speech, they're true figures representing real bodily parts.

Since we don't have God's authority for claiming He's a spiritual "nothing," and since we only have human opinions, let's forget the doctrine that God is unreal and simply believe the Bible literally about God—just as we do about other subjects.

The statements quoted above directly conflict with Scripture:
- God *can* be seen and *has* been seen with natural eyes.
- God *does* have a tangible body that can be touched and can do everything any other body can do.
- God *does* have bodily parts and passions.
- God *can* be comprehended by the senses (the senses are part of the soul).
- God consists of more than intellectual and moral qualities.
- The expressions describing God's bodily parts are real and literal, not figurative.

### 5. Angels and Spirit-Beings Have Bodies

The Bible teaches that angels, seraphim, cherubim, and all spirit-beings have bodies, souls, and spirits like humans, as we'll see in the next two lessons.

Angels have always appeared as men in real spirit-bodies when seen with human eyes. They have bodily parts and can do everything humans can do. They can engage in physical combat. They can be bound by chains and confined to material places. They have feelings and passions. They drive horses, guard gates, and perform many activities (Lesson Six).

If they're spirit-beings with bodies, soul passions, and spirit faculties, why couldn't God also have a real spirit-body with soul passions and spirit faculties and still be divine?

### 6. Understanding God Through Creation

Paul makes it very clear that people *can* understand God. He gives us a simple illustration of the Godhead and says even sinners can comprehend God:

"Because that which may be known of God is manifest in them; for God hath shewed it unto them. For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and godhead; so that they are without excuse" (Rom. 1:19-21).

If the invisible things, the eternal power, and the Godhead are clearly seen through visible creation, then all we need is a little common sense combined with plain Scripture to understand what the Godhead is really like. If sinners can understand God, certainly believers should be able to!

This certainly doesn't match the opinions that no one can comprehend God. It doesn't sound like God is such a mystery. If He can be clearly seen by something visible in this world, what visible thing gives us a picture of the invisible God?

The answer must be humanity. Humans have body, soul, and spirit; so if we're the visible thing that clearly illustrates God, then God must also have a personal body, soul, and spirit.

If the Godhead consists of three separate and distinct persons, as plainly stated in 1 Jn. 5:7-8, then we should understand that each person has a personal body, soul, and spirit—just like each human. If there are three persons in the Godhead existing as "one," we must understand this oneness the same way we understand several people being "one"—one in unity, as in Jn. 17:11, 21-23; Mt. 19:5; Heb. 2:11; 1 Cor. 6:17; Acts 4:32.

What's hard to understand about this, since we have concrete examples on earth of every fact stated here?

- What's hard about three persons in the Godhead being three separate persons in the same sense as any other three persons?
- What's hard about three persons being one in unity, just as we conceive of any number of people being unified?
- Do we have to believe that three persons must become one person to be "three in one"? Is that what happens when three people become unified in purpose?

If people can retain their individuality while being one in unity, couldn't this be true of the Godhead? Couldn't God exist as three separate persons with three separate bodies, souls, and spirits, and still be one in unity?

Then why claim this can't be comprehended when we have concrete examples of this kind of unity all around us?

The Father is called God (1 Cor. 8:6); the Son is called God (Isa. 9:6; Heb. 1:8; Jn. 20:28); and the Holy Spirit is called God (Acts 5:3-4). All three persons of the Godhead are divine and can be spoken of individually as God and collectively as one God in the sense of unity.

Each one is called Lord, and collectively all three can be called one Lord in the sense of unity. The Father and Son are both called Lord and God in the same passages, yet they're clearly distinguished as two separate persons (Heb. 1:8-9; Ps. 110:1; Gen. 19:24; 1 Cor. 11:3). It's similar to how any family members could individually or collectively be called by the family name.

To sum it all up: everything that could be spoken collectively of God could be spoken of each person in the Godhead individually. However, there are certain things said of each individual person regarding position, office, and work that couldn't be said of the other members:
- Only the Father is the Father and the head of Christ (1 Cor. 11:3)
- Only the Son was begotten of the Father (2 Jn. 3)
- Only the Holy Spirit proceeds from both the Father and the Son (Jn. 14:16, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15; Acts 2:34)

There are many other distinctions between the three persons of the Godhead that will be covered in Lessons Twenty-one, Twenty-five, and Twenty-seven.

According to all these points, a spirit is far different from what we've been taught. Spirit-beings can and do have real, material, tangible spirit-form, shape, and size, with bodily parts, soul passions, and spirit faculties. Their material bodies are of spiritual substance and are just as real as human bodies. This will be abundantly proven in Lesson Six.

Even human bodies after resurrection will be able to pass through material substances, as Jesus did after His resurrection. He had a flesh and bone body (Lk. 24:39), yet in its changed, spiritualized, glorified state, Jesus could transform His body from one form to another at will. He could appear and disappear, pass through material substances (including closed doors), and be visible or invisible (Lk. 24:16, 31, 34, 36-45; Mk. 16:12-14; Jn. 20:14-18).

If this is true of glorified human bodies, then we can see how God, angels, and other spirit-beings can have bodies of material spiritual substance that are just as real as spiritualized human bodies.

### 7. God's Spirit-Body with Bodily Parts

God has a spirit-body with bodily parts like humans. This is proven by hundreds of clear scriptures that don't need interpretation—they're too plain and literal to misunderstand. All we can do is either deny what the Bible says or believe it.

If we're not going to believe, let's at least be honest and admit we don't believe the Bible. It doesn't make sense to pretend to believe the Bible while refusing to trust it, except for a few things we've traditionally been taught to accept. If we haven't been taught to believe the Bible, we can decide right now to become full believers in everything plainly written in Scripture. If we don't, God will hold us accountable on Judgment Day.

What does it matter if some preachers and teachers don't believe the Bible's simplicity? We can choose to believe.

Space doesn't allow quoting every passage, so we'll simply state what they reveal about God. If you want to verify these facts, read each passage yourself and see if they're not plainly written in your own Bible.

**God is a person who is Spirit—infinite, eternal, immutable, self-existent, omnipresent, omniscient, omnipotent, invisible, perfect, impartial, immortal, absolutely holy and just, full of knowledge and wisdom, in whom all things have their source, support, and end.**

God is known in Scripture by over 200 names. He is described as having a body, soul, and spirit (Job 13:8; Heb. 1:3; Dan. 7:9-14; 10:5-7). He is a Spirit-being with:

- A body (Dan. 7:9-14; 10:5-6, 9-19; Exod. 24:11; Gen. 18; 32:24-32; Ezek. 1:26-28; Acts 7:54-59; Rev. 4:2-4; 5:1, 5-7; 22:4-5)
- Shape (Jn. 5:37)
- Form (Phil. 2:5-7; same Greek word as in Mk. 16:12, referring to bodily form)

lessons.title: The Original Creations of God
lessons.content:
# The Original Creations of God

## The Antechaotic Age (Gen. 1:1-2)

### Part I: The Origin of All Things (Lessons 1–8)

### Earth's First Sinless Career (Gen. 1:1; Ezek. 28:15)

### The Earth Made Perfect the First Time

When we talk about God's original creations, we're looking at everything He brought into existence at the very beginning—the heavens, the earth, and everything in them. Genesis 1:1 gives us the summary: "In the beginning God created the heaven [Hebrew, heavens] and the earth."

This takes us back to the dateless past. It covers only a portion of what we call the creative ages (as we discussed in Lesson One, Point III, 1, (4))—specifically from the first moment of creation until the chaotic period of Gen. 1:2, when the earth and its original inhabitants were destroyed by the first flood.

You'll find many other scriptures that reference this same period (Job 38; Ps. 8:3-8; 19:1-6; Prov. 8:22-31; Jn. 1:3, 10; Acts 17:24-26; Col. 1:15-18; Heb. 1:1-12; 11:3; Rev. 4:11).

---

## I. God in the Eternal Past

What exactly was God doing throughout the eternal past? Honestly, we don't know—He hasn't chosen to reveal all His activities from infinity (Deut. 29:29). What we do know is that He has existed from all eternity, and at some point in that dateless past, He brought the heavens, the earth, and everything originally in them into existence.

The things we see on earth today were created during six days—but that came after the destruction of the first creation of free moral agents who lived here (Gen. 1:3–2:25).

Scripture makes it abundantly clear that God has existed forever: "Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever thou hadst formed the earth and the world, even from everlasting to everlasting, thou art God" (Ps. 90:2). "Thy throne is established of old: thou art from everlasting" (Ps. 93:2; Prov. 8:22-31; Mic. 5:2; Heb. 9:14; Jn. 1:1-3; Rev. 1:4-8; 4:8; 22:5, 13).

And Scripture also makes clear that God created the heavens, the earth, and everything in them sometime in that eternal past. "In the beginning"—whenever that was—points to the dateless past because no date is given. Anyone who tries to pin down exactly when that beginning was is adding to the Bible.

So let's leave it as it stands. It could have been millions or billions of years ago, or it could have been a shorter time. If geologists can prove the earth is as old as they claim, why argue with them? Whether they're right or wrong, it doesn't contradict Scripture since the Bible doesn't give us a time element for the original creation.

Here's what we can say with certainty from Scripture: the earth is more than 6,000 years old, and there were inhabitants on this planet before Adam's day. We'll prove this from many clear scriptures in Lessons Seven and Eight. Keep in mind that the heavens and earth were created in the dateless past (Gen. 1:1), the earth was cursed and flooded because of sin (Gen. 1:2), and all of this happened before the six days of re-creation (Gen. 1:3–2:25).

Lucifer was already a fallen creature when he showed up in Adam's Eden. He had already ruled the earth, already rebelled, and already caused the first flood before Adam ever drew breath. Don't miss the next two lessons where we prove these facts!

Another certainty: God made His plan for the heavens, the earth, and everything in them before He began bringing them into existence. And it's equally certain that God brought them into existence—they are not a product of evolution, as some foolish people teach in their opposition to the Bible.

The Bible opens with God as Creator of all things and closes with God reigning over all things after rebellion against His moral government has been put down (Gen. 1:1; 1 Cor. 15:24-28; Eph. 1:10; Phil. 2:9-11; Rev. 21–22).

---

## II. The Drafting of God's Plan

As we'll see in Supplement Five, God's plan was made by and is being carried out by three separate and distinct persons. Scripture shows us that all three had their part in designing the plan now revealed in the Bible. The three members of the deity consulted together at various points in working out this plan.

In Gen. 1:1 and about 2,700 other places in the Hebrew Bible, the word for God is *Elohim*—a uni-plural noun meaning "Gods." It's actually translated "Gods" 239 times in the King James Version (Gen. 3:5; Exod. 22:28; 1 Sam. 4:8; Dan. 2:11; 4:6-9; 5:11, 14; etc.). It's the plural of *Eloah*, meaning deity or God.

If we translated this literally into English, Gen. 1:1 would read: "In the beginning Gods created the heaven and the earth." Sometimes it's used with plural pronouns, like "And Gods said, Let us make man in our image"—showing not only plurality of persons, but that all persons had the same kind of image and likeness, and that not just one of them had a spirit-body.

Gen. 3:22 puts it this way: "man is become as one of us," showing that all three persons in the deity were alike. The phrase "as one of us" means "like each one person of several persons of the same kind"—that's what it means wherever it's used in Scripture or any other literature. In Gen. 11:7, the Gods said, "Let us go down, and there confound their language."

Plural pronouns are used of God in other places throughout both Testaments (Isa. 6:8; Ps. 2:3; Jn. 14:23; 17:11, 22). It's perfectly scriptural to use the plural word "Gods" when speaking of three separate persons in the Godhead, as explained in Lesson Four, Point I, 3, and Point II, 6.

Think of it like a business partnership where three equal partners work together on every major decision. There's no sense in talking about "unity" if only one person exists. So it's perfectly natural that all three persons in the deity would participate and consult each other in drafting the plan.

Where did the Godhead dwell in all eternity? What did they all do? When and where was the plan drafted? We don't know these things. But Scripture does tell us that God planned the worlds (ages) by Christ (Heb. 1:1-3; 11:3), and by Him all things were brought into being (Col. 1:15-18; Eph. 3:9; Jn. 1:1-3, 10).

The following passages prove that the plan was made and known to God from the very beginning: "Declaring the end from the beginning, and from ancient times" (Isa. 46:10-11); "This is the purpose that is purposed upon the whole earth" (Isa. 14:26-27); "Thou hast established the earth, and it abideth. They continue this day according to thine ordinances" (Ps. 119:89-91); "Known unto God are all his works from the beginning of the world" (Acts 15:18). See also Eph. 1:4-11; 2:7; 3:5-11; 1 Pet. 1:20.

---

## III. The Dispensational Plan of God from Eternity through Eternity (Eph. 3:11)

The following points give you a bird's-eye view of God's entire plan as revealed in the Bible—from the eternal past to the eternal future. This outline of just thirty points is worth memorizing so you'll always have the general scope of God's plan of the ages and dispensations in your mind (as illustrated by the author's Plan of the Ages bible chart).

It's helpful to study this plan alongside the chart so you can grasp God's program from eternity to eternity. Then you can begin rightly dividing the Word of Truth by connecting various scriptures to their proper periods, ages, and dispensations.

> 1. God in the eternal past (Ps. 90:2; 93:2; Mic. 5:2; Heb. 9:14).

> 2. The drafting of God's plan (Heb. 1:3; 11:3; Eph. 3:11; 1 Pet. 1:20).

> 3. Creation of the heavens, including the sun, moon, and stars (Gen. 1:1; Col. 1:15-18; Ps. 8:3; 90:2; 95:5; 102:25; Isa. 40:12, 26; 45:12; 48:13).

> 4. Creation of the spirit world (Job 38:4-7; Col. 1:15-18).

> 5. Creation of the earth. The earth made perfect the first time (Gen. 1:1; Job 38:4-7; Isa. 45:18; Heb. 11:3).

> 6. Creation of "the world [kosmos, social order] that then was" (2 Pet. 3:5-9; Isa. 14:12-14; 45:18; Jer. 4:23-26; Ezek. 28:11-17).

> 7. Lucifer's reign over "the world that then was" (Isa. 14:12-14; Jer. 4:23-26; Ezek. 28:11-17; Col. 1:15-18). The length of his rule is unknown.

> 8. Other thrones, dominions, principalities and powers placed over other parts of the universe (Col. 1:15-18; Eph. 1:21; 3:10; 1 Pet. 3:22).

> 9. The kingdom of God universal; God the Supreme Moral Governor of the universe, and all in harmony with Him (Job 38; Dan. 4:17, 25, 32, 35). The length of the rule of the universal harmonious kingdom before Lucifer started his rebellion is unknown.

> 10. Lucifer, the original ruler of the planet earth, conceives the idea that he can get the cooperation of other angelic beings of the universe, dethrone God, and become the exalted supreme ruler of the universe (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17; 1 Tim. 3:6).

> 11. Lucifer carries out his plans and through pride falls and incites rebellion by slander and accusations against the Almighty. He causes his own earth-kingdom subjects and over one-third of God's angels (Rev. 12:7-12) to rebel against Him. The earth enters its first sinful career (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17; 1 Tim. 3:6). The length of the uprising is unknown.

> 12. Lucifer instigates rebellion and persuades everyone possible to rebel. He openly breaks relations with God and His government, and leads his rebels from the appointed place of mobilization on earth into heaven to dethrone God; but he is met by Michael and the faithful angels, and is defeated and cast as lightning back to the earth (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17; Lk. 10:18).

> 13. God completely destroys Lucifer's kingdom on earth, and curses the earth by destroying every bird, animal, fish, city, inhabitant, and all vegetation. He then turns the earth upside down and by means of a great flood makes it empty and a waste (Gen. 1:2; Ps. 104:5-9; Isa. 14:12-14; Jer. 4:23-26; Ezek. 28:11-17; 2 Pet. 3:5-9). The length of the pre-Adamite flood on the earth is unknown.

> 14. The Spirit of God begins to move upon the flooded earth and in the darkness which covered the waters to restore the earth to a habitable state, to create new land animals, fish, fowls, vegetation, and Adam as the new ruler of the earth (Gen. 1:2; Ps. 104:6-9). Length of the re-creation is six days (Gen. 1:3–2:25; Exod. 20:8-11; 31:15).

> 15. The earth is made perfect a second time and all things in the universe are again in harmony with God as before Lucifer's rebellion; except Lucifer and his spirit-rebels are still at large in the heavenlies to further God's plan in the probationary periods of the human race should man fall (Gen. 1:3–2:25; Eph. 2:1-3; 6:10-18; Job 1:6; 2:1; Rev. 12:7-12).

> 16. THE FIRST PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Innocence. The length of this period was about six days. (Gen. 2:17–3:24).

> 17. Lucifer (now the devil or adversary of God and man called Satan) enters the restored earth, tempts man and causes his fall, thus regaining dominion of the earth and all things therein (Gen. 3; Lk. 4; Jn. 12:31; 2 Cor. 4:4; Rev. 11:15; 20:1-10).

> 18. Rebellion starts again on the earth by the second ruler of earth. Man is judged, the earth is again cursed and enters its second sinful career, and all creatures are brought under the bondage of sin and corruption (Gen. 3; Rom. 5:12-21; 8:19-23).

> 19. "The heavens and the earth which are now" since the restoration work of the six days, and since the new curse on the earth await the time of the second renovation, and the third perfect state of the earth, called the New Heavens and the New Earth (2 Pet. 3:5-13; Rom. 8:19-23; Heb. 1:10-12; 12:24-28; Rev. 21–22).

> 20. THE SECOND PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Conscience (Gen. 3:1–8:14). The length of this period was 1,656 years (Gen. 5:1-29; 7:11).

> 21. THE THIRD PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Human Government (Gen. 8:15–11:9). The length of this period was 427 years (Gen. 11:10–12:9).

> 22. THE FOURTH PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Promise (Gen. 11:10—Exod. 12:51). The length of this period was 430 years (Exod. 12:40; Gal. 4:30).

> 23. THE FIFTH PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Law (Exod. 13:1—Mt. 4:1; 11:11; Lk. 16:16). The length of this period was over 1,718 years from Moses to Christ.

> 24. THE SIXTH PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Grace (Jn. 1:17; Mt. 4:1—Rev. 19:21). The length of this period will be from the first advent to the second advent of Christ, and the binding of Satan in the abyss at the end of this age. It has already lasted over 2,000 years.

> 25. THE SEVENTH PROBATIONARY PERIOD—The Dispensation of Divine Government or Millennium (Rev. 20:1-10). The length will be 1,000 years (Rev. 20:1-10).

> 26. At the end of the Millennium Satan will be loosed from the abyss to deceive the nations on the earth, then will come the last rebellion on the earth and the destruction of all human rebels (Rev. 20:7-10).

> 27. The second resurrection and final judgment at the end of the Millennium (Rev. 20:11-15).

> 28. The end of the earth's second sinful career by the renovation of the immediate heavens and the earth and the removal of the curse in its entirety and all its effects (2 Pet. 3:10-13; Rom. 8:19-23; Heb. 1:10-12; 12:24-28).

> 29. The confinement of all spirit and human rebels of all ages in the Lake of Fire forever (Mt. 25:41, 46; Rev. 14:9-11; 20:10-15; Isa. 66:22-24).

> 30. The New Heavens and the New Earth—the eternal perfect state with God all-in-all again, as before rebellion started in the universe with Lucifer and Adam (1 Cor. 15:24-28). The earth will be made perfect the third time (Isa. 65:17; 66:22-24; 2 Pet. 3:13; Rev. 21–22).

---

## IV. The Creation of the Material Universe (Gen. 1:1)

When we talk about the creation of the material universe, we mean the creation of the heavens, the earth, and everything originally made in them. The heavens were created first, then the earth—that's proven in Gen. 1:1; Job 38:4-7.

The word "beginning," as we've seen, refers to the dateless past when no material universe yet existed. Otherwise, the heavens and earth couldn't be described as "created" or "brought into existence" at that time. There was no day and night yet, no time on earth or in heaven, because they hadn't been created.

Whether day and night or times and seasons existed where God lived before that—Scripture doesn't say. Speculation about it is pointless since we can't prove it one way or the other.

The word "created" comes from the Hebrew word *bara*, meaning to create, to make new, or to bring into existence without using pre-existing material. This is certainly true of the materials from which the heavens and earth were formed.

Hebrews 11:3 tells us that "things which are seen [the visible things] were not made of things that do appear" or that were visible. If the heavens and earth were brought into existence, then at one time they clearly didn't exist.

*Bara* appears 49 times in the Hebrew Bible. It's translated "create" 8 times; "created" 33 times; "make" 4 times; "Creator" 3 times; and "createth" once. The core idea is bringing something new into existence—even if that something new is made from already existing material. Perfection is generally implied, and always implied when anything is a creation of God.

Moses said, "His work is perfect" (Deut. 32:4). David said, "His way is perfect" (2 Sam. 22:31). Solomon said that God "made everything beautiful in his time" (Eccl. 3:11). A perfect God simply cannot make anything imperfect.

*Bara* is used only seven times in Gen. 1:1–2:4—the passage recording all the creative ages. It's correctly translated "created" each time. In all other verses of this passage, the word "made" is used. That comes from the Hebrew *asah*, meaning to make something out of already existing material, as we'll explain in Point VII below.

In Gen. 1:1, the heavens and earth are created or brought into existence. In Gen. 1:21, the sea creatures are created or brought into existence. In Gen. 1:28, man is created or brought into being. So *bara* is reserved for introducing each of the three great spheres of existence: the world of matter, the world of natural life (all living creatures), and the world of spiritual life (represented by man).

The heavens and earth were brought into existence "in the beginning," while living creatures and man were brought into existence on the fifth and sixth days during the restoration of the earth. All other work done in the six days wasn't creative in nature—it was making things from already existing material that had been created during the various periods "in the beginning."

The Hebrew word for heaven is *shamayim*, meaning lofty, sky, or the higher ether where celestial bodies revolve. It appears 657 times, and in most places should have been translated "heavens." God created the heavens—including the sun, moon, stars, all inhabitants of heaven, and everything in them. Then He created the earth with its inhabitants and everything in it.

That the heavens are inhabited is clear from Rev. 12:12; 13:6; Col. 1:15-18; Dan. 4:35. There are at least three heavens, and all were created and inhabited "in the beginning." The third heaven is God's dwelling place—a real created planet like earth, as proven in Gen. 1:1; 2 Cor. 12:1-3; Heb. 11:10-16; Col. 1:15-18; Deut. 10:14; Ps. 115:16.

Here's something fascinating: astronomers have discovered planets about 150 million light years from earth. That's roughly 14,690,062,800,000,000,000 miles—the distance light travels in 150 million years at 186,000 miles per second. Astronomers say there may be other universes beyond this one!

They also tell us that each star is actually a sun to other planets, but they're so far from our solar system that they appear as small lights. About six billion stars have been discovered, and they're all suns to other planets.

If this is true, the universe is vast beyond what we can conceive. And this makes sense when you think about it—it's hard to imagine God existing from all eternity doing nothing until a few thousand years ago when He finally decided to make something. It's much easier to picture God always busy, always creating throughout the eternal past.

The Bible itself says the heavens cannot be measured and the host of heaven cannot be counted (Jer. 31:37; 33:22-25).

The Hebrew word for earth is *erets*, meaning dry ground: "And God called the dry land earth" (Gen. 1:9-10). So we can read Gen. 1:1 as: "In the beginning God created the heavens and dry land." In Gen. 1:2, that dry land is then wet land—proving it was flooded after its original creation (Ps. 104:6-9; 2 Pet. 3:5-6). We'll see this more clearly in the next two lessons.

*Erets* is translated "earth" 708 times; "land" 1,523 times; "ground" 102 times; "country" 102 times; and "countries" 53 times. The heavens and earth were completed and inhabited, and then the earth was flooded as in Gen. 1:2—all before the beginning of the six days in Gen. 1:3–2:25.

---

## V. The Creation of the Spirit World

Job 38:4-7 makes it clear that the spirit world—angels, seraphim, cherubim, and other spirit-beings—was created before the earth. The "sons of God" were present and shouting for joy when God laid the foundations of the earth.

This passage also shows that the heavens, including the sun, moon, and stars, were created before the earth, since "the morning stars" sang together when God laid the earth's foundations.

Scripture doesn't specifically say whether spirit-beings were created before the heavens. But if we take God's work in creating the earth as a pattern for His work in the heavens, then it seems clear the heavens were created first, then their inhabitants.

Think of it like building a house before moving in the furniture. That's the logical order—make the place before creating inhabitants to live there. Since the inhabitants were part of creation and not part of the Creators (Col. 1:15-18), they naturally had to be created after their dwelling places were made. This is exactly how it happened on earth during the six days when God restored it from chaos to its second habitable state (Gen. 1:3–2:25).

---

## VI. How God Created and Made the Material Universe

God undoubtedly created and made the different parts of the material universe—and each thing in it—with the same care and time He used in the six days when He restored planet earth and made a new order of creatures.

During the six days, Scripture tells us God formed each living creature and man with His hands out of the dust of the ground (Gen. 1:20-27; 2:7-25; Job 26:13; Rom. 9:20; 1 Tim. 2:13). It's clear that God not only created the heavens and earth and everything "in the beginning" (each in its own period), but He also formed everything with His hands.

God formed both light and darkness (Isa. 45:7). He didn't do this on the first day of Gen. 1:3-5—at that point He merely divided them. So they must have been created and formed before the first day.

Scripture also says God formed the earth with His hands (Ps. 8:3, 6; 90:2; 95:5; 102:25; Isa. 40:12); the heavens (Ps. 8:3; 19:1; 102:25; Isa. 40:12); the planets (Ps. 8:3; Isa. 40:26; 45:12; 45:13; Heb. 1:10); and all things (Prov. 26:10).

When you study all these scriptures together with those on creation, the picture becomes clear: by the Word of God, materials were brought into existence, and then with His hands He formed those materials into everything we see. Picture it this way: as God spoke, materials came into existence, and as fast as they materialized, He used them to form all things with His hands (Ps. 8:3; 2 Pet. 3:3-9; Prov. 26:10).

---

## VII. When God Created and Made the Material Universe

The Bible's answer? "In the beginning [literally, by periods or ages] God created the heaven [Hebrew, heavens] and the earth"—not "Six thousand years ago God created the heavens and the earth," as many commonly teach.

Can we pinpoint exactly when the beginning was? If we can, we know more than God has revealed. If we can't, then we obviously don't know and shouldn't teach that the beginning was 6,000 years ago. For all we know, it could have been six billion years ago just as easily as 6,000.

When we talk about the six days and the creation of present life on earth, we can speak with definite biblical authority that it was about 6,000 years ago. You can see this by adding up the lengths of the various dispensations since Adam in the outline in Point III above. But nowhere does Scripture tell us the heavens and earth were originally created during those six days, about 6,000 years ago at the time of Adam.

Some people use Exod. 20:8-11; 31:17 to argue that the heavens and earth were created during the six days of Gen. 1:3–2:25, making them about 6,000 years old. But these passages say nothing about the original creation of the heavens and earth.

In these verses, the Hebrew word *asah* is used—meaning to make out of already existing material—instead of *bara*, to create. These verses picture the re-creation work of the six days, not the original creation "In the beginning."

*Asah* never means to create. It's translated "made" 659 times; "make" 449 times; "maketh" 59 times; "makest" 16 times; "maker" 13 times; "making" 11 times; and "madest" 3 times.

When God said, "In six days the Lord made heaven and earth," He was referring to the restoration of the heaven (the firmament or clouds) and the earth to a habitable state—like it was before Lucifer's kingdom was destroyed by the flood of Gen. 1:2. We'll dig deeper into this in the next two lessons.

---

## VIII. How Long Was God Creating and Making the Material Universe?

Based on what was accomplished in six days, it appears God took a very long time to originally create and form each sun, moon, star, planet, and creature inhabiting the vast universe.

Consider this: God took one day just to divide light from darkness on this one little planet earth. How much time did He originally take to bring into existence all the darkness and light throughout the entire vast universe? We don't know, but evidently much longer than a day.

God took one day to divide the waters covering the earth and restore the firmament. He took another day to restore the dry land and set boundaries for the seas. Naturally, He would have taken much longer to originally bring all those materials into existence and form the waters, firmament, and earth with its mountains and valleys.

God took one day to complete solar regulation in connection with the restored earth. But He evidently used a much longer period to originally create and form with His own hands the vast heavens and all the countless suns, moons, stars, and planets.

God took two days to form the bodies of fish, fowl, beasts, man, and woman. He naturally took much longer to originally create and form every inhabitant of the vast heavens and all the animals and creatures who lived on earth during Lucifer's kingdom—long before the chaos of Gen. 1:2 and the six days of Gen. 1:3–2:25.

It's similar to comparing a home renovation project with building an entire neighborhood from scratch. If God took six days to restore one little planet and form new inhabitants, He would naturally take much longer to originally create and form the entire universe with its innumerable suns, planets, and inhabitants.

---

## IX. Why God Created and Made the Material Universe

God created the material universe to be inhabited by intelligent, free moral agents to whom He could reveal Himself—beings who could enjoy all the rich blessings of life and the goodness of the Creator forever. He also created plant and animal life, along with everything else necessary to sustain life throughout the universe (Ps. 8:3-8).

Isaiah 45:18 tells us: "For thus saith the Lord that created the heavens, God Himself that formed the earth and made it; he hath established it, he created it not in vain [Heb. *tohu*, desolate, as in Gen. 1:2] he formed it to be inhabited."

Paul explained it this way: "All things were created by him, and for him . . . that in all things he might have the preeminence . . . that we should be holy and without blame before him in love that in the ages to come he might shew the exceeding riches of his grace in his kindness toward us through Jesus Christ" (Col. 1:15-18; Eph. 1:3-11; 2:7; 3:9-11).

Revelation 4:11 puts it beautifully: "Thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created."

---

## X. Creation of the Original Social System

For an unknown period, an original social system existed on this earth ruled by Lucifer—before the social system created in Adam's day. This might be a new concept for you, so we'll provide plenty of scriptural proof in Lesson Seven.

For now, it's enough to say there had to be a social order on earth for Lucifer to have a kingdom here, as proven in Isa. 14:12-14; 45:18; Jer. 4:23-26; Ezek. 28:11-17; 2 Pet. 3:5-7.

Along with this social system, there were land animals, birds, fish, and other creatures—all destroyed in the flood of Gen. 1:2; Ps. 104:5-9; 2 Pet. 3:5-7. All fossils and remains of ancient animals belong to this original period, as do the geologic formations of the earth. Demons also belong to this period, as they are not the souls of human beings—we'll explore this in the next lesson.

Between these original creations and the creations of the six days, Scripture reveals: Lucifer's rule on earth for an unknown period; his rebellion against God; the earth's first sinful career; Lucifer's defeat by God; and the overthrow of his kingdom by the first recorded flood in Gen. 1:2.

Lucifer was the first ruler on earth. He had already completed his period of earth rule and experienced his fall by the time of Adam. When he entered Adam's Eden, he was already a fallen creature.

---

## Questions on Lesson Five

- What do the original creations of God include?
- How much of the creative ages is covered by this period?
- Discuss God in the eternal past.
- Prove from

lessons.title: Satan and the Spirit World
lessons.content:
# Satan and the Spirit World

The Bible stands alone in giving us a window into the heavenly and infernal realms. You'll find hundreds of scriptures revealing everything we need to know — and frankly, everything we'd want to know — about these unseen worlds. When we talk about the spirit world, we mean all the various spirit-beings that inhabit the unseen material worlds around us.

There are many kinds of spirit-beings revealed in Scripture. And by "unseen worlds," we're talking about all the heavens, the underworld of departed spirits, and even the invisible things right here on earth. The Scriptures are saturated with the supernatural — there's really just a step between the natural world and the spiritual world. Let's look at some of the spirit-beings that exist in this realm.

## I. Angelic Beings Called Seraphim

These magnificent creatures appear only twice in Scripture, but their description makes it clear they're angelic, heavenly beings. Picture the scene Isaiah describes:

"I saw also the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up, and his train filled the temple. Above it stood the seraphims: each had six wings: with twain he covered his face, and with twain he covered his feet, and with twain he did fly. And one cried unto another, and said, Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord of hosts: the whole earth is full of his glory. And the posts of the door moved at the voice of him that cried, and the house was filled with smoke . . . Then flew one of the seraphims unto me, having a live coal in his hand, which he had taken with the tongs from off the altar. And he laid it upon my mouth, and said, Lo, this hath touched thy lips; and thine iniquity is taken away and thy sin purged" (Isa. 6:1-7).

## II. Angelic Beings Called Cherubim

Cherubim show up in several passages — too lengthy to quote in full, so let's walk through a brief description. Ezekiel saw them when the heavens opened and he received visions of God.

The cherubim look like men, except each one has four faces and four wings, with feet like a calf. Two of their wings join tip to tip, and the other two cover their bodies. Their faces are like those of a man, a lion, an ox, and an eagle. Their general appearance? Like fire and lightning, and they move with the speed of lightning.

They're connected to wheels that look like a wheel within a wheel — very high, colored like beryl, with many eyes in them. There's a crystal-like firmament over their heads, and above that sits a throne where God appears in the form of a man. Fire, lightning, and glory surround the throne with a rainbow encircling it (Ezek. 1:5-28; 8:1-4; 10:1-22). These same cherubim drove man from Eden and guarded the Tree of Life (Gen. 3:24).

## III. Angelic Beings Called Zoa or Living Creatures

These beings are similar to the cherubim, except they have only one head each and are full of eyes before and behind. Like the seraphim, they have six wings. One has a face like a lion, one like a calf, one like a man, and one like a flying eagle. Day and night, they cry, "Holy, holy, holy" to God (Rev. 4:6-9).

The word translated "beast" in this passage is *zoa*, meaning "living creature." In Rev. 4:6–5:14, the zoa are pictured with harps, singing and worshipping God. You'll also see them throughout Revelation, saying and doing things much like humans do (Rev. 6:1-8; 7:11; 14:3, 9-11; 15:7; 19:4).

## IV. Spirit Horses and Chariot Drivers

Paul taught that many things in the invisible world mirror what we have in the visible world: "For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made" (Rom. 1:20). Think of it like this: the created world around us is a kind of picture book helping us understand spiritual realities.

Paul also mentioned that during his trip into the third heaven, he "heard unspeakable words, which is not lawful for a man to utter" (2 Cor. 12:1-4). In other words, if he'd told people everything he saw and heard in heaven, they would have thought he'd lost his mind. It wasn't wise to share all that he knew — it might have caused people to reject his gospel message entirely.

Most of us have such a vague conception of heaven and spiritual things that it's hard to grasp the realities of the spirit world. All we truly know is what the Bible reveals. And if we take Paul literally in Rom. 1:20, we could easily believe there are many kinds of heavenly creatures corresponding to what we have on earth. Paul didn't limit how many invisible things are made clear by created things — so let's be wise and not limit them either.

We do know for certain there are horses in heaven. Consider these scriptures: "There appeared a chariot of fire, and horses of fire, and parted them both asunder; and Elijah went up by a whirlwind into heaven. And Elisha saw it, and he cried, My father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof" (2 Ki. 2:11-12).

Later, when the Syrian army came to capture Elisha, he prayed that God would show his servant who was really on their side. God opened the young man's eyes "and he saw: and, behold, the mountain was full of horses and chariots of fire round about Elisha" (2 Ki. 6:13-17).

Zechariah saw red horses, speckled and white, with riders on them. He also saw chariots drawn by red, black, white, grisled, and bay horses — called "the spirits of the heavens" doing scout work for God (Zech. 1:8-11; 6:1-8). And in Rev. 19:11-21, we see the armies of heaven on white horses following Christ, who also rides a white horse, coming from heaven to seize the governments of this world and reign forever.

If there are horses in heaven, there may well be all kinds of animals. After all, heaven is called a "better country," and it's sure to be at least as good as planet earth (Heb. 11:10-16). We know there are cities, streets, mansions, rivers, trees, and other things in heaven, as we'll see in Lesson Forty-two. The more we embrace the plain scriptures about heavenly things, the more real the next life becomes to us right now.

## V. Angelic Beings Called Archangels

The word "archangel" appears only twice in Scripture — once in connection with Christ coming in the air to take all the dead and living saints out of the world (1 Thess. 4:16), and once referring to Michael, who disputed with the devil over the body of Moses (Jude 9).
