The word "arch" simply means "chief," suggesting there's a class of beings higher than ordinary angels. In Dan. 10:21 and 12:1, Michael is called "the great prince which standeth for the children of thy people" (the Jews). Rev. 12:7-9 describes him as the commander of God's angels who will fight against the devil and his angels, casting them to earth. He's called "one of the chief princes" of God in Dan. 10:13.

If Michael is "one of" the chief princes, there must be more archangels. Gabriel is another, according to Dan. 8:16-19; 9:20-23; 10:8–11:1; Lk. 1:19, 26. Lucifer was one of the chief angels and was given charge to rule the earth, as we'll see in the next lesson.

In Col. 1:15-18, we read of thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers in heaven — so there must be many chief angels. Some of them rebelled along with the devil and now serve under him as rulers of certain kingdoms of this world (Dan. 10:13–11:1).

## VI. Angelic Beings Known as Common Angels

The words "angel" and "angels" appear 294 times in Scripture. The word comes from Hebrew and Greek terms meaning "messenger." It's used of the second person of the Trinity who appeared to men as a man, or as a messenger of God.

He is called the angel of God (Gen. 21:17; 31:11; Exod. 3:2; Judg. 6:20; 13:9; Acts 27:23); the angel of the Lord (Gen. 16:7-11; 22:11, 15; Exod. 14:19; Num. 22:22-35; Judg. 2:1, 4; 6:11-12, 21-22; 13:3-6, 13-21; 2 Ki. 1:3, 15; 19:35; 1 Chron. 21:12-30; Ps. 34:7; 35:5-6; Isa. 37:36; Zech. 1:9-19; 3:1-6; 12:8; Acts 7:30-38); His angel (Gen. 24:7, 40; Exod. 23:20; 33:2; Num. 20:16; 2 Chron. 32:21; Dan. 3:28; 6:22); the angel (Gen. 48:16; Eccl. 5:6; Hos. 12:4); mine angel (Exod. 23:23; 32:34); and angel of His presence (Isa. 63:9).

It's clear all these passages refer to manifestations of God, not an ordinary angel. In all other places where "the angel of the Lord" is found, the term refers to ordinary angels, as in Mt. 1:20, 24; 2:13, 19; 28:2-5; Lk. 1:11-38; 2:9-21; Acts 5:19; 7:26; 10:3; 12:7-23. The word "angel" is used of Christ in Rev. 8:3-5; 10:1–11:3, and of men in Rev. 1:1, 20; 2:1, 8, 12, 18; 3:1, 7, 14; 19:9-10; 22:6-8, 16. In most other places, it's clear that common angels — spirit-beings and heavenly creatures — are meant.

### The Nature of Angels

Angels are intelligent and wise (2 Sam. 14:20; 19:27; Mt. 24:35); patient (Num. 22:22-35); meek (2 Pet. 2:11; Jude 9); joyful (Lk. 15:1-10); modest (1 Cor. 11:10); holy (Mk. 8:38); glorious (Lk. 9:26); immortal (Lk. 20:36); powerful and mighty (2 Thess. 1:7-10; Rev. 18:1); obedient (Ps. 103:20; Mt. 6:10); heavenly spirit-beings (Ps. 104:4; Mt. 22:30; 24:36; Heb. 1:14); not demons (Acts 23:8-9); not human (Heb. 2:16); limited in knowledge (Mk. 13:32); higher than men (Ps. 8:5); and always spoken of as male beings, not as sexless as some teach from Lk. 20:36.

They have wills (Isa. 14:12-14) and spirit-bodies with hands, feet, eyes, voice, head, mouth, hair, face, and other parts and passions like men (Judg. 13:6; Dan. 10:5-21; Rev. 8:1–9:21; 14:6-11; 15:1–16:21; 2 Thess. 1:7-10). They need no rest (Rev. 4:8); eat food (Gen. 18:8; 19:3; Ps. 78:25); can appear visible and invisible (Num. 22:35; Jn. 20:12; Heb. 13:2); can operate in the physical realm (Gen. 18:1–19:24; 22:11; 2 Sam. 24; 2 Ki. 19:35; Ps. 78:49; 91:11; Mt. 28:2; Acts 10 and 12); can travel at inconceivable speed (Rev. 8:13; 9:1); can ascend and descend (Gen. 28:12; Jn. 1:51); can speak languages (1 Cor. 13:1); and can act and do all things man can do.

### The Work of Angels

Angels drive spirit horses (2 Ki. 2:12; 6:13-17; Zech. 1:7-11; 6:1-6); guard gates (Rev. 21:12); wage war in actual bodily combat (Rev. 12:7-9; 2 Thess. 1:7-10); execute judgments (Gen. 19; 2 Sam. 24; 2 Ki. 19:35; 2 Chron. 32:21; Ps. 78:49; Mt. 13:41-42; Acts 12:23; Rev. 8:1–9:21; 15:1–16:21); minister to saints (1 Ki. 19:5-7; Dan. 6:22; Mt. 4:11; Acts 10; Heb. 1:14); rule nations (Dan. 10:13-21; 12:1); help each individual (Mt. 18:10); sing, praise, and worship God (Lk. 2:13; Ps. 103:20; 148:2; Rev. 5:11); strengthen in trial (Mt. 4:11; Lk. 22:43); lead sinners to gospel workers (Acts 10:3); direct preachers (Acts 8:26; 27:23); appear in dreams (Mt. 1:20-24; 2:13-19); minister before God (Rev. 8:2; 14:15-19); bind Satan (Rev. 20); guard the abyss (Rev. 9:1; 20:1-3); regather Israel (Mt. 24:31); protect saints (Ps. 34:7; 91:11; Acts 12:7-10); separate the good and bad (Mt. 13:39-41); accompany Christ to earth (Mt. 16:27; 25:31; 2 Thess. 1:7-10); witness confessions (Lk. 15:8-9); receive departed spirits (Lk. 16:22); give laws (Acts 7:53; Heb. 2:2); guard the Tree of Life (Gen. 3:24); give revelations (2 Ki. 1:15; Dan. 8:19; 9:21-23; 10:10-20); impart God's will (Acts 5:19-20; 10:1-6); bring answers to prayers (Dan. 9:21-23; 10:12-13; Acts 10); and do many other things for God and man.

### Angels Are Called

Angels are called watchers (Dan. 4:13-23); sons of the mighty (Ps. 89:6); sons of God (Gen. 6:1-4; Job 1:6; 2:1; 38:7); congregation of the mighty (Ps. 82:1); saints (Ps. 89:7); hosts (Ps. 33:6; 103:21; Lk. 2:13); spirits (Heb. 1:14); and the elect (1 Tim. 5:21).

### General Facts About Angels

Angels were created by Christ before the earth (Job 38:4-7; Ps. 148:2-5; Col. 1:16). They are not to be worshipped (Col. 2:16). They are organized into principalities and powers with thrones (Col. 1:16; 2:16; Rom. 8:38; Eph. 6:10-18; 1 Pet. 3:22). They are innumerable (Lk. 2:13; Heb. 12:22); to be judged or ruled by saints (1 Cor. 6:3); subject to God (Mt. 22:30); interested in earthly affairs (Lk. 9:31; 15:7-10; 1 Tim. 5:21; 1 Pet. 1:12); and being taught wisdom by the church (Eph. 3:10; 1 Cor. 4:9).

They desire to look into the things of salvation (1 Pet. 1:10-12); observe us (1 Cor. 4:9); can cook (1 Ki. 19:5-7); wear garments (Jn. 20:12); appear unawares (Heb. 13:2); have been tested (1 Tim. 5:21; Job 4:18); dwell in heaven (Rev. 12:12; 13:6); stand before God (2 Chron. 18:18); and Christ is better than angels (Heb. 1:5–2:16).

### Satan's Angels

Satan's angels are made subject to Christ (1 Pet. 3:22); sinful and rebellious (Job 4:18; 2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7; Rev. 12:7-9); evil (Ps. 78:49); to be punished (Mt. 24:41; Isa. 24:21-23; 25:7; Jude 6-7); to be cast out of heaven (Rev. 12:7-9); to fight against Christ at Armageddon (Rev. 12:7-9; 19:11-21; 20:1-3; Isa. 24:21-23; 25:7); and organized into principalities and powers (Eph. 1:21; 3:10; 6:10-17; Col. 3:10, 15; Rev. 12:7-12).

They deceive men (2 Cor. 11:14); oppose saints (Rom. 8:38; Eph. 6:10-18); originally fell with Lucifer (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17; Mt. 24:41; Rev. 12:7-12); and hell is prepared for them (Mt. 24:41).

### Two Classes of Fallen Angels

There are two classes of fallen angels: those bound (Rev. 9:11-14; 11:7; 17:8; 2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7) and those still loose with Satan who will be cast down to the earth in the middle of Daniel's Seventieth Week of the future Tribulation (Rev. 12:7-12; Eph. 6:10-17). The reason some of Satan's angels are now bound will be discussed in Lesson Eleven.

### Appearances of Angels to Men

Angels appeared to Abraham (Gen. 18:2; 22:11-18); Hagar (Gen. 16:7); Lot and the Sodomites (Gen. 19); Jacob (Gen. 28:12; 31:11-13; 32:1, 24-32); Moses (Exod. 3); Israel (Exod. 14:19; Judg. 2:1-4); Balaam (Num. 22:22-31); Joshua (5:15); Gideon (Judg. 6); Manoah (Judg. 13:6-20); David (2 Sam. 24:16-17); Elijah (1 Ki. 19:5); Elisha (2 Ki. 6:16-17); Daniel (Dan. 6:22; 8:16; 9:21; 10:5-21; 12:5-7); three Hebrew children (Dan. 3:25-28); Zechariah (2:3; 3:1-2; 4:1); Joseph (Mt. 1:20; 2:13-19); Mary (Lk. 1:26-38); Jesus (Mt. 4:11; Lk. 22:43); Zacharias (Lk. 1:20-38); shepherds (Lk. 2:9-14); women (Mt. 28:2-5); disciples (Acts 1:11); Peter and John (Acts 5:19); Phillip (Acts 8:26); Cornelius (Acts 10:3, 30-32); Peter (Acts 12:7-11); Paul (Acts 27:23); and John (Rev. 5:2; 7:11; 14:6-21).

### Angels Have Real, Tangible Bodies

Angels have real, tangible bodies with bodily parts like men. They've appeared to people in real bodies, and people couldn't tell them from other humans. Paul said, "Be not forgetful to entertain strangers: for thereby some have entertained angels unawares" (Heb. 13:2).

This couldn't be true — nor could scores of facts in the scriptures mentioned above — if all these spirit-beings weren't real and didn't have real bodies. The facts also prove they have souls and spirits, for they have feelings, passions, appetites, and desires (which constitute soul) and wills, knowledge, intellect, consciousness, and other faculties (which constitute spirit).

So all seraphims, cherubims, living creatures, archangels, angels, and other spirit-beings have bodies, souls, and spirits like men. If we can conceive that all these spirit-beings are real with personal bodies, souls, and spirits, then it's certainly conceivable that God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit, as three spirit-beings, also have spirit-bodies, souls, and spirits.

## VII. Devils or Unclean Spirits Called Demons

The subject of demons is very real according to the Bible. The word "demon" isn't actually found in Scripture, but it means evil spirit or devil. The word "devil" is used of Satan, the prince of demons (Mt. 9:34; 12:24). He's the chief evil spirit and the original source of evil in the universe.

The Greek word for devil used in connection with Satan is *diabolos*, meaning adversary, false accuser, slanderer, devil. It's translated "false accuser" and "slanderer" and is used of men in 1 Tim. 3:11; 2 Tim. 3:3; Titus 2:3. It's translated "devil" once while referring to Judas when he became an adversary of Christ (Jn. 6:70). In 34 other places it's used of Satan as the chief adversary of God and is translated "devil."

In the other 76 places where "devil" and "devils" are found, they refer to evil spirits or demons, and the word comes from two different Greek words — *daimonion* and *daimon*, meaning evil spirits or devils. There is only one devil, but there are many demons.

Here's a crucial distinction: the devil has an angelic body and cannot enter bodily into anyone, but demons are disembodied spirits and don't seem able to operate in the material world except through possessing men and beasts who have bodies for them to work through. Think of it like this: the devil is like a general commanding from outside, while demons are like operatives who need a "vehicle" (a body) to do their work in the physical realm.

### The Nature of Demons

Demons are evil (Judg. 9:23; 1 Sam. 18:9-10); intelligent and wise (1 Tim. 4:1; 1 Ki. 22:22-24; Acts 16:16); powerful (Mk. 5:1-18); disembodied spirits (Rev. 16:13-16); not angels (Acts 23:8-9); not human, for they possess men and can be cast out (Mt. 10:7; Mk. 16:17); and are individuals (Mk. 16:9).

They have knowledge (Mt. 9:29; Lk. 4:41; Acts 19:15); faith (Jas. 2:19); feelings (Mt. 8:29; Mk. 5:7); fellowship (1 Cor. 10:20-21); doctrines (1 Tim. 4:1); wills (Mt. 12:43-45); miraculous powers (Rev. 16:13-16); intelligence (1 Tim. 4:1; 1 Jn. 4:1-6); emotions (Acts 8:7); desires (Mt. 8:28-31); and other soul and spirit faculties, as defined in Lesson Four, Point I, 9.

### The Work of Demons

Demons possess people and cause: dumbness and deafness (Mt. 9:32-33; Mk. 9:25); blindness (Mt. 12:22); grievous vexation (Mt. 15:22); lunacy and mania (Mt. 4:23-24; 17:14-21; Mk. 5:1-18); uncleanness (called unclean spirits 21 times, Lk. 4:36); supernatural strength (Mk. 5:1-18); suicide (Mt. 17:15; Jn. 10:10); fits (Mk. 9:20); lusts (Jn. 8:44; Eph. 2:1-3; 1 Jn. 2:15-17); counterfeit worship (Lev. 17:7; Deut. 32:17; 2 Chron. 11:15; Ps. 106:37; 1 Cor. 10:30; Rev. 9:20); error (1 Jn. 4:1-6; 1 Tim. 4:1); sickness and disease (Mt. 4:23-24; Acts 10:38); torments (Mt. 4:23-24; Mt. 15:22); deceptions (1 Tim. 4:1-2; 1 Jn. 4:1-6); lying (1 Ki. 22:21-24); enchantments and witchcraft (2 Chron. 33:6); heresies (1 Tim. 4:1); false doctrines (1 Tim. 4:1); wickedness (Lk. 11:26); fear (2 Tim. 1:7); worldliness (1 Jn. 2:15-17; 1 Cor. 2:12); bondage (Rom. 8:15); discord (Mt. 13:39; 1 Ki. 22:21-24); violence (Mt. 17:15); betrayals (Jn. 13:2; 1 Ki. 22:22-23); oppression (Acts 10:38); sin (Jn. 8:44; 1 Jn. 3:8); persecution (Rev. 2:10; 1 Pet. 5:8); jealousy (1 Sam. 16:14; 18:8-10); false prophecy (1 Sam. 18:8-10; 1 Ki. 22:21-24); and cause every evil they possibly can to man and God.

### What Demons Can Do

They can teach (1 Tim. 4:1); steal (Mt. 13:19; Lk. 8:12); fight (Eph. 4:27; 6:10-18; 1 Pet. 5:8); get mad (Mt. 8:28; Rev. 12:12); tell fortunes (Lev. 20:27; Acts 16:16); be friendly (called familiar spirits 16 times, Lev. 20:6, 27); go out and come back into men as they will, unless cast out and rejected (Mt. 12:43-45); travel (1 Ki. 22:21-24; Mk. 5:7, 12); speak (Mk. 1:34; 5:12; Acts 8:7); imitate departed dead (2 Sam. 28:3-9; 1 Chron. 10:13; Isa. 8:19; Deut. 18:11); and do many things when in possession of bodies through whom they operate.

### What Demons Are Called

They are called devils (Mk. 16:17); familiar spirits (Lev. 20:6); unclean spirits (Mk. 1:27); evil spirits (Lk. 7:21); seducing spirits (1 Tim. 4:1); and other things. They are made subject to Christ and believers by the Atonement, the name of Jesus, and the Holy Spirit (Mt. 8:16-17; 12:28; Mk. 16:17; Lk. 10:17; Acts 19:15).

Thousands of them can enter into and take possession of one man at the same time (Mk. 5:9). They must be discerned, tested, resisted, and rejected by believers (1 Jn. 4:1-6; 1 Cor. 12:10; Eph. 5:27; 6:10-18; 1 Pet. 5:8-9).

They possess more than ordinary intelligence (Mt. 8:29). Their rightful place is in the abyss (Lk. 8:31; Rev. 9:1-21). They have personality (Lk. 8:26-33); are disembodied (Mt. 12:43-45); are Satan's emissaries (Mt. 12:26-29); and are numerous (Mk. 5:9).

They enter into and control both men and beasts (Mk. 5:1-18) and seek embodiment (Mt. 12:43-45; Lk. 8:32). Demon possession and demon influence are different (Mt. 4:23-24 with 16:21-23). They know their fate (Mt. 8:31-32) and those who have power over them (Acts 19:13-17).

They fear God (Jas. 2:19); inflict physical maladies (Mt. 12:22; 17:15-18); war on saints (Eph. 6:10-18); and influence men (1 Tim. 4:1-5; 2 Pet. 2:10-12). All unbelievers are more or less possessed with them (Eph. 2:1-3). The only resources against them are prayer, bodily control, and the whole armour of God (Mt. 17:21; Eph. 6:10-18).

There are demon spirits for every sickness, unholy trait, and doctrinal error known. They must be cast out to get relief from them. Disease germs, which are closely allied with unclean spirits, are really living forms of corruption that come into men's bodies and bring them to death. Just as refuse breeds maggots, man in his fallen state of corruption breeds germs through unclean living and contact with corruptions in the fallen world. They are agents of Satan, corrupting the bodies of his victims.

Traffic with demon spirits is forbidden in both Testaments (Lev. 19:31; 20:6; Deut. 18:10; Isa. 8:19-21; 1 Chron. 10:13-14; Lk. 4:41; Acts 16:16; 1 Tim. 4:1-5; 2 Pet. 2:1-3; 1 Jn. 4:1-6).

## VIII. Other Spirit-Beings in Scripture

Scripture mentions many kinds of demon spirits, fallen angels, and other spirit-beings. It's certain they weren't created wicked. They were originally created perfect and sinless, and became fallen and unclean. Even the prince of demons is spoken of as being "perfect in thy ways from the day that thou wast created, till iniquity was found in thee" (Ezek. 28:11-17).

God could not and did not create anything sinful. Everything in the spirit and human realms that is wicked and rebellious has become that way because of sin and free choice.

The fallen angels, demons, and those mentioned below are all rebels connected with planet earth. They sinned in the original rebellion under Lucifer, or they wouldn't be confined in prisons under the earth or be operating on earth now against God

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Angels
lessons.content:
# 7. THE DISPENSATION OF ANGELS

## REBELLION AND OVERTHROW OF THE FIRST SOCIAL SYSTEM

(Gen. 1:2; Isa. 14:12-14; Jer. 4:23-26; Ezek. 28:11-17; 2 Pet. 3:5-6)

## Earth's First Sinless Career

(Gen. 1:1; Ezek. 28:15)

### I. The Antechaotic Age—The Dispensation of Angels

Let's explore a fascinating period that many people have never heard of: the Antechaotic Age. This era stretched from the original creation of the heavens and earth to the rebellion and overthrow of the first *kosmos*, or social order, on our planet.

Think of it like this: there was a "before" to our "before." This was a dateless period between Gen. 1:1—when the earth was finished and inhabited in the beginning—and Gen. 1:2, when the earth was first flooded, destroying all life. We're talking about that unknown span of time when the earth existed in its first perfect state, ruled by Lucifer before his rebellion caused the catastrophic flood described in Gen. 1:2.

In Ezek. 28:11-17, we read of Lucifer: "Thou wast perfect in thy ways from the day that thou wast created, till iniquity was found in thee." This tells us plainly that there was a period of perfection and sinlessness on earth before its curse and chaotic state. That's why we call this period the Antechaotic Age.

During this age, spirit beings ruled the earth and other planets—so we might call it the "Dispensation of Angels." This administration was evidently a moral or probationary period designed to test the angels before trusting them with eternal responsibilities. The word "dispensation" means a moral or probationary period in which God tests free moral agents according to a fixed standard of conduct.

Here's something important to understand: all spirit beings must be tested, just like human free moral agents, to see if they will obey God before being trusted with higher and eternal responsibility. These angels were fresh from God's creative hand, made with free wills, capable of choosing whether to obey or not.

They had to learn through experience whether they would obey God. They had to learn that God was just and holy in all His ways—that He could and should be trusted even in matters that couldn't be fully explained to them all at once. They needed to discover that God was the Supreme Moral Governor of the universe, and that all creatures should dedicate themselves to the same goal God Himself was dedicated to: the highest good of the universe and everyone in it.

They had to prove themselves true to God to receive rewards for obedience. They had to learn that God's Word was absolutely reliable—including His warnings about penalties for disobedience.

God's dealings with spirit beings served the same purpose as His dealings with humans. These purposes are fully explained in Lesson One, Point VII, and I'd encourage you to review that section to see more clearly what God was doing with spirit beings during this period.

If Lucifer and the other spirit beings had remained true to God, there would have been no universal curse on the earth. There would have been no need for the re-creation of the atmospheric heavens, the earth, and life on our planet, as recorded in Gen. 1:3–2:25. In that case, man and the present animals on earth would never have been created!

The Bible's record of the first curse on earth and the cancellation of the angels' administration should make present-day rebels fear God. Peter gives us this sobering warning: "Whose judgment now for a long time lingereth not, and their damnation slumbereth not. For if God spared not the angels that sinned, but cast them down to hell, and delivered them into chains of darkness, to be reserved unto judgment; and spared not the old world but saved Noah the eighth person, a preacher of righteousness, bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly; and turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah into ashes condemned them with an overthrow, making them an example unto those that should live ungodly; and delivered just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked . . . The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations, and to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished" (2 Pet. 2:4-9).

Jude also speaks of God's judgment upon both angels and men in the past for rebellion (Jude 5-7).

Paul gives us the example of how God cut off Israel because of rebellion and unbelief, warning the Gentiles to "take heed lest He spare not thee" (Rom. 11:1-26). He also describes how the Gentile world didn't appreciate the knowledge of God, becoming vain in their imaginations and stubborn in their rebellion until God gave them over to vile affections to destroy themselves (Rom. 1:16-32).

We could give many more examples of God's judgment upon men in various ages—and we will as we continue our study. But these are enough to show why God had to deal with the angelic administrators of the earth long before Adam ever walked the planet.

The "ways" in which Lucifer was perfect until iniquity was found in him prove that angels were placed under certain restrictions for testing purposes. Think of it like quality testing in manufacturing—tests are absolutely necessary for anything that's made to discover if it will perform as designed. How much more true this is for free moral agents capable of making voluntary moral choices! Review Lesson One, Point VII, 1, to understand why such moral tests are necessary for beings with free wills.

What would be the point of creating free wills if there were no laws to obey—no restrictions to guide them in their relationships with other creatures? Why create beings capable of voluntary obedience without explaining right from wrong so they can make their choice?

Even of the Sinless One, Scripture says: "Though he were a Son, yet learned he obedience by the things which he suffered; and being made perfect, he became the author of eternal salvation unto all them that obey him" (Heb. 5:7-10).

So here's an important truth: sinless creatures can learn obedience to the right ways without ever committing sin. They can learn by experience how to know God and walk in His ways without experiencing sin at all. This is exactly what happened with Jesus: "Who did no sin, neither was guile found in his mouth" (1 Pet. 2:21-25; Heb. 2:14-18; 4:14-16; 5:7-10).

Neither angels nor men had to sin in order to learn obedience and be tested. The old theory that sin is necessary for free moral agents to learn obedience simply isn't true. In the eternal future, when humans multiply forever in the New Earth, new generations will be true to God from childhood, learning all necessary obedience without ever committing sin (Rev. 21:1-7).

There are some of the richest studies awaiting you in the coming lessons—truths that will be priceless to you, your friends, and loved ones. Whatever you do, don't miss learning all of God's plan for your life!

If it was necessary to test human free wills and even the Son of God, it certainly was necessary to test spirit beings for the same purpose. How was God to know which spirit beings would remain true and which would not, until they were all tested and proven faithful or unfaithful?

They couldn't have been tested without definite tests and laws to obey. Naturally, not all free wills would choose the same things or exercise their creative powers of free choice in the same way or with the same degree of willful obedience or disobedience. This has been demonstrated by free wills ever since they were created. You know this from your own experience—you can make free and voluntary choices about any detail of life.

It wasn't until Adam and Eve actually sinned that God knew the full results of their test (Gen. 3). It wasn't until humanity proved such a failure before the flood that God regretted having made them (Gen. 6:5-7). It wasn't until God came down to see the rebellion of those after the flood—Sodom, Gomorrah, and others—that God took action (Gen. 11:1-9; 18:21).

So it is with every free moral agent in every period. Each one has to be tried and purged of any possibility of falling before God places them in an eternal state with eternal responsibility.

In God's moral government, there must be laws for all creatures. Each creature must be assigned some particular responsibility so the universe runs in perfect harmony. God couldn't rule free wills if each was free to do whatever they pleased. No government could survive with such a plan—this is obvious to anyone living in any kind of society.

It's similar to how any project works in human affairs: each person must do something and be responsible for something if anything is to be accomplished. This is true from the smallest to the greatest undertaking. The greater the project, the greater the responsibility for those trusted with its completion. The greater the project, the greater the need for many people to be responsible for some part of the plan.

How much more true would this be in running the vast universe? God is methodical in everything and has fixed laws governing every detail. Each free will is given a part in carrying out God's mutual plan for all concerned. God has a plan for the life and work of every creature and a purpose for every material thing created.

Every planet, sun, moon, and star must run in its own creative orbit, each one on time—or the entire planetary system would become undependable. Times and seasons would be thrown into chaos, and all life on every planet would soon be destroyed. So it is with the moral creation.

God did not make the universe for chaos and ruin, nor did He create individuals to be lawless, irresponsible creatures who could stop governmental function or do whatever they please. There must be order and someone to maintain it. There must be one law for all to ensure justice for all. There must be voluntary and mutual cooperation from each free will for everyone to fit into God's plan for the highest good of all.

Each creature must be held responsible for their own actions—rewarded for obedience or punished for disobedience—or no government can properly function for everyone's good. If God is to maintain the respect and willing obedience of all creatures, He must be fair and just to all, meting out punishments for sin and distributing rewards for obedience.

Government by rewards and penalties for all alike is the only just form of government—and the only one that can last forever. When government becomes corrupt, showing favoritism to some and injustice to others, it ceases to fulfill its purpose. This leads to rebellion and eventual overthrow.

If rebels against proper, just government were allowed to openly rebel without punishment, there would be no incentive for others to remain true and support the government. It would soon collapse. So to be just, God is forced by circumstances to deal with rebels and put down rebellion if He is to maintain His government for the highest good of all. He is equally obligated to reward the faithful and preserve just government for them.

On these grounds, every child of God can claim the rewards and benefits promised in Scripture. Every child of God can have perfect health, freedom from want and pain, happiness, and untold blessings if they will simply obey the laws of rewards and blessings. We'll address these laws in some measure in each lesson, with special lessons dedicated to them later. First, we need a general understanding of God's dealings with free moral agents before studying His specific plan for us today.

The Bible teaches that one-third of the angels proved untrue, as did all the subjects of the first social order on earth over whom Lucifer ruled before Adam. We don't know exactly how many rulers and subjects of other planets rebelled, but Scripture does reveal the following:

- That there are thrones and kingdoms in the heavens as well as on the earth, visible and invisible (Col. 1:15-18).
- That God charged some of His angels with folly (Job 4:18).
- That over one-third of God's angels rebelled with Lucifer (Rev. 12:3-12; Mt. 25:41).
- That redeemed human beings are to judge (rule) angels (1 Cor. 6:3).
- That women are not to cut their hair, but keep it long as a sign of subjection to their own husbands so that angels will have good examples to be in subjection to God (1 Cor. 11:10).
- That the purpose of the Millennial reign of Christ is to "gather in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth; even in him" (Eph. 1:10).
- To rid the earth of all rebels (1 Cor. 15:24-28).
- That all rebels of every kind and every realm will have to finally acknowledge that Jesus Christ is Lord to the glory of God the Father (Phil. 2:9-11).
- That it is Christ's work to reconcile both the things in heaven and the things in earth (Eph. 1:10; Col. 1:20).
- That Christ has already triumphed over the rebels and is exalted above all other angelic rulers and powers far above all heavens (Eph. 1:21; 6:10-18; Col. 2:14-17; 1 Pet. 3:22).
- That Christ is waiting now until the completion of man's probationary test in this age before He comes down from heaven to put down all rebellion on earth (Acts 3:19-21; 1 Cor. 15:24-28; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Rev. 19:11–20:3; Zech. 14:1-21).
- That God is now schooling the principalities and powers in heavenly places in His manifold wisdom by making an example of submission by an actual demonstration of the Church (Eph. 3:1-11; 1 Cor. 4:9; 11:10).
- That some rebel angels are now bound in hell awaiting judgment (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7).
- That one-third of God's angels who rebelled are now still loose with Satan carrying on rebellion against God (Rev. 12:3-12; Dan. 10:13-21; Eph. 6:10-18).
- That they will be defeated in the middle of the last 7 years of this age and will be cast down to the earth for 1,260 days and then they will be cast into the pit for a thousand years (Rev. 12:3-14; 20:1-3; Isa. 24:21-23).
- That they will be liberated for a little season at the end of the Millennium to deceive men living on the earth at that time, and then they will meet their final defeat and be cast into the Lake of Fire which is prepared for them (Rev. 20:7-10; Mt. 25:41; Isa. 24:21-23).

These simple biblical facts help us understand the outcome of the angelic administration of the earth and the probationary period of earth creatures before the flood of Gen. 1:2.

We don't know what means of reconciliation God offered to the spirit beings and the first rebels on earth, but there must have been such means. God has always dealt with rebels in mercy and longsuffering—it's His nature. It would be contrary to His very Being to cut them off without some means of reconciliation, without giving them a chance to lay down their arms of rebellion.

Remember that spirit beings are immortal in body, soul, and spirit. They're not subject to physical death—the separation of the inner person from the body (Jas. 2:26)—so the penalty they faced couldn't have been the same as what humans face in this respect. However, they will be tormented in literal fire and brimstone forever, just as unrepentant humans will be (Mt. 25:41; Jude 6-7; Rev. 20:10).

It's also important to remember what the Bible is and isn't. It's not a book that deals specifically with the administration of the angels, their rebellion, their means of reconciliation (if any), their former relationship to God, or the degrees of punishment they'll suffer. The Bible is a book revealing the origin of all things, including angels and humans, but it primarily deals with humanity—our rebellion and our future in God's plan.

As we've seen above and in Lesson Six, Scripture contains hundreds of references to spirit beings: their origin, fall, present work, future destiny, and much more. These things are revealed because of their role in furthering God's plan for humanity.

### II. The Kingdom of God over All—God All-in-All

After the original creation of the heavens and the earth and all things in them, as we saw in Lesson Five, the kingdom of God was universal. God was all-in-all, with not one creature lifting a finger in rebellion against Him.

Everyone was willingly subject to His sovereign will, walking in perfect obedience to God and His moral law. Each ruler and each subject of every kingdom throughout the universe was faithful in personal responsibility and dedicated to the highest good of all beings and the universe itself.

No free moral agent acted from selfishness or apart from the Creator's will. Everything was in harmony and perfection.

How long this condition lasted is unknown, and all speculation is useless. In the following sections, we'll prove that rebellion was started by the angelic ruler of the earth, who conceived the idea and boldly attempted to dethrone God and become the supreme ruler of the universe.

From this point forward, the Bible reveals that the earth has been made perfect and inhabited two times, and that there have been two universal rebellions on earth as well as two sinful careers. It records two past curses on the earth and reveals that the earth will be made new one more time, that righteousness will dwell on earth forever, and that God will become all-in-all again—just as He was before rebellion was started by Lucifer and Adam (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Rev. 21–22; 2 Pet. 3:13; Isa. 65:17; 66:22-24).

## Earth's First Sinful Career

(Gen. 1:2; Isa 14:1-14; Jer. 4:23-26; Ezek. 28:11-17; 2 Pet. 3:5-6)

### III. Moses' Teaching on the Overthrow of the Pre-Adamite World

In Gen. 1:2, we learn that the earth was already in existence before the Spirit of God began to move (brood) upon the face of the waters covering the earth. The conjunction "and" connects about 200 separate acts of God in Gen. 1 and 2. These acts are all equally independent and important.

Verse 2 is as independent of verse 1 as all other separate acts in these two chapters. In verse 1, we have the original creation of the heavens and the earth. In verse 2, we have the originally perfect earth made chaotic and flooded with water, which destroyed all life.

The word "was" in verse 2 comes from the Hebrew *hayah*, which is a verb meaning "to become," not "to be." It's translated *became* 67 times (Gen. 2:7; 19:26; 20:12; 24:67; Exod. 4:3-4; Num. 12:10; etc.); *becomes* (1 Chron. 17:22; Ezek. 16:8); *came* and *came to pass* 505 times (Gen. 4:3; 6:1, 4; 11:2, 5; etc.); *become* 66 times (Gen. 3:22; 18:18; 48:19; etc.); *come* and *come to pass* 131 times (Gen. 4:14; 6:13, 18-20; 27:40; etc.); and many times *be* in the sense of become (Gen. 1:3, 6, 9, 14; 3:5; etc.).

The phrase "without form" comes from the Hebrew *tohu*, meaning waste, desolation, or confusion. It's translated *waste* (Deut. 32:10); *without form* (Gen. 1:2; Jer. 4:23); *vain* (Isa. 45:18; 1 Sam. 12:21); *confusion* (Isa. 24:10; 34:11; 41:29); *empty* (Job 26:7); *vanity* (Isa. 40:17, 23; 44:9; 59:4); *nothing* (Job 6:18; Isa. 40:17); and *wilderness* (Job 12:24; Ps. 107:40).

From these passages, you can see what the word really means and what condition the earth was in during Gen. 1:2. God did not originally create the earth in such a waste and ruined state. Isa. 45:18 explicitly states that God did not create the earth *tohu* (vain or desolate)—yet in Gen. 1:2, the earth was *tohu*.

If the earth wasn't originally created desolate, then it must have been created, inhabited, and later *became* desolate. Even the English verb "was" indicates it had to become desolate before it could be desolate.

The Hebrew word for "void" is *bohu*, meaning empty, ruin, or void. It's translated *void* (Gen. 1:2; Jer. 4:23) and *emptiness* (Isa. 34:11). The Hebrew phrase *tohu va bohu* (waste and ruin, or desolate and empty) describes the chaotic condition of the earth since "the beginning" and before the six days of reconstruction in Gen. 1:3–2:25.

God did not create the earth as a ruin or waste. It became so because of sin, as we'll see.

We can read Gen. 1:1-2 literally this way: "In the beginning [by periods, ages] God created the heavens and the earth. And the earth became waste and ruin [desolate and empty]; and darkness was upon the face of the deep [the flood]. And the Spirit of God moved [brooded] upon the face of the waters."

In these verses we have the whole span of the creative ages—from all the original creation of the heavens and earth and everything in them, through to the six days of restoring the earth to a habitable state. The original creations include the sun, moon, and stars, as proven in Lesson Five.

In these two verses alone we find these facts: that in the dateless past God created the heavens (including the sun, moon, and stars) and then the earth; that the heavens were created before the earth; that the heavens, the earth, the waters, and the darkness were all created before the Spirit began to brood over the waters; and that these things were already in existence before the first of the six days—proving they were not created in any one of those days.

How long the earth was waste and ruin, desolate and empty, since its original habitation is unknown. How long it existed and was inhabited before it became desolate and empty is unknown. But *why* and *when* it was cursed and became desolate and empty is known and clearly revealed in Scripture, as we'll see below.

In Scripture, all cases of obscuring the sun and bringing consequent darkness, and all cases of floods, are the result of judgment—never an act of creation—unless Gen. 1:2 is an exception. But we have no authority to believe it is. Why couldn't Gen. 1:2 be the result of a curse, like all other floods and darkness on earth revealed in Scripture (Gen. 6–8; Exod. 10:21-23; Isa. 5:30; 13:10; Jer. 4:23-26; Amos 5:18-20; Zeph. 1:15; Joel 2:30–3:16; Mt. 8:12; 9:2; 16:10)?

The fact that Moses, by inspiration, said God told Adam to multiply and *replenish* the earth proves there was a social system on earth before Adam. After all, you can't *re*plenish something that had never been plenished before!

Some argue that the Hebrew word for "replenish" means *fill*, not *refill*, but this proves nothing. An examination of all places where the word is used disproves this argument. Try making "replenish" mean just "plenish" in Gen. 9:1; Isa. 2:6; 23:2; Jer. 31:25; Ezek. 26:2; 27:25, and note the results.

Where the Hebrew *mala* is translated "fill," it doesn't mean the thing referred to had never been filled before. For example, when Joseph commanded his brethren to "fill their sacks," does this mean those sacks had never been filled before? They had no doubt been filled many times (Gen. 42:25; 44:1; 1 Ki. 18:33; Hag. 2:7; etc.).

To say, "Fill that glass with water," doesn't prove it had never been filled before. But to say, "Refill that glass with water" proves it had been filled before. When God told Noah, "Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth" (Gen. 9:1), it's clear the earth had been filled or "plenished" before. So why not believe God meant the same thing when He said it to Adam?

The same Hebrew statement is found in both passages (Gen. 1:28; 9:1), and it's translated exactly the same in English—so would it be wrong to believe it means the same thing? If the earth had been "plenished" before Adam, then it was overthrown by judgment before the six days, as shown in Gen. 1:2.

### IV. Isaiah's Teaching on the Overthrow of the Pre-Adamite World

In Isa. 14:12-14, we have conclusive proof that Lucifer ruled the earth before Adam's days. This couldn't have happened since Adam—because Lucifer was already a fallen creature before Adam was created. In Adam's day, Satan regained dominion of the earth and has been the prince of this world ever since.

In this passage, both Satan and the king of Babylon are in view. This is what's known as the law of double reference—a visible person is immediately addressed while at the same time an invisible person, who is using the visible person as a tool to hinder God's plan, is also addressed.

Picture it this way: when Jesus said to Peter, "Get thee behind me, Satan: for thou art an offense to me: for thou savourest not the things that be of God, but those that be of men" (Mt. 16:23), He didn't mean Peter was the personal devil. Rather, Peter was being used as Satan's tool to keep Christ from reaching the cross. Hence, both Peter and Satan are addressed in that statement.

To understand such passages, understand that the part that can refer to an earthly person refers to a human, while the part that cannot possibly refer to an earthly person refers to the invisible person also addressed.

In Isa. 14:12-14, we find statements that cannot possibly be made of any earthly king of Babylon. This passage is almost universally accepted as referring to Satan's fall. Let's quote it and examine the facts:

"How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer, son of the morning! how art thou cut down to the ground, which didst weaken the nations! For thou hast said in thine heart, I will ascend into heaven, I will exalt my throne above the stars of God: I will sit also upon the mount of the congregation, in the sides of the north: I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will be like the most High."

Note these facts about Satan:

> 1. That his name is Lucifer.

> 2. That he was a son of the morning, and therefore, no earthly man.

> 3. That he fell from heaven. This could never be spoken of a man. Satan is the only person in all Scripture who is spoken of as falling from heaven (Lk. 10:18).

> 4. That he was cut down to the ground in his fall.

> 5. That he had weakened nations on earth.

> 6. That he was exalted in his heart.

> 7. That he invaded heaven, where God rules.

> 8. That he was a king, for he had a throne and subjects over whom he ruled.

> 9. That he wanted to exalt his kingdom above the clouds, stars, and into heaven itself to dethrone God.

> 10. That he wanted to be worshipped in the congregation of God (Ps. 72:2; 82:1; Isa. 6:1).

> 11. That he led a rebellion against God but was defeated.

> 12. That his kingdom was on earth, or he never could have tried to ascend above the clouds, stars, and into heaven. When a kingdom is located under the clouds it has to be on the earth, for one can stand on certain parts of the earth and still be above clouds.

> 13. That the ground, clouds, stars, and heaven were already created.

> 14. That it had to be before Adam, for this was not the position of Lucifer when he was in Adam's Eden. Adam had dominion at that time. He had no kingdom on earth at the time of Adam and has not had thereafter, in the sense of a visible, personal rule on earth. He has only ruled through others since Adam's day, so this must refer to a time before Adam.

> 15. That he was not in heaven when he rebelled, or else he could not have desired to ascend into heaven. He was under the stars, or he could not have desired to be exalted above the stars. He was under the clouds, or he could not have desired to ascend above them.

> 16. That God's throne is located in the north part of the universe (Ps. 75:6-7).

This passage proves the location of Satan's original kingdom and the time of his fall. His kingdom was on earth, and his fall occurred before Adam.

In Col. 1:15-18, we read of Christ creating thrones, dominions, principalities, and powers in heaven and on earth. They were located somewhere in the heavens and on the earth. Lucifer was given a kingdom here on earth, as proven by the passages cited above.

His own subjects were earthly creatures of various kinds, all destroyed when the flood of Gen. 1:2 came upon the earth. God created the earth to be inhabited by earthly creatures (Isa. 45:18). They are called "nations" in the above passage.

The demons who are now Satan's emissaries could be the spirits of the pre-Adamites. At any rate, they're not part of the creation at the time of Adam. Thus, Isaiah teaches that the earth was inhabited before Adam and was ruled by Lucifer, whose kingdom was overthrown when he rebelled.

### V. Ezekiel's Teaching on the Overthrow of the Pre-Adamite World

In Ezek. 28:11-17, we have another description of Lucifer before he fell—his position on earth and the cause of his rebellion. This passage is another example of the law of double reference explained in Point IV. The earthly man addressed was the king of Tyre (see Josephus, *Apion* Book I, 21).

Let's quote this passage and examine the main facts concerning Satan:

"Thus saith the Lord GOD; Thou sealest up the sum, full of wisdom, and perfect in beauty. Thou hast been in Eden the garden of God; every precious stone was thy covering, the sardius, topaz, and the diamond, the beryl, the onyx, and the jasper, the sapphire, the emerald, and the carbuncle, and gold: the workmanship of thy tabrets and of thy pipes was prepared in thee in the day that thou wast created. Thou art the anointed cherub that covereth; and I have set thee so: thou wast upon the holy mountain of God; thou hast walked up and down in the midst of the stones of fire. Thou wast perfect in thy ways from the day that thou wast created, till iniquity was found in thee. By the multitude of thy merchandise they have filled the midst of thee with violence, and thou hast sinned: therefore I will cast thee as profane out of the mountain of God: and I will destroy thee, O covering cherub, from the midst of the stones of fire. Thine heart was lifted up because of thy beauty, thou hast corrupted thy wisdom by reason of thy brightness: I will cast thee to the ground, I will lay thee before kings, that they may behold thee. Thou hast defiled thy sanctuaries by the multitude of thine iniquities, by the iniquity of thy traffick; therefore will I bring forth a fire from the midst of thee, it shall devour thee, and I will bring thee to ashes upon the earth in the sight of all them that behold thee" (Ezek. 28:11-19).

Note these facts about Satan:

> 1. That he was full of wisdom and perfect in beauty.

> 2. That he had been in Eden, the garden of God—not a mere summer residence of some earthly king of Tyre, but the garden of God as referred to in other passages (Isa. 51:3; Ezek. 31:9, 16, 18; 36:35; Joel 2:3). This Eden, however, was the one that was on the original earth before Adam, for "every precious stone" was not the covering of Satan, nor was he perfect and sinless and the ruling cherub when he was in Adam's Eden. It was the garden that God made as the location of Lucifer's kingdom, as pictured in this whole passage.

> 3. That he was the ruler of this Eden, for at that time

lessons.title: The Story of Re-Creation
lessons.content:
# 8. THE STORY OF RE-CREATION

## THE STORY OF RE-CREATION

**Gen. 1:3–2:25**

### Earth Made Perfect a Second Time (Gen. 1:3–2:25)

In this lesson, we're going to explore one of the most exciting chapters in all of Scripture — the end of chaos on Earth and the beginning of its restoration to a second perfect state. We'll walk through the work of the six days and the beginning of "the heavens and the earth, which are now" (Gen. 1:3–2:25; 2 Pet. 3:5-7).

The biblical evidence we covered in Lessons Five and Seven should automatically clear up questions that naturally arise from a wrong understanding of the creation story. Questions about the original creation of the heavens and earth before the flood of Gen. 1:2, the pre-Adamite world, the chaotic period, where demons came from, how old the universe is, where Lucifer's kingdom was located and why he fell, and the cause of sin on earth — all these have been fully answered.

Now we can turn our attention to how and when the present creation was made. The time of re-creation was during the six days of Gen. 1:3–2:25. Let's look at the main points of this remarkable story.

### I. Are the Six Days of Gen. 1 Literal Days?

The six days of Gen. 1 were literal 24-hour days, just like the days we've known ever since. Scripture makes this very clear. Here's why:

> 1. The word "evening" comes from the Hebrew *ehred*, meaning dusk, evening, or night. It's translated "evening" 49 times and is never used in a figurative sense. The word "morning" comes from the Hebrew *boker*, meaning dawn, break of day, morning, or early light. It's translated "morning" 187 times — again, never figuratively.
>
> This shows us that "day" and "night," or "light" and "darkness," are literal periods regulated by the sun, moon, and stars, just as Scripture describes elsewhere (Gen. 8:22; Ps. 19:2; Job 38:12; Jer. 31:35-37; 33:19-26). There's no hint anywhere in Scripture that day and night ever came from a different source, or that we should understand them symbolically.

> 2. It's true that the word "day," used 2,182 times as a literal day, can sometimes refer to a longer period when qualified as "the day of the Lord" or "the day of God." However, when it's used with qualifying words like "evening" and "morning" to mark its beginning and end, it can only be understood literally.
>
> This is further proven by the numbering of each day — first, second, third, and so on — just as you'd naturally number literal days. No symbolic period is ever numbered in Scripture.

> 3. Exod. 20:8-11 and 31:14-17 definitely state that God *made* (not created) the heavens (the firmament, not heaven where God dwells) and the earth in six days. Man was told to work the same length of time it took God to do the work of Gen. 1:3–2:25: "Six days shalt thou labour . . . For in six days the Lord made heaven and earth."
>
> Think of it like this: it would be just as logical to argue that man was supposed to work 6,000 years or six indefinite periods before resting as it would be to argue that about God's six days of work. The exact same words are used in both passages referring to the same kind of days.
>
> Personal interpretation of identical words to prove two different ideas can't be a valid basis for claiming literal days are meant in one case but not the other. No one argues that the six days of Exod. 20 are long periods of time, yet they don't even have the clear qualifying words "evening" and "morning" and "first," "second," "third" that the days of Gen. 1 have.
>
> From the standpoint of plain language and common sense, the days of re-creation are just as literal as man's work days. Everyone knows work days for man couldn't be indefinite periods — days in Moses' time were as long as days now, and the same was true of the days of re-creation in Adam's time. Where would anyone get the idea that Genesis days aren't ordinary days? It couldn't come from the plain language of Scripture, and we must reject any other source.

> 4. Remember our fundamental principle of Bible interpretation: take the Bible literally wherever possible. When the language can't be literal or when it states otherwise, then the passage is figurative.
>
> On this basis, we have to take the days in Genesis as literal. Couldn't God do this work in six literal days just as easily as in 6,000 years? If He can, and if the Bible says so, then let it stand and don't change it.

> 5. Some might argue that Genesis days couldn't be literal because the sun, moon, and stars hadn't been created yet to regulate day and night. But we've already seen that they were created originally in the beginning when God created the heavens before the earth (Gen. 1:1; Job 38:4-7).
>
> If the earth existed before day 1 (as is clear in Gen. 1:2), and if the heavens were created before the earth, then the sun, moon, and stars also existed before day 1. This means the light of the first three days came from the same source it has come from every day since.
>
> There had been days and nights throughout Lucifer's reign, just not during the total darkness of chaos while the earth was cursed and the lights were withheld from it (Gen. 1:2; Jer. 4:23-26). The work of day 1 was simply the restoration of day and night as it had been when Lucifer ruled.

> 6. The 1,000-year-day theory is ridiculous when you examine the facts. If this theory were true, it took God a very long time to do the work of these six days, and even longer originally to create all things (as we discussed in Lesson Five, Point VIII).
>
> If this theory were true, the waters remained on the earth at least 1,000 years before being divided. The earth was still desolate another 1,000 years before vegetation was planted. And vegetation was on earth 1,000 years before the sun, moon, and stars were created (if, as some suppose, they were created on the fourth day).
>
> Here's the problem: How could vegetation live so long without the sun? Furthermore, vegetation would have been here about 2,000 years before animal life, so the earth must have been a dense forest for 2,000 years. Fish and fowls would have been here 1,000 years before man and other land animals — making them rulers of the earth all that time.
>
> We'd also have to conclude it took God 1,000 years to create fish and fowls and another 1,000 years to create land animals and man. According to Gen. 2:7-25, man was created before the animals, and the animals were all created before the woman — meaning Adam was created at the beginning of the sixth day and Eve at the end. If this day was 1,000 years long, man was 1,000 years old before a wife was made for him!
>
> God rested another 1,000 years between making Eve and man's fall, so there would be about 2,000 years from Adam's creation at the beginning of the sixth 1,000-year period to the fall of man after the seventh 1,000-year period — yet Adam was only 130 years old when Seth was born (Gen. 5:1-3). How foolish human theories look when examined in the light of common sense and Scripture!
>
> We conclude, therefore, that the six days of Genesis were literal 24-hour days, as the facts plainly show.

> 7. Some translations read "age" for "day," but that's incorrect. Even if an age were meant, we've seen in Lesson One, Point IV, 1, that an age is any period of time, whether long or short. A literal day is an age — a short one. Since "day" is limited and qualified as literal in Gen. 1:3-31, we're right to believe literal days are meant. This is the only theory that harmonizes with all the facts and scriptures on the subject.

### II. The Work of the First Day—Light Restored (Gen. 1:3-5)

The work of day one was nothing more or less than the restoration of light and the division of light and darkness — bringing back day and night on earth as they had been when Lucifer ruled, before the curse of total darkness in Gen. 1:2 and Jer. 4:23-26.

This happened through the brooding of the Spirit of God over the waters covering the earth and by God's direct command: "Let there be [become] light: and there was [became] light" (Gen. 1:3-5).

The word "let" is used the same way we'd say, "Turn on the light." Both light and darkness already existed — they were created originally with the heavens and earth. The word "let" never denotes creation but rather *permission* (as in Gen. 1:3, 6, 9, 11, 14, 15, 20, 22, 24, 26) or *determination* (as in Gen. 11:3, 4, 7).

This further indicates that Gen. 1:2 describes an act of judgment, and here God is permitting that judgment to cease, allowing the sun, moon, and stars to shine again on the darkened planet according to their original creative purpose. Neither here nor in Gen. 1:14-19 is an original creative act implied. The sun, moon, and stars were created in the beginning with the heavens — before the earth, as we've seen.

The light of days 1, 2, and 3 came from the same source it has come from every day since. The work of day 4 is the permanent regulation of the solar system in connection with the restored earth that emerged on day 3. God called the light "day" and the darkness "night" — names for regular periods of light and darkness ever since.

God began His restoration work on the morning of the first day. When He said, "Let there be light," the first day began. It was day (or light) for a period until evening, when the first period of darkness after restoration began. This continued until the next morning — the second period of light.

Thus, the first day and night, the first period of light and darkness since the curse on earth, were divided and distinctly marked off by evening and morning — the first day. It takes evening and morning, a period of light and a period of darkness, to make one day.

### III. The Work of the Second Day—Firmament Restored (Gen. 1:6-8)

The work of day two was nothing more or less than the restoration of the firmament — the clouds — to hold the waters again that had fallen on the earth to cause the first flood, destroying the first social system (Gen. 1:2).

These waters had been in the firmament and poured out as rain to water the earth throughout Lucifer's kingdom. When Satan fell, he said, "I will ascend above the heights of the clouds" (Isa. 14:12-14). The clouds were originally created to hold moisture to water the earth (Job 38:4-9, 25-30; Ps. 104:2-3, 13-14).

So the firmament was created in the beginning along with the heavens and earth, but on day 2 it was restored to its original purpose. This was done by *making*, not creating, the clouds. God put part of the waters back in the clouds and named the firmament "heaven."

The firmament is called clouds in Scripture (Job 26:8; 38:9; Ps. 77:17; Prov. 8:28). Heaven as a planet and the heavens as clouds are distinguished (Judg. 5:4; Ps. 147:8; Isa. 14:12-14; Dan. 7:13; Mt. 24:29-31).

The second day's work began on the morning of the second day (the second period of light), continued to the second period of darkness (night), and went through the night until the third morning (the third period of light). Thus, the evening ending the second period of light and the morning ending the second period of darkness were the second day.

### IV. The Work of the Third Day—Earth and Vegetation Restored (Gen. 1:9-13)

The work of day three was nothing more or less than the restoration of the earth from its water baptism (which had lasted throughout chaos and through the first two days) and the restoration of vegetation that had grown on the earth when Lucifer ruled before Gen. 1:2.

As we saw in Lesson Seven, Point VI, there were fruitful places on the earth, but they had become a waste because of judgment when Lucifer rebelled. This work was accomplished by God's direct command and the creative power of the Holy Spirit.

At God's rebuke, the waters that hadn't been put in the clouds on day 2 fled and hastened away into the low places of the earth. God then set bounds around the waters to keep them from covering the earth (Ps. 104:5-9).

God called the dry land "earth." If dry land is earth, then we can read Gen. 1:1 as: "In the beginning God created the heaven, and the dry land." The dry land had become wet land by the flood of Gen. 1:2, and now dry land was being restored for the habitation of land animals and man.

Neither the waters nor the earth were created on day three any more than the planets were created on day four, or the light and darkness and firmament on days one and two. All the heavens and earth were created in the beginning before Lucifer ruled. All the work of day 3 is *re-constructive*, not creative.

When dry land appeared, vegetation could be planted and grow, since the sun was already shining — it had been for the first two days, as well as this day and all days since.

The purpose of vegetation was to sustain life on the restored earth. In all likelihood, the life germ in seeds was destroyed during the chaotic period when the earth was waterlogged for an indefinite time — no doubt longer than Noah's flood, which didn't destroy vegetation.

Gen. 2:5 plainly states that all vegetation on the restored earth was absolutely new: "And every plant of the field before it was in the earth, and every herb of the field before it grew." This proves all vegetation was a new work.

God began the work on the morning of day three (when the second period of darkness ended) and worked throughout the third day (period of light) until evening (the third period of darkness). He worked through the night until the morning of day four. Thus, the evening ending the third period of light and the morning ending the third period of darkness were the third day.

### V. The Work of the Fourth Day—Solar Regulation Restored (Gen. 1:14-19)

The work of day four was nothing more or less than the restoration of the solar system in connection with the restored earth, which work had been completed the day before.

Permanent regulation of the solar system to give light on earth and regulate times and seasons couldn't be done until the earth was restored. In this passage we learn where the light of the first three days came from and how light and darkness were divided and ruled on those days and all days since. Here God fixed the solar system in connection with the earth as it was before chaos.

From this day on, the sun, moon, and stars were commanded to govern seasons on the newly restored earth. These lights had given light on the earth and regulated times and seasons throughout Lucifer's rule, but in the curse on the first social system they had been withheld from shining on the cursed earth (Gen. 1:2; Jer. 4:23-26). Here God commanded them to renew their original creative purpose — to sustain life on earth and regulate times, seasons, day, and night forever.

Some translations read, "God *had* made two great luminaries . . . and God *had* fixed them," proving God had done this work of actually creating them in the past. When? In the beginning, before Lucifer ruled the earth and when it was first created.

God began this work on the morning of day four (period of light) and worked until evening (the fourth period of darkness). He then worked all night until the morning of day five. Thus, the evening ending the fourth period of light and the morning ending the fourth period of darkness were the fourth day.

### VI. The Work of the Fifth Day—Fish and Fowl Restored (Gen. 1:20-23)

The work of day five was nothing more or less than the creation and formation of new sea animals and fowl to fly in the clouds. Here we have the second creative act of God mentioned in the Creative Ages — the first being the original creation of the heavens, earth, and all things in the beginning (Gen. 1:1).

On day 5, God creates and makes fish and fowl. Between the two creations (the dateless past and day five), God merely restores day and night, firmament, earth and vegetation, and solar regulation. The new vegetation of day three is also a creation of God, but all the rest is merely restoring already-created things as they were before in Lucifer's kingdom.

God first fixed the realms where fish and fowl were to live, then created and made them to reproduce their own kind and live according to certain laws. The bodies of all fish and fowl were formed out of the dust of the ground, and life was created in them (Gen. 2:19).

The Hebrew word for "bring forth" is *shawrats*, meaning to wriggle, swarm, abound, breed, increase, and move. Its meaning is seen in Gen. 1:20; 9:7; Exod. 8:3.

Things of the animal and plant worlds multiply abundantly. Think of it like this: under the present curse, a watermelon seed reproduces a product 200,000 times its own weight, and each melon produces hundreds of seeds, each capable of producing such a product! Each grain reproduces many of its own kind. Each fruit tree produces hundreds of seed-bearing fruits, each capable of producing another tree with thousands more.

Loggerhead turtles lay 1,000 eggs at a time, and a cod fish produces 10 million eggs annually. Other organisms multiply abundantly, each after its own kind as God commanded. There's an abundant supply of anything man wants — all he has to do is follow certain laws, and all things are his.

The word for "life" is *nephesh*, meaning soul or feelings, emotions, appetites, and desires, as we saw in Lesson Six, Point IX, 2, (1). All animals have souls (feelings and appetites), but their souls are not immortal and created in the likeness of God's soul, as is the soul of man.

Every creature was to bring forth "after his kind" — this expression states the law of reproduction for all things. It occurs ten times in Gen. 1:11, 12, 21, 24-25. The same law was still in force 1,656 years later after the flood (Gen. 8:19). It's still in force today, and different species cannot be crossed to reproduce other than their own kind, as is well known. Man and monkey and other animals are so far apart that they never were related.

God began this work on the morning of day five (period of light) and worked all day until evening (the fifth period of darkness). He then worked all night until the sixth period of light. Thus, the evening ending the fifth period of light and the morning ending the fifth period of darkness were the fifth day.

### VII. The Work of the Sixth Day—Land Animals and Man Restored (Gen. 1:24-31)

The work of day six was nothing more or less than the creation and formation of new land animals and man to take the place of the animals and inhabitants of the first social system over which Lucifer ruled, the naming of all things by Adam, and the giving of commands by God to the new creation. The bodies of man and all animals were formed out of the dust, and life was created (Gen. 2:7, 19).

The phrase "Let us" states the divine purpose, but the divine act isn't described until Gen. 2:5-25. In other words, Gen. 1 states *what* God did and some things about *how* He created and made all new things, while Gen. 2 goes into more detail about *how* He formed and created man and animals and planted a garden. Gen. 2 explains more fully the work of days 3, 5, and 6.

The image and likeness of God referred to in Gen. 1:26 ("Let us make man in our image and likeness") was already discussed in Lesson Four, Point II, 2-3.

God began His work on day six in the morning (sixth period of light) and worked through the day to evening (sixth period of darkness). He worked through the night until the seventh morning when He ended His work. Thus, the evening ending the sixth period of light and the morning ending the sixth period of darkness were the sixth day.

### VIII. The Seventh Day of Rest (Gen. 2:1-4)

**(End of the Creative Ages—Beginning of the Seven Dispensations for Men)**

The seventh day was one of rest for God — not that He needed rest, but because His work of restoring the earth and its inhabitants was finished. He had worked constantly six days and six nights and was now done.

"Thus the heavens and the earth were finished, and all the host of them [all the inhabitants and creatures of all kinds in the heavens and the earth]. And on the seventh day God ended his work . . . which God created and made. These are the generations [family history or productions] of the heavens and of the earth when they were created, in the day [period] that the Lord God made the earth and the heavens" (Gen. 2:1-4).

The order of all the Creative Ages (as discussed in Lesson One, Point IV, 1, (4)) is as follows:

> 1. The original creation and perfection of the heavens and the Earth and all things therein as when Lucifer ruled before the six days (Gen. 1:1).

> 2. The Earth part of the creation made chaos and imperfect and all life destroyed in the Earth, because of Lucifer's rebellion (Gen. 1:2).

> 3. The restoration of the Earth to perfection and the creation of new life to take the place of that which was destroyed (Gen. 1:3–2:25).

### IX. Why the Earth Was Restored to a Habitable State

The earth was restored because God had originally intended it to be inhabited: "For thus saith the Lord that created the heavens; God himself that formed the earth and made it; he hath established it, he created it not in vain [Hebrew, *tohu*, a waste or desolation — so if it was desolate in Gen. 1:2, it later became that way], he formed it to be inhabited" (Isa. 45:18).

The fall of Lucifer and the total rebellion of all inhabitants of the first social system under Satan didn't discourage God or cause Him to blot out the earth. He had started with a purpose — that the earth should be inhabited with free moral agents to whom He could reveal Himself and show the riches of His grace, and who would serve Him by free choice. He continued that original purpose by making a new creation when the old one had to be destroyed.

The rebellion of the new creation made in the six days didn't discourage God or cause Him to want to blot it out and start over. He had purposed that as truly as He lived, "the earth shall be full of the glory of the Lord" (Num. 14:21), and this purpose will yet be realized in the New Heavens and New Earth of the future.

God didn't create the earth to turn it over to rebels. He will yet see to it that only the meek and righteous inherit the earth (Mt. 5:5; Ps. 37:9-11, 29). So God restored the earth from chaos and ruin to replenish it with free moral agents, ensuring His original purpose will finally be realized. (See Reason for God's Dispensational Dealings, Lesson One, Point VIII.)

### X. God's Plan for the New Social System on the Earth

God's plan for the new social system was the same as for the first — that all free wills consecrate themselves to the highest good of being and of the universe.

This means free moral agents must consecrate themselves to the same end God is consecrated to. They must submit wholeheartedly to what's best for all society. They must recognize God as the Supreme Moral Governor of the universe. Every thought, word, and deed should be for the betterment of society, including their own good. They must live dependent upon God for needed grace for body, soul, and spirit, and appreciate life and all it holds.

God planned to make the new creation an example to all angelic powers of His manifold wisdom concerning His eternal plan (Eph. 3:10-11; 1 Cor. 4:9). He planned to manifest His grace and goodness to man and make him ruler of all the earth, including the sun, moon, and stars (Ps. 8:3-7).

He planned that man should be faithful to his responsibility if he so desired and be greatly rewarded, continuing to rule over all. If man failed, he would be punished according to the law. God also planned redemption for man should he fall, as we saw in Lesson Seven, Points VII, IX and X, and as we'll see more fully in future lessons.

---

## Earth's Second Sinless Career (Gen. 2:15-25)

### ADAM THE NEW RULER OF THE EARTH

God created Adam on day six of Gen. 1 to take Lucifer's place as earth's ruler. He was the earth's second worldwide dictator and could have remained so if he hadn't sinned.

Lucifer, the first worldwide dictator, had sinned and failed by not becoming reconciled to God. Adam also failed, and it's uncertain whether he became reconciled to God. Looking at the biblical record, it seems he never did.

Adam isn't mentioned in the list of faith heroes in Heb. 11. He's not mentioned once as a righteous man in either Testament — which is striking when you consider others are mentioned several times. From the Antediluvian period, only Abel, Enoch, and Noah are mentioned as righteous ones. We know from Gen. 6:3 that Adam wasn't reconciled to God when he was 810 years old, as we'll see in Lesson Eleven.

Jesus Christ will be the third and final worldwide dictator, and He will eternally prove true to God and head God's government forever, as we'll see in later lessons.

Adam had the greatest responsibility of any man who has ever lived, besides Christ. He was given dominion over all creation to rule for God and be subject to God forever. He was charged to keep out all enemies and resist all claims of spirit-beings concerning dominion of the earth.

God knew Lucifer and the fallen spirits were still around and would contest man's claim of dominion. God permitted this to test the new free moral governor of the earth, as we saw in Lesson Six, Point X, 9.

Adam's responsibility included bringing his own kind into the world and training them to respect God, continue in righteousness and true holiness, reject all temptation, and overcome all enemies.

The charge to Adam and Eve is stated in Gen. 1:26-29 and 2:15-17:

"And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness: and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth. So God created man in his own image, in the image of God created he him; male and female created he them. And God blessed them, and God said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth, and subdue it: and have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth. And God said, Behold, I have given you every herb [grain bearing plant and vegetation] . . . and every tree, in the which is the fruit of a tree yielding seed; to you it shall be for meat . . . And the Lord God took the man, and put him into the garden of Eden to dress it and to keep it [protect, preserve it, as in Gen. 3:24; 17:9-10; 18:19]. And the Lord God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die."

In Ps. 8:3-9 we read that man was to have dominion over "thy heavens, the work of thy fingers, the moon, and the stars, which thou hast ordained . . . For thou hast made him a little lower than the angels, and hast crowned him with glory and honour. Thou madest him to have dominion over the works of thy hands; thou hast put all things under his feet."

From these and other passages, we learn God's plan was for man to have dominion over everything. What a tremendous responsibility! What an incredible position!

Man was fully capable of ruling over all things, possessing super-intelligence and the ability to name everything in creation. It's similar to how a brilliant CEO might be given complete authority over a vast corporation — except Adam's "corporation" was the entire earth and potentially the planets beyond! He was likely meant to have access to the planets and rule them for God, but he sinned and came "short of the glory of God" (Rom. 3:23).

In the final restitution of all things, man will again have dominion and help God administer the affairs of the universe (Rev. 1:5-6; 2:26-29; 3:21; 5:8-10; 22:4-5; 1 Cor. 6:2-3; 2 Tim. 2:12). This future dominion includes more than planet Earth, as Rom. 8:17-18 makes clear: "and if children, then heirs, heirs of God, and joint-heirs with Christ." Everything God owns will become the inheritance of the saints, and they shall reign forever and ever (Rev. 1:5-6; 2:27; 5:9-10; 22:4-5; Dan. 7:13-14, 18, 27).

If people would only realize the future that could be theirs by simply submitting to God and laying down their arms of rebellion, there would be few, if any, who wouldn't wholeheartedly surrender to God and conform to His eternal will.

But the devil keeps man occupied with trifling and temporary things, making him think there may not be a future. This causes man to neglect God and life's essentials, cutting him off from his rightful inheritance in Christ. If someone chooses to have nothing to do with God and His plan, then God has planned to separate that person from society and carry on His eternal purpose with those who will choose to be part of the plan.

---

## Questions on Lesson Eight

- What is the subject of Lesson Eight?

- When was the time of the re-creation of the Earth and all things therein?

- Prove fully from Scripture that the six days were literal days and not periods of 1,000 or more years each.

- Prove from the standpoint of common sense and reason that they were literal days.

- Prove from the Scripture that the heavens were created before the Earth.

- How does this prove that the light of the first three days came from the planets?

- How does this prove that there was chaos on Earth in Gen. 1:2?

- Explain fully the work of the first day.

- Does day 1 include the creation of the heavens, the Earth, the darkness, and the waters that covered the Earth? Why?

- When did God begin His work? Did He begin it in total darkness or in light?

- When did day and night begin in the restored creation?

- Explain the word "let." How does it indicate that the sun was in existence?

- Prove from Scripture that there will be day and night forever.

- Explain fully the work of the second day.

- Had there been a firmament before this time? Prove.

- Is Heaven as a planet and the heavens as clouds distinguished in Scripture?

- Explain fully the work of the third day.

- Was the Earth originally created on day 3 or merely restored?

- Prove from Scripture that there had been vegetation on Earth before this.

- What is the name for dry land? When did dry land become flooded? Why?

- Did God originally create the Earth dry? Prove.

- What was the purpose of God in planting vegetation on Earth again?

- Prove from Scripture that no vegetation remained from the pre-Adamite period.

- Explain fully the work of the fourth day.

- Did God create the sun, moon, and stars in day 4 or merely regulate them?

- Did the planets regulate times and seasons on Earth before Adam?

- Explain fully the work of the fifth day.

- Did God make a place for each and every creature before creating them?

- How fast do some creatures multiply?

- What is the law of reproduction given by God? Is it still in force?

- Explain fully the work of the sixth day.

- Why did God create land animals, fish, fowls, and man?

- Were there two creations of man—one in Gen. 1 and another in Gen. 2?

- What is the purpose of the two records of man's creation?

- When did the Creative Ages end?

- When did the seven dispensations for man begin?

- What happened on day 7? Did God have to rest because He was tired?

- Give the three main points on the Creative Ages.

- Why was the Earth restored to a habitable state?

- What was God

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Innocence
lessons.content:
# The Dispensation of Innocence
(Gen. 2:15–3:21)

---

## PART II: GOD'S HISTORICAL DEALINGS WITH MAN
(LESSONS 9–18)

---

## THE ANTEDILUVIAN AGE (Gen. 1:3–8:14)

The Antediluvian Age covers the period from when God re-created the Earth and made man, all the way to the flood of Noah. Within this age, we find two dispensations: **Innocence** and **Conscience**.

As we study each dispensation, there are nine key points you'll want to understand if you're going to really grasp what God was doing in that period. Here they are:

- The definition of the name of the dispensation.
- The length of the dispensation.
- The favorable beginning for man in each age.
- The test for man in each period.
- The purpose of God in each test for man.
- The means of God in accomplishing His purpose.
- The failure of man to meet the test.
- The judgment of God upon man because of his failure.
- God's provision of redemption for man at the end of each age.

Once you learn these nine points for each of the seven dispensations, God's plan for humanity becomes remarkably simple to understand. Think of it like learning the rules of a board game—once you know the structure, every move makes sense.

If you'll study these periods carefully and concentrate on how all the parts connect to God's whole purpose, you'll find that every important question gets answered. The mysteries that once seemed so difficult will clear up, and the rest will become simple to solve as you study with these facts in mind.

You'll be able to see things from God's standpoint—what He had in mind before He even began bringing His plan to pass, as revealed in the Scriptures.

---

## I. Definition of Innocence
