The word "innocence" is pretty straightforward. It simply means being without any consciousness of evil—harmlessness, freedom from crime, guilt, or sin. An innocent person is one who hasn't violated any law, who is guiltless, sinless, pure, upright, and harmless.

When we apply "innocence" to a period of time or dispensation, we're describing an age of sinlessness on the part of the person responsible to rule for God. Adam was perfectly innocent when God placed him in the Garden of Eden.

He wasn't even self-conscious enough to be ashamed of his nakedness. "They were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed" (Gen. 2:25). It was only after sin entered that they became self-conscious: "The eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked" (Gen. 3:7).

When a person exists in this state, we could say they're God-conscious—perfectly innocent of all wrongdoing. In the Fall, man lost this God-consciousness and gained self-consciousness instead. Conscience was awakened, and both man and woman became sinners, knowing by experience both good and evil.

We call this the Dispensation of Innocence because man was tested during this first probationary period while he was still in a state of innocence. He was tested regarding right and wrong—whether he would freely exercise his will while still sinless and free from all evil.

---

## II. The Length of the Dispensation of Innocence

We don't know exactly how long the Dispensation of Innocence lasted. But judging from Satan's character and how he's dealt with people throughout history, we can reasonably conclude that he didn't let God's work go very long before trying to defeat it.

Satan was jealous of Adam and wouldn't have been satisfied with a man ruling in his place as governor of the earth. He no doubt got busy immediately to cause man's fall and regain dominion of the restored earth through man.

On the chart, you'll notice we've marked this dispensation as six days followed by a question mark. We don't know for certain, but putting it at about one week probably isn't far off. Here's why:

> 1. Satan doesn't leave people alone for very long today, unless he already controls them and there's no fight to be had.

> 2. The record of man's creation and fall flows continuously without any break or time gap between them, suggesting they followed each other in close succession.

> 3. We only have record of God resting on one sabbath day (Gen. 2:1-3).

> 4. The next statement about God is that He was walking in the Garden in the cool of the day (Gen. 3:8). This had to be after the sabbath of rest, since God rested on that day.

> 5. Neither Adam nor Eve had yet visited or eaten from the Tree of Life. When they sinned, they were driven out of the Garden and kept from this tree so they wouldn't eat of it and live forever (Gen. 3:22-24).

> 6. Both the Tree of Life and the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil were evidently shown to man so he could distinguish them from all other trees and know which was forbidden. The fact that they went first to the Tree of Knowledge suggests their curiosity was aroused—and when curiosity gets stirred up, people generally don't wait long to satisfy it.

> 7. The serpent was near the forbidden tree when man came to satisfy his curiosity—curiosity that was likely aroused when God gave the commandment not to eat of it. That happened on the day of man's creation when he was placed in the Garden. Man could have sinned the very next day while God was resting, since there's no command for man to rest and no record that he did rest that day. It would have been ridiculous to ask man to rest before he'd worked even one day. His first day of work was the day he was created—he named the animals, which was a good day's work for anyone! If God had taught him to rest on the seventh day after six days' work, Adam's first sabbath would have been on the fourth day of the second week, not the seventh day.

> 8. Adam fell before he knew his wife and had children, because the first children born were just as sinful as children born today—death has passed upon all men, for all have sinned (Rom. 5:12-21). This proves he sinned within just a few days. He was married to the woman on Day 6 of Earth's restoration, for he said that day, "This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh: she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man. Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother, and shall cleave unto his wife: and they shall be one flesh" (Gen. 2:21-25). Judging that he was a normal man, he wouldn't have waited indefinitely to love his wife and carry out his creative purpose. Few men, if any, wait a week to fulfill the purpose of marriage—and we can believe Adam was no exception. So we conclude he sinned before Eve conceived, and man wasn't in a state of innocence very long before eating the forbidden fruit.

Now, we need to add something important here: the forbidden fruit was *not* intercourse with his wife, as some who rebel against truth teach. He was created for that very purpose—otherwise he could never multiply and replenish the Earth as God commanded on the first day of his existence (Gen. 1:26-28). He was commanded to have children but forbidden to eat of the tree. These couldn't have been the same act.

The forbidden fruit was simply a natural fruit that grew on a tree, as Scripture plainly states: "The Lord God planted a garden eastward in Eden . . . And out of the ground made the Lord God to grow every tree that is pleasant to the sight, and good for food; the tree of life in the midst of the garden, and the tree of knowledge of good and evil . . . And the Lord God commanded the man, saying, Of every tree in the garden thou mayest freely eat: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die . . . The woman saw that the tree was good for food . . . she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also to her husband with her; and he did eat" (Gen. 2:9, 16-17; 3:1-6, 11-12).

It's pure nonsense to claim that such simple language about eating fruit from a tree was really about sexual intercourse. The words "food" and "eat" appear 14 times in Gen. 2-3, proving that natural food and real eating caused the Fall. The Tree of Knowledge is listed among many other trees in the Garden—it's just as easy to prove that eating from *every* tree was sexual intercourse as to prove it for just this one tree.

Right from the start of our Bible study, we must learn to reject the foolish theories of men and believe what is plainly written. This is the only way to gain a working knowledge of the Bible. If we're going to question and throw out what's written, we might as well put the Bible away forever. We'll receive less judgment in the end than if we change everything it says.

Taking the Bible literally enables us to get a simple knowledge of the truth about what caused man to fall and when. There's no mystery attached to the Fall, and there can be no misunderstanding if we believe what God's Word actually says.

The Fall could have been caused by doing *anything* God commanded man not to do. It wasn't the nature of the fruit that caused sin—it was disobedience to God that constituted the sin. This is plainly stated in Rom. 5:12-21: "For if by one man's offense . . . by one man's disobedience many were made sinners, so by the obedience of one shall many be made righteous."

Again, "where no law is, there is no transgression," and "sin is the transgression of the law" (Rom. 4:15; 1 Jn. 3:4). If man had been told not to do any one of a thousand different acts, breaking any one commandment would have constituted the sin that caused the Fall and brought the death penalty.

---

## III. The Favorable Beginning of Man in Innocence (Gen. 1:26-30; 2:8-24)

Man and woman, fresh from the Creator's hand, had everything going for them:

- Physical, spiritual, and eternal life
- Communion and fellowship with God and all creatures in the new creation
- Dominion over that creation
- The revealed will of God, His law, and knowledge of both penalties and rewards
- God-consciousness, or innocence
- A natural disposition of love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, and self-control
- The power to make free choices and rule his dominion while defending it from all enemies
- A right to attain an even higher glory if he proved true in his test

Man was a miniature of God in his soul and spirit faculties, with a physical body made in the image and likeness of God. He was uncorrupt, free from prejudices, sinful lusts, and all evil. He existed in a highly enlightened state with a glorious spiritual, moral, and mental makeup. He could enjoy the pleasures of that state to the full.

He had power over Satan and all fallen spirits loose in the Earth. He could have resisted them and remained true to God if he had wanted to. His animal and sensitive natures were free to enjoy themselves in everything except the one forbidden tree. Nothing else he could have done would have been sin at that time.

As time went on, God would no doubt have revealed more of His moral law and government. But at that moment, there was only one command to obey.

Man's body was perfect. His senses were quick and lively, able to perform their various operations with vigor and delight. He enjoyed nature in all its original beauty, purity, and harmony—revealing the goodness of the Creator.

His soul passions, appetites, feelings, and desires, along with his spirit-faculties, were in perfect union with God and were exercised with dignity over the new Earth and everything in it.

He enjoyed the fullness of natural, spiritual, and eternal life and could have remained in that condition simply by obeying one commandment. The law of his nature contained those moral principles concerning good and evil that measured his duty to God, to the universe, and to himself and his posterity. He understood fully the law, its penalties, and his great responsibility.

God placed in man's reasonable spirit and sensitive soul the principle and power of obedience, and He made a covenant with man on the condition of obedience. Man was perfectly holy, but in a changeable state. He was sinless, but capable of sin. He was eternal, but placed on probation. He was invested with power to prevent his falling, yet lived under the possibility of it. He was complete in his being, but receptive to sinful impressions.

Man had a wonderful helper in his wife, created from one of his ribs. Just as it's true today, a woman can either make or break a man.

Of woman's creation, Matthew Henry beautifully says, "Not out of man's head to be topped by him, nor out of his feet to be trampled on by him, but out of his side to be equal with him; under his arm to be protected by him, and from near his heart to be beloved by him."

This expresses that man and woman were meant to be partners in life. If both had cooperated with God, the curse never would have come. There would have been no sin, no sickness, no death, no pain, no failure, no unhappiness in all creation—and no cancellation of eternal life.

Before the Fall, man wasn't supposed to be lazy. God put him in the Garden to dress it and keep it (preserve it from enemies, as expressed in Gen. 3:24; 17:9-10; 18:19). He was to eat of the wonderful fruits, nuts, grains, and vegetables growing in the restored Earth. He was to reproduce his own kind and keep them obedient to God and in harmony with all creation.

He was free to use the whole creation for the best good of all concerned. He was to consecrate himself to the best and highest good of being and of the universe, and remain a free and eternal subject of the Moral Governor of all.

These and other favorable conditions made it entirely and easily possible for man to remain true to his trust and rule the Earth for God forever.

---

## IV. The Test—Man on Probation (Gen. 2:16-17)

Man was created as a free moral agent, so he needed to be tested to see whether he would remain true to God before being placed in the eternal responsibility God had in mind for him. (See Lesson One, Point VIII, for a full discussion of why man had to be tested.)

Adam had the power to choose for himself whether to obey or disobey God. He simply needed to be told what God's will was. God made known His will, the rewards of obedience, and the penalty for disobedience—so there was no excuse for man to choose evil and cooperate with the enemy of both God and man.

Being created with power to prevent his own Fall and with free choice in the matter, man was and still is held entirely responsible to God for his choice and its results.

Adam was placed on probation—a trial period. Think of it like a new employee's probationary period at work, except the stakes were infinitely higher. If he had proved true, he would have attained a higher glory than before (Rom. 3:23) and could have lived forever enjoying the fruits of obedience.

The penalty for sin was eternal death, so naturally the reward for obedience was life—eternal life. He would have lived forever, physically as well as spiritually.

If you can imagine how man would have continued forever physically, multiplying and living a natural existence, you can also imagine how this will be true in the New Earth. When the curse is removed and man is again placed in the sinless state he was in before the Fall, there will be eternal generations of natural people on Earth, as we've proven in Supplement Two, Question 2.

Even after the Fall, man could have lived forever if he had eaten of the Tree of Life. The reason God drove him out of the Garden and placed cherubim to guard the Tree of Life was "lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever" (Gen. 2:22-24).

If sinful man could have lived forever physically by eating of the Tree of Life, then certainly natural, redeemed man in the New Earth will also be able to eat of this tree and live forever (Rev. 21:1-7; 22:1-5).

Here's an important point: the fact that man would have lived forever physically even after sinning—if he had eaten of the Tree of Life—proves that the penalty for sin wasn't merely physical death. It was *eternal* death. No fruit from the Tree of Life could have cancelled that eternal death penalty.

What the tree *would* have done is make man incapable of dying physically—and therefore incapable of being redeemed from death, Hell, and the grave so he could become a higher being than he ever could be in his natural fallen state.

God really wanted man to live forever physically, but not in spiritual and eternal death—not separated from Him by sin. So it was in God's plan to redeem man spiritually and eternally *before* placing him back on the Earth to live forever physically.

The specific test for man in the Dispensation of Innocence was this: he was not to eat of the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. Failure would result in losing his dominion and fellowship with God.

It would seem that sinless, perfect man could have obeyed one simple law—especially when sinful, imperfect people today are obligated to keep many laws! It would seem man could have kept himself true to God, when today redeemed man lives in righteousness and true holiness according to the gospel (Rom. 6:1-23; 8:1-13; 12:1-2; Gal. 5:16-26; Eph. 4:22-24; Titus 2:11-13; 3:5-7; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-4, 18).

---

## V. The Purpose of God in This Dispensation

God's purpose in testing man while in a state of innocence was to see whether or not man would obey Him—so He could trust him with an eternal responsibility connected to the Earth and God's universal kingdom.

God had watched Lucifer, the first ruler of the Earth, become lifted up in pride and rebel against His sovereignty. So it was only natural for God to test the new ruler of the Earth to see if he would exalt himself as Satan did (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17; 1 Tim. 3:6).

God planned that if man fell, he should do it *before* eating of the Tree of Life—so he wouldn't have to live forever in a sinful state. That's why God tested man soon after creation instead of later in life.

God further intended that if man fell, he should do it *before* having offspring. That way his children could be born on the same level with himself, allowing God to have mercy upon all alike.

This kind of plan made all people equally dependent upon God for needed grace for body, soul, and spirit. It gave every person the same opportunity to be saved or lost as they freely chose.

God wanted to demonstrate to man that He wasn't the tyrant Lucifer and other fallen spirits accused Him of being. As it stands now in God's plan for man, God can speedily and eternally redeem the race and the Earth to perfection—just as it was before the Fall—and be assured His plan for man throughout all eternity will never be marred again.

---

## VI. The Means of God in Accomplishing this Purpose

The restriction from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil and the temptations offered by the devil—these were the means God used in testing man's ambitions to become like God (Gen. 3:1-6).

Becoming like God had been Lucifer's main desire. He had said in his heart, "I will ascend into heaven, I will exalt my throne above the stars of God . . . I will be like the most High" (Isa. 14:12-14).

Lucifer knew that the way to become *unlike* God was to exalt self. He also knew that if he could appeal to man to misuse his faculty of humility—which is the glory of the creature and his native makeup—he could cause God to curse man.

Without the tempter, the first man might have remained true to God. But he was attacked by the most subtle of spirit-beings, who used the most subtle of Adam's creatures to cause man to fall and corrupt himself along with all his posterity (Rom. 5:12-21), as we'll study in the next point.

---

## Earth's Second Sinful Career (Gen. 3:1—Rev. 20:15)

## VII. The Failure of Man—The Fall

In Gen. 3, Rom. 5:12-21, 1 Tim. 2:13-14, and other passages, we have a simple record of man's fall—including what caused it. Here's something vital to understand: without a clear faith in the fall of man, there can be no clear faith in the redemption of man.

Those who preach and teach that man didn't really fall—or that if he did, he "fell upward"—that man is simply a victim of environment and incapable of sin—such beliefs can never lead souls to redemption from sin through Jesus Christ. They destroy the very foundation of Scripture and of man's eternal life and hope.

You must believe in the Fall to be saved. Jesus Christ didn't come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance (Mt. 9:13). If man never had a fall, he could never be redeemed. Likewise, if a person doesn't believe they're a sinner, they can't be saved.

Repentance is required for salvation (Mk. 1:4; 6:12; Lk. 13:1-10; 24:47; Acts 2:38; 3:19; 17:30; 1 Jn. 1:7-9). This is the chief fundamental of the Gospel of Christ, which is the power of God unto salvation to all who believe (Rom. 1:16; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; Rev. 1:5-6; Eph. 1:7).

In Gen. 3 we have the historical record of man's fall. If anyone denies this truth, they're incapable of true faith in any other part of the Bible, because all Scripture centers around man—his creation, Fall, and redemption through Jesus Christ.

The cause of the Fall is stated clearly: breaking God's commandment by eating of the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. God had said, "Of every tree of the garden thou mayest freely eat: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat of it: for in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die" (Gen. 2:16-17).

The whole temptation centered around this tree and its fruit. Why Adam and Eve happened to go near the forbidden tree isn't stated, so we have to believe they were curious about why God wouldn't permit them to eat from this one tree when all the others were available. At any rate, they were at this tree together, as is clear from the statement: "she took of the fruit thereof, and did eat, and gave also unto her husband with her; and he did eat. And the eyes of them both were opened, and they knew that they were naked" (Gen. 3:6-7).

It's true that the serpent talked with the woman, but that doesn't prove the man wasn't present. There's no statement that Adam was absent, so we naturally conclude he was there. Here are the main facts of the fall of man:

### 1. DOUBT CONCERNING GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:1)

The serpent raised a doubt—a question about whether God really would permit man to eat of every tree in the Garden. Thus, Satan's first utterance in Scripture shows opposition to the Word of God.

Satan's opposition to the Bible accounts for so many false theories about God's Word today. Any theory that teaches God doesn't mean what He says—that adds to, misinterprets, takes from, or changes what Scripture plainly says—is satanic.

All theories claiming the Bible is hard to understand, or that it's a mystery, or that it means whatever you interpret it to mean, or that one man's interpretation is as good as another, or that it must be changed before it can be understood—these are all satanic.

### 2. ADDITION TO AND MISQUOTING GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:2-3)

The woman answered the serpent's doubt by adding to God's Word the statement "neither shall ye touch it"—which is a misquotation of Gen. 2:16-17. God didn't say touching the tree would bring death, only eating of it.

### 3. CONTRADICTION OF GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:4)

Next, the serpent directly contradicted God's Word from Gen. 2:16-17. God said, "thou shalt surely die," and the devil said through the serpent, "ye shall not surely die."

Both statements can't be true. You must choose to believe one or the other.

Those who believe God today believe that people will die if they sin. Those who believe Satan believe that people won't die if they sin. This is a definite contrast requiring definite decisions today—just as it required a definite stand from Adam and Eve.

God still says, "the soul that sinneth, it shall die" (Ezek. 18:4); "If ye live after the flesh, ye shall die" (Rom. 8:1-13); "he that soweth to the flesh shall of the flesh reap corruption" (Gal. 6:7-8); "the end of those things is death . . . For the wages of sin is death" (Rom. 6:14-23); "If any man defile the temple of God, him shall God destroy" (1 Cor. 3:16-17); "they which do such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God" (1 Cor. 6:9-11; Gal. 5:19-21); and "If we deny him, He also will deny us" (2 Tim. 2:12).

If you'll honestly read these passages, you'll see they were spoken to Christians (except Ezek. 18:4, which applies to all men). Just as Gen. 2:17 applied to everyone, these passages do too—God cannot be a respecter of persons, allowing one to die for sin but not another (Jas. 2:9).

### 4. MISINTERPRETATION OF GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:5)

Satan's second statement to the woman through the serpent became the basis of many false doctrines: "Ye shall be as gods."

Today this sounds like: "We all have the God-power in us," or "we are all a part of God," and all we need to do is look to the God within us and use the divine power of which we're a part.

### 5. TEMPTATION TO TRANSGRESS GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:6)

Satan's appeal that man would become like God was powerful. This desire in itself isn't sin—it's actually a high ideal! Every Christian should strive to be like God.

But there are two ways to pursue this: the divine way Christ demonstrated, and the selfish, sinful way Lucifer, Adam, and Eve demonstrated. Seeking to become like God through self-gratification and rebellion makes you *unlike* God. But becoming like God through self-emptying and humiliation, as Christ did, is the true path—as we'll see in Lesson Twenty-one.

When Eve "saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was pleasant to the eyes, and a tree to be desired to make one wise," she was tempted to sin.

Paul tells us in 1 Tim. 2:13-15 that Adam was not deceived, but the woman, being deceived, was in the transgression. In 2 Cor. 11:3 we read of Eve being beguiled by the serpent.

This appeal consists of three main lines of temptation—the only three with which we ever have to deal. John calls them "the lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life" (1 Jn. 2:15-17). These are the same three temptations Christ faced and overcame in the wilderness (Mt. 4:1-11). If you overcome these three, you're an overcomer of Satan, the flesh, and the world.

Here's how these three temptations appear in Gen. 3:6:

> **(1) "Good for food," or "the lust of the flesh."** In Christ's temptation, it was "command that these stones be made bread"—essentially, "you're hungry, make yourself some bread so you can live." Jesus, the second Adam, answered, "man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God." In other words: I won't use my power to satisfy personal appetites and desires. I'll only use my power for others. My appetites are crucified and controlled, and I won't yield them at your suggestion.

> **(2) "Pleasant to the eyes," or "the lust of the eyes."** In Christ's temptation, it was "cast thyself down . . . He shall give his angels charge concerning thee"—essentially, you need personal protection, use your power to preserve your bodily perfection and beauty. Christ answered, "Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God." In other words: I won't use my power for any personal bodily benefit. My body is controlled and yielded to God for the bodily needs of others.

> **(3) "A tree to be desired to make one wise," or "the pride of life."** In Christ's temptation, it was "All these things [the worldly kingdoms and their glory] will I give thee, if thou wilt fall down and worship me"—act wise and yield your spirit to me as Adam did, and be rewarded. Christ answered, "Get thee hence, Satan . . . Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve." In other words: true wisdom and God-likeness is to yield my whole spirit to God for the exaltation of others, not for my own personal gain and glory.

### 6. TRANSGRESSION OF GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:6)

Adam and Eve went through the same progression of temptation until actual sin was committed—just as many people do today.

James said, "Every man is tempted, when he is drawn away of his own lust and enticed. Then when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin when it is finished, bringeth forth death" (Jas. 1:13-16).

Sin isn't sin until lust has conceived and the law has been broken. Any temptation short of breaking the law is not sin. Any created faculty exercised along any line is perfectly sinless and legitimate within the bounds of the law. It's only when we exercise these faculties in breaking the law that sin is committed.

Think of it like this: it's similar to driving a car. Driving isn't illegal—but driving through a red light is. The act itself isn't the problem; it's crossing the boundary of the law.

It was perfectly sinless and legitimate for Adam and Eve to eat and to desire to be like God. The sin was in eating what was *forbidden* by God's law. There's a right and wrong way, a right and wrong time, to exercise our faculties. When we're tempted to use them in self-gratification and sin, the exercise is wrong. But when they're exercised lawfully, no sin is committed.

### 7. THE RESULTS OF TRANSGRESSION OF GOD'S WORD (Gen. 3:7-19)

In the Fall, man lost spiritual, physical, and eternal life—and gained instead the opposite: spiritual, physical, and eternal death (separation from God). He was cut off from the purpose for which he was created.

He was created to live forever spiritually, but spiritual death—separation from God by sin—deprives him of that privilege (Isa. 59:2; Eph. 2:1-10).

He was created to live forever physically, but physical death—the separation of soul and spirit from the body—deprives him of that benefit (Jas. 2:26).

He was created to live forever both spiritually and physically, but eternal death—eternal separation from God in Hell—deprives him of that great blessing.

Thus it became necessary to redeem man—body, soul, and spirit—before he could fulfill the purposes for which he was created.

In the Fall, man lost the following:

> (1) Spiritual, physical, and eternal life (Isa. 59:2; Eph. 2:1-10; Mt. 25:46).

> (2) Communion and fellowship with God (Isa. 59:2; Col. 1:20).

> (3) Fellowship and pleasure with animals (Gen. 2:19; 9:2).

> (4) Dominion over all the works of God's hands (Ps. 8:3-7).

> (5) Rulership of the Earth (Jn. 14:30; Eph. 6:10-18; 2 Cor. 4:4).

> (6) God-consciousness or innocence (Gen. 2:25; 3:7, 10).

> (7) The power to do good and refuse evil (Gen. 6:5-7; Eph. 2:1-10; Rom. 7).

> (8) God's perfect image and likeness (Phil. 3:20-21).

> (9) Control of soul passions and spirit faculties (Eph. 2:1-10; Gal. 5:19-22).

> (10) The right to the Tree of Life (Gen. 3:22-24).

lessons.title: God's Plan for the Needs of Man
lessons.content:
# God's Plan for the Needs of Man

## Providence: God's Plan for the Needs of Man

Have you ever heard the idea that God wants His people to be poor, sick, defeated, and miserable — that suffering keeps us humble and godly? Let's be honest: these ideas aren't from Scripture. They're actually contradicted by what the Bible clearly teaches.

In this lesson, we're going to dig into what the Bible really says about God's providence — His foresight, care, and provision for your life. You might be surprised to discover that God's plan is actually the opposite of those gloomy doctrines. If there's one teaching that gets misunderstood, it's this one. So let's take our time and be as clear as possible.

## I. The Definition of Providence

The word "providence" means foresight and forethought — God's care over His creatures, His divine direction for all of creation. Think of it like a master architect who doesn't just design a building and walk away. He oversees every detail of construction, anticipating needs before they arise and ensuring everything comes together according to plan.

When we talk about foresight and forethought, we're talking about having a goal in mind and a definite plan to reach it. All rational beings act with some degree of planning. Even animals provide for their young. So how much more does the infinite God make provision for everything He has made?

Now, while all rational beings exercise providence to some degree, this word reaches its full meaning only when applied to God. Divine providence is the care and government God exercises over everything He has created — so that all things accomplish the purposes for which they were made.

It's the infinite attention God gives to His universe, from numbering each hair on your head to the falling of every sparrow, all the way to sustaining the vast galaxies by His power. It's His nature at work, without pause, over everything His hands have made. It's God actively preserving and governing all things — ruling out luck, fortune, or random accidents.

We can think of providence in three ways: universal (over all things), special (over moral beings), and particular (over believers). Providence is perhaps the most comprehensive word in theology. It's the foundation behind so many other doctrines. It connects the seen with the unseen, the visible with the invisible, creation with redemption, and your personal salvation with the final end of all things.

So what is providence? It's God's agency — through Christ, by the Holy Spirit, using holy angels, redeemed people, and even demons and the unsaved — to make all things work together for good for those who love Him (Rom. 8:28). It's how He makes every event in the physical and moral universe fulfill His original purpose.

Creation explains why the universe exists. Preservation explains why it continues. Providence explains how God is working out His purposes in all things according to His eternal plan. It's His active control and care, ensuring that everything — past and future, great and small — moves toward the goal He has set.

## II. The Fivefold Sphere of Divine Providence

### 1. The Material Universe

Scripture makes clear that God's "kingdom ruleth over all" (Ps. 103:19) and that He "worketh all things after the counsel of his will" (Eph. 1:11). He commands the snow, the rain, the whirlwind, the cold, fair weather, and lightning (Job 37:5–38:41). "Whatsoever the Lord pleased, that did he in heaven, and in earth, in the seas, and all deep places" (Ps. 135:6-7). He "maketh his sun to rise" and "sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust" (Mt. 5:45). The sun, moon, stars, and all the Earth are under His rule (Job 37:6-24; 38:4-40; Ps. 19:1-6; 68:9-13; 104:10-30; 107:1-43; 136:4-9; Jer. 10:13; 31:35; Acts 14:17).

### 2. The Vegetable and Plant World

God "causeth the grass to grow" (Ps. 104:14). He clothes "the grass of the field" and the flowers (Mt. 6:28-30). He causes the Earth to bring forth vegetables, grains, and fruits for the good of people and animals (Gen. 1:29; 2:16-17; Lev. 26:4-10; Ps. 65:9-13; 67:6; 104:10-30).

### 3. The Animal World

God's providence extends to every creature. He provides food for birds, fish, and all animals (Job 38:41; Ps. 104:10-30; 136:25; 145:15-16; 147:8-13; Mt. 6:26-30). We're told that not one sparrow falls to the ground without God knowing it (Mt. 10:29).

### 4. The Rational World of Spirit-Beings

Scripture reveals how God cares for holy angels and punishes rebellious spirits. (See Lesson Six for more on spirit-beings and their relationship to God.)

### 5. The Rational World of Flesh-Beings — Man

The Bible shows that God's providence covers all nations and extends to every individual life: "He changeth times and the seasons: he removeth kings, and setteth up kings: he giveth wisdom to the wise... There is a God in heaven that revealeth secrets and maketh known what shall come to pass... the most High ruleth in the kingdom of men, and giveth it to whomsoever He will... and He doeth according to his will in the army of heaven and among the inhabitants of the earth; and none can stay his hand, or say unto him, What doest thou?" (Dan. 2:21-30; 4:17, 25-35).

The smallest detail of your life is known by God, and He helps those who follow His plan (Jer. 1:5; Gal. 1:15-16; 1 Sam. 16:1). The very hairs of your head are numbered, and sparrows are counted (Mt. 10:29-31). So if God knows all that, you can trust your Heavenly Father with your needs right now — and with the life to come.

The Bible is full of examples of God's providence in the lives of nations and individuals. His involvement extends to actions and thoughts. He has prevented sin when people would listen. He has permitted the rebellious to commit evil. He has worked out problems for those who served Him and protected them in countless ways. Reading Scripture reveals all of this. If you want God's best, get on His side and stop cooperating with the devil and the forces that oppose God's will.

Scripture is literally packed with God's dealings with people and with promises covering every need — for this life and the next. We don't have space to quote them all, but let's look at enough to show you that God loves you and has provided for your every need.

## III. God's Free and Abundant Promises for the Needs of Man

Let's put to rest a false idea that floats around in many churches: that God wants His children to suffer disease, poverty, and lack so they'll stay humble and saved. That's simply not what the Bible teaches.

All of God's promises reveal that He has provided for you here and now — even in a fallen world. You can enjoy the full benefits of these promises if you surrender your life to God, receive genuine new birth, and live a godly life according to the gospel. Many blessings are even available to the unsaved as proof that God provides for all creation — even those in rebellion. The purpose? To lead them to repentance (Rom. 2:4). If these things are for the unsaved, how much more are they for believers?

So here's the wise move: get right with God and stay right with Him. Then you won't need to question what He wants for you.

God created humanity to be prosperous, healthy, successful, happy, wise, and blessed with good things. He created everything and gave it to man for his good and enjoyment. If having these things were sinful, God wouldn't have promised them. The sin of Adam and Eve wasn't in prosperity or happiness — it was in eating the forbidden fruit.

The abundance all around us shows that God wants us to have plenty. He made enough for everyone, and anyone can have what they need by following certain principles. What parent doesn't want their children to have the best? Is God less loving than we are? Jesus said, "If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children; how much more shall your heavenly Father give good things to them that ask him?" (Mt. 7:7-11).

Some people worry that prosperity leads to backsliding. But honestly, those who would backslide over blessings would backslide over anything. Some people stumble over food or clothing — should we stop eating or go naked? Of course not. Let's not use a few failures as an excuse to doubt God's generous provision. Stay faithful, use prosperity wisely to help others, and God will bless you even more.

The devil loves to spread lies about God's character to turn people away from Him. But if you read Scripture, you'll see that God abundantly promises everything good and legitimate for your enjoyment. Here's what the Bible actually says:

### 1. Prosperity

"Be thou strong and very courageous, that thou mayest observe to do according to all the law... thou shalt make thy way prosperous, and then thou shalt have good success" (Josh. 1:5-9). "The Lord maketh poor, and maketh rich: he bringeth low, and lifteth up" (1 Sam. 2:7-8). "Keep the charge of the Lord thy God... that thou mayest prosper in all that thou doest" (1 Ki. 2:3-4). "Both riches and honour come of thee... and in thine hand it is to make great, and to give strength to all" (1 Chron. 29:12). "The hand of our God is upon all them for good that seek Him" (Ezra 8:22). "If they obey and serve him, they shall spend their days in prosperity, and their years in pleasures" (Job 36:11). "Blessed is the man that walketh not in the counsel of the ungodly... whatsoever he doeth shall prosper" (Ps. 1:1-3).

In Deuteronomy alone, the word "prosper" appears many times (Deut. 5:16, 29, 33; 6:3, 18; 12:28; 19:13; 30:15). Prosperity is promised when we turn from sin. Yes, sometimes sinners prosper — but usually through wrong dealings (Ps. 73).

Now, some might say these promises are Old Testament and don't apply to us. But here's the thing: we're under a better covenant with even greater promises! Paul argues that the glory of the Old Covenant wasn't as great as what we have now (2 Cor. 3:6-15). He says the New Covenant is "a better testament... established upon better promises" (Heb. 7:22; 8:6). If the shadow of the law produced prosperity, how much more will the reality of the New Covenant?

And the New Testament has its own clear promises: "What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them" (Mk. 11:22-24). "Ask, and it shall be given you... how much more shall your Father, which is in heaven, give good things to them that ask him?" (Mt. 7:7-11). "Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you" (Mt. 6:31-33). "God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work" (2 Cor. 9:6-8). "My God shall supply all your need according to his riches in glory by Christ Jesus" (Phil. 4:19). "Beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, even as thy soul prospereth" (3 Jn. 2).

What could be clearer? Why limit God's bounty because we fear backsliding? Let's decide to handle blessings wisely, use them for God's glory, and there will be no limit to what He will do.

### 2. Healing and Health

There are dozens of promises about healing and health in both Testaments: "If thou wilt diligently hearken to the voice of the Lord thy God... I will put none of the diseases upon thee... for I am the Lord that healeth thee" (Exod. 15:26). Psalm 91 promises deliverance from pestilence and plague. "Who forgiveth all thine iniquities; who healeth all thy diseases" (Ps. 103:3-5). "With his stripes we are healed" (Isa. 53:4-5). "Thine health shall spring forth speedily" (Isa. 58:8).

The New Testament continues: "He cast out the spirits with his word, and healed all that were sick: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Isaiah the prophet, saying, Himself took our infirmities and bare our sickness" (Mt. 8:16-17). "The prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up" (Jas. 5:14-16). "By whose stripes ye were healed" (1 Pet. 2:24). "Beloved, I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper and be in health, even as thy soul prospereth" (3 Jn. 2).

Christ came "to destroy the works of the devil" (1 Jn. 3:9) and to deliver "all that were oppressed by the devil" (Acts 10:38) — not just for three years, but forever. He commissioned His disciples to carry on His work (Mt. 28:19-20; Mk. 16:15-20; Jn. 14:12-15; Acts 1:1-2, 8). Early believers did this until faith waned (Acts 3; 5:16; Jas. 5:14-16). We're to contend for the faith once delivered to the saints (Jude 3; 2 Cor. 10:4-6; Eph. 6:10-18).

We'll explore healing more fully in Lesson Fourteen. But for now, no one with faith in God's Word can deny that healing and health are biblical. Don't be deceived by teachings that say otherwise. Believe what Scripture says, cooperate with God against the devil, and you will be healed.

### 3. Wants and Needs

All your wants and needs are covered by God's promises through Christ. The common theory is that God only provides bare necessities — and grudgingly at that. But the Bible teaches that your desires are abundantly provided for and easy to receive by faith.

"All things are possible to him that believeth" (Mk. 9:23). "What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them" (Mk. 11:22-24). "Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do" (Jn. 14:12-15). "If ye abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you" (Jn. 15:7, 16). "Whatsoever we ask, we receive of him" (1 Jn. 3:21-22; 5:14-15).

These verses are so clear they don't need interpretation — just faith and practice. If we believe the Bible, then we believe we can receive whatever we ask in Jesus' name. If we don't believe, there's no point in asking. It's that simple: ask and believe, you receive. Ask without faith, you don't.

Think of it like a child asking a loving parent for help. The parent isn't stingy or reluctant — they delight in meeting their child's needs. That's how God is with you, only infinitely more generous.

God's plan for your needs becomes crystal clear when you accept His promises at face value. There are no limits in God's provision. All limitations come from our unbelief. Because God gives "to all men liberally, and upbraideth not" (Jas. 1:5-8), let's have faith without wavering (Heb. 11:6; Mk. 11:22-24) and receive what we need.

This doctrine of "no want" was taught in the Old Testament too. David said, "The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want" (Ps. 23:1-6). "There is no want to them that fear him... they that seek the Lord shall not want any good thing... Delight thyself in the Lord; and He shall give thee the desires of thine heart" (Ps. 34:9-10; 37:3-6). "No good thing will he withhold from him that walketh uprightly" (Ps. 84:11).

### 4. Specific Benefits God Has Promised

Beyond prosperity, healing, and general provision, God has specified many blessings for this life and the next:

> (1) Salvation from sin (Mt. 1:21; Eph. 1:7; 2:8-9; 1 Jn. 1:7-9).

> (2) The baptism in the Holy Ghost (Mt. 3:11; Jn. 1:33; Acts 1:4-5; 2:38-39).

> (3) The kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 5:3; 13:43; 25:34).

> (4) Comfort (Mt. 5:4; Jn. 14:16, 26).

> (5) The Earth as an inheritance (Mt. 5:5; Ps. 37:9, 29).

> (6) Filling of righteousness (Mt. 5:6; 1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-4, 18).

> (7) Mercy from God (Mt. 5:7; Rom. 12:1; Eph. 2:4; 1 Pet. 1:3).

> (8) Heart purity (Mt. 5:8; Jn. 15:3; 1 Pet. 1:22-23).

> (9) Sonship (Mt. 5:9; Jn. 1:12; Rom. 8:14-16; Gal. 3:26-28).

> (10) Great reward in Heaven (Mt. 5:11-12; Rom. 8:17-18; 1 Pet. 1:3-4).

> (11) Answers to all prayers (Mt. 6:4-6, 18; 7:7-11; 18:18-20; Mk. 11:22-24; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; 1 Jn. 3:21-22; 5:14-15).

> (12) Abundant provision for life here and now (Mt. 6:25-33; Mk. 11:22-24; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; 2 Cor. 9:8-11; Phil. 4:19; 3 Jn. 2).

> (13) Infinite Fatherhood of God (Mt. 6:33; 7:7-11; 10:29-32; Rom. 8:32).

> (14) Eternal life (Jn. 3:16, 36; 5:24; 17:3; Gal. 6:7-8).

> (15) Reward for the smallest work done for others (Mt. 10:39-42; 16:27).

> (16) Rest to the soul (Mt. 11:28-30; Rom. 5:1-11; 8:1-13).

> (17) Power with God to do all things: nothing shall be impossible to you (Mt. 17:19-21; 18:18-20; 21:21-22; Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; Lk. 24:49; Acts 1:8).

> (18) The presence of God (Mt. 18:18-20; 28:19-20).

> (19) Miraculous signs and wonders (Mk. 16:15-20; Jn. 14:12-15; Acts 1:8).

> (20) Power over all the power of the devil (Lk. 10:19; 24:49; Mk. 16:15-20; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; Acts 1:8; 2 Cor. 10:4-5; Eph. 6:10-18).

> (21) Wisdom above all adversaries (Lk. 21:15; Jas. 1:5-8).

> (22) Complete spiritual satisfaction (Mt. 5:6; Jn. 4:13-14; 6:35-63).

> (23) Resurrection of the body to immortality and eternal life (Jn. 5:28-29; 6:39-54; 1 Cor. 15:51-58; Phil. 3:20-21; 1 Thess. 4:13-17).

> (24) Complete knowledge of the truth beyond all doubt (Jn. 7:16-17; 8:31-32; 14:16-17, 26; 15:26-27; 16:7-15; 2 Tim. 2:15; 3:16-17).

> (25) Rivers of living waters flowing out of the innermost being (Jn. 7:37-39; Acts 2:1-11, 38-39; 5:32; Eph. 5:18; Mt. 5:6).

> (26) Freedom from darkness into complete light (Jn. 8:12, 31-32; Acts 26:18; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; 2 Cor. 3:18; Eph. 4:17-21; 5:8-16).

> (27) Complete freedom from bondage of sin, false doctrines, and demon powers (Jn. 8:31-32; Rom. 8:1-16; Gal. 5:1-26).

> (28) Security in (not out of) Christ (Jn. 8:31-32; 10:27-29; 15:1-7; Rom. 8:1-13; Col. 1:23; 2:6-7; 1 Thess. 3:8; Heb. 3:6, 12-14; 6:11-22; 10:23-39).

> (29) Honor from God (Jn. 12:26; 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26).

> (30) An eternal home in the New Jerusalem (Jn. 14:1-3; Heb. 13:14; Rev. 3:12).

> (31) Power to do the works of Christ (Jn. 14:12-15; Lk. 24:49; Acts 1:8; Mk. 16:15-20; 1 Cor. 12:4-11; 2 Cor. 10:4-5).

> (32) Healing and sound health (Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; Rom. 8:11; 3 Jn. 2; Mt. 8:16-17; 1 Pet. 2:24; Heb. 11:6).

> (33) The gifts of the Spirit (1 Cor. 1:7; 12:4-11, 31; 14:1; Rom. 12:4-8).

> (34) The fruit of the Spirit (Gal. 5:22-24).

> (35) Power to exercise unlimited authority in this life in all the fullness of God according to the gospel (Lk. 24:49; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; 16:15-20; Mt. 17:20; 21:21-22; Acts 1:8; 2:38-39, 43; 3:6; 4:10-16, 29-33; 5:1-16; 6:3-8; 8:5-20; 9:17-18, 32-43; 11:21; 13:6-12; 14:3, 8-20, 27; 15:4, 12; 16:16-34; 19:1-20; 20:9-12; 28:1-10; Rom. 1:11; 15:18-19, 29; 1 Cor. 1:7, 18-24; 2:1-5; 4:19-21; 5:1-5; 9:18; 12:1-11, 28-31; 13:1-3; 14:1-40; 16:10; 2 Cor. 3:6-18; 4:7; 5:20; 6:7; 8:7; 10:3-11; 12:9, 12; 13:4, 10; Gal. 3:3-5, 14; Eph. 1:19-20; 6:10-18; Phil. 4:9; Col. 1:11; 2:10; 1 Thess. 1:5; 2:13; 2 Thess. 1:11; 2:17; 1 Tim. 4:14; 2 Tim. 1:6-8, 14; 2:21; 3:5; Titus 2:14; Heb. 2:3-4; 6:1-2; Jas. 2:18; 5:14-16; 2 Pet. 2:4; 3 Jn. 2; Jude 3, 20-24).

Looking at all these promises, you can see that no believer is limited in receiving what they need for this life and the next. We've included so many Scripture references because we want you to realize just how thoroughly God has provided for you. The only thing that can hinder you is unbelief. And there's no excuse for unbelief with this many promises — especially when early believers (not just apostles, but ordinary Christians) experienced these blessings.

If early believers enjoyed these blessings, why can't we? Does God love us less? Has He lost His power? Don't we still need these benefits? Are the promises still valid? Has God changed His program? Has He withdrawn what He offered? Is He a respecter of persons? Where does Scripture say these things aren't for us today?

There's not one hint in the Bible that God has canceled the blessings available to early Christians. This idea is a theory invented by modern unbelievers who would rather create doctrines of excuse than face the facts and meet the conditions for receiving God's provision.

You'd have to be a rank unbeliever to deny that you can receive what you ask from God. If you choose the side of those who reject God's Word, don't complain when prayers go unanswered or blessings don't come. God won't force you to be blessed against your will. You're a free moral agent, and God respects your choices. He expects you to use common sense and cooperate with Him instead of His enemies.

## IV. Proof of Divine Providence

> 1. Scripture proves that divine providence is at work in all things and has operated in every age. Providence brought all things into existence (Isa. 45:18; Col. 1:15-19), provided redemption after the Fall (Gen. 3:15; Eph. 1:1-11), preserved the race through the flood (Gen. 6:8–8:22), dealt with humanity in each dispensation, called people to carry out divine purposes, revealed the eternal plan in Scripture, recorded God's promises, documented countless examples of His care, established order in the universe, and sent Christ to redeem all things.

> 2. The continued preservation of all things since creation proves that God's providence is working out His will.

> 3. God's love in creating and redeeming all things proves His desire to preserve creation and reconcile it to Himself. Parents protect their children. Makers preserve what they've made. God is no exception.

> 4. Divine perfection requires God to preserve and govern all things for the best good of every being and the universe. Because of His nature — and the selfishness of some in the universe — there's a conflict between rebels and God. God is committed to preserving creation and ultimately removing all rebellion.

> 5. The prevalence of order proves divine providence. Fixed and eternal laws govern everything, evidencing an all-wise ruler and preserver.

> 6. History is full of proof of God's providence. What happens isn't random. A supernatural power has always guided the affairs and destinies of creation according to a plan.

> 7. Religion proves divine providence. In every age, people have found God through exercising religious faculties according to Scripture.

> 8. Common intelligence in all rational beings reveals divine providence. Sane people experience and recognize it in many ways.

## V. The Ultimate Purpose and Final End of Providence

What's the goal of providence? It's to create a race of beings who find their highest happiness through the highest holiness — and who, in doing so, glorify their Creator. The purpose of the universe can't be found in unconscious, unthinking, or unmoral creation. It's found in the highest, most rational, and most moral part of creation. Lower forms exist for higher ones; material and animal for the moral and spiritual.

God's final purpose is the highest development of character in free moral agents, so they can glorify Him supremely and devote themselves to the best good of themselves and the universe (Gen. 1:26-28; Isa. 43:7; 45:18; Eph. 1:12; Col. 1:15-22; Rev. 4:9-11; 5:11-14; 7:10-12).

Scripture shows that providence is working toward this end and that there's a special providence for saints not enjoyed by sinners (Ps. 34:7-10; 37:1-12, 23-28; Prov. 3:6; Mt. 6:33; Rom. 8:28).

Now, here's where many people get confused. They think sickness, accidents, and hardships are manifestations of providence. But these things are never God's direct will or directly caused by God. They come from evil forces that currently influence the world's social order during this period of rebellion against God.

The only glory God receives from these troubles is in delivering His people from them. God wants you to take a stand against these evils and cooperate with Him to work things out for His glory. This is how He develops faith and patience in us and demonstrates His power over His enemies.

It's similar to being a soldier stationed in enemy territory. You're going to face attacks — that's part of being behind enemy lines. But your Commander has given you weapons and promised to deliver you. The battle isn't evidence that your Commander doesn't care; it's an opportunity for Him to show His power and for you to grow in trust and strength.

We must recognize that we're on enemy territory in a rebellious world. If rebels had no power to affect us, God could never demonstrate His victory over them. It's only when we stop blaming God for everything and instead trust Him for deliverance that He's pleased with us. We need to recognize Satan's works so we can stand against him with God's help.

As we saw in Lesson Six, the devil and evil spirits dominate the present world system — except among God's children. Satan is called the prince of this world. God rules among men by overruling evil forces to carry out His plan, with the ultimate goal of ridding Earth of all rebellion. Naturally, certain events happen that God doesn't directly prevent. He could prevent them, but His plan involves training His people to become rulers over all things in the end.

To say that God directly willed the Fall, Cain's murder of Abel, the rise of idolatry, or humanity's wickedness in every age is saying too much. To teach that God directly causes wars, strife, sickness, disease, accidents, and calamities is unscriptural. Blaming God for millions of evil things puts the blame on the wrong person.

God is absolutely holy and just in all His dealings. Everything He does is for our betterment and good. He has a plan for each person and tries to guide everyone toward His highest will. But when someone rebels and gets into trouble, God isn't to blame. Sin, stubbornness, rebellion, the conditions sin creates, plus the tireless efforts of evil spirits — these cause events beyond God's direct control.

Scripture records hundreds of instances where people deviated from truth and suffered consequences. But not once does it say God was directly responsible for the evil that came upon anyone. God has allowed Satan to test people for purification and training, but He always delivers

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Conscience
lessons.content:
# The Dispensation of Conscience

**(Gen. 3:22–8:14)**

**THE ANTEDILUVIAN AGE (Gen. 1:3–8:14)**

---

## I. The Definition of Conscience

This period is called the Dispensation of Conscience because, during this time, God tested humanity based on one thing: obedience to the inner voice of conscience. You could call this "the age of freedom." People were free to follow or ignore what their conscience told them—without any formal law to hold them accountable (Rom. 2:12-16).

Here's an important point: there was no revealed code of law until after the flood. The old idea that Adam had the Ten Commandments, and that people kept them before Moses, simply isn't supported by Scripture.

The law Adam broke wasn't the Ten Commandments. It was the specific command not to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil (Gen. 2:16-17; 3:1-24). Any command from God carries the weight of law, but Scripture makes clear that the Ten Commandments weren't given to anyone before Moses (Deut. 5:2-21; 29:14-15).

### What is Conscience?

> "Conscience is the knowledge of our acts, states, or character as to right or wrong: the faculty, power, or principle which decides on the lawfulness of our actions and affections, and approves or condemns them; the moral faculty or sense."—Webster.

Think of conscience like an internal alarm system. It was activated—switched on—when humanity chose to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. Ever since then, conscience has served as the guiding faculty in our moral decisions.

The Bible has a lot to say about different kinds of conscience:

- "Seared" conscience (1 Tim. 4:2)
- "Awakened" conscience (Jn. 8:9)
- "Purged" conscience (Heb. 9:9, 14; 10:2)
- "Pure" conscience (Acts 24:16)
- "Weak" conscience (1 Cor. 8:7, 12, 13)
- "Defiled" conscience (Titus 1:15)
- "Witnessing" conscience (Rom. 2:12-15; 9:1; 2 Cor. 1:12)
- "Good" conscience (Acts 2:13; 1 Tim. 1:5, 19; 1 Pet. 2:19; 3:16, 21; Heb. 13:19)
- "Convicting" or "healthy" conscience (Gen. 3:10; 4:13; Mt. 27:3)

---

## II. The Length of the Dispensation of Conscience

By tracing the genealogies in Gen. 5:1-29 and Gen. 7:11, we can calculate that this dispensation lasted **1,656 years**—from the creation of Adam to the 600th year of Noah's life when the flood came. Here's how it breaks down:

1. Adam was 130 years old when Seth was born (Gen. 5:3)
2. Seth was 105 years old when Enos was born (Gen. 5:6)
3. Enos was 90 years old when Cainan was born (Gen. 5:9)
4. Cainan was 70 years old when Mahaleel was born (Gen. 5:12)
5. Mahaleel was 65 years old when Jared was born (Gen. 5:15)
6. Jared was 162 years old when Enoch was born (Gen. 5:18)
7. Enoch was 65 years old when Methuselah was born (Gen. 5:21)
8. Methuselah was 187 years old when Lamech was born (Gen. 5:25)
9. Lamech was 182 years old when Noah was born (Gen. 5:28)
10. Noah was 600 years old when the flood came (Gen. 7:6, 11)

**TOTAL: 1,656 years from Adam to Noah's flood.**

---

## III. The Favorable Beginning of Man in Conscience

In Gen. 3:15, we find God's first promise of redemption—through the seed of the woman, pointing to Christ. Humanity was being given another chance, another opportunity to prove faithful to God.

People had the promise that the curse would eventually be lifted and that they would be restored to their original position as rulers over the Earth. They had the ability to choose right from wrong. They had the capacity for faith in God and hope for redemption.

Here's something fascinating: humanity also started out with a knowledge of true worship. God had slain animals in the Garden and clothed Adam and Eve with their skins (Gen. 3:21). Blood had been shed—a preview of the blood that would be shed by the coming Redeemer.

We see proof of this in the story of Cain and Abel. When they were old enough to worship, Abel brought the right kind of sacrifice—a blood sacrifice—showing that Adam and Eve had passed down the true way of worship. There was even a tabernacle for worship in those days! In Gen. 4:7, God told Cain, "If thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door." The Hebrew word *chata* here means "a sin offering" (as in Exod. 30:10; Lev. 4:3; 6:25). The "door" refers to the tabernacle door where sacrifices were offered. Gen. 4:3-5 makes clear there was a specific place where offerings were brought to God.

So humanity knew that God was just and merciful, that His Word was true, and that He would be with them if they chose obedience. They had witnessed the curse of sin firsthand. They had personal experience of both sin and the means of reconciliation with God.

Everything was as favorable as it could possibly be, given the fallen state. People could have proved faithful to God—but as time went on, humanity grew worse and worse until destruction became necessary.

---

## IV. The Test for Man in Conscience

In Gen. 4:7, we find the specific test for this period: "If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted?"

God required that people obey the dictates of their conscience regarding His revealed will and their own inner knowledge of right and wrong. The test was simple: do good, refuse evil, and have faith in God and the coming Redeemer who would bruise the serpent's head (Gen. 3:15).

Here's what happened at the Fall: humanity lost God-consciousness and gained self-consciousness. We lost the inner power to do good and gained the power to do evil.

People woke up to a hard reality: they needed God's help to live rightly and obey His will. They needed divine assistance to overcome the evil spirit forces they had yielded to. On their own, they were powerless—but they still had the power of choice. They could voluntarily acknowledge their lost state and helpless condition and accept God's help. This would enable them to be redeemed and overcome their spiritual enemies.

This is still all God requires of helpless, sinful people today. The moment anyone acknowledges their sin and need for God—the moment they call upon Him for deliverance from satanic forces and sinful habits—they are instantly heard. They become a new creature in Christ, born again, empowered with righteousness and true holiness, renewed in spiritual and moral power to overcome the flesh, the world, and the devil (Eph. 2:1-19; 4:22-24; Gal. 5:16-26; 2 Cor. 5:17; Rom. 6:1-23; 8:1-16; 1 Jn. 1:7; 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-4, 18).

---

## V. The Purpose of God in This Dispensation

What was God trying to accomplish in this age? He wanted to teach humanity—right from the beginning of the Fall—that obedience to Him, faith in His Word, and conformity to His will were the only sources of true pleasure, happiness, health, and prosperity. These were the only paths to restoration.

God wanted to test humanity under complete freedom of will, without any restraint or compulsion, to see if people would choose to follow Him instead of their new master—the devil.

Think of it like this: someone might argue, "I didn't really understand right from wrong! I should get a pass for that first sin." So God met this argument by giving humanity complete freedom of action to choose for themselves which master they wanted to serve.

In this period, there was no Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil to avoid. There were no specific laws or commandments to obey. The test was simply a choice between two masters—and the free choice to conform to what their rational nature told them was right.

The broad principles of right and wrong were known. People had within them certain created faculties and knowledge of how to use their whole being for the best good of themselves, others, and the universe as a whole. They had to make the free choice to consecrate themselves to what their rational being dictated—the same end to which all society must conform if it is to continue eternally: that each person choose and wholeheartedly cooperate with others for the preservation of that society.

Humanity had within them the knowledge of the principles of righteousness and true holiness that God would later require by law. Paul described it as doing by "nature the things contained in the law, these having not the law, are a law unto themselves: Which shew the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience also bearing them witness, and their thoughts the mean while accusing or else excusing one another" (Rom. 2:12-16).

Through this test of freedom, people could learn lessons they couldn't have learned any other way. God wanted to see—and wanted humanity to see and experience—just how sinful they could become if they chose self-gratification and selfishness over the good of others.

God permitted evil spirits to operate in the human realm to teach people from the very beginning their need for dependence on God and submission to His will as the best choice for life and conduct.

---

## VI. The Means of God in Accomplishing This Purpose

Based on what we've discussed, it's clear that God used several things to accomplish His purpose during this period:

- **Human conscience** demonstrated how exceedingly sinful people could become if they chose to continue in rebellion.
- **Complete freedom of action** demonstrated how far humanity would go in rebellion before God would have to intervene for the sake of His eternal plan.
- **The malice of the devil** demonstrated the contrast between the two masters humanity could choose to serve while on probation.

This complete freedom of will was what humanity chose in the Fall. So God permitted people to go to the full limit of wickedness in this age, so they might learn the folly of such a course—and so all future generations throughout eternity might profit from this experience.

Now, this freedom of conduct without restraint, and its results, are matters of historical record. They will serve forever as a warning against the wrong use of free will.

---

## VII. The Failure of Man in Conscience

### 1. THE FAILURE OF ADAM

> "My spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years" (Gen. 6:3).

This verse has been used through the ages to teach that someday God's Holy Spirit will stop striving with all people and they'll be cut off without hope. Some use it to claim that the door of mercy to Gentiles will close soon and God will only save Jews. Both of these ideas are man-made and have no place in Scripture or God's plan, as we'll see in Lesson Fifteen, Point VIII.

That Adam is personally referred to in Gen. 6:3 is clear from the following:

> (1) The word "spirit" is correctly written without a capital letter and doesn't refer to the Holy Spirit. It refers to the created spirit of man manifested in his conscience and other spirit faculties. It's called "My spirit" because Adam's personal spirit was directly created and given to him by God. God is the God of all spirits, and all are considered His in the sense of creation, origin, and rulership (Num. 16:22; 27:16; Isa. 42:5; Heb. 12:9, 23).

> (2) The word "strive" comes from the Hebrew word *doon*, meaning "to rule," "judge," "strive," "contend," "convict," "minister," "execute," "plead." It's translated "judge" (Gen. 15:14; 49:16), "contend" (Eccl. 6:10), "execute" (Jer. 21:12), "plead" (Prov. 31:9), and "minister" (Ps. 9:8). The Septuagint, Arabic, Syriac, and Latin versions translate this as "remain in"—that is, "My spirit I have created in man will not always remain in him."

> (3) The word for "man" is singular, used with the definite article. It's the Hebrew *Adam*, and with the definite article should read "the Adam"—that is, "My Spirit will not always remain in or strive with the Adam." This is made clearer by the singular pronouns "he" and "his," proving one man is referred to, not the whole human race.

> (4) The word "also" makes it clear this refers to the one man Adam. It has no meaning if "man" is read as plural. If all men are referred to, then who else besides all men could "also" refer to? It can only mean that Adam—the only one who was a direct product of creation—was also flesh, just like those born of men rather than directly created. The whole verse teaches that Adam, like other men, had become sinful and would be cut off even though he was the only created man. It reveals that God gave Adam 120 more years to live, making clear that Gen. 6:3 refers to a time 810 years after Adam's creation, since he lived only 930 years.

> (5) The verse couldn't refer to cutting off all men in 120 years, because all men weren't cut off then, and all men still haven't been cut off after many times 120 years. This verse sheds light on the ungodliness of men 810 years after creation and 846 years before the flood. It shows why, even in Enoch's day, God had already determined to destroy humanity by a flood. Enoch divinely named his son "Methuselah," which in Hebrew means "when he is dead it [the deluge] shall be sent." Enoch saw such ungodliness that he even prophesied of the destruction of the ungodly at Christ's second coming (Jude 14).

Enoch was born 627 years after Adam's creation and was a contemporary of Adam for 303 years. Methuselah, who died the year the flood came, was a contemporary of Adam for 238 years. So two men lived through the entire Dispensation of Conscience!

This shows that humanity was so wicked that God planned, when Methuselah was born, to send a flood—969 years before it actually came. Adam himself was so wicked that God gave him only 120 more years to live and repent. Whether Adam ever repented isn't clear. He's not mentioned among the faithful worthies of the Old Testament in Heb. 11. He's never mentioned anywhere in Scripture as being a godly man—a striking fact when you consider that Abel, Enoch, and Noah from this Antediluvian Age are mentioned thirteen times as being godly (Gen. 4:4; 5:22; 6:8, 9; 7:1; Lk. 11:51; Heb. 11:1-7; Rev. 3:20; 2 Pet. 2:5; Jude 14; 1 Pet. 3:20).

If Adam—after being a direct creation of God and after seeing God face to face—failed the Creator, what must have been the sinful condition of many others?

### 2. THE FAILURE OF CAIN (Gen. 4:1-16)

This passage records the failure of the first man born on Earth, just as the passage above (Gen. 6:3) records the failure of the first and only created man.

Here we have the story of two of Adam's many sons. One was humble and godly; the other was self-willed and rebellious. Both were full-grown and knew the true way of worship. They'd been taught this by Adam and by God Himself, since God was present when the brothers offered their sacrifices.

God even gave Cain another chance. He personally told Cain about the sin offering at the tabernacle door (Gen. 4:4, 7). Cain also knew when God accepted Abel's offering what he should do—but he rejected the counsel of both God and man and started his own religion.

The fire of God evidently consumed Abel's offering, as happened on other occasions when God accepted a sacrifice (Gen. 15:17; Lev. 9:24; Judg. 6:21; 1 Ki. 18:38; 1 Chron. 21:26; 2 Chron. 7:1).

Instead of conforming to God's will and welcoming fellowship with Him, Cain began scheming about how to get even with Abel. This desire for vengeance led to murder. God appeared a second time to Cain, cursed him from His own presence and from other men, and condemned him to be a wanderer in the Earth.

Cain was afraid someone would take vengeance on him, so God gave him a pledge of protection. The Hebrew word for "mark" is *owth*, meaning "a pledge," "token" (Gen. 9:12, 17; 17:11), or "a sign" (Exodus 4:8; 8:23). It never means a literal physical mark on the body, but a pledge. This pledge is stated in the same verse (Gen. 4:15): "Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold."

### 3. THE FAILURE OF CAIN'S DESCENDANTS (Gen. 4:17-24)

This passage reveals the activities of the godless civilization produced by Cain's descendants and others associated with him. This part of the human race tried to make their land of exile into a paradise instead of following God's directions and cooperating with Him to remove the curse altogether.

They were plunderers and profligates. Cain himself set a wicked example. He started the first city. He aimed to secure everything for his own bodily pleasure, even if it harmed others. He became wealthy through violence and robbery and led others into sinful ways. He changed the simplicity of living and became the inventor of weights and measures. He set boundaries around lands and fortified cities against wild beasts and enemies.

His children became more wicked as time went on. Many marks of modern civilization appeared in this early one:

> 1. City life and its attendant evils first appeared (Gen. 4:17).

> 2. Jabal, the first cattleman and nomad dwelling in tents, introduced flesh and milk as food to escape tilling the soil (Gen. 4:20).

> 3. Jubal invented "the harp and organ" (wind instruments) for songs, dances, and pleasures of all kinds associated with crowded populations (Gen. 4:21).

> 4. Tubal-Cain, a worker in brass and iron, forged weapons of war and made things for the comfort of civilization. The Earth was filled with violence, bloodshed, and wars (Gen. 4:22; 6:14).

> 5. Lamech was a murderer, the first polygamist, and the first songwriter on record (Gen. 4:23-24).

From these few facts, you can picture the scene: envyings, strifes, deeds of licentiousness, violence, lowing of herds, strains of music and dancing to soothe the conscience, clatter of hoofs, sounding of anvils, boasting of proud men—all the mingled noise rising from a godless civilization struggling to overpower the curse.

### 4. THE FAILURE OF SETH'S DESCENDANTS (Gen. 4:25–5:32)

This passage shows the failure of Seth's descendants and the line of ten generations from Adam to Noah. Some believe there were two distinct family lines in this period—the ungodly line of Cain and the godly line of Seth—but this isn't taught in Scripture:

> (1) Such an idea isn't once mentioned in Scripture. Seth's descendants as a whole may not have been as ungodly as Cain's, but clearly there were more than just two branches of the human family coming from just two men. Adam had "sons and daughters" (Gen. 5:4). Every son of Adam started a branch of the race, as has generally been the case throughout history. Not all of Adam's sons and daughters are mentioned in Scripture, nor are the multitudes of other families through this whole 1,656-year period. There were also races of giants in the Earth that weren't descendants of either Cain or Seth, as we'll see below.

> (2) There were many people on Earth when Cain killed Abel, or God wouldn't have protected Cain from others who might want to kill him (Gen. 4:14-15). Cain was evidently married by the time he slew Abel, for immediately afterward he went to the land of Nod with his wife, and there she had a son (Gen. 4:16-17). The curse on woman was to multiply greatly, and this was true of early women—when Cain had his first child, there were enough people even in his land of exile to build a city (Gen. 4:17). Cain wouldn't build a city for just one wife and one child.

> (3) The history of the godless civilization shows a considerable population. Bible students estimate there could have been at least 150 million people at the time of the flood—a very conservative estimate. Israel multiplied in Egypt from 70 to about six million people in 215 years. When people lived nearly a thousand years, they could multiply infinitely faster than Israel did in Egypt, when lifespans were about a hundred years. The fact that Scripture doesn't explain the population before the flood suggests that growth occurred at a normal rate and the Earth was filled with people. So the idea that there were just two sons whose immediate families stayed separated for 1,656 years is absurd. We might as well argue that the few people mentioned in other genealogies were all the people who lived on Earth in those periods.

> (4) Seth's line is highlighted in Gen. 5 to show the particular branch through which the seed of the woman descended. The purpose isn't to show a godly line for over 1,600 years. There couldn't be such a thing as generation after generation with all offspring being godly for that length of time. This is contrary to all history and Scripture. This won't happen until the New Heavens and New Earth, when all human and spirit rebels are confined to the Lake of Fire forever. There has never been, and never will be until the final restitution of all things, such a thing as godly people reproducing godly people by nature. So the belief that there were many generations of godly people just because they descended from Seth is ridiculous.

> (5) The firstborn of Seth was ungodly—he was the one who started idolatry on Earth, according to Gen. 4:26, which says, "Then began men to call upon the name of the Lord." According to many ancient versions, this reads, "Then began men to call themselves by the name of Jehovah" and "to call their gods by the name of Jehovah." People began worshipping stars and making idols like all kinds of animals (Rom. 1:21-32). The Targum of Onkelos reads, "Then in his days the sons of men desisted from praying in the name of the Lord," and the Targum of Jonathan says, "That was the generation in whose days they began to err, and to make to themselves idols, and surnamed their idols by the name of the Lord." The Scofield Bible reads, "Then began men to call themselves by the name of Jehovah."

> We know that true worship didn't begin with Enos, because Abel and others had already worshipped God the true way many years before. Enos wasn't born until 235 years after man's creation. We read of true worship during this time (Gen. 3:21; 4:1-16; Heb. 11:4), so Enos didn't start true worship—he started false worship. If Seth didn't worship the true God, and if true worship began with Enos, then Seth was ungodly—which destroys the theory of Seth and all his sons being godly men. If idolatry started with Enos, then he was ungodly—which also destroys the theory of the godly line of Seth. This proves Seth's line wasn't all godly, and that the sons of God in Gen. 6 were not godly men from his line. If there had been thousands of godly descendants from Seth, they certainly would have been mentioned somewhere in Scripture, especially when only three godly men from this period are mentioned thirteen times, as seen above. Apart from these three, the ungodliness of men in this period is emphasized throughout.

### 5. THE FAILURE OF ALL MEN IN GENERAL (Gen. 6:1-7, 11-13)

The ungodliness of all people in this age is stated in many Scriptures (Gen. 4:1-26; 6:1-13; 7:1; Mt. 24:37-39; Lk. 17:26-27; 1 Pet. 3:20; 2 Pet. 2:4-5). The fact that only three men are mentioned as being godly in this entire period proves the general ungodliness of humanity.

God will judge sin wherever it's found, whether it delays His original purpose or not. If there hadn't been one godly man on Earth at the time of the flood, the present social order would have been totally destroyed, just as Lucifer's order was. God would have had to make a new social order, as He did in Adam's day, because He created the Earth "to be inhabited" (Isa. 45:18). His original purpose will be finally carried out even if He has to destroy and remake several social systems. Failure on the part of any one social system merely delays the original purpose.

At the time of Moses, God considered destroying all Israel because of sin and raising up a new nation under Moses. But Moses interceded, the nation was spared, and God's original purpose continued (Exod. 32:7-14). If He had destroyed Israel and made a new nation from Moses, His plan would have been merely delayed, not defeated.

The failure of people in general during the Dispensation of Conscience can be summarized as follows:

> (1) A tendency to worship God merely as Creator and Benefactor, not as Savior or Covenant God (Gen. 4:1-7; Rom. 1:17-32).

> (2) Undue prominence of the female sex and disregard of the primal law of marriage (Gen. 4:19; Mt. 24:37-39; Lk. 17:26-30).

> (3) Rapid progress of mechanical arts and inventions to make the curse easier (Gen. 4:17-24; Rom. 1:30).

> (4) An alliance between nominal worshippers of God and the world (Mt. 24:37-39; 1 Pet. 3:20; 2 Pet. 2:4-5).

> (5) Rejection of the preaching of righteousness (2 Pet. 2:5; Mt. 24:37-39).

> (6) Appearance on Earth of spirit-beings in unlawful intercourse with the human race (Gen. 6:1-7; 2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7).

> (7) Murders and violence of all kinds (Gen. 6:1-7, 11-13; Rom. 1:17-32).

> (8) Disregard for restraint of conscience (Gen. 4:17-24; 6:1-7, 11-13; Mt. 24:37-39; 2 Pet. 2:4-5).

> (9) Sins and pleasures of all kinds to satisfy every lust (Gen. 4:17-24; 6:1-7, 11-13; Mt. 24:37-39; Rom. 1:17-32. Compare 1 Tim. 4; 2 Tim. 3; 2 Pet. 2; Jude 3-19).
