> (10) The appearance on Earth of ungodly and inhuman giants, as discussed below.

### 6. THE FAILURE OF THE SONS OF GOD (Gen. 6:1-7, 11-13)

The subject of the sons of God marrying the daughters of men is often misunderstood. We should be fair and open-minded to everything in Scripture, letting the Word of God settle every question.

Some hold that the sons of God were the sons of Seth, and that they married the daughters of Cain, breaking down the godly line of Seth. But there was no such thing as a godly line producing sons of God for 1,656 years, as we've proven above—so the sons of God could not have been sons of Seth. Here's why this theory doesn't hold up:

> (1) **The timing of these marriages disproves this theory.** If the sons of God were sons of Seth, these marriages couldn't have happened during the last 600 years of this age, because Noah was the only godly man in this period: "for thee have I seen righteous before me in this generation... Noah was a just man and perfect in his generations, and Noah walked with God" (Gen. 6:9; 7:1; 2 Pet. 2:4-5). There were no godly men to be called sons of God during Noah's lifetime; so these marriages, if they involved ordinary men, had to occur during only the first 1,000 years. But Gen. 6:4 says such marriages continued to "those days," which everyone agrees includes Noah's days up to the flood.

> In Gen. 6:1-2, we see that the sons of God began to marry the daughters of men from the very beginning when daughters were born. Adam had the first daughters (Gen. 3:20; 5:1-5), and it must have been some of these that the sons of God began to marry. This was before Seth's birth, or at least before Seth had sons of marriageable age. Seth had no children until age 105, or 235 years after creation (Gen. 5:1-6). Enos had his first son 325 years after creation (Gen. 5:1-9). If the sons of God married daughters of men "when men began to multiply on the face of the earth" (Gen. 6:1), they couldn't be sons of Seth, because he had no sons yet.

> Also, Gen. 6:4 mentions the sons of God marrying daughters of men "also after that"—that is, after the flood. If the daughters of men were ungodly daughters of Cain, how did these ungodly women survive the flood, since only Noah's family was saved by the ark? (1 Pet. 3:20; Gen. 6:18).

> (2) **The expressions "sons of God" and "daughters of men" indicate that one is the product of God and the other is the product of men.** The facts above prove that Seth's sons were just as ungodly as Cain's, so they couldn't be called sons of God in the godly sense any more than Cain's sons could. Only two of Seth's sons were godly, so Seth's sons couldn't be godly sons of God. We have just as much scriptural authority to teach that Cain's sons were godly as to teach that Seth's were. We've also seen there weren't two distinct lines of the race—one of Cain, one of Seth. So teaching that these marriages broke down the separation between godly and ungodly lines is unscriptural. Scripture specifically states that "all flesh had corrupted his way upon the earth" (Gen. 6:1-7, 11-13), which is why only one family was saved in the ark. It wouldn't have been just for God to kill godly men along with sinners. If the sons of God were godly men marrying ungodly women, this wouldn't in itself make them backslidden and lost. Many saved people today are married to unsaved companions and still remain godly. So we must conclude that this theory is unscriptural.

> (3) **It's only through a divine specific act of creation that any being can become a son of God.** Adam was a direct product of creation and was called a "son of God" (Lk. 3:38). Adam's sons aren't and couldn't be called sons of God by natural generation. His sons can only become sons of God in a godly sense by being born again and created anew in Christ (Jn. 3:1-5; Eph. 4:22-24; 2 Cor. 5:17). Since Seth's sons weren't godly by the new birth, they could never be called sons of God.

> (4) **The expression "sons of God" is used only five times in the Old Testament, and every time it refers to angels**—unless Gen. 6 is an exception. In Job 1:6 and 2:1, it's used of angels in Heaven presenting themselves before the Lord. Satan came with them too. The scene was in Heaven, as proven by Satan having come from Earth. These sons of God couldn't have been sons of Seth, because godly men didn't go to Heaven

lessons.title: Why God's Plan for the Needs of Man is Not Realized
lessons.content:
# Why God's Plan for the Needs of Man is Not Realized

In Lesson Ten, we explored God's comprehensive plan for meeting every human need—both for this life and the life to come. We saw that His promises cover absolutely everything you could ever want or need.

And honestly? Those promises are almost too good to believe. Even spiritually mature believers struggle to fully grasp them—not because the words are difficult, but because it's simply overwhelming that the Living God would make such extravagant provision for us. But it's absolutely true!

No reasonable person can look at these promises and deny what they plainly say. No Christian would dare claim God won't keep His word. But here's where the rubber meets the road: the challenge for believers is actually meeting the conditions for these promises in everyday life.

Let me walk you through some of the most common reasons why people don't experience the full abundance God has provided for them.

## 1. Christians Have Been Misled by False Teachings

False cults and misguided religious leaders have spread all kinds of harmful ideas. Here are some of the most common ones circulating today:

- That God wants you poor and struggling so you'll stay saved
- That you're destined for defeat and powerless against sin, sickness, and failure
- That pain and suffering are everyone's lot—secretly blessings in disguise
- That you must sin daily in thought, word, or deed, and no one can truly live holy
- That you should just accept these conditions as normal for everyone
- That trying to overcome these problems shows pride and rebellion against God
- That the age of miracles has ended
- That healing isn't part of the atonement or God's will
- That the baptism of the Holy Spirit and spiritual gifts were only for the apostles
- That inspiration has ceased and we don't need supernatural confirmation of God's Word today
- That all supernatural manifestations are just "the flesh"
- That expecting God to do everything you ask is foolish rebellion
- That humans aren't free moral agents
- That God is responsible for your circumstances and whether you're saved or lost

These false doctrines fill thousands of pulpits today. Is it any wonder there's so much unbelief? Is it any wonder Christianity is plagued with confusion and bitter arguments over teachings that should be clear and unified?

This chaos wouldn't exist if everyone who claims to follow Christ would set aside church traditions and personal opinions, and simply return to wholehearted acceptance of the Bible—committing to obey whatever truth they find there.

Anyone can improve their life and experience health, happiness, and prosperity by doing this. The Bible is simple and clear. Even if your pastor won't preach the truth, you can discover it yourself by reading Scripture. If you can read what it says, you can believe what it says—so there's no excuse for not knowing God's truth personally.

Not one of these false teachings has any scriptural support. The promises we've studied in Lessons Ten, Twelve, Fourteen, and Sixteen disprove every single one. If people choose Satan's lies over God's truth, let them settle for their current lot. Let them refuse the gospel's power to transform their lives. Let them dishonor God's character with false doctrine—judgment will come in due time. Let them endure suffering they don't have to bear, when they could simply turn to God and His truth.

## 2. Men Don't Understand They're Free Moral Agents

People fail to receive God's benefits because they don't realize they can choose which master to serve. They don't understand they can escape the devil's work by switching allegiance from Satan to God according to the gospel.

Think of it like this: you're standing at a crossroads every single day, and you get to decide which direction to walk.

We saw in Lesson Eight, Point X, that Adam received full responsibility as Earth's ruler. God's plan was for humanity to enjoy all the blessings of a perfect creation. Lesson Ten showed us that even under the present curse, God wants us to fully enjoy these benefits. Why would He make such provision and give such promises if He didn't intend for us to receive them?

Here's what you need to do if you want these blessings:

First, realize they're actually meant for you—God genuinely wants you to have them. Believe that He'll make sure you receive them if you'll cooperate with Him against demonic powers and false human teachings.

Recognize that you're a free moral agent responsible for losing your dominion. Acknowledge your sinfulness, confess your sins to God, abandon the sin business, and align yourself with the gospel.

Then take an aggressive, victorious stance against Satan's forces—by the Holy Spirit's help and through faith in God and Christ's blood. Overcome these powers and claim the victory that rightfully belongs to you.

If God had His perfect way in your life, you'd automatically have all these blessings without any fight. But you must sever your relationship with evil forces and fight for God's promises to manifest in your life.

Let me be direct: it's a fight to get the full benefits of the gospel. There's no point hiding this from anyone. The devil tried to defeat Christ and the apostles. He wages constant war against everyone who refuses to serve him. He hates God and everyone who serves Him.

Some modern false religions teach you to simply ignore biblical ideas about the devil, sin, and disease—just look to some "God-law inside." This is complete nonsense. It leads you into captivity to the very devil you're ignoring. It makes you cooperate with Satan against God.

Naturally, if the devil can get people to deny the Bible and its truths, to reject Christ and the atonement, he knows they're safely headed toward Hell. He'll cooperate with such deceived souls—even helping them get material benefits that are available to everyone, saint or sinner. He'll even remove sickness and other works of his from such people, just to eventually damn their souls.

The wise path is to acknowledge truth and facts, following God's ways even when it brings opposition from Satan. God has made complete provision to overcome sin, sickness, and all the devil's works. You can get all the material and spiritual benefits that false religions offer—and be certain of eternal life in the end.

You must choose which power will operate in you and which master you'll serve. You can get some benefits from either master, and the more you consecrate yourself to one, the more you become subject to that master's operation in you.

Some people sell out completely to the devil, yielding to demon spirits who possess and assist them in various activities. Others consecrate themselves wholly to God, becoming new creatures, filled and baptized in the Holy Spirit, empowered from on high to destroy the devil's works.

As we saw in Lesson Six, Point X, Satan counterfeits God in every possible way to deceive people. You must be careful to be completely free and biblical in all your religious, spiritual, and material activities—to have the right Spirit in you and the right power working through you.

Here's the general rule: any power, doctrine, or spirit that reflects against God's character, rejects Christ, the cross, and blood atonement, or teaches contrary to Bible truth is from the devil and should be rejected immediately.

You're not only responsible for your choice of masters—you're responsible for your condition in life. You don't have to tolerate sin, sickness, poverty, and want. Through God, by faith in His promises and by meeting a few simple conditions, you can eliminate these things and enjoy the fullness of God's goodness.

Review God's plan in Lesson Ten, then start making it personal for your life. Things will change beyond your wildest dreams.

## 3. Unbelief and Too Many Manufactured Conditions

People fail to receive all God has provided because of unbelief and because they create countless conditions they think must be met before God will answer prayer.

Once in a classroom, we asked ministry students to list all the conditions they believed must be met for God to answer every prayer. It was astonishing to hear the scores of conditions they thought were necessary. The whole room seemed filled with nothing but conditions!

When they finished, we said, "It's no wonder we have so few prayers answered. The great wonder is how anyone could ever get a single prayer answered if they had to meet all those conditions."

Then we gave them the all-inclusive condition for answered prayer: "If ye abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you" (Jn. 15:7).

That's really all you have to do. This implies believing God's promises from your heart, living a righteous and holy life in Christ, and expecting God to answer when you pray. Anyone who does this without wavering in faith is just as certain to receive what they've asked for as they are certain they're alive.

You must "ask in faith, nothing wavering . . . and it shall be given him" (Jas. 1:5-8). You "must believe that he [God] is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him" (Heb. 11:6).

Is this too much to ask? Is this too much to do in order to get what you want in life? God is asking very little. Why shouldn't He require unwavering faith? Why should He freely give to people who don't believe He'll keep His Word? Should anyone expect God to answer on any other basis?

God has never—not once in all human history—failed to keep a single promise. There's no reasonable excuse for unbelief. People who don't choose faith are freely choosing unbelief. There's no neutral ground.

People easily believe Satan's suggestions to doubt God's faithfulness. But you can choose to believe God instead of Satan. And God can—and does—expect this belief before He delivers what He's promised.

## 4. The Greatness of the Promises Overwhelms People

It's true that God's promises are staggering when you first encounter them. But the more you meditate on them and experience them personally, the more simple and comprehensive they become.

Don't stumble over the greatness of the promises—the God behind them is infinitely great! He created everything and can do anything we ask or think, according to His power working in us.

It's similar to a small child struggling to understand that their wealthy parents can easily provide for them—the child's limited perspective makes generosity seem impossible.

Let's learn to accept God's statements at face value and boldly claim the full benefits of His provision. We are greatly loved—or such provision would never have been made.

Nothing is impossible for the believer who will believe. Don't tolerate the slightest doubt or suggestion that God won't hear and answer. If you do, you're already defeated.

James says of the doubter: "he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed. For let not that man think that he shall receive anything of the Lord. A double-minded man is unstable in all his ways" (Jas. 1:5-9).

There's no limitation to the gospel's provision for body, soul, or spirit—for any legitimate need or want. The promises are too plainly unlimited to be limited by humans. If there were limitations, God should have made them. Since He didn't, how dare anyone limit them if they want what's freely given?

Because there's no limitation to the promises or the abundance promised, make sure you don't limit yourself and your faith. Don't bring condemnation on yourself by lowering the gospel standard or questioning God's goodness.

## 5. Not Understanding Moral Obligation

Sometimes your supply is cut short because you don't understand your moral obligation and don't put forth every effort to live up to it. You are subject to moral law and moral government, under moral obligation to live accordingly.

**Moral law** is simply the rule of action for moral agents, established by recognized authority to enforce justice and prescribe duty. It governs free and intelligent action, as opposed to bondage and unintelligent action. It declares what a moral agent ought to choose, will, and intend.

**The purpose of moral law** is to reveal and declare the rule of moral action for free wills—concerning the highest good of being and the universe—and to govern acts and intentions in relation to God and His eternal plan.

**Moral law is unchangeable.** It can never vary in requiring that all free wills consecrate themselves to the same end God pursues—the highest good of the universe and everything in it.

All free moral agents are obligated to keep moral law because it governs the best good of being. Whatever harms being and society is clearly illegal and unwise, and must be prohibited. Free moral agents must choose to cooperate for the public good and oppose whatever brings misery and hardship. It's a mutual plan benefiting each person and the whole universe, and everyone is naturally obligated to maintain it according to their understanding.

**Moral government** is necessary to direct, guide, and control moral agents. It must include rewards for good deeds and punishment for evil, or no society could function harmoniously for long. There must be standards everyone conforms to, proving themselves worthy members of society. There must be ways of dealing with rebels who work to destroy it.

Law without penalties and rewards isn't really law—it's just advice that free wills can accept or reject without consequences. Moral government is obligated to faithfully execute moral law for the good of everyone governed.

**A moral governor** is also necessary—someone whose will and decisions are considered infallible by all subjects. He must be authoritative, not merely advisory. He must maintain his subjects' respect through faithful, unselfish execution of duties. He must be able to dispense penalties and rewards with justice and holiness.

Only one person in the universe is qualified for this role: **God**. His natural attributes, perfect moral character, relationship to the universe as Creator and source of all, and history of absolute justice qualify Him as Supreme Moral Governor. Since His glory and our good depend on mutual conformity to the same end, He's also obligated to keep moral law and require His subjects to be holy, consecrated to the same goal of securing the highest good for everyone.

**Free moral agency** consists of intellect, sensibility, and free will—these form the foundation of moral obligation. The intellect includes reason and self-determination. The sensibility includes self-consciousness, feelings, emotions, desires, passions, and appetites. Free will is the power to choose concerning moral law—the faculty of choosing good or evil without compulsion. It was originally created in humanity and will exist through all eternity.

**The basis of right choice** is always acting and choosing for the best good of all. Nothing happens in life except by someone's choice. Right choices that effect the greatest public and private good are all-important.

Moral obligation extends to ultimate acts of will—the intention. You're obligated to choose holiness as the means to the best good and happiness of being. You're obligated to have faith instead of unbelief, to choose God instead of the devil, good instead of evil, and living for others instead of self.

**The basis of justice** is the intention to act and choose for the best good. Even civil governments judge based on intent to do good or evil. The Bible values intention more than results or outward actions (2 Cor. 8:12). All vice and virtue come from the heart (Mk. 7:15-23; Mt. 7:19-21; Jas. 1:13-15; 3:9-14). Where the heart is right, all is considered right; where the heart is evil, all is considered evil.

Even sinners do some outwardly good things, but their hearts aren't right. The intention is generally selfish, and good acts don't change the heart.

Since the foundation of moral obligation is the highest good of all, entire consecration of free wills to secure this end constitutes obedience to moral law. Obedience must be whole and complete. You cannot choose the best good of others while simultaneously choosing self-gratification.

God cannot tolerate half-heartedness in choice and service (Rev. 3:15-16; Mt. 22:36-40). He cannot justify partial obedience according to your light. You must walk in the light as He is in the light to be cleansed from all sin (1 Jn. 1:7).

If you're constantly falling short of full obedience to known duty, there's never a moment when you're not under the curse of broken law (Jas. 2:10; 4:17). God cannot suspend execution until repentance, forgiveness, and full obedience are realized (Rom. 8:1-4, 13; Gal. 5:16-26; Rom. 6:16-23; Jn. 8:34; 1 Jn. 3:8-10).

If obedience to moral law means entire consecration to secure the best good of all according to your light, then **sin and disobedience** mean entire consecration by free choice to secure self-gratification regardless of others' rights. It's committing your will to serve Satan and the senses instead of God and intelligent moral law. It seeks governance by impulses and passions instead of intelligence and reason.

Self-gratification is the root of all sin. Human selfishness is closely allied to Satan's self-interest. Your will is free to oppose desire and lust contrary to the law, and when it doesn't, sin is committed. Your mind knows its obligation, so choosing contrary to the law isn't ignorance—it's free action bringing condemnation. Selfishness is always unreasonable. It denies true humanity and rational nature. It shows contempt for God's law in human reason.

No sinner chooses reason and common sense, or they would turn to God and quit sinning. Sinners seldom consult reason for their actions. They usually obey lust in stubborn rebellion against moral law and reason. They're lustful in heart whether they obey all lusts or not.

As long as they remain this way, they're condemned before God and need regeneration to bring them to obedience. If they abstain from certain sins, it's for selfish reasons—for loved ones, reputation, fear of punishment or disgrace—not because they're good at heart.

With all this in mind about moral law, moral government, and moral obligation, we can see that it's actually sinful to lack faith in God and to miss the good things He's promised in His Word.

If it's God's will and Word for you to have all the good things of life, then you're under moral obligation to seek accomplishing God's known will to get them. When you're passive, indifferent, and faithless, you're sinning against moral law and government—not living up to your moral obligation to get these benefits so you can be a good example to other moral agents.

It's absolute folly to think you're living up to your moral obligation, being the best example, having faith, obeying God, and securing the best good for yourself and others—while you're weak, sickly, defeated, sinful, corrupt, depraved, helpless, unhappy, and poverty-stricken.

Yet multitudes of Christians think—and are taught—to be satisfied with this lot in life, to patiently endure these limitations, to accept these evils as God's will, and to make no effort to escape these conditions. They actually believe they best glorify God and exemplify Christianity by willingly enduring these sufferings "for Christ's sake."

If they could see they're really enduring evils for the sake of men's false doctrines, they'd realize they're submitting to Satan's program to dishonor God's goodness and cause thousands of sinners to stumble over such "Christian examples"—rejecting a God who would supposedly will this kind of life on His children.

If they genuinely cared about acting "for Christ's sake," they'd embrace God's plan for their lives and receive abundant life through unlimited promises. They'd understand that it's wrong and contrary to moral law to be satisfied with their current lot.

Christians can point to one or two isolated examples in Scripture—like the poor beggar Lazarus—as evidence that God wills saints to endure afflictions. But they need to realize we don't know why these individuals didn't appropriate Old Testament benefits. They didn't have the New Testament promises quoted in Lesson Ten.

If Christians would forget these few exceptions and focus on the scores of examples in both Testaments who were healthy, wealthy, and wise, it would at least show some effort to glorify God.

Anyone with common intelligence knows it would better glorify God to be well and able to do His will in deed and truth, rather than being sick and unable to fulfill His purpose. It would better glorify God to have victory over Satan than to be defeated by him. It would better glorify God to be materially prosperous rather than begging from an ungodly world that laughs at how God "blesses" His children with nothing.

These conditions certainly don't prove God is being glorified. They only prove lack of faith and initiative in people who've been taught false doctrines about God and His providence. These doctrines serve mainly as excuses for unbelief and failure to attain what God provides.

Just as truth sets free, lies and false doctrines bind, enslave, and give false security—making people think they're getting the best of the gospel under fallen conditions when they're not.

## 6. Not Using What They Have for Others

People fail to prosper and receive from God because they don't use what they have for others. They're too selfish and stingy. They have no faith that God will replenish if they give to help others.

They're full of worry and unbelief. They're afraid to launch into new opportunities. They have no vision to tackle anything. They pray, and when God answers with material blessing, they hesitate until the opportunity passes.

They're not diligent in business and lack unselfish motives for gaining wealth, health, and happiness. These conditions must be corrected in your personal life before you can expect anything definite from the gospel's promises.

We'll see in Lesson Twenty that the New Testament program gives every believer full and absolute power over demonic powers—God has made final, complete provision for each person to attain all known needs and wants spiritually, physically, and materially.

In Lesson Twenty-two, we'll prove that every believer has full power of attorney to act in Christ's stead, representing God and demonstrating His power to fallen humanity so they can know there's greater power than the devil and sin.

In Lessons Fourteen, Twenty-three, and Twenty-four, we'll prove you can have bodily healing, health, and freedom from sin in this life. In Lessons Sixteen, Twenty-eight, and Thirty, we'll prove from Scripture how to attain this place in God.

What remains to address here is the believer's material needs. If you think we emphasize this too much, we do so because few, if any, reveal the Bible's true teaching on this aspect of life—which is, after all, a major part of human life and concern. We simply want our students to get every phase of Bible teaching and be well grounded in knowledge and experience.

Why should we withhold this aspect from those we've promised to help? It would be sin on our part to know abundant truth on this subject and fail to make it clear to the multitudes seeking truth, salvation, health, happiness, and prosperity.

No one can be at their best when constantly oppressed and worried about the next car payment, house rent, or enough food and clothes for loved ones. These are great burdens to average people, and it's a shame that all churches don't reveal God's abundant provision for such needs.

It would benefit the churches themselves, and it would bring great glory to God when sinners see believers happy and blessed with health and material things. Conversely, when sinners see saints living far below their own standard—knowing they're prospering more than Christians—it certainly doesn't make them want to exchange their life for the Christian life.

If the full truth about material help from God were taught biblically, preachers wouldn't have to beg for money in every service or use unscriptural means to raise funds. If church members were taught the gospel of prosperity and actually experienced material help from God as they do spiritual and physical help, the minister would only need to remind them: "I've taught you and led you into prosperity. God has been good and prospered you, so come now and meet all the needs of God's work." More and more, God would bless such people of faith (Mal. 3:10-12).

Members who realize God has been good to them—that they have what they have because of His goodness—would gladly give to supply others. After all, this is what money and material things are for.

Note these passages proving this: "And as ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them likewise. For if ye love them which love you, what thank have ye? For sinners also love those that love them. And if ye do good to them which do good to you, what thank have ye? For sinners also do even the same. And if ye lend to them of whom ye hope to receive, what thank have ye? For sinners also lend to sinners, to receive as much again. But love your enemies, and do good, and lend, hoping for nothing again, and your reward shall be great, and ye shall be called the children of the Highest: for he is kind unto the unthankful and to the evil. Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful. Judge not, and ye shall not be judged: condemn not, and ye shall not be condemned; forgive, and ye shall be forgiven. Give, and it shall be given unto you; good measure, pressed down, and shaken together, and running over, shall men give into your bosom. For with the same measure ye mete withal it shall be measured to you again" (Lk. 6:30-38).

This passage not only gives God's law and moral obligation to give, but also reveals laws of prosperity.

Here are more passages on what to do with material gain: "Distributing to the necessity of the saints; given to hospitality . . . love worketh no ill to his neighbor; therefore love is the fulfilling of the law" (Rom. 12:13; 13:10); "If we have sown unto you spiritual things, is it a great thing if we shall reap your carnal things? . . . Even so hath the Lord ordained that they which preach the gospel should live of the gospel" (1 Cor. 9:7-14); "He which soweth sparingly shall reap also sparingly; and he which soweth bountifully shall reap also bountifully. Every man according as he purposeth in his heart, so let him give; not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful [hilarious] giver. And God is able to make all grace abound toward you; that ye, always having all sufficiency in all things, may abound to every good work" (2 Cor. 9:6-15); "Let him that is taught in the word communicate [give of material gain] unto him that teacheth in all good things" (Gal. 6:6); "That they do good, that they be rich in good works, ready to distribute, willing to communicate" (give of good things to others, 1 Tim. 6:17-19); "I have shewed you all things, how that so labouring ye ought to support the weak, and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, how he said, It is more blessed to give than to receive" (Acts 20:35); "Let him that stole steal no more: but rather let him labour, working with his own hands the thing which is good, that he may have to give to him that needeth" (Eph. 4:28).

"But whoso hath this world's good, and seeth his brother have need, and shutteth up his bowels of compassion from him, how dwelleth the love of God in him? My little children, let us not love in word, neither in tongue, but in deed and in truth. And hereby we know we are of the truth, and shall assure our hearts before him . . . And whatsoever we ask we receive of him, because we keep his commandments, and do those things that are pleasing in his sight" (1 Jn. 3:17-22).

If all Christians would have faith in God and follow the simple laws of prosperity in these passages and the following supplement, God would bless them abundantly—with more and more to help others. The all-inclusive law of prosperity is to consecrate yourself to helping others and use what you have, and God will give the increase.

God will make ways and present opportunities for success. Don't be afraid to launch into new ventures as you learn God's will through prayer and as possibilities arise. Have faith—reckless faith if necessary—and God will honor it. Then as you use material gains for others' good, God will enlarge your capacity for more, making you an ever-increasing channel of supply and blessing.

Picture it this way: it's like a water pipe—the more freely you let water flow through, the more the source keeps filling it. But if you pinch the pipe shut, the flow stops entirely.

You must start with love for doing things for others. Love is the beginning of all worthwhile achievements in life. You cannot love God and keep the first commandment without also keeping the second.

Jesus said, "Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with all thy heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy mind. This is the first and great commandment. And the second is like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself. On these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets" (Mt. 22:34-40).

If you obey these commandments, you'll observe the golden rule. You'll be interested in helping others succeed. You'll work for others. You'll sacrifice self-interest for others' interests. You'll make many friends, and you cannot make friends without gaining prosperity yourself. Your friends will want to help you succeed in business.

Don't forget to seize opportunities that come your way. Make the best of them—always intending to do good with what God prospers you with. Give without thought of getting. Serve without thought of reward. You will succeed spiritually, physically, and financially.

The law of Christ is: "whosoever will save his life shall lose it, but whosoever will lose his life for my sake shall find it" (Mt. 16:25). In doing things for Christ, you do things for others: "Inasmuch as ye have done it unto one of the least of these my brethren, ye have done it unto me" (Mt. 18:1-10; 25:34-40).

Christ taught that the law of increase is using what we have. The man with five talents gained five more; the one with two talents gained two more; but the one with one talent hid it and gained nothing. "For unto everyone that hath shall be given, and he shall have abundance; but from him that hath not [did not use what he had, thinking it too small] shall be taken away even that which he hath" (Mt. 25:14-30).

The widow in Elisha's day simply used what she had, and there was great increase (2 Ki. 4:1-7). She wanted the money to pay debts and save her children. God met her faith, and she had more than enough.

Any legitimate desire for any good thing in life is perfectly normal and right. God will not withhold it, provided proper unselfishness and faith are used. The selfish person always asks, "What will I gain?" The unselfish person asks, "How can I help others when I get what I want?"

Those who go through life solely getting instead of giving seldom acquire prosperity. When we start with what we can do for others, there's every chance for success.

Look forward to real service to others. Soon your hands will be full of good deeds and loving memories, and others will give back to you (Lk. 6:30-38). Look for work that helps others. Sell goods and services that solve people's problems. Give sound, godly, biblical advice to everyone you meet.

Be honest in the smallest business details. Thank God for blessings you need and want. Have faith they're yours. Make plans for proper, unselfish use of blessings. Be pleasant and happy before others. Be thankful for your work.

Don't try to reach the top in one day. Be diligent in business, cheerful in giving, merciful in dealings, kind and sympathetic and understanding to all. Put your faith in God and His promises. Claim their benefits. Trust God in adversity. Let any failure become a stepping-stone to something greater.

Use your influence for good, for God, and for lost souls. Help those in sickness and need. Pray earnestly to succeed and be blessed with all the good things of life.

Above all, get right with God and consecrate yourself to the best good of being and of the universe—and you will prosper in all things along with God.

Believe God wants you to have prosperity, health, and the good things of life. Believe they are His will for you. Live in faith for the things you want, for "Faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen" (Heb. 11:1). It's the life principle that "calleth those things which be not as though they were" (Rom. 4:12-24).

Faith is making a blueprint of what you want and working toward that end. Before you get what you want, act as though you already have it. Visualize it as yours. See it with your mind and heart. Put forth every effort to bring it about.

Turn over to God the things you cannot do—He will bring them to pass. Just relax and have faith in God for matters where you're helpless. When you worry and fear that events won't work out, you actually hinder God from working things out for you.

## 7. Not Following the Laws of Answered Prayer

People fail to get what they want from God because they don't faithfully follow the laws of answered prayer as Scripture plainly states. If you want answers to prayer, faithfully observe these laws:

> (1) Study the Bible promises until you're convinced your need is covered by them and it's God's will for you to have what you desire (1 Jn. 5:14-15).

> (2) Concentrate on eliminating all selfishness concerning what you want; desire it for the good of being and of others (1 Jn. 3:17-24).

> (3) Be sure you're right with God, in Christ, and His Words are in you, according to Jn. 15:7; 2 Cor. 5:17.

> (4) Be sure you want it badly enough to firmly resist doubt and the devil (Jas. 4:7; 1 Pet. 5:8-9).

> (5) Ask in faith, nothing wavering (Heb. 10:19-23; 11:6; Jas. 1:4-8).

> (6) Never be fearful of the outcome. Simply cast all your anxiety and worry on God (1 Pet. 5:

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Human Government
lessons.content:
# The Dispensation of Human Government

**(Gen. 8:15–12:1)**

## THE PRESENT AGE (Gen. 8:14—Rev. 19:10)

### THE PRESENT OR POST-DILUVIAN AGE

**(Gen. 8:15—Rev. 19:21)**

This age stretches from the flood all the way to the Millennium. It's a long stretch of time, and within it we find four distinct dispensations:

> 1. The Dispensation of Human Government—from the flood to Abraham (Gen. 8:15–11:9).

> 2. The Dispensation of Promise—from Abraham to Moses (Gen. 12:1—Exod. 12:37).

> 3. The Dispensation of Law—from Moses to Christ (Exod. 12:38—Mt. 2:25).

> 4. The Dispensation of Grace—Christ to the Millennium (Mt. 3:1—Rev. 19:21).

## I. The Definition of Human Government

So why do we call this the Dispensation of Human Government? Because right after the flood—after that long age of freedom—God set up something new: human government by law.

He gave Noah specific laws to govern the race, and from that point on, humanity was responsible for governing itself (Gen. 9:1-7). Here's what God laid out:

> 1. "Be fruitful and multiply, and replenish the earth" (Gen. 9:1, 7).

> 2. "Into thine hand are they [animals] delivered" (Gen. 9:2).

> 3. "Every moving thing [clean or unclean] that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things" (Gen. 9:3).

> 4. "The blood thereof [of animals] shall ye not eat" (Gen. 9:4).

> 5. "Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he him" (Gen. 9:5-6).

> 6. "I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you; and with every living creature that is with you . . . for perpetual generations . . . the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is upon the earth" (Gen. 9:8-17).

These few laws have formed the foundation for all laws—divine and human—in every age since the flood. This is when God instituted capital punishment, and here's the thing: He has never revoked it. It continues as an eternal law and will even be in force during the Millennium (Isa. 11:4-9; 65:20-25).

Paul reaffirms this plainly in Rom. 13:1-6, teaching that even in this Age of Grace, law-enforcement officers are ordained of God. They are His ministers who do not bear "the sword in vain" but are supposed to "execute wrath upon him that doeth evil."

Think of it this way: just like a family can't function if parents never address wrongdoing, societies can't survive without consequences for crime. Without punishment for wrongdoing, no human government could endure for long.

Human governments are part of God's moral government. The highest good of all creation is God's chief aim in moral government, and civil and family governments are necessary to accomplish that goal. This isn't just clear in Gen. 9 and Rom. 13—you'll find it throughout Scripture (Dan. 2:21; 4:17-25; 5:21; 1 Pet. 2:13-14).

It's the duty of all people to help establish and support human government for the preservation of society. Every person has a moral obligation to use their influence and power to promote good government that conforms to God's law and serves the highest good of all people.

Now, administrators who rule contrary to God's law and the best good of all will eventually face God's punishment for misusing their authority. God never approves selfish and wicked administration, and if it continues too long, it will be overthrown.

We're obligated to obey government when its requirements don't conflict with God's moral law—regardless of the personal character or motives of the ruler. As Scripture says, Christians are always to obey "every ordinance of man for the Lord's sake: whether it be to the king, as supreme; or unto governors, as unto them that are sent by him for the punishment of evildoers, and for the praise of them that do well. For so is the will of God, that with well doing ye may put to silence the ignorance of foolish men" (1 Pet. 2:13-17).

Christians shouldn't leave human government entirely to the ungodly. Upholding and assisting good government isn't neglecting the salvation of souls—it's actually one of the greatest means of saving souls! Promoting public and private good creates an environment where people can hear the gospel.

It makes no sense to say that saved people are obligated to obey government while also arguing they should have nothing to do with choosing those who govern.

Many Christians have wrong ideas about government. They think they should never use the legal system against the ungodly and that they should just suffer whatever harm anyone wants to heap on them. But while the Bible does teach that we shouldn't personally avenge our wrongs, it also teaches that avenging such wrongs is exactly what law and government are for (Rom. 13:1-6; 1 Tim. 1:8-10).

Believers aren't supposed to sue each other but rather settle differences within the church, not before unbelievers (1 Cor. 6). But there's no sin in seeing justice done according to the law.

Some Christians argue that if everyone walked in love and holiness, we could do away with all law. But that's not quite right. As we saw in earlier lessons, law and government are necessary even for holy beings filled with love. It's similar to how a loving family still needs house rules—love doesn't eliminate the need for clear guidelines.

Law is the rule of duty that distinguishes right from wrong between moral beings. Without it, there could be no holy, loving society where righteousness prevails. Law will always continue as the standard of right in all eternal societies. And as long as law remains, there must be penalties—otherwise law becomes mere advice. It will always be the duty and right of rulers to enforce law and administer punishment when needed.

Some Christians argue that political participation is sinful because government is maintained by force, which seems inconsistent with the gospel. But these are false theories. As we've seen, God Himself instituted human government, and Christians are commanded in the New Testament to pay taxes and support government (Rom. 13:1-7; 1 Pet. 2:13-17).

In a democratic government, politics should be an important part of the Christian's religious life. No one can be fully good to the extent of their moral obligations without concerning themselves with governmental affairs.

Now, Christians certainly have better things to do than follow one political party exclusively, take part in any party's evil, or meddle in politics selfishly. But they are obligated to participate in government in a godly manner. Why? Because they're obligated to seek the best good of the society they're part of, to promote the good of all people, and human government is one area of life that materially, morally, and spiritually affects everyone's well-being.

Even if God didn't specifically command it, Christians would still have a duty to help establish and support human government for the best good of all. It's a basic principle of Christianity: whatever is essential to the public and private good of all moral beings, Christians have the right and obligation to pursue.

Both reason and experience, along with Scripture, make this obligatory. Since government is necessary for the best good of all, every citizen is obligated to help bring about the best government possible. When Christians fail to do their duty, they shouldn't complain when government turns bad and the wicked take control. There are enough church members in America to have excellent government in conformity with God's law—if they would all do their duty.

Regarding government by force: this is necessary as long as wicked people refuse to dedicate themselves to their own good and the good of others. Just as God commanded punishment for crime in Gen. 9 and under the Mosaic law, it remains the duty of governments today to impose penalties when it's for the public good. Family governments must punish wrongdoing. God Himself does the same in His moral government.

For Christians to think their faith makes them flawless or exempts them from working toward the goals government is meant to achieve is simply wrong.

Human governments aren't founded on God's arbitrary will but on the genuine needs of human beings to secure the highest good. Picture it this way: if even a small family needs rules and consequences, how much more do communities, states, and nations need them?

Christians, instead of tearing down human governments, are obligated to support them. When government becomes corrupt, it needs reforming—not abandoning. Just as we don't abandon the Church when it needs reformation, we shouldn't abandon government either.

Christians are obligated to support governments when:
- They don't require anything contrary to God's law
- They don't demand acts that violate moral obligation
- They don't force people to violate their conscience
- They meet the needs of the people being governed

On these grounds, both governors and the governed should recognize their relationship and responsibility to each other in maintaining good government. If the right to govern is based on public interest, then the right and duty to use necessary means to govern must be recognized by all.

It's absurd to believe rulers have a right to govern but no right to use the necessary means to carry out government. Making this same error, many Christians object to capital punishment, dealing with mobs, suppressing rebellions, or making war. They think government can always be carried on without taking life. Others go so far as to claim government can function without force to sustain the authority of law.

These positions can't be maintained with any logic, and they're not supported by God or Scripture.

It's absurd to hold that rulers can rule only as long as subjects voluntarily obey—and when they refuse, government should cease to exist. The right to govern can't exist where the right to enforce obedience doesn't exist.

This attitude denies the right to maintain government for everyone's good. It denies the right to use force to execute law or take life to preserve government. This theory claims that governments should love others too much to use force—or that society should be loved too much to execute law upon those dedicated to destroying the good.

Such thinking overlooks the very foundation of moral obligation, which requires everyone to dedicate themselves to the public good. It destroys all morality and religion. It's foolish to think enlightened love should forbid necessary execution of law.

Some claim the commandment "thou shalt not kill" means no human life should ever be taken for any crime. But this actually sets itself against God's Word while pretending to follow it.

This commandment, taken literally, would forbid killing animals or plants as much as humans. The question is: what kind of killing does it forbid? Certainly not all killing of human beings, because the very next chapter (Exod. 21) commands taking human life for certain crimes.

Six times in that one chapter—and scores of times elsewhere—God commanded the death penalty. Many times God commanded Israel to kill the giant races and enemies of God in the promised land. Clearly, the commandment against murder doesn't apply to capital punishment or war.

Governments have an undoubted right to do whatever is necessary for the highest public good. Nothing is wrong with taking human life when necessary to accomplish the purpose of government. The Bible recognizes this right everywhere—and even if it didn't, this right would still exist.

No one has a right to keep their life when they forfeit it by breaking a law carrying the death penalty. Whenever someone dedicates themselves to destroying the public good, and taking their life becomes necessary to secure that good, their life is forfeited. It's the government's duty to take it.

The same principle applies to mobs, insurrections, rebellions, and wars that endanger the good of all. It must be both the right and duty of government and its citizens to use every possible means to suppress rebellion and war—which are simply crime on a massive scale.

That war is necessary under certain circumstances is clear because moral law permits it and God has commanded it. If it were wrong, God wouldn't have commanded it. Rulers are God's ministers to execute wrath upon the ungodly and preserve moral law and government for everyone's good. They're obligated to make war if that's the only way to attain the highest public good.

Under any other circumstances, war is a horrible crime. Selfish war is wholesale murder, and participating in it makes you complicit in mass murder—unless it becomes God's will and the only way to preserve society.

Christians aren't obligated to support unjustly waged wars. On the other hand, just as individual criminals must be punished, there may be times when criminal nations must be punished for society's good as a whole.

Our duty isn't to judge when this becomes necessary just for ourselves but for the world as a whole. When organized crime—whether a small group, a nation, or multiple nations—determines to destroy society, it becomes necessary to suppress that crime just as much as with individuals. Nations are bound by the same moral law as individuals and must be punished when they commit crimes.

Thus God, in replenishing the race after the flood, instituted human government and capital punishment for crime. Since He never changes, this is still His will for people and governments. God knows how to rule rebellious free moral agents and curb crime. If human governments were as strict as God commands, there would be infinitely less crime.

## II. The Length of Human Government (Gen. 11:10-32)

This dispensation lasted from the flood to Abraham's call—a period of 427 years, proven by Gen. 11:10-32 as follows:

| Generation | Years | Event | Reference |
|------------|-------|-------|-----------|
| 1. Shem | 2 years after the flood | begat Arphaxad | Gen. 11:10 |
| 2. Arphaxad | 35 years | begat Salah | Gen. 11:12 |
| 3. Salah | 30 years | begat Eber | Gen. 11:14 |
| 4. Eber | 34 years | begat Peleg | Gen. 11:16 |
| 5. Peleg | 30 years | begat Reu | Gen. 11:18 |
| 6. Reu | 32 years | begat Serug | Gen. 11:20 |
| 7. Serug | 30 years | begat Nahor | Gen. 11:22 |
| 8. Nahor | 29 years | begat Terah | Gen. 11:24 |
| 9. Terah | 205 years | died at Haran | Gen. 11:32 |

**TOTAL: 427 years.** Abraham was 75 years old at this time.

Shem was still alive and about 525 years old when 75-year-old Abraham was called to leave Ur of the Chaldees for Canaan. Shem lived as a contemporary of Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and Melchisedec (Gen. 11:10-32; 12:4; 25:7, 20-26).

## III. The Favorable Beginning (Gen. 8:15–9:17)

Noah was 601 years old—ripe in experience and wisdom. All the family were fully grown and married. They had so much going for them:

- The warning of the flood behind them
- The eternal covenant (Gen. 8:20–9:17)
- Knowledge of true worship (Gen. 8:21-22)
- God's will concerning human government and its laws (Gen. 9:1-7)
- The promise of eternal preservation for humans and animals on Earth (Gen. 8:21-22; 9:1-17)
- Full control of the Earth and responsibility to govern it for God (Gen. 9:1-7)
- Full liberty to govern themselves

They had countless favorable conditions under which to prove true to God.

## IV. The Test of Man in Human Government (Gen. 9:1-7)

The test for humanity in this age was to obey the laws of human government, prove true to God in executing these laws, remain faithful in governing the race for everyone's highest good, and have faith in God while dedicating their lives to His will according to the light they received.

## V. The Purpose of God in Human Government (Gen. 9:1-7)

God's purpose in this age was to give humanity another chance to prove true to their responsibility according to His known will.

God had tested humanity in a state of innocence to see if they would prove worthy of higher glory—and they failed. He had tested humanity in the long age of freedom to see how they would follow their own conscience about right and wrong—and they failed.

Now God's purpose was to give humanity some definite laws governing their actions concerning good and evil. Since humanity had failed to choose good without law, they would now be compelled by law to do good and refuse evil.

God purposed for humanity to have self-government, so He gave them all necessary laws to govern themselves. Since He planned for people to know Him personally and obey His will, He revealed the means of reconciliation.

He had Noah preserve seven clean animals for sacrifice so people could show their faith in the blood of the coming Redeemer and be reconciled to God. God planned for humanity to preserve pure Adamite stock so the seed of the woman could come into the world, bringing about full redemption of the Earth according to His eternal purpose.

## VI. The Means of God in Accomplishing This Purpose (Gen. 9:1-6)

The laws of human government were established so humanity could rule itself in righteousness and justice forever. True worship of God began, and God's full revelation of His will for this period was made known so people could be fully reconciled to God and eternally redeemed.

All the giant races had been destroyed in the flood, leaving only eight pure Adamites, so human stock could remain pure until the seed of the woman could come to complete the work of eliminating all rebellion and fully restoring humanity's dominion.

Everything necessary to accomplish God's will and humanity's highest good was within their control, so they could prove true to God during this probationary period.

## VII. The Failure of Man in Human Government (Gen. 9:18–11:9)

### 1. THE FAILURE OF NOAH (Gen. 9:20-24)

Noah, a preacher of righteousness (2 Pet. 2:5), who had condemned drunkenness before the flood, became drunk himself and was shamefully uncovered in his tent.

This perhaps wasn't deliberate on his part, but it happened. Whether a sin is deliberate or not, it's still sin. Noah was old enough and had witnessed enough drunkenness before the flood to have no excuse for this condition. Regardless of all his godly living before and after this incident, the fact remains: he failed God and humanity on this occasion.

### 2. THE FAILURE OF HAM (Gen. 9:22-27)

Ham sinned by revealing his aged father's shame. He showed lack of honor to his parents and broke the law of parental authority. For this he was cursed to be "a servant of servants" to his brethren (Gen. 9:25).

### 3. THE FAILURE OF MANKIND IN GENERAL (Gen. 9:1-6; 11:1-9)

In Gen. 9:1-6 we have God's definite laws for humanity in this period. In Gen. 11:1-9 we see humanity's deliberate rebellion against God and His laws.

People disobeyed God's command to spread across the Earth, to worship Him properly, and to live godly and upright in the fear of God. Pride, self-boastfulness, and hero-worship became prevalent in this age. Nimrod was the most conspicuous example of this failure (Gen. 10:8-9; 1 Chron. 1:10).

Josephus says, "Nimrod persuaded mankind not to ascribe their happiness to God, but to think that his own excellency was the source of it. And he soon changed things into tyranny, thinking there was no other way to wean men from God and fear of Him, than by making them rely upon his own power."

Nimrod founded a kingdom in Shinar with four great cities—Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh (Gen. 10:8-10). In those days, wild beasts were so numerous they became a menace to life and property (Exod. 23:29; Lev. 23:22). Nimrod became a leader by hunting down and killing these beasts.

He taught people to build walls around cities and dwellings for protection from wild animals at night. He became the great leader and benefactor of humanity—which gave him the opportunity to lead people away from God.

He inspired people to build a city and tower to reach Heaven and make a great name for themselves. He taught them to centralize instead of spreading across the Earth as God commanded. He defied God to send another flood and taught people to seek vengeance on God for flooding their ancestors.

Nimrod and his queen Semiramis started the "Babylonian Cult," a new religion claiming the highest wisdom and ability to reveal the most divine secrets. The purpose of this religion was to rule the world and keep people in blind obedience to the Supreme Pontiff.

Once admitted as members, people were no longer Babylonians, Assyrians, or Egyptians—they became members of a mystical brotherhood under a high priest whose word was final in all their affairs.

The objects of worship were the Supreme Father, the Queen of Heaven, and her Son. The last two were really the only objects of worship since the Supreme Father supposedly didn't interfere with mortal affairs.

Semiramis was a paragon of unbridled lust and was worshipped as "Rhea, the Mother of the Gods." Prostitution was compulsory in this religion. She made Babylon the seat of idolatry and sin.

The so-called Christian festivals such as Christmas, Lady Day, Easter, and Lent, and many other modern rituals like the sign of the cross, the Rosary, monastic orders, and certain church rituals all came from this Babylonian religion—observed centuries before Christ. For proof, see the Encyclopedia Britannica, the book of Nimrod III, page 239; Chronican Paschale Vol. 1, page 65; Hesiod Theogonia, Vol. 36, page 453; Herodotus, book 1, cap. 199, page 92; and Two Babylons by Hyslop.

In Nimrod's days, this cult gained a great hold on the whole race before the confusion of tongues scattered people abroad. This explains why religions in all heathen lands have similar traditions and practices. Changes developed over the ages to suit various peoples after they were scattered. (See our book Revelation Expounded for details.)

This religion was Satan's attempt to counterfeit true worship and satisfy people's religious nature. His effort to rule through Nimrod was his first attempt to raise up a universal ruler through whom he could control the entire race. It failed because God raised up Israel to defeat this plan.

Many other attempts have been made since to control the race through one absolute ruler, but all have failed. Satan will make one last effort through the future Antichrist, who will attempt to become a worldwide dictator. He too will fail because Christ will return from Heaven to defeat him before he takes all Jerusalem—much less all the nations—as we'll see in later lessons.

There's a Babylonian description of the tower of Babel discovered in 1876. According to this description, there was a grand court about 900 x 1,156 feet and a smaller one 450 x 1,056 feet. Around the court were six gates leading to temples. Beyond that was a platform with walls and four gates on each side, enclosing a large building about 200 feet square.

Around the tower's base were many small shrines dedicated to various gods. On the East were sixteen shrines; on the North, two temples; on the South, one temple; and on the West, the principal buildings including a double house with a court between two wings 58 feet wide. The wings measured 34 x 166 feet and 108 x 166 feet. In these western chambers was the main god's couch (about 7 x 15 feet), a golden throne, and other valuable objects.

In the center stood the tower itself, built in stages that decreased from bottom to top, each square:

- First stage: 300 feet square, 110 feet high
- Second stage: 260 feet square, 60 feet high
- Third stage: 200 feet square, 20 feet high
- Fourth stage: 170 feet square, 20 feet high
- Fifth stage: 140 feet square, 20 feet high
- Sixth stage: 110 feet square, 20 feet high
- Seventh stage: 80 feet long, 60 feet wide, 50 feet high—containing a sanctuary for the God Bel-Merodach

The total height was 300 feet, matching the breadth of its base.

The tower was apparently completed since Scripture doesn't mention any work stoppage on it—only on the city (Gen. 11:4, 8). The phrase "reach unto heaven" literally means a high tower. Building a tower that could literally reach the planet Heaven, millions of miles away, would be impossible. The sun is 93 million miles away, and Heaven is beyond that.

The tower and city were Nimrod's project, stopped by the confusion of tongues. One ancient Babylonian tablet reads: "The building of this illustrious tower offended the gods. In a night they threw down what they had built. They scattered them abroad, and made strange their speech. Their progress was impeded. They wept hot tears for Babylon."

### The Second Eruption of the Sons of God

As we saw in an earlier lesson, there were two eruptions of fallen angels who married the daughters of men and produced two races of giants—one before the flood and one after. This is plainly stated in Gen. 6:4, "and also after that"—meaning after the flood, the sons of God married daughters of men and they bore children who were giants.

The second eruption occurred in this dispensation, for when Abraham entered the land at the beginning of the next age, "the Canaanite was then [already] in the land" (Gen. 12:6; 13:7).

The Canaanites were offspring of the sons of God and daughters of men after the flood, as seen in earlier lessons. This second eruption happened soon after the flood, reflecting Satan's quick work to always defeat God if possible.

The giant couch in Nimrod's temple suggests giants were already on Earth in Nimrod's time—about 100 years after the flood, or the second generation according to Gen. 10:6-10. The second generation in Shem's line was Salah, born 37 years after the flood. If Nimrod was born around that time, he rose to prominence at least 100 years after the flood.

This explains how the whole race was so deep in sin by Abraham's time, 427 years after the flood. This period, like the Age of Conscience, is referred to in Rom. 1:17-32.

God had promised not to send another flood, so Satan attempted again to corrupt pure Adamite stock so the seed of the woman, Christ, couldn't come to destroy Satan's hold on the race.

Since God couldn't kill the giants by flood, He had to use other means. God's plan was to raise up a strong nation through Abraham to kill the second race of giants as the flood had destroyed the first.

Thus the race became sinful and idolatrous, setting the stage for the next part of God's program, which we'll study in the coming lessons.

## VIII. The Judgment of God upon Man in Human Government

In Gen. 11:1-9, we learn God sent confusion of tongues upon humanity and scattered them across the Earth. This was the first judgment for failure to carry out His will and purpose.

By doing this, God broke up humanity's united rebellion against Him and fulfilled His original purpose of spreading people across the Earth.

The second judgment came in Peleg's days—he died 340 years after the flood. This judgment was the division of the Earth into islands and continents, as explained in an earlier supplement.

This division happened after the confusion of tongues and the scattering of people (Gen. 11:9). Before this, the whole race spoke one language and lived centralized in one place (Gen. 11:1). After they scattered, the continents divided—explaining how different branches of the race are found on various continents and islands.

Think of it like this: take a world map and remove the Atlantic Ocean, and the eastern parts of the Americas fit perfectly with the western parts of Europe and Africa. When exactly during Peleg's life the Earth divided isn't known—and it doesn't matter. The fact is, it happened in his days, explaining how the Indians got to the Americas and how other peoples reached various parts of the divided Earth.

Seeing He couldn't deal with humanity as a whole, God gave them up and turned to Abraham and his descendants, dealing almost exclusively with them during the next two ages. This abandonment of humanity in general to further His plan was the worst judgment that could have come to the human race.

This is how this age ended. This is how the stage was set for the next phase of God's dealings with the human race through the chosen family.

## IX. God's Provision of Redemption (Gen. 8:20; 12:8)

God's provision of redemption remained the same as in previous dispensations. Salvation was available to all who would believe in the blood of Christ that was to be shed. Animal sacrifices were tokens of faith for those who conformed to God's redeeming will.

---

## Questions on Lesson Thirteen

- Define "The Present Age." How many dispensations are included in it?

- Why is this age called "Human Government"?

- Name six main laws of this period that were proposed by God to rule man.

- Prove from Scripture that God has an eternal plan for man.

- What will be the eternal relationship between natural generations and the resurrected saints of all ages?

- Who instituted capital punishment for certain crimes?

- Has God ever revoked the law of capital punishment? Will He ever revoke it?

- Could human governments long endure without punishments for crime?

- Are human governments part of the moral government of God?

- What is the chief aim of moral government?

- Why were human governments instituted?

- What is the moral obligation of all men concerning human governments?

- Should Christians abandon their moral obligation in times when civil rulers become corrupt? What should they do?

- How long and to what extent should Christians obey human governments?

- Is helping promote good government neglecting the salvation of souls?

- Should Christians go to law with each other? Prove.

- Is it biblical for them to go to law with the ungodly in cases of necessity?

- Could all laws be dispensed with if all beings were holy? Why?

- Will God have eternal laws for eternal societies? Why?

- Could there be law without penalties? Why?

- Is it sinful and unbiblical for Christians to partake in politics? Why?

- What is one of the first principles of Christianity?

- Is it biblical to carry on government by force?

- Did God demand one kind of righteousness in the Old and another kind in the New Testament? Prove.

- When is it the duty of governments to inflict penalties?

- Upon what basis are human governments founded?

- Upon what grounds should governors and those governed realize their relationship and responsibility to each other?

- Is it biblical for government to make war? Upon what grounds?

- Are war and murder the same thing? Why?

- What broad powers do governments have the right to exercise?

- When does war or participation in war become a crime?

- What kind of wars can Christians justly support?

- What is the length of the Dispensation of Human Government?

- State the favorable beginning of man in Human Government.

- What was the test for man in Human Government?

- What was the purpose of God in Human Government?

- What were the means of God in accomplishing this purpose?

- Explain the failure of Noah, Ham, and mankind in general.

- Who was the great leader and rebel against God in this age?

- State fully his rebellion against God.

- How did he gain such leadership among men?

- What kind of religion did he start? What was its purpose? Did it succeed?

- What was Satan's purpose in this religion? Has he ever succeeded? Will he ever succeed in this purpose?

- State the general plan for the city and tower of Babel.

- Was the tower ever completed? The city?

- Did man actually conceive the idea to build a tower to reach Heaven?

- When did the second eruption of the sons of God take place?

- What was the result of the marriages of the sons of God and daughters of men?

- What was Satan's purpose in this?

- Explain fully the judgments of God upon men in Human Government.

lessons.title: Divine Healing and Divine Health
lessons.content:
# Divine Healing and Divine Health

Divine healing and health is one of the most remarkable subjects you'll find in the entire Bible. What follows is a focused yet thorough look at what Scripture actually teaches about this doctrine. If you're someone who needs healing or wants to walk in lasting physical health, this lesson could be life-changing for you.

## I. What Divine Healing and Health is Not

Let's start by clearing up what divine healing is *not*. It's not healing through natural remedies, imagination, willpower, or personal magnetism.

It's not metaphysics, demonology, or spiritualism. It's not a promise of immunity from death or an excuse for presumption or insubordination toward God's will.

It's also not "mind over matter," a denial of the reality of sin, sickness, and disease, or simply the body's natural ability to heal itself through inherent laws and creative powers.

## II. What Divine Healing and Health Is

So what *is* divine healing and health? It's a definite act of God—something He does through your faith in Jesus, by the power of the Holy Spirit, the Word of God, and the precious blood of Christ.

Through this work, the human body is cured, healed, repaired, and delivered from sickness and its power. The result? You're made as whole, sound, and healthy as you were before the attack came.

## III. Is Divine Healing and Health Scriptural?

Absolutely. There are at least fourteen Hebrew and Greek words found in hundreds of Scriptures that plainly teach the ideas of full salvation, deliverance, preservation, soundness, healing, health, and wholeness—for body, soul, and spirit through the gospel.

These words are used many times for the body *as well as* the soul. Sometimes they appear in the same passages referring to both, proving they aren't limited to spiritual healing as some modern Bible students believe.

There are some passages where these words speak of other things being healed—the soul (Ps. 41:4), a vessel (Jer. 19:11), land (2 Chron. 7:14), waters (Ezek. 47:8-11; 2 Ki. 2:21-22), and backslidings (Jer. 3:22). If healing is meant literally in these cases, then it should also be understood literally when it refers to the human body.

Take a look at how often bodily healing is mentioned in Scripture:

> 1. The Hebrew word **rapha** means and is translated "cure" (Jer. 33:6); "to make whole" (Job 5:18); "to heal" (Num. 12:13; Deut. 32:39; 2 Ki. 20:5, 8; 6:2; Isa. 19:22; 57:18-19; Jer. 17:14; 30:17; Hos. 6:1; Zech. 1:16); "be healed" (Gen. 20:17; Exod. 21:19; Lev. 13:18, 37; 14:3, 48; Deut. 28:27, 35; 1 Sam. 6:3; 9:15; 2 Chron. 22:6; 30:20; Ps. 30:2; 107:20; Isa. 6:10; 53:5; Jer. 17:14; Ezek. 34:4; Hos. 7:1; 11:3); "healeth" (Exod. 15:26; Ps. 103:3; 147:3; Isa. 30:26); "be thoroughly healed" (Exod. 21:19); and "physician" (Gen. 50:2; 2 Chron. 16:12; Job 13:4; Jer. 8:22).

> 2. The Hebrew **marpay** means and is translated "healing" (Jer. 14:19; Mal. 4:2) "health" (Prov. 4:22; 12:18; 13:17; 16:24; Jer. 8:15); "cure" (Jer. 33:6); "remedy" (2 Chron. 36:16; Prov. 6:15; 29:1); "sound" in health (Prov. 14:30); and "wholesome" (Prov. 15:4).

> 3. The Hebrew **yeshuwah** means and is translated "health" (Ps. 42:11; 43:5; 67:2); "deliverance" (Ps. 18:50; 44:4); and "salvation" (Ps. 91:16). The whole 91st Psalm teaches perfect healing and health physically; so the salvation of v. 16 includes the body as well as the soul.

> 4. The Hebrew **arubah** means "restoring to sound health" (Isa. 58:8; Jer. 8:22; 30:17; 33:6).

> 5. The Hebrew **chabash** means "healer" (Isa. 3:7) and "to bind up or heal" (Isa. 30:26; 61:1; Ezek. 34:16; Hos. 6:1; Job 5:18; Ps. 147:3).

> 6. The Hebrew word **rifooth** means "health" (Prov. 3:8).

> 7. The Greek word **therapeuo** means and is translated "heal" (Mt. 8:7; 10:1, 8; 12:10; Mk. 3:2, 15; Lk. 4:23; 6:7; 10:9; 14:3); "healed" (Mt. 4:23-24; 8:16, 17; 9:35; 12:15, 22; 14:14; 15:20; 19:2; 21:14; Mk. 1:34; 3:10; 6:5, 13; Lk. 4:40; 5:15; 6:18; 8:43; 9:6-11; 13:14; 14:4; Acts 4:14; 5:16; 8:7; 28:9); "cure" (Mt. 17:16; Lk. 9:1); and "cured" (Lk. 7:21; Jn. 5:10).

> 8. The Greek **iama** means "healing" or "repairing" (1 Cor. 12:9, 28, 30).

> 9. The Greek **iaomai** means and is translated "heal" (Mt. 13:15; Lk. 4:18; 5:17; 9:2; Jn. 4:47; 12:40); "healed" (Mt. 8:8, 13; Mk. 5:29; Lk. 6:17, 19; 7:7; 8:2, 47; 17:15; 22:51; Jn. 5:13; Acts 3:11; 28:8; Heb. 12:13; Jas. 5:16; 1 Pet. 2:24); "healing" (Acts 10:38); "whole" (Mt. 15:28; Acts 9:24).

> 10. The Greek **iasis** means "healing" and "cure" (Lk. 13:32; Acts 4:22, 30; 28:27).

> 11. The Greek **hugiano** means "sound health" (3 Jn. 2).

> 12. The Greek **hugies** means "healthy," "sound" and "whole" (Mt. 12:13; 15:31; Mk. 3:5; 5:34; Lk. 5:31).

> 13. The Greek **sodzo** means "to save," "deliver," "protect," "heal," "preserve," and "make whole." It is used 86 times in reference to saving the soul, but in the following passages it refers to saving the body and healing it of sickness (Mt. 9:21-22; Mk. 5:29, 34; 6:56; 10:52; Lk. 8:36, 48; 8:50; 17:19; Acts 4:9; 14:9).

> 14. The Greek **dia-sodzo** means "to save" and "to heal thoroughly," "make perfectly whole" and is used of the body being healed (Mt. 14:36; Lk. 7:3).

By reading the 177 Scriptures above—and the many more we'll cover—you can see clearly that both divine healing and divine health are taught in Scripture for everyone who will meet the conditions of faith required by the gospel.

## IV. Cases of Sickness and Healing in the Old Testament

1. **Barrenness of the Philistine Women:** cause—divine intervention for Sarah (Gen. 20:1-7, 18); remedy—prayer (Gen. 20:7, 17); result—healing (Gen. 20:7, 17).

2. **Barrenness of Sarah:** cause—unknown (Gen. 16:1–21:8); remedy—prayer and faith (Gen. 18:10; 21:1-8; Rom. 4:17-22); result—healing (Gen. 21).

3. **Barrenness of Rebekah:** cause—unknown (Gen. 25:19-26); remedy—prayer (Gen. 25:21); result—healing (Gen. 25:21-26).

4. **Isaac's Blindness and Sickness:** cause—old age (Gen. 27:1-2, 41); remedy—none stated, but evidently he was renewed in body by faith (Heb. 11:20); result—lived over twenty more years (Gen. 31:38; 35:27-29).

5. **Barrenness of Rachel:** cause—unknown (Gen. 29:31; 30:1); remedy—prayer (Gen. 30:22); result—healing (Gen. 30:22-24; 35:16-20).

6. **Jacob's Death:** cause—old age (Gen. 47:27-31; 48:1; 49:33); remedy—none, because of the divine appointment for all men to die (Heb. 9:27). The Hebrew word for "sick" in Gen. 48:1 is *calah*, meaning "to be worn," "weak." Jacob died of old age like an apple falling off the tree because of ripeness—not because of disease.

The Bible teaches that all true believers can live to a ripe old age (Ps. 34:12-14; 91:16; 1 Pet. 3:10-12). Not long ago, there was a woman 95 years old who was as healthy as could be expected at that age. One day she told her children she was going to die on a certain day. When that day came, after saying her goodbyes, she simply lay down, folded her hands, and gave up her spirit without a struggle.

This is how Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, and many others passed away. Think of it like this: a person doesn't have to die of a disease any more than an apple has to fall because of worms or rot. It's possible to live in health to a ripe old age and die a natural death. By faith in God, you can ward off many of life's ills and live long before natural death comes.

7. **Boils on the Egyptians:** cause—hardness of heart and rebellion against God (Exod. 4:21; 5:2); remedy—unknown; result—unknown.

8. **All Sicknesses of Israel:** cause—misuse of the tongue and rebellion (Exod. 15:24-26); remedy—the Word of God, prayer, and obedience (Exod. 15:26; Ps. 107:20); result—the whole nation made perfect in health (Ps. 105:37).

9. **Plague in Israel:** cause—misuse of the tongue and idolatry (Exod. 32:1-6); remedy—prayer, atonement, and discipline (Exod. 32:7-28); result—many rebels died, but the rest were delivered from the plague (Exod. 32:27-35).

10. **Plague upon Priests:** cause—drunkenness and disobedience; remedy—none; result—death by judgment from God (Lev. 10:1-9).

11. **Plague in Israel:** cause—misuse of the tongue; remedy—prayer; result—plague stopped (Num. 11:1-3).

12. **Plague in Israel:** cause—misuse of the tongue and lust (Num. 11:4-20); remedy—none because of judgment (Num. 11:19-20); result—death to thousands (Num. 11:33-35).

13. **Plague of Leprosy upon Miriam:** cause—pride and misuse of the tongue (Num. 12:1-10); remedy—prayer and discipline (Num. 12:13-14); result—healing (Num. 12:14-16).

14. **Plague upon the Ten Spies:** cause—unbelief and misuse of the tongue (Num. 13:26-33); remedy—none because of judgment; result—death (Num. 4:37).

15. **Plague in Israel:** cause—misuse of the tongue and rebellion (Num. 14:1-19); remedy—none because of judgment (Num. 14:22-35); result—death (Num. 14:20-35).

16. **Plague in Israel:** cause—pride and misuse of the tongue (Num. 16:1-14); remedy—judgment upon the rebels (Num. 16:21-30); result—death (Num. 16:31).

17. **Plague in Israel:** cause—rebellion and misuse of the tongue (Num. 16:1-14); remedy—judgment upon the rebels (Num. 16:29); result—death (Num. 16:35).
