18. **Plague in Israel:** cause—rebellion and misuse of the tongue (Num. 16:41-42); remedy—atonement and judgment (Num. 16:46-50); result—14,700 were killed and the plague was stayed (Num. 16:49-50).

19. **Plague in Israel:** cause—discouragement and misuse of the tongue (Num. 21:5); remedy—prayer, faith, and confession of sin (Num. 21:7-9); result—healing for all who obeyed and looked on the serpent of brass, a type of Christ (Num. 21:9; Jn. 3:14).

20. **Plague in Israel:** cause—adultery and idolatry; remedy—judgment upon the rebels; result—24,000 were killed and the plague was stayed (Num. 25:1-15).

21. **Barrenness of Hannah:** cause—the Lord caused her to be barren to test her and teach coming generations certain lessons (1 Sam. 1:2-9); remedy—prayer and humility (1 Sam. 1:9-19); result—healing and fruitfulness (1 Sam. 1:17-28).

22. **Plague of Emerods upon the Philistines:** cause—taking the ark of God from Israel; remedy—trespass offering and ark sent back; result—unknown (1 Sam. 5:1–6:21).

23. **Plague in Israel:** cause—looking into the ark; remedy—judgment upon the transgressors; result—many killed and the plague stayed (1 Sam. 6:19-21).

24. **Insanity of Saul:** cause—disobedience and jealousy (1 Sam. 15:22-35; 18:8-30); remedy—inspired music (1 Sam. 16:14; 19:9-10); result—temporary relief (1 Sam. 16:14-23).

25. **Sickness of an Egyptian:** cause—unknown; remedy—care and natural food; result—he was restored to health (1 Sam. 30:11-16).

26. **Sickness and Death of David's Child:** cause—adultery and murder (2 Sam. 12:9-15); remedy—fasting and prayer (2 Sam. 12:16-23); result—prayer unanswered because of punishment for sin (2 Sam. 12:9-15).

27. **Plague in Israel:** cause—David's sin in numbering Israel (2 Sam. 24:1-25); remedy—atonement, confession of sin, and prayer; result—70,000 were killed and the plague was stayed (2 Sam. 24:10-25).

28. **Jeroboam's Withered Hand:** cause—attempted violence to a man of God (1 Ki. 13:4); remedy—prayer; result—healing (1 Ki. 13:6).

29. **Abijah's Sickness:** cause—sins of the parents (1 Ki. 14:8-9); remedy—none because of judgment; result—death (1 Ki. 14:12-18).

30. **Asa's Foot Disease:** cause—unknown; remedy—physicians; result—death for not trusting God (1 Ki. 15:23-24; 2 Chron. 16:12-14; Jer. 17:5-13).

31. **Sickness and Death of the Widow's Son:** cause—unknown; remedy—prayer of Elijah; result—resurrection and healing (1 Ki. 17:8-24).

32. **Ahaziah's Disease:** cause—fell from an upper story; remedy—trust in Baalzebub, the god of flies, instead of the true God; result—death (2 Ki. 1:1-18).

33. **The Shunammite Woman's Barrenness:** cause—unknown; remedy—faith and prophecy; result—healing and fruitfulness (2 Ki. 4:1-17).

34. **Sickness and Death of the Shunammite's Son:** cause—sunstroke; remedy—faith, prayer, and the power of God in Elisha's body; result—resurrection and healing (2 Ki. 4:18-37).

35. **Naaman's Leprosy:** cause—unknown; remedy—trust in God and obedience; result—healing (2 Ki. 5:1-19).

36. **Gehazi's Leprosy:** cause—greed, lying, and disobedience; remedy—none because of the curse upon him; result—no healing (2 Ki. 5:20-27).

37. **Blindness of the Syrian Army:** cause—prayer of Elisha; remedy—prayer of Elisha; result—healing (2 Ki. 6:13-23).

38. **Sickness of Ben-Hadad:** cause—unknown; remedy—unknown; result—might have recovered if he had not been murdered according to prophecy (2 Ki. 8:10-15).

39. **Sickness of Jehoram:** cause—unknown; remedy—unknown; result—recovered but slain by Jehu (2 Ki. 9:14-29).

40. **Sickness of Elisha:** cause—unknown, but could have been old age, for he was nearly 100 years old; remedy—unknown, but if the cause was old age, there could be no remedy; result—death (2 Ki. 13:14-20).

41. **Death of an Israelite:** cause—unknown; remedy—touching the bones of Elisha; result—resurrection and healing (2 Ki. 13:21).

42. **Uzziah's Leprosy:** cause—pride and usurping the priesthood; remedy—none because of judgment; result—no healing (2 Ki. 15:5; 2 Chron. 26:16-21).

43. **Sickness of Hezekiah:** cause—pride and unthankfulness to God (2 Chron. 32:25-31); remedy—prayer and humiliation (2 Ki. 20:3; Isa. 38:2-3); result—healed and fifteen years added to his life (Isa. 39:5).

44. **Jehoram's Bowel Trouble:** cause—many sins (2 Chron. 21:6-7; 10:13; 2 Ki. 8:18-19); remedy—none because of judgment; result—death (2 Chron. 21:12-20).

45. **Plague in Israel:** cause—many sins; remedy—none because of judgment; result—many killed (2 Chron. 21:11-15).

46. **All Sicknesses of Israel:** cause—breaking the law in eating of the Passover unworthily (2 Chron. 30:18); remedy—prayer and confession of sin; result—healing (2 Chron. 30:18-20).

47. **Job's Boils:** cause—Satan (Job 1:6-22; 2:1-10); remedy—prayer and humiliation (Job 42:6-17); result—healing and prosperity (Job 42:6-17; Jas. 5:11).

48. **David's Venereal Disease:** cause—sin (Ps. 38:3); remedy—prayer and confession of sin (Ps. 38:1-22); result—healed (Ps. 6:2; 30:2; 103:3).

49. **Famine in Israel:** (Jer. 14:18); cause—sin and rebellion (Jer. 14:7, 10-22; Deut. 28:15-68); remedy—none because of judgment (Jer. 14:12-18); result—death and captivity (Jer. 14:11-18).

50. **Sickness and Death of Ezekiel's Wife:** cause—made a sign to Israel (Ezek. 24:24); remedy—none; result—death (Ezek. 24:15-27).

51. **Sickness of Daniel:** cause—long fasting; remedy—prayer, rest, and nourishment; result—complete recovery (Dan. 8:27; 10:2-3).

52. **Blindness of Eli:** cause—old age (1 Sam. 4:15).

53. **Blindness of Ahijah:** cause—old age (1 Ki. 14:4).

54. **Blindness of Sodomites:** cause—sin; remedy—none (Gen. 19:1-11).

## V. Cases of Sickness and Healing in the New Testament

1. **Innumerable Cases of Healing by Christ** (Mt. 4:23-24; 8:16-17; 9:35-36; 11:5; 12:15; 14:14, 33-36; 15:30-31; 19:2; 21:14; Mk. 1:32-34, 39; 3:10-12; 6:5, 55-56; Lk. 4:40-41; 6:17-19; 7:21-23; 9:11; Jn. 2:23; 20:30-31; 21:25); cause—the devil and demons (Acts 10:38; 1 Jn. 3:8; Mk. 5; 16:17; Lk. 13:14); remedy—faith and the anointing with the Holy Spirit (Isa. 11:2; 42:1-7; 61:1-2; Mt. 12:22-32; Acts 10:38); result—healing in every case (Acts 10:35; Mt. 4:23-24; 8:16-17; 9:35; 12:15; 14:14, 35-36; 15:30-31; 19:2; 21:14).

2. **Innumerable Cases of Healing by Believers Before Pentecost** (Mt. 10:1-7; Mk. 6:7-13; 9:38; Lk. 9:1-6; 10:1-20); cause—the devil and demons (Mt. 10:1-7; Lk. 10:1-20; Acts 10:38); remedy—faith and temporary enduement of power for the one mission representing Christ from city to city (Mt. 10:1-20; Lk. 10:1-20); result—healing in every case while on this one mission (Mk. 6:7-13; Lk. 10:1-20). The failure in Mt. 17:14-21 was after they had come back from this mission and before their permanent enduement of power at Pentecost.

3. **Innumerable Cases of Healing by Believers After Pentecost** (Mk. 16:17-20; Acts 2:43; 5:12-16; 6:8; 8:7-13; 11:19-22; 14:3, 27; 15:4, 12; 19:11-12; 28:9; Rom. 15:18-19, 29; 1 Cor. 16:10; Heb. 2:3-4); cause—the devil and demons (Mk. 16:17; Acts 5:16; 8:6-7; 10:38; 19:11-12); remedy—faith and the anointing with the Holy Spirit (Lk. 24:49; Jn. 14:12-15; Acts 1:8; 3:6; 6:8; 19:12; 1 Cor. 12:7-11, 28-31); result—healing in every case (Jn. 14:12; Acts 5:12-16; 19:11-12; Heb. 2:3-4; Mk. 16:20).

4. **Ten Cases of Demon-Possession Healed by Christ and Paul, and Others:**

> (1) A maniac of Gergesa (Mt. 8:28-34)

> (2) A maniac of Gadera (Mk. 5:1-21; Lk. 8:26-39)

> (3) A dumb man (Mt. 9:32-34)

> (4) A blind and dumb man (Mt. 12:22-32)

> (5) An epileptic girl (Mt. 15:21-28)

> (6) An epileptic boy (Mk. 9:17-29)

> (7) A maniac (Mk. 1:23-28; Lk. 4:21)

> (8) Mary Magdalene (Lk. 7:36-50; 8:2)

> (9) An infirm woman (Lk. 13:16-17)

> (10) A fortuneteller (Acts 16:16-24)

5. **Twenty-Nine Cases of Healing by Christ, Peter, John, and Paul:**

> (1) A leper (Mt. 8:1-4; Mk. 1:20)

> (2) A centurion's servant (Mt. 8:5-13)

> (3) Peter's mother-in-law (Mt. 8:14)

> (4) A woman (Mt. 9:18-26; Mk. 5:22)

> (5) Two blind men (Mt. 9:27-31)

> (6) A withered hand (Mt. 12:9-14)

> (7) Two blind men (Mt. 20:29-34)

> (8) A deaf-dumb man (Mk. 7:31-37)

> (9) A blind man (Mk. 8:22-26)

> (10) A man with dropsy (Lk. 14:1-6)

> (11) Ten lepers (Lk. 17:11-19)

> (12) A nobleman's son (Jn. 4:46-54)

> (13) An infirm man (Jn. 5:1-15)

> (14) A blind man (Jn. 9:1-38)

> (15) A lame man (Acts 3:1–4:22)

> (16) A paralyzed man (Acts 9:32-35)

> (17) A lame man (Acts 14:9-10)

> (18) Father of Publius (Acts 28:7-9)

6. **Six Cases of Resurrection from the Dead by Jesus, Peter, Paul, and Others:**

> (1) Jairus' daughter (Mt. 9:18-26)

> (2) A widow's son (Lk. 7:11-18)

> (3) Lazarus (Jn. 11:1-44)

> (4) Tabitha (Acts 9:36-42)

> (5) Paul (Acts 14:19-20)

> (6) Eutychus (Acts 20:7-12)

7. **A Paralyzed Man:** cause—sin; remedy—faith in Christ; result—healing (Mt. 9:1-12; Mk. 2:3-12; Lk. 5:18-26).

8. **Malchus:** cause—ear severed by Peter's sword (Jn. 18:10); remedy—power of Jesus; result—healed (Lk. 22:50-51).

9. **Sickness and Death of Herod:** cause—pride and self-exaltation; remedy—none because of judgment; result—eaten by worms (Acts 12:20-25).

10. **Blindness of Bar-Jesus:** cause—unbelief and rebellion against the gospel; remedy—none until Paul's curse was over (Acts 13:6-12).

11. **Paul's Snakebite:** cause—accident; remedy—faith in Jesus Christ; result—healing (Acts 28:1-6).

Immunity from poisons and snake bites is promised all believers (Mk. 16:17-20; Lk. 10:19; Ps. 91). This does not mean, however, that believers can tempt God and get away with deliberately drinking poison or getting bitten by snakes, as some foolish and ignorant people do today.

The Greek word for "take up" in Mk. 16:18 is *airo*. The way it's used shows its true meaning. It's translated "take away" when speaking of Christ taking away the sins of the world (Jn. 1:29, 1 Jn. 3:5); of branches being cut off the vine (Jn. 15:2); of the bodies of Christ and the thieves being removed from the crosses (Jn. 19:31, 38); of a fornicator being excommunicated from the Corinthian church (1 Cor. 5:2); of taking a man to prison (Mt. 22:13); of the removal of a stone from Lazarus' grave (Jn. 11:39); of the destruction of a nation (Jn. 11:48); and of the removal of knowledge and judgment (Lk. 11:52; Acts 8:35).

It's also translated "put away" concerning removing sin from one's life (Eph. 4:31); "took it out of the way" referring to abolishing the Law of Moses on the cross (Col. 2:14-17); "remove" referring to a literal mountain being cast into the sea (Mt. 21:21; Mk. 11:23); and "away with" referring to the destruction or murder of Jesus and Paul (Lk. 23:18; Jn. 19:15; Acts 21:36; 22:22).

It's never used for making a sideshow of the thing being removed or destroyed. So modern snake-handling fanaticism is not biblical. If people would remove snakes like Paul did—the only New Testament example of how to "take up" snakes—then it would be biblical. Paul shook the viper into the fire and destroyed it (Acts 28:1-6).

If people would use power—if they have real power from God—to heal the sick and do something truly worthwhile for humanity, they'd show some intelligence and be a blessing. You can go to India and other heathen lands and see better demonstrations of snake handling than from modern religious fanatics who make a sideshow of the gospel idea of power over all the power of the devil, as taught in Lk. 10:19 and Ps. 91. These passages simply mean that believers can have power over all enemies—but they never mean to bring reproach upon

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Promise
lessons.content:
# The Dispensation of Promise

**(Gen. 12:1—Exod. 12:37)**

**THE PRESENT AGE (Gen. 8:14—Rev. 19:10)**

## I. The Definition of Promise

This dispensation gets its name from the promises and covenants God made with Abraham and his descendants. These promises became the foundation for everything God did with His chosen people during this era.

Here's what makes this period so significant: it's the first time God began to emphasize that Christ would come through a specific branch of the human family. Now, it's true that looking back at history, we can trace Christ's lineage through Seth and his descendants as listed in Gen. 5 and Lk. 3:23-38. But before Abraham, God never prophesied that the Messiah would definitely come through any particular line.

The only clear prophecy of Christ up to this point had been given to Adam and Eve in the Garden after the Fall (Gen. 3:15). But that promise didn't specify which of Adam's sons would carry the line. Think of it like an announcement that a royal heir would be born somewhere in a vast kingdom — the promise was certain, but the location was yet to be revealed.

The prophecy Noah gave concerning Shem (Gen. 9:24-27) is often understood as the next prediction of Christ's coming. But that passage doesn't actually mention the Messiah directly. It simply predicts that God would have a special relationship with Shem. We understand it as referring to Christ now, in light of what has happened since, but at the time it wasn't clear that the Messiah would come through Shem.

Starting with Abraham, everything changed. God singled out his branch of the race as the very one through whom the seed of the woman would come. Over this period of 430 years, God gave many promises and prophecies about the coming Messiah through Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and his descendants.

In Gen. 12:1-3, we have the first prophecy in the Bible that still awaits complete fulfillment. When will it be fully realized? In the Millennium and forever, when "In thee [Christ, the seed of Abraham] shall all families of the earth be blessed."

This prophecy and promise was confirmed later:
- To Abraham (Gen. 17:19; 21:12; 22:17-18)
- To Isaac (Gen. 26:3-4)
- To Jacob (Gen. 28:3-4, 13-15)
- To Judah (Gen. 49:8-12)

## II. The Length of the Dispensation of Promise: 430 Years

According to Exod. 12:40 and Gal. 3:14-17, this dispensation lasted exactly 430 years — from the call of Abraham to Moses. Here's how those years break down:

1. From the 75th year of Abraham to the birth of Isaac (Gen. 12:4; 21:5) — **25 years**
2. From Isaac's birth to that of Jacob (Gen. 25:26) — **60 years**
3. From Jacob's birth to his death (Gen. 47:28) — **147 years**
4. From Jacob's death to that of Joseph (Gen. 37:2; 41:46; 47:27; 50:22) — **54 years**
5. From Joseph's death to the exodus from Egypt (Exod. 12:40) — **144 years**

**TOTAL: 430 years**

Now, you might wonder about the 400 years mentioned in Gen. 15:13 and Acts 7:6. Those years are calculated from a different starting point — from the confirmation of Isaac as the seed when Ishmael was cast out (Gen. 21:12; Gal. 4:30). This happened five years after Isaac's birth.

The 430 years, on the other hand, are counted from Abraham's departure from Haran, which was twenty-five years before Isaac was born. Here's an important detail: Abraham's seed was actually in Egypt for only 215 years. The entire "sojourn" in various "strange" countries — Canaan, Philistia, Egypt, and others — totaled about 400 years in round numbers, or precisely 430 years from Gen. 12:1-3.

You can trace this sojourn through many passages: Gen. 12:1-20; 13:1-18; 15:13-14; 20:1-18; 21:22-34; 23:4; 26:3-35; 28:10; 29:1; 31:13-55; 35:6; 37:1; 46:1-7; 47:27; 50:22-26; Exod. 1-12; Heb. 11:8-10.

## III. The Favorable Beginning of the Dispensation of Promise

The Dispensation of Promise started with God's call of Abraham. God revealed Himself to Abraham and made an eternal covenant with him — to bless him and his descendants forever and to give them the land of Canaan as an everlasting possession (Gen. 12:1-3; 13:14-18; 15:17-21; 17:7-19).

Abraham had the true knowledge of God and understood how to worship Him properly (Gen. 12:4-9; 13:1-4; 14:17-24; 15:1-21; 17:4-19). He had the gospel of Christ and possessed faith in God and in the blood of Christ for redemption from all sin (Gal. 3:6-9; Rom. 4:1-25).

Let this sink in: Abraham was a saved and born-again man in the same sense that you and I can be saved and born-again today (Rom. 4; Jas. 2:21-24). He had the promise of eternal life and an eternal home. He looked forward to a city to come, where he could live forever with God (Heb. 11:8-19).

Abraham believed in the resurrection of the dead and personally experienced many aspects of the gospel we enjoy now (Heb. 11:19; Rom. 4). He also believed in divine healing and was greatly used by God in the healing of an entire nation (Gen. 20:7, 17).

Abraham lived separated from the idolatrous nations and served as God's representative on Earth. Think of him like an ambassador stationed in a foreign land — set apart, yet positioned to influence everyone around him.

The promises he received were remarkable:
- All nations would be blessed through him
- His descendants would be as numerous as the stars and the sand of the sea
- He would have a great name on Earth
- He would be the father of a great nation
- Kings would come from his line
- His natural descendants would be eternal
- He would be great and wealthy on Earth

Abraham had a personal relationship with God that few people have ever experienced. He received many revelations about this life and the one to come. He became wealthy, and God caused everything he touched to prosper.

There simply couldn't have been a better or more favorable beginning for anyone starting their stewardship in God's plan for this age. If you read Genesis from chapter twelve onward, you'll see just how many favorable conditions existed for people in this era.

### Human Government Enlarged

Many laws of human government were added beyond those God originally gave to Noah in Gen. 9. As sins against society emerged, new laws became necessary. Some of these laws are recorded in Genesis and concerned:

- Monogamy (12:18; 16:1)
- Adultery (12:18; 20:3-9)
- Priesthood (14:18)
- Tithes (14:20; 28:22)
- Covenant-making (15:10-18; 21:27-34; 31:44-55)
- Circumcision (17:10)
- Hospitality (18:1-8; 19:8)
- Licentiousness (18:20-21)
- Fornication (34:7)
- Oaths (21:23; 24:41)
- Birthright (25:33)
- Anointing oil (28:18; 31:13)
- Vows (28:18)
- Idolatry (31:32-35)
- A brother's widow (38:8)
- Dowry (34:12)

Some of these laws came directly from God, while others were man-made. Many of them were later included in the laws of Moses as God continued to expand human government and hold people more accountable for their sins against others.

Some of these civil laws were part of the code established by Amraphel, the ruler of Babylon, which governed various peoples from Persia to the Caspian Sea and the Mediterranean Sea. Gen. 14 mentions the vast Babylonian Empire that ruled many peoples in those early days after the flood.

Other man-made civil laws concerned:
- Adoption (Gen. 15:1-6)
- Concubines (Gen. 16:1-3; 30:1-24)
- Burial places (Gen. 23)
- Execution for stealing (Gen. 31:32)
- Execution by burning (Gen. 38:24)
- Death for stealing in a palace (Gen. 44:9)
- A special portion allotted to a favorite son (Gen. 48:22)

You'll discover more of these as you read carefully through Genesis.

Here's an important principle: God didn't do away with human government or man's responsibility to execute laws against crime just because He began to more fully introduce the gospel of grace. Human government is here to stay. It will continue even in the Millennium and the New Earth, as we'll see in Lessons Forty-nine through Fifty-two.

## IV. The Test in the Dispensation of Promise

The specific test for people in this age was to believe God's promises and obey the gospel as revealed in the Abrahamic Covenant. This test primarily concerned Abraham's chosen family.

God recognized that He could no longer deal with humanity as a whole because people were determined to rebel against Him, perpetuate idolatry, and live lives devoted to self-gratification. His new approach was to work through His chosen people of this period — Abraham's family.

To them He gave the specific promises and covenants, along with the responsibility of remaining separated from all other peoples and being wholly consecrated to the true God and His worship. God required them to leave their relatives behind and sojourn in a strange land, remaining set apart as His representatives on Earth.

Beyond their particular calling, God also required His chosen people to obey all civil laws that didn't conflict with His divine plan for them.

What about the other nations? Their test in this period was to obey human government laws and also have faith in God, conforming to the gospel as revealed to them by the chosen race and by the conscience within every person (Rom. 2:12-16).

Nobody in this period had any excuse for not knowing they were lost or what God required for salvation. Knowledge of God existed among many peoples. All the inhabitants of Canaan knew about God because of Abraham, Melchisedec, Isaac, Jacob, and others who lived among them.

God dealt personally with many groups:
- The Egyptians (Gen. 13:10-20; 39:1–50:26)
- Hagar (Gen. 16)
- The cities of the plain (Gen. 19)
- Philistia (Gen. 20 and 26)
- Rebekah's family (Gen. 24:1-67; 28:1–31:55)

And there were others throughout this age. The founders of the nations of Moab, Ammon, Edom, Midian, and other branches of the race were all raised in the households of Lot and Abraham.

Abraham's family sojourned for 430 years in various lands, and all these peoples received testimony about God from them. By the end of this age, many nations witnessed God's mighty power in the plagues of Egypt, in the wilderness, and in Canaan and surrounding countries — miracles that only God could perform.

God has never been without witnesses among the nations in any age. People have always been required to live according to the light they received, and they will be judged accordingly.

## V. The Purpose of God in the Dispensation of Promise

Because humanity had utterly failed God in every test — proving sinful, idolatrous, and rebellious — the Lord saw that He could no longer deal with humanity as a whole. So He purposed in this dispensation to call out one man and make him and his descendants God's representatives among all nations.

God planned to give them the promised land as an everlasting possession and as a base of operations for His greater purpose: the coming of the seed of the woman and the defeat of the giants and other satanic enemies on Earth. This land would be where the Messiah would be born to rule all nations forever.

God's plan was to keep the chosen race as pure Adamite stock, free from corruption by the giant races that were filling the land. This way, there would be no failure in bringing the seed of the woman into the world to redeem humanity and carry out His eternal purpose.

Here's something crucial to understand: the second eruption of the sons of God had already occurred before Abraham's call. God knew Satan's purpose in this — it was the same as before the flood: to corrupt the human race and eliminate pure Adamite stock so the seed of the woman couldn't come into the world. If Satan succeeded, it would avert his own doom and that of the fallen angels, allowing their rulership of Earth to continue.

Already, the sons of God were marrying human women and producing giants on Earth, just as before the flood. These beings had taken possession of Canaan and surrounding territories — the very lands God had chosen as the base for His missionary and governmental operations among the nations.

But God had a plan. He would let Israel multiply in another land until they became strong enough to destroy these giants and possess the lands He intended. In Lesson Seventeen, we'll see how these giants were destroyed by Israel's sword, accomplishing what God planned in this dispensation. (See Lessons Eleven and Thirteen for more on the sons of God and daughters of men before and after the flood.)

Through choosing Abraham and his descendants, God purposed to make them His physical, financial, and spiritual representatives on Earth. He wanted to demonstrate to all the heathen nations what serving the true and living God would be like.

Picture it this way: Israel was meant to be a living advertisement — a demonstration of the dramatic contrast between the blessings of serving a living God versus the dead idols the nations worshiped.

God wanted Israel to be:
- A spiritual power in His hands to defeat Satan's forces and show all people they could use God's power to conquer sin and sickness
- Perfect in physical health, proving it's God's will for all to be free from disease
- Prosperous and wealthy, proving it's God's will for all to be free from want and poverty
- Happy and victorious over all enemies
- A true example of God's highest will for fallen humanity
- The ideal nation — people who were happy, prosperous, sinless, and healthy, living in perfect peace and blessedness

All of this would draw the heathen nations back to God and lead them to accept Him as the rightful Moral Governor of the universe.

Through this actual demonstration of God's blessings on Israel, God planned to make His will perfectly clear to all mankind. Israel was called to be missionaries for God to all nations. If they had remained faithful, the Earth could have been evangelized centuries before Christ came!

All people could have seen definite proof of truth demonstrated in the bodies, souls, and spirits of the chosen nation — in their religion and government. They would have been the head, not the tail. They would have been the esteemed leaders of all Gentiles, and all nations would have been blessed alongside them — just as they will be in the Millennium and forever.

We would already have the days of Heaven on Earth, which won't fully come until Jesus returns the second time, as we'll see in Lesson Forty-nine.

God planned to banish poverty, want, sickness, sin, and all misery from Earth in the lives of everyone who followed Israel in serving the true God and believing the gospel of Christ.

No nation has ever had a better opportunity to be used by God among all other nations than Israel did — from Abraham's call to Christ's first coming. No people on Earth have ever been given a greater program than what God had in mind when He called Abraham and chose his descendants to represent truth.

Here's something wonderful: most of what we now include in the gospel of grace actually had its beginning and purpose in the Age of Promise. God preached the gospel beforehand to Abraham and his seed, saying, "In thee shall all the nations be blessed" (Gal. 3:7-9).

All the wonderful doctrines of the New Testament are found in the promises and covenant made with Abraham:
- Salvation in all its aspects
- Remission of sin
- Conversion
- Repentance
- Faith
- Consecration
- Eternal life
- Spiritual revelations
- Redemption
- Sanctification
- Justification
- Bodily healing
- Health
- Grace
- Heirship
- Reconciliation
- Imputation of righteousness
- Answers to prayer
- Bodily resurrection
- Fruit of the Spirit

And many other parts of the present gospel were experienced by Abraham's descendants before the cross. We'll explore this more in Lessons Thirty-three and Thirty-four.

## VI. The Means of God in Accomplishing His Purpose

What tools did God use to accomplish His purpose in this age? The call of God, the covenant and promises (which included "the gospel" — Gal. 3:6-9, 13, 14), and personal dealings and revelations of His plan to Abraham and his descendants.

God had confidence in Abraham that he would remain separate and represent Him to all other nations according to the gospel. He trusted Abraham and his seed to preserve their family line and remain free from mixture with the giants, so that the seed of the woman could eventually come and bruise the serpent's head.

Abraham had no idea at first what all God had in mind when He called him. But he was obedient, following God step by step as God revealed His plan. He left his own country and went into the land of the giants to live as a sojourner.

In that land, God revealed many things to him. One important revelation was that his descendants would sojourn in Egypt, become a great nation, and then be brought back to possess the land (Gen. 15:13-21). This was fulfilled in this Dispensation of Promise.

His descendants multiplied slowly at first — only 70 souls in 215 years. But in the next 215 years, they grew rapidly to 600,000 men of war, plus their wives, children, and elderly. Many Egyptian converts also joined the nation (Exod. 12:38; Num. 11:4; Neh. 13:3). There must have been over six million people when they left Egypt!

God wanted them strong enough to destroy the giants occupying the promised land and eliminate all abnormal beings that came from the sons of God and daughters of men since the flood (Gen. 6:4). In Gen. 15:16, we learn that even in Abraham's time, God purposed to destroy these giants.

The sword of Israel was God's instrument for destroying the mighty race of giants that had emerged since the flood, as we explained in Lesson Eleven. Israel became a great nation in Egypt and was used powerfully by God to fulfill His prophetic Word concerning Egypt, Canaan, and surrounding lands (Gen. 15:13-16; Deut. 20:17; Josh. 3:10).

When Israel surrendered to God's will through obedience, they demonstrated before all nations God's power over sickness, poverty, want, and sin. God made the entire nation healthy — there wasn't one feeble person among all their tribes (Ps. 105:37; 107:20). He promised continued freedom from sickness and disease (Exod. 15:26; 23:25; Ps. 91). He promised wealth and happiness forever, and Israel enjoyed these blessings as long as she walked in God's will (Lev. 26; Deut. 28).

During the reigns of David, Solomon, and a few other kings of Judah, all nations were drawn to the God of Israel. This showed, at least in part, God's purpose for Israel throughout the period from Abraham to Christ.

## VII. The Failure of Men in the Dispensation of Promise

The history of Abraham and his descendants recorded in Genesis 12–50 and Exod. 1–12 reveals the failure of Israel in particular and all humanity in general. Let's examine these failures:

### 1. THE FAILURE OF ABRAHAM (Gen. 12:11–25:11)

Abraham's first failure was not leaving all his relatives behind when he went to the Promised Land. God had clearly told him: "Get thee out of thy country, and from thy kindred, and from thy father's house" (Gen. 12:1-3). But Abraham allowed Terah, his father, to lead the journey into Canaan — not what God commanded (Gen. 11:31-32).

Several family members started from Ur of the Chaldees, and they were delayed in Haran until Terah's death. Then some family members stayed in Haran while others continued with Abraham to Canaan (Gen. 12:1-6).

Abraham's next failure was showing unbelief in God's protection. He left Canaan to go to Egypt during a famine. This move itself might have been permitted, but his fear that the Egyptians would kill him for Sarah's sake and his deception of Pharaoh was inexcusable (Gen. 12:10-20).

It was a clear failure to believe God's promise that his descendants would bless all nations. He should have realized that if God's promises were true, the Egyptians couldn't kill him — he had no children yet, and he would have to live to have any! In Egypt, Satan tried to corrupt Sarah and frustrate God's purpose in bringing the seed of the woman through Abraham, but God intervened, protected Sarah, and exposed Abraham's deception. Abraham was expelled from Egypt, so his time there was clearly outside God's will.

God was with Abraham when he returned to Canaan. He was finally separated from Lot, and then God promised him descendants as numerous as the stars and sand of the sea (Gen. 13:1-18).

Abraham delivered Lot from his enemies as recorded in Gen. 14. Then God appeared again, promising a son and offspring as countless as the stars and sand (Gen. 15).

Fifteen years passed with no promised son. Sarah came up with an idea to "help" God fulfill His word. She suggested Abraham take a second wife, giving him her handmaid Hagar, who bore Ishmael (Gen. 16). This wasn't God's plan for producing the promised seed, and it caused tremendous family trouble. Abraham had failed God again by not having faith for a child through Sarah.

Fourteen more years went by — still no promised seed. God appeared to Abraham and renewed the covenant, confirming it by commanding circumcision. Abraham was 99 years old, and Ishmael was thirteen when they were circumcised (Gen. 17). A few weeks later, God appeared personally and promised a son through Sarah, who was 89 years old. Both were past the age of having children, but God renewed His promise — they would have a child within the year.

Around this same time, Abraham went to Philistia and failed God again through unbelief. He gave Sarah to the king for his harem. This was another attempt by Satan to corrupt Sarah, but God intervened by making all the women in the nation barren. If God hadn't protected Sarah, any child she bore could have been suspected as the king's, not Abraham's — causing endless scandal and family trouble. God rescued Sarah so that Abimelech never touched her, ensuring Abraham would know without doubt that Isaac was truly his — the long-promised child.

From that point on, Abraham no longer staggered at God's promise through unbelief. He became strong in faith, giving glory to God, counting Him faithful to His word. God renewed the youth of both Abraham and Sarah, and Isaac was born (Gen. 21; Rom. 4:12-21; Heb. 11:11-12).

At this same time, God appeared to Abraham, ate a meal with him, and told him about the coming destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen. 18-19). Lot was rescued from Sodom, though the last we read of him concerns his shameful incident with his two daughters, from which came Moab and Ammon (Gen. 19:33-38).

Hagar and Ishmael were sent away when Isaac was old enough to be weaned (Gen. 21). Abraham and Abimelech made a covenant together, and Abraham lived in Philistia for many days. After this, Abraham agreed to offer Isaac as a sacrifice, but God intervened and provided another sacrifice (Gen. 22).

Sarah died when Isaac was thirty-seven years old and was buried in the cave of Machpelah (Gen. 23). At forty years of age, Isaac married Rebekah, whom Abraham's servant found among his relatives in Haran (Gen. 24).

Abraham married Keturah and had six sons by her, plus other children by his concubines. He left all his wealth to Isaac, except for gifts given to his other children, whom he sent away to the East country. Abraham died at 175 years of age and was buried by Isaac and Ishmael beside Sarah in the cave of Machpelah (Gen. 25).

So Abraham wasn't absolutely perfect, as many people today assume. For about twenty-four years, he struggled with faith and patience concerning the promised son. After several failures to believe God without wavering, in the twenty-fifth year he became strong in faith with no more recorded failures (Rom. 4:17-25).

After that, there was no need for faith in a promised son — he had what was promised (Rom. 8:24-25). He lived as a good man, full of experience, for seventy-five more years before dying in faith toward God.

### 2. THE FAILURE OF ISAAC (Gen. 21:1–35:29)

Isaac seemed less spiritual than Abraham. He had fewer revelations than his father. He loved Esau more than Jacob, which later caused much family trouble.

Like Abraham, Isaac failed in faith when famine came. He went to Philistia and committed the same sin his father had concerning his wife. This was Satan's attempt to corrupt Rebekah, through whom the Messiah was to come, but God intervened, and she was spared from sinning against God and man (Gen. 26).

God had promised that Isaac's descendants would be as numerous as the stars and sand. If Isaac had believed God, no man could have killed him to get his wife!

Isaac experienced more conflicts with the land's inhabitants than Abraham did and also had more trouble with his children. Overall, we see a decline in spiritual and home life in Isaac's family compared to Abraham's.

Twenty years passed after Isaac's marriage with no child. He prayed to God, and twins were born (Gen. 25:19-34). God predicted that Jacob would be the greater of the two boys, and the Messiah would come through him. Yet despite this, Isaac loved Esau and tried to give him the blessing, but was thwarted by Rebekah's scheming (Gen. 27).

After this, Isaac lived in the land for many years — the twenty years Jacob was away and many years after Jacob's return. He died at 180 years of age, and Jacob and Esau buried him (Gen. 35:27-29).

### 3. THE FAILURE OF JACOB AND HIS SONS (Gen. 25:24–49:33)

Jacob was less spiritual than Isaac. His disposition always drove him to get ahead of everyone else. In fact, he was called "Jacob" or "supplanter" because in the womb he tried to get ahead of his brother and be firstborn (Gen. 25:24-26).

His story begins with twins struggling in Rebekah's womb and the prophecy that he would be greater than his brother (Gen. 25:22-23). When grown, he bought his brother's birthright and stole his blessing (Gen. 25:27-34; 27:1-46). For this, he had to flee to Haran to escape Esau's anger (Gen. 28).

In Haran, he raised a large family and schemed constantly to get his father-in-law's wealth, tampering with nature and working against him behind his back (Gen. 29-30). This caused Laban's sons to plot against Jacob, which prompted his decision to return home. He fled from Laban, who pursued and caught him in Gilead and would have harmed him if God hadn't intervened (Gen. 31).

When Jacob heard Esau was coming after him with 400 armed men, he failed God by being afraid he would be killed. He wrestled with God all night, and his entire life and disposition were transformed (Gen. 32). God made Esau friendly, and Jacob settled in Canaan (Gen. 33).

There, he began reaping what he had sown through years of trying to get ahead of everyone:
- His daughter was defiled, causing her brothers to kill all the men of Shechem
- Some family members became idolaters (Gen. 35:2-5)
- Rachel, his beloved wife, and Isaac, his father, both died (Gen. 35)
- Reuben committed adultery with his father's concubine (Gen. 35:22)
- His sons became lawless, unfaithful, and deceitful
- They sold Joseph into Egypt (Gen. 37)
- Judah brought family shame through his daughter-in-law and by marrying outside the chosen family (Gen. 38)

Jacob ended his life in Egypt with his sons, dying at 147 years of age (Gen. 47).

Jacob's descendants were generally less spiritual than he was, except for Joseph. Over 215 years, they became almost completely backslidden and had forgotten God. For this, God allowed them to be oppressed by the Egyptians, hoping their troubles would bring them back to repentance.

Their oppression did produce repentance — they cried out to God for mercy. God heard them and raised up Moses to deliver them. You can trace their backsliding in passages like Exod. 2:23-25; 3:13-18; 4:1-9, 29-31; 5:1-23.

So we see that throughout these failures, the devil tried repeatedly to defeat God's plan for bringing the seed of the woman into the world:
- He tried three times to corrupt Sarah and Rebekah
- He tried several times to make Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, and his sons fear death before God's promises were fulfilled
- He tried famine, unbelief, fear, war, deceit, lying, slavery, and murder
- He tried cutting off male children to destroy the race
- He tried provoking Moses into attempting a premature deliverance so Israel's chosen leader would be killed
- He tried hardships and discouragements of all kinds
- He tried causing Israel to sin so God would destroy them

All to keep the seed of the woman from coming into the world as God promised.

Satan was defeated every time. The specific line through whom the Messiah would come was preserved until Christ actually appeared and defeated him on the cross. This battle between God and Satan over the seed of the woman continued through this age and carried on through the Dispensation of Law in many forms, with God always victorious, as we'll see.

### 4. THE FAILURE OF ALL MEN IN GENERAL

Not only did the chosen family fail God on many occasions to live up to their best light, but the Gentile nations among whom Israel lived rebelled as a whole against God. Few righteous people are mentioned in this period.

We can name Melchisedec, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Sarah, Moses, Job, and a few others that Scripture identifies as righteous during this time. But as a whole, the nations were given over to idolatry and sin.

Some cities and nations are specifically called out as very sinful: the Amorites, Sodom, Gomorrah, Egypt, and others (Gen. 13:13; 15:13-16; 19:1-25; 31:30).

The majority of people in every age have failed to live up to the light they received. For this, they will receive God's judgment (Rom. 2:12-16).

## VIII. The Judgment of God upon Men in the Dispensation of Promise

The judgment ending this age was twofold:
1. Upon Israel, the chosen family who had special responsibility as God's representatives on Earth during this period
2. Upon Egypt because of their persecution of Israel

### 1. THE JUDGMENT UPON ISRAEL (Exod. 1:7–6:30)

Because of Israel's continual spiritual decline throughout this age and their almost universal backsliding from the original faith given to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, God allowed His chosen people to be greatly oppressed by the Egyptians. His purpose was to bring them to repentance.

The oppression worked. It brought them to repentance, and they cried out to God for mercy. God heard them and raised up Moses for their deliverance (Exod. 2:1–4:31; Acts 7:17-36; Heb. 11:23-29).

### The Beginning of "the Times of the Gentiles"

This term refers to the period when the Gentiles served as God's rod of discipline upon Israel to further His purposes concerning them.

It began with Israel's first oppression by the Gentiles in Egypt and continues through Israel's history even into this Dispensation of Grace. It will end at Christ's return in glory, when He will deliver Israel from the Gentiles under Antichrist and exalt them as the head of all nations in the Millennium and forever (Lk. 21:24; Rev. 11:1-2).

### The Door of Mercy Always Open to Gentiles

This dispensation of the Gentiles is mentioned only in Lk. 21:24 and referred to in Rom. 11:25 as "the

lessons.title: Asking and Receiving from God
lessons.content:
# Asking and Receiving from God

This lesson might be one of the most life-changing studies you'll ever dig into. Without a clear, unwavering understanding of how to ask and receive from God, you'll miss out on so much that's rightfully yours—both now and in eternity.

Here's the good news: if you truly master these truths and commit to practicing them faithfully, you'll never have to wonder why your prayers go unanswered like so many people do. You really can get what you want from God if you simply do what He says. The certainty of receiving from Him is as solid as anything can possibly be—if you follow the instructions laid out here.

God cannot fail. His promises cannot fail. And you cannot fail if you're just as determined to refuse failure as most people seem determined to accept it. Let's dive into these great lessons on prayer until asking becomes second nature and receiving becomes your normal experience.

## I. What Prayer Is

So what exactly is prayer? It's bringing your desires for things you need, things that are lawful, and things God has promised—with a humble confidence that you'll receive them through Jesus Christ, for God's glory and your good.

Think of it like pleading your case in God's courtroom. It's reaching out for help in matters beyond your own power. It's a personal appeal to a personal, present God—based on His will, His Word, and your legitimate desires.

Prayer is cooperating with God's willingness to show His goodness to everyone who has faith in Him and depends on Him for help. Simply put, prayer is asking and receiving from God—and that's what this whole lesson is about.

## II. General Facts about Asking and Receiving

**1. COMMANDS TO PRAY:** (1 Chron. 16:11; 2 Chron. 7:14; Ps. 105:4; Isa. 55:6; Mt. 7:7-11; Eph. 6:18; Phil. 4:6; 1 Thess. 5:17; Heb. 4:16).

**2. TIME TO PRAY:** "daily," in the morning (Ps. 5:3; 88:13; 143:8); "twice daily" (Ps. 88:1); "thrice daily" (Ps. 55:17; Dan. 6:10); "without ceasing" (1 Thess. 5:17); "at night" (Mk. 1:35; Lk. 6:12); "night and day" (Lk. 2:37).

**3. ATTITUDES IN PRAYER:** "standing" (Lk. 18:13); "sitting" (Acts 1:14; 2:2; Luke 10:13); "lying" (2 Sam. 12:16-20); "kneeling" (Ps. 95:6; Dan. 6:10; Lk. 22:41; Acts 9:40; 20:36; 21:5); "weeping" (Ezra 10:1; 1 Sam. 1:10); "talking" (Gen. 18:23-33); "meditating" (Ps. 5:1); "agonizing" (Lk. 22:44); "groaning" (Rom. 8:26-27); and "in the Spirit" (Eph. 6:18; Jude 20).

**4. WHERE TO PRAY:** "in closets" (Mt. 6:6); "on mountains" (Mt. 14:23); and in homes, in temples and everywhere (Acts 1:13-14; 2:46; 3:1; 12:12; 1 Tim. 2:8).

**5. HOW NOT TO PRAY:** Jesus taught that you shouldn't pray like the hypocrites who want to be seen by others, and not like the heathen who think God hears them because of their many words (Mt. 6:5-8).

**6. THE PURPOSE OF PRAYER:** The main goal of all prayer should be to glorify God and to make your joy complete (Jn. 14:13-15; 16:24).

**7. GROUND FOR ASKING AND RECEIVING:** The true foundation for prayer and its answer is being born-again and having a right relationship with God. Only true, saved people have the right to ask and receive whatever they want from God.

Sinners who rebel and refuse to surrender their lives to God have no real grounds to approach Him. They have no foundation for their faith. Now, God in His mercy does sometimes help sinners and hear their cries of despair—but He's not obligated to do for them what He's promised until they obey Him. He helps them simply because He wants to prove His love and mercy, even to rebels.

Paul said, "the goodness of God leadeth thee to repentance" and "God commendeth his love toward us, in that, while we were yet sinners Christ died for us" (Rom. 2:4; 5:8).

The first promise a sinner can legally claim through Christ's death is forgiveness and cleansing from all sin (Acts 2:38; 3:19; 1 Jn. 1:9). Once someone truly repents and gets born-again, they're immediately on praying ground for anything else God has promised. A right relationship with God is the true foundation for asking and receiving (Mt. 7:7-11; Lk. 11:1-13; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; 1 Jn. 3:20-24; 5:13-15).

With the proper relationship with God, it becomes your family right, your legal right, your redemptive right, your gospel right, your needful right, and your creative right to ask for and receive anything the Father has promised His children. There should be no question about your personal rights or the outcome of prayer—all things belong to God and His children, and His sons will inherit all things when rebellion is finally put down (Rom. 8:17-18; 1 Pet. 1:2-5; Rev. 21:7).

If all things belong to us and we'll eventually inherit them, why not get what we want of them here and now? At the very least, why shouldn't it be our right to receive what God has already promised and provided?

## III. The Certainty of Answered Prayer

According to Jesus, asking is receiving: "Ask, and it shall be given you; seek and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you: for every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened" (Mt. 7:7-8).

Here's something interesting: the first letters of "ask," "seek," and "knock" spell A-S-K. This isn't just a coincidence—it's a divine reminder that all you have to do is ask. But asking presupposes a God who hears and answers.

If you ask in mockery or just going through the motions without faith, you're not truly asking. You must ask in faith, nothing wavering, and then you'll receive (Jas. 1:5-9; Heb. 11:6). There can be no failure, or God isn't true—so don't doubt it, because God is true.

Jesus continued by showing us the infinite fatherhood of God. He explained that God loves His children more than any human parent could possibly love their own flesh and blood.

This means it's not—and cannot be—God's will for any one of His children to be sinful, rebellious, defeated, unhappy, sick, poor, wanting, worrying, complaining, or discouraged about anything in life. No earthly parent would want these things for their children, and they would do everything possible to deliver them from such troubles. How much more does God want to help you?

Jesus proved God loves His children more than earthly parents when He said: "Or what man is there of you, whom if his son ask bread, will he give him a stone? Or if he asks a fish, will he give him a serpent? If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your heavenly Father which is in Heaven give good things to them that ask him?" (Mt. 7:9-11).

What more could you want than such a broad, all-inclusive promise of God's infinite fatherhood? What more proof could you need that it's God's will for you to ask for anything—wealth, health, cleansing from sin, keeping from sin, or anything else you desire in life?

In the same Sermon on the Mount, Jesus taught us to pray, "Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven" (Mt. 6:10). Now ask yourself: Is it God's will for people to be sick and sinful in Heaven? Is it His will for people to be defeated, poverty-stricken, and unhappy there? Is it His will for people to be tormented by demons and carry around the works of the devil forever in Heaven?

You know the answer. If these things aren't God's will in Heaven, they're not God's will on Earth either. You can have freedom from these things here just as much as you'll be free from them there—if you'll just ask and receive according to God's will, right here and now.

May God Himself wake us up to see our privileges in the gospel here and now when we need so many things! Prayer will never fail when it's done right. Heaven and Earth will pass away more easily than prayer will become powerless when people pray correctly.

Prayer never fails when you never fail to believe God. Prayer is absolute and unfailing when faith is the same. At the grave of Lazarus, Jesus said, "Father, I thank thee that thou hast heard me. And I knew that thou hearest me always" (Jn. 11:41-42). This is a normal, truthful attitude for every child of God to take and maintain.

You can say just as easily, "I can, I will, I do believe that God hears me and answers my prayer," as you can say, "It's hard to believe, I don't know if I can, I hope God hears me." You can form right words and cause them to come out of your mouth just as easily as you could tell a lie—and if you're that kind of person, it's infinitely easier to tell the truth.

All statements of unbelief, all doubts about whether God answers prayer—these are from the devil. Anyone who gives in to these ideas is cooperating with the enemy. You're making God a liar and hindering Him from answering your prayers.

God will not, on any occasion or in any case, transgress His law of faith and work contrary to it. If you believe God will answer, He will. If you believe He won't, He won't. This is truth, plain and simple (Heb. 11:6; Jas. 1:5-9).

In Detroit, Michigan, about three years ago, a deacon took me to pray with a man who had been paralyzed on one side and hadn't walked for about eight months. I spoke to him about faith, and his very first words were, "I believe in healing all right, but I can't seem to have faith for my healing."

I got up, walked over to his bed, and said, "Don't you say that again. That's a lie. You can have faith, and you must have faith if you expect to get healed." I quoted Mk. 11:22-24, emphasizing it this way: "Believe you've got it and you shall have it." I said that several times and got him to promise to believe God would heal him. Then I prayed.

While I was praying, the paralyzed man said, "If I've got it, what am I lying here for?" With that, he jumped out of bed and shouted all over the place! I told him, "Now stay up until bedtime, go to bed, get up in the morning perfectly well, and forget you ever had paralysis."

It's true—God will meet faith to the full extent of that faith. Jesus would sometimes say, "According to your faith so be it done unto you" or "as thou hast believed, so be it done unto thee" (Mt. 8:13; 9:29). It's the same today. Aggressive faith produces aggressive results. Living faith produces life. Dead, passive faith produces death and failure.

In Jn. 14:12, Jesus promised power for praying and working. He promised that each believer would do what He did—and even greater works—upon one condition: "He that believeth on me."

We can argue that this isn't for us today or that there must be some mistake about such a broad promise. But the fact that He said it still faces us, and we dare not make Him a liar if we expect to see Him in peace. This passage is plain; it means what it says. It will pay us to believe it, whether we want the experience of it or not.

In many other passages, Jesus gave believers full power of attorney to use His name to get anything in life they want (Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; Mk. 16:16-20). We'll explore this more fully in Lesson Twenty-two. For now, just understand the absolute certainty of answered prayer for anything and everything you want in life.

There are no impossibilities with God or with the believer who will believe Him (Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; Mt. 17:20; 21:21-22; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16). Possibilities in prayer are vast, and we should wake up to attain the blessings of the gospel. The world is dying for this kind of program.

God is going to hold modern believers accountable. He will judge every preacher, teacher, and the least person who claims to believe the gospel for not boldly setting aside unbelief and striving for the faith once delivered to the saints (Jude 3).

The certainty of answered prayer is also expressed in the Old Testament:

"The eyes of the Lord are upon the righteous, and his ears are open unto their cry. The righteous cry, and the Lord heareth, and delivereth them out of ALL their troubles" (Ps. 34:15-17).

"Delight thyself also in the Lord; and he shall give thee the desires of thine heart. Commit thy way unto the Lord; trust also in him; and he shall bring it to pass" (Ps. 37:4-5).

"He shall call upon me, and I will answer him: I will be with him in trouble; I will deliver him, and honour him" (Ps. 91:15).

"No good thing will he withhold from them that walk uprightly" (Ps. 84:11).

## IV. Scientific Proof of Answered Prayer

Prayer and its answer can be tested scientifically just like anything else. Now, we're not saying God bows to every whim of unbelieving people or that He meets every test imposed by mere curiosity or skepticism.

Jesus went about answering prayer and performing all kinds of miracles in the moral, spiritual, material, and physical realms. But when unbelieving Jews sought a sign from Heaven, He didn't satisfy their unbelief (Mt. 12:39). Some skeptics set up their own arbitrary "prayer gauge," and anything that doesn't meet their requirements doesn't count as an answered prayer to them. People have dared God to strike them dead, thinking He wouldn't—and when He didn't, they went off boasting there was no God.

But God has answered legitimate tests in the material realm. When Elijah had a contest with the prophets of false gods, God answered powerfully (1 Ki. 18:36-38). In Egypt, God manifested His power so all the Egyptians would know He was the true and living God (Exod. 7:5; 8:10, 22-23; 9:16, 29; 11:7).

God answered the sign Gideon asked for (Judg. 6:37-40). He answered Hezekiah with a sign, turning the sun backward ten degrees (Isa. 38:5-8). He even rebuked Ahaz for not asking a sign "either in the depth, or in the height above" (Isa. 7:10-13). Jesus promised that people could move literal mountains, just as He cursed the fig tree and performed other signs before witnesses (Mt. 21:21-22). But this was never done to satisfy mere unbelief and curiosity—it can only happen in sincerity and faith.

When we have thousands of definite, concrete answers to prayer on record—both in the Bible and throughout history—we don't need to obligate God to perform some sign just to satisfy skeptics. The proof of answered prayer fills many pages of history, and this evidence is sufficient to convince any honest inquirer.

The Lord heard Abraham and healed a whole nation (Gen. 20:7, 17). He heard Eliezer and gave Isaac a wife (Gen. 24). He heard Isaac, and children were born (Gen. 25:21-26). He heard Moses, and Israel was spared from death with many signs on many occasions (Exod. 5:22–6:18; Exod. 32). He heard judges, prophets, priests, and leaders in Israel—and early Christians—times without number (Num. 12:13-15; Ps. 3:4; 6:8; 18:6; 21:2-4; 22:24; 28:6; 30:2-3; 31:22; 34:4-6; 40:1; 66:19-20; 77:1; 81:7; 99:6-8; 106:44; 107:6-7, 13-20; 116:1-2; 118:5, 21; 138:3; Hos. 12:4; Jonah 2:1; Lk. 23:42-43; Jn. 1:42; Acts 4:31; Jas. 5:17; etc.).

These and scores of other testimonies in the Bible prove that God has heard prayer for people of all past ages.

The history of the Church and of people's lives throughout this age overflow with testimonies of answered prayer. We have more testimony proving answered prayer than we have for any scientific fact ever proven. If there's any fact capable of proof, it's that God answers prayer.

Hundreds of thousands of people today testify to answered prayer in every area of life. Let any skeptic go to any church, and they'll find people who will affirm under oath that God has answered their prayers. Space won't permit sharing all the testimonies I've personally experienced or know that others have had. If you're doubtful, you can get proof from your next-door neighbor. Ask any true Christian, and you can collect all the evidence you need. Now let's move on to the practical help in prayer that people need today.

## V. The Secrets of Asking and Receiving

### 1. LEARN TO PRAY RIGHT

This is one of the greatest lessons to learn about prayer. You can't get an answer unless you pray right.

In Lk. 11:1, we see the disciples watching how Jesus prayed. They knew God always heard Him. There was such absolute simplicity and assurance when He prayed that a hunger grew in them to know how to pray like that. They asked Him, "Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples."

This should be the daily longing of every child of God. The need to know how to pray right is the need of the hour.

Christ is the greatest teacher of prayer—He practiced it Himself. We must learn to pray like Jesus, and God will always hear us, just as He always heard Him. God is no respecter of persons.

The following points will enable anyone to learn the art of prayer as far as knowing what to do. But they'll never give you practical experience in prayer—that comes only through much practice, doggedly following what you've learned. Knowing what to do and actually doing it are two different things. You need both knowledge and obedience to get answers.

Since we know our need and we have the greatest teacher to help us, let's apply ourselves until prayer becomes second nature—just as it was with Him.

### 2. THE MODEL PRAYER (Lk. 11:2-4)

The so-called Lord's Prayer is really a prayer for disciples—learners of how to pray. It was given when the disciples asked, "Lord, teach us to pray, as John also taught his disciples" (Lk. 11:1-4).

All believers can consider themselves learners and model their prayers after this one until they graduate from the school of prayer—or at least until they get every prayer answered. With that as our goal, we can all consider ourselves in the same school, learning the same lessons. Here are several things we need to learn:

> **(1) "OUR FATHER."** This teaches a new and right relationship to God through the new birth (Jn. 3:1-8; 2 Cor. 5:17; Gal. 5:24; Jn. 15:7; 1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-4, 13-18).
>
> It's generally taught that people in Old Testament days never dared address God as "Father" because they weren't born-again. But searching Scripture actually proves the contrary. People of all past ages were born-again by faith in the coming Redeemer and His blood that was to be shed, just as we're born-again today by faith in the blood that has been shed.
>
> This is clear from Deut. 14:1; 32:18; 2 Sam. 7:14; 2 Chron. 28:6; Isa. 1:2; 63:16; Hos. 1:10; Ps. 82:6; Gal. 4:28-30; Rom. 4:1-25; Heb. 11. People in past ages asked and received on the same grounds we do today, as the Scriptures under Point IV show.
>
> The right relationship to God means everything and is one of the greatest secrets of answered prayer. Why would anyone be so presumptuous as to approach God stained with sin and in open rebellion, then expect Him to do what He's promised only to His children?
>
> Not only should we have the right relationship, but we must learn to whom prayer should be addressed. God the Father—our Father—is the one we should address in prayer. Jesus taught this in Jn. 16:23-26: "In that day [when I go back to the Father] ye shall ask me nothing . . . Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in my name, he will give it you. Hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name: ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full . . . At that day ye shall ask in my name."
>
> God the Father is the head of Christ and the Holy Spirit, and therefore He should be the one directly addressed in prayer (1 Cor. 11:3; Eph. 3:14-16).
>
> The habit of praying directly to Jesus or to the Holy Spirit should be broken, because we have no authority for such praying. God overlooks ignorance of this fact, just as He does other things—but if we want to learn to pray right, we should learn correctly and practice what we learn. God's Word shouldn't be ignored here any more than anywhere else.
>
> Children of God sometimes, because of the freedom they've found in Christ, willfully ignore certain technical truths and never seem concerned to obey fully. This may be why so much prayer goes unanswered—"to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin" (Jas. 4:17).
>
> Some people decide they're not going to pay attention to "little details" like this. But we warn you: it's best to keep the letter of the Word, since an attitude that disregards what's written certainly isn't the spirit of the Word either. God commands obedience to His Word, not to what we think is right. It won't be a sin to obey. When we transgress the letter, we obey neither the spirit nor the letter. Saul learned this, and his lesson was recorded for our benefit (1 Sam. 15:22-30).
>
> We're not saying you shouldn't recognize Christ or the Holy Spirit in prayer and communion. Give them their proper scriptural place—worship all three persons of the Godhead and commune with all three appropriately. But address all petitions to the Father, in the name of Jesus, by the power of the Holy Spirit. Praying in Jesus' name to Jesus is foolish, and praying to God without using Jesus' name has no promise of reward. Any prayer that ignores Jesus or the Holy Spirit in their proper place isn't truly biblical and has no assurance of being heard.

> **(2) "WHICH ART IN HEAVEN."** This teaches that we're associated with God in Heaven itself and identifies where God dwells, as we saw in Lesson Four, Point 9.
>
> Paul said, "our conversation [citizenship] is in heaven; from whence we look for the Saviour" (Phil. 3:20-21). As God's child, you should constantly recognize the source of your blessings and your citizenship, which guarantees certain rights—just like being a citizen of any country.

> **(3) "HALLOWED BE THY NAME."** This teaches the proper manner of approaching God. Worship, adoration, and praise should come before your petitions and continue alongside them (Phil. 4:6; Heb. 13:15-16). This pattern runs throughout the Psalms.
>
> If our praise balanced our asking, we'd receive more. Praise demonstrates faith, and faith brings the answer. If you don't seem to have faith, praise God much for what you've received and what you're going to receive—and watch faith grow until you dare believe God for anything.

> **(4) "THY KINGDOM COME."** This puts God's interests and His kingdom first—the needs of others before your own desires. This is loving your neighbor as yourself. The kingdom of God and its interests are the chief subjects for prayer.

> **(5) "THY WILL BE DONE, AS IN HEAVEN, SO IN EARTH."** This accepts and agrees with the Father's will in all things. It's submission to God's will in prayer, yet it refuses to be denied concerning everything He's promised as His will.
>
> If we were just as stubborn in refusing failure in prayer as we are in accepting it, there would be very little failure. (See Point III above concerning God's will.)

> **(6) "GIVE US DAY BY DAY OUR DAILY BREAD."** After you've followed the instructions above—you have the right relationship with God, you've directed your prayer to the right source, you've worshipped Him not just to get from Him but because you love Him, you've put kingdom interests first, you have definite promises before you that cover your needs, and you accept what you're praying for as God's will—then you're ready to make your petition known.
>
> Sadly, most people who pray have their own interests first and always. All they know about prayer is "Give me, give me, give me"—and that's about all God hears. This is fine in its proper place, but we must show God we have His interests at heart as well as others', that we love Him, and that we want to bless others regardless of our personal needs. When Solomon took this attitude, God heard him (1 Ki. 4:4-15).
>
> When Charles Page, a multi-millionaire from Tulsa, Oklahoma, prayed for his wife's healing and tried to bargain with God—promising to help the poor if God would heal her—he got no answer. He went back to God, confessed his bargaining, and promised to help the poor because it was right, not because he wanted something in return. His wife was instantly healed, and he gave millions to the poor over many years.

> **(7) "AND FORGIVE US OUR SINS; FOR WE ALSO FORGIVE EVERY ONE THAT IS INDEBTED TO US."** Confession of sins and making things right with others goes a long way in getting answers to prayer. You can't expect to keep sinning or have a wrong attitude toward others and still be heard by God.
>
> If people would quit the sin business, live in peace, and love others as the gospel teaches, answers could be expected without going through a long process of rebuilding faith. In Mt. 6:14, Jesus based answered prayer on showing mercy to others. When we forgive, we can expect God to forgive. When we're kind, good, and merciful, we can expect the same from God.

> **(8) "LEAD US NOT INTO TEMPTATION, BUT DELIVER US FROM EVIL."** Guidance and deliverance from God should be part of your prayer life—and you can expect it when you've faithfully met all the conditions above.

### 3. THE BOLDNESS OF GOD'S FRIENDS (Lk. 11:5-8; Heb. 4:14-16; 10:19-23)

One of the greatest secrets of asking and receiving is being bold enough to come to the throne of grace. This shows both your immediate need and your friendship with God.

When people are afraid to approach God boldly, that fear indicates lack of faith and trust—which in turn suggests either that the heart isn't right or that the person is under direct attack from demons. When you live clean and holy before God, there's a natural confidence that comes from walking closely with Him.

If you're living a true Christian life and still feel afraid or timid about coming to God, you may be under demonic attack. In that case, resist the devil, ignore those feelings, and approach God with full confidence. You can always tell whether you've sinned and are under conviction or not. If there's been no known sin, then evil spirits are likely trying to counterfeit conviction where no sin has been committed.

Jesus illustrates this boldness in friendship in Lk. 11:5-8. One friend went to another for bread at midnight because he had to have it right then—not the next day. He got it not because of friendship alone, but because of his bold persistence. Think of it like this: friendship creates the boldness to make demands on a friend. The greater the friendship, the greater the boldness in times of need.

In Heb. 4:14-16, we're commanded to come boldly to the throne of grace to obtain mercy and help in time of need. This boldness is based on the fact that we have a great high priest who is touched by our weaknesses and has been tempted in all points just like us. With such a mediator between God and us, with such a friend representing us before the Father, how could we fail? (Rom. 8:34; 1 Tim. 2:4-6).

The basis for coming to God in Heb. 10:19-23 is the blood of Jesus Christ, which gives us a new way of approach so we can "draw near to God with a true heart in full assurance of faith, having our hearts sprinkled from an evil conscience . . . Let us hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering; for he is faithful that promised."

### 4. FAITH IN THE CERTAINTY OF ANSWERED PRAYER AND THE INFINITE FATHERHOOD OF GOD (Lk. 11:9-13)

This has already been covered in Point III above.

### 5. THE ALL-COMPREHENSIVE GIFT (Lk. 11:9-13)

Jesus promised that God's children have a greater gift due them than what they received in the new birth. The gift of the Holy Spirit is promised here to every child of God, and His help is one of the secrets of successful praying.

The fact that this gift is promised only to God's children proves it's not the same as receiving the Holy Spirit in measure at the new birth, which every child of God receives when born-again (Rom. 8:9, 14-16).

This all-inclusive gift refers to the fullness of the Holy Spirit in the believer's life after the new birth. You must be a child of God before you can receive the fullness of the Spirit. This was true for Jesus (Mt. 3:16-17; Lk. 4:18-21; Jn. 3:34), the disciples (Acts 2), the Samaritans (Acts 9:5-25), Paul (Acts 9:1-17), and the Ephesians (Acts 19:1-7).

In Cornelius's case, God saved them and gave them the Spirit baptism all at once (Acts 10:43-48; 11:14-18; 15:7-11)—so you can receive the Spirit baptism at the same time you receive the new birth if you believe. If you don't, which is true of many, then you should ask God for it, as Jesus taught: "How much more shall your heavenly Father give the Holy Spirit to them that ask him?" (Lk. 11:13).

This whole passage (Lk. 11:9-13) proves the subject is gifts for people who are already children. God gave this gift to Cornelius and his household when they were saved to prove there's not necessarily a time gap between various blessings of God. If there is a gap, it's because individual knowledge and faith limit how God can work.

The Holy Spirit is one of our great helpers in prayer: "Likewise the Spirit also helpeth our infirmities: for we know not what we should pray for as we ought: but the Spirit himself maketh intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered" (Rom. 8:26-27, 34).

With both Christ and the Holy Spirit as our helpers, how could there possibly be failure in prayer? When we receive the all-comprehensive gift, we receive the all-comprehensive Helper in prayer. So let's have faith and boldness in prayer for all we want in this life and the life to come.

### 6. THE POWER OF PERSEVERING PRAYER (Lk. 18:1-8)

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Law
lessons.content:
# The Dispensation of Law

**(Ex. 12:37—Mt. 2:23)**

## I. The Definition of the Dispensation of Law

We call this period "the Dispensation of Law" because of the law God gave to Moses. This law became the guiding framework for faith and daily life throughout this entire era.

## II. The Length of the Dispensation of Law

This dispensation stretched from Moses all the way to Christ — or more specifically, from the exodus out of Egypt to John the Baptist's preaching about the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 11:12-13; Lk. 16:16). It lasted more than 1,718 years, and here's how we can trace that out:

### 1. FROM THE EXODUS FROM EGYPT TO THE ENTRANCE INTO CANAAN WAS OVER 41 YEARS

Israel camped at Sinai for one year and one month (Num. 10:11-12). After that, they wandered in the wilderness for forty years following their rebellion at Kadesh-barnea — giving us over 41 years total (Num. 14:33-34; 32:13; Deut. 2:7; 8:2-4; 29:5; Josh. 5:6; Acts 7:23-42; 13:18; Heb. 3:9, 17).

### 2. FROM THE ENTRANCE INTO CANAAN TO THE REIGN OF SAUL WAS OVER 520 YEARS

Here's the breakdown:

| Period | Duration |
|--------|----------|
| (1) Joshua in Canaan (Josh. 14:7; 24:29) | 30 years |
| (2) Elders that outlived Joshua (Judg. 2:7–3:7) | ? years |
| (3) The first servitude (Judg. 3:7-8) | 8 years |
| (4) Othniel, the first judge (Judg. 3:9-11) | 40 years |
| (5) The second servitude (Judg. 3:12-14) | 18 years |
| (6) Ehud, the second judge (Judg. 3:15-30) | 80 years |
| (7) The third servitude (Judg. 3:31) | ? years |
| (8) Shamgar, the third judge (Judg. 3:31) | ? years |
| (9) The fourth servitude (Judg. 4:1-3) | 20 years |
| (10) Deborah and Barak, the fourth and fifth judges (Judg. 4:4–5:31) | 40 years |
| (11) The fifth servitude (Judg. 6:1-10) | 7 years |
| (12) Gideon, the sixth judge (Judg. 6:11–8:32) | 40 years |
| (13) Abimelech, the seventh judge (Judg. 8:33–9:57) | 3 years |
| (14) Tola, the eighth judge (Judg. 10:1-2) | 23 years |
| (15) Jair, the ninth judge (Judg. 10:3-5) | 22 years |
| (16) The sixth servitude (Judg. 10:6-18) | 18 years |
| (17) Jephthah, the tenth judge (Judg. 11:1–12:7) | 6 years |
| (18) Ibzan, the eleventh judge (Judg. 12:8-10) | 7 years |
| (19) Elon, the twelfth judge (Judg. 12:11-12) | 10 years |
| (20) Abdon, the thirteenth judge (Judg. 12:13-15) | 8 years |
| (21) The seventh servitude (Judg. 13:1) | 40 years |
| (22) Samson, the fourteenth judge (Judg. 13:2–16:31) | 20 years |
| (23) Civil war (Judg. 17:1–21:25) | ? years |
| (24) Eli, the fifteenth judge (1 Sam. 4:1; 15-18) | 40 years |
| (25) Samuel, the sixteenth judge, at least 40 years between Eli and Saul (1 Sam. 4:1, 15-18; 7:2-15; 8:1-5) | 40 years |
| **TOTAL:** | **520 years** |

You'll notice that Scripture doesn't give us definite dates for points (2), (7), (8), and (23), so the actual period was likely longer than 520 years. The 450 years mentioned in Acts 13:19-21 "until Samuel the prophet" probably begins with Point (3) and ends with Point (24). This list brings us down to Samuel, leaving those undated periods unspecified.

### 3. FROM SAUL TO THE BABYLONIAN CAPTIVITY WAS 513 YEARS

| Period | Duration |
|--------|----------|
| (1) Saul's reign over all Israel (Acts 13:21) | 40 years |
| (2) David's reign over Judah and all Israel (2 Sam. 5:4) | 40 years |
| (3) Solomon's reign over all Israel (1 Ki. 11:42) | 40 years |
| (4) Rehoboam's reign over Judah after the kingdom was divided 1009 b.c. (1 Ki. 14:21) | 17 years |
| (5) Abijam's reign over Judah (1 Ki. 15:1-2) | 3 years |
| (6) Asa's reign over Judah (1 Ki. 15:10) | 41 years |
| (7) Jehoshaphat's reign over Judah (1 Ki. 22:41-42) | 25 years |
| (8) Jehoram's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 8:16-17) | 8 years |
| (9) Ahaziah's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 8:25-26) | 1 year |
| (10) Athaliah's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 11:3) | 6 years |
| (11) Jehoash's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 12:1) | 40 years |
| (12) Amaziah's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 14:1-2) | 29 years |
| (13) Azariah or Uzziah (2 Ki. 15:1-2) | 52 years |
| (14) Jotham's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 15:32) | 16 years |
| (15) Ahaz's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 16:1-2) | 16 years |
| (16) Hezekiah's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 18:1-2, six years before and twenty-three years after the ten-tribe captivity, 749 b.c.) | 29 years |
| (17) Manasseh's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 21:1) | 55 years |
| (18) Amon's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 21:19) | 2 years |
| (19) Josiah's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 22:1) | 31 years |
| (20) Jehoahaz's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 23:31-33) | 3 months |
| (21) Jehoiakim's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 23:36) | 11 years |
| (22) Jehoiakin's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 24:6-16) | 3 months |
| (23) Zedekiah's reign over Judah (2 Ki. 24:18) | 11 years |
| **TOTAL:** | **513 years** |

The 480-year period ending with Solomon's fourth year (1 Ki. 6:1) runs from the entrance into Canaan to that point, but it excludes the 111 years of servitude and the three years of chaos under Abimelech. So the 480 years represents Israel's time as a nation free from foreign oppression and civil conflict.

### 4. FROM THE BABYLONIAN CAPTIVITY TO THE RESTORATION WAS 164 YEARS

| Period | Duration |
|--------|----------|
| (1) The Babylonian captivity (Jer. 25:11-12; 29:10) | 70 years |
| (2) Darius the Mede (Dan. 5:31; 8:3, 20; 9:1-2) | 2 years |
| (3) Cyrus the Persian (Ezra 1:1-4; 3:8; Is. 44:28; 45:1) | 9 years |
| (4) Cambyses, the son of Cyrus (Ezra 5:1–6:22) | 7 years |
| (5) Darius I, of profane history (Ezra 5:1–6:22) | 35 years |
| (6) Xerxes, the fourth king of Dan. 11:1-3 | 21 years |
| (7) Artaxerxes (Neh. 2:1) | 20 years |
| **TOTAL:** | **164 years** |

(The dates for the Medo-Persian kings were taken from the Encyclopedia Britannica.)

### 5. FROM THE RESTORATION TO CHRIST WAS 483 YEARS

In Dan. 9:24-27, we find a prophecy about Seventy Weeks. Each "Week" represents seven years, as we can see from the last half of the Seventieth Week being 3½ years (Dan. 12:7; Rev. 12:6, 14; 13:5). This makes the entire Seventy Weeks equal to 490 years — or 483 years to the crucifixion of Christ. (We'll dive deeper into these Seventy Weeks in Lesson Forty.)

This 483-year period begins with the command to restore Jerusalem and ends with the Messiah's crucifixion. It includes the Seven Weeks and the 62 Weeks of Dan. 9:24-26, but not the one Week of Dan. 9:27.

The Seven Weeks (49 years) covered the restoration of the temple and city — starting with the command to rebuild and ending when the work was complete. The 62 Weeks (434 years) began immediately after the city's completion and ended with the Messiah's crucifixion. The remaining one Week awaits fulfillment in the future, when Israel will once again be a nation in full control of Palestine.

### The Grand Total

When we add up all five periods, we get 1,721 years or more. But since this age really ended with John the Baptist's ministry (Mt. 11:11-13; Lk. 16:16), we subtract Christ's three-year ministry, leaving us with 1,718 years.

This brings our total from Adam to Christ to 4,234 years or more. Add the 1,915 years since the crucifixion, and we reach 6,143 years from Adam to 1949. Factor in the years from 1949 to the Antichrist's revelation, the seven years from his revelation to the Millennium, and the Millennium itself — and we arrive at a grand total of 7,149 years or more from Adam to the end of the Millennium. What happens after the Millennium? We'll explore that in Lessons Forty-nine through Fifty-two.

## III. The Favorable Beginning of this Dispensation

Never did fallen humanity have a better starting point than Israel did at the dawn of this dispensation. Think about what they had witnessed! They saw God's mighty power through signs and wonders in Egypt, during the exodus, and throughout forty-plus years in the wilderness. No people in history had ever experienced such miracles or such displays of God's power.
