God spoke the law to them in an audible voice (Deut. 5:22-24). He was visibly present with them — a pillar of fire by night and a cloud by day (Exod. 14:19-31). He supernaturally provided bread and kept their clothes from wearing out for forty years (Deut. 29:5; Exod. 16:35).

He gave them quail and water whenever they needed it (Exod. 16:12; 17:6). He removed sickness from among them — not a single weak person could be found in all their tribes (Exod. 15:26; 23:25; Ps. 105:37; 107:20). He gave them the riches of Egypt as payment for their long years of slavery (Ps. 105:37; Exod. 12:36).

He gave them revelations, made personal appearances, and led them to victory over every enemy (Exod. 24:9-11; Deut. 4:6-8; 5:1-6; Josh. 5:13-15). He brought them into a land flowing with milk and honey and promised prosperity and protection if they would serve Him (Num. 13:27; Exod. 23:20-33).

He gave them a complete legal code covering every aspect of human life. He made new eternal covenants with them and gave promises of blessing that addressed every human need — in this life and the next. They had the old covenants and promises about God's eternal purpose on Earth with them. They had rich spiritual experiences of the gospel, as we'll see in our next lesson.

What more could they have asked for? What better beginning could there possibly have been?

## IV. The Test for Man in This Dispensation

The test for people in this dispensation was straightforward: obey the Law of Moses in every detail. This law included three main categories:

> 1. **THE COMMANDMENTS** (Exod. 20:1-17; Deut. 5:1-21). These express the heart of God's holy will for every area of human life. They summarize all of God's laws, judgments, and ordinances.

> 2. **THE JUDGMENTS** (Exod. 21:1–23:33). These spell out how people should relate to one another in daily life. They're the detailed social regulations that flow from the Ten Commandments.

> 3. **THE ORDINANCES** (Exod. 24:12–31:18). These outline the religious life and worship of God's people. They're the detailed religious regulations that expand on the Ten Commandments.

## V. The Purpose of God in This Dispensation

Every previous dispensational test had proven humanity a failure — utterly incapable of doing God's will on their own. So in this new age, God purposed to give people His will in complete detail. Think of it like a parent who, after watching a child repeatedly make poor decisions, finally sits down and spells out exactly what they expect — leaving nothing to guesswork.

God planned to organize a commonwealth of nations with Israel at the head, governed by His laws and led by people He personally chose. He established a visible worship system and a place where His glory could shine continually — a place where He could maintain ongoing contact with humanity (Exod. 19:3-7; Deut. 28).

He also purposed to use Israel to destroy the second generation of giants — those offspring of the sons of God and daughters of men born after the flood — and to defeat the devil through the coming Messiah from Israel's line.

God intended to bless Israel so abundantly that they would become a living example to all nations. It's similar to how a successful business becomes a model for others: "Look what's possible when you do things right!" God wanted the nations to see Israel's prosperity and be drawn to turn from their idols to serve the true Living God. His purpose in other respects remained the same as in the Dispensation of Promise (See Lesson Fifteen, Point V).

### God Speaking Through Prophets

This dispensation marked the beginning of God speaking to people through prophets more than ever before (Heb. 1:1-3). Before this time, God Himself would come down to talk and personally intervene in human affairs — especially at the ending of one age and the beginning of another.

During the Dispensation of Innocence, God came and fellowshipped with humanity in the cool of the day. When humanity fell, God appeared personally to deal with them, driving them from the Garden and cursing the Earth. He started them under conscience with new conditions and responsibilities.

Humanity continued in the Dispensation of Conscience until wickedness grew so extreme that God came down again to bring judgment. He personally dealt with and spoke to people at the beginning and end of both Innocence and Conscience (Gen. 2:15-25; 3:1-24; 4:1-16; 6:9-22; 7:1-5); of Human Government (Gen. 8:15–9:17; 11:1-9); and of Promise (Gen. 12:1-4; 13:14-18; 15:1-21; 17:1-22; 18:1-33; 22:1; 26:2-5, 24-25; 31:3; 35:1).

There was a long period during the Dispensation of Promise when God's voice wasn't heard. Then at that period's end, He revealed Himself again (Exod. 2:23–4:31). From that point through the Dispensation of Law, He sent scores of prophets to His chosen people — until 400 years before Christ. After those 400 silent years, He began speaking through a prophet-forerunner and through His own Son (Mt. 3:1-17; 17:1-5; Heb. 1:1-3).

After the Son returned to Heaven, God spoke through His apostles and preachers, giving them gifts of the Holy Spirit according to His will (1 Cor. 1:18-31; 12:1-28; Eph. 4:11-16; Heb. 2:1-4). This remains God's program throughout the Age of Grace.

At the end of this Age of Grace, God will once again deal personally with the human race, sending His Son from heaven to establish an earthly kingdom, put down all rebellion, and rule the Earth in righteousness (Mt. 24:1–25:46; Lk. 21; 1 Cor. 15:24-28; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:1-12; Jude 14; Rev. 19:11-21; 20:1-15). Grace began with the Son's coming and will end the same way (Mt. 1-2; Rev. 19:11-21). Similarly, the Millennium will begin with war and end with war (Rev. 19:11-21; 20:1-10).

So we see that God is the Great Overseer of all His creation, guiding it toward a desired end — until the universe is cleansed of all rebellion and righteousness reigns forever (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Eph. 1:10; Rev. 21-22).

### God's Purpose in Giving the Law of Moses

> 1. God's purpose was for the Law of Moses to govern Israel until the Messiah came. It "prophesied until John" (Mt. 11:11-13; Lk. 16:16-17).

> 2. It stopped every mouth and made the whole world guilty before God, so He could have mercy on everyone alike: "Now we know that what things soever the law saith, it saith to them that are under the law: that every mouth may be stopped, and all the world become guilty before God" (Rom. 3:19-20).

> 3. It brought people to a knowledge of sin: "Therefore by the deeds of the law there shall no flesh be justified in his sight: for by the law is the knowledge of sin . . . The law worketh wrath: for where no law is there is no transgression sin is not imputed where there is no law . . . Moreover the law entered that the offense might abound" (Rom. 3:19-20; 4:15; 5:13, 20; 7:5, 7-14).

> 4. "It was added because of transgressions, till the seed should come . . . The law was our schoolmaster to bring us unto Christ, that we might be justified by faith" (Gal. 3:12-13, 19-24).

> 5. The law brought bondage: "These are the two covenants; the one from Mount Sinai which gendereth to bondage" (Gal. 4:24-31; 5:1).

> 6. The law was a "shadow of good things to come [imposed upon men] until the time of the reformation" (Col. 2:14-17; Heb. 8:5; 9:1-10, 24; 10:1).

### The Fulfillment of the Law and Why it Was Done Away in Christ

Given God's purposes in giving the law, it makes perfect sense that the law would be set aside once Christ fulfilled it and it had served its purpose. Think of it like scaffolding on a building — essential during construction, but removed once the building is complete.

That there was a change in God's law to humanity after Christ came, and that He fulfilled the law, isn't just implied in the passages above — it's stated directly:

"I am come to fulfill the law . . . one jot or tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled" (Mt. 5:17-18). "The law prophesied until John" (Lk. 16:16). "All things must be fulfilled, which was spoken in the law of Moses" (Lk. 24:44). "For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change also of the law" (Heb. 7:12). "The first covenant . . . was a figure for the time then present . . . imposed on them until the time of the reformation" (Heb. 9:1, 9, 10). "Cast out the bondwoman and her son" (an allegory teaching the law was to be done away, Gal. 4:24-31). "Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone that believeth" (no one is made righteous by keeping it, Rom. 3:19-31; 8:3; 10:4; Gal. 2:16-21; 5:4; Eph. 2:15; Col. 2:14-17; Acts 13:39; 15:5-29). "He taketh away the first, that he may establish the second" (Heb. 10:1-9).

The word "fulfill" means "completed," "brought to an end," "expired," and "finished" — in the same sense that many prophecies were fulfilled in Christ. Because they're fulfilled, they're no longer in force. They've served their purpose. See how "fulfill" is used in Mt. 1:22; 2:15, 17, 23; 4:14; 8:17; 12:17; 13:35; 21:4; 26:54, 56; 27:9, 35; and elsewhere.

Three times in 2 Cor. 3:6-18, Paul states that the Old Covenant (the Law of Moses) was done away. Once he says it was abolished, and that the New Covenant has taken its place. What specifically was done away and abolished? "The ministration of death written and engraven in stones" — the Ten Commandments, since they were the only part of the law written on stones.

Now, we're not obligated to keep the Ten Commandments of the Old Covenant. We're obligated to keep only those commandments that were brought into and made part of the New Covenant. You can find nine of the ten in the New Covenant — all except the fourth commandment about the sabbath.

Why wasn't the sabbath included? Because it was a special day commemorating Israel's deliverance from Egyptian bondage (Deut. 5:15). It was a day of rest specifically for Israel and for Gentiles who became part of that nation (Exod. 31:12-18; Ezek. 20:12-20).

Since Gentiles and Christians weren't delivered from Egyptian bondage, it doesn't make sense for us to commemorate that day as Israel was commanded to do. Christians are freed by the New Covenant from the Jewish sabbath and are free to observe any day (Rom. 14:5-6; Gal. 4:9-10; Col. 2:14-17). Early Christians observed the day of Christ's resurrection to celebrate their deliverance from slavery to sin and Satan (Jn. 20:1, 19, 26; Acts 20:7-11; 1 Cor. 16:1-2).

### What Was Wrong With the Law of Moses That it Needed Changing?

> 1. It had a sinful mediator (Gal. 3:10).

> 2. It could not give eternal inheritance (Rom. 4:13-25; Gal. 3:18).

> 3. It was dedicated by the blood of animals (Exod. 24:6-8; Heb. 9:16-22).

> 4. It brought condemnation, not life (Rom. 3:19-20; 4:15; 7:1-25; 8:3-4; Gal. 3:21).

> 5. It could not justify anyone (Acts 13:39; Gal. 2:16-21).

> 6. It could only benefit when kept perfectly, but it was impossible for anyone to keep perfectly on their own (Rom. 2:25; 7:7-25; 8:7; Gal. 3:10-25).

> 7. It brought everyone under a curse and executed them (Gal. 3:10-14).

> 8. It was not based on faith or grace (Gal. 3:11; Jn. 1:17).

> 9. It was a law of works and bondage (Rom. 3:26-31; Gal. 3:10-12; 4:21-31).

> 10. It gave knowledge of sin and made people sinners, but it couldn't save them from their sins (Rom. 3:19-20; 4:15; 7:7-25; 8:3-4).

> 11. It brought wrath instead of blessing (Rom. 4:15).

> 12. It wasn't the same as the gospel given to Abraham (Rom. 4:3-16; Gal. 3:6-18).

> 13. It was only a temporary law (Gal. 3:19-25; 2 Cor. 3:6-18; Eph. 2:15; Col. 2:14-17; Heb. 7:16).

> 14. It could never make anyone perfect (Heb. 7:11-12, 18-19; 9:9-10; 10:1-18).

> 15. It was set aside because of its weakness and lack of usefulness (Heb. 7:18).

> 16. It could not redeem or make people dead to sin (Rom. 8:1-4; Gal. 2:16-21; 3:13-21), but it could bring death and condemnation (Rom. 7:7-25; 2 Cor. 3:6-18).

> 17. It was not established by an oath, and neither were its priests — therefore both the law and priesthood could be changed (Heb. 7:12, 20-22).

> 18. It wasn't as good as the New Covenant (Heb. 7:22).

> 19. It had a constantly changing priesthood, while the New Covenant does not (Heb. 7:11-28).

> 20. It had sinful priests, while the New Covenant does not (Heb. 5:1-4; 7:26-28; 9:7).

> 21. It had a temporary, shadowy sanctuary (Heb. 8:1-5, 13; 9:9-25; 10:1-18).

> 22. It had a flawed ministry and a weaker foundation than the New Covenant (Heb. 8:6; 7:11-28).

> 23. It demanded righteousness but couldn't produce it (Rom. 4; 8:1-4; Gal. 3).

> 24. It was faulty (Heb. 8:7, 13), while the New Covenant is not (Jas. 1:25).

> 25. It kept people away from God (Heb. 9:7-8; 10:19-22).

> 26. It was made necessary by sin and was added and imposed until Christ should come (Gal. 3:19-25; Heb. 9:9-10, 23-28; 10:5-18).

> 27. It had animal sacrifices that had to be offered daily, while the New Covenant has Christ as a once-for-all sacrifice (Heb. 9:11-28; 10:3-4).

> 28. It was merely a shadow of things to come (Heb. 8:5; 9:11; 10:1-18).

> 29. It could not satisfy God's demands and could not impart the Holy Spirit or produce miracles (Gal. 2:21; 3:1-5; Rom. 8:1-4).

> 30. It was designed to be changed when Christ came (Gal. 2:19-21; 3:19-25; Heb. 7:11-22; 8:5-6; 9:9-10; 10:1-18).

## VI. The Means of God in Accomplishing His Purpose

God used several means to further His plan during this age: He completed Israel's organization and established them as a nation. He destroyed the giants in the land. He gave Israel complete victory over all their enemies. He manifested His glory and power. And He blessed Israel so abundantly that the surrounding nations could see that God is the true Living God.

## VII. The Failure of Israel in This Dispensation

The history of Israel during this dispensation reads like one long record of failure: in the wilderness, under Joshua, under the judges, under the kings, during the captivities, in the restoration — and finally reaching its climax in the rejection and crucifixion of their Messiah and the persecution of His followers until God's wrath came upon them in full. Let's trace these failures:

### 1. FAILURES IN THE WILDERNESS FOR FORTY-ONE YEARS

> (1) Murmured for water at Marah (Exod. 15:22-26)

> (2) Murmured for bread and meat (Exod. 16:1-22)

> (3) Murmured for water at Rephidim (Exod. 17:1-7)

> (4) Murmured for flesh the second time (Num. 11)

> (5) Murmured because of the giants (Num. 13:31–14:5)

> (6) Murmured for water the third time (Num. 20:1-12)

> (7) Murmured because of the death of the princes (Num. 16:41)

> (8) Murmured because of the hardships in travel (Num. 21:1-9)

> (9) Leaned on the arm of flesh (Exod. 18:1-27)

> (10) Sinned in worshiping the golden calf (Exod. 32)

> (11) Miriam and Aaron's sin of self-exaltation (Num. 12)

> (12) Refused to enter Canaan through unbelief (Num. 14)

> (13) Gainsaying of Korah (Num. 16; Jude 11)

> (14) Sin of adultery (Num. 25)

> (15) Sin of Moses (Num. 20:1-12; 27:14)

**NOTE:** Eleven different plagues and judgments fell upon Israel in the wilderness for these sins. Nine of them came because of the misuse of the tongue. This is still the cause of most trouble in the human race, as Scripture makes clear (Ps. 34:13; Prov. 6:17; 12:18; 21:23; Mk

lessons.title: The Old Testament Church
lessons.content:
## 18. THE OLD TESTAMENT CHURCH

### THE OLD TESTAMENT CHURCH

(Acts 7:38)

### I. What the Old Testament Church is Not

Before we dive into what the Old Testament Church actually is, let's clear away some common misconceptions. Understanding what it's *not* will help us grasp what it truly *is*.

> 1. It is not the New Testament Church of this Age of Grace (Mt. 16:18).

> 2. It is not the Gentiles of any or all past ages.

> 3. It is not the Jews who will become heavenly, glorified saints (Heb. 11).

> 4. It is not the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 3:2; 4:17).

> 5. It is not the kingdom of God (Mt. 21:43).

### II. What the Old Testament Church Is

The Old Testament Church was made up of living Jews and proselytes during the Old Testament period. The nation of Israel is actually called "the Church in the wilderness" (Acts 7:38).

Now, you might be surprised to see the word "church" used for Israel. The Greek word in this passage is *ekklesia* — the exact same word used for the New Testament Church.

It means "assembly," "a popular meeting," or "political gathering" — especially a religious congregation of people called out from the mass of humanity. Think of it like this: if you were called out of a crowded stadium to join a special group with a unique purpose, you'd be part of an *ekklesia*.

The word comes from two Greek roots: *ek* meaning "from" or "out of," and *kaleo* meaning "to call." In the Septuagint (the Greek Old Testament), it's used for the Hebrew *kahal*, from which we get our word "call."

This word appears 114 times in the New Testament — eighty times in the singular and thirty-four times in the plural. It's translated "assembly" (Acts 19:32, 39, 41); "church" (Mt. 16:18; 18:17; Acts 2:47; 5:11; 7:38; 8:1, 3; 11:22, 26; 12:1, 5; 13:1; 14:23, 27; 15:3, 4, 22; 18:22; 20:17, 28; Rom. 16:1, 5-23; 1 Cor. 1:2; 4:17; 6:4; 10:32; 11:18, 22; 12:28; 14:4, 5, 12, 19, 23, 28, 35; 15:9; 2 Cor. 1:1; Gal. 1:13; Eph. 1:22; 3:10, 21; 5:23-32; Phil. 3:6; 4:15; Col. 1:18, 24; 4:15-16; 1 Thess. 1:1; 2 Thess. 1:1; 1 Tim. 3:5, 15; 5:16; Phm. 2; Heb. 2:12; 12:23; Jas. 5:14; 1 Pet. 5:13; 3 Jn. 6, 9-10; Rev. 2:1, 8, 12, 18; 3:1, 7, 14); and "churches" (Acts 9:31; 15:41; 16:5; 19:37; Rom. 16:4, 16; 1 Cor. 7:17; 11:16; 14:33-34; 16:1, 19; 2 Cor. 8:1, 18-19, 23-24; 11:8, 28; 12:13; Gal. 1:2, 22; 1 Thess. 2:14; 2 Tim. 1:14; Rev. 1:4, 11, 20; 2:7, 11, 17, 23, 29; 3:6; 22:16).

Israel was a "called-out" nation in Old Testament times, just as Christians today are a "called-out" people from the world. Israel was supposed to be separate from the world and wholly given over to serving God and evangelizing the nations (Gen. 12:1-3).

Here's an important distinction: their calling was *earthly*, but the present Church's calling is *heavenly*. Israel was governed by natural laws and ruled by earthly men who were accountable to God for their leadership. They were promised an everlasting possession of the land.

They had the covenants, the promises, the callings, and the service of God — and they were supposed to bring the knowledge of God to all nations (Rom. 2:17-29; 9:4-7; 11:25-29).

Sadly, Israel failed in every century from her calling to the present day. God sent prophets and teachers time after time, urging them to fulfill their calling, but they rebelled more and more. Eventually, they had to be scattered among the nations.

They won't be fully gathered and made a blessing to all nations until their rejected Messiah returns in glory (Mt. 24:29-31; Rom. 11:25-29; Isa. 11:10-15).

The Old Testament Church was — and will continue to be — the earthly nation of Israel with an earthly calling forever. It was made up of living Jews and proselytes in Old Testament days, and it will be again in the future.

This company has always been made up of living earthly people, and it always will be. Those who have died are no longer members by virtue of their death. They will be resurrected to become part of the heavenly family of God and the bride of Christ (Heb. 11; 13:14; Eph. 3:15; Rev. 21:9).

### III. The Calling and Purpose of the Old Testament Church

As we noted, the Hebrew *kahal* carries the same meaning as the Greek *ekklesia*. It's used 123 times in the Old Testament and is translated "congregation" eighty-six times, "assembly" seventeen times, "company" seventeen times, and "multitude" three times.

Israel was called out of all nations to accomplish specific things in God's eternal earthly plan:

> 1. To remain separate from all nations (Gen. 12:1-3; Exod. 19:5-6).

> 2. To evangelize the nations (Gen. 12:1-3; Mt. 21:33-46; Rom. 9:4-5; 11:29).

> 3. To write, preserve, and transmit the Holy Scriptures (Jn. 1:16-17; Rom. 9:4-5; 1 Pet. 1:10-12; 2 Pet. 1:19-21).

> 4. To bring the Savior into the world (Gen. 49:10; Deut. 18:15-19; 2 Sam. 7; Isa. 9:6-7; Mic. 5:1-2; Lk. 1:32-35; Rom. 9:4-5; Heb. 2).

> 5. To save the world from physical and moral decay (Gen. 12:1-3; Exod. 20:1-19; Rom. 9:4-5; 2 Pet. 2:9).

> 6. To head the nations of the Earth in all ages under the government of their Messiah (Deut. 28:13; Isa. 9:6-7; Dan. 7:13-14; Lk. 1:32; Rom. 11:29). See Lesson Fifteen, Point V, for other reasons for Israel's calling.

### IV. The History of the Old Testament Church

The history of the Jews began about 4,100 years ago with the call of Abraham. For all these years, they have remained distinct and separate from all peoples, maintaining their own laws and customs.

They have produced some of the greatest men in human history. Their faithfulness to their calling has been temporary and local, but in the Millennium and future ages, it will be permanent and worldwide.

A brief history of Israel is recorded in Lesson Fifteen, Point VII, and Lesson Seventeen, Point VII. Practically all the important events of their history are in the Bible.

You can read about the origin of Israel in Genesis; the establishment of the theocracy in the other books of the Pentateuch; the entrance into Canaan in Joshua; the period of the judges in Judges; the monarchy and its development in 1 and 2 Samuel and 1 Kings; the division of the kingdom and the history of the two kingdoms down to the captivities in Kings and Chronicles; and the return from captivity in Ezra, Nehemiah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi.

From there until Christ, we have only scattered dates — except for the second century B.C., which is partially recorded in 1 and 2 Maccabees. The prophetic books provide much historical data from the reign of Amaziah (about 884 B.C.) to the return from captivity and the restoration (about 400 B.C.). Much of Israel's history during this period has been illuminated by monuments recently found in Palestine, Egypt, Babylon, Assyria, and other countries.

The history of Israel from Abraham to our day reveals continued oppression because of her sins. During this time, she has undergone numerous sieges, servitudes, and captivities.

The history of Jerusalem, her capital city, is especially striking. It has undergone twenty-eight sieges:

> 1. By the tribe of Judah against the Jebusites about 700 years before the founding of Rome (Judg. 1:8).

> 2. By David against the Jebusites (2 Sam. 5:6-10; 1 Chron. 11:4-7).

> 3. By Shishak, king of Egypt, against Judah (1 Ki. 14:25; 2 Chron. 12).

> 4. By the Philistines, Arabians, and Ethiopians against Judah (2 Chron. 21:16-17).

> 5. By Israel against Judah (2 Ki. 18:13–19:37).

> 6. By Syria and Israel against Judah (1 Chron. 28).

> 7. By Assyria against Judah (2 Ki. 18:13–19:37).

> 8. By Babylon against Judah (2 Chron. 36:6-7).

> 9. By Babylon against Judah (2 Chron. 36:10).

> 10. By Babylon against Judah (2 Chron. 36:17-20).

> 11. By Egypt against Judah after the captivities, 320 B.C.

> 12. By Scopus, a general of Alexander, about 299 B.C.

> 13. By Syria against Judah after the captivities, 203 B.C.

> 14. By Syria against Judah, 168 B.C.

> 15. By Syria against Judah, 162 B.C.

> 16. By Syria against Judah, 135 B.C.

> 17. By Hyrcanus, the priest of Aristobulus, 63 B.C.

> 18. By Pompey against Aristobulus, 63 B.C.

> 19. By Herod with a Roman army, who besieged the city in 39 B.C.

> 20. By Titus in 70 A.D. (Mt. 24:1-2; Lk. 19:42-44; 21:20-24).

> 21. By the Romans again in 135 A.D. against the false messiah, Bar-Cochebas, who had acquired possession of the ruins. The city was obliterated, renamed Elia Capitolina, and a temple to Jupiter was erected.

> 22. After 400 years of so-called Christian colonization, Chrosroes the Persian (about 559 A.D.) swept through the country; thousands were massacred, and the Church of the Holy Sepulchre was destroyed. The emperor Heraclius afterwards defeated him and restored the city and the church.

> 23. The Caliph Omar, in 636-637 A.D., besieged the city against Heraclius. It passed into the hands of the Turks, in whose hands it remained until 1917 A.D.

> 24. Afdal, the Vizier of the Caliph of Egypt, defeated the Muslims in 1098 A.D. and pillaged the city.

> 25. In 1099 it was besieged by the army of the First Crusade.

> 26. In 1187 it was besieged by Saladin for seven weeks.

> 27. The wild Tartar hordes in 1244 captured and plundered the city, slaughtering the monks and priests.

> 28. It was taken by General Allenby and the British in 1917 A.D. At this writing (1949), there is war between the Jews and Arabs over control of the city.

The history of Israel from the rejection of their Messiah to our day reveals terrible sufferings.

Beginning about 50 A.D., 30,000 were killed by the Romans. In 66 A.D., Titus with a Roman army of 66,000 met Josephus and killed 40,000 Jews and subjugated Galilee.

In 70 A.D., Titus with 100,000 men captured Jerusalem after four months. One million were killed, 97,000 were captured, and the city was destroyed — fulfilling Dan. 9:26; Mt. 24:1-2; Lk. 21:20-24.

In 135 A.D., the Jews revolted again. After a war of three-and-a-half years, 580,000 were killed, the land was made utterly desolate, a plow was run over Zion (Mic. 3:12), and Jews were forbidden to enter the land. Since then, Jews have been few in the land until recent years.

From 140 to 312 A.D., the Jews flourished in Rome with imperial favor and played a significant part in the persecutions of Christians. When Constantine became Emperor, they lost imperial favor and became a condemned sect.

In England in 1020 A.D., Canute banished them from the country. In 1096 A.D., war was waged against Jews who would not submit to baptism.

Henry II made them pay $291,000.00 to help the crusades. In 1189 A.D., murderous riots broke out against the Jews, and every Jewish home in London was plundered.

In 1190 A.D., the chief rabbi of York and 500 Jews were besieged until they slew one another. In 1272 A.D., Edward I claimed to own the Jews and all they possessed, letting them get rich only to rob them. He finally drove them from England, and for four centuries no Jew was found there.

In France in 1306 A.D., 100,000 Jews were stripped, robbed, and banished. In ten years they were allowed to return, but fanatical shepherds killed thousands. In 1683 A.D., they were again ordered to leave France and her colonies and weren't permitted to own real estate there until 1723.

In 1348-1350 A.D., the black plague swept Europe but affected Jews much less because of their simple life and the hygienic requirements of the law. They were suspected of poisoning Europe's waters.

In Germany, thousands were killed by cauldron, fire, and many other means. They were banished from Prague and Constantinople.

In Spain during the Inquisition, thousands disappeared never to return. They were finally banished from Spain. Many were sold into slavery. Their sufferings were indescribable, and for 200 years they were not permitted to enter Spain.

### V. The Present State of the Old Testament Church

At present, the Jews enjoy relative peace, except in many parts of Europe and Asia where they are again being persecuted, killed, and used as slaves. They are looking forward to a homeland of their own again.

Their ambition will be partially realized before they face another period of persecution in their land under the Antichrist. In fact, even now the Jews have a nation of their own again with about 700,000 population.

They have recently fought a war with all the Arab states — Egypt, Syria, TransJordan, Iraq, Iran, and others — and have won the respect of many nations. Various nations are now establishing diplomatic relations with the new state.

Many thousands of Jews are returning to the land of their fathers. But their final restoration awaits the return of their Messiah in glory to deliver them from the Gentiles and set up an eternal kingdom over them, as predicted by the prophets.

### VI. The Future of the Old Testament Church

When it comes to the future of the Jews, God's Word speaks in remarkable detail. Nothing is clearer in the Bible than this subject. Let's look at the key facts:

**1. THE FACT OF THEIR RESTORATION.** This is called the "second time to recover the remnant of his people."

The first time was the restoration of Israel after the Babylonian captivity, as in the days of Ezra and Nehemiah. Since that first restoration, Israel became a nation again, then rejected their Messiah and crucified Him. Because of this, they were led away captive into all nations, and Jerusalem has been "trodden down of the Gentiles," just as Jesus predicted in Lk. 21:20-24.

The Scriptures are so clear on Israel's future restoration that we'll simply quote a few of the many hundreds of passages revealing this truth.

Isaiah said, "It shall come to pass in that day [the Millennium], that the Lord shall set his hand again the second time to recover the remnant of his people . . . he shall set up an ensign for the nations, and shall assemble the outcasts of Israel, and gather the dispersed of Judah from the four corners of the earth" (Isa. 11:10-16).

"Fear not [Israel]: for I am with thee: I will bring thy seed from the east, and gather thee from the west, I will say to the north, Give up; and to the south, Keep not back: bring my sons from far, and my daughters from the ends of the earth" (Isa. 43:1-7).

Jeremiah foretold that the days will come "that it shall no more be said, The Lord liveth, that brought up the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt; But, The Lord liveth, that brought up the children of Israel from the north, and from all lands whither I have driven them: and I will bring them again into their land that I gave unto their fathers" (Jer. 16:14-15).

"I will save thee from afar, and thy seed from the land of their captivity; and Jacob shall return, and shall be in rest, and be quiet, and none shall make him afraid . . . though I make an end of all nations whither I have scattered thee, yet will I not make a full end of thee: but I will correct thee in measure, and will not leave thee altogether unpunished . . . I will punish all that oppress them . . . Ye shall be my people, and I will be your God . . . In the latter days ye shall consider it. And at that time . . . I will build thee . . . I will bring them from the north country, and gather them from the coasts of the earth . . . He that scattered Israel will gather him, and keep him, as a shepherd doth a flock . . . they shall come and sing in the height of Zion" (Jer. 30:10-24; 31:1-40; 32:37-44; 33:7-26).

Ezekiel also prophesied of Israel's final gathering: "I will even gather you from the people, and assemble you out of the countries where you have been scattered, and I will give you the land of Israel" (Ezek. 11:17-21).

"As I live, saith the Lord God, . . . I will bring you out from the people and will gather you out of the countries wherein ye are scattered . . . I will bring you into the land of Israel" (Ezek. 20:33-44).

"When I shall have gathered the house of Israel from the people among whom they are scattered . . . then shall they dwell in their land" (Ezek. 28:25-26; 34:11-31; 36:16-38).

"I will take the children of Israel from among the heathen . . . and bring them into their own land. And I will make them one nation in the land upon the mountains of Israel; and one king shall be king over them all: and they shall be no more two nations, neither shall they be divided into two kingdoms any more at all . . . my sanctuary shall be in the midst of them for evermore" (Ezek. 37:1-28; 47:13–48:35).

Other Old Testament prophets speak of this final regathering:

"The children of Israel shall abide many days without a king, and without a prince, and without a sacrifice, and without an image, and without an ephod, and without teraphim: Afterward shall the children of Israel return, and seek the Lord their God, and David their king; and shall fear the Lord and his goodness in the latter days" (Hos. 3:4-5).

"For, behold, in those days, and in that time, when I shall bring again the captivity of Judah and Jerusalem . . . the Lord will be the hope of his people, and the strength of the children of Israel . . . Judah shall dwell for ever, and Jerusalem from generation to generation" (Joel 3. See also Amos 9:9-15; Obadiah 15-21; Mic. 4:1-13; 5:3-15; 7:7-20; Zeph. 3:8-20; Zech. 8:1-8, 20-23; 12:1-14; 13:1-9; 14:1-21; Mal. 4:5-6).

Jesus predicted that Israel will be finally gathered at His second advent (Mt. 24:29-31), and the apostles taught that God would build again the house of David after the Church age (Acts 15:13-18; Rom. 11:25-29).

So it's clear from both Testaments that Israel is yet to be regathered, converted, and ruled by their Messiah forever (Lk. 1:32-35). The reason they are now scattered is their rebellion against God (Lev. 26; Deut. 4:22-28; 28:1-68; Judg. 2:11-19; 1 Sam. 8:6; 2 Ki. 21:11; 25:3; Jer. 15:4; Mt. 23:37-39; 27:25).

**2. THE TIME OF THEIR RESTORATION.** Israel's partial restoration will come before the Antichrist reigns, but their final restoration will occur at the close of "the times of the Gentiles" at Christ's second coming (Lk. 21:24; Mt. 23:38-39; 24:27-31; Zech. 12:10–14:21; Isa. 11:10-11).

**3. THE MANNER OF THEIR RESTORATION.** They will be gathered back unconverted (Ezek. 36:24-27). Before their conversion, they will go through the greatest period of trouble they have ever experienced — the great tribulation — which will bring them back to their God.

After their restoration and conversion to their Messiah, they will have a most glorious future. They will become the richest and most powerful nation on Earth.

The land of Palestine is already rich in natural resources and coveted by many nations. This partially explains why Palestine will become the center of many wars just before Christ's second coming.

Who among the nations wouldn't want the mineral wealth of the Dead Sea alone? According to the British Government, it's estimated to be worth about $1.19 trillion — nearly three times the total wealth of the United States!

Here's the fascinating breakdown: The Dead Sea covers 340 square miles, with its surface 1,292 feet below Mediterranean Sea level. It contains potash amounting to 1.3 billion tons, valued at $68.040 billion; bromide 835 million tons, valued at $252.72 billion; salt 11.9 billion tons, valued at $46.17 billion; gypsum 81 million tons, valued at $116.64 million; and magnesium chloride 22 billion tons, valued at $797.04 billion.

### VII. Who Belong to the Old Testament Church

The earthly Jews — the natural seed of Abraham — who obeyed the covenant terms and met the conditions of the promises belong to the "called-out" people in the wilderness.

Those who sinned were cut off from the covenant blessings, just as people in the present Church are cut off through sin (1 Cor. 10:1-13; Heb. 2:1-4; 3:7–4:11; Jude 5).

Think of it like membership in a club that requires certain conduct. If you violate the rules, you lose the privileges — even if you were once a member in good standing.

Saints who lived before Abraham's calling and the founding of Israel couldn't be considered part of the Jewish "called-out" body, simply because they lived before that time.

In a technical sense, those who died "in faith" and in a justified state before God during the whole period from Abraham to Christ don't belong to the Old Testament Church either. Why? Because it's an earthly Church with an earthly calling.

Those who died will be resurrected to become immortal and will have rulership over the future Old Testament Church (the Jews) after Christ comes. In other words, God's promises and callings with Israel are irrevocable and earthly, connected with earthly living people forever (Rom. 11:29).

These promises will be fulfilled with the earthly Jews of the future when they are reconciled to God (Rom. 11). Jews who have died and become part of the heavenly, glorified family of God will have no part in the earthly fulfillment of Israel's promises. By their death and resurrection to immortality, they become severed from the natural people of Israel and its earthly blessings.

Neither the New Testament Church nor future living Gentiles belong to the Old Testament Church. They will have no part in Israel's restoration or in any prophecies concerning the Jews in their land in the eternal future.

So we conclude that the Jews of today and the future make up the original Old Testament Church, called out of all nations to evangelize and reconcile them to God. The Jews were temporarily set aside from their calling because they failed to fulfill it, but they will be redeemed and will yet accomplish their calling among all nations in the Millennium.

The Old Testament Church is an eternal "called-out" body of people, merely temporarily inactive and set aside because of sin. But it will become the channel of blessings to all nations in the future, after the New Testament Church has fulfilled its earthly ministry (Isa. 52:7; 61:6; 66:18-21; Rom. 11; Acts 15:13-18).

### VIII. The Marriage of the Old Testament Church

You may have heard that the Jews are the Bride of God the Father, while the present Church is the Bride of Christ. But this isn't quite accurate.

It's generally held that Israel is now God's wife and that the present Church isn't yet Christ's wife but will become so at the marriage of the Lamb after the rapture. But there's an inconsistency here: if Israel is God's wife now through covenant relationship, then the body of Christ is also already married to Him on the same basis.

**Was Israel the Wife of God in Old Testament Times?**

The Bible definitely states that Israel was God's wife, that she is now the estranged or divorced wife of God, and that she will become His wife again in the future. Israel is called the "wife" of God in multiple passages (Isa. 54:1-6; Jer. 3:1-25; Hos. 1:1-11; 2:1-23; Mal. 2:14-16).

In what sense could Israel be called God's "wife"? It can only be by covenant relationship — not physical. And if by covenant relationship, then she became God's covenant wife when He made covenants with her through Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Moses, and David.

Only those who entered covenant relationship with God can be considered married or united to Him by covenant. Abraham, as the father and source of the chosen family, was as much united to God as any of his descendants.

Think of it like a marriage contract: the different covenants made between God and the chosen race were entered voluntarily and were binding on both parties from the moment the contract was finalized. That's why Israel can be called the covenant wife of God.

In Jeremiah, Hosea, and other books, Israel is pictured as divorced from God because she broke the covenant terms and refused to continue in covenant relationship. She will remain divorced until her final conversion and reconciliation to God.

Then Israel will once again be considered God's wife, just as she was in Old Testament times, because of the New Covenant God will make with her (Jer. 31:31-40; Ezek. 36:16-38; 37:15-28; Hos. 2:14-23; Rom. 11:25-32; Heb. 8:6-13; 10:16-18).

So we conclude that Israel was God's wife in the past, is currently divorced, and will become His covenant wife again in the future.

**Now, what about the idea that Israel is only the wife of God the Father?** This teaching doesn't harmonize with Scripture for several important reasons:

1. Any person or group entering relationship with God — whether by covenant or by actual re-creation — does so equally with God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit. Israel's God was "one [united] God" (Deut. 6:4), existing in three separate and distinct persons, as we saw in Lesson Four (1 Jn. 5:7-8). Therefore, Israel being the covenant people of the Triune God is related to each person in the "one" God through the same covenant terms.

2. It's impossible for God the Father to have a wife with which the Son has nothing to do or isn't related to. The same is true of the Spirit. Likewise, the Son cannot have a wife unrelated to the Father or Spirit by the same covenant and redemptive work. Such teaching would make the Father, Son, and Spirit independent of each other regarding different groups of redeemed people — something the Bible never teaches, as proven in Lesson Four, Point III.

3. Both Testaments speak of the Son as Israel's direct, personal, and visible king — meaning He must be related to the Jews as much as the Father or Spirit (Isa. 9:6, 7; Mic. 5:1-2; Lk. 1:32-35). Would the Father permit the Son to take His own wife and rule over her forever if the Son had no covenant relationship with Israel?

4. The whole family of God in Heaven and Earth is one family (Eph. 3:14, 15). It includes all spiritual and material, glorified and natural, heavenly and earthly free moral agents who are in harmony with God. God is the Father of all, the Son is the Savior of all, and the Spirit is the Helper of all (Eph. 4:4-6). If God the Father is the Father of all, isn't He the Father of New Testament saints as well as Old Testament saints? If the Son is the Savior of all, isn't He the Savior of Old Testament saints as well as New Testament saints? If the Spirit is the Creator and Helper of all, hasn't He been the agent of regeneration for Old Testament saints as well as New Testament saints? The relationship of the Father, Son, and Spirit is the same with each redeemed person and must be the same with each redeemed company of saints. It's unscriptural to teach that the Father has a bride (Old Testament saints), the Son has a bride (New Testament saints), and the Holy Spirit has no bride. It's equally erroneous to teach that the Son won't marry the New Testament Church but will marry Israel, as some teach.

We conclude that Israel is simply one part of the great universal family of God, related to each person of the Triune God equally according to His respective relationships.

### IX. The Blessings and Glory of the Old Testament Church

There's a widespread misconception among Christians that Old Testament saints didn't experience the New Testament spiritual blessings promised to the present Church.

Some theories suggest that only a few special prophets were filled with the Spirit, that these were temporary anointings, and that Old Testament saints weren't born again, sanctified, made holy, or blessed with the many graces available today.

Every one of these ideas is mistaken, as the following facts from both Testaments demonstrate:

**Old Testament Saints Experienced Many Spiritual and Physical Blessings Such As:**

> 1. The Lord as their Savior and salvation (Exod.

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Grace
lessons.content:
# The Dispensation of Grace

(Mt. 3:1—Rev. 19:10)

## I. The Definition of the Dispensation of Grace

We call this period "the Dispensation of Grace" because God's grace shines through everything He does during this time. As John put it, "The law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ" (Jn. 1:16, 17).

Let's take a closer look at what grace actually means and how it works in every age.

### 1. The Definition of the Word "Grace"

At its heart, grace means the free, eternal, and completely unearned love and favor God shows toward the beings He created—whether human or angelic—who are capable of knowing Him and being morally responsible.

Think of it like this: grace is the spring, the source, the very fountainhead from which all of God's blessings flow to everything He has made (Jn. 1:14-17; 3:16; Rom. 3:24; 5:17-21; 11:5, 6; 2 Cor. 9:8; Eph. 1:6, 7; 2:5-8; Jas. 4:6; 1 Pet. 5:5).

The Greek word *charis* appears 156 times in the New Testament. It's translated "grace" 130 times, "favor" six times, "thank" and "thankworthy" twelve times, "pleasure" twice, and "acceptable," "benefits," "gift," "gracious," "joy," and "liberality" once each.

Interestingly, you won't find this word in Matthew or Mark. But it shows up eight times in Luke, four times in John, sixteen times in Acts, a whopping 110 times in Paul's letters, sixteen times in James through Jude, and twice in Revelation—once at the beginning and once at the end.

Grace also describes the favor people show each other (Gen. 32:5; 33:8-15; 34:11; 39:4; 47:25, 29; Ruth 2:10; Esther 2:17).

Here's something important to understand: we can't limit grace to just God's New Testament dealings with humanity. Wasn't God just as gracious and loving to angels and people in Old Testament times as He is to us today? Of course He was!

He couldn't be any other way toward His creation—except when they were in rebellion and sin. The very fact that creatures exist and continue to exist is itself an act of grace. Grace even extends to animals, providing everything that sustains life. The animals don't earn this any more than we do. Grace flows freely to all creatures, whether they realize it or not.

### 2. The Benefits of Grace

Every good thing from God comes through His amazing grace. We deserve nothing, yet He gives us everything!

Grace moves God to act for the best and eternal good of all creation. You see grace at work in His acts of judgment just as much as in His acts of mercy. It works for the benefit of the few and the many alike. All living creatures have an eternal guarantee of God's benefits and loving care through grace.

Through grace, we receive "every good and perfect gift" and "all things that pertain unto life and godliness" (Jn. 3:16; Rom. 3:24; 5:2, 17-21; 6:14; 8:32; 2 Cor. 8:9; 9:8; Eph. 1:6-7; 2:5-8; Jas. 1:17; 4:6; 1 Pet. 5:5; 2 Pet. 1:3-4).

But here's the key: we receive these benefits only when we humble ourselves and depend entirely on God by faith. We must recognize that we are nothing, that God is everything, and that His blessings come completely apart from our works (Rom. 3:24-31; 4:1-4, 16; 5:15-21; 6:14, 15; 11:6; Gal. 2:16; 3:1-12; Eph. 2:7-9).

### 3. The Grace of God in All Ages

Nobody can dispute that God showed grace in every age. He has always been gracious, loving, and merciful to all people. Every act of mercy from God has flowed from His grace. Every favor He shows comes through grace.

When John wrote that "grace and truth came by Jesus Christ" (Jn. 1:16-17), he couldn't have meant there was no grace before Jesus came. That would be like saying "the law was given by Moses" means there was no law at all before Moses—but we know there were many laws before him (see Lesson Thirteen).

There was grace before Christ came to earth, but it was the *fullness* of grace that arrived through Him (Jn. 1:14-17). God has shown different graces—different favors—to people at different times. The idea that grace never brought spiritual blessings before Christ comes from unbelief and predetermined ideas designed to prop up false doctrines.

All through the Old Testament, people received God's grace and favor in many ways. Some lost that favor and never got it back. Others maintained it, more or less, throughout their lives (Gen. 6:8; 19:19; Exod. 33:12-17; 34:9; Ps. 84:11; Prov. 3:34; Lk. 2:40).

In Lesson Eighteen, Point IX, we saw that Old Testament saints enjoyed nearly every blessing that we receive through grace today. The only real difference between New Covenant blessings and those before or under the Old Covenant is this: we can have in fullness what they had in part.

They had the Holy Spirit in a measure; we can have Him in all fullness (Jn. 3:34; 7:37-39). They had grace in a measure; we can have it fully through Jesus Christ (Jn. 1:17). They had God's glory in part; we can experience it in a greater way (2 Cor. 3:6-15).

We've seen that each dispensation began in God's favor and ended in His judgment because people lost that favor. Every unsaved soul in all of history proves there was a fall from original grace. Millions have never regained their standing in grace.

Grace provided the means of reconciliation even in Old Testament times, but not everyone accepted God's grace then—just like not everyone accepts it today when it comes to spiritual things. When it comes to material things, though, everyone in every age has received God's blessings through grace. All these blessings of life are God's unearned favors, designed to lead us to repentance (Mt. 5:44-48; Rom. 2:4-6; Acts 17:28; Jas. 1:17).

People in Old Testament times became partakers of spiritual blessings—even the Holy Spirit Himself—by grace through faith. You can find many examples in Rom. 4:1-25; Gal. 2:15-19; 3:1-18; Heb. 11. These passages prove that people were justified by faith through grace, completely apart from works or law. If not through works, how else could they have received except by grace?

That Old Testament saints received the Holy Spirit by faith through grace is clear from Gen. 41:38; Exod. 28:3; 31:3; 35:31; Num. 11:17-29; 14:24; 24:2; 27:18; Deut. 34:9; Judg. 3:10; 6:34; 11:29; 14:6, 19; 15:14; 1 Sam. 10:6-10; 11:6; 16:13-23; 18:10; 19:20-23; 2 Sam. 23:2; 2 Ki. 2:15; 2 Chron. 15:1; 20:14; 24:20; Ps. 51:10-11; Isa. 11:2; 26:9; 42:1; 61:1-3; 63:10-14; Ezek. 2:2; 3:24; 11:5, 19; 36:27; 37:14; Dan. 4:8-9, 18; 5:11-14; 6:3; Mt. 10:1-21; Lk. 1:15, 41-46, 67, 70; 2:25-35; Acts 1:16; 3:18-21; 2 Tim. 3:15-17; Heb. 1:1-2; 2 Pet. 1:20-21.

These were permanent anointings and abidings of the Spirit—not just occasional visits, as some teach. John the Baptist was "filled" with the Holy Spirit from birth, and this was before the Spirit came in fullness at Pentecost!

Did all these people receive the Holy Spirit by works? Of course not—they received by grace. And if they received by grace, then grace was clearly evident in Old Testament times.

Now, these people didn't receive the baptism in the Spirit that all believers can have since Pentecost (Mt. 3:11; Jn. 1:31-34; 7:38-39; Acts 1:4-5; 2:1-21, 33, 38-39; 8:5-25; 9:1-7; 10:44-48; 11:15-16; 19:1-7), but they certainly had the Spirit in a measure.

Scripture makes clear there's a measure of the Spirit that all believers receive (Num. 11:16-17; 2 Ki. 2:9; Lk. 1:17; Jn. 3:34; 1 Cor. 12:13; 2 Cor. 1:22; 3:18; 5:5; Phil. 1:19; 1 Jn. 4:13). These passages speak of the Spirit "by measure" and "without measure."

This is the difference between receiving the Spirit at conversion—as all believers do (Jn. 3:3-8; Rom. 5:1-11; 8:1-17; 1 Cor. 3:16-17; 6:11-21; 12:3-13; 2 Cor. 3:18; Eph. 2:18; 4:4; Titus 3:5; 1 Jn. 4:13)—and the baptism in the Spirit that all believers may receive simply by asking their Heavenly Father (Lk. 11:13; 24:49; Acts 1:8-15; 8:1-21).

### 4. The Fullness of Grace and Power Promised Modern Believers

We've seen that Old Testament saints experienced different measures of the Spirit and God's power. Christ was the first to receive the Spirit "without measure" (Jn. 3:34).

John said, "of his fullness have we all received, and grace for grace," showing us that grace comes in measures (Jn. 1:16-17). The Weymouth translation reads "grace upon grace," and Moffatt's translation says "grace after grace"—plainly showing that we don't necessarily receive all of grace at once.

Why not? Often it's due to lack of knowledge and faith. As your knowledge grows, your faith grows. And as your faith grows, you receive more grace—more of God's favor—at every stage of your growth.

There's no limit to God's grace or what we can receive through it. But the benefits of grace don't come to everyone alike or all at once, despite what some modern teachers claim. If everyone received the same fullness of grace, everyone would have the same benefits from God. The fact that some receive more than others proves that these blessings come by faith, according to each person's faith. Faith governs what you receive through grace, and faith is based on knowledge.

Today, we're promised "the fullness of God" and the same power to do the same works Jesus did (Jn. 14:12-15; Jn. 7:37-39; Acts 1:8; Eph. 3:16-20). Since believers have varying degrees of power and faith, this proves there are also varying degrees of grace received from God. (See Lesson Thirty for more on this.)

### 5. False Theories About Grace

There's a school of Bible interpreters that magnifies grace above everything else in God's program. They ignore God's justice, His laws, and all the conditions that govern His dealings with us. They essentially cancel out thousands of Scriptures that reveal how God relates to free moral agents.

They say some good things about grace, but they go to an extreme, trying to make grace the sum total of everything about God and His plan.

Yes, it's true that from God's standpoint grace cannot be withheld because of our unworthiness, cannot be lessened by our failures, and cannot be mixed with earning salvation through works. But this doesn't erase the fact that there are conditions we must meet if we want certain benefits of grace.

Not one statement in the entire Bible says God's grace is unconditional, or that we can receive grace while living however we please in disregard of God's laws and justice.

When we say grace isn't withheld because of our unworthiness, we simply mean God's grace will cause Him to forgive all sin *when certain conditions are met*. When we say grace isn't lessened by our failures, we mean sin doesn't diminish God's grace toward us *when we meet the conditions laid out in His Word*. When we say grace can't be mixed with works, we mean no human effort can earn the blessings that come only by faith through grace—by meeting the plain conditions God has established for receiving these blessings.

Here's how it works: When you realize you're a sinner, that God's grace is greater than your sins, that you have no merit of your own to earn God's favor, and that you come to God meeting the requirements for reconciliation—at that moment you become a recipient of God's grace.

Think of it like a free gift that requires you to open your hands to receive it. If God withheld His grace from a penitent sinner simply because that person was a sinner, nobody could be saved. If sin lessened God's grace toward a penitent sinner, nobody could be blessed—sin would be greater than grace and would prevent grace from being shown. If blessings were earned by works, they wouldn't come by grace at all.

It's also true that God isn't obligated to save sinners because of anything we've done. But God, of His own free will and because of grace, obligated Himself to pay humanity's debt of sin.

Since God has chosen to take on this task, He is now obligated to give every person the same freedom to accept the cancellation of their debt. God is now obligated to save everyone who accepts Christ's work for them. He cannot refuse to show His grace to any sinner who accepts Christ's work on their behalf.

But God is not obligated to bless anyone who refuses and rejects His offer and Christ's work on the cross. The choice is now up to each of us—not up to God. God has already made His choice. His work in paying humanity's debt is finished. He is obligated to give full benefits to all who accept.

Of course we're saved by grace! But not without our free and voluntary choice to accept Christ's work, proper confession of sins to God, and faith in Christ's blood. All the grace in existence cannot save a single soul that refuses its benefits.

So in the final analysis, you govern your own salvation by your power of free choice. Yes, salvation is God's work for us—but God cannot save us without our free consent and cooperation, from the new birth to the grave. The idea that salvation depends *only* on God's grace and is *only* God's work is false.

If everything depended solely on God to save all sinners without any action on their part, then everyone would be saved—because that's God's desire (1 Tim. 2:4; 2 Pet. 3:9). If it were left entirely to God, He would be morally obligated to save everyone regardless of their consent, or He would be a respecter of persons and a breaker of His own law (Jas. 2:1, 9).

Yes, grace is pure kindness and unmerited favor from God. But since God voluntarily chose to pay our debt and save us from Hell on the condition that we accept this work, He is now fulfilling an obligation—one of His own free choice, not one we earned through merit. Our works didn't earn God's obligation to save us. This obligation is voluntary on God's part, for helpless humanity. This is what makes God's grace truly unmerited favor and love.

The school of thought we're discussing fails to recognize this human element in how grace works. They magnify grace as the only thing capable of saving people and insist that we can do nothing to receive its benefits.

They ignore our free moral agency. They ignore our responsibility for our own damnation if we're lost. They brush aside repentance, confession of sins, prayer, turning from sin—everything God requires of us to be saved.

They ignore the human element after salvation and dismiss all the conditions for continued salvation that Scripture plainly states we must meet to be saved in the end. They lay all blame and responsibility on God if anyone fails to do what God requires. They make God personally accountable for every lost soul.

This school holds some of the most foolish and unbiblical theories about God, grace, and salvation of any group claiming faith in Christ's gospel.

When they argue that grace is pure kindness without obligation; that it's God's kindness regardless of how much or little we sin; that it's unrelated to human merit; that it's not treating a person as they deserve or better than they deserve; that it treats people graciously without any reference to what they deserve; that it never decreases or increases from God's standpoint; and that it offers the same unvarying blessing to everyone alike—their arguments are partly true.

But they've overlooked one crucial thing: the manifestation of grace in your life is governed by your free moral agency. God is limited in what He can and will do for you by your own will, faith, and obedience.

To teach that God doesn't forgive sinners out of big-heartedness, or that He doesn't show mercy but saves solely because of grace, demonstrates ignorance of the gospel and of what grace actually is.

Yes, Christ has already "taken away" the world's sin on the cross. Yes, He is our substitute who has already borne God's righteous judgment against sin. But this doesn't prove that God is without mercy, that He isn't big-hearted, or that grace is something separate from Christ's redemptive work.

The truth is: God shows grace to people who don't deserve it and can't earn it. And it's also true that God is love. He is big-hearted. He is merciful, kind, and compassionate toward those who have strayed from His family.

When people get so technical that they separate grace from God's mercy, love, and kindness, they show the worst kind of ignorance. Their high-sounding phrases about grace as something distinct from all redemptive processes may impress people who love finding hidden mysteries in Scripture, but to simple believers, such talk is foolish.

These teachers may feel wonderful manufacturing technicalities and trying to magnify grace. But in the light of plain Scripture and common sense, their theories are foolish and false.

Let's briefly study how "grace," "kindness," "love," and similar terms are actually used of God, people, and redemption, and see whether these theories hold up.

A study of these words shows that "grace" is never used in either Testament as something separate from big-heartedness, love, compassion, and kindness between individuals—whether between people or between God and people. It's used repeatedly for the favor one person shows another, and this favor is governed by the recipient's disposition, life, service, faith, relationship, and attitude.

Noah found grace in God's sight because he was righteous—God favored him because of this (Gen. 6:8; 7:1). God had mercy on him and his family. If Noah hadn't been righteous, God wouldn't have had mercy on him. He would have destroyed Noah with the rest of the ungodly. This is indisputable if we believe the record.

When Lot found "grace" or "favor" in God's sight, it was because of God's mercy and because of Abraham, God's friend (Gen. 19:19, 29). When Moses and Israel found grace with God, it was because of God's mercy and choice (Exod. 33:12-17; 34:9). They were His chosen people because of Abraham, whom God saw would obey Him and command his children to serve the Lord (Gen. 18:17-19; 22:12).

Those same people who found grace in God's sight were destroyed because of sin—because grace does not tolerate sin in those once blessed with grace (Exod. 32:30-35; Num. 14:22-35; Jude 5).

When people in the early Church found grace from God, it was because they humbled themselves and freely accepted God's salvation. When they failed God, they were cursed just like the Israelites and the angels and people of past ages (Acts 1:25; 5:1-10; Rom. 11; 1 Tim. 1:19-20; 5:11-15; Heb. 6:4-9; 10:26-29; 2 Pet. 2:20-22; Rev. 2:5).

It's foolish to talk about being forgiven or saved without this being an act of grace and mercy. Everything God does for us is an act of grace and mercy!

The fact that God has already paid our debt doesn't mean that actual forgiveness of sins today isn't an immediate act of His grace. No sinner is personally saved until they accept Christ's work. Since we must do this in our own lives today, God's grace is manifested only when we accept Christ as personal Savior.

This doesn't mean God becomes "good enough" to excuse sins apart from Christ's work. It means God becomes personally gracious to each sinner the moment they accept Christ's work. Christ's work was done centuries ago, but it doesn't benefit you until you choose to accept it.

God blesses the sinner by His grace when that sinner surrenders. This can't happen until the sinner turns to God and permits grace to be manifested to them. No sinner will ever receive God's grace until they personally humble themselves and call on God for mercy. God is free to forgive the moment someone confesses because Christ has already paid the debt. It's only when we know the truth and accept it that we're set free (Job 33:23-24; Jn. 8:31-32).

The gospel of redemption is called "the word of his grace" (Acts 14:3; 20:24, 32). We're justified by grace (Rom. 3:24; Titus 3:7). All blessings come by grace (Jn. 1:16; Eph. 1:7; 2:7). Grace brings salvation (Titus 2:11-13). It's the source of answered prayer (Heb. 4:16). It can transform our lives (1 Cor. 15:10). It enables us to make great sacrifices (2 Cor. 9:8).

Grace comes through faith (Eph. 2:8-9), the Holy Spirit (Zech. 12:10; Heb. 10:29), God's choice (Rom. 11:5-6; Gal. 1:15; Exod. 33:19), Jesus Christ (1 Cor. 1:4), and humility and choice on our part (Prov. 3:34; Jas. 4:6).

Grace comes in various measures (Eph. 4:7; Rom. 12:3-6; 15:15) and is governed by individual faith (Rom. 4:16; 5:1-2), humility (Jas. 4:6), and the willpower and life of each believer (Heb. 12:15, 28; 13:9; Jas. 4:6; 1 Pet. 5:5).

Here's further proof that grace in your life is governed by your own will and choices: You can receive God's grace in vain (2 Cor. 6:1). You can frustrate grace (Gal. 2:21). You can fall from it (Gal. 5:4). You can cause it to fail in your life (Heb. 12:15). You can turn it into license for sin (Jude 4). You can sin despite grace (Rom. 6:1). And you can continue or discontinue in it as you please (Acts 13:43).

Christians can minister grace to others (Eph. 4:29; Phil. 1:7; 1 Pet. 4:10) and grow in it (2 Pet. 3:18). Grace is an attribute of God used alongside "mercy" and "compassion" when dealing with sinners (Exod. 34:6; 2 Chron. 30:9; Neh. 9:17, 31; Ps. 86:15; 103:8; 111:4; 112:4).

These facts might seem like blasphemy to some, but facts are facts. When they're stated in plain Scripture that anyone can read, it's foolish to reject them. Holding to some man-made theory that a church makes the sum total of its religion isn't worth the price.

It's better to be honest with all the Scriptures on a subject and believe them all, rather than twisting a few to say something they don't and flatly rejecting many others that speak to the same subject.

Some may think we're making human will greater than God's grace. But this is true not only of grace but of many other attributes of God that cannot bless rebels who choose not to accept these blessings.

It's not so much that human will is greater than any attribute of God. It's that God cannot and has promised not to do certain things for us until we accept His grace and freely submit to His work in us. God simply cannot and will not break His own laws or act as a despot toward anyone. He will not force any free moral agent to conform to His will.

Therefore, it's up to us to choose whether we want God's grace, love, and favor—and to what extent. If it were entirely up to God's will, all free moral agents would conform to Him, all would be blessed alike, and all would enjoy His grace and favor fully.

As it stands, no one can accuse God of not loving all people if they want to receive that love. The fact that not everyone is saved—and that even among the saved, not everyone partakes of God's love equally—proves that God's blessings through grace don't depend wholly on Him. Neither do they depend wholly on our free will.

It takes both God's will and our will in full cooperation to demonstrate the fullness of His love and grace. You can't work for the good of one master while serving another. So God's grace and love are naturally limited by our free moral agency.

To argue that forgiveness isn't an act of grace contradicts the many Scriptures cited above that say we're justified and saved by grace. To separate grace from God's mercy in forgiveness is also unbiblical—God cannot be gracious to anyone without showing mercy. Mercy and forgiveness go hand in hand (Deut. 5:9-10; Neh. 9:17; Ps. 26:11; Lk. 18:13-14).

Throughout Scripture, people asked for mercy when seeking forgiveness and blessing (Ps. 51:1-13; Prov. 16:6; etc.). God is by nature gracious, merciful, loving, kind, good, and compassionate (Exod. 34:6; Neh. 9:17; Ps. 86:15; 111:4; 145:8).

To separate His graciousness from His other characteristics and magnify it above His justice and everything else about God just to uphold a church theory about grace—that's more than thinking people can do.

### 6. Thirty Things Grace Cannot Do

> (1) Grace cannot set aside all condemnation for future sins forever. It can only set aside condemnation as long as you stay free from sin. The modern idea that judicial forgiveness covers ALL sins—past, present, and future—that God never holds believers' sins against them, and that God charges all sins of believers to Christ rather than condemning them, is one of the most unbiblical and deceptive theories in any church.
>
> Those who hold this theory argue that no earthly court could punish someone twice for the same crime, and that since Christ bore all sins, He can't bear them twice. This argument is true as far as it goes—but it's only half the truth. Christ bore the sins of all, but nobody is saved until they repent and ask for mercy.
>
> Yes, a person can't be punished twice for the same crime. But if that same person commits the same crime again after being cleared of the first, they must be punished again. No court gives anyone a blanket pardon to keep committing the same crimes without punishment each time. Being pardoned for one crime doesn't excuse all future similar crimes.
>
> Think of it like a governor pardoning a murderer and restoring full citizenship. That murderer can't go out and keep murdering just because of the pardon. It would be ridiculous to argue that the pardon for one murder gives freedom to murder at will without punishment. No government could function if it dealt with criminals this way. No court could justly free someone to commit unlimited crimes just because they were punished or pardoned once. Is God the only Governor lacking the intelligence to not let His subjects be as lawless as they please?
>
> The same principle applies to salvation. God couldn't justly give anyone a blank check to commit any sin while being immune from punishment. That would be like giving a murderer a pardon that includes permission to commit unlimited murders—making the governor responsible for all future murders!
>
> Naturally, all sins have been paid for, and Christ bore all sins in His body on the tree. He will forgive all sins that are confessed. But this doesn't give the saved person a blank check to continue in sin without accountability. Salvation doesn't include freedom to live in sin. It doesn't guarantee immunity from Hell for those who return to sin and die in it.
>
> There's no Scripture teaching that God forgives past, present, and future sins all at once. Scripture tells us that all sins committed up to the time of repentance are forgiven and blotted out—so at the moment of forgiveness, God holds nothing against the person. God promised to keep us free from sin from that point forward if we meet certain conditions. God never promised more than this. No one ever received more than this. God only requires confession of sins already committed. Only sins that have been committed can be forgiven.
>
> This is clear from all Scripture (Exod. 32:30-34; Lev. 4:2-35; 5:1-17; 6:2-30; Num. 5:1-7; Ps. 32:1-5; 38:1-18; 51:1-13; Mt. 3:6; 18:21-35; Acts 2:38; 3:19; 5:31; 26:18; 2 Pet. 1:5; 1 Jn. 1:9; Rev. 1:5). Teaching that future sins are forgiven before they're committed is simply ridiculous.
>
> The fact that sins committed after the cross occurred after Christ died, or that Christ died for all future people's future sins, doesn't prove that all your future sins are forgiven before you commit them. Christ died for all people—but if everyone is forgiven simply because Christ died for them, and if people don't need to confess sins to be forgiven, then everyone is saved and there's no need for anyone to ever confess sins.
>
> The sins of Old Testament saints weren't committed after Christ died, yet Christ atoned for all those past sins (Rom. 3:24-25; Heb. 9:15). They all had to confess sins when and after they were committed, just as we must today, according to the passages cited above. We must conclude that no sin can be forgiven before it's committed. If this is true, there's no such thing as all future sins being blotted out by one act of faith in Christ, giving immunity from all future punishment.
>
> Statements like "taketh away the sin of the world" and "take away our sins" refer to Christ bearing the sins of all people in all ages—past, present, and future. But redemption benefits only those who personally confess their sins and accept Christ's work for them. If Christ saves everyone simply because He died for them, regardless of meeting any conditions, then everyone is saved from sin and will go to Heaven—and there's no need for further gospel preaching.
>
> God's will is that after forgiveness and becoming a new creature in Christ, we quit the sin business. Jesus told several people, "Sin no more, lest a worse thing come unto thee" (Jn. 5:14; 8:11). Jesus taught that "Whosoever committeth sin is the servant of sin" (Jn. 8:34). He taught that the Holy Spirit's work throughout this age would be to "reprove the world of sin" (Jn. 16:7-11).
>
> Paul stated that we shouldn't continue in sin just because God keeps forgiving (Rom. 6:1); that we "should not henceforth serve sin" after being born again (Rom. 6:1-6); that saved people are "freed from sin" (Rom. 6:7-23; 8:14); that we should walk in Christ as we received

lessons.title: The New Testament Program for the Modern Church
lessons.content:
# The New Testament Program for the Modern Church

Here's something that might surprise you: the program God designed for the Church hasn't changed one bit since the very beginning. The same approach that made Christianity famous throughout the Roman Empire in just a few short years is still the blueprint for today.

So what's different? It's not the program — it's us. The Church has drifted.

The good news is that Scripture spells out this program clearly, and history shows us exactly how powerfully it worked in the lives of early believers. There's really no mystery here if you're honestly looking for the truth. Anyone who can read simple language can discover it right in the New Testament.

## I. What the New Testament Program for the Modern Church Is

This program isn't complicated at all. It boils down to two main things: **preach the gospel** and **confirm the gospel**. That's it.

Let's look at how Jesus Himself modeled this. "The former treatise have I made, O Theophilus, of all that Jesus began both to do and to teach" (Acts 1:1-2). Notice that — He both *did* and *taught*.

"Jesus went about all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness and all manner of diseases among the people" (Mt. 4:23-24). See the pattern? Preaching *and* healing went hand in hand.

When John the Baptist sent messengers to ask if Jesus was the Messiah, Jesus pointed to the evidence: "Go and shew John again the things which ye do hear and see. The blind receive their sight, and the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, and the deaf hear, the dead are raised up, and the poor have the gospel preached unto them" (Mt. 11:1-6).

Mark records the same two-part ministry: "He healed many that were sick of divers diseases, and cast out many devils . . . And he preached in their synagogues" (Mk. 1:34-39).

Jesus Himself declared His mission this way: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me TO PREACH the gospel to the poor; he hath sent me to heal . . . to set at liberty them that are bruised, to preach the acceptable year of the Lord" (Lk. 4:16-19).

### The Disciples Carried On This Same Work

Preaching and healing was the great work Christ started, and it's exactly what He commanded His disciples to continue — both before and after the cross.

Look at what happened before the crucifixion: "He called unto him his twelve disciples [Judas included], he gave them power against unclean spirits, to cast them out, and to heal all manner of sickness and all manner of disease . . . These twelve [Judas included] Jesus sent forth and commanded them . . . as ye go preach, saying, The kingdom of heaven is at hand. Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out devils: freely ye have received, freely give" (Mt. 10:1-7).

Mark tells us: "He ordained twelve [Judas included], that they should be with him, and that he might send them forth to preach, and to have power to heal sicknesses, and to cast out devils" (Mk. 3:13-15).

And they actually did it! "And they [the twelve, Judas included] went out, and preached that men should repent. And cast out many devils, and anointed with oil many that were sick and healed them" (Mk. 6:7-13).

Luke confirms: "He sent them [the twelve, Judas included] to preach the kingdom of God, and to heal the sick . . . and they departed, and went through the towns, preaching the gospel, and healing every where" (Lk. 9:1-6).

### The Seventy Were Also Empowered

It wasn't just the twelve apostles. Christ ordained seventy others and sent them out two by two to do the exact same work. He told them: "into whatsoever city ye enter . . . heal the sick that are therein, and say [preach] unto them, The kingdom of God is come nigh unto you" (Lk. 10:1-24).

### The Great Commission Continues This Pattern

When Jesus was ready to return to Heaven, He specifically commanded His followers to carry on what He "began both to do and to teach" (Acts 1:1-2).

He declared: "All power [authority] is given unto me in heaven and in earth." Then He commissioned them: "Go ye therefore, and teach all nations baptizing them in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost: Teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you: and, lo, I am with you alway, even unto the end of the world" (age, Mt. 28:18-20).

But first, He told them to wait for power: "but tarry ye in the city of Jerusalem, until ye be endued with power from on high" (Lk. 24:49; Acts 1:4-8).

### The Promise of Signs Following

Jesus didn't just send them out hoping for the best. He promised specific results: "These signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues; they shall take up serpents [see Lesson Fourteen, Point V, 11 for the doctrine of taking up serpents]; and if they drink any deadly thing, it shall not hurt them; they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover" (Mk. 16:15-18).

"Ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost not many days hence . . . Ye shall receive power, after that the Holy Ghost is come upon you: and ye shall be witnesses unto me . . . unto the uttermost parts of the earth" (Acts 1:4-8).

Jesus made this stunning promise: "He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do; because I go unto my Father. And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son. If ye shall ask any thing in my name, I will do it . . . ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you . . . I have chosen you, and ordained you, that ye should go and bring forth fruit, and that your fruit should remain: that whatsoever ye shall ask of the Father in my name, he may give it you" (Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16).

### No Limitations on God's Promises

These promises — and hundreds more like them — were made to believers. You won't find limitations or qualifications attached to them. They're unlimited and unqualified, so that "all things are possible to him that believeth" (Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24).

Think of it like this: God didn't fill up the Bible with promises just to make it a bigger book. He gave these promises to give you absolute assurance that you can carry on the work Jesus "began both to do and to teach" and confirm the Word of God as you preach it.

### The Early Church Demonstrated This Program

The Lord backed up the preaching of the early Church with power, proving that He wanted everyone in this age to know that preaching is only *part* of the program. Confirmation of what's preached is equally essential.

Here's the evidence from Scripture:

"They cast out many devils, and anointed with oil many that were sick, and healed them" (Mk. 6:7-13).

"They departed, and went through the towns, preaching the gospel, and healing everywhere" (Lk. 9:16).

"And the seventy returned again with joy, saying, Lord, even devils are subject unto us through thy name" (Lk. 10:17).

After Jesus ascended: "So then after the Lord had spoken unto them, he was received up into heaven, and sat on the right hand of God. And they went forth, and preached everywhere, the Lord working with them, and confirming the word with signs following" (Mk. 16:19-20).

The book of Acts is full of this pattern: "And many wonders and signs were done by the apostles . . . such as I have give I unto thee: In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth rise up and walk . . . and immediately his feet and ankle bones received strength" (Acts 2:43; 3:6-11).

"And when they had prayed the place was shaken . . . and they were all filled with the Holy Ghost . . . and by the hands of the apostles were many signs and wonders wrought among the people . . . Insomuch that they brought forth the sick into the streets, and laid them on beds and couches, that at least the shadow of Peter passing by might overshadow some of them.
