There came also a multitude . . . bringing sick folks, and them that were vexed with unclean spirits: and they were healed every one" (Acts 4:29-31; 5:12-16).

"Peter said unto him, Jesus Christ maketh thee whole: arise, and make thy bed. And he arose immediately . . . Tabitha, arise. And she opened her eyes: and when she saw Peter, she sat up" (Acts 9:32-43).

### Not Just the Twelve Apostles

Some people — even entire denominations — argue that only the twelve apostles received the baptism in the Spirit and had power to confirm God's Word. But Scripture proves this is false.

In Acts 6, we see seven men chosen from among ordinary believers specifically because they were "full of the Holy Ghost." One of them, Stephen, "full of the Holy Ghost and power, did great wonders and miracles among the people" (Acts 6:3, 5-10).

Philip, another of these business elders, had a great revival and did great miracles among the people in Samaria (Acts 8:5-20).

When believers were scattered by persecution, "went everywhere preaching the word . . . And the hand of the Lord was with them: and a great number believed, and turned to the Lord" (Acts 8:4; 11:19-21). The phrase "the hand of the Lord was with them" proves they did miracles, as is clear from Acts 4:30; 13:11. People turned to the Lord in great numbers *because* of miracles (Acts 9:35; 15:12, 19).

Ananias — not one of the twelve — was filled with the Holy Ghost and had power to lay hands on Paul, baptize him in the Spirit, and heal him of blindness (Acts 9:10-19).

Paul and Barnabas did great signs and wonders (Acts 13:11; 14:3, 8-20; 15:12; 16:16-34; 19:1-6). God worked special miracles through Paul so that handkerchiefs and aprons from his body healed the sick and cast out demons (Acts 19:11-20; 28:7-9; Rom. 15:18, 29).

Paul's immunity from a snakebite is one of the signs Jesus said would follow all believers (Acts 28:1-6; Mk. 16:17-20). Timothy, one of Paul's converts, did the same kind of miracles Paul did (1 Cor. 16:10).

The pattern continues throughout the New Testament. Ordinary elders of all the churches were to be called to heal the sick (Jas. 5:14-16).

Paul issued this warning: none of us will escape God's judgment if we neglect so great a salvation "Which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him; God also bearing them witness, both with signs and wonders, and divers miracles, and gifts of the Holy Ghost, according to His own will" (Heb. 2:3-4).

In 1 Cor. 12, Paul describes nine gifts of the Spirit distributed among all believers who earnestly desire them. So the teaching that miracles only happened among the twelve apostles — and that we can't experience the same things today — is simply false.

### This Program Is for Today

The New Testament program of preaching and confirming the Word is clearly established for the modern Church — if believers actually want these experiences.

People throughout this age were commanded to observe all that Jesus commanded His disciples (Mt. 28:20). The same baptism in the Spirit is promised to everyone who believes in Christ (Mt. 3:11; Jn. 7:38-39; Acts 2:38-39; 5:32). All the promises of God are yea and amen to those who believe (2 Cor. 1:20).

Here's what we've established: Christ started this program. The disciples were commanded to continue it. They were promised power to make it successful. They *did* make it successful, and they commanded believers throughout this age to continue such a program and contend for the faith once delivered to the saints (Jude 3).

Why did many in the early Church fail? Why has the Church failed as a whole from the apostolic period until today? That's explained in Lesson Nineteen, Point VII, 2 and 3.

## II. The Power Every Believer in Christ Should Exercise

According to Jesus's promise, every believer should have power to do the works of Christ — and even greater works than He did (Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16).

The apostles promised that all believers could have the same baptism in the Spirit they received (Acts 2:38-39; 5:32; 2 Cor. 1:20) and that they could have the gifts of the Holy Ghost (1 Cor. 1:7; 12:1-11, 31; 14:1; Rom. 1:11; 12:3-8; Eph. 4:7-16; 1 Tim. 4:14; 2 Tim. 1:6).

I have watched for years as our churches have drifted from these truths, and I'm passionate about seeing them return to the old ways. Millions of people are leaving Christian churches and turning to false religions because the modern church program offers them nothing.

It's similar to a store that advertises amazing products but has empty shelves. Eventually, people stop coming and go somewhere else — even if what they find elsewhere is counterfeit.

False religions are demonstrating more power and getting people more results — physically, materially, and spiritually — than the average modern church. So people are turning to whatever offers them tangible benefits.

I'm against these false religions. I'm *for* the churches and want to help all of them rediscover the original type of Christianity that will draw these people back.

People are hungry for supernatural power to heal when they need it. They want answers to prayer. They want material help. They want freedom from sin and failure.

If they can't get it from one source, they'll accept it from another. If they can't get the real thing the gospel offers, they'll settle for Satan's substitute. Satan knows he can deceive people with his counterfeit power, and he's winning millions who should be in Christian churches — and would be, if ministers would wake up to their responsibility and get God into the churches in a supernatural way.

## III. Christian Doctrines and Experiences Seldom Seen in Modern Churches

Years ago, I became deeply troubled by something: God was not doing for modern believers what I read about in the New Testament. Being someone who values sincerity, honesty, and at least ordinary intelligence, I decided to find out what was wrong.

Think about it this way: when a machine won't run or something fails to carry out its purpose, you know something needs to be fixed before it will work properly. I applied this same common sense to spiritual things. I had read the New Testament enough to see a massive difference between the modern church and the early one.

So I developed a plan: investigate and experiment with the spiritual truths taught in the New Testament. I was pastoring a church whose members were nearly all honest and open to all the Bible's teachings.

I said to the church, "Let's gather night after night and read the New Testament through to discover what it teaches that we're not doing and what kind of spiritual experiences it describes that we haven't received. Let's bring pencils and paper and write down everything we find that we're not practicing as a church and every blessing the early believers had that we don't have today."

We gathered on Monday night and read through Matthew 10. That first night, we discovered why we didn't have the power the early disciples had. Christ "gave them power" and said, "heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out devils: freely ye have received, freely give" (Mt. 10:1-8).

I stopped and commented that we'd found what we were looking for the very first night. The reason we couldn't freely give was that we hadn't freely received.

To make a long story short, when we finished reading through the entire New Testament and honestly listing everything we weren't practicing and every experience that wasn't normal in our lives, I had **160 points** on my list.

I could hardly believe my eyes — and yet there was chapter and verse for every single point. I knew these things weren't happening in our church because I had never seen them.

From that moment, I became a different man. I had to choose: either remain honest, face the facts, and acknowledge the truth I had discovered — or reject my findings, turn dishonest, and make excuses for why we don't have these blessings today.

I decided — as I always have and always will — to be honest with the eternal Word of God and with my own soul and the souls of everyone I teach. I have received a measure of power from God and am expecting the fullness in my life so I can help all people get free from the devil and all his works.

Here's the truth: John 14:12 states the divine standard for every believer. Until a believer can do the works of Christ, they're not a normal New Testament Christian in terms of power.

You may have many other blessings from God, but if you don't have power like Christ's, you're falling short of your privileges. There's nothing to be gained by denying this fact.

This doesn't mean you're not saved or not going to Heaven. It simply means you're not up to the New Testament standard of power with God. Anything short of this power reveals a human limitation to the promises. The New Testament standard of normal power for every believer is clear — and we can have it if we want it and if we obey all the New Testament to the letter.

These teachings and practices, along with scores of others from the New Testament, are more or less foreign to the average church member. Even the average preacher knows little about them.

Is it any wonder the modern church is powerless? Is it any wonder multitudes are chasing counterfeit power and outward refinement in false religions?

Many thousands of people in local churches are following leaders who deny that New Testament doctrines and experiences are even for us today. Until the modern church again believes in the faith once delivered to the saints, it cannot contend for that faith. So it will continue in helplessness and defeat. It will continue in worldliness and formality — and people searching for the supernatural will keep leaving, even if it means denying Christ and the Bible.

The modern church must wake up to its responsibilities and privileges in the gospel, or millions more will be lost to false religions and demonic powers.

Time is short. What the church must do, it must do quickly.

Let everyone who names the name of Christ depart from iniquity and contend for the fullness of power and faith promised to those who believe the gospel of Jesus Christ, the only begotten Son of God.

God will back us up by the power of the Holy Ghost and all the hosts of Heaven. The devil is already a defeated foe, and his power has been broken. He's already been proven in the Court of Heaven as a usurper and intruder into humanity's dominion, holding people in his power only as they consent to it.

All that remains is to carry out the execution of the sentence against him. This will be done at the second advent of Christ. But right now, believers can defeat him and all his works in their personal lives and enjoy the fullness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ.

Let all who read these lessons unite to pray and work for New Testament power in all our churches.

## IV. Questions the Modern Church Must Answer

> 1. Is this program for the modern church?

> 2. Does God desire to carry on this program today?

> 3. Do we need such a program in the modern church?

> 4. Why does not God carry on this program more fully than He does now?

> 5. Has God a lower standard of world-attraction for His gospel than in the beginning?

> 6. Is God able to confirm the gospel today as recorded in the New Testament?

> 7. Are supernatural powers necessary today to confirm the Word of God?

> 8. Does the Word of God need confirmation today before the unbelieving world?

> 9. Are there any sick, lame, demon-possessed, and defeated people today?

> 10. Why would not God want to see men delivered today from the devil as in days of old?

> 11. Why did Jesus die for all men of all ages to deliver them from these troubles if His work was to be limited only to the early Church period?

> 12. Why did the Holy Spirit come to abide forever with men if He was to work only in the lives of the apostles and a few special pets of God in the early Church?

> 13. Why were the hundreds of promises given that all men of all ages should be victorious over all the works of the devil if they were to be limited to the early Church?

> 14. Why would God desire to see His people today tormented of the devil any more than He desired it in the days of Christ and the apostles?

> 15. Why would God want His children oppressed by His enemy when no other father would tolerate such a thing if he could help it?

> 16. Are we receiving new light about God's New Testament program for the Church?

> 17. What should be the attitude of the Church if these things be true?

> 18. What is the responsibility of the Church in view of these facts?

> 19. How can we get the New Testament program into operation today?

> 20. What would be the results of such a program in the modern Church?

These questions — and many others — must be answered by modern believers. By now, it should be clear to you what the answers are.

If this program was for the early Church, it's still for the Church today. The apostles were commanded to teach all nations to observe everything Christ commanded them (Mt. 28:19-20). Jesus Christ is the same today as ever (Heb. 13:8).

Nowhere in Scripture did God say this program would change when the apostles died or that it was only for a certain period. On the contrary, Christ promised to be with the Church until the end of the age (Mt. 28:20). Paul declared that all the promises of God are yea and amen to those who believe (2 Cor. 1:20).

God is the same. The devil is the same. Sin is the same. Sickness and demon-possessed people are still with us.

The power of God needs to be manifest today as much as ever. So we must conclude that it's God's will for the modern Church to face the facts and contend for the faith once delivered to the saints (Jude 3).

The work of the Spirit, the power of the blood, and the Word of God were meant to continue being effective in the lives of all believers throughout this age. Even if the Church as a whole refuses to accept these truths or put forth effort to carry on this program, you as an individual can get benefits through faith.

### Why Doesn't God Do More Today?

The only reason God doesn't carry on this program more fully today is the Church's failure to cooperate with Him. There's no question that such a program is needed to bless people according to their needs — and that God needs it to attract multitudes to His gospel.

God works only through people who have faith and who will permit Him to work in their lives. This has always been true and always will be (Rom. 10:10-21).

God hasn't lowered His standard of world attraction to His gospel. He's waiting for the Church to pray for a spiritual revival that will produce signs and wonders again. God is just as able today to save and heal multitudes — if preachers would wake up to their true calling and work at it.

### The Contest Is On

Demon-inspired religions are being evidenced and confirmed by the supernatural power of the devil. The need for God's greater power is obvious — to prove to people that Christianity promises and delivers everything they need in this life as well as the life to come.

Picture it this way: there's a contest happening between the actual results and power of the gospel versus false religions. And right now, false religions are showing up with demonstrations while many churches show up with empty talk.

The Word of God needs confirmation today in the sense of proving that the gospel is the same power of God unto salvation today as it was in the beginning. The unbelieving world has come to believe in supernatural powers, and multitudes are attracted wherever there's a real manifestation of the supernatural — whether it's the devil's power or God's.

### The Church's Opportunity

The Church stands today on the threshold of great and mighty achievements if it will wake up and wage an aggressive fight against Satan and his demon-forces. The Church can prove to the world once again that God's power is greater than Satan's.

There are sick and demon-possessed people in nearly every home who would gladly turn to God and be healed and saved from sin — if all the churches would stop fighting each other and the gospel itself, and start teaching people how to receive the benefits God promises.

Jesus died so that this program could be carried on throughout this age. The Holy Spirit was sent to carry it on. The Word of God was given to teach us what the program is and how to get its full benefits.

The program was started right, as demonstrated by Christ and the early Church. It can be fully revived again if we'll accept the truth.

God has always been ready to defeat Satan and his works in the lives of His children. When the Church gets ready and anxious to see God work, then our desires will be granted. God told Jeremiah that if people would call upon Him, He would show them great and mighty things (Jer. 33:3). Jesus and others promised that these things would happen in the lives of every believer — so there's no excuse but hardness of heart and the sin of unbelief.

This might sound impossible, like an idle dream. But just as sure as God is in Heaven and His Word is true, these changes and many others will literally come to divided and worldly Christendom today — if all churches will adopt the program of the New Testament as it is.

## Questions on Lesson Twenty

- Has the New Testament program changed, or has the modern Church? Prove.

- How can we get to know what the New Testament program is?

- What is the New Testament program? Prove from Scripture.

- Who started the program? Who were commanded to carry it on?

- How long was the program to continue? Prove.

- Who was sent from Heaven to carry on this program?

- Has He failed? Who has? Why?

- Is there a limitation to the needful benefits of this life in the promises?

- Why were the promises given and for whom?

- Through whom did God continue the program in the first century?

- Were the apostles the only ones who carried on the program? Prove.

- To what extent was the program successful in winning souls in the beginning?

- What kind of power should every believer have in Christ? Prove.

- Why do not all Christians have this power?

- Why are multitudes of professed Christians going after demon religions?

- What should the Church do to counteract this exodus from Christ and to attract men again?

- Name a number of Christian experiences seldom seen in the average church of today.

- What verses of Scripture state the normal standard of New Testament power?

- Name a number of Christian principles and practices seldom seen in churches.

- Discuss the modern revivals of the power of God.

- What should the Church awaken to?

- What are some of the questions the modern church must answer?

- Give your own answer to these questions by giving Scripture to prove the answers.

- Why is not God fully carrying out this program today?

- How are false religions attracting people?

- Should the Church be satisfied to let demon powers work more than God?

- What should believers do who are getting more light?

- What should they do if preachers and churches reject truth?

- What kind of results can the Church see if it will obey the Bible?

- Is this program with the results pictured possible today? How?

lessons.title: The Truth About Jesus Christ
lessons.content:
# The Truth About Jesus Christ

Everything we learned in Lesson Four about God applies to Jesus Christ before He became human. Why? Because He is one of the three distinct Spirit-beings who together make up the Godhead. Up until about 1,900 years ago, the second person of the Trinity had the same kind of Spirit-body, personal soul, and spirit that the Father and the Holy Spirit still have today.

Then something remarkable happened—one of the three divine persons took on human form to redeem the world. This is what sets Jesus apart from the other members of the Godhead during these past 1,900 years. Let's explore who Jesus Christ really is as the visible manifestation of the invisible God among us.

## I. The Pre-Existence of Jesus Christ

The Bible makes it absolutely clear that the person we now know as Jesus Christ was one of the three divine persons of the deity. As God, He had no beginning. When we talk about His "pre-existence," we're referring to the time before He became a man.

Technically speaking, there's no such thing as existence "before" Him as God—but He certainly existed before becoming human. Mic. 5:1-2 speaks of Him as existing from all eternity. John describes Him as existing in the very beginning with the Father (Jn. 1:1-5).

Jesus Himself spoke of being before Abraham and before the world was even created (Jn. 8:58; 17:5, 24). Paul tells us He existed before all things and that He is both the Creator and Upholder of everything (Col. 1:15-18; Heb. 1:1-3, 8; 2:10). God the Father created all things through Him (Eph. 3:9) and the Holy Spirit (Gen. 1:2).

## II. The Deity of Jesus Christ

### 1. Divine Names and Titles Are Ascribed to Him

The names and titles given to Jesus in Scripture prove beyond doubt that He is divine by nature and a full member of the Godhead. Think of it like someone's credentials—if a person holds every title of a particular office, they clearly occupy that position.

He is called "God" and "Immanuel" (Mt. 1:23; Jn. 1:1; 20:28; Acts 20:28); "Lord" (Lk. 19:34; Acts 2:36); "Lord of All" (Acts 10:36); "Lord of Glory" (1 Cor. 2:8); "Wonderful, Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace" (Isa. 9:6-7); "Christ the Lord" (Lk. 2:26); "The Son of God" (Mt. 4:3; 14:33; Lk. 22:70; Jn. 1:34; Rom. 1:4); "His Son" (Mt. 22:45; Jn. 3:16-18); "My Son" (Mt. 3:17); "The Only Begotten Son" (Jn. 1:18; 3:16-18; 1 Jn. 4:9).

He's also called "The First and the Last, Alpha and Omega, The Beginning and the Ending" (Rev. 22:12, 13, 16); "The Lord" (Acts 9:17); "The Son of the Highest" (Lk. 1:32; Mk. 14:61); "The Bread of God" (Jn. 6:33); "The Holy One of God" (Mk. 1:24); "Thy Holy Child Jesus" (Acts 4:30); "King of Kings and Lord of Lords" (Rev. 19:16); "Lord and Saviour" (2 Pet. 3:2); and "The Word of God" (Rev. 19:13).

These names and titles—along with many others throughout Scripture—prove Christ's deity. Some of them appear hundreds of times in the Bible. If we're going to believe the Bible, we must believe in the divinity of Christ.

### 2. Divine Attributes Are Ascribed to Him

This becomes crystal clear in Phil. 2:5-11, where Paul explains that Christ existed in God's form. He then laid aside this form and limited His divine attributes and powers to become a man. After His earthly limitation, these powers were restored to Him, as we'll see in Point VIII below.

### 3. Divine Offices Are Ascribed to Him

Jesus holds offices that belong only to God. He is called "The Creator" (Jn. 1:3; Col. 1:16; Heb. 1:1-3); "Mediator" (1 Tim. 2:4-5; Heb. 8:6); "Head of the Church" (Eph. 1:22; Col. 1:16-24); "Saviour" (2 Pet. 3:2); "Judge" (2 Tim. 4:1); "Preserver" (Heb. 1:1-3); "Life Giver" (Jn. 10:28; 17:2); "Lord and Christ" (Acts 2:36); "The Resurrection and the Life" (Jn. 11:25).

These offices and works prove that Christ is divine and one with the Father as part of the deity. Scripture calls Him the fellow and equal of God in divinity (Zech. 13:7; Jn. 5:17-23; 10:30-38; 17:10).

### 4. Divine Character Is Ascribed to Him

All ordinary human beings are sinners by nature (Ps. 51:5; Eph. 3:1-3; Rom. 5:12-21). But Christ is different. He is "holy by birth" (Lk. 1:35); "righteous" (Isa. 53:11; Heb. 1:9); "faithful" (Isa. 11:5; 1 Thess. 5:24); "true" (Jn. 1:14; 14:6); "just" (Jn. 5:30); "guileless" (1 Pet. 2:22); "sinless" (2 Cor. 5:21); "spotless" (1 Pet. 1:19); "innocent" (Mt. 27:4); "harmless" (Heb. 7:26).

He was "obedient to God" (Jn. 15:10; Heb. 5:8-10) and "His earthly parents" (Lk. 2:51); "zealous" (Jn. 2:17); "meek" (Mt. 11:29); "lowly in heart" (Mt. 11:29); "merciful" (Heb. 2:17); "patient" (Isa. 53:7); "long-suffering" (1 Tim. 1:16); "compassionate" (Mt. 15:32); "benevolent" (Acts 10:38); "loving" (Jn. 15:13); "self-denying" (2 Cor. 8:9); "humble" (Phil. 2:5-11); "resigned" (Lk. 22:42); and "forgiving" (Lk. 23:34).

### 5. The Works of God Are Ascribed to Him

Scripture attributes God's works directly to Christ (Jn. 1:3; Col. 1:15-18; Heb. 1:1-3, 10; Jn. 5:19-23; Rev. 3:14).

### 6. Divine Worship Was Given to Him

Both angels and humans worship Jesus—yet both refuse worship for themselves (Mt. 4:9-10; 14:33; 28:9; Lk. 24:52; Jn. 5:23; 14:14; Acts 7:59; Rom. 10:9-13; Heb. 1:6; Phil. 2:10-11; Rev. 5:12-14). When people tried to worship Peter or when John attempted to worship an angel, they were immediately stopped (Acts 10:25-26; Heb. 1:6; Rev. 22:8-9). But Jesus accepted worship—because He deserves it as God.

### 7. His Name Is Associated with the Father and Holy Spirit

Jesus' name is joined with the Father and the Holy Spirit as one of the three divine persons in the deity (Mt. 28:19; Jn. 5:19-23; 14:1, 23; 17:3; Rom. 1:7; 2 Cor. 13:14; 1 Jn. 5:7-8; Rev. 5:13; 7:10; 20:6).

### 8. Equality with God Is Definitely Stated

Scripture explicitly declares His equality with God in divinity (Jn. 5:19-29; Phil. 2:5-11).

### 9. Divine Characteristics Are Ascribed to Him

The Bible attributes divine characteristics to Christ (Jn. 5:19-29; 14:26; Heb. 1:9).

### 10. He Is Expressly Called "God" and "Lord"

Scripture directly calls Jesus "God" and "Lord" (Jn. 1:1-3; 20:28; Acts 2:36; 20:28).

## III. The Humanity of Jesus Christ

### 1. Human Names Are Ascribed to Him

Jesus bears human names that prove His humanity: "Rabboni" (Jn. 20:16); "Messiah" or "Christ" (Jn. 1:41; 4:25; Lk. 2:26); "Jesus" (Mt. 1:21); "Master" (Mt. 9:19); "Son of man" (Mt. 8:20); "Son of Mary" (Mk. 6:3); "Son of Abraham and David" (Mt. 1:1); "Seed and Offspring of David" (Rom. 1:3; Rev. 5:5; 22:16); "The Second Man" and "The Last Adam" (1 Cor. 15:45-47); "The King of the Jews" (Mt. 2:2); "Lamb of God" (Jn. 1:29).

### 2. He Is Called a "Babe," a "Child," and a "Man"

Scripture refers to Him with these distinctly human terms (Lk. 2:16; Isa. 9:6; Acts 17:31; 1 Tim. 2:4-5; Rom. 5:12-21; Jn. 8:40; Acts 2:22; 1 Cor. 15:21, 45-47).

### 3. Prophecy of His Human Birth Proves His Humanity

The Old Testament predicted He would be born of a human mother (Gen. 3:15; Isa. 7:14; 9:6-7; 11:1; 53:1-12; Ps. 22).

### 4. Historical Record of His Birth Proves His Humanity

The New Testament records His conception and birth from a woman (Mt. 1:18-25; 2:2; Lk. 1:32-35; 2:1-52; Gal. 4:4).

### 5. He Had Flesh and Blood Like All Other People

Jesus possessed a physical body just like ours (Jn. 1:14; Heb. 2:14-15; 1 Jn. 4:1-6; Lk. 24:39; Jn. 19:34).

### 6. He Had a Human Body Even After the Resurrection

His resurrected body was still physical (Lk. 24:39; Jn. 20:27).

### 7. He Had Human Limitations and Passions Like Us

Jesus experienced everything we experience: He "wept" (Jn. 11:35); "hungered" (Mt. 4:1-11); "thirsted" (Jn. 4:7; 19:28); "slept" (Mt. 8:24); "grew weary" (Jn. 4:6); "sorrowed" (Isa. 53:3-4); "suffered physical agony" (Lk. 22:44); "craved sympathy" (Mt. 26:36-40; Lk. 22:15-27); "was tempted in all points as men" (Heb. 4:14-16); "suffered physical death" (Jn. 19:30; 1 Cor. 15:3). He endured many human sufferings just as we do.

### 8. He Was Human in All Things

Jesus was subject to the physical, mental, and moral conditions of human existence just like the rest of us (Heb. 2:14-17; 4:14-16).

### 9. He Lived a Normal Human Life

He lived in total dependence upon God through prayer and faith for daily grace for body, soul, and spirit—exactly as all human beings should (Jn. 5:30-46; 6:57; 7:16; 8:27-29; Heb. 5:7-9; Phil. 2:5-10).

### 10. He Was Limited Like Other Humans

Jesus was limited in wisdom, knowledge, and power like other men and was subordinate to the Father, as we'll see in Point VIII below. In fact, His human nature is denied only by antichrists and demons (1 Jn. 2:18-23; 4:1-6).

## IV. The Union of the Two Natures of Jesus Christ

Everything we've studied about Christ's divinity and humanity proves He was a divine human being. The orthodox understanding holds that both natures of Christ were complete in themselves, yet so organically and permanently united that no third nature was formed. We must not divide His person or confuse His two natures.

Think of it like this: because He is truly divine, He perfectly represents God; because He is truly human, He perfectly represents us.

Christ always spoke of Himself as a single person, not as two persons in one. There's no back-and-forth conversation between His two natures as if they were separate people talking to each other. The attributes and powers of both natures belong to one person and operate as part of a single individual. There's no double personality—just one unified being with characteristics of both the human and the divine.

It's similar to how a father and mother each contribute traits to their child, creating a single person with characteristics from both parents. In the same way, the human and divine united in the one person of Jesus Christ—with one body, one soul, one spirit, one consciousness, and one will.

The Fatherhood of God and the motherhood of Mary produced a single personality. Remember that God made human beings with the same bodily parts He has in His Spirit-body—only our bodies are earthly and human while His is spiritual and divine. He made us with the same kind of soul with feelings, emotions, passions, desires, and appetites, capable of the same soul-acts as He is—only our souls are finite and His is infinite. He made us with spirits having all the attributes and powers that He has—only our spirits are finite and His is infinite.

In other words, we're endowed with the same traits, characteristics, attributes, powers, feelings, and passions as God—just on a finite scale.

Understanding this helps us see that the soul and spirit faculties born in Jesus Christ by a divine Father and human mother were exactly the same as in any being like God. When Christ exercised any attribute or power as a man, it was like God exercising those same aspects—only His faculties were perfectly untainted by the Fall and its effects. When Christ acted, He was like man before the Fall, not like sinful humanity after it.

Every fallen person who is recreated in Christ and made a new creature becomes capable of properly exercising their faculties in holy and lawful ways. (See Lesson Four, Point II, 8 and 9.)

Here's another way to think about it: unfallen humans acted exactly like God in exercising their faculties—just with limited attributes and powers. What is finite in us is infinite in God. When holy people are energized and endued with supernatural powers, they can exercise their natural attributes to a supernatural degree, depending on how much they yield to and are energized by the Spirit of God.

For example, Christ and the disciples, when endued with power from on High, were capable of God-action to destroy sin and sickness as effectively as if God Himself were doing the work without using them as instruments.

Remember also that when people are born again, they become partakers of the divine nature. To the extent that nature controls and works through their created faculties, they live divine lives and do divine works. In such people, their faculties are freed from evil acts and evil powers and become channels of divine energy through the Holy Spirit.

Just as Christ was perfectly helpless in Himself and acted, spoke, worked, and lived entirely through the Holy Spirit's anointing, believers who become like Christ become God-inspired, God-energized, and God-operated. (See Point V, 31 for Scriptures about a perfectly divine life in human form.) Christians fully living in God's fullness live a divine-human life in the Holy Spirit through God's very presence and power in their soul and spirit.

If we can grasp these truths, we can certainly understand how God could become so perfectly human while remaining so perfectly divine—a perfect union of God and man in one personality.

Whether Christ's divine attributes and powers were limited, and to what extent, remains a significant question in Christian circles. Whether He laid them aside entirely for a time or retained them but voluntarily limited their use will always be debated. However, this much is settled: He was limited during His days in the flesh, as we'll see in Point VIII.

Whether this limitation was constitutional or voluntary isn't really the main point. If it was done constitutionally, it was still voluntary, as stated in Jn. 10:18; Heb. 10:5-9. He wasn't forced to do anything—everything was a voluntary action on His part. Whether the limitation was built into His incarnation or whether He retained all divine powers and chose not to use them—the practical result was the same: He was limited as a man.

It was crucial that He limit Himself as a man to set the right example for us, so we can be inspired to live like God on Earth using the same means Christ used. Despite all the theological debates about whether He had two personalities, two natures in one personality, or some other arrangement—the fact remains that He was both human and divine. Even if some can't understand the "how" of it, the "fact" of it can be believed and must be believed if we want all the Scriptures to harmonize.

One certainty stands: His human nature had no separate existence before its union with the divine and is not in itself a separate personality from the divine person who became incarnated in human flesh.

His twofold nature was important not only for our sake, but also for God's sake, so He could be a true mediator between God and humanity. His dual nature gives Him fellowship with both parties and the ability to represent both in reconciliation.

As God, He upholds the dignity of deity. As man, He is truly sympathetic and able to meet human needs. Because He is God, His atonement has infinite value and effect. We'll discuss His dual natures further in Point VIII below.

## V. Jesus Christ Is Not the Father or the Holy Ghost

Many people are misled into making Jesus the only person in the Godhead—more than what the Bible says He is. In doing so, they rob the Father and the Holy Spirit of their own separate personalities and make them less than what Scripture reveals, depriving them of their rightful and separate places in the unity of God.

It's vitally important to know whether there are three persons in the Godhead so we can give each His rightful place, proper love, and worship—and so we can understand the Bible correctly without being led astray into false doctrines that come from twisting Scripture to fit human church creeds.

The following points prove that Jesus Christ is not the Father or the Holy Ghost:

### 1. The Father Was in Heaven While Jesus Was on Earth

The Father remained in Heaven the entire time Jesus walked the Earth, so the Father could not have been incarnated in Jesus (Mt. 5:16, 45, 48; 6:1, 9; 7:21; 16:17; 18:10; 23:9).

### 2. Jesus Would Confess Men Before His Father

Jesus said He would confess people "before my Father" and "before the angels"—impossible if He were the same person as the Father or the angels (Mt. 10:32-33; Lk. 12:8-9; Rev. 3:2-5). The word "before" means "in the presence of" or "face to face with," requiring both the Father and angels to be distinct persons from Jesus. This kind of language couldn't be used if only one person were involved, any more than it could in the 1,767 similar expressions throughout Scripture (Mt. 14:6; 17:2; 1 Tim. 5:19-20; 6:13; Rev. 4:5-6; 5:8; 7:9, 11, 15; 8:2; etc.).

### 3. Jesus Always Prayed to the Father

Jesus consistently prayed to the Father and addressed Him as a separate person (Mt. 11:25; 26:39, 42-46; Lk. 10:21; 22:42; 23:34; Jn. 11:41; 12:28; 17:1-25). Nowhere do we read of the Father praying to anyone—but the Son constantly prays to someone outside Himself.

### 4. The Father Protected Jesus from Outside His Body

The Father was outside Jesus' body, protecting Him, so He couldn't have been incarnated inside Jesus or be "all of God" inside Jesus as some teach (Mt. 2:12-23; 3:16-17; 17:5; Lk. 22:39-46; Jn. 12:27-30).

### 5. Old Testament Prophets Distinguish Father and Son

All the Old Testament prophets quoted in the New Testament prove that the Father is separate from the Son. It was the Father who spoke "by the prophets" and "through the Spirit" concerning the Son (Heb. 1:1-3; Acts 3:21; Rom. 1:1-4; 1 Pet. 1:1-16; 2 Pet. 1:21).

Note the speaker and the person spoken of in Mt. 2:15, 23; 4:6; 12:17-21; 22:41; 27:9-11; Lk. 4:16-21; 24:27, 44-46; Jn. 18:9; Acts 2:22-34; 3:13-24; 4:25-31; 7:2-50; 8:32-37; 10:34-43; 13:23-41; Heb. 2:3-9; 5:5-10. Human language is meaningless if two or three persons aren't understood in these statements.

### 6. Both Jesus and Satan Referred to the Father as Separate

Both Jesus and Satan spoke of the Father as a separate person from the Son: "He [one person] shall give his angels charge concerning thee" refers to Jesus, the Son of the Father, another person (Mt. 4:6).

### 7. Jesus Constantly Distinguished Himself from the Father

Jesus consistently referred to the Father as a separate person dwelling in Heaven, as far from Earth as Heaven is above Earth (Mt. 7:21; 10:32-33; 11:27; 15:13; 16:17, 27; 18:10-35; 20:23; Mk. 12:32; Jn. 5).

### 8. God Is Called "The God and Father of Our Lord Jesus Christ"

New Testament writers called the Father "The God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ"—impossible if He were the Lord Jesus Christ (Eph. 1:3, 17; 3:14; 1 Pet. 1:3; Mt. 27:46; Jn. 20:17).

### 9. Terms Like "Son of the Father" Require Two Persons

Phrases like "the Son of the Father" (2 Jn. 3), "his Father" (Mt. 16:27; Rev. 1:6; 14:1), "my Father" (used 57 times, Mt. 7:21; Jn. 15:1; Rev. 2:27; 3:5; etc.), and "my God" (Jn. 20:17; Rev. 3:12) couldn't be true if Jesus were the Father and the only person called God.

If Jesus spoke of God the Father as being His Father and His God, then He either lied or told the truth. Such language demands we believe in another person who is the God and Father of Jesus Christ. Not once did Jesus or any Bible writer use unscriptural terms like "Jesus, the Father," "the Father Jesus," "spirit-Jesus," "Father Jesus," "one person in the Godhead," or "Jesus only."

### 10. Jesus' Parables Show His Relationship to the Father

In His parables, Jesus illustrates His relationship to the Father as that of a Son—a separate person from the Father (Mt. 21:33-46; Lk. 20:9-18; Jn. 15:1-10).

To believe only one person is both Father and Son in these passages would make Jesus a plain liar. If He said He was "the vine" and the Father was "the husbandman" (Jn. 15), and if God the Father is compared to "a certain householder" while the Son is compared to "his son" and "heir," then this relationship is the truth—distinguishing two persons known as "the Father" and "the Son" (Mt. 21:33-46; Lk. 20:9-18).

### 11. Jesus Taught Us to Pray to the Father, Not to Himself

Jesus instructed us to go directly to the Father in prayer and not to pray to Him at all: "Ye shall ask me nothing . . . ask the Father in my name, he will give it you" (Jn. 14:12-15; 15:16; 16:23-28).

What could be clearer? If we're commanded to "ask me nothing" but to "ask the Father" instead, then Jesus is not the Father. It's unreasonable to teach that Jesus and His Father are the same person—with the body being the Son and the inner man being the Father.

It would be absurd to ask us to pray to one part of a person in the name of another part of the same person, or to call different parts by different names. Even more problematic, some people ignore the Father entirely and pray only to the Son. If Jesus' language doesn't refer to two persons, we'd have to conclude He didn't know how to use human language.

### 12. Jesus Thanked the Father by Looking Up to Heaven

On certain occasions, Jesus thanked the Father "looking up to Heaven," where the Father dwelt—not looking inside Himself to a Father within (Jn. 11:41; Mt. 26:25-27; Mk. 8:6; 14:23). Was Jesus giving thanks to Himself and teaching us self-praise? Or was there a real Father outside of Him who dwelt in Heaven as a separate person?

### 13. Many Statements About the Father Couldn't Be True of Jesus

Consider these distinctions: The Father was in Heaven while Jesus was on Earth (Mt. 5:1, 48). The Father knew things Jesus didn't know (Mt. 10:29-31; Mk. 13:32; Acts 1:7; Rev. 1:1). The Father was "good," but Jesus didn't claim that quality for Himself (Mt. 19:17). The Father was on a throne, and Jesus was not (Mt. 23:22). Jesus is coming in the glory of the Father, not His own glory (Mt. 16:27).

Jesus prayed to the Father, never to Himself (Mt. 26:39-42; Jn. 17). He prophesied He would be exalted at the Father's right hand (Mt. 26:64), and the apostles later confirmed He was there (Acts 2:33-36; Eph. 1:20; Col. 3:1; Heb. 1:3; 8:1; 12:2; Rom. 8:34). Stephen actually saw Jesus with his own eyes at God's right hand (Acts 7:56-59). Jesus committed His spirit to God the Father at death, proving He died while the Father did not (Lk. 23:46). Others also saw Jesus as a separate person from the Father (Dan. 7:9-14; Rev. 5:1-7).

### 14. Jesus Claimed to Be Sent by God

Jesus said He was sent by God, came from God, and was going back to God (Mt. 15:24; Jn. 3:16-18, 34; 5:30, 36-37; 6:29-40, 44, 57; 7:16, 28-29; 8:16-18, 29, 42; 9:4; 10:36; 11:42; 12:45, 49; 15:21; 16:5; 17:3, 8, 21-25; Gal. 4:4; 1 Jn. 4:9).

These Scriptures wouldn't make sense if only one person were referred to. The sense in which God sent Jesus is the same sense in which Jesus sent His disciples (Jn. 17:18; 20:21) and the same sense in which the Father and Son sent the Spirit (Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15).

Being sent doesn't make someone the same person as the sender—otherwise the disciples would have become Jesus when He sent them. If being sent proves two persons (sender and sent), then both Jesus and the Holy Spirit are separate persons from each other and from the Father, since both were sent by the Father.

### 15. Jesus Was Flesh and Blood, Not a Spirit

Jesus plainly told Peter that His Father in Heaven was not "flesh and blood," and He told the Samaritan woman His Father and God was "Spirit" (Mt. 16:16-17; Jn. 4:24; 19:34).

Because Jesus was flesh and blood and didn't claim to be "spirit" even in His resurrected state, He couldn't be the Father (Lk. 24:39; Jn. 19:34; Rom. 8:3).

### 16. The Father's Voice Came from Heaven

Peter received a revelation from the Father in Heaven about Jesus' Sonship (Mt. 16:17) and actually heard the Father's voice from Heaven saying of the Son on Earth, "This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased, hear ye him" (Mt. 17:5).

Peter later testified this voice came from Heaven—not from inside Jesus through some kind of ventriloquism. He said it came "from God the Father . . . from the excellent glory . . . from heaven" (2 Pet. 1:16-18). John the Baptist also heard this voice from Heaven while Jesus was on Earth (Mt. 3:16-17). They never said or believed Jesus was the Father in Heaven speaking.

### 17. The Jews Understood Jesus Claimed to Be the Son, Not the Father

The Jews never understood Jesus to claim He was the Father—they understood He claimed to be the Son, thus making Himself equal with God (Mt. 26:64; 27:40-43; Jn. 5:17-35; 6:45; 8:13-38; 10:34-39; 19:7).

If He had claimed to be the Father, the only God, all of God, and the only person of the Godhead, they would have had a legitimate case against Him—because not one prophet ever foretold this doctrine. They did prophesy that God would have a Son as a separate person from Himself.

### 18. Jesus Called the Father "My God" Even After the Resurrection

Even after rising from the dead, Jesus called the Father "my God" (Jn. 10:17; Rev. 3:12; Ps. 22:1-10). He couldn't be His own Father and His own God. If He were the only person in the Godhead, this would be a false statement.

### 19. The Angel Gabriel Distinguished Jesus from God

The angel Gabriel—"the angel of the Lord" (whom some sects claim was God Himself)—didn't know Jesus was the only person in the Godhead. He spoke of a God still in Heaven and called Jesus only "the Son of God" and "the Son of the Highest" (Mt. 1:18-25; Lk. 1:19, 27-38; 2:21).

### 20. All the Angels Distinguished Jesus from God

All the angels in Heaven shared Gabriel's understanding, praising and giving glory to a "God in the highest" who was outside the baby Jesus in the manger (Lk. 2:8-16).

It's no sin for us to believe that these angels—fresh from Heaven, having come

lessons.title: A Christian's Power of Attorney
lessons.content:
# A Christian's Power of Attorney

The New Testament teaches that unlimited power is available to every believer. We've touched on this in other lessons, but now let's dig into the scriptural foundation for this incredible truth.

## I. Definition of Agency and Power of Attorney

The Bible teaches a clear doctrine of divine agency. In the business world, agency describes a relationship where one person (the agent) represents another (the principal) in legitimate business transactions.

There are two types of agents. A General Agent has authority to represent the principal in all business matters or in a particular type of business. A Special Agent is authorized to handle specific tasks—like selling a farm, buying a house, or completing a particular transaction.

Anyone capable of free and intelligent action can serve as either a principal or an agent. However, someone who lacks sound mind and understanding—such as a person who is mentally incapacitated—cannot act in either role.

An agent's authority can be established in three ways: by a sealed deed, by a written document, or simply by spoken word. When authority is given through a formal written document, it's called a power of attorney.

The principal is responsible for the agent's actions when the agent is conducting authorized business. If an agent hides who their principal is, the agent becomes personally responsible to third parties. An agent cannot appoint a substitute or pass their authority to someone else without the principal's consent.

Authority can be terminated in several ways:
- When the principal revokes it
- When the agent renounces the power
- When either party dies
- When the authorized time period expires
- When the principal's business goes bankrupt or is sold
- When either party becomes mentally incapacitated

Termination takes effect the moment both the agent and any third parties receive notice. An agent cannot receive personal profit from business transactions beyond their agreed salary or commission.

If you're dealing with an agent, you should investigate their authority. Always ask for evidence of an agent's authority before doing business with them.

When you need to delegate authority for signing notes, checks, or other legal documents, it's wise to create a written document and have it recorded in court. This document—the power of attorney—should be signed by the principal, witnessed, and acknowledged before an officer, just like a deed. It must clearly and explicitly state exactly what the agent has power to do.

Here's a simple example of what a power of attorney looks like:

> "KNOW ALL MEN BY THESE PRESENTS, That, I, John Doe, of Atlanta, County of Fulton, and State of Georgia, have made, constituted and appointed, and BY THESE PRESENTS do make, constitute and appoint, John Smith, true and lawful attorney for me and in my name, place and stead, (here state the purpose for which the power is given), giving and granting unto my said attorney full power of authority to do and perform all and every act and thing whatsoever, requisite and necessary to be done in and about the premises, as fully, to all intents and purposes, as I might or could do if personally present, with all power of substitution and revocation, hereby ratifying and confirming all that my said attorney or substitute shall lawfully do or cause to be done by virtue thereof.
>
> IN WITNESS WHEREOF, I have hereunto set my hand and seal the second day of January, one thousand nine hundred and forty-nine.
>
> Signed, Sealed and Delivered in the Presence of
>
> John Doe (Seal)

## II. Biblical Proof for the Christian's Power of Attorney

Here's the amazing truth: every believer has the legal and redemptive right to use the name of Jesus when asking and receiving from God. This puts prayer on a purely legal basis.

Jesus Himself said, "Where two or three are gathered together in my name, there am I in the midst of them" (Mt. 18:19-20). He also declared, "These signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues; They shall take up serpents (see Lesson Fourteen, V, II); and if they drink any deadly thing it shall not hurt them; they shall lay hands on the sick, and they shall recover" (Mk. 16:15-20).

Listen to these powerful promises: "Verily, verily, I say unto you, He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do; because I go unto my Father. And whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do, that the Father may be glorified in the Son. If ye shall ask any thing in my name, I will do it" (Jn. 14:12-15).

And there's more: "Ye have not chosen me, but I have chosen you, and ordained you, that ye should go and bring forth fruit, and that your fruit should remain: that whatsoever ye shall ask of the Father in my name, he may give it you" (Jn. 15:16). Jesus also said, "Whatsoever ye shall ask the Father in my name, he will give it you. Hitherto have ye asked nothing in my name: ask and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full . . . At that day ye shall ask in my name" (Jn. 16:23-26).

These are Jesus's direct words—and they express your power of attorney as a Christian. He has given you unqualified use of His name to carry on God's work in this world. Everything God has invested in His name belongs to you.

As Paul put it, "All things are yours . . . And ye are Christ's; and Christ's is God's" (1 Cor. 3:21-23). Jesus assures us that all prayer prayed in His name receives special attention from both Himself and His Father. Think of it like this: it's as if Jesus is saying, "You ask the Father in My name; I'll endorse it, and the Father will give it."

If modern Christians could wake up and realize their New Testament rights—if we would exercise our full authority—there would be no failure, no sin, no bad habits, no unhappiness, no defeat, no sickness, and no want among us. As we've seen in Lesson Ten, God has promised not just our needs but all of our wants.

When you pray in Jesus's name, you pray by His authority. It's as if Jesus Himself were praying. And God always hears Him—the Gospel records prove it. At Lazarus's grave, Jesus said, "Father, I thank thee that thou hast heard me. And I knew that thou hearest me always" (Jn. 11:41-42).

If we understood the power of praying by Christ's authority, we'd have so much more confidence in answered prayer. We'd realize that God always hears Jesus, and when we pray by His authority, our prayers will always be answered—just as Scripture promises.

When you pray in Jesus's name, you're not just praying by His authority—your prayers pass into His hands, and He takes responsibility for the answer. He says, "Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, that will I do" and "I will do it" (Jn. 14:12-15). Of the Father, He said, "Whatsoever ye shall ask in my name, he will give it you" (Jn. 16:23-26). Paul added, "He that spared not his own Son, but delivered him up for us all, how shall he not with him also freely give us all things" (Rom. 8:32).

Christ isn't just our substitute in atonement—He also stands before God on our behalf, ever living to make intercession according to God's will (Rom. 8:34; Heb. 7:25). His work wasn't completed at the cross. He remains our High Priest before God (Heb. 4:14-16). He's our Advocate—our Defense Attorney—when Satan accuses us before God (1 Jn. 2:1-2; Rev. 12:10).

Where's the room for failure? We're commanded to come boldly to the throne of grace to obtain mercy and help in our time of need, leaving our case in His hands (Heb. 4:14-16; 10:19-23). There can be no failure when true, unwavering faith is exercised (Jas. 1:5-8; Heb. 11:6). Prayer is a business proposition and should be the chief business of our lives—because the chief business of our lives needs much prayer to succeed.

These Scriptures about praying in His name were among Jesus's last words before ascending to the Father. They're part of the Church's commission to evangelize the world and reconcile it to God. In these passages, we find the Christian's power of attorney over satanic forces that hinder the gospel. "In my name they shall cast out devils."

This was true during the disciples' preaching tours before the cross (Mt. 10:1-20; Lk. 9:1-9; 10:1-20; Mk. 6:7-13). It was true after the cross (Acts 2:43; 3:6-7; 5:12-16; 6:8; 8:1-21; 16:16-34; 19:11-20).

There is power in the name of Jesus. The demons said, "Jesus I know, and Paul I know"—meaning, we recognize Jesus and Paul as our masters whom we obey, but we know nothing about you seven men whom we've never encountered in battle (Acts 19:11-20).

Jesus declared, "All authority is given unto me in heaven and in earth" (Mt. 28:18). By His authority, believers were commissioned to go into all the world, preach the gospel to every creature, and watch God defeat demon powers in Jesus's name. This was to continue "even unto the end of the world" (Mt. 28:18-20). In other words, when we preach truth, it sets people free from demon powers—wherever and as long as truth is preached.

Jesus sent the disciples to make disciples in all nations, promising to back them up with confirming signs (Mk. 16:15-20; Heb. 2:3-4). Everyone who preaches has a perfect right to cast out devils and see all the gospel's signs following them. If this isn't happening, they're poor representatives of God, failing to fulfill their commission.

The name of Jesus means everything to the Father. He will always honor this name because it represents completed redemption and the world's salvation from Satan's eternal rule. It means the Father's eternal plan for a perfect, sinless society on Earth and throughout the universe is guaranteed. This means more to the Father than our finite minds can grasp—because we don't yet fully understand the scope of God's plan.

The Father recognizes everything Jesus's name implies. He knows we have a legal and family right to use this name in prayer. He knows its proper use delivers all people from sin, sickness, and failure. It's God's glory to recognize Jesus's name in prayer and answer according to our faith.

To Satan and demons, the name of Jesus means utter defeat, humiliation, and banishment to the lowest Hell. They're already defeated and must release anyone they control when Jesus's name is used against them. They obey that name. They fear and tremble when it's used in faith. They flee when resisted and rebuked by this name.

To the Church, the name of Jesus means that anything needed or wanted—now and forever—will be granted when used in unwavering faith. All of God's infinite resources belong to the Church. Nothing is impossible to believers who fully and freely use Jesus's name.

God's plan for the Church is to take and use the full power of attorney, conducting business for God and Christ by the Holy Spirit's power—just as Christ carried on God's work by the Spirit when He was on Earth. We saw this in Lesson Twenty.

If there's any failure to carry on this work and do even greater works than Christ did, it's not God's failure. It's not lack of power in Jesus's name. It's not that redemption is incomplete or our case has been lost in Heaven's High Court. It's not that the devil is stronger than God. It's not that the Holy Spirit has failed to empower people.

It's simply that the Church as a whole and individuals in particular have failed to exercise their God-given rights and privileges as taught in the gospel—and to become endued with power from on High.

The Church today largely doesn't understand the body of Christ's true purpose in the world. Most people don't understand the real issues between God and Satan. Satan takes advantage of this ignorance and unbelief, holding back the Church's usefulness on Earth. He's defeating Christians by the millions.

He convinces them it's not God's will for them to get what they want in life. He makes them afraid to use Jesus's name in prayer and daily conflict against him and his demons. As long as the Church lives in this ignorance, it will be defeated in doing God's works—works that should be manifest in every local church today as they were in early churches.

Many thousands of religious people deny the essentials of gospel faith—more or less—things like the virgin birth, the incarnation, Jesus's deity and sinlessness, His redemptive work, His death, burial, resurrection, and ascension, the Holy Spirit's descent, the power of Jesus's name, the Christian's power of attorney, and many truths that would make the Church victorious. As long as this continues, New Testament Christianity will be rarely seen.

It's crucial that people gain true knowledge of Jesus Christ. That's why we've devoted most of these two lessons to the truth about Jesus. If He is divine, He is our Lord. If He is our Lord, He has the right to intervene in human affairs.

His gospel should be obeyed. His name should be honored and exercised with all authority over demons, sin, sickness, and life's troubles. Everything built around Him should be cherished and upheld. Men should follow His pure example of godliness and consecrate themselves to righteousness and true holiness. Women should pledge their lives to Him, preserving their purity for themselves and coming generations. Children should be raised to love, respect, and follow Him throughout life to the glorious future with Him.

All should obey His teachings and strive to reproduce New Testament Christianity in life and power—so multitudes can be saved, healed, and blessed with prosperity, health, and victory over all enemies of God and humanity.

## III. Christ's Power of Attorney

Christ obtained all authority in Heaven and Earth through several means—authority He gives to us to carry on His work throughout this age. If we're to be given power of attorney, we should understand that Christ has the right to give it and the extent to which we can exercise it.

Here's the sevenfold way Christ obtained His great name and power of attorney:

### 1. His Deity

By virtue of being God by nature and the creative agent in making and upholding all things, He has the right to this authority. As God, He alone could receive this honor (Col. 1:15-18; Heb. 1:1-3).

### 2. By Birth

Not only because of His deity but also His humanity, Christ obtained His place before God and man as Savior, Life-giver, and Power of God. "Thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins" (Mt. 1:21). This person was "Emmanuel" or God with us (Mt. 1:23; Isa. 7:14; 9:6-7). Jesus was born to be King of the Jews and Savior of the world (Jn. 19:36-37; Lk. 2:11, 25-32).

### 3. By His Sinlessness

Jesus was the only human being since the Fall who lived without sin. He alone could atone for humanity because the substitute had to be sinless, as we saw in Lesson Nineteen, Point IX.

He was made in the likeness of flesh that sin controlled, but His flesh was not sinful flesh or under sin's dominion like all other people (Rom. 8:1-3; 2 Cor. 5:21; 1 Pet. 2:21-25). Because of His sinless life, God blessed Him above all others: "Thou hast loved righteousness, and hated iniquity; therefore God, even thy God, hath anointed thee with the oil of gladness above thy fellows" (Heb. 2:9).

### 4. By His Preparation

From childhood, Christ prepared Himself for the work His Father sent Him to do. Certain passages make this clear (see Lesson Twenty-one, Point IX).

### 5. By His Anointing

Because of His humanity and earthly limitations as a man—and because of His sinlessness and preparation—God gave Him the full anointing of the Holy Spirit to achieve greatness in three worlds: Heaven, Earth, and Hell.

By this anointing, as predicted in Isa. 11:1-2; 43:17; 61:1-2 and fulfilled in Mt. 3:16-17; Lk. 4:16-21; Acts 10:38; Jn. 3:34, He gained eternal fame throughout the universe.

### 6. By His Achievements

What He accomplished for God and humanity makes Him great. Death, Hell, and the grave were mastered by Him, along with sin and demons on Earth. Every enemy of God and humanity was fully conquered. Redemption was completed. God and humanity became reconciled, and God's eternal plan for us will be realized.

His fame reached the highest Heaven and lowest Hell. All creatures must bow their knees to Him (Phil. 2:5-11; Col. 1:16-21; 2:14-17; Eph. 1:20-23; 4:7-11; Heb. 2:14-15; 1 Pet. 3:22; Rev. 5:1-14; 7:11).

His achievements in life were so numerous that if they were all written, even the world couldn't contain the books (Jn. 21:25). His achievements in death were even greater. For His work in death, God highly exalted Him above every name, and every tongue must confess Him as Lord to God's glory (Phil. 2:5-11).

### 7. By Inheritance

Paul speaks of Christ as having been appointed "heir of all things . . . he hath by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they" (the angels, Heb. 1:1-14).

Some have achieved great names and fame through birth, education, preparation, and achievements. But no one has obtained such fame across all realms of the universe as Christ—and by all these means combined. Some achieve temporary, local fame. Christ, through all the above, has gained universal and eternal fame.

This super-exalted, eternal Person has authority in Heaven and Earth and wants to confer power of attorney upon all believers. Now all believers can do Christ's works—and greater works—because, as He said, "I go unto my Father" (Jn. 14:12-15).

Today He's exalted at God's right hand, with angels, principalities, and powers subject to Him (Eph. 1:20-23; 1 Pet. 3:22). He upholds all things by His powerful Word. As Head of the Church and Custodian of the power of attorney, He bestows full legal authority upon all believers who learn the truth and tarry until endued with power from on High (Lk. 24:49; Acts 1:8).

God has on deposit in Heaven everything the Church needs now or ever will need. This is exclusively for the Church to carry on the work Jesus began—both to do and to teach. The Church has the right to draw to the limit of its faith.

Think of it like a bank account with unlimited funds. There are no limits if our faith is unlimited. The only limitation is our unbelief and doubt about what we can receive from God—for ourselves and for the world in which we serve as God's agents in Christ's place.

All the power in Jesus's name is ours. If there's any limitation in God, then there's a limitation to the power in Jesus's name—and naturally there will be limits to what we receive. But since God cannot be limited, let's not limit what's in Christ's name or what we can get through it. We have the right to use His name against our enemies, in our petitions, in daily conflict, in our work, in worship, and in everything we do.

There's no question about Christ's authority to confer power of attorney on all Christians. There's no question about the legality of this transaction. He demonstrated His own power of attorney using His Father's name. He said, "I am come in my Father's name," and the works He did were "in my Father's name" (Jn. 5:43; 10:25; 17:6, 11, 26).

Because He faithfully exercised His power of attorney—acting in His Father's place on Earth and doing the works His Father would have done—God gave Him all the glory He had before coming into the world (Jn. 17:1-5). God gave Him all power in Heaven and Earth so He could confer this power on anyone worthy to represent Him as He represented God.

Christ conferred this power on others while on Earth (Mt. 10; Lk. 10). How much more can He confer it now—since He has spoiled principalities and powers and finished redemption!

In actual combat with Hell's powers, when all demon hosts intended to overwhelm Him, He proved Himself more than their master. He shook them off and hurled back these evil forces, as pictured in Col. 2:14-17; Heb. 2:14-15.

At that moment, Satan was defeated and his power over humanity was broken forever. The devil is now a defeated foe, waiting only until Christ dispossesses him from Earth. Christ is the undisputed victor over all evil forces. He champions humanity's cause to regain original dominion.

He is Lord of all with a name above every name except His Father's. He holds all power to confer upon anyone who will fully cooperate with Him in the age-long struggle to defeat Satan in people's lives. The early believers after the cross fully exercised this power (Acts 1:8; 2:43; 3:6-7; 5:12-16; 19:11-20; etc.).

## IV. Christian Exercise of the Power of Attorney

All Christians are commanded to use Jesus's name:

- "in salvation" (Mt. 1:21; Acts 4:12)
- "in baptism" (Mt. 28:19)
- "in healing" (Acts 3:6-7, 16)
- "in preaching" (Lk. 24:47; Acts 8:12)
- "in miracles" (Acts 3:16; 4:30; Mk. 9:29)
- "in judgment" (1 Cor. 5:1-5)
- "in appeals to others" (1 Cor. 1:10)
- "in prayer" (Jn. 14:12-15; 16:23-26)
- "in faith and repentance" (Jn. 1:12; 3:18; Acts 10:43)
- "in praise" (Heb. 13:15)
- "in all gatherings" (Mt. 18:20)
- "in consecration" (Mt. 19:29)
- "in charity" (Mk. 9:41)
- "in singing" (Rom. 15:9)
- "in combat" (Mk. 16:17; Lk. 10:17; Eph. 6:10-18)
- "in all things" (Col. 3:17; Eph. 5:20)

The name of Jesus and free use of it is the Church's deposit. But you must make definite contact with God, exercise faith in the name, and settle your authority as God's child to write checks on the Bank of Heaven before properly using Jesus's name.

Sinners have no right to use His name except in repentance (Acts 2:21; 10:43; Lk. 24:47). Sinners cannot perform miracles in His name (Acts 19:11-20). But any believer can use His name in all the ways listed above.

No special faith is required—just simple, ordinary, childlike faith in God and Christ's work is all that's needed. "Have faith in God . . . shall not doubt in his heart, but shall believe that those things which he saith shall come to pass; he shall have whatsoever he saith . . . What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them" (Mk. 11:22-24; Mt. 21:21-22).

The idea that faith isn't necessary to use Jesus's name isn't scriptural. Faith is necessary in everything we ask of God (Heb. 11:6; Jas. 1:5-8). Just because faith isn't emphasized in certain passages doesn't mean it's not required. Study and believe all Scripture on a subject, obeying it all, if you want results.

Let me illustrate with a personal story. Years ago, a man gave me a blank check to buy the best overcoat I could find. He put no limit on the amount—I was to get the best coat in town, or whatever I wanted.

But I was timid and worried. What if I wrote too much and he didn't like it? What if he never gave me anything again? So I wrote the check for $18.50 and got the cheapest coat in town. It was a good coat, but not the best.

If I could do it over, I wouldn't hesitate to get what I wanted—what he really wanted me to have. But fear of what he might think kept me from receiving what was freely offered.

That's exactly how most Christians treat God.

There's no question about how much is deposited to your name in Heaven. There's plenty for all. But we're fearful we'll bankrupt God if we ask for what we really want. We receive according to our faith—according to the amount we write the check for—and no more. Heaven's divine cashier always honors checks signed in Jesus's name by any believer. Many times we go without because we don't write enough.

Here's another story. Years ago in California, a woman prayed for $50,000 to build rest homes for missionaries. She prayed until she had God's assurance her prayer was answered.

She had a vacant lot. One day a man came asking to lease it for oil drilling. The Lord told her this was her answer. She leased the lot and kept royalty rights. A well was drilled and produced oil until she had exactly what she'd prayed for. Then it went dry.

She got exactly what she had faith for. This is always the case. Jesus said, "according to your faith be it unto you" (Mt. 9:29). You can sign a check on Heaven's Bank for what you want—or sign one for bare needs—and receive as you believe (Ps. 84:11; Mk. 11:22-24).

Christ and the early Church demonstrated God's power and showed us how to ask, receive, and use the power of attorney. They performed miracles and signs, which opened doors to win souls and gave them authority in their calling. They believed God and manifested the supernatural, confirming what they preached.

Every born-again Christian has the right to use this name for what they want. The weakest child of God will be honored if they serve Jesus Christ and walk in God's Word's light. Healing, health, salvation from sin and habits, and material blessings of all kinds are available in abundance.

All that Jesus was, His name still is to believers. Very few are availing themselves of this power, but if Jesus tarries and this message spreads, Christians will see the truth and practice it. The time will come when multitudes understand the secret of faith and use their power of attorney for great and mighty things in Jesus's name.

You who read these lessons should be openhearted and believing. Seek God and build up faith in the Christian's power of attorney. Realize that Satan is a defeated foe, helpless before Jesus's name when invoked by faith. Sicknesses, sins, and all results of the Fall have been conquered by Jesus Christ. At His rebuke, they all must give way. May God open our eyes to the blessings for all who accept truth and exercise faith in Jesus's name and the New Testament gospel.

The Christian's power of attorney includes authority to do Christ's works and greater works (Jn. 14:12-14). It covers everything God has promised in hundreds of wonderful, unlimited promises. It covers every need in this life and the life to come (2 Cor. 1:20; 2 Pet. 1:3-4; 1 Tim. 4:8).

It's appalling how far the Church has backslidden from the New Testament program. True spiritual life and power with God is poorly understood even by today's best Christians. Listening to average preachers, you'd think the devil went out of business. The sooner we believe the heavenlies are filled with demons seeking to defeat believers and hinder prayers, the sooner we'll take up arms and cooperate with God against them.

When dealing with demon spirits, we must be clean Christians, free from sin—or they'll mock our attempts to cast them out. We must know the power of Jesus's name and how to use it. We must take a definite stand against demons in His name and refuse defeat.

Whatever the need, it can be supplied in Jesus's name. The enemy hindering us must be resisted, and he will flee. James said, "Resist the devil and he will flee from you" (Jas. 4:7).

We must understand that we're God's representatives, taking Christ's place in this world to cast out demons. Christ has taken our place before the Father to represent us and defeat Satan, who accuses saints before God day and night. We act in Christ's stead on Earth as He acts in our stead in Heaven. Our position in Christ gives us the standing before God that Christ had when here. We rebuke demons and heal the sick in His place, at His command. We are in Christ, Christ is in us, and we have the legal right to represent Him on Earth.

Christianity began in miracles and is propagated by miracles. Its founder was a miraculous man and miracle worker. Every phase of His life and death was miraculous. His resurrection guarantees continued miracles.

Every new birth, every answered prayer, every Christian victory is a miracle. When reasoning and questioning replace faith and power, Christianity becomes fruitless and dead. Christianity is God's life in us through Christ—the Holy Spirit's power through faith in Christ's cross.

People crave the miraculous, and those who produce divine works gain great followings. Many are deceived by demons and counterfeit miracles because of the natural human hunger for God's power. How sad! People turn from the miracle-working Christ to demon powers while the Church sleeps, unaware of what's happening. When anyone tries to restore Christianity's power to churches, they're often cast out as fanatics and deceivers. But if we remove God's power from Christianity, we have only a form of godliness (2 Tim. 3:5).

The Bible records miracles, and whoever produces them draws crowds—whether false or true. This love of the miraculous isn't ignorance; it's humanity's longing for spiritual power that comes from knowing and hungering for God.

Every revival in any generation has produced miracles. Education doesn't eliminate the desire for the miraculous—it intensifies it as education reveals our dependence on the unseen. Denying the miraculous isn't scholarship; it's foolishness. Any outpouring of the Spirit will produce miraculous revival. All normal people crave the supernatural. No one is satisfied until finding God's reality in their life.

Think of it like this: the miracle element in humanity has driven us to invent, discover, investigate, and experiment with unseen laws around us. That's why we have so many wonderful modern things. The miracle realm is humanity's natural realm. We were created to experience the supernatural. People are again coming to know that faith and trust in God come as we simply and consciously live in God's realm through Jesus Christ.

The name of Jesus stands for justice, love, righteousness, holiness, peace, power, influence, happiness, liberty, life, light, joy, friendship, judgment, salvation, home, Heaven, and all that is good. It has been history's greatest name. Some have died for it; others have hated it enough to be damned because of it. It is the world's hope and all saints' joy.

The most wonderful truth for believers is the free, unreserved use of His name to carry on God's work on Earth and act in His stead among humanity, defeating sin and Satan.

Think of it! An ordinary believing human being—given full power of attorney to act in God's place in the world! We are God's only visible agents on Earth. Why wouldn't God want us to fully represent Him with great power and authority, proving to people that God is great and powerful?

From how most people represent God, you'd think Satan is greater and more powerful. Weak, failing representatives don't convince unbelievers of God's greatness.

The purpose of the power of attorney and its normal use is to confirm God's Word and prove to humanity that God is the Supreme Being of the universe.

All God's representatives should be like Paul, who told the Romans, "I will not dare to speak of any of those things which Christ hath not wrought by me, to make the Gentiles obedient by word and deed, through mighty signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God . . . I have fully preached the gospel of Christ . . . And I am sure that, when I come unto you, I shall come in the fullness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ" (Rom. 15:18-19, 29).

He told the Corinthians, "I will come unto you shortly, if the Lord will, and will know, not the speech of them which are puffed up, but the power. For the kingdom of God is not in word, but in power" (1 Cor. 4:18-20).

The proper use of Jesus's name will heal the sick, cast out devils, make one immune to poisons, answer every prayer, give health and prosperity, perform miracles, and defeat all the devil's powers. God never intended any change in His methods of preaching or confirming His Word.

Before the cross, the disciples used His name to defeat Satan (Mt. 10:1-20;

lessons.title: The Bible Doctrine of Sin
lessons.content:
# The Bible Doctrine of Sin

## I. Moral Law and Moral Government

To really understand sin in a clear, straightforward way, we first need to grasp what moral law and moral government are all about.

### 1. LAW MEANS A RULE OF ACTION

Law is a rule of action established by a recognized authority to enforce justice and define what we owe to one another and to those in charge. Physical law governs the material universe—it keeps planets in orbit and seasons cycling according to God's design.

Moral law, on the other hand, is the rule of action for beings like us—free moral agents—guiding how we use our choices and creative abilities according to God's plan. Think of it like this: physical law is the rails a train runs on automatically, but moral law is the road map for someone who can choose which way to drive.

### 2. THE PURPOSE OF MORAL LAW

Moral law exists to reveal and declare the right way for free wills to act regarding the highest good of everyone and everything in the universe. It governs our acts and intentions in our relationship with God and all creation.

It speaks to our reason, showing us what we *ought* to choose, will, and intend.

### 3. THE IMMUTABILITY OF MORAL LAW

Moral law can never change. Its demand remains the same: that all free wills devote themselves to the same goal God pursues—the highest good of the universe and everything in it.

Even when free wills rebel, God's plan for creation's good doesn't budge. Because of this, the moral law stays constant forever.

### 4. THE OBLIGATION OF MORAL LAW

Because moral law exists for the good of all beings, it's naturally binding on everyone. Whatever works against the good of beings is obviously wrong and must be prohibited.

We, as free moral agents, must work together to bring about the greatest public good and stand against whatever brings misery and hardship to society. It's a mutual plan benefiting each person and the whole universe—and we're all obligated to keep it according to the light we've received.

### 5. THE REQUIREMENT OF MORAL GOVERNMENT

For moral law to work, there must be moral government to direct, guide, and control moral agents. Think of it like traffic laws: without enforcement—without penalties for running red lights and rewards for safe driving—roads would be chaos.

There must be a standard of living to which all must conform to be part of society, and there must be a way of dealing with rebels who disturb the peace. Law without penalties and rewards isn't really law at all—it's just advice people can take or leave without consequences.

Moral government is obligated to execute the moral law faithfully and completely.

### 6. REQUIREMENT OF A MORAL GOVERNOR

Since we need moral law and moral government, we also need a moral governor—someone whose will and decisions are considered infallible by everyone under that government. This governor must be authoritative, not merely advisory.

He must maintain the respect of his subjects through faithful, unselfish service. He must be able to execute penalties and distribute rewards. Naturally, whoever is best qualified to rule and secure the highest good of all should be the one to rule—it's both his right and his duty.

No one but God can meet these requirements. His natural attributes, His perfect moral character, His relationship to the universe as Creator, and His history of absolute justice qualify Him alone to be the Supreme Moral Governor.
