Since our good and His glory depend on our mutual commitment to the same goal, He is obligated to require us to be holy and dedicated to helping Him secure the highest good of all.

### 7. FREE MORAL AGENCY DEFINED

Free moral agency consists of three things: intellect, sensibility, and free will. These form the foundation of our moral obligation to moral government.

The intellect includes reason and self-determination. Sensibility includes self-consciousness, all sensation, desire, emotion, passion, and feeling. Free will is the power to choose concerning moral law—it's your ability to choose good or evil without being forced.

This capacity was originally created in humanity, and we'll have it for all eternity.

### 8. THE BASIS OF RIGHT CHOICE

Our moral obligation to always act for the best good of all is the foundation of right moral action. Nothing happens in life except what someone chooses.

It's absolutely critical that we make right choices that bring about the greatest public and private good. This obligation extends to the deepest intentions of our will. We're obligated to choose holiness as the path to the greatest good and happiness.

### 9. THE BASIS OF JUSTICE

Judging based on intention is the right foundation for justice under moral government—and this is how civil governments operate too.

If you make a choice with good intentions but things don't turn out well, or if you make a choice with bad intentions and it happens to turn out okay, you should be held responsible for your *intention*, not just the results. The Bible values intention more than outward results (2 Cor. 8:12; Mk. 7:15-23; Jas. 1:13-15; 3:9-14).

All vice and virtue are considered as coming from the heart. Where the heart is right, all is considered right; where the heart is bad, all is considered bad (Mt. 7:15-21). Even sinners do some things outwardly that God requires, but if the heart isn't right—if the intention is selfish—the acts don't change the heart.

Virtue means being devoted to the same goal God is devoted to. Vice means being devoted to what Satan pursues—self-gratification contrary to moral law and the highest good of the universe.

### 10. THE EXTENT OF OBEDIENCE TO THE MORAL LAW

Since the foundation of moral obligation is the highest good of all, complete devotion to that goal must constitute obedience to moral law. You can't have divided loyalties here.

You can't choose the good of others and self-gratification at the same time. God can't tolerate half-heartedness in choice and service (Rev. 3:15-16; Mt. 22:36-40). He can't justify someone who continually falls short of full obedience to known duty.

If you're always coming up short of what you know you should do, there's not a single moment when you're not under the curse of a broken law (Gal. 3:10-14; Jas. 2:10; 4:17). God can't set aside the penalty until repentance, forgiveness, and full obedience are realized (Rom. 8:1-13; Gal. 5:16-26; Rom. 6:16-23; Jn. 8:34; 1 Jn. 3:8-10).

### 11. OF WHAT DOES DISOBEDIENCE TO THE MORAL LAW CONSIST?

Disobedience consists in choosing self-gratification as your life's goal instead of the greatest good of the universe. It means committing your will and dedicating your life to serving sin, Satan, and your senses rather than the moral law and intelligence.

It's like choosing to be governed by your impulses and passions instead of by reason and wisdom. Self-gratification is the root of all sin, and human selfishness is closely allied to Satan's own self-interest.

Your will is always free to resist desire and lust contrary to the law, but when it doesn't, sin happens. Your mind knows its obligation, so when you choose contrary to the law, it's not a choice made in ignorance—it's a free action that brings condemnation.

Selfishness is always unreasonable. It denies true humanity and rational nature. It shows contempt for God's law in your own reason.

No sinner chooses the way of reason and common sense. They rarely consult reason for their actions—they usually just obey their lusts and stay in stubborn rebellion against moral law and reason. They're lustful at heart whether they can act on all those lusts or not. As long as they remain that way, they're condemned before God and need regeneration to bring them to obedience.

### 12. THE BASIS OF DEGREES OF GUILT AND VIRTUE

Both reason and Scripture confirm that there are degrees of guilt and virtue. Some people are more guilty or more virtuous than others, and any individual may be more guilty or more virtuous at one time than another—whether saint or sinner.

All guilt and virtue depend on exercising moral obligation, and that depends on your light and knowledge concerning moral law. Think of it like this: a doctor who harms a patient through negligence is more culpable than someone with no medical training who makes the same mistake.

The sinner's guilt equals their knowledge of the value of the interests they reject. You're held more responsible today than people were in ancient times because you know more (Acts 17:30; Jas. 4:17). Your guilt or virtue matches the knowledge you have about a matter and your conformity to it (Rom. 2:12-16; Jn. 9:41; 15:22-24; Mt. 13:11-12; Lk. 12:47-48).

Selfishness is the rejection of all moral obligation, regardless of how much light you have. Sin lies in the intention, and intention can only be measured by the knowledge under which it's formed and maintained.

If a selfish person preaches the gospel, it's because it happens to be the most gratifying thing they could do for themselves. They might even preach for the good of others while having personal benefits as their chief motive. Take away personal gain—financially or socially—and they'd stop preaching. If that same person became a robber, it would be for the same reason: not to do evil for its own sake, but to gratify themselves.

If a sinner abstains from some evil for the sake of loved ones, their reputation, fear of judgment or disgrace, or any other reason, it's not because they're good at heart or because they think the act is wicked. It's merely for selfish reasons and personal gain.

This is true for every area of life where the heart isn't right. Natural people look for gain or advantage in everything. It's only when sinners devote themselves to the highest good of being that they stop being sinners by nature and practice and begin acting from an unselfish standpoint instead of living for self-gratification.

## II. The Bible Definition of "Sin" and "The Old Man"

The Hebrew and Greek words translated "transgression" mean "moral, religious and national rebellion," "revolt," "unrighteousness," "wickedness," "violation," and "breaking the law." Sin is twofold:

### 1. The Bible Definition of Outward Sin

> (1) "Sin is transgression of the law" (1 Jn. 3:4).

> (2) "Where no law is, there is no transgression" (Rom. 4:15).

> (3) "By the law is the knowledge of sin" (Rom. 3:20; 7:7).

> (4) "Sin is not imputed when there is no law" (Rom. 5:13).

> (5) "Without the law sin was dead" or dormant (Rom. 7:8).

> (6) "The thought of foolishness is sin" (Prov. 24:9; 2 Cor. 10:4-5).

> (7) "Every idle word that men shall speak" is sin (Mt. 12:36-37).

> (8) "All unrighteousness is sin" (1 Jn. 5:17).

> (9) "Whatsoever is not of faith is sin" (Rom. 14:23).

> (10) "If ye have respect of persons, ye commit sin" (Jas. 2:9).

> (11) "He that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin" (Jas. 4:17).

> (12) All "actions" contrary to the law are sin (1 Sam. 2:3; Jas. 1:13-15).

> (13) "Rebellion is as the sin of witchcraft" (1 Sam. 15:23).

> (14) Any "omission" of the law is sin (Mt. 23:23).

> (15) All "desires" and "lusts" contrary to the law, inwardly or outwardly manifested, are sin (Mk. 7:19-21; Rom. 1:29-32; Gal. 5:16-21; Eph. 2:3; Col. 3:5-10; Jas. 1:13-15).

These passages describe outward acts of something far more sinister and powerful than appears on the surface, as we'll see in the next point.

### 2. The Bible Definition of Inbred Sin

> (1) "The prince of the power of the air, the spirit that now worketh in the children of disobedience" (Eph. 2:1-3; 1 Jn. 3:8; Jn. 8:41-44). This single statement defines "the old man" and inbred sin. It's the devil himself working in disobedient people—in contrast with "the new man," which is the Spirit and nature of God working in obedient sons and daughters.

> (2) Other terms Scripture uses for "the old man" as the devil working in people include: "sin" (Jn. 1:29; 8:34; Rom. 6:4-22; 7:7-25; 8:2); "the body of sin" (Rom. 6:6); "darkness" (Acts 26:18); "the body of this death" (Rom. 7:24); "the old man" (Rom. 6:6; Eph. 4:22-24; Col. 3:9); "the lusts of the flesh" (Eph. 2:3); "the lust of the flesh" (1 Jn. 2:15-17; Gal. 5:16-21); "the lust of the eyes" (1 Jn. 2:15-17); "the pride of life" (1 Jn. 2:15-17); "all ungodliness and unrighteousness" (Rom. 1:18); "the world" (1 Jn. 2:15-17); "vile affections" (Rom. 1:26); "the lusts of the mind" (Eph. 2:3); "a reprobate mind" (Rom. 1:28); "the lusts of sin" (Rom. 6:11-12); "the motions of sins" (Rom. 7:5); "the law of sin and death" (Rom. 7:7-25; 8:2); "the carnal mind" (Rom. 8:1-13); "the works of the flesh" (Gal. 5:16-21, 24); "the flesh" (Rom. 8:1-13; Gal. 5:24); "the carnal man" (Rom. 7:14-25; 8:1-13); "the power of Satan" (Acts 26:18); "the god of this world" (2 Cor. 4:3-4); "principalities . . . powers . . . the rulers of darkness of this world . . . spiritual wickedness in high places" (Eph. 6:12); "the lusts of your father" the devil (Jn. 8:44); "sin that dwelleth in me" (Rom. 7:7-25); "the course of this world" (Eph. 2:2); and "the body of the sins of the flesh" (Col. 2:11).

These Scripture statements plainly tell us that sin is something more than "transgression of the law" or outward sin—it's something separate from the person themselves. Every one of these expressions proves there's a real law of sin and death, and behind this law, enforcing it, is a real power from a real nature and a real person who is stronger than we are.

It's the "old man," "the body of sin," and "the body of this death" that are to be destroyed—not your body, soul, and spirit (Rom. 6:1-22; 8:1-13; Gal. 5:24; Col. 2:11). When this "old man" is delivered from or annulled from operating in you, your body, soul, and spirit—as originally created—are still there. You're liberated from the devil for the first time in your life when this happens.

It's the "lusts," "desires," and "motions of sin" from the devil in your body, soul, and spirit that are destroyed, put to death, and "put off"—not any part of your created being. These "lusts" are plainly stated as being "of the devil" and "of the world" system, which will finally pass away and won't operate in people anymore (Jn. 8:44; Eph. 2:1-3; 1 Jn. 2:15-17; 3:8; Rom. 6:11-22).

Theologians call this aspect of sin by various names: inherent sin, sin principle, infection of nature, moral disease, contagious corruption, incentive to sin, depraved nature, sinful propensities, evil tendencies, manifold infirmity, the carnal nature, the flesh, the carnal mind, the presence and pollution of sin in the heart, the remains of sin, inbred sin, and many other things. These are theological terms expressing the same scriptural truth: sin is something real in you, separate from your body, soul, and spirit as originally created.

## III. Sin is Not Man's Body, Soul, or Spirit

The following points prove that sin is not only "transgression of the law," but that inbred sin is the very spirit and nature of the devil working in people through Satan's lusts. It's something real and living and entirely separate from our body, soul, and spirit as originally created by God.

### 1. Statements That Prove Sin Is Separate From Man

Phrases like "taketh away the sin of the world" (Jn. 1:29); "servant of sin" (Jn. 8:34); "under sin" (Rom. 3:9); "by one man sin entered the world" (Rom. 5:12); "sin abounded" (Rom. 5:20); "sin hath reigned" (Rom. 5:12); "dead to sin" (Rom. 6:1-12); "not serve sin" (Rom. 6:6); "freed from sin" (Rom. 6:7); "let not sin therefore reign" (Rom. 6:12); "sin shall not have dominion over you" (Rom. 6:14); "servants of sin" (Rom. 6:20); "free from sin" (Rom. 6:18, 22); "wages of sin" (Rom. 6:23); "sin revived" (Rom. 7:7-11); "sold under sin" (Rom. 7:14); "sin dwelleth in me" (Rom. 7:17, 20); "into captivity to the law of sin" (Rom. 7:23-25); "made me free from the law of sin and death" (Rom. 8:2); "condemned sin in the flesh" (Rom. 8:3); "deceitfulness of sin" (Heb. 3:13); "put off" and "put away sin" (Eph. 4:22; Heb. 9:26); "striving against sin" and "wrestle against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places" (Eph. 6:12; Heb. 12:4); "against the wiles of the devil" and "his devices" (Eph. 6:11; 2 Cor. 2:11); and "He that committeth sin is of the devil" (1 Jn. 3:8)—all these prove that "sin" is something more than a person outwardly transgressing the law.

All these statements imply that sin is a slave master and a tyrant over people, causing death by forcing transgression (Rom. 7:7-25; 8:2). Sin is a real person apart from us, or it couldn't reign, dominate, enslave, deceive, slay, be present with, control, dwell in, or dictate to us.

Sin is also a law. If sin is a law, there must be someone who made that law and enforces it. There could be no law unless someone made it, and it would be worthless unless it could be enforced according to its purpose. The devil made and enforces the law of sin and death through demons and spirit forces, and it can only be annulled in free moral agents by a greater law enforced by a greater power (Heb. 2:14, 15).

Free wills seek the annulment of this law by applying to the Court of Heaven according to the moral law (Rom. 8:1-13). Both laws conflict, as stated in Rom. 7:7-25, proving there are real persons and powers behind each law. This demonstrates that sin is a real spirit and nature in us, separate from our own body, soul, and spirit.

When you're "freed from sin," you're cut loose from the body of sin and don't need to be chained to it again—if you walk and live in the Spirit (Gal. 5:16-26; Rom. 8:1-13).

The expressions "the body of sin" and "the body of this death" probably allude to a horrible ancient punishment where a condemned criminal was chained to the corpse of the person they murdered and left to die in the loathsome company of that decaying body. It's similar to being handcuffed to a rotting corpse—that's what being bound to sin is like.

### 2. Being "Under Sin"

People are described as being "under sin" (Rom. 3:9; Gal. 3:22); as being "sold under sin" which made them "carnal" (Rom. 7:14); and as being "under the elements of the world" (Gal. 4:3, 9).

The Greek word for "under" is *hupo*, meaning "underneath, in place or condition," "an inferior position"—it implies a power higher than us which has brought us under sin's control.

Look at how the word is used elsewhere: "under authority" and "soldiers under me" (Mt. 8:9); "not under the law, but under grace" (Rom. 6:14-15); "bruise Satan under your feet" (Rom. 16:20); "I keep under my body" (1 Cor. 9:27); "under obedience" (1 Cor. 14:34); "put all enemies under his feet" (1 Cor. 15:24-28); "under tutors" (Gal. 4:2); and "under the mighty hand of God" (1 Pet. 5:6).

All these prove that a real power and real person are involved in executing these laws and accomplishments. If we're "under sin," then there must be a real person and power greater than us keeping us there and ruling over us.

The phrase "sold under sin" implies a slave master as well as a slave—and this is exactly what Paul experienced when he was helpless "under sin" (Rom. 7:7-25).

### 3. Knowledge of Sin Came Later

The phrase "by the law is the knowledge of sin" proves that sin wasn't in humanity originally—it wasn't created in us. Sin came later, entering the world through the devil's malice.

Sin is so closely connected to the devil that Scripture says, "He that committeth sin is of the devil" (1 Jn. 3:4-10; Jn. 8:44; Eph. 2:1-3). Sin, then, is something separate from both humanity and God's law.

### 4. Sin Has Its Own Lusts

Sin is "in the mortal body" but isn't the mortal body or any part of it—and this "sin" has its own lusts (Rom. 6:12, 16-17; 7:5; Jn. 8:44).

We're commanded not to yield to these lusts or to the spirit and nature to whom these lusts belong. Instead, we're to yield to God and to the desires of His Spirit and nature (Rom. 6:16-22).

This contrast proves that the sins and lusts in us are from the devil's spirit and nature, just as new desires in a born-again soul come from God's Spirit and nature (2 Cor. 5:17).

Both the devil and God have desires of their own, and we become partakers of them when we serve one or the other (Jn. 8:44; 1 Jn. 3:8; 2 Pet. 1:4). If the old lusts are from Satan and the new desires are from God, then sin is the devil's spirit and nature working in sons of disobedience, while righteousness is God's Spirit and nature working in sons of obedience.

### 5. Dead to Sin, Not Dead to Your Original Being

When believers are made "dead indeed unto sin" and "freed from sin" at the new birth, they don't become dead to or freed from their original body, soul, and spirit. Every original part of us was created sinless—sin couldn't be part of our own being (Rom. 5:12-21; 6:5-22; 8:1-13).

Death to and freedom from sin refer to being free from the person of the devil and his spirit that took control of humanity at the Fall.

### 6. Christ's Death Dealt With Sin, Not Human Nature

Christ "died unto sin once," to "take away sin" and to "put away sin"—but He didn't die to destroy any part of our bodily members, soul passions, or spirit faculties, since these were originally created sinless (Rom. 6:10; Jn. 1:29; Heb. 7:27; 9:12, 26).

The whole person becomes sinless when becoming a new creature in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17). If you stay one (and you can), you continue in a sinless life (Col. 2:6-7; 1 Jn. 2:1-2, 29; 3:8-10; 5:1-5, 18).

The Greek word for "put away" is *athetesis*, meaning "to annul" or "to cancel sin"—to cancel the devil's operation in a person. The word is translated "disannul" in Gal. 3:15 and Heb. 7:18, showing how and to what extent sin is "put away" in the believer.

The Greek word for "take away" is *airo*, meaning "to take out of the way," "do away with," "loose," "put away," or "remove." It means to remove sin from the believer by virtue of Christ taking it in His own body on the cross.

### 7. Romans 6:1–8:13: Five Illustrations

A study of Rom. 6:1–8:13 reveals that sin is the devil's spirit and nature working in sons of disobedience. The old relationship to the devil through obeying the law of sin and death, and the new relationship to God through obeying the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus, are illustrated through five main ideas:

> **(1) By union with Christ in His death, burial, resurrection, and new life:** "If we have been planted together in the likeness of his death, we shall also be in the likeness of his resurrection: Knowing this, that our old man was crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin. For he that is dead to sin is freed from sin . . . Knowing that Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more; death hath no more dominion over him. For in that he died, he died unto sin once: but in that he liveth, he liveth unto God" (Rom. 6:1-10).

> **(2) By reckoning the "old man" dead and the "new man" alive:** "Likewise reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through our Lord Jesus Christ. Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, that ye should obey it in the lusts thereof. Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin: but yield yourselves unto God, as those that are alive from the dead, and your members as instruments of righteousness unto God. For sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under law, but under grace" (Rom. 6:11-14).
>
> How could anyone reckon themselves dead to something that wasn't constantly present to deal with? When believers become dead to sin and alive to God, they no longer live after the flesh, in sin, under Satan's power and dominion. But they still have to "reckon" themselves dead to sin—proving that the "old man," the devil, is still present to deal with. He doesn't have to be inside you to be present.

> **(3) By the master and servant relationship (Rom. 6:15-23).** Paul illustrates freedom from sin and the new life of service to God through the imagery of a servant who can choose between two masters—sin or righteousness, Satan or God.

> Paul asks: "Shall we sin, because we are not under the law, but under grace?"—meaning, shall we yield again to the "old man" or the devil after being made free? The answer: "God forbid" (Rom. 6:15).

> **A. Acts of obedience indicate the master served:** "Know ye not, that to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are to whom ye obey; whether of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness?" (Rom. 6:16). This applies to saints as well as sinners. If a saint yields to sin and Satan, they become a sinner and servant of sin again. Whoever you yield to is your master—and you're dead to the one you're not serving.

> **B. Change in obedience indicates a change in masters:** "But God be thanked, that ye were the servants of sin, but ye have obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine which was delivered you. Being then made free from sin, ye become the servants of righteousness" (Rom. 6:17-18). When you change masters from Satan to God (or vice versa), your changed actions show it.

> **C. The master served indicates the nature of obedience:** "For as ye have yielded your members servants to uncleanness and to iniquity; even so now yield your members servants to righteousness unto holiness. For when ye were the servants of sin, ye were free from righteousness" (Rom. 6:19-20). Under the devil's control, there's ever-increasing iniquity and transformation toward Satan's likeness (Jn. 8:44). Under God's mastery, there's ever-increasing holiness and transformation into Christ's image (2 Cor. 3:18).

> **D. The nature of obedience indicates the wages received:** "What fruit had ye then in those things whereof ye are now ashamed? For the end of those things is death. But now being made free from sin, and become the servants of God, ye have your fruit unto holiness and the end everlasting life. For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord" (Rom. 6:21-23).

> **(4) By marriage (Rom. 7:1-6).** Paul illustrates freedom from sin and new life with God through marriage. He starts by stating that "the law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth," then continues: "The woman which hath an husband is bound by the law to her husband so long as he liveth; but if her husband be dead, she is loosed from the law of her husband" (Rom. 7:1-3).

> He applies this to believers: "Wherefore, my brethren, ye also are become dead to the law by the body of Christ; that ye should be married to another, even to him who is raised from the dead, that we should bring forth fruit unto God" (Rom. 7:4). Sin is like the husband—there's no legal freedom until death. Sin was crucified with Christ so we can be free and married to God (Rom. 6:6-12). When sin was alive in us, it produced death; now that it's been killed, we can be free and produce life (Rom. 7:5-6). We become free from sin at the new birth (1 Jn. 2:29; 3:8-10; 5:1-5, 18).

> **(5) By Paul's own experience (Rom. 7:7–8:13).** Paul shares his own story of being bound to the law of sin and death and his deliverance from it. The main points:

> **A.** By the law is the knowledge of sin (Rom. 7:7).

> **B.** Sin itself is what's sinful in me, working evil desires in me and refusing to let me obey God's law (Rom. 7:7-8).

> **C.** Sin rebels against the law and makes me break it, so the death penalty can be carried out against me (Rom. 7:9).

> **D.** The law was ordained to give life, but sin wouldn't let me obey it, cutting me off from that life and bringing the curse of the broken law (Rom. 7:10; Gal. 3:10).

> **E.** Sin used the commandment as an opportunity to demonstrate its authority over me, deceived me, and through it killed me (Rom. 7:11). Sin betrayed me by taking advantage of my ignorance and helplessness.

> **F.** I conclude that the law is holy, but I

lessons.title: How to Get Rid of Sin and Sickness
lessons.content:
# How to Get Rid of Sin and Sickness

In our last lesson, we took a thorough look at what the Bible teaches about sin. Now let's turn to something even more hopeful — how you can be set free from both sin and sickness. The points we'll cover show you clearly how to be rid of both.

## I. Recognition of Sin and Sickness

The first step to getting free from sin and sickness is recognizing that you're a sinner who stands guilty before God and deserving of eternal punishment. You experience sickness because of sin — not necessarily your own personal sin, but always because of the Fall and because we live in a broken, corrupted world.

Here's the good news: Christ died to save you from your sins and sicknesses and to deliver you from all the works of the devil. Once you understand that, you can go to God in faith — trusting in what Christ accomplished on the cross and coming in Jesus' name — just like you'd go to your earthly father to receive something he'd already promised and provided for you.

If you don't believe you're a fallen creature, you naturally won't see any need for redemption from the Fall. So a true understanding of both the Fall and redemption is essential before you can receive any deliverance. In Lesson Nine, Points IV, VII, and VIII, we made the doctrine of the fall of man clear. In Lesson Twenty-three, we defined sin and showed the awful guilt that hangs over every person.

If you're not convinced by now that you're a sinner who needs to be saved, something is seriously wrong. The thing to do is lay aside all rebellion and unbelief toward God and His Word. Acknowledge your guilt and be genuinely sorry for your sin (2 Cor. 7:9-11). You must do this before you can be saved.

In order to see God, you must be saved from sin (Mt. 1:21; Heb. 12:14). You must be born-again and become a new creature (Jn. 3:1-8; 2 Cor. 5:17). You must be made alive from death in trespasses and sins to a new life (Eph. 2:1-10; Col. 2:13).

You must cooperate with God to be cleansed from all sin (Isa. 1:16-18; Jn. 1:12; Eph. 4:20-25; 5:13-14; Col. 3:9-17). You must confess all sin, repent of being a sinner, and turn from Satan to God — from darkness to light — from sin to a life of holiness in order to be born-again (1 Jn. 1:9; Lk. 13:1-5; Acts 26:18).

You must have faith in Christ and in the atonement (Rom. 3:24-25). You must identify yourself with Christ in His death, burial, resurrection, and new life (Rom. 6:1-13). These are "musts" for everyone who wants to be saved. There are many other things you'll need to do after you've been saved, but before that happens, don't worry about those duties yet.

## II. Two Necessary Things to Do to Be Saved or Born-Again

There are only two things necessary for you to be saved from all sin — just two. Of course, this assumes you already recognize that you're a sinner and that Christ died to save you. With that understood, here's what must be done:

### 1. Confession of Sin or True Repentance

Some ultra-grace teachers claim they've discovered a "super-grace" that frees you from any responsibility in salvation — except maybe one single act of faith. They say once you believe one time, that's all that's ever needed. At that point, responsibility for your eternal salvation supposedly shifts from you to God, and if you're ever lost, it's God's fault.

They claim confession of sins isn't necessary for salvation; that people are incapable of repenting; that if you had to confess all your sins you'd never be saved; that prayer isn't necessary for salvation; that requiring repentance is an error in this age; that no Scripture after the cross requires confession as a condition of salvation; that salvation isn't conditioned on asking, seeking, or praying; that this was fine for Israel before the cross but isn't required now; that only Israel was commanded to seek the Lord, pray, and repent; and that all Gentiles need to do is believe and accept Christ.

The Bible doesn't teach any of this. Scripture teaches that people not only can repent but must repent to be saved. You must confess your sins before you can be forgiven. You must continue in faith after you're saved. You must walk in the light of God's Word to keep being cleansed from all sin. And there are many things you must continue doing if you want to please God and live as He demands.

The word "repent" actually appears more often in the New Testament than in the Old. There's no biblical authority for teaching that salvation before the cross was different from what it is now when it comes to seeking God, repenting, praying, and confessing sins. There's no authority for saying Jews and Gentiles are saved differently.

Scripture has always demanded — and always will — that all people be saved by repentance and faith in God. The Bible doesn't teach that repentance was only for Jews before the cross and not required since. That's a clear display of ignorance — willful ignorance, really — because no one who genuinely wants to teach what the Bible says could come away with these conclusions. There's no excuse for not knowing what Scripture says; it's plain to anyone who will investigate the truth.

True repentance includes a sense of personal guilt (Ps. 51:1-7; 1 Tim. 1:13), godly sorrow for sin (2 Cor. 7:9-11), an understanding of God's mercy through Christ (Isa. 53:4-6; Lk. 1:76-78; Titus 3:5-6), and a turning away from sin to be free from further guilt (2 Chron. 7:14; Acts 26:18; Jer. 7:3; 18:11; Ezek. 18:30; 33:10-20). Scripture states clearly that if people don't repent, they will perish — so repentance is absolutely necessary (Mt. 11:20-22; Lk. 13:1-5; Rom. 2:5).

In the New Testament, repentance is mentioned thirty-seven times in passages after the cross and only twenty-five times before. The word "repent" with its various forms appears sixty-four times in the New Testament and only forty-six times in the Old Testament. So this is just as much a New Testament teaching as an Old Testament one. Not a single passage says people shouldn't repent, pray, confess, or seek God since the cross. That idea is purely a lie of the devil — a doctrine created by ultra-grace teachers to prop up their false theories.

In Mt. 28:19-20, the apostles were commanded to make disciples of all nations and to teach people throughout this entire age to observe everything Christ had commanded them. This proves that all the passages on repentance, praying, confessing sins, and meeting the requirements of salvation that Christ commanded before the cross were to be taught to all nations after the cross and throughout this age.

**Before the Cross:** Christ and others commanded all people to "repent and believe the gospel" (Mk. 1:15; 6:12), to "bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance" (Mt. 3:8; Lk. 3:8), and warned "except ye repent, ye shall all likewise perish" (Lk. 13:3-5). Christ repeatedly said He came to call "sinners to repentance" (Mt. 9:13; Mk. 2:17; Lk. 5:32), and that there was more rejoicing in Heaven over one sinner who repented than over ninety-nine just persons who needed no repentance (Lk. 15:1-7).

**Since the Cross:** Christ again commanded the disciples "that repentance and remission of sins should be preached in his name among all nations, beginning at Jerusalem" (Lk. 24:46-48). At Pentecost, Peter commanded Jews and Gentile proselytes to "Repent, and be baptized every one of you [not just the Jews] in the name of Jesus Christ for [because of] the remission of sins, and ye shall receive the gift of the Holy Ghost. For [because] the promise is unto you [Jews and Gentiles of that generation], and to your children [Jews and Gentiles of the new generation], and to all that are afar off [Jews and Gentiles of all generations], even as many as the Lord our God shall call" (Acts 2:38-39).

In other sermons since the cross, Peter demanded that people repent (Acts 3:19; 5:31; 8:22). The entire group of apostles recognized that God required Gentiles to repent in order to be saved (Acts 11:18). Peter, many years later, wrote in his epistles that both Jews and Gentiles had to repent to be saved (2 Pet. 3:9).

Paul taught that both Jews and Gentiles had to repent to be saved (Acts 17:30-31; 20:21; 26:20; Rom. 2:4; 2 Cor. 7:8-10; 2 Tim. 2:25; Heb. 6:1, 6). Jesus commanded people to repent as late as 96 A.D. when the last book of the Bible was written (Rev. 2:5, 16, 21-22; 3:3, 19; 9:20-21; 16:9-11).

People in both Testaments were also commanded to confess their sins to be saved (Lev. 5:5; 16:21; 26:40; Prov. 28:13; Rom. 10:9-10; 14:11; 1 Jn. 1:9). People in both Testaments were saved by confessing sins (Ps. 32:5; Neh. 9:2-3; Mt. 2:6; Mk. 1:5; Acts 19:18).

The use of prayer in getting forgiveness is commanded (Lk. 11:4; Acts 8:22). Prayer brought salvation to people in both Testaments (2 Chron. 7:14; Ps. 51; Lk. 18:10-14; Acts 9:11; 22:16). People are promised salvation through prayer and by calling upon the name of the Lord (Acts 2:21; Rom. 10:9-14). We're also told to seek God in order to find Him (Lk. 12:31; 13:24; Acts 15:17; 17:27; Rom. 2:7; 3:11; Heb. 11:6; Mt. 6:33; 7:7-11; Gal. 2:17; Col. 3:1).

No one should try to get right with God while refusing to repent, confess, pray, seek, turn from sin, forsake all, and believe the gospel. When you truly turn to God, you do all these things and more. Imagine trying to accomplish all this through just one act of faith and nothing else! To teach that people don't have to do these things in this age — or that Jews must do them while Gentiles don't — is to demonstrate ignorance of the Bible.

God has the same standards for one person as for another. All people must meet the same conditions to be saved and to stay saved. "For there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek: for the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him. For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" (Rom. 10:9-13).

Before the law, Abraham was saved by faith and grace. During the law, David and others were saved the same way (Rom. 4:1-8). Since the law, people are saved in the same way — by faith and grace (Eph. 2:1-10).

Confession of sins and true repentance includes prayer, turning away from sin, forsaking sin, turning to God with godly sorrow, seeking God, asking God, believing God, and having faith in the atonement of Christ. All of this is necessary to be saved from sin.

### 2. Believe That God Has Forgiven You

The second thing you must do to be saved — after you know you're a sinner and have confessed your sins to God — is to believe that God has forgiven you.

"Without faith it is impossible to please him: for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him" (Heb. 11:6). "For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God: not of works, lest any man should boast" (Eph. 2:8-9). "For God so loved the world that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life" (Jn. 3:16).

God has promised to forgive if you confess. Regardless of your feelings, you must believe that you are forgiven (1 Jn. 1:9). It must be faith in the right thing — the blood of Jesus (Rom. 3:24-25).

Faith in water baptism, church membership, or religious rituals will never save your soul. It must be faith in the sacrifice of Christ and in Jesus as your personal Saviour. Nothing else will bring peace to your soul or give you any assurance of eternal life.

Think of it like this: a check is worthless unless it's drawn on a real account with actual funds. Your faith has to be "drawn on" the right account — the finished work of Christ — or it can't deliver what you need.

This faith must come from your heart, not just be mental agreement with a fact. It must be a faith of surrender to the whole will of God. Paul said, "That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved. For with the heart man believeth unto righteousness; and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation" (Rom. 10:9-13). This one passage states both necessary things you must do to be saved — confess sins (or repent) and believe the gospel.

When you believe from the heart, you give up everything for Christ and wholeheartedly serve God and righteousness. You renounce sin and everything that would hinder your new life of faith.

Jesus said, "If any man come to me, and hate not his father, and mother, and wife, and children, and brethren, and sisters, yea, and his own life also he cannot be my disciple . . . So likewise, whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple" (Lk. 14:26, 33).

The word "hate" here is a Greek idiom of preference. The idea is that unless you prefer Christ above everything else in life, you cannot be saved. This is the same as the first commandment — loving the Lord God with all your heart, all your soul, all your mind, and all your strength (Mt. 22:37).

## III. How to Get Healed

The same two things necessary to save your soul are necessary to heal your body. You must recognize the sickness and its source, then pray and ask God to heal, and then believe that it's done.

Faith is just as necessary for healing the body as for saving the soul. The law of faith is this: "According to your faith be it unto you" (Mt. 9:29). If you believe you're healed, you're healed. If you doubt or wonder whether you're healed, it won't be done. You cannot be healed without unwavering faith that you are healed.

James said, "And the prayer of faith shall save the sick, and the Lord shall raise him up; and if he have committed sins, they shall be forgiven him" (Jas. 5:14-16).

In other words, healing comes on the same basis as forgiveness of sins — the atonement (Mt. 8:17; 1 Pet. 2:24). Jesus had power on Earth to do both, and He did heal when prayer and faith were present on the part of people (Mt. 9:1-7).

Since Christ has been given "All power in heaven and in earth," He certainly has as much power now as when He walked the Earth. If He has such power today, He will freely use it to deliver people from the works of the devil. Paul said, "Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and today, and for ever" (Heb. 13:8).

The only thing standing between any person's healing and God is that person's own failure to connect with God through Christ. If that's all that stands in the way, it seems like anyone in need would put forth the greatest possible effort to be healed.

God promises it. Jesus died for it. The Holy Spirit is here to enable you to receive it. Faith in the atonement will bring it. That's the truth in a nutshell.

If you want healing, first go to the Bible to see if these things are true. If they are — and you find them plainly written in Scripture — then deal with yourself and bring yourself to the terms plainly stated in the promises. There will be no question about the results.

By this method, you can get rid of both sin and sickness and stay free from them. The same means you use to get rid of these enemies will keep you free from them. Prayer, faith, and complete daily surrender of your life to God will bring the results you desire.

Every person should thank God daily for forgiveness and healing, and God will honor this daily faith and keep you saved and healed.

## IV. Identification With Christ

The chief secret of all redemptive benefits is your identification with Christ in His work for fallen humanity. By identification with Christ, we mean your union with Him in all phases of His redemptive work. Everyone who believes in Christ must become identified with Him in every phase of His life, death, burial, resurrection, and manifestation.

Believers are spoken of as being identified with Christ in:

> 1. His sufferings (Rom. 8:17; 1 Pet. 4:1-2; Jn. 15:20; 16:2-3; Mt. 10:25).

> 2. His work (Mk. 16:20; Jn. 14:12-15; Heb. 2:3-4; Acts 1:1-2; Mt. 28:19-20).

> 3. His crucifixion (Rom. 6:6; 8:12-13; Gal. 2:20; Col. 3:5-17).

> 4. His death (Rom. 6:3-11; 8:17; 1 Pet. 4:1-2; Col. 3:3).

> 5. His burial (Rom. 6:4; Col. 2:12).

> 6. His resurrection (Rom. 6:5; Col. 2:12; 3:1; Eph. 2:5-6).

> 7. His exaltation and glory (Rom. 8:17; Col. 3:4; Eph. 2:5-6).

> 8. His security (Col. 3:1-4; 1 Thess. 4:16-17).

Christ identified Himself with humanity and became sin for us so that we might become the righteousness of God in Christ (2 Cor. 5:21). He became our substitute and died in our place. He died to make us live, to make us well and healthy, to make us strong and victorious over all our enemies, to make us happy and prosperous, and to take us to Heaven to live forever with Himself and God.

It's similar to how a power of attorney works: when someone grants you power of attorney, you have the legal right to act on their behalf. Christ has granted us His authority — when we're united with Him, we share in everything He accomplished.

Union with Christ means we are crucified to the world and the world to us (Gal. 2:20; 6:14). There's nothing in us the world wants, and nothing in the world we desire. We are free from sin and Satan, from sickness and failure. We are sons of God with power, capable of representing God and exercising the full power of attorney according to the gospel.

Christ didn't just become sin for us — He became sick for us so that we might be made well. The literal rendering of the Hebrew in Isa. 53:3-5 reads: "He was despised, and forsaken of men; a man of pains [or anguish], and acquainted with sickness: and as one from whom men hide their face he was despised [or scorned]; and we esteemed [or regarded] him not. Surely he hath borne our sicknesses, and carried our pains; yet we did esteem him violently beaten, slain of God, and degraded. But he was slain for our crimes, he was beat to pieces [or maimed] for our guilt; the corrections of our peace was upon him; and with his wounds [or bruises] we are healed."

In Isa. 53:10, the Hebrew text reads: "Yet it pleased Jehovah to beat him to pieces; he hath made him sick: when thou shalt make his soul an offering for sin."

These are hard statements to comprehend — it's difficult to understand how any human being could have suffered this much — but it's true. Isaiah further speaks of Christ as being so marred that He was hardly recognizable as a man (Isa. 52:14). The literal translation of this verse is: "Just as many were dumbfounded at Him, for deformed was His appearance so as not to be human, or His figure no more resembled man."

We have no true conception of how He suffered, taking in His own body the beatings and insults of the mob and the sins and sicknesses of the whole world. Who can be so hard-hearted and ungrateful for His sufferings that they can go on in sin and rebellion against Him?

It shocks us to know that all these sufferings were heaped upon Him and that it pleased God to cause Him such agonies so that all creation could be redeemed. He was so sinful and sick during this period, so forsaken by God who detested the sins and sicknesses of the whole world concentrated in His body, that He cried, "My God, my God, why hast thou forsaken me?"

If every seeking sinner could see that they died with Christ on the cross — that their sins and sicknesses were borne away at that time — it would be easy to accept both forgiveness and healing from God. Such teaching isn't even a known doctrine to the average church member, much less a reality to their soul. Is it any wonder, then, that so few attain the full benefits for which Christ died?

Christ not only bore sins, sicknesses, and pains, but they were laid upon Him so He could do away with them. His whole person — body, soul, and spirit — was involved in this sacrifice and suffered this agony. Every person was nailed to the cross with Christ, and every sin and sickness and pain that any person ever had or ever will have was put upon Him.

When we as believers recognize this fact, believe it, and identify ourselves with Christ, we will receive the benefits made possible by this sacrifice. If He bore our sins, we're no longer required to have them or be bound by them. If He bore our sicknesses, we no longer must suffer them. Satan has no legal right to put any sin or sickness upon us, and we have redemptive rights to reject such works of the devil in our lives.

The Scriptures are clear that neither sin nor sickness shall have dominion over those who are delivered from sin and filled with God's divine life. Since Satan has been defeated, he cannot put sickness upon us unless we give consent and fail to resist him in faith.

We are new creations and the Spirit of God dwells in us. If this is true, and if we believe it, the Spirit will quicken our mortal bodies because He dwells in us (Rom. 8:9-13). If Christ put away sin and sickness, we don't have to tolerate them in our lives.

With all of our sins and sicknesses, we were nailed to the cross. If we cease all struggle, exercise true faith, and reckon that we have already died with Christ, that we are buried with Him, that we were resurrected with Him to walk in newness of life, and that we are raised together with Him and are now sitting together with Him in heavenly places — we will know in reality the power of His life in us.

We were freed from sin when He was freed from the sins He atoned for on the cross. We were healed with Him when He was healed after taking our sicknesses on the cross. As we identify ourselves with Him in His new freedom from sin and sickness, we are made free from them.

Let's all take our place in Him and identify ourselves with Him in His freedom from both sin and sickness, and fully realize the benefits of Calvary.

If we have to suffer the sins, pains, and sicknesses that Christ bore in our place, His substitutionary work for us counts for nothing. Through the death of Christ we are made alive. It was our sin that caused Him to die. When death slew Him, it destroyed itself. When sin conquered Him, it was conquered by Him. When disease conquered Him, it was conquered by Him. When He put away our sins, He also put away our diseases. By His stripes we were healed.

We partake of His righteousness and healing when we accept His work for us. Just as our identification or union with sin, sickness, and Satan brought defeat, so our union with Christ brings victory over these things.

We were crucified with Christ, and if we believe, we live with Him by resurrected power. He is our perfect Saviour, Healer, Provider, and everything we need in this life and in the one to come.

Just as Christ conquered sin, sickness, and Satan, we can conquer them in Him through proper identification with Him. His victory was our victory. He was victor in our place, and we are victors in Him. We were there when the devil was defeated. We were identified with Him when He rose from the dead as master over all Hell. We are masters of these same powers through Christ.

We are the ones who must carry on the work of God that Jesus started. There can be no defeat or failure. We are free now if we will only accept freedom in Christ. We have salvation and healing now if we will take it.

We must refuse to have either sin or sickness. We must take our rightful place in Christ and conquer in His name. We have the power of attorney — all we need to do is use it to get what we want and what is rightfully ours in Christ.

We have the power of God as sons of God. We have God's Word abiding in us. We have the name of Jesus. We have the Holy Spirit. We have faith, knowledge, and wisdom. We have the righteousness of God and the experience of a true new creature. We have everything we need to get what we want — so let's cooperate with God and the means of grace, and we cannot fail to succeed in what we undertake in Christ.

---

## Questions on Lesson Twenty-four

- What is the first thing to do to get rid of sin and sickness? Why?

- Name a few "musts" of the gospel if one wants its benefits.

- What are the two necessary things to do to get saved and healed?

- Name the things that ultra-grace people claim are not necessary to be saved?

- Can these claims of ultra-grace people be proven by the Bible? What does the Bible teach on these points?

- Is the doctrine of repentance only an Old Testament one? Prove.

- Is there any authority in Scripture for the doctrine that men were required to get salvation in one way before the cross and another way after the cross?

- Is there any authority for the doctrine that Jews get saved differently from Gentiles?

- What does true repentance include? Are all men required to repent to get saved?

- Is there any Scripture that says men are not to repent, pray, confess sins, or seek God in getting saved?

- What were the apostles commanded to teach all nations? Does this include repentance, confession of sins, praying, and seeking God to be saved? Prove.

- What was required of men before the cross in order to get saved?

- What is required of men since the cross in order to get saved?

- Prove that both Jews and Gentiles have to do the same things to be saved?

- Prove from both Testaments that men have to repent, confess sins, pray, seek God, and meet other conditions to be saved.

- Are men saved by feelings or by faith?

- Will faith in anything save the soul? Why?

- What does true heart repentance and faith include?

- Explain the word "hate" in Lk. 14:26.

- What two things are necessary in order to be healed?

- What is the law of faith?

- On what basis do men get healing from God?

- What stands between man's healing and God?

- What should one do who wants healing for the body?

- Define identification with Christ.

- In what aspects are men to identify themselves with Christ?

- Prove from the Bible that Christ not only became sin but also became sick for us.

- How does this give us a basis of claiming healing for the body?

- Should sin or sickness have dominion over saved people? Why?

- If we suffer the things for which Christ died to take from us, does His work prove a failure? Where is the failure? How can we correct this failure?

lessons.title: The Truth About the Holy Spirit
lessons.content:
## The Truth About the Holy Spirit

The Holy Spirit is pictured in Scripture as God — a real person who is separate and distinct from both the Father and the Son. He's an individual member of the Godhead, and He has His own body, soul, and spirit, just as the Father and the Son do.

## I. The Deity of the Holy Spirit

### 1. He Is Spoken of as God

Let's start with something that might surprise you: the Bible calls the Holy Spirit "God" directly. When Ananias lied about the money he received for his property, Peter said, "Why hath Satan filled thine heart to lie to the Holy Ghost . . . thou hast not lied unto men, but unto God" (Acts 5:3-4). Did you catch that? Lying to the Holy Spirit equals lying to God.

Paul drives this home when he writes, "Know ye not that ye are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you? If any man defile the temple of God, him shall God destroy; for the temple of God is holy, which temple ye are" (1 Cor. 3:16-17; 6:19-20). And in 1 Cor. 12:1-11, Paul mentions "the same Spirit . . . the same Lord . . . the same God which worketh all in all" — putting the Spirit right alongside the Father and Son.

### 2. Divine Names and Titles Are Ascribed to Him

The names and titles given to the Holy Spirit throughout Scripture prove His deity and His oneness with the Father and the Son. Think of it like a king's titles — each one reveals something about his authority and identity. Here's what Scripture calls the Spirit:

> 1. He is called Holy Ghost and Holy Spirit about 97 times.

> 2. The Spirit of God (1 Cor. 3:16; Gen. 1:2; 1 Sam. 19:20-23; Mt. 3:16).

> 3. The Spirit of the Lord (Judg. 3:10; Isa. 11:2; 6:1; Lk. 4:16-21).

> 4. The Spirit of the Living God (2 Cor. 3:3).

> 5. The Spirit of Christ (Rom. 8:9; 1 Pet. 1:11).

> 6. The Spirit of His Son (Gal. 4:6).

> 7. The Spirit of Jesus Christ (Phil. 1:19; Acts 16:7).

> 8. The Spirit of Burning (Isa. 4:4).

> 9. The Spirit of Holiness (Rom. 1:4).

> 10. The Holy Spirit of Promise (Eph. 1:13; Acts 2:33).

> 11. The Spirit of Truth (Jn. 14:17; 15:26; 16:13; 1 Jn. 4:6).

> 12. The Spirit of Life (Rom. 8:2; Rev. 11:11).

> 13. The Spirit of Wisdom and Understanding (Isa. 11:1-2).

> 14. The Spirit of Counsel and Might (Isa. 11:1-2).

> 15. The Spirit of Knowledge and of the Fear of the Lord (Isa. 11:1-2).

> 16. The Spirit of Grace and of Supplications (Zech. 12:10; Heb. 10:29).

> 17. The Spirit of Glory and of God (1 Pet. 4:14).

> 18. The Eternal Spirit (Heb. 9:14).

> 19. The Comforter (Jn. 14:26; 15:26).

> 20. The Spirit of Your Father (Mt. 10:20).

> 21. The Spirit of Prophecy (Rev. 19:10).

### 3. Divine Attributes Are Ascribed to the Holy Spirit

The Holy Spirit possesses every attribute that belongs to God alone. He has "Eternity and Self-existence" (Heb. 9:14), "Omnipresence" (Ps. 139:7-10), "Omnipotence" (Lk. 1:35; Acts 10:38), and "Omniscience" (1 Cor. 2:10-11; Jn. 14:26; 16:12-13).

He's involved in "Creation" (Job 33:4; Ps. 104:30) and possesses "Holiness" (Rom. 1:4). The fruit He produces — "Love, Joy, Peace, Longsuffering, Gentleness, Goodness, Faith, Meekness, and Self-control" (Gal. 5:22-23) — reveals His character.

He offers "Fellowship" (Phil. 2:1), "Glory" (1 Pet. 4:14; 2 Cor. 3:8-18), and "Life" (Rom. 8:1-13). He possesses "Wisdom and Knowledge" (Isa. 11:2; 1 Cor. 12:8), "Immutability" (Heb. 9:14), "Invisibility" (Jn. 3:8; Col. 1:16), and "Infinity" (Rom. 1:4; Jn. 14:26; Eph. 4:30).

His nature includes "Grace" (Heb. 10:29), "Truth" (Jn. 14:17, 26; 15:26; 16:13), "Justice" (Isa. 11:2-5; 4:4), "Will" (1 Cor. 12:11; Rom. 8:27), "Mind" (Rom. 8:27; 1 Cor. 2:10-11; 12:11), and "Intelligence" (1 Cor. 2:10-11; 12:11; Rom. 8:27).

He displays "Fear, Might, and Judgment" (Isa. 4:4; 11:2; 1 Cor. 12:8-11; Jn. 16:7-15), "Power" (1 Cor. 12:8-11; 2 Tim. 1:7), and "Decision" (1 Cor. 12:9-11; Acts 13:2). He shares "Unity" with the Father and Son (Mt. 28:19; 2 Cor. 13:14; 1 Jn. 5:7-8) and exercises "Authority" (Mt. 12:28; Acts 10:38; 20:28).

He has "Hearing and Speech" (Jn. 15:26; 16:13-15; Acts 2:4; 10:44-48; 19:1-7; 1 Cor. 12:1–14:40; Isa. 28:11), "Unselfishness" (Jn. 16:13-15), and "Self-action" (Gen. 1:2; Jn. 14:16-17, 26).

### 4. Divine Works Are Ascribed to the Holy Spirit

What does the Holy Spirit actually do? The list is remarkable. He "creates" (Ps. 104:30), "heals" (Rom. 9:11), "gives life" (Rom. 8:2), and "casts out devils" (Mt. 12:28). He "convicts of sin" (Jn. 16:7-11), "redeems" (Jn. 3:3-5; Titus 3:5-7), and "resurrects" (Rom. 8:9-11).

He "inspires Scripture" (2 Tim. 3:15-17; 2 Pet. 1:21), "distributes gifts" (1 Cor. 12:4-11), and "forms the body of Christ" (1 Cor. 12:13). He "endues with power" (Judg. 11:29; 13:25; 14:6), "changes lives" (1 Sam. 10:6-9; Jn. 3:3-5; 1 Cor. 6:11), and "directs gospel work" (Isa. 48:16; 61:1-2; Acts 11:12; 16:7).

The Spirit "frees from sin" (Rom. 8:2), "bears witness" (Jn. 15:26; Rom. 8:14-16), and "searches the deep things of God" (1 Cor. 2:10-12). He "brings things to remembrance and teaches and guides into all truth" (Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15) and "reveals secrets" (1 Cor. 2:10).

He "dwells in believers" (Rom. 8:9; 1 Cor. 3:16-17), "gives victory over sin" (Rom. 8:1-13), "guides in daily problems" (Rom. 8:14-16), and "adopts into God's family" (Rom. 8:14-16). He "helps in prayer" (Rom. 8:26) and "washes from sin, sanctifies, justifies" (1 Cor. 6:11).

He "imparts and causes supernatural powers to be manifested through believers" (1 Cor. 12:1–14:40) and "imparts love, joy, peace, longsuffering, goodness, meekness, faith, and self-control through believers" (Gal. 5:22-23). He "seals men to divine ownership" (Eph. 1:13; 4:30), "gives access to God" (Eph. 2:18), and "imparts revelations" (Eph. 3:1-11).

He "fills believers" (Lk. 1:15; Acts 4:31; Eph. 5:18), "causes obedience to truth" (1 Pet. 1:22), and "speaks of Christ and glorifies Him" (Jn. 16:13-15). He "confirms the gospel" (Heb. 2:3-4) and "carries on the work of God in the world" (Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15; Acts 20:28; etc.).

### 5. The Holy Spirit Is Associated with the Father and the Son as an Equal Member of Deity

Scripture consistently places the Holy Spirit alongside the Father and the Son as a co-equal member of the Godhead (Mt. 28:19; 2 Cor. 13:14; Eph. 4:1-6; 1 Cor. 12:1-11; 1 Jn. 5:7-8).

### 6. Divine Offices Are Ascribed to the Holy Spirit

The Spirit holds incredible offices within God's plan. He is the "Author of the New Birth" and the "Direct Executive of God" in all phases of creation and redemption (Gen. 1:2; Jn. 3:3-5; 1 Cor. 6:11; Titus 3:5-7). He's the "Inspirer of the Holy Scriptures" (2 Tim. 3:15-17; 2 Pet. 1:21) and the "General Overseer of the churches" (Acts 20:28).

He serves as the "Bestower of Spiritual Gifts" (1 Cor. 12) and the "Comforter," "Helper" and "Guide" of all saints (Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Rom. 8:26). He is the "Convicter of Sin" (Jn. 16:7-15) and the "Power of God in the Earth" (Acts 10:38; Mt. 12:28; Acts 1:8).

He's the "Great Teacher of Men" (Isa. 11:1-2; 61:1-2; Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:25-26; 16:7-15; 1 Cor. 2:10-12), the "Preserver of All Things" (Ps. 104:29-30), and the "Chief Witness for God on Earth" (Jn. 15:26; 16:7-15; Acts 5:32; Rom. 8:14-16). He is also the "Healer of Saints" (Rom. 8:11) and the "Character Builder" (2 Cor. 3:18; 2 Pet. 1:5-10).

### 7. The Divine Character and Personal Traits of the Holy Spirit Prove Him to Be Divine

As we'll see in the next section on His personality, the Spirit's character and traits demonstrate His divine nature.

## II. The Personality of the Holy Spirit

It's incredibly important that you believe not only in the divinity of the Holy Spirit but also in His personality. Only when you grasp these truths can you give Him the honor, worship, adoration, and personal respect that you give to God.

Here's what you need to understand: the Holy Spirit is not a mere power for you to grab hold of and use. He is a person — infinitely wise, holy, just, and gracious — who seeks to get hold of you and use you! You need to become acquainted with Him as a person, not merely as an influence or power derived from a person.

Think of it like this: you wouldn't try to "use" your best friend like a tool. You'd build a relationship with them. That's how we should approach the Holy Spirit.

The following points prove that He is a real person:

> 1. Personal names are given to Him, as proven in Point I, 2, above.

> 2. Personal pronouns are used of Him (Jn. 14:16-26; 15:26; 16:7-15).

> 3. Personal attributes are ascribed to Him, as proven in Point I, 3, above.

> 4. Personal works are ascribed to Him, as proven in Point I, 4, above.

> 5. Personal references are made concerning Him, as proven in Point I, 5, above.

> 6. Personal treatment is ascribed to Him.

He can be "resisted" (Acts 7:51), "tempted" (Acts 5:9), "grieved" (Eph. 4:30), "lied to" (Acts 5:3-4), "blasphemed" (Mt. 12:31-32), "insulted" (Mt. 12:31-32; Heb. 6:4-6; 10:26-29), "quenched" (1 Thess. 5:19), "vexed" (Ps. 78:40; Isa. 63:10), "quieted" (Zech 6:8), and "fellowshipped" (Phil. 2:1). He can be mistreated or obeyed like any other person.

In the Holy Spirit's relationship to people, He is spoken of as "searching hearts," "regenerating," "sanctifying," "helping," "convicting," "teaching," "quickening," "guiding," "witnessing," "interceding," "revealing," "working," "hearing," "speaking," "communing," "appointing," "commanding," "counseling," "comforting," "inspiring," "assuring," and "calling." He acts in many ways as a real person, as seen in Point I above.

As defined in Lesson Four, Point I, 5, a person is anyone who can think, feel, and act — anyone capable of self-consciousness and self-determination, any individual having legal rights and duties, a rational being with bodily presence, soul passions, and spirit faculties.

If this is not true of the Holy Spirit, given everything we've seen in the facts above, then nothing in Scripture is clear. How could the Holy Spirit do all the things spoken of Him without being a person? How could a mere impersonal force be spoken of as God, have many divine personal names, titles, offices, acts, and attributes, receive personal treatment, and be spoken of with personal pronouns? We must conclude that the Holy Spirit is a real person if we want to harmonize all Scripture.

## III. The Holy Spirit Is Not the Father or the Son

### 1. He Has Been Seen as a Separate Person

The Spirit has been seen with the natural eyes as a separate person from the Father and the Son (Mt. 3:16-17; Lk. 3:21-22; Jn. 1:31-34; Rev. 4:5; 5:6).

### 2. The Vision in Revelation Shows Three Distinct Persons

In Rev. 1:4-6; 3:1; 4:5; 5:6, the Spirit is symbolized by seven lamps of fire, seven eyes, and seven horns "before the throne" upon which God the Father is sitting, and "upon the Lamb," which symbolizes Jesus Christ.

The Spirit could not be the person of God the Father sitting on the throne, or the Lamb who comes to the Father and takes a seven-sealed book out of His right hand. So we have three persons, each distinguished from the others: the Father on the throne, the Son symbolized by the Lamb before the throne, and the Spirit symbolized by seven lamps also before the throne.

It's similar to watching three different actors on the same stage at the same time — you'd never confuse them as the same person. The fact that Christ and the Spirit are separate symbols seen at the same time and at the same place, both before God the Father on the throne, proves they are two separate and distinct persons from each other and from the Father.

A symbol stands for or represents something else. Since the Father is seen on the throne and Christ and the Spirit are symbolized by two separate beings before the throne, there must have been three separate and distinct persons involved in the scenes John saw when he arrived in Heaven (Rev. 4:1).

### 3. Jesus Called Him "Another Comforter"

In Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15, the Holy Spirit is spoken of as "another Comforter" (Helper), who could not be sent until Christ had gone back to Heaven and was glorified (Jn. 7:37-39; Acts 2:33-36). He would then be sent as a separate person from the Father and the Son.

The Greek word "allos," like the English "another," means "another of the same kind, but different from," "not the same one," "distinct from another," and "one more." This is proven everywhere the word is used (Mt. 2:12; 8:9; 10:23; 13:24, 31, 33; 21:33; 26:7). Otherwise the passages wouldn't make sense.

Common sense tells us that when the word "another" is used, it means "two or more." It's used of the Father as being another from the Son (Jn. 5:32). If the Spirit is another from the Son who promised Him, and another from the Father who sent Him, and if the Father was another from the Son, there must be three distinct persons in the Godhead — and all three can be called "God." All three persons are distinguished from each other by the word "another" in Jn. 5:32; 14:16.

### 4. They Have Separate Names

A clear distinction is made between "the name of the Father" and "of the Son" and "of the Holy Ghost" (Mt. 28:19). All three have separate and personal names by which they are known, as is clear from this passage and many others.

### 5. Jesus Prayed for Another Comforter to Come

A clear distinction is made between the Son who prays, the Father to whom He prays, and "another Comforter" for whom He prays. This Comforter was given in answer to prayer and came from the Father and the Son as our "Helper" (Jn. 14:16).

### 6. Jesus Left, Then the Spirit Came

A clear distinction is made between Jesus who went away as one distinct person (Acts 1:11) and the Holy Ghost who came to take His place as "another" distinct person (Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15).

### 7. Three Distinct Locations

A clear distinction is made between the Son who is exalted at the right hand of the Father, the Father who is on the left hand of the Son, and the Holy Ghost who is sent from the Father and the Son to take the place of Jesus among people (Acts 2:33-36; 7:56-59; Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15; Rom. 8:34; Eph. 1:20; Col. 3:1; Heb. 1:3; 8:1; 12:2).

### 8. Different Timing of Their Giving

A clear distinction is made between the Son who was already given (Lk. 1:35; Jn. 3:16) and the Holy Ghost who was not yet given before Jesus was glorified (Jn. 7:37-39; Acts 2:33-36; 5:31).

### 9. The Spirit and the Child Were Distinct at Jesus' Conception

A clear distinction is made between the Holy Ghost that came upon Mary and the child that was born of Mary by the Holy Ghost (Mt. 1:18-25; Lk. 1:32-35).

### 10. Blasphemy Against Them Has Different Consequences

A clear distinction is made between the Son who can be blasphemed with forgiveness possible, and the Holy Ghost who cannot be blasphemed with forgiveness (Mt. 12:31-32; Mk. 3:29-30; Lk. 12:10). If the Son and Holy Ghost were not two distinct persons, they could not be blasphemed with different results.

A third person, the Father, must be understood in such forgiveness, for it is the Father who forgives people as the head of the Godhead (1 Cor. 11:3; Mt. 6:9-15; 18:35; Eph. 4:32).

### 11. Jesus Was in Mary's Womb; the Spirit Filled Others

A clear distinction is made between Jesus who was bodily in Mary's womb and the Holy Ghost who was not bodily in Mary's womb, and who filled others (Lk. 1:15, 39-41, 67-79).

### 12. At Pentecost, Jesus Was in Heaven While the Spirit Filled People on Earth

A clear distinction is made between Jesus outside of the womb of Mary and the Holy Ghost who filled and baptized Mary thirty-three years later at Pentecost (Acts 1:14; 2:1-4).

The same distinction is made in the case of disciples at Pentecost: The Holy Ghost came and filled and baptized them while the Son was at that time "exalted at the right hand of the Father" in Heaven (Acts 2:1-4, 33-36). The same distinction is clear with Stephen (Acts 7:56-59), Paul (Acts 9:17), and others who were filled and baptized in the Holy Ghost when Jesus was in Heaven at the right hand of God (Rom. 8:34; Eph. 1:20; Heb. 1:3).

### 13. The Samaritans Had Received Jesus but Not Yet the Spirit

A clear distinction is made between Jesus whom the Samaritans had received and the Holy Ghost whom they had not yet received (Acts 8:5-25).

### 14. Jesus Was Once Powerless Until the Spirit Anointed Him

A clear distinction is made between Jesus who was once powerless to do miracles and the Holy Ghost who anointed Him at thirty years of age to do miracles (Jn. 2:11; Acts 10:38; Isa. 11:1-2; 42:1-5; 61:1-2; Lk. 4:18-21; Mt. 3:16-17).

### 15. Three Distinct Roles in the Anointing

A clear distinction is also made between the Son who was "anointed," the Father who sent the Spirit to anoint Him, and the Holy Ghost Himself who did the anointing (Mt. 3:16-17; Acts 10:38; Isa. 11:1-2; 42:1-5; 61:1-2; Lk. 4:18-21).

### 16. The Spirit Glorifies Christ, Not Himself

The Holy Ghost is distinguished from Christ by the fact that Jesus said, "He shall not speak of himself," but "He shall glorify me" (Jn. 16:13-15).

## IV. The Holy Ghost Has a Personal Spirit-Body

As we've seen in Lesson Four, God has a personal body, soul, and spirit like humans do. We've established that there are three distinct persons in the Godhead, all called "God." If God has a body and there are three distinct persons in the Godhead, then each person naturally has a personal body, soul, and spirit, just as each human being does.

Even if Scripture said nothing more about them having bodies, our intelligence and reason would convince us they do. No person can exist without a body or a shape.

Picture it this way: even demons, who are classed as disembodied spirits, have shapes and forms. The inner person of each human being has a shape and form that is tangible and real. It can wear clothes, eat, feel, know, and do everything out of the body that it could do while in the human body, as we proved in Lesson Four, Point II, 7.

Disembodied spirits all had outer forms or bodies at one time when they were first created. Paul argues in 1 Cor. 15:35-50 that all things in creation have bodies: bodies for human beings, fish, birds, beasts, sun, moon, and stars; heavenly bodies and earthly bodies; natural bodies and spiritual bodies. No person was ever without a body in a normal creative state.

Should we believe that God alone of all beings in the universe is the only real person without a body or shape? Should we believe He is a disembodied Spirit? Should we believe He is not as real as other beings? There's no scriptural basis for such ideas, so we should forget them.

If the fact is revealed that there are three separate distinct beings in the Godhead, this would be sufficient to conclude that each of them has separate bodies, souls, and spirits, like all other separate and distinct beings. Even disembodied spirits are separate and distinct from each other and can be numbered like all other beings.

Should we conclude that only one member of the Godhead has a body, soul, and spirit, and the other two persons are bodiless without souls and spirits? That would mean there's only one person. But since there are three persons entirely separate and distinct from each other, it's only reasonable that each of them is the same in substance and nature, and that they all have had from eternity the same kind of spirit-bodies, soul passions, and spirit-faculties. Scripture says nothing to the contrary.

In fact, two and three persons have been seen with separate bodies at the same time and at the same place by people.

Daniel saw two of them with separate bodies at the same time and place (Dan. 7:9-14). Stephen saw two of them at the same place (Acts 7:56-59). Others saw different members of the Godhead at different times and places, and every time any one of them was seen, He appeared in a real body. See Lesson Four, Point II, 3, 7, 8, and 9 for proof that God has a body, soul, and spirit! In Lesson Twenty-seven we'll study many plain statements of three separate and distinct persons in the Godhead.

If these facts are true, then it's only logical and scriptural to understand each of the three persons in the Godhead as having a personal spirit-body, soul, and spirit like all other persons in existence. If they are separate and distinct persons, then each one would have to have His own personality, spirit-body, soul, spirit, and His own individuality in every sense required of any other person in existence.

If we spoke of three persons among angels or people and described the body, soul, and spirit of only one of them, it would be clearly understood that the other two were similar to the one described. All persons of like nature, powers, attributes, and works are naturally the same regardless of how many exist. The members of the Godhead are exactly the same in every sense and have been from all eternity, so if one of them had a body by nature, then all of them had spirit-bodies exactly the same — until one of them took a human body to redeem us.

To be more specific: all angels have like spirit-bodies, souls, and spirits; all people have the same kind of bodies, souls, and spirits; all animals of the same species have the same likeness; and all demons are similar. Every person or thing in existence is similar to all other persons and things of the same nature and essence.

So it is with God. All separate persons in the Godhead are the same in essence. They were the same in body, soul, and spirit until Christ became a man. If one has a personal body, they all have bodies; if one has a personal soul, they all have souls; if one has a personal spirit, they all have spirits. On the other hand, if no one of them has a personal body, soul, and spirit, then none of them do.

We cannot think of the deity as existing without personality, without body, without soul, without spirit, without form and shape, and without all the bodily parts, soul passions, and spirit faculties that the Bible teaches. If we don't believe what the Bible says about God, let's be frank and say that the Bible is plainly wrong in hundreds of places and we don't know what to believe. Let's say that God doesn't know enough about Himself to know what to say about Himself, and that He doesn't know human language well enough to make Himself clear. Let's throw the Bible away and live without any fear of ever meeting anyone who is real and who will someday judge us. If God is not real, then we will never stand before Him to be judged.

Should we take this attitude toward God and His Word? If not, then let's come to our senses and give God credit for being able to use human language and reveal Himself to us in plain language. Let's throw away all the foolish and unscriptural theories about God and believe the wonderful truths plainly revealed in plain language in the Bible, as we have seen in Lessons Four, Twenty-one, and in this lesson.

Not one of the plain facts in Points I to III above could possibly be understood if we don't consider the Holy Spirit as a real person with a spirit-body, soul, and spirit, like the Father has. If personal names, attributes, works, offices, characteristics, treatment, personal pronouns, personal references, and descriptions of a person are used of the Holy Spirit, then He must be recognized as a real person with a spirit-body, soul, and spirit.

If He is distinct from both the Father and the Son (as proven in Point III above), then He's bound to have a separate spirit-body, soul, and spirit from either the Father or the Son. The same Scriptures that prove one person of the Godhead has a personal body, soul, and spirit will prove that each of the three separate and distinct members of deity also has them.

The following points prove that the Holy Ghost has a spirit-body:

1. He is distinctly called God in Acts 5:3-4. God has a body, as we proved in Lesson Four, Point II, 1-9.

2. The personal names given to the Holy Spirit prove that He has a body. Personal names are never given to an abstract power or an influence belonging to some person.

3. The divine attributes — which are faculties of a real person — prove the Holy Spirit to be a real person. He wouldn't have these personal attributes if He weren't a person. He couldn't be a person without bodily parts through which these personal powers are manifested. He would have to have a body, or He couldn't manifest Himself and His personal powers in the material world.

For example, how could He be present if there's nothing about Him to make Himself known? How could anyone tell He's present if He's incapable of manifesting His presence? How could He move upon the face of the waters if there's nothing to move, as required in Gen. 1:2; Ps. 104:30? How could He manifest His power if He's incapable of free and independent choice in doing so, or if He were a mere influence? Do influences have power of choice and responsibility?

How could He search and know the things of God and impart that knowledge to others if He were a mere power of another person? How could He prophesy or know what to predict if He weren't a real person, and how could He be such a person without powers of visible manifestation? How could He have will power, mind, intelligence, choice, judgment, fear, power, decision, authority, hearing, speech, unselfishness, and other attributes and powers if He's not a real person having a spirit-body like angels and other spirit-beings?

4. The divine personal acts of the Holy Spirit, which require real bodily action, prove that He has a body. The Spirit has been the direct power of operation and the agent of God in creating all things, directing gospel work, revealing and inspiring Scrip

lessons.title: The Gifts and Fruit of the Holy Spirit
lessons.content:
# The Gifts and Fruit of the Holy Spirit

Let's talk about something that's often misunderstood in Scripture — the gifts of the Spirit. Throughout this study, we've emphasized that being called "children of God" and "sons of God" actually *means* something. When we say God is our Father and we're His children, we're saying He hasn't just accepted us — He's given us power to become His sons, with the authority to represent Him in the world the way He would represent Himself. As God's children, we share in the supernatural, divine power that belongs to Him.

God has made complete provision for His power and life to be fully expressed through His people. In earlier lessons, we've shown what God's plan is for meeting human needs and how every believer has the power of attorney to act in Christ's place among people. We're partakers of the divine nature (2 Pet. 1:4). We have access to God and the freedom to exercise the divine powers that belong to God's family.

Think of it this way: everything in existence shares the nature, powers, and characteristics of its own kind. Fish have all the abilities of fish — they swim, they breathe underwater. Birds have every bird-like trait. Animals follow their own kind. Humans are the same. And so it is with God's family. The moment anything is born, it has certain powers from its parents. A baby fish can swim; a bird can fly; a dog can bark. It would be a miracle for a dog to live in water or a fish to fly through the sky — that would go against nature.

In the same way, it goes against nature for people to be spiritually born of God, share His nature, and then live contrary to that nature. We as God's sons should manifest a supernatural nature through the Holy Spirit's indwelling and through faith in Jesus Christ's name and His atonement. God has provided the baptism in the Spirit and the gifts of the Spirit for exactly this purpose — to show His divine life in His sons.

Nothing in Scripture is more specific and detailed than God's plan for divine-human sons equipped with power to represent Him on Earth. In previous lessons, we've given hundreds of Scriptures proving this. The spiritual tools God gives us are the gifts of the Spirit, and He wants us — all of His children on Earth — to exercise them fully. He has repeatedly demonstrated His will through the prophets, through Jesus Christ, and through early believers. He has repeatedly promised this power to every believer, so there's no excuse for not knowing about it.

Paul started his teaching on spiritual gifts by saying, "Now concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I would not have you ignorant" (1 Cor. 12:1), and he ended by saying, "But if any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant" (1 Cor. 14:38). It seems like the simpler and more detailed God's truths are, the more people remain ignorant of them! Every time Paul uses a phrase like "I would not have you to be ignorant," the subject turns out to be one of the simplest in Scripture to understand. If anything seems difficult, it's explained so simply and clearly that there can be no misunderstanding — if you actually want to understand (see Rom. 1:13; 11:25; 1 Cor. 10:1; 12:1; 2 Cor. 1:8; 1 Thess. 4:13-17).
