The only reason anyone could possibly misunderstand is if they deliberately refuse to, as Paul plainly stated: "But if any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant."

In Lesson Eighteen, Point IX, we saw that the gifts of the Spirit were active throughout Old Testament times, except for the gift of tongues and interpretation of tongues. Saints of old had tremendous power with God — in many cases even greater power than what we see among modern believers today. Let any honest person compare what power they have with what Old Testament saints had, or with what Christ and the apostles demonstrated, and they'll have to admit they're falling far short. Is this normal? Is this what the Bible teaches? Is this really God's will for believers today?

We can't bring ourselves to say this is God's best, not with hundreds of Scriptures on this subject. God has been a God of signs and wonders in both Testaments. He's always the same. His abilities are constant, His attributes eternal, His divine powers unchanging. He has never decreased in power and never will. He's not evolving and never will evolve in divine power. He is today what He has always been and always will be. He will never become what He isn't, and what He ever was, He still is. God's energies don't grow, and His plan of using power for creation's highest good never changes.

The gifts of the Spirit aren't new. They were exercised in ancient times with greater power than we often see today. And these weren't just temporary loans of power to Old Testament saints, as some teach. They were permanent anointings and gifts, just as "without repentance" on God's part then as they are now (Rom. 11:29). No calling of God and no gift of the Spirit in any age has ever been a mere loan or temporary ability if it was truly given to a person. Solomon said about his gift of wisdom, even after he had backslidden: "Also my wisdom remained with me" (Eccl. 2:9). There's no statement anywhere saying Old Testament gifts and anointings were only loans or temporary. That's simply a human theory and should be valued only as such.

## I. Pertinent Questions Concerning Spiritual Gifts

### 1. HOW MANY GIFTS ARE THERE?

There are nine gifts (1 Cor. 12:4-11):

> (1) The gift of the word of wisdom.

> (2) The gift of the word of knowledge.

> (3) The gift of faith.

> (4) The gifts of healing.

> (5) The gift of the working of miracles.

> (6) The gift of prophecy.

> (7) The gift of discerning of spirits.

> (8) The gift of divers kinds of tongues or languages.

> (9) The gift of the interpretation of tongues or languages.

### 2. WHOSE GIFTS ARE THEY, AND WHO WORKS THEM?

They belong to the Holy Spirit, and He's the one who operates them in believers' lives. "Now there are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit. And there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord. And there are diversities of operations, but it is the same God which worketh all in all. But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit withal. For to one is given by the Spirit the word of wisdom; to another the word of knowledge by the same Spirit; to another faith by the same Spirit; to another the gifts of healing by the same Spirit; to another the working of miracles; to another prophecy; to another discerning of spirits; to another divers kinds of tongues; to another the interpretation of tongues. But all these worketh that one and selfsame Spirit, dividing to every man severally as he will" (1 Cor. 12:4-11).

### 3. WHAT IS THE PURPOSE OF THE GIFTS?

The purpose is to enable all believers to do Christ's works and continue what He began both to do and to teach (Acts 1:1-2, 12-15; 1 Cor. 1:7; Rom. 12:3-8; 1 Cor. 12:4-31; 14:1-40; Heb. 2:3-4). They were given to confirm the gospel and demonstrate God's power among people: "These signs shall follow them that believe; In my name shall they cast out devils; they shall speak with new tongues; they shall take up serpents; and if they drink any deadly thing it shall not hurt them: they shall lay hands on the sick and they shall recover... And they [early believers] went forth, and preached every where, the Lord working with them, and confirming the word with signs following" (Mk. 16:15-20).

"How shall we escape if we neglect so great salvation; which at the first began to be spoken by the Lord, and was confirmed unto us by them that heard him; God also bearing them witness, both with signs and wonders, and with divers miracles, and gifts of the Holy Ghost, according to his own will?" (Heb. 2:3-4).

"For I will not dare to speak of any of those things which Christ hath not wrought by me, to make the Gentiles obedient, by word and deed, through mighty signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit of God; so that from Jerusalem, and round about unto Illyricum, I have fully preached the gospel of Christ... And I am sure that, when I come unto you, I shall come in the fullness of the blessing of the gospel of Christ" (Rom. 15:18-19, 29).

"He that believeth on me, the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do; because I go to my Father" (Jn. 14:12).

"God hath set some in the church, first apostles, secondarily prophets, thirdly teachers, after that miracles, then gifts of healings, helps, governments, diversities of tongues... But covet earnestly the best gifts: and yet shew I unto you a more excellent way... Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts" (1 Cor. 12:28-30; 14:1).

These and other Scriptures explain the purpose of spiritual gifts. God's purpose hasn't changed. It will remain the same throughout this age, as we've proven in Lessons Twenty and Twenty-two. Only humanity has changed, losing the powers of the original church. When people change again to a normal New Testament faith, they'll once more see the gifts of the Spirit operating perfectly and fully in the modern church.

In 1 Corinthians 12 and 14, Paul deals with supernatural equipment for service and worship and how certain gifts of the Spirit should be used in public believers' meetings. It's impossible to have a true New Testament gathering of believers without exercising spiritual gifts of some kind. These gifts are essential not just for service but for worship too. With all our claims to godliness and loyalty to church practices and rituals, we fail God if there's no knowledge of, respect for, or use of spiritual gifts.

First of all, it's God's will for people to be highly informed about spiritual gifts, as proven in 1 Cor. 12:1: "Now concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I would not have you ignorant." The modern church's ignorance about these spiritual abilities is simply staggering. Rarely, if ever, are these gifts preached about in the average church. When they are mentioned, it's usually in a negative way. Some are ridiculed and labeled as demonic or fanatical. If they were exercised today in most churches, they'd be rejected. If the Corinthians, who had these gifts, were called ignorant about using them properly in public meetings, what term should describe modern churches' knowledge of them? The average preacher today doesn't even know what these gifts are, let alone their purpose and proper use in local churches. The average church member doesn't know such gifts exist for believers.

The Corinthians had been hopeless idolaters and were familiar with supernatural powers and how they operated. Both among Gentiles and Jews there were magicians, wizards, sorcerers, and fortune-tellers who yielded themselves to demons and satanic powers. These Gentile converts were often ignorant of the difference between demon powers and the true spiritual gifts operated by the Holy Spirit. Their idols were actually deified demons (1 Cor. 10:16-21).

Paul gave them a way to discern between good and evil spirits: "Ye know that ye were Gentiles, carried away unto these dumb idols, even as ye were led. Wherefore I give you to understand, that no man speaking by the Spirit of God calleth Jesus accursed; and that no man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Ghost" (1 Cor. 12:2-3). John said, "Every spirit that confesseth not that Jesus Christ is come in the flesh is not of God: and this is the spirit of antichrist... and the spirit of error" (1 Jn. 4:1-6).

In other words, any power doing miracles or otherwise manifesting itself — if it calls Jesus accursed or claims He didn't come in the flesh — it's from the devil, not God. Many false religions today perform miracles while denying Jesus's reality, deity, humanity, atonement, and work for humanity. Some claim He's merely one prophet among many leaders God has raised through the ages, grouping Him with Mohammed, Buddha, Moses, and other religious founders. Other false religions deny altogether the reality of God, Christ, the Holy Spirit, the devil, demons, sin, sickness, and all material and spiritual substance. Any religion that denies these things is of the devil and serves Satan. Any person under demonic influence won't acknowledge that Jesus is Lord or that He came in the flesh. On the other hand, Christians whose lives and words proclaim Jesus Christ's Lordship, that He came in the flesh, and that the entire gospel is truth — these should be accepted as from God.

Early believers lived in an atmosphere charged with the supernatural, where signs and wonders were daily occurrences, where such manifestations were expected, and where counterfeit demon-workings always pressed for recognition as divine. In all spiritual revivals past and present, these counterfeits have appeared — sometimes so prominently that worldly and formal religious people turned against all supernatural manifestations.

What a change from the early church to the modern one! In ancient times, God's ministers would throw down rods that swallowed up those of demon powers (Exod. 7:10-14). They completely destroyed the counterfeit workings of Satan through Simon Magus, Bar-Jesus, and others who bewitched people into believing they were great ones on Earth (Acts 8:5-24; 13:6-13). Must today's prophets of the Lord bow the knee to Satan's power and cut themselves with knives to get an answer from their God who has gone on a journey, or is asleep, or has stopped caring for the lost, sick, and dying for whom Jesus died (1 Ki. 18)? Must the modern church be satisfied with seeing the Holy Spirit defied and demonic powers enthroned in religious circles? Must the Church constantly cry like Gideon: "If the Lord be with us, why then is all this befallen us? And where be all his miracles which our fathers told us of? But now the Lord hath forsaken us" (Judg. 6:13-14)?

The Church must awake from its slumber and stop criticizing spiritual gifts, experiences, and manifestations. It must repent and cry out mightily to God for power from on High until signs and wonders again defeat sin and Satan. It must earnestly desire the gifts of the Spirit and contend for the faith once delivered to the saints (Jude 3).

### 4. WHO MAY HAVE THE GIFTS OF THE SPIRIT?

Every believer can have one or all the gifts, as they desire them and seek God for the Holy Spirit's anointing (1 Cor. 12:8-31). In this passage, we read that every member of Christ's body should normally function in some capacity to build up the whole body. Paul wrote to this church: "That in every thing ye are enriched by him, in all utterance, and in all knowledge. Even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you. So that ye come behind in no gift" (1 Cor. 1:5-7). He wrote to Rome: "For I long to see you, that I may impart unto you some spiritual gift, to the end ye may be established" (Rom. 1:11). And again: "According as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith... Having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us, whether prophecy, let us prophesy according to the proportion of faith; or ministry, let us wait on our ministry; or he that teacheth, on teaching" (Rom. 12:3-8).

Jesus taught that "He that believeth" should be able to do His works and that "these signs shall follow them that believe" (Jn. 14:12; Mk. 16:15-20). He said all things would be possible to those who believe (Mk. 9:23) and that anything asked in prayer with faith would be received (Mk. 11:22-24; Mt. 21:21-22).

This proves every believer can attain the gifts of the Spirit in fullness and that there's no limit to how many you can have. Everyone can have them all — and must have them all before doing the works Christ did. So many Scriptures assure us of unlimited access that each person can have faith for and receive everything they want from God. You can limit God's gifts and grace in your life, or you can have unlimited faith and come short in nothing. This is either truth, or the Scriptures above are lies — and no child of God dares believe that. People have taught for so long that we can only have one gift and can't get what we want from God that it's become second nature to live in unbelief and question every truth that isn't commonly accepted. No one has to remain in this state of constant questioning and doubting. You can stop right now — and you must, if you want to attain God's fullness, as Paul prayed for all believers to do in 1 Cor. 1:7; Eph. 3:19.

In 1 Cor. 12:4-11, we're told the gifts are for "every man" and for "all," and that "to one is given... to another... to another..." and so on. Gifts then are for every person who will believe in them, ask God for them, and receive them. How many believers claim one or more of these gifts? Most are full of questions and arguments against them being for every believer. Most are more afraid of words like "miracle," "sign," and "wonder" than they are of sin and the devil. The gifts of the Spirit are signs of the Holy Spirit's indwelling, showing that someone is a normal New Testament Christian spiritually.

### 5. ARE THE GIFTS THE SAME AND ARE THEIR OPERATIONS THE SAME?

We've already seen that there are nine different gifts with different operations. The gifts differ, but the giver is one. The source is the same, but the streams are many. The variety in the Spirit's operations creates unity, not division. The diversity is corporate, not competitive.

Think of it like an orchestra: different instruments playing different parts, but all following the same conductor to create one beautiful symphony. The differences in administration fit perfectly with the different types and personalities of various people, accomplishing God's varied purposes in redeeming and blessing all creation.

The gifts are the division of God's omnipotence and omniscience into varied divine abilities for God's children to use in defeating the devil's works and confirming God's Word among people.

In the gifts, we have divine abilities that meet every known human need in this life and the next. These nine gracious elements make God's powers available to all believers for the known needs, wants, and deliverances of God's sons in this rebellious world of evil spirits and sin-darkened Earth. In them we have: glorious revelations of God in wisdom and knowledge; unwavering faith that brings into reality things that don't yet exist; disease-withering and demon-destroying beams from the Sun of Righteousness who has healing in His wings; miraculous powers that know no defeat and create life's necessities; radiant prophetic insight into past, present, and future; illuminating ability to impart eternal truths and discern the minds and activities of both people and evil spirits; linguistic powers to converse in human languages and tongues of angels; and life-giving, soul-cleansing, spiritual helps capable of defeating all satanic opposition to the gospel.

God's program is still "Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, saith the Lord of hosts" (Zech. 4:6). The divine energies and abilities of God's unchanging Spirit are still available for Earth's desperate needs and especially the Church's needs. The stone rejected by the builders will yet become the chief cornerstone. The world will soon see the full power of the Spirit's gifts in modern believers.

### 6. CAN ONE PERSON HAVE ALL THE GIFTS, OR ARE THEY LIMITED TO ONE PER INDIVIDUAL OR TO A FEW SPECIAL INDIVIDUALS AS THE SPIRIT WILLS?

It's true that 1 Cor. 12:4-11 states the Spirit gives gifts as He wills, but there's no statement that He doesn't will to give all of them to every child of God, or that He only wills to give them to a few special people. It also states He gives "to every man" — not just a few — and that the Spirit "worketh all in all." There's absolutely no limiting statement anywhere saying God will give only one gift to one person and no more. There's positively no limitation!

On the contrary, many passages teach that no believer is limited and that every saved person can get from God what they want. We've proven this in Lessons Ten, Twelve, Fourteen, Sixteen, Twenty, and Twenty-two. If you limit your knowledge, you limit your faith. If you limit your faith, you limit what you get from God. If you want only one gift and believe that's all you can have, that's all you'll get. If you want all the gifts and believe you can have all, you'll get all as you believe and receive. Jesus taught that everyone could get all the gifts He had (Mt. 9:29; 21:21-22; Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; Heb. 11:6; Jas. 1:5-8; Ps. 84:11).

### 7. HAS ANYONE EVER RECEIVED AND EXERCISED ALL THE GIFTS?

Several men in the Old Testament had several gifts, though some weren't yet given to people — specifically tongues and interpretation of tongues. These were foretold by prophets as gifts to be given in this age (Isa. 28:9-11; Joel 2:28-31; Mk. 16:15-18; Jn. 15:26; 16:13-15; Acts 1:4-5; 2:14-21, 38-39; 10:44-48; 19:1-6; 1 Cor. 12:1–14:40; etc.).

People in this age have received and exercised all nine gifts of the Spirit, because this is the age when God's fullness is promised to us (Jn. 1:16; 3:34; 7:37-39; 14:12-17, 26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Mk. 16:15-20; Eph. 3:19; 1 Cor. 1:7; 12:4-11; etc.). Jesus Christ was the first ever to receive the Spirit's fullness (Jn. 3:34). All nine gifts can be traced in the Holy Spirit's operations through His ministry. Notice the following gifts in Christ's life:

> (1) The gifts of wisdom and knowledge (Lk. 2:40-52; 22:10-12; Jn. 2:24-25; 4:16-19; 6:64; 13:1; 16:30; 21:17).

> (2) The gift of faith (Mt. 8:23-27; 14:22-32; 16:8-12; 17:15-21; 21:21-22; Mk. 9:17-29; 11:12-24; Heb. 12:1-2; Gal. 2:20).

> (3) The gifts of healing (Mt. 4:23-24; 8:16-17; Acts 10:38).

> (4) The gift of the working of miracles (Mt. 8:26; 14:22; 15:32-39; 17:27; 21:18-22; Lk. 22:49-51; Jn. 2:1-12; 6:21; 11:43-44).

> (5) The gift of prophecy (Mt. 5:7; 16:21; 24:1-25:46).

> (6) The gift of discerning of spirits (Mt. 9:4; 12:25; 16:23; Mk. 1:34; 2:8; Lk. 7:36-50; 8:2; Jn. 1:48-51; 2:24-25; 4:16-19).

> (7) The gifts of tongues and interpretation of tongues. These are the only gifts of the nine that people had never experienced before Pentecost, except for Christ, who received the Spirit in all fullness (Jn. 1:16; 3:34). We therefore couldn't question His ability to exercise both of these gifts.

Paul was another person who had all the gifts of the Spirit and God's fullness in his life. He had power to impart gifts to others (Rom. 1:11; 1 Tim. 4:14; 2 Tim. 1:6). He had power to impart the baptism of the Spirit to others (Acts 19:1-7). He had the gift of knowledge and wisdom as proven by his many writings (Gal. 1:11-17; Eph. 1:8-19; 3:1-20; etc.), the gift of faith (Gal. 2:20; 3:2), the gifts of healing and miracles (Acts 13:6-13; 14:3-10; 15:12; 13:16-24; 19:11-20; etc.), the gift of prophecy (Rom. 11:25-27; 1 Cor. 14:6; 15:21-58; 2 Thess. 2; etc.), the gift of discerning of spirits (Acts 13:9-11; 1 Cor. 1:10-12; 13:1-3; 2 Cor. 10:3-6), and the gifts of tongues and interpretation of tongues (1 Cor. 14:6-22).

The other apostles and ordinary believers also exercised many gifts of the Spirit, as seen in Acts 2:14-21, 43; 3:6-9; 4:33; 5:3-16; 6:5-10; 8:4-40; 9:17, 32-43; 11:21; etc. God confirmed His Word with signs following and gave His early workers gifts of the Holy Spirit to defeat Satan (Mk. 16:15-20; Heb. 2:3-4). Jesus promised the fullness of God to everyone who would believe in Him (Jn. 14:12-15). If every person is promised power and divine gifts to enable them to do Christ's works, then it's certain that all the gifts can be received by anyone who will follow the Lord in the fullness of the gospel. Paul prayed for early Christians to be filled with all God's fullness — if that weren't possible, he wouldn't have prayed for it (Eph. 3:19). He taught that every believer should be a vessel of honor, sanctified, fit for the master's use and prepared for every good work (2 Tim. 2:19-21). He speaks of Timothy as being able to do all the works he did, and if Paul had all the gifts, then Timothy clearly had them too (1 Cor. 16:10). Ordinary elders had power to impart spiritual gifts (1 Tim. 4:14; 2 Tim. 1:5-6) and have signs following them (Mk. 16:15-20).

These and other facts prove that early Christians were far ahead of modern ones in exercising spiritual gifts and that nowhere in Scripture are the gifts limited to a few people or a certain period, as some denominations teach today.

### 8. HOW MANY GIFTS OF THE SPIRIT WERE MANIFEST IN THE OLD TESTAMENT?

At least seven of them were given to people in ages before Pentecost, as we've seen in Lesson Eighteen, Point IX.

### 9. ARE GIFTS ONLY FOR THOSE BAPTIZED IN THE SPIRIT?

No, they're for God's children whether baptized in the Spirit or not. Old Testament saints, before people were baptized with the Spirit at Pentecost, had at least seven of the gifts, as proven above. Many people since Pentecost who have never received the real baptism in the Spirit have received gifts and done great things for God. Such people would have done even greater work if they had been baptized in the Spirit. No person should be satisfied with only one gift, and certainly shouldn't be even partly satisfied without any gifts. A person without gifts is like someone who has a job to do but no tools to work with. Why should Christians carry on a sham battle with satanic forces and work hard all their lives trying to do what God commands without the spiritual tools known as the gifts of the Spirit, when they can have power to defeat these forces?

### 10. CAN GIFTS BE ABUSED AND MISUSED?

Yes, they can be. Solomon, while backslidden, used his wonderful gifts of wisdom and knowledge to further his rebellion against God (Eccl. 1:12-18; 2:4-11). Moses misused his gift of miracles, and for striking the rock instead of speaking to it, he was forbidden to enter Canaan (Num. 20:7-13; Deut. 32:48-52). Paul taught that you may have the gifts of the Spirit but still lack love, making them profit nothing (1 Cor. 13:1-3). He further corrected many abuses of gifts in local churches, as is clear in 1 Cor. 14:1-40.

### 11. CAN GIFTS BE EXERCISED BY MAN WITHOUT THE HOLY SPIRIT?

At least certain gifts — or more specifically, the abilities from certain gifts that become part of the individual's soul and spirit — can be exercised by anyone, even in a backslidden condition. This was true of Solomon. The wisdom and knowledge God gave him became part of his natural ability, and he continued using them while backslidden. Any knowledge a person learns by divine revelation, experience, or teaching can still be used whether saved or not, because it becomes part of their mental knowledge and wisdom. The same is true with some other gifts.

Paul said, "Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity [divine love], I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal. And though I have the gift of prophecy, and understand all mysteries, and all knowledge; and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains, and have not charity, I am nothing. And though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be burned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing" (1 Cor. 13:1-3). At minimum, this passage teaches the possibility of having and exercising certain gifts without divine love and the Spirit's direct anointing.

It's possible to exercise the natural remains of certain abilities imparted by the Spirit that become part of your natural abilities. You can use them anytime and anywhere, backslidden or not. It's just as possible to use them as any other natural ability. Anyone who receives one of the gifts and normally exercises it for any length of time will naturally absorb some of these supernatural abilities. They'll be able to use whatever becomes part of their natural makeup even after backsliding. The reception of these abilities is by divine inspiration and from God, but their continued use may not always be directly empowered by God, especially what a person can do on their own.

A person endowed with wisdom and knowledge will always retain these powers to a great extent, even after ceasing to be anointed by the Spirit. Wisdom and knowledge become a natural part of whoever has received these divine abilities. Discerning of spirits will give a person insight into things they never could see before and the ability to distinguish between good and evil spirits. This also becomes a natural ability to some extent. There are certain traits, laws, characteristics, and powers of discernment that stay with a person who may fall back into the depths of sin.

The gift of prophecy includes not only foretelling but forthtelling. Prophecy is mainly the ability to speak to people for edification, exhortation, and comfort by direct anointing. The predictive element wasn't the main part of this gift (1 Cor. 14:3, 29-33). Prophets were primarily preachers of righteousness, and anyone who receives a gift along this line can still outwardly use it when backslidden. In other words, they can still speak to people for edification, exhortation, and comfort. They may be living in sin, but can still use their gifts in outward form because they retain certain truths, knowledge, wisdom, and intelligence that can help people live right — whether they themselves live right or not. Most of us have heard preachers who were living in open sin while still carrying on an outward ministry of teaching and preaching truths they knew to be true.

The gifts of faith, miracles, healings, tongues, and interpretation of tongues also leave natural marks on people who have greatly exercised them. People who had these gifts while serving God have been able to exercise more faith and power from the natural remains of these abilities — even in a backslidden state — than many clean Christians struggling to have faith and receive from God. It would naturally be easier for such people to believe and get answers to prayer, because they've experienced the power of faith, miracles, healings, and tongues while living for God. Through experience they have certain knowledge and retain natural abilities beyond those who've never had the slightest experience of such things. Wouldn't it be easier for someone who has had many wonderful experiences with God, who has exercised gifts and can remember the signs and wonders God performed through them, to exercise gifts than for someone totally ignorant and inexperienced? These facts are too self-evident to deny.

All these natural abilities remaining with people when they fall back into sin, if exercised, may be mistaken for real personal acts of the Holy Spirit through

lessons.title: The Doctrine of the Trinity
lessons.content:
# The Doctrine of the Trinity

## I. Names of God in the Old Testament

Let's explore some of the names God uses for Himself in the Old Testament. This isn't just an academic exercise—understanding these names helps us see that Scripture itself reveals more than one person in the Godhead. It also shows us that God has many names, not just one.

### 1. ELOHIM

This is the Hebrew word for "God" that you'll find in Genesis 1:1 and about 2,700 other places throughout the Old Testament. Here's what's fascinating: it's a uni-plural noun, meaning "Gods," and it's actually translated that way 239 times (Gen. 3:5; Exod. 22:28; 1 Sam. 4:8; Dan. 2:11; 4:6-9; 5:11, 14; etc.).

Elohim is the plural form of *Eloah*, which means "deity" or "God." When used with the definite article, it refers to the Supreme God. It's also translated "judges" in Exod. 21:6; 22:8-9 and "angels" in Ps. 8:5.

Sometimes Elohim appears with plural verbs and pronouns in the Hebrew text. For example, Gen. 20:13 literally reads "the Gods they caused me to wander," and Gen. 35:7 says "there the Gods they appeared unto him." You'll also notice plural pronouns used when God speaks of the different members of the Godhead—words like "us" (Gen. 1:26; 3:22; 11:7; Isa. 6:8; Jn. 17:21), "our" (Gen. 1:26; Jn. 14:23), "we" (Jn. 14:23; 17:11, 22), and "their" (Ps. 2:3).

Think about this: first, second, and third personal pronouns are used hundreds of times in Scripture—sometimes of one, two, or three persons in the deity. In John 17 alone, Jesus uses 162 pronouns when speaking to and about His Father! Psalm 119 contains 610 pronouns used by man to and of God.

Here's the thing: when the different members of the Godhead speak to and about each other just like people do, should we accuse them of misrepresenting themselves or of not knowing how to use human language properly? If we can understand what pronouns mean when people use them with each other, we should be able to understand them the same way when the members of the Godhead use them.

When singular pronouns are used of deity, it's because one of the three persons of the Trinity is speaking of Himself, representing the whole Godhead, or speaking to one person about another—as you can clearly see in Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15; 17:1-25.

In the Old Testament, the Father was the prominent speaker (Heb. 1:1-3; Acts 3:21). In the Gospels, the Son took center stage (Jn. 5:47; 6:33; 8:26, 28, 38; 12:49-50; 17:8). And now, the Holy Spirit is the prominent speaker (Jn. 14:26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Acts 2:4; 13:2; 15:28; 20:28).

Sometimes singular pronouns refer to the whole Godhead as a unit (Exod. 20:3; Isa. 44:6, 8; 45:5, 21; 46:9; Hos. 13:4; etc.). It's similar to how the whole Church, as the body of Christ, is spoken of as a "man" and "he" (Eph. 2:14-15; 4:13; 5:25-27; 2 Thess. 2:7-8).

So the one Elohim isn't one person or one in number—it's one in *unity*. Elohim isn't a divided deity but three persons in "one God," or one deity. The word itself doesn't tell us there are exactly three, but it does tell us deity is plural. And here's something striking: God is referred to in the plural twice as many times as the word "Jesus" appears in both Testaments combined!

### 2. YEHOVAH

This Hebrew word for "Lord" appears in Deut. 6:4 and about 7,600 times throughout the Old Testament. It means "the Self-existent," "Eternal and Immutable One," or "Unity."

Now, this word doesn't tell us *how many* persons are in the "One [Unified] Lord," but Scripture mentions three persons who are called "Lord," who are "Self-existent" and "Eternal"—so all three can be called "Jehovah."

In Gen. 19:24; Ps. 110:1; Mt. 22:44; Zech. 2:10-11, we see both the Father and the Son called "Lord." And when you compare Exod. 16:7 with Heb. 3:7-8, and Isa. 6:8-9 with Acts 28:25, and Exod. 17:7 with Heb. 3:7-9, and Jer. 31:31-34 with Heb. 10:15-16, you'll see that the Holy Spirit is also called "Lord" or "Jehovah." (See Lesson Four, Point 6, where all three are called "God.")

Yehovah is rendered "Jehovah" four times (Exod. 6:3; Ps. 83:18; Isa. 12:2; 26:4). Jehovah also appears in many compound names of deity, which we won't list here.

### 3. EL

This Hebrew word for "God" appears in about 250 places. It means "the Strong One" or "Elohim the Omnipotent." While Elohim is "God the Creator," El emphasizes His omnipotence put into action. All three persons of the Trinity are omnipotent, as we've seen in earlier lessons.

### 4. ELOAH

This Hebrew word for "God" appears in 56 places. It refers to "Elohim who is to be worshipped." You'll often find it used when worship of idols is contrasted with worship of the true God—the "living God" compared to dead gods. It means "deity" and "God."

### 5. ELYON

This Hebrew word for "Most High" is translated that way 40 times. It means "the Supreme," "Most High," "Highest," "Lofty." It's El, the possessor of Heaven and Earth, and Elohim, the Creator of Heaven and Earth.

It's translated "High" in Ps. 78:35; Dan. 4:2 and "higher" when referring to Jesus as God's "firstborn" in Ps. 89:27. But here's something important: Jesus in the New Testament is never called the Most High—only the *Son* of the Most High God (Mk. 5:7; Lk. 1:32-35; 8:28).

When Christ was born, the angels sang to the "God in the highest," who was not Jesus (Lk. 2:14). The Father is still the "head of Christ" (1 Cor. 11:3), so He is rightfully the Most High God.

### 6. ADON

This Hebrew word for "Lord" is translated that way 195 times in the Old Testament. It means "Sovereign," "Ruler," "Master."

*Adonai* is the emphatic form and is translated "Lord" 430 times in the Old Testament. It's also translated "lords" four times (Gen. 19:2; Deut. 10:17; Ps. 136:3; Isa. 26:13). Two Adonais are mentioned in Ps. 110:1 and referred to in the New Testament as the Father and the Son (Mt. 22:44).

### 7. Other Names

A few other names of God in the Old Testament include:

- "JAH" (Ps. 68:4, 18)
- "I AM THAT I AM" and "I AM" (Exod. 3:13-15)
- "God Almighty" (Exod. 6:3)
- "Lord God" (Gen. 2:4 and hundreds of other times)
- "Jealous" (Exod. 34:14)
- "The Lord of Hosts" (Isa. 47:4 and 281 other times)
- "Holy" (Isa. 57:15)
- And many others

All of this proves that the teaching that "Jesus" is the only God and the only name of that God simply isn't scriptural. If God said these were His names and some human beings say they're not, who should we believe?

Some claim all these names are merely titles signifying office, rank, or relationship, but that's just human theory contradicting the Scriptures, which clearly call them real names.

God's names appear over 19,000 times in the Bible, always in connection with His eternal plan of creation, redemption, and eternal rulership over all things (Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14, 27; Lk. 1:32-33; 1 Cor. 15:24-28; Eph. 1:10; 2:7; Rev. 5:10; 11:15; 22:4-5).

---

## II. Names of God in the New Testament

### 1. THEOS

This is the Greek word for "God," used 1,332 times in the New Testament. It means "deity" and "God," and with the definite article means "the Supreme Deity." The Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost are all called God, as we saw in Lesson Four, Point 6.

### 2. KURIOS

This Greek word for "Lord" is used 672 times in the New Testament. It means "Lord," "Master," and "Sir," and is used of men 31 times.

### 3. IESOUS

This is the Greek word for "Jesus," appearing 979 times in the New Testament. It's the Greek form of the Hebrew *Yehowshua*, rendered "Joshua" 215 times in the Old Testament. In fact, Joshua of the Old Testament is actually called "Jesus" in the New Testament in Acts 7:45 and Heb. 4:8.

Here's something crucial: Jesus is *never* the name of the Father or the Spirit in either Testament. It wasn't even the name of the second person of the Godhead until He became man and the Son of God about 1,900 years ago. It's the human name of the Son of God, as we covered in Lesson Twenty-one, Point VI.

### 4. CHRISTOS

This Greek word, which we translate "Christ," is used 575 times in the New Testament. It literally means "anointed" and is equivalent to the Hebrew *mashiyach*, translated "Messiah" twice in the Old Testament (Dan. 9:24-26) and twice in the New Testament (Jn. 1:41; 4:25).

Like the name "Jesus," it doesn't refer to deity but to the humanity of Jesus Christ. He became the Christ—the Anointed One—thirty years after He was born in a human body and after He was named "Jesus," as we saw in Lesson Twenty-one, Point VI.

Many other names of God's Son weren't actually true of Him until He took on these roles. For example, He had to become man before He could be called the Son of man, the Son of God, Jesus, Christ, Lord Jesus Christ, Messiah, Emmanuel, Son of David, the Lion of the Tribe of Judah, the Lamb of God, Mediator, and many other names.

Some use two passages (Gal. 3:17; 1 Pet. 1:11) to teach that "Christ" was a name of Jesus back in Old Testament times, but when you examine them, that's not what they say. The phrase "in Christ" in Gal. 3:17 is omitted in the best texts and was added by some copyist. You won't find it in the R.V., Weymouth, Centenary Translation, Concordant Version, Nestle's Greek Text, Moffatt, and many other translations.

The second passage (1 Pet. 1:11) should be understood in light of how Peter would naturally speak about Jesus *after* He became the Christ. When referring to what the second person of the Trinity did in Old Testament times, we might speak of Christ doing those things—but in reality, He wasn't yet "the Christ" when He did them. At that time, He was God but not yet the Son of God or the Anointed of God. Now that He's become man and "Christ," this name can be used of Him even when speaking of what He did before He received that title.

### 5. THE FATHER, THE SON, AND THE HOLY GHOST

(Mt. 28:19; 1 Jn. 5:7-8)

Some teach that these aren't names but mere titles. But that teaching shows a misunderstanding of how language works and contradicts God's Word. Our dictionaries and grammars call such words "nouns" or "substantives"—and all nouns are names. Not all are "proper names" of specific individuals, but usage in any particular context shows whether a word becomes a proper name.

In Mt. 28:19, it's not just any father, any son, or any ghost. It's specifically "the Father," "the Son," and "the Holy Ghost." The definite article "the" is used before each word in both Greek and English. These terms, used and associated this way, can apply to no other persons on Earth or in Heaven.

"The Father" in the New Testament is *never* applied to men, to Christ, or to the Holy Ghost—only and always to the first person of the Godhead, "the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ" (see Lesson Twenty-one, Points 8, 9, and 35).

"The Son" is *never* applied to men, to "the Father," or to "the Holy Ghost"—only and always to the second person of the Godhead, "the Son of the Father" (see Point 35, Lesson Twenty-one).

"The Holy Ghost" is *never* applied to men, to "the Father," or to "the Son"—only and always to the third person of the Godhead, who proceeds from the Father and the Son (see Lesson Twenty-five, Point VI).

The fact that these terms are always spelled with capital letters—like all proper names—proves they are proper names in Scripture. It was the consensus of all Bible scholars that these are true and proper names.

Funk and Wagnall's Dictionary says "a name is the appellation by which a person or thing is known." Webster says it's "the title by which any person or thing is known or designated." The Greek word *onoma* means "the name or word by which a person or thing is called, and by which he is distinguished from all others."

So "the Father," "the Son," and "the Holy Ghost" are names by which the three persons in the Godhead are known and distinguished from each other. They're not the *only* names of each, but they're the ones Jesus used when giving the Church the baptism formula and authority to baptize.

Now, some ask how "name" in the singular could be used of three persons and three names. They argue that if there were three persons, it would read "in the *names* of" not "in the *name* of." But this just shows a misunderstanding of how language works.

Think of it like this: when we send an ambassador to a foreign land, he does business "in the name of" (not "in the names of") the United States. Does that prove there's only one state? Of course not—there are fifty separate states in the one United States! The singular "name" in Mt. 28:19 affirms the equality and oneness of the Godhead—a perfect union of three persons in "one God"—just like "the United States" affirms the equality and oneness of fifty states in a perfect Union.

We could also speak of each state separately: "In the name of Missouri and of Illinois and of Ohio," and so on. It's not necessary to add "in the name" before each state, because once it's stated at the beginning, it's understood to apply to each one.

The same is true in Mt. 28:19. We don't need to repeat "in the name" before each person—once stated, it applies to each. Consider these examples:

- "All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth" (Mt. 28:18) doesn't need to repeat "all power is given unto me" for us to understand it applies to both places.
- "In the beginning God created the heaven" (Gen. 1:1) doesn't need to repeat "In the beginning God" for us to understand He also created the Earth at that time.
- "The God of Abraham, and of Isaac, and of Jacob" (Acts 3:13) doesn't need to change to "the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob" for us to understand He's the God of all three.

Jesus knew how to use language without having to repeat "in the name" before each person. It's just as correct to say it this way as to repeat it. Acts 3:13 is just as clear as Acts 7:32, where "the God of" *is* repeated before Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

Jesus knew the disciples understood there were three persons in the Godhead. And He knew everyone else in any age could understand the same if they'd be honest and believe the plain Scriptures.

One of the most foolish arguments goes like this: "Father is not a name; so what is the name of the Father? Son is not a name; so what is the name of the Son? Holy Ghost is not a name; so what is the name of the Holy Ghost?" That's like saying: "Missouri is not a name; what is the name of Missouri? Abraham is not a name; what is the name of Abraham?"

If a name is any word by which a person or thing is known, then "the Father," "the Son," and "the Holy Ghost" are names—just as much as "Missouri," "Abraham," or any other name. It doesn't have to fit someone's pet doctrine to qualify as a name.

Think about how any father and son can address each other using only those words—"Father" and "Son"—and never use any other name. This is true not only of people but also of God the Father and God the Son.

Not once did Jesus address the Father by any name except "Father" (Mt. 11:25-27; 26:39, 42; Mk. 14:36; Lk. 11:2; 23:34, 46; Jn. 12:27-28; 17:1-25). And not once did the Father address the Son by any name except "Son" (Mt. 3:16-17; 17:5; Jn. 12:28).

Jesus repeatedly used "Father" when speaking about God to others. When He spoke of "the name of the Father," He assumed the disciples knew exactly whom He meant. They also knew whom He meant by "the Holy Ghost," because they'd heard much about Him coming to take Jesus' place (Jn. 14:16-17, 26; 16:7-15; 20:22; Acts 1:1-8; Mt. 10:1, 20; 12:28-32; etc.). John the Baptist had even taught them about the Holy Ghost before Jesus appeared (Mt. 3:11; Jn. 1:32-34).

The disciples knew the Father was separate from the Son and Spirit because Jesus referred to God as "Father" 181 times. Later, the disciples called Him "Father" 78 times. Not once is He confused with or called "Jesus" or "the Holy Ghost."

They knew the Son was separate from the Father and Holy Ghost because Jesus distinguished between Himself and the Father 110 times; God the Father did so ten times; demons did ten times; and the disciples later did 85 times. Not once did anyone confuse the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost or call the Son "the Father" or "the Holy Ghost."

Jesus spoke of the Spirit 24 times as a separate person from Himself and the Father (see Lesson Twenty-five). Bible writers refer to the Spirit as separate from the Father and Son about 170 times. Not once is the Spirit called "the Father" or "the Son," and not once is He confused with them as being the same person.

So "the Father" is a name designating Him as separate from "the Son" and "the Spirit." "The Son" designates Him as separate from "the Father" and "the Spirit." And "the Holy Ghost" designates Him as separate from "the Father" and "the Son."

The phrase "in the name of" means "by the authority of." Webster says it's used "in behalf of, by the authority of; as, 'in the name of the law.'" This is how Jesus and many Bible writers used it:

- "In the name of Jesus Christ" means by the authority of Jesus Christ (Acts 2:38; 4:10; 16:16; 1 Cor. 5:4; 6:11; Eph. 5:20; 1 Thess. 3:6)
- "In the name of David" means by the authority of David (1 Sam. 25:5-9)
- "In the name of Paul" means by the authority of Paul (1 Cor. 1:13-15)
- "In the name of the Lord" means by the authority of the Lord (Deut. 18:5-7, 22; 21:5)
- "In the name of the Lord of Hosts" means by the authority of the Lord of Hosts (1 Sam. 17:45)
- "In my Father's name" means by the authority of my Father (Jn. 5:43; 10:25; 17:12)
- "In my name" means by the authority of my name (Mk. 16:17; Jn. 14:12-15, 26; 15:16; 16:23-26)

"By the name of" means the same as "in the name of" (Acts 4:10, 30). Speaking "in the name of" (Acts 4:17-18; 5:28, 40; 8:12; 9:27-29) means "by the authority of" the one who sent the speaker—just like in the Old Testament (Jer. 20:9; 26:9-20).

So "in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost" means we're to baptize *by the authority* of the Father, Son, and Holy Ghost—not by the authority of the Son only.

If we baptize only in the Son's name, we rob the Father and Holy Ghost of their rightful authority in redemption, which baptism symbolizes. We also directly violate the last commandment Jesus gave before He left, setting aside the very authority He said He received in Heaven and Earth (Mt. 28:18).

If He had authority to command this form of baptism and never authorized a change, then that authority still stands. To reject it is to reject Jesus Christ.

Those who believe Jesus is the only God shouldn't reject and contradict His authority. They should find a way to harmonize Acts with obedience to Mt. 28:19.

It's similar to this: suppose you were authorized to do a job in the name of J.C. Penney and Co., Sears Roebuck and Co., and Montgomery Ward and Co. If you only did it in the name of J.C. Penney and Co., rejecting the others because "in the name" wasn't repeated before each, you'd be robbing them of their rightful recognition and authority. Would that make all three companies one company? Of course not.

In Mt. 28:19, we have the full authority of the whole Godhead and of each member specifically. Refusing to recognize more than one of the three doesn't make the other two disappear or make them all one person.

In Acts 2:38; 8:16; 10:48; 19:5, the emphasis is on Jesus' authority—because *His* authority was what the Jews were rejecting at that time. They didn't question the Father's or Spirit's authority; they believed and accepted it. But accepting Jesus' authority like they accepted God's? That was another matter entirely.

The apostles in Acts simply demanded that the Jews accept Jesus' name—being baptized, saved, and healed by His authority. But nowhere do they reject or change Mt. 28:19 by saying "in the name of Jesus only." Not one passage demands the Jews accept Jesus as the *only* God and the *only* authority. Instead, they were commanded to accept Him *like* they accepted God.

To baptize only in Jesus' name instead of following Mt. 28:19—based merely on an inference that because all three names aren't mentioned in Acts they shouldn't be used—is to risk God's judgment.

Can't we obey *both* Mt. 28:19 and Acts? We can and must, because both are God's Word, and both must be obeyed (2 Tim. 3:16-17). Anyone obeying Mt. 28:19 automatically obeys Acts. But interpreting Acts to mean we should baptize in Jesus' name only means disobeying Mt. 28:19.

In other words, baptizing by the authority of all three in Mt. 28:19 automatically satisfies Acts. But baptizing by Jesus' authority *only* dishonors the other two in Mt. 28:19 and directly transgresses it. Obeying Acts while disobeying Matthew isn't excusable for thinking people.

And even if we were to follow Acts alone as the baptism formula—which passage in Acts should we use? They all differ in wording!

Those who insist baptism should be only in Jesus' name, and who prey upon honest and uninformed people, may claim they obey Mt. 28:19. But they never actually obey it, never recognize it in baptism, and always re-baptize those who were baptized according to Mt. 28:19.

If they claim to obey Mt. 28:19, ask them to baptize you that way and see how quickly they back down. There you'll see a concrete example of deliberately rejecting part of the Bible.

Couldn't Jesus have said what these groups teach, if that's what He meant? If He didn't say what they teach, then that's not what He means—and we'd better reject their false teaching on baptism and obey Mt. 28:19. We must either reject this false teaching or reject God. We can't believe and obey both, because they're opposites.

The preposition "of" and the conjunction "and" repeated before each of the three names in Mt. 28:19 further prove three separate persons. If someone said "a gift of John and of James and of George," he means a gift from *three* persons, not one.

Jesus didn't say to baptize only by His authority, but also by the authority of the Father and of the Holy Ghost. He didn't tell them to do this only until He came back in ten days as the Holy Ghost (as some teach). Instead, He said, "Teaching them to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you... even unto the end of the world."

There was no better time for Jesus to tell the disciples He was the only God, the Father, the Holy Ghost, and all of God—but instead He revealed three separate persons in the Godhead.

### 6. EVERLASTING FATHER

Some groups argue that "Father" isn't a name, yet they insist "the everlasting Father" is a name of Jesus and try to prove from Isa. 9:6-7 that Jesus is "the Father" of the New Testament.

But here's how ancient Hebrews, Greeks, and other Eastern peoples used such language: anyone who originated a custom or had a distinguishing characteristic was called the "father" of that thing. So:

- Satan is called "father of lies" because he originated them (Jn. 8:44)
- Jabal is called "the father of tent dwellers" because he originated that lifestyle (Gen. 4:20-21)
- Abraham is called "the father of circumcision" because it started with him (Rom. 4:12)
- God is called "the Father of mercies" because He is merciful (2 Cor. 1:3)
- People are called "children of disobedience" and "children of wrath" because they live in disobedience and are destined for wrath (Eph. 2:2-3; see also Mt. 9:15; 13:38; Lk. 16:8; Col. 3:6; 2 Sam. 7:10; Job 41:34)

"The everlasting Father" literally means "the Father of eternity," "the planner of the ages," or "the giver of eternal life"—as in Heb. 1:2 (margin); Jn. 3:15-18; 6:54, 68; 10:28; 14:6; 17:2-3; Rom. 5:21; 1 Jn. 1:2; 5:11.

The Son is called this because He died to give eternal life to others, and because through Him the Father planned the ages and gives eternal life. But He's not the *only* person in the Godhead who could be called this—both the Father and the Spirit also give life and had a part in God's plan (Rom. 6:23; 1 Pet. 5:10; Jn. 3:15-18; 15:24, 26; 17:3; Rom. 8:2, 10-11; Gal. 6:8).

The reason the Son is called this in Isa. 9:6-7 is to establish marks of divinity for the coming Son, so people would recognize He's as much God in nature as the Father and Spirit. Without these divine markers in prophecy, no one would have recognized Him as Divine.

But "the everlasting Father" doesn't prove Jesus is the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ in the New Testament. All three persons, by virtue of being God, could be called "the everlasting Father" or "giver of eternal life"—because this term has nothing to do with the Father "begetting" a child. It's about that idiomatic Hebrew usage we discussed.

All three persons couldn't be "the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ," because only one could be the Son and only one could be the Father, as we've already seen.

---

## III. Other Proofs in the Old Testament of Three Persons in the Godhead

### 1. Plural Names, Verbs, and Pronouns

Right from the beginning, God used plural names, verbs, and pronouns when speaking of the Godhead (see Point I.1 above). He's also revealed the Godhead as consisting of several persons, as we'll see below.

Scripture contains many clear statements about two and three persons in the Godhead. In Gen. 3:22, He plainly said, "The man is become as one of us."

The phrase "as one of" means "like each person of several persons of the same kind." You can verify this wherever "as one of" appears in Scripture (Gen. 19:14; 42:27; 49:16; Exod. 12:48; Lev. 19:34; 24:22; Num. 12:12; 2 Sam. 13:13; 14:13; Job 12:4; 13:9; 19:11; 29:25; Ps. 119:162; Mk. 1:22; 6:15; 9:26).

No one could use such a phrase without referring to more than one person who could make "us." If God refers to the Godhead as "us" here and elsewhere, we certainly ought to believe He knows what He's talking about and stop insisting on "one person only." We'll see many more examples in the following points.

### 2. Theophanies—God Appearing to People

The appearances of God to humans prove plurality of persons. Two and three persons called "Lord" and "God" are seen at the same time and place—so the deity must be as many persons as are clearly seen. Each has been seen with a separate body performing separate actions, just like other persons.

> **(1)** God appeared to Abraham in Gen. 18. Then in Gen. 19:24, this "Lord" who was visibly present before Abraham rained fire upon Sodom "from the Lord [another one] out of heaven."

> **(2)** Two persons are seen with human eyes as two separate beings with two separate bodies in Dan. 7:9-14. "The Ancient of Days" is sitting on a throne, and "one like the Son of man" is coming with the clouds of Heaven down to where the other one sits. The one on the throne gives the Son of man a kingdom.
>
> Anyone reading this passage honestly will see two separate persons. The one on the throne had to have a body—otherwise, how could He sit down, wear clothes, or have hair on His head? He's described as a real person just like others. Seven times singular personal pronouns are used of Him. God was actually seen by the prophet—or else the prophet lied.
>
> The literal description proves God is a

lessons.title: Faith — How to Attain to All the Known Needs of Life
lessons.content:
# Faith — How to Attain to All the Known Needs of Life

## I. The Definition and Use of Faith

Let's start with some fascinating numbers. The word "faith" appears only twice in the Old Testament, but shows up 245 times in the New Testament! The word "believe" (with all its forms) occurs 45 times in the Old Testament and 268 times in the New Testament. And "trust" — that's the Old Testament's main word for faith and believe — appears 154 times in the Old Testament and 35 times in the New Testament.

So what do these words actually mean? They carry the idea of confiding in someone so completely that you feel secure without fear. They mean fleeing to someone for refuge, taking shelter in them, staying and resting on them, relying on them completely. It's about believing someone — taking them at their word — and putting absolute trust in a person without any questioning or doubts about their faithfulness.

The Bible gives us its own definition in Heb. 11:1: "The substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen." Various translations help us see the richness of this verse:

> "Now faith is a well-grounded assurance of that for which we hope, and a conviction of the reality of things which we do not see" (Weymouth)

> "Now faith means we are confident of what we hope for, convinced of what we do not see" (Moffatt)

> "Now faith is the title-deed of things hoped for; the putting to proof of things not seen" (Centenary Translation)

> "Now faith is an assumption of what is being expected, a conviction concerning matters which are not being observed" (Concordant Version)

> "Now faith is the persuasion of the things that are in hope, as if they were in act; and it is the manifestness of the things not seen" (The Syriac)

> "Now faith is assurance of things hoped for, a conviction of things not seen" (Revised Version)

Paul describes true faith as an attribute of God Himself in Rom. 4:17 — God "quickeneth the dead, and calleth those things which be not as though they were." Think of it like this: faith is a union of assurance and conviction, counting something done as if it were already accomplished.

Faith doesn't need to see before it believes. It laughs at impossibilities! It laughs at circumstances that seem to contradict it! Faith counts the thing done the moment you ask God for it.

Here's what's remarkable about faith: it's not swayed to believe God only when things seem possible. And it doesn't waver or question when things seem to go contrary to what you've asked. Faith doggedly keeps going — counting the impossible as possible, counting as done what isn't yet seen, and counting things that don't exist yet as though they already do.

## II. What Faith is Not

Faith is not *feeling* that your prayer is answered. The average person who tries to exercise faith depends on what they can see, hear, or feel. Most testimonies about faith are tied up with feelings, emotions, or physical sensations. But sense-faith — faith based on physical evidence or your soul's emotions — will eventually deceive you.

Your faith should be based on God's Word regardless of any sense-knowledge or feeling-evidence. Here's what happens to so many people: they constantly look to their feelings to determine whether prayer has been heard. If they feel good, or if something encouraging happens, they think believing is easy. But when setbacks come and those good feelings disappear? These same people plunge into despair.

They're quick to accuse God of being unfaithful to His Word. Or if they don't go that far, they quickly assume it wasn't God's will to grant the answer. They become satisfied to go without what God has plainly promised.

Let me tell you something liberating: faith has absolutely no relationship with feelings and sense-evidence. At the very moments when you think you have all the faith in the world because of how you feel — that's when you have the least! And when you think your faith is nothing — that's often when you have more than at any other time in your life.

Many people are surprised to get answers to prayer because when they prayed, they thought their faith was worthless. But here's the key: you shouldn't believe you're healed because the pain is gone, or that you're saved because you feel forgiven, or that your prayers are answered because things are working out. Instead, always maintain faith because of what the Word of God says!

God's Word should have first place in your life — not your senses. All basing of faith upon what you've done, how well you live, or what experiences you've had will lead to failure in answered prayer. God doesn't answer on those grounds. He answers solely on the grounds of grace and faith in Him and His Word.

True faith is not trusting in another person's goodness or faith. It must be *personal* faith in God and His Word. People constantly go around seeking someone who has faith, someone who can get answers for them. This might work temporarily while you're learning about God and His Word, but if you don't develop personal faith that refuses to be denied, you'll eventually revert to failure and unbelief. You'll have to settle for the modern theory that answered prayer isn't for everyone.

The program that actually pays off is having personal faith in God, faith in Jesus Christ, faith in the Holy Spirit, faith in the Word, faith in the atonement, and total personal consecration to believe God regardless of anything that might hinder prayer. The right approach isn't talking about faith or the need for it — it's the actual exercise of it!

Simple faith in the Word — regardless of feelings and circumstances — is never possible for someone who lives only in the realm of their senses. Such a person believes only what they can see, feel, hear, or understand to be possible. This was Thomas's kind of faith when he declared he wouldn't believe until he had seen. It was Martha's kind when she could see nothing but the natural fact that Lazarus had been dead four days and "by this time he stinketh." This is the kind of faith modern religious leaders teach and encourage, but it's not what the New Testament requires.

Mental faith — mere assent to truth combined with dependence on feelings — isn't enough either. You must come to life and action before you'll realize the benefits of active, living faith that refuses to know defeat and failure.

You must learn to fight the fight of faith and lay hold of God and His Word. You must understand that you're surrounded by an unbelieving world and an atmosphere of doubt. Demons, fallen angels, and wicked men have lived for centuries in unbelief, creating subtle currents of doubt and mistrust. The effects of the Fall have left deep wounds of doubt and wavering in fallen humanity that must be healed.

You must learn that you have to wrestle with powers of darkness and currents of mistrust and unbelief that make it a struggle to exercise active, living faith for things not yet seen. And you must not only learn how to do this — you must actually do it to get results.

## III. Kinds of Faith

Scripture mentions several kinds of faith. Understanding these will help you identify the kind of faith you should have:

### 1. Common Faith (Titus 1:4)

This is also called "the mutual faith" (Rom. 1:12). It's the faith common to all who claim to be Christians — belief in Christ as Savior and in eternal life, along with a few other commonly accepted truths held more or less alike by all Christians.

The *uncommon* kind is that rare faith in apostolic power and in the fullness of God in believers' lives. Faith in these truths brings controversy because the average church member hasn't been taught that they can have all the benefits of the gospel that early Christians experienced. That all men can have this uncommon faith and power has been made clear in Lessons Twenty and Twenty-two.

### 2. Weak Faith (Rom. 4:19; 14:1-23; 15:1-4; 1 Cor. 8:1-13)

This kind of faith constantly limits personal benefits and privileges in the gospel due to wrong teachings and personal scruples about non-essentials of life. Literally thousands of people spend much of their time arguing and condemning each other over small details that Scripture doesn't even specifically mention — what one may or may not eat, drink, wear, or do and still be a Christian.

The Scriptures above make clear that the kingdom of God doesn't consist of meat, drink, and personal details not specifically forbidden in Scripture. Instead, it is "righteousness, and peace, and joy in the Holy Ghost" (Rom. 14:17).

If the devil can't get you to commit great sins forbidden in Scripture, he'll urge honest-hearted people to go to the extreme and condemn this or that in personal liberties that God hasn't strictly condemned. Multitudes today go to the utter extreme and condemn almost every phase of human living in some degree.

The law of Scripture concerning anything not definitely forbidden by God is this: "He that doubteth is damned if he eat, because he eateth not in faith: for whatsoever is not of faith is sin" (Rom. 14:23).

The Christian law of love to others is:

> "Let us therefore follow after the things which make for peace, and things wherewith one may edify another. For meat destroy not the work of God. All things are pure; but it is evil for that man who eateth with offence. It is good neither to eat flesh, nor to drink wine, nor any thing whereby thy brother stumbleth, or is offended, or is made weak. Hast thou faith? have it to thyself before God. Happy is the man that condemneth not himself in the things which he alloweth . . . We then that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak, and not to please ourselves. Let everyone of us please his neighbor for his good to edification" (Rom. 14:19-22; 15:1-2).

### 3. Strong Faith (Rom. 4:20)

This is the faith that refuses to be defeated. It refuses to take no for an answer! It laughs at circumstances, symptoms, all outward appearances, and what is seen, heard, or felt. It doggedly holds to the fact that what you've asked of God is granted.

Abraham had this kind of faith:

> "Who against hope believed in hope, that he should be the father of many nations, according to that which was spoken, So shall thy seed be. And being not weak in faith, he considered not his own body now dead, when he was about an hundred years old, neither yet the deadness of Sarah's womb [who was about ninety years old]: He staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief; but was strong in faith, giving glory to God; and being fully persuaded that, what he had promised, he was able to perform. And it was counted to him for righteousness" (Rom. 4:18-22).

This is the kind of faith all of us should have — and could have — if we would simply persuade ourselves that God is true to His Word. Most people claim they have faith in God to this extent, and no one wants to say boldly that He's a liar who won't keep His word. But when it comes to actually exercising faith that won't doubt or waver in prayer? Very few will maintain such strong faith and thank God for the answer even before they see it.

Very few will simply refuse to question in the slightest when things seem to go contrary to what they've asked. And that's exactly why they don't get definite answers from God.

There is no person who exercises faith as strong as Abraham's who will go very long without an answer from God. The idea that God would refuse someone who came to Him in such unwavering faith is literally impossible and unheard of (Jas. 1:5-8; Heb. 11:16). All of us are definitely assured of getting from God what He has promised if we will "walk in the steps of that faith of our father Abraham" (Rom. 4:12, 23-25).

### 4. Little Faith (Mt. 6:30; 8:26; 14:31; Lk. 12:28)

This is essentially the same as no faith because it's the wavering kind (Heb. 10:23; Jas. 1:5-8).

### 5. Great Faith (Mt. 8:10; 15:28)

This is true, unwavering faith that will always move God to answer according to His abundant promises (Heb. 11:6).

### 6. Unfeigned Faith (1 Tim. 1:5; 2 Tim. 1:5)

This faith knows no hypocrisy, sham, or counterfeit. It doesn't brag or put on an outward show to prove it's real. It's simply genuine, real, and sincere in every aspect. It proceeds out of a pure heart and from a good conscience, as these Scriptures state.

This faith isn't hereditary, although in 2 Tim. 1:5 Paul speaks of it being in three successive generations. It's the kind of faith all honest, pure, and sincere people have. It's unselfish, holy, and godly — the kind that naturally increases in the life of every true child of God as they grow in grace and knowledge (2 Pet. 1:4-9; 3:18).

### 7. Temporary Faith (Lk. 8:13)

This faith believes for a while and springs up like a mushroom, but because of its shallowness and lack of root, it fails in times of temptation and testing.

People through the ages have been this way. They receive God's Word with great joy, and it seems they're going to outstrip everyone else in faith. But after a few days or weeks, you don't hear from them again — or if you do, it's in another revival meeting where their emotions get temporarily stirred and they make another start in faith, soon to fall again.

They never fully come clean with God or make the full surrender of their lives except for the moment. They don't prepare the soil so the seed can take root and produce fruit.

Some teach this can't happen, but it does — despite the so-called impossibility of falling away from faith and being renewed in repentance. Some teach that one can't fall from the faith, but Jesus certainly said that some would "receive the word with joy; and these have no root, which for a while believe, and in time of temptation fall away" (Mt. 13:20-21; Lk. 8:13).

Paul speaks of these as those who "concerning faith have made shipwreck" (1 Tim. 1:19), as "having cast off their first faith" by going "aside after Satan" (1 Tim. 5:12-15), and as having "erred concerning the faith" (1 Tim. 6:21).

Many Scriptures say that men can depart from and fall from the faith (Acts 14:22; 1 Tim. 2:15; 4:1; 5:8-15; 6:10, 21; 2 Tim. 2:18; Heb. 3:6, 12-14; 6:11-12; 10:22-28). Men are repeatedly told to continue in the faith (Acts 14:22; Col. 1:23; 2:6-7). Anybody with common intelligence knows they can disbelieve anything they choose to lose faith in — especially things that aren't seen.

### 8. Historical Faith (1 Jn. 5:10-13)

This is faith in the history or record of God concerning the past and His work for men. You can believe God's record to the letter and still not be saved. It's simply believing in the record of anything from the past. No consecration to God is necessary to believe history.

### 9. Mental Faith (Jas. 2:14-26)

Mental faith is similar to historical faith in that it believes history, but goes further and believes every part of the Bible — past, present, and future — as well as all the truths about God's blessings. However, it doesn't act upon God's Word. Faith without works is dead, being alone. It's passive faith or mere mental assent to truth.

Thousands of sinners have mental faith in God and the Bible, but they keep neglecting to definitely obey truth.

True faith is not mental assent to truth as a fact. Most people have such assent. They'll believe God is able, that He has promised to do certain things, and that He would act if true faith were exercised. But there's little effort to cooperate with God or get what He's promised. It's merely mental assent to truth without active faith in it.

It's like having faith that will turn to every source of help *except* God, while claiming to believe in God and His Word. It's similar to believing food is good without ever eating to get its benefits. Mental assent — passive faith — is one of the most dangerous enemies of true faith in God. It claims all the faith in the world and is easily satisfied with anything or nothing from God.

If it doesn't get what it wants, it doesn't care. It would accept something from God if He came personally, laid the answer in your lap, and begged you to keep it. But apart from that, there's little effort to act in faith concerning God's promises.

Mental faith may go as far as believing every truth and every part of God's program. It may even claim to be contending for certain benefits — yet it dares not act on God's promises.

### 10. Active Faith (Jas. 2:14-26; Heb. 10:19-38)

This is the kind that acts upon God's Word as it becomes clear. James put it this way: "shew me thy faith without thy works, and I will shew thee my faith by my works." All of us must have this faith to get anything from God.

As long as faith is purely mental and passive, you won't take action to obey truth. You must get beyond the stage of hearing and into actually *doing* what God says before you get results.

Active, living faith moves to obey every truth of God to the letter and to appropriate what God has promised. It acts as if unseen things are reality. It's quick to provide works to prove it's sincere and obedient.

Think of having a real, active, living faith of your own! Imagine the thrill of having your own prayers answered!

As I write, I have a large stack of letters on my desk marked "Testimony." These letters, received in recent months, tell how God has answered prayer. People hear the radio programs and are inspired to take God at His Word. As they act, they receive answers.

One lady said she had been sick for ten long years and hadn't done any housework. She heard me say on the air that if you believe God and refuse to be sick and defeated, you can have whatever you want. She said, "I acted upon the Word and got out of bed, and God met my faith. I have been doing my work ever since."

Another is thrilled that her prayers have been answered three times from laying her hands on the radio and praying with us. Many more are deeply stirred to new life and deeper consecration because they're learning to act upon the Word.

Multitudes have never had an answer to prayer, and they're missing life's greatest blessings by such neglect. Most people depend on others to get answers for them. They pray — as they think — but they want someone else to do the believing. In reality, they're not praying at all. They're merely saying words and going through a form of prayer.

True New Testament praying is always heard by God, and the answer is always sure. God has planned for all of us to have this kind of faith and results. "These signs shall follow them that believe," and "all things are possible to him that believeth" (Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; 16:15-20; Jn. 14:12-15).

Faith can be developed into a mighty living force that knows no defeat! You can know that you are master of all demon powers and circumstances through Jesus Christ. You no longer need to be a slave to fear, timidity, weakness, failure, sickness, poverty, helplessness, and despair. You can be a conqueror in the very areas where you've suffered defeat.

> "If you abide in me, and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you" (Jn. 15:7, 16).

> "He that abideth in me, and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit: for without me ye can do nothing" (Jn. 15:5).

You must awake to the consciousness of your new life in Christ! As a branch, you have His life, His health, His power, His love, and His nature flowing through you. You are the fruit-bearing part of Christ in the Earth. You are in Him and He is in you.

You have His words abiding in you, giving you faith for whatever you ask. You have as much right in Him to ask and receive from God as He had. You have a right to expect the same answers from God that He got. You represent God here as He did — so don't be satisfied to go without the benefits He died to give you. (See Lessons Sixteen and Twenty-two).

You are commanded to be a doer of the Word — and that means ask and receive! You're not merely to be a hearer. You're not deceiving yourself when you act on the Word. It will be confirmed because it is truth. It must be a living, active faith, not a mere mental passive something that even sinners can have.

You are in Christ, and as God's child and heir, you have the authority to act on the Word. You are a fruit-bearer, a producer for God — so get busy and produce for Him according to His Word!

### 11. Wavering Faith (Jas. 1:5-8)

This is faith that doubts God and refuses to believe. To truly believe and have faith is to act on the Word. It means taking what is already yours.

To believe on Jesus means taking Him for all that the Bible declares Him to be. It means taking salvation from sin, healing for the body, answers to your prayers, and all that He died to bring you.

Believing is an act of the will. When you really believe, you have acted. You've taken the necessary step to get what you want from God. Faith is action — counting things that don't exist as though they do (Rom. 4:17; Mk. 11:22-24).

Doubting is refusing to act on the Word. Unbelief is either refusing to act according to the knowledge you have, or it's a manifestation of ignorance of God's Word. If you don't know, you can't act because you don't understand. If you don't understand, you're afraid to act because you don't know how. The cure for all unbelief is a thorough knowledge of the Word and consecration to obey it to the letter, regardless of how impossible it may seem at the moment.

Wavering faith is called double-mindedness — a constant change of mind about what you want or whether you even want it. It's an attitude of yes—no, yes—no, yes—no, until God Himself can't tell whether it's yes or no!

One minute you decide something is wanted, maybe even with real desperation, but the next minute it's not so important. People with wavering faith don't really make up their minds to see the fight of faith through to an answer. They'd accept the answer if it came without any hesitation or effort on their part — but taking the necessary steps to get an answer? Fighting even a little for what they've asked? That's another question entirely.

### 12. Unwavering Faith (Heb. 10:23; 11:6)

This is faith taking God at His Word without any question. God commands us:

> "Ask in faith, nothing wavering. For he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed. For let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord. A doubleminded man is unstable in all his ways" (Jas. 1:5-8).

We're told:

> "Hold fast the profession of our faith without wavering; (for he is faithful that promised)" (Heb. 10:23).

And again:

> "But without faith it is impossible to please him: for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him" (Heb. 11:6).

This is faith refusing to doubt, wonder, question, or reason about any detail of what you've asked God for. It has absolute confidence that what was asked is according to God's Word, and therefore it *knows* prayer is answered.

It rests upon the promises as the basis of the answer instead of upon feelings and things seen. It refuses to act contrary to what it has asked or to question any delay in the answer. It counts the thing done regardless of all outward evidence to the contrary.

It laughs at impossibilities and goes on in utmost confidence that what God has promised, He is able to perform. It gives thanks for the answer from the moment it asks and looks forward with childlike expectation of receiving it.

### 13. Human Faith (Mk. 11:22-24)

This is simply the exercise of your human faculties in having confidence and conviction that you can believe God and that God is true to all He has promised. All of us are capable of faith in ourselves and faith in anyone else who makes a promise to us.

Faith is an attribute of your created being. It was natural for man to believe God before the Fall. There was no such thing as doubt, questioning, or unbelieving reasoning before that time.

It was the devil who injected doubt and unbelief into mankind's moral and spiritual makeup. And since humanity's Fall — because of the near-total depravity of our being — it has become one of the greatest struggles of our redemptive journey to have that same simple, unwavering faith that was natural before the Fall.

It was doubt and unbelief that caused the Fall, and it's of the greatest importance to get rid of all such in redemption.

### 14. Divine Faith (Heb. 11:3; Gal. 2:20; 1 Cor. 13:14)

Faith isn't only a natural attribute of man — it's also an attribute of God. It's God's absolute confidence and conviction in His own Being and Word.

Heb. 11:3 teaches that the ages were planned by faith through God's Word, and things were brought into existence that never existed before. God had faith in Himself when He planned the worlds! He believed He could do what He commanded, and He counted things that weren't as though they were (Rom. 4:17).

Gal. 2:20 speaks of "the faith of the Son of God," proving that Christ also has faith as the Father does. In fact, He is the author and finisher of faith (Heb. 12:1-2).
