And 1 Cor. 13:14 speaks of "faith" abiding along with hope and love — meaning these three qualities are eternal attributes of God.

We will have to exercise faith in the infinite throughout eternity, for there will always be the need for confidence in God and His eternal plan concerning matters the finite hasn't yet comprehended of the infinite (Isa. 32:17). If faith will be necessary in eternity, how much more is it needed now when we don't know as we're also known!

This life is the probationary period of our eternal existence, and it's necessary to learn faith and obedience to carry out God's plan for us both now and forever. Faith is acting on God's Word, and this will be necessary throughout eternity because God will eternally give His Word to the saved and make known His will concerning all things to come.

So it's clear: God has faith, Christ has faith, the Holy Spirit has faith, angels have faith, demons have faith (Jas. 2:11), and we must have faith to please God (Heb. 11:6).

Faith is absolutely necessary in carrying on moral government. If moral agents can't have faith in the Moral Governor of the universe, they won't trust Him. Lack of faith on the part of subjects in human government tends to create anarchy and rebellion. When doubt, questioning, unbelief, dissatisfaction, and lack of confidence show up in subjects of any government, it cannot long endure.

Faith is an absolute necessity. Childlike faith and confidence in God — with utter abandonment of self-interest, trusting Him to leave everything in His hands for the common good of all — is not only demanded by God but is the only reasonable attitude for us to take.

The history of mankind, angels, and demons proves that God has been good, merciful, loving, kind, and consecrated to the best good of all creation. Not one person in all of moral creation can point a finger of accusation that God has dealt with them unjustly. No one today can testify that God has been unfaithful if they've done His will and conformed to His Word in faith.

Not one free moral agent can accuse God of the least degree of unfaithfulness in any dealing. Not one will be able throughout eternity to accuse Him of being a respecter of persons. God has been and always will be good to all.

He longs to bless everyone today! He will heal every disease, save from every sin and bad habit, deliver from failure and poverty, and answer every prayer prayed in faith in the name of Jesus Christ. He longs to do what He has promised. He desires to supply all our needs.

But He will not break His own law by blessing those who don't have faith. He has laid down plain laws of faith whereby anyone can get what they want in life, and He will hold us to obedience to these laws before granting our desires.

If you want the benefits, then intelligently obey God's laws to the letter — and by doing so, you'll get what He has promised. If the benefits aren't worth the price, then don't complain that you're not supplied. If they *are* worth the simple effort to get them as God has made clear, then don't doubt or waver one moment about the outcome!

It's already settled that the benefits are guaranteed and sure if true, simple, childlike faith is exercised. There's no longer any question about this if we believe the Bible — so let it be settled forever.

## IV. The Importance of Faith

The Bible definitely declares that faith is all-important:

> "But without faith it is impossible to please him: for he that cometh to God must believe that he is, and that he is a rewarder of them that diligently seek him" (Heb. 11:6)

> "Now faith is the substance of things hoped for, the evidence of things not seen" (Heb. 11:1)

> "But let him ask in faith, nothing wavering. For he that wavereth is like a wave of the sea driven with the wind and tossed. For let not that man think that he shall receive any thing of the Lord" (Jas. 1:5-8)

> "Whatsoever is not of faith is sin" (Rom. 14:23)

> "Above all, taking the shield of faith, wherewith ye may be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked" (Eph. 6:16)

> "Cast not away therefore your confidence, which hath great recompense of reward . . . Now the just shall live by faith: but if any man draw back, my soul shall have no pleasure in him" (Heb. 10:35-39)

> "And all things, whatsoever ye shall ask in prayer, believing ye shall receive" (Mt. 21:21)

> "If thou canst believe, all things are possible to him that believeth" (Mk. 9:23)

> "Have faith in God. For verily I say unto you, That whosoever shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea, and shall not doubt in his heart, but shall believe that those things which he saith shall come to pass; he shall have whatsoever he saith. Therefore, I say unto you, What things soever ye desire, when ye pray, believe that ye receive them, and ye shall have them" (Mk. 11:22-24)

These scriptures need no interpretation. They plainly promise all of us that we can get whatever we have faith for. There are no limitations or qualifications concerning known needs of this life or the life to come — so don't limit them!

They're clear that faith is absolutely necessary to get what you desire in life. No one should expect to get anything from God if they refuse to have faith. There's no such thing as the impossibility of having faith, so if you don't have faith, it's your own choice and responsibility. Jesus *commanded* us to "Have faith in God" — and such is possible, or it wouldn't be mandatory.

It's all-important to have faith because:

- No prayer will be answered without it
- God cannot be pleased without it
- You won't get anything from God without it
- You'll be disobedient to God without it

If faith is commanded, then not having faith is breaking God's law and committing sin. If we believers realized this, we'd become desperate about this law-breaking just as we are about breaking other laws of God!

If we could only realize that unbelief and doubting God is *sin*, we'd at least be moved to action against such law-breaking. The trouble has been that most people consider unbelief and doubt as part of human nature — something to be expected, not changed. The attitude has been indifference. When it's easy to believe, there's no problem. But when believing becomes a struggle, it's passed off as something that can't be helped.

This is where the devil has gained the upper hand. He makes people think that not everyone can believe God — and that if you can't, it's perfectly fine to live in unbelief and doubt all you please.

If prayer isn't answered, most people take for granted that it's not God's will to grant an answer. They're quickly turned aside from the purpose of getting an answer and are satisfied to be defeated. Blame for such defeat is laid on God and considered His will without any question.

But the truth is that this attitude is surrender to the devil and evil spirit-forces who oppose answered prayer! It's so habitual for the average Christian to be defeated that they give it little thought. It's taken for granted when offering prayer that not getting an answer will be all right, and that this "reveals" God's will not to answer.

This condition has been created by wrong teachings in the churches for

lessons.title: The New Testament Church
lessons.content:
# The New Testament Church (Mt. 16:18)

The doctrine of the Church is clearly laid out in hundreds of Scriptures. Everything you need to know about the subject is fully stated in the Bible, so there's really no need for different theories about any aspect of Church doctrine — if we're simply willing to take exactly what Scripture says on every point.

The words "church" and "churches" appear 108 times in the New Testament. (See Lesson Eighteen, Point II.) You won't find them once in the Old Testament. All the truths about the Church — its organization, government, officers, discipline, ordinances, and other important questions — we'll cover in the studies that follow.

## I. What the New Testament Church is Not

> 1. It is not any one or all the denominations and independent parts of Christendom.

> 2. It is not the continuation of the Old Testament Church of Lesson Eighteen.

> 3. It is not the beginning of the eternal kingdom of Christ and of God.

> 4. It is not the visible part of this kingdom in the world, through which God is trying to rule the nations, as taught by some.

> 5. It is not the kingdom of Heaven and the kingdom of God, and it is never confused with them in Scripture.

We are "heirs" of the kingdom (Rom. 8:17; Jas. 2:5), but not of the Church. Think of it this way: you belong to the Church — you can't inherit yourself! But you will inherit the kingdom.

We are "children of the kingdom" (Mt. 8:11-12), but not of the Church. The kingdom is yet to come (Mt. 6:10; Rev. 11:15), but the Church is here now. We will receive the kingdom (Heb. 12:28), but not the Church.

Also, the Church is a new mystery revealed (Eph. 3), while the kingdom has been the subject of the prophets for ages past (Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14; Isa. 9:6-7; etc.). These and many other points of contrast between the Church and the kingdom prove that the Church is not the same as the kingdom.

## II. What the New Testament Church Is

The New Testament Church is a "called out" body of people from all nations who have been born-again, lived a true Christian life, and died in the faith. It also includes all those who are alive in Christ today. This present Church is the *ekklesia* of the New Testament times — called out of the world to evangelize the world in this age. (See the definition and usage of *ekklesia* in Lesson Eighteen.)

The Church, in its largest sense, is the whole company of the redeemed in all ages, in Heaven and on Earth (Eph. 3:1-11; Heb. 12:23). It's the spiritual people who have been made members of the universal family of God.

The Church in this sense is the body of Christ, and it doesn't consist of saved and unsaved mixed together. It's not merely a group of individuals associated for social and benevolent purposes, as seems to be the idea on every hand. It's the body of people indwelt by God through the Holy Spirit — and through whom God works in the world.

The Bible teaches one universal Church and many local churches in which the universal Church takes local and temporal form. It also teaches a spiritual and invisible Church and many visible and material churches through which God is made known to the world.

A local church may be defined as "a company of redeemed people in any locality or community, who unite themselves voluntarily together as a part of the universal Church for the purpose of establishing themselves in the faith of the gospel and to evangelize their community and to propagate the gospel to the ends of the Earth."

Local churches are mentioned many times in Scripture (Mt. 18:17; Acts 14:23; Rom. 16:5; 1 Cor. 1:2; 4:17; 16:19; Phil. 4:15; Col. 4:15; 1 Pet. 5:13; Rev. 1:11; 2:1, 8, 12, 18; 3:1, 7). A local church may consist of as few as two or three people (Mt. 18:15-20).

Many human organizations that claim to be Christian are not churches. Any organization that is instituted and managed solely by man, that permits unsaved men in it, that has no definite form of doctrine according to the Scriptures, that does not have the Church laws and ordinances, and that does not follow the whole Bible as the rule of faith and practice — cannot be a Church. Such organizations may be good in supplementing the work of the Church, but they cannot be classified as churches.

The laws of the Bible through which believers unite themselves into churches may be summarized as follows:

> 1. The sole authority of the entire Bible, rightly divided, as the rule of faith and practice in life and conduct.

> 2. The new birth and genuine Christian living as necessary to church membership.

> 3. Willful obedience to the Bible in all of its ordinances, doctrines, and laws.

> 4. Consecration to the will of God and to the end to which He is consecrated.

> 5. The right of each member of the church to a voice in government and discipline (Mt. 18:15-20; 1 Cor. 5:1-7; Rev. 2-3).

> 6. The freedom of the conscience and personal responsibility to God.

> 7. Total independence of the church and state from each other.

The Church is not an organization based on the common brotherhood of man and resting upon humanitarian principles only. It's a supernatural body of believers filled with the presence and power of God as individuals conform to the gospel of Christ.

The Church is like a family in its social life, yet it's unlike a family in its formation. Each local church is a part of the universal Church in the broad sense. However, there are many in local churches that don't belong to the body of Christ, and therefore they are not a true part of the universal Church of the New Testament.

The following terms used of the Church in the New Testament reveal in more detail what the Church really is:

## III. Names and Appellations of the New Testament Church

Under this heading we'll list the 105 names of the Church under three main points, and deal with each group in its relation to God, to the saved, and to the world.

### 1. THE CHURCH AS "THE BODY OF CHRIST."

The Church is referred to by this term several times in various ways (1 Cor. 10:16; 12:27; Eph. 4:12). This truth is otherwise expressed in Scripture by the following terms: "one body" (Rom. 12:4-5; 1 Cor. 10:17; 12:12-13, 20; Eph. 2:16; 4:4; Col. 3:15); "the body" (1 Cor. 12:14-16, 18, 19, 22-25; Eph. 4:16; 5:23); "the whole body" (1 Cor. 12:17; Eph. 4:16); "his body" (Eph. 1:23; 5:30); "same body" (Eph. 3:6); "the body, the church" (Col. 1:18); "his body's sake, which is the church" (Col. 1:24); and "all the body" (Col. 2:19).

All these passages clearly show that all saved men are members of the body of Christ, which is the Church of the New Testament. In this sense — with all the saved being members of Christ — the Church is called "the Christ." Picture it this way: the Church as the body of Christ, and Christ as the head of the body, together make the complete Christ (1 Cor. 12:12).

This relationship speaks of the union of the saved with Christ. They are in Him and He is in them (2 Cor. 5:17; Col. 1:27; 2:10).

The Church is also called "one new man" (Eph. 2:15) and "a perfect man" (Eph. 4:13), made up of both Jews and Gentiles (Eph. 2:10-22; 3:1-6; 1 Cor. 12:12-13). These two terms beautifully convey the idea of Christ as the head of the body, the Church. The Church and Christ are so united as to be one body of people. Paul expressed it this way: "But he that is joined unto the Lord is one Spirit" (1 Cor. 6:17).

The "one new man" and the one "perfect man" is made up of only new and perfect men in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17; Phil. 3:15). It's true that not everyone in Christ is as mature and perfect in development as they will be if they continue to grow in grace and knowledge. But everyone in Christ is a new creature and is perfect as far as being cleansed and free from committing the sins that will damn the soul — and they will always be that way if they walk in Christ as they have received Him (Col. 2:6-7; Gal. 5:16-26; Rom. 8:1-13; 2 Cor. 5:17; 1 Jn. 2:29; 2:5-10; 5:1-4, 18).

It's generally held by Bible students that the Church is called a woman, a virgin, a lady, and a bride, and that feminine pronouns are always used in referring to the Church — but just the opposite is taught in Scripture. In no Scripture is the Church referred to by a feminine pronoun and as a virgin or a woman, married or unmarried.

Why should the Church be called a woman when it is the body of Christ? Was Christ a woman or a man? If He is a man, then why shouldn't the Church be called a "man" instead of a woman? This would be the only natural and scriptural analogy. (See Supplement Eight, Questions 1 and 2. We'll deal fully with the bride of Christ in Lesson Fifty-two.)

The members of the body of Christ have a threefold relationship to each other. They are "organically related" (1 Cor. 12:12-27; Rom. 12; Eph. 4); "co-operatively related" (1 Cor. 12:21-23; Eph. 4); and "sympathetically related" (1 Cor. 12:24-26; Rom. 12). They are related to Christ as being His body and members of His flesh and of His bones (Eph. 5:30). Christ is the head of the body in all things (Eph. 1:20-22; 5:23-27; Col. 1:18, 24; Eph. 4:7-11).

The members of the body of Christ are called "babes in Christ" (1 Cor. 3:1-3). All saved men are like this in the initial stage of the Christian experience, until they learn to discern between both good and evil and live according to that knowledge (Heb. 5:11-14; 1 Pet. 2:2).

They are called "the branches" (Jn. 15:1-10). All men in Christ are branches in Him who is the true vine and the root and fatness of the olive tree (Rom. 11:13-25). They are called "my friends" because of the close relationship of Christ and His "branches" (Jn. 15:15). As "branches" in the vine they are called "members of Christ" (1 Cor. 6:15; Eph. 5:30). They are also called "the ambassadors of Christ" and His "witnesses" (Acts 1:8). They are also spoken of as "the epistle of Christ" (2 Cor. 3:15).

The body of Christ is called "The church of God" (Acts 20:28; 1 Cor. 1:2; 10:32; 11:22; 15:9; 2 Cor. 1:1; Gal. 1:13; 1 Tim. 3:15) and "the church of the living God" (1 Tim. 3:15). It's called this because it belongs to God. Even as Christ belongs to God, so His Church would also be God's (1 Cor. 3:21-23; 11:3).

It is called "the pillar and stay of truth'' because it upholds the truth of God in the world (1 Tim. 3:15). The one great truth that the Church upholds in the universe is the headship of God (1 Cor. 11:3; Eph. 1:10; Phil. 2:11; Col. 1:15-19). This is the truth that Lucifer did not abide in (Jn. 8:44; Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17). He is now denying it (1 Jn. 2:18-23).

Failure to uphold the headship of God is what caused the Fall; so the redeemed are now given the responsibility to uphold it before men by an actual demonstration of it (Eph. 3:10; 4:11-16; 5:24; Heb. 13:7, 17, 24; Phil. 2:1-4; 1 Pet. 5:1-6).

The history of the devil and fallen angels and men reveals one effort after another to destroy the sovereignty of God and subjection to proper authority. The great struggle in the world today is that of rebelling against law and order. Rebellion is manifest among angels and men, including husbands and wives, parents and children, masters and servants, pastors and people, and rulers and subjects. The Church is called to be a stabilizing influence among all classes of people and even to demonstrate to the angels the manifold wisdom of God by an example of submission to God and to His law (Eph. 3:8-11).

The body of Christ is called by Jesus "my church" (Mt. 16:18). It's made up of people who belong to Jesus as well as to God. Those who are truly saved recognize both the Father and the Son. Those who deny the Son cannot have the Father, for He is the way to God (Jn. 14:1-14; 1 Jn. 2:22-25). The Church belongs to Jesus because He purchased it with His own blood and redeemed them to God (Acts 20:28; 1 Cor. 6:20; 1 Pet. 1:18-23).

The body of Christ is called "the church" in many passages (Mt. 18:17; Acts 2:47; 5:11; 8:1-5; 12:1-5; Eph. 1:22; 5:23-32; Col. 1:18, 24). Local churches are referred to as "the church" in a certain locality (Acts 11:22-26; 13:1; 14:27; Jas. 5:14).

Many local churches are mentioned in Scripture: "the church that is in their house" (Rom. 16:5; 1 Cor. 16:19; Col. 4:15; Phlm. 2), "the church of the Thessalonians" (1 Thess. 1:1; 2 Thess. 1:1), "the church that is at Babylon" (1 Pet. 5:13) "the church of Ephesus" (Rev. 2:1), "the church in Smyrna" (Rev. 2:8), "the church in Pergamos" (Rev. 2:12), "the church in Thyatira" (Rev. 2:18), "the church in Sardis" (Rev. 3:1), "the church in Philadelphia" (Rev. 3:7), and "the church of the Laodiceans" (Col. 4:16; Rev. 3:14).

Terms like the following are used in Scripture of the universal Church and of local churches: "every church" (Acts 14:23; 1 Cor. 4:17; Phil. 4:15), "the whole church" (Acts 15:22; Rom. 16:23; 1 Cor. 14:23), and "assembly" (Jas. 2:2).

All local churches on Earth make the visible and universal Church, and all truly saved men in all local churches make the invisible body of Christ. Universal terms are used of all local churches such as "the churches of God" (1 Cor. 11:16; 1 Thess. 2:14; 2 Thess. 1:4), "the churches of the Gentiles" (Rom. 16:16), "the churches of Christ" (Rom. 16:16), "the churches" (Acts 9:31; 15:41; 16:5; 19:37; 1 Cor. 14:34; 2 Cor. 8:19-24; Rev. 2:7, 11, 17, 29; 3:6, 13, 22; 22:16), "all churches" (1 Cor. 7:17; 2 Cor. 8:18; 11:28), and "all churches of the saints" (1 Cor. 14:33).

Sectional churches are referred to such as "the churches of Galatia" (1 Cor. 16:1; Gal. 1:2), "the churches of Asia" (1 Cor. 16:19), "the churches of Macedonia" (2 Cor. 8:1), "other churches" (2 Cor. 11:8; 12:13), "the churches of Judea" (Gal. 1:22), and "the seven churches which are in Asia" (Rev. 1:4, 11, 20).

The body of Christ is also called "the church of the firstborn" (Heb. 12:23), because it belongs to Christ, Who is "the firstborn of every creature . . . the first-begotten of the dead . . . the firstborn among many brethren" (Col. 1:15-18; Rom. 8:29; Rev. 1:5). It is also called "the general assembly" (Heb. 12:23). This term refers to the redeemed of all ages who will inhabit the Holy City, the New Jerusalem, which is called "the bride, the Lamb's wife" (Rev. 21:9-10).

### 2. THE BODY OF CHRIST, THE CHURCH, AS THE DWELLING PLACE OF GOD AMONG MEN.

The New Testament speaks of the Church as follows: "the house of God" (1 Tim. 3:15), "the house" (Mt. 10:25; 2 Tim. 2:20-24; Heb. 3:6) "an habitation of God" (Eph. 2:22), "an holy temple" (Eph. 2:21), "a spiritual house" (1 Pet. 2:5), "the building" (Eph. 2:21; 1 Cor. 3:9), "God's building" (1 Cor. 3:9), "lively stones" (1 Pet. 2:5), "the temple of God" (1 Cor. 3:16), "the temple of the Living God" (2 Cor. 6:16), and the "house" of Christ (Heb. 3:6).

All these passages teach that God dwells in men and that they are the outward manifestations of divinity on Earth. It's similar to how an embassy represents a foreign nation on another country's soil — saved men are demonstrators of God and His power among the unsaved. They are to be God's representatives to bring the knowledge of God to all nations.

Scriptures speak of God dwelling in men (Jn. 14:23; 2 Cor. 6:14-18; Rom. 8:9; 1 Cor. 3:16-20), but this must be understood in the sense of union with men to a common end, as defined in Lesson Four, 1, 4.

### 3. THE BODY OF CHRIST, THE CHURCH, AS "THE HOUSEHOLD OF GOD."

The New Testament speaks of the Church as "the household of God" (Eph. 2:19); "the household of faith" (Gal. 6:10); "children of God" (Mt. 5:9); "the children of the kingdom" (Mt. 13:38); "firstfruits of his creatures" (Jas. 1:18); "sons of God" and part of "the whole family" of God (Rom. 8:14; 1 Jn. 3:1; Eph. 3:15); "the elect of God" (Rom. 8:33; Titus 1:1); "the called of God" (Rom. 8:28; 1 Cor. 1:9, 24-26; Eph. 4:1-4; Col. 3:15); "a chosen generation" (1 Pet. 2:9); "the flock of God" (1 Pet. 5:2-3); "the flock" (Mt. 26:31; Lk. 12:32; Acts 20:28-29); "one fold" (Jn. 10:16); "the sheep" (Mt. 26:31; Jn. 10:1-18, 26-27; Heb. 13:20; 1 Pet. 2:25); and "my lambs" (Jn. 21:15).

As God's people, the members of the body of Christ are called "the sect of the Nazarenes" (Acts 24:5; Mt. 2:23; Jn. 1:45-46); "Christians" (Acts 11:26; 26:28; 1 Pet. 4:16); "saints" (60 times, Acts 9:13, 32, 41; 26:10; Rom. 1:7; 8:27; 12:13); "believers" (Acts 5:14; 1 Tim. 4:12); "beloved of God" (Rom. 1:7; 2 Thess. 2:13); "holy brethren" (Heb. 3:1); the true "circumcision" (Phil. 3:3; Rom. 2:29; Col. 2:11-17); "fellow-citizens" (Eph. 2:19); "disciples" (Mt. 5:1; 10:1; Jn. 20:19); "the faithful" (Eph. 1:1; Col. 1:2); "followers of God" (Eph. 5:1); "heirs of God" (Rom. 8:17); "God's husbandry" (1 Cor. 3:9); "God's heritage" (1 Pet. 5:3); "kings and priests" (Rev. 1:6; 5:10); "a royal priesthood" (1 Pet. 2:9); "a peculiar people" (1 Pet. 2:9); "an holy priesthood" (1 Pet. 2:5); "vessels of mercy" (Rom. 9:23); "vessels of honor" (2 Tim. 2:21); "wise men" (1 Cor. 10:15); "worshippers" (Heb. 10:2); "workers" (2 Cor. 6:1); "a sweet savour of Christ" (2 Cor. 2:15); "the righteousness of God" (2 Cor. 5:21); "ministers of God" (2 Cor. 6:4); "the salt of the earth" (Mt. 5:13); "one bread" (1 Cor. 10:17); and "the light of the world" (Mt. 5:14).

These many passages not only reveal what saved men are called, but they also make clear the true character of believers. They are all Christians, meaning "Christ-like" — not only in word but in deed and in truth. They are all holy, faithful to the truth, and followers of God according to the gospel, and when they cease to be this they no longer are part of the family of God. Just plain common sense proves this as well as all Scripture, as we have seen in Lesson Nineteen.

When they cease being like Christ they are no longer Christians. When they cease being holy they are unholy (1 Jn. 3:8-10). When they cease to keep God's laws they break them and incur the death penalty (Ezek. 18:4; Rom. 8:1-13). When they cease to be followers of God they become followers of the devil (1 Jn. 3:8). When they cease being a peculiar people they are like the world, and the world loves them (Jn. 15:18-20). And when they transgress the law of God they are plain sinners like all others who break the law (Jas. 5:19-20; Gal. 5:19-21).

Believers are compared to a human body, a man, a house, a building, a temple, a household, a pillar, a flock, a sheep-fold, sheep, lambs, babes, branches, vessels, salt, and lights, and other symbols. How little do modern men realize that they are called to manifest certain characteristics of all these symbols! The many lessons that can be given to men by these figures are too numerous to mention in this short study of the body of Christ.

Anyone who is open to truth can see from these expressions found in Scripture that any attempt to make any one local church or denomination the New Testament Church is unscriptural. Each of these expressions has a different phase of truth to express about the Church on Earth. Some of these names, titles, and designations apply to the body of Christ in general and some to only one part of it in particular, as represented in the local churches.

Local churches in the world today have a right to call themselves by any of these names if the name applies to that particular body of believers, or any group of churches can go by any one of these names if the churches are qualified to live up to the name they choose to go by.

## IV. The Calling and Purpose of the New Testament Church

The present Church is called out of all nations to do the same work of evangelizing the world that Israel and others were called for in other ages. Its calling is heavenly, while Israel's calling is earthly; its blessings are mainly spiritual, while Israel's are mainly earthly.

The Church is to become a heavenly people while Israel will always be an earthly people, except those who die in Christ before the final and eternal restoration of Israel to her original calling.

The mission of the Church during its earthly pilgrimage is to bring men to the knowledge of the truth, to learn the art of submission, and to prepare for eternal service as rulers over earthly nations forever. It is not to be an outward organization only, but a living organism. It is not to be a social club, a place of amusement, or a house of merchandise.

Its members must be "lights" to shine (Mt. 5:14; Phil. 2:15); "salt" to preserve (Mt. 5:13); "evangels" to preach (Mk. 16:15-20); "examples" to live like Christ (Col. 1:26-27); "ambassadors" to represent the kingdom of God (2 Cor. 5:20); "pillars" to uphold the truth of God (1 Tim. 3:15); "members" of the invisible body of Christ to form a habitation for God (Eph. 2:19-22); and men who seek to meet all the above-stated qualifications of true members of the body of Christ and who do His whole will.

## V. The Formation of the New Testament Church

The body of Christ is being formed or built by the Spirit upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ Himself being the chief cornerstone (Eph. 2:19-22). Of course, Christ is the builder, but the Spirit is the main agent in the making of the body (Mt. 16:18; 1 Cor. 12:13).

The Spirit uses other agencies, such as the different ministries, to form the body of Christ (1 Cor. 12:1-28; Eph. 4:7-11). Each person who repents is re-created by the Spirit and made a member of that body (1 Cor. 12:12-13).

When a person is born-again he is saved and redeemed from all sin and made a new creature in Christ (1 Jn. 2:29; 3:5-10; 5:1-4, 18). He is baptized into Christ and into His body by the agency of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor. 12:12-13; Gal. 3:26-29), as we shall see more fully in Lesson Thirty-four.

## VI. The Origin of the New Testament Church

The Church had its origin in the mind of God "before the foundation of the world" (Eph. 1:3-5). It did not become a reality until the earthly ministry of Christ. Since then it has been in the building and will not be complete until it is presented to Christ (Eph. 5:25-27; 1 Thess. 4:13-18; 1 Cor. 15:51-58).

The Church then is older in election than the kingdom prepared "since the foundation of the world" (Mt. 25:34). Christ first announced the building of the Church. It is now being built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ Himself being the chief cornerstone (Eph. 2:19-22; 1 Cor. 3:11).

## VII. Who Belongs to the New Testament Church?

The people saved from the ministry of Christ to the rapture of the Church belong to the present Church. The Old Testament saints could not belong to the present, called-out body of people for the reason that they did not live during this time. For this same reason the Church now could not have belonged to any company of saints in the Old Testament times.

The 144,000 Jews who are to be sealed after the rapture of the Church could not belong to the present Church, for they are saved and sealed after the complete company of this period is caught out of the world (Rev. 7:1-8; 14:1-5). The future tribulation-saints do not belong to the present company of saints because they are saved and martyred after the rapture of the Church (Rev. 7:9-17; 15:2-4; 20:4).

All these different companies of redeemed will have their respective places in God's future plan as each individual is rewarded at the Judgment Seat of Christ according to the deeds done in the body, whether they be good or bad. All of them will finally be united in one family living in the New Jerusalem, the bride of Christ, as will be seen in Lesson Fifty-two. (See Chapter Nine, Point 3, and Chapter Forty-five of my book *Revelation Expounded*.)

## VIII. Was the Church Founded on the Day of Pentecost?

This is the general conception, but it's not true. The Church really began with the ministry of Christ, for He is the one who founded the Church (Mt. 16:18). He is the head of it (Eph. 1:21-23).

The law and the prophets were until John; since then the kingdom of God is preached and all men press into it (Lk. 16:16). God hath "set some in the Church, first apostles" (1 Cor. 12:28; Eph. 2:19; 4:11). When did He set apostles in the Church? Certainly before Pentecost (Mt. 10; Acts 1:15-26).

What is the Church? It is a "called-out" body of people from the world. Weren't believers before Pentecost "called-out" of the world, and weren't they just as much followers of Christ before as after Pentecost? (Jn. 17).

The apostles were the first chosen and "called out" (Mt. 10), then seventy others (Lk. 10) and later many others were chosen (Acts 1:12-15; 1 Cor. 15:6). All of this selecting was done before Pentecost.

What took place at Pentecost was the enduement of power upon the newly formed Church (Jn. 14:16, 17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15; Lk. 24:49; Acts 1:4-8; 2:1-47). Think of it like this: the Church already existed, but at Pentecost it received its power source — like a building that's already constructed but then gets its electricity turned on.

At Pentecost 3,000 more were added to the called-out people, the Church (Acts 2:40-46). A few days later 5,000 more were added (Acts 4:4). These additions still continue and will continue until Christ comes in the air to take away the New Testament Church, together with the redeemed of all ages past. Then this particular called-out body of people will

lessons.title: The Truth About the Baptism of the Holy Spirit
lessons.content:
# The Truth About the Baptism of the Holy Spirit

The baptism in the Holy Spirit is honestly one of the most misunderstood topics in the entire Bible. Christians everywhere are divided on it, which is exactly why every thoughtful believer should dig into Scripture with an open mind, paying careful attention to everything God's Word says about it.

Let's walk through what the Bible actually teaches about this important subject.

## I. General Facts About the Spirit Baptism

> **1.** There really is a baptism in the Holy Spirit — this isn't something people made up (Mt. 3:11; 20:22-23; Mk. 1:8; Lk. 3:16; Jn. 1:31-34; Acts 1:4-8; 2:1-39; 8:14-20; 9:17; 10:44-48; 11:14-18; 15:7-11; 19:1-7; Gal. 3:14; Heb. 6:2).

> **2.** Jesus Himself was the first person to be baptized in the Holy Spirit (Mt. 3:16-17; 20:22-23; Lk. 3:21-22; 4:16-21; Jn. 1:31-34; 3:34; Acts 10:38).

> **3.** This baptism fulfilled Old Testament prophecy (Isa. 11:2; 42:1-7; 61:1-2; Mt. 11:2-6; 12:18; Lk. 4:16-21).

> **4.** Regular people weren't baptized in the Spirit until Pentecost — and that couldn't happen until after Jesus was glorified (Jn. 7:37-39; 14:16-17, 26; 16:13-15; Acts 1:4-8; 2:1-21, 33-39).

> **5.** Now here's something important to understand: even though people weren't baptized in the Spirit until Pentecost, they absolutely did receive the Holy Spirit in their lives in various ways throughout all of history up to that point.
>
> Old Testament believers — including Jesus's own disciples before Pentecost — experienced all kinds of spiritual blessings. They had physical and material blessings from God. They bore the fruit of the Spirit. They operated in the gifts of the Spirit (except tongues and interpretation). They experienced different measures of the Spirit, the new birth, sanctification, justification, and countless other benefits of the gospel, including power to witness.
>
> They were "filled" with the Spirit. They had the Spirit "in" and "upon" them. They were anointed with the Spirit and given power in varying degrees. And yet — they were never baptized in the Spirit until Pentecost came. For proof of all this, see Lesson Eighteen, Point IX.
>
> Think about John the Baptist. He was "filled" with the Spirit from birth, but he was never baptized in the Spirit (Lk. 1:15-17). Mary was "filled" with the Spirit before Jesus was even born, but she wasn't baptized in the Spirit until thirty-three years later at Pentecost (Lk. 1:41-56; Acts 1:12–2:4). Others were "filled" with the Spirit before Pentecost too, but that wasn't the same as being baptized in the Spirit (Lk. 1:41-67; 2:25-38).
>
> Even Jesus Himself was "filled" with the Spirit from birth (Isa. 50:4-5; Lk. 2:40, 52), but He wasn't baptized in the Spirit until He was about thirty years old (Mt. 3:16-17; 20:22-23; Jn. 3:34; Lk. 4:16-21; Acts 10:38; Isa. 11:1-2; 42:1-5; 61:1-2).
>
> The disciples were "filled" with the Spirit before Pentecost (Mt. 10:1-8, 16-20; 16:17; Mk. 6:7-13; Lk. 10:1-24; Jn. 17:14, 16, 22; 20:22), but they weren't baptized in the Spirit until three years later at Pentecost (Acts 1:4-8; 2:1-4, 16, 33-34; 10:44-48; 11:14-18; 15:7-11).
>
> Let's be clear about the disciples' spiritual condition before Pentecost. They were saved, born-again, converted — their names were written in Heaven (Mt. 4:20; 10:1-8, 16-20; Lk. 10:20; Jn. 17). They were "clean" (the Greek word means "pure," as in Mt. 5:8; 1 Tim. 1:5; 3:9; 2 Tim. 1:3; 2:22; Rev. 15:6; 19:8, 14) and therefore sanctified (Jn. 13:10-11; 15:3; 17:6, 14). They were "not of the world even as Christ was not of the world" (Jn. 14:16-17; 15:19; 17:14, 16). They were fully trained in God's ways by Jesus Himself (Jn. 15:15; 17:6, 14). They were called "sheep" (Mt. 10:16). They were "of one accord and one mind" (Acts 1:12–2:1). And they were even commissioned to evangelize the world before they were baptized in the Spirit (Mt. 28:19-20; Mk. 16:15-20; Acts 1:4-8).
>
> The disciples had the Spirit in them and were anointed to preach, teach, heal, and cast out demons (Mt. 10:1-8, 20; Mk. 6:7-13; Lk. 10:1-19). They had been baptized in water and had baptized others (Jn. 1:35-46; 3:22; 4:1-2). They had kept God's Word (Jn. 17:6). They had eternal life (Jn. 17:2-3; 10:27-29). They had experienced spiritual revelations (Mt. 11:25; 16:16-17; Jn. 15:15). They had received the glory of God (Jn. 17:22). They had learned to pray (Mt. 6:1-15; 7:7-11; 18; 19; Lk. 11; 18). They had seen thousands healed and had healed many people themselves (Mt. 8:1-17; 9:35; 10:1-7; Mk. 6:7-13; Lk. 10:1-19). They had confessed Jesus as the Son of God (Mt. 16:16-17; Jn. 6:67-69). They had watched many backslide while they stayed true (Jn. 6:66-71). They had been branches in the vine for years (Jn. 15:1-17). They had so many spiritual blessings — all before they were baptized in the Spirit.
>
> People in the Old Testament received different measures of the Spirit and did work according to whatever measure of Spirit and power they received. This is clear from both Testaments.
>
> In Num. 11:16-25, the one portion of the Spirit that Moses had was divided into seventy-one parts. Elisha received a "double portion" of Elijah's Spirit, and the record shows he did twice as many miracles that were twice as great (2 Ki. 2:9). Elijah did fifteen miracles; Elisha did thirty. Elijah shut the heavens for 3½ years; Elisha shut them for seven (1 Ki. 17:1–2 Ki. 13:21; Jas. 5:17).
>
> John the Baptist had the same measure of the Spirit that Elijah had — not the "double portion" of Elisha or the seventy-one portions of Moses (Lk. 1:15-17). The disciples had a great measure of the Spirit and power of Christ (Mt. 10:1-8, 20; Mk. 6:7-13; Lk. 10:1-20), but they didn't have the fullness of the Spirit until Pentecost (Lk. 24:49; Jn. 7:37-39; 14:12-17, 26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Acts 1:4-8; 2:1-39). Jesus had the Spirit "without measure" (Jn. 3:34).
>
> Paul speaks of "the supply of the Spirit" (Phil. 1:19), "the earnest of the Spirit" (2 Cor. 1:22; 5:5), and "the fulness of God" (Eph. 3:19). John speaks of men receiving "of his Spirit" and "of his fulness" — implying only a part of the fulness of God (Jn. 1:16; 1 Jn. 4:13).
>
> Today we all recognize that people have different measures of the Spirit, power, faith, and other spiritual blessings. So it shouldn't be hard to understand that these same experiences existed both before and after Pentecost. If everyone today had the exact same gifts and blessings, then everyone could do the same works and have the same success.

> **6.** Every believer today receives the Spirit of adoption and is filled with the Spirit in a measure when they're born again: "Ye are not in the flesh, but in the Spirit, if so be that the Spirit of God dwell in you. Now if any man have not the Spirit of Christ, he is none of his... ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father. The Spirit itself beareth witness with our spirit, that we are the children of God" (Rom. 8:9-16; 1 Cor. 3:16; 12:13). You simply cannot be a child of God without receiving the Spirit in some measure.
>
> It's by the Spirit that people are "convicted" (Jn. 16:7-11), "born-again" (Jn. 3:1-8), able to "call Jesus 'Lord'" (1 Cor. 12:3) and "God 'Father'" (Rom. 8:15; Gal. 4:6), "baptized into the body of Christ" (1 Cor. 12:13) and "into Christ" (Gal. 3:27-29), "changed into the image of Christ" (2 Cor. 3:18), "made new creatures" (2 Cor. 5:17), "freed from sin" (Rom. 8:1-13), "kept free from sin" (Gal. 5:16-24; Rom. 6:3-23), "led and receive witness of sonship" (Rom. 8:14-16; 1 Jn. 5:6), given "access to God" (Eph. 2:18), given "gifts" (1 Cor. 12:14), given "life" (2 Cor. 3:6), given "liberty" (2 Cor. 3:17), given "love, joy, peace, etc." (Gal. 5:21-22), "washed, sanctified and justified" (1 Cor. 6:11; 2 Thess. 2:12; 1 Pet. 1:2), enabled to "worship God" (Phil. 3:3), given "grace" (Heb. 10:29), "invited to Christ" (Rev. 22:17), and given "many other spiritual blessings" (Rom. 5:5; 14:17; Eph. 1:3).
>
> Here's the key: you can have all of these blessings and still not be baptized in the Spirit, because people in all ages before Pentecost had these same experiences.

## II. When the Baptism in the Spirit Was First Given

The following facts prove that no one before Pentecost — besides Christ — ever received the Spirit-baptism:

> **1.** Several prophets foretold a great outpouring of the Holy Spirit upon all flesh (Isa. 28:9-12; 32:15; 44:3; Ezek. 39:29; Joel 2:28-29). This outpouring had to be something the Old Testament saints didn't experience, or it wouldn't have been prophesied as something still to come. When you study these passages, you'll notice they have a Millennial setting, but both Peter and Paul taught that these passages were also being fulfilled in this present age. Compare Isa. 28:9-12 with 1 Cor. 14:21-22 and Joel 2:28-29 with Acts 2:15-21.

> **2.** Jesus was the first ever to receive the baptism in the Spirit — the Spirit in all fullness — "for God giveth not the Spirit by measure unto him" (Jn. 3:34). This implies that God did give the Spirit by measure to everyone else before Christ. We've already proven this in Point I, 5 above. That this Spirit "without measure" was the baptism in the Spirit is clear from Mt. 3:16-17; 20:22-23. It was the fullness of the Spirit and power foretold for the Messiah (Isa. 11:1-2; 42:1-7; 61:1-2), fulfilled in Mt. 3:16-17; 11:2-6; 12:18; Lk. 4:16-21; Acts 10:38.

> **3.** John wrote that "the Holy Ghost was not yet given; because that Jesus was not yet glorified" (Jn. 7:37-39). This couldn't be referring to the different measures, fillings, and blessings given to people before Pentecost (as we discussed in Point I, 5 above and in Lesson Eighteen, Point IX), because those experiences had been given before Jesus was glorified. This could only refer to the baptism in the Holy Spirit — the Spirit in all fullness — that people wouldn't receive until Jesus was glorified. Peter taught at Pentecost that the baptism in the Spirit was given precisely because Jesus was glorified (Acts 2:33).

> **4.** John the Baptist taught that Christ would baptize His followers in the Spirit (Mt. 3:11; Jn. 1:31-34). This couldn't happen until the Messiah came. John himself was "filled" with the Spirit from birth, but when he saw Jesus, he desired the baptism in the Spirit that Christ was going to baptize people with (Mt. 3:14).

> **5.** It wasn't until Jesus came that He promised the Spirit-baptism to people (Jn. 7:37-39; 14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Acts 1:4-8). Peter then promised it to people after that (Acts 2:38-39; 5:32).

> **6.** The Spirit-baptism was first experienced by people at Pentecost (Jn. 7:39; Acts 1:4-8; 2:1-4, 33). After that, it was given to the Samaritans (Acts 8:5-20), to Paul (Acts 9:17), to the Gentiles (Acts 10:44-48; 11:14-18; 15:7-11), and to the followers of John (Acts 18:24-28; 19:1-7).

## III. What the Baptism in the Spirit is Not

Spirit-baptism is not a "filling" or a "measure" of the Spirit. It's not receiving the gifts or fruit of the Spirit. It's not salvation, sanctification, justification, or any one — or even all combined — of the spiritual blessings we listed in Point I above and in Lesson Eighteen, Point IX.

None of these experiences should be mistaken for the baptism in the Holy Spirit, nor should they be used as proof of such baptism. Here's why: God's people in every Old Testament age before Pentecost had all these experiences, while the baptism in the Spirit wasn't given to anyone until Pentecost.

Jesus plainly said that the Holy Spirit in baptismal measure was not given because He was not yet glorified (Jn. 7:37-39). Peter confirmed this on the day of Pentecost (Acts 2:33).

If the Spirit baptism wasn't given before Pentecost, but people did have all the blessings we mentioned before Pentecost, then there's a world of difference between the baptism in the Spirit and all these other blessings. These blessings will naturally be present in the lives of those baptized in the Spirit, but they don't constitute that baptism, and they shouldn't be taken as evidence of it.

The baptism in the Spirit is the Spirit without measure (Jn. 3:34; Mt. 20:20-23; Lk. 4:18-21; Acts 10:38). All these other blessings are simply different measures of the Spirit — only a part of the fullness of God.

## IV. What the Baptism in the Spirit Is

The Spirit-baptism is the immersion or burial of the believer in the Spirit. At this moment, you receive the Spirit in your life in all "fullness" and "without measure," and you're "endued with power from on high" to do the works of Christ (Lk. 24:49; Jn. 7:37-39; 14:12-17; Acts 1:4-8; 3:6; 5:12-16; 19:11; Mk. 16:16-20; Heb. 2:3-4; etc.).

It's the same full anointing of the Spirit that Christ received (Isa. 11:1-2; 42:1-7; 61:1; Mt. 11:4-6; 12:18; 20:22-23; Lk. 4:16-21; Jn. 3:34; 14:12; Acts 10:38).

It's the Spirit coming "in, upon, filling, overwhelming, infusing, anointing, and enduing with full power" — not just a "measure" like in Old Testament days. It's the Spirit taking full possession of the believer to live, speak, and work through them in the same degree that was manifested through Christ and the apostles. It's the fullness of what people had only in part before Pentecost.

## V. A Filling and a Baptism Illustrated

Think of it like a glass and a pitcher of water. To whatever extent you pour water into a glass, to that extent it's filled. You can fill the glass by pouring different amounts into it at different times until it's full, or you can fill it all at once. You can even keep pouring until the glass is overflowing — but it's still not baptized. It's just full and running over.

But if you take that glass and immerse it — bury it completely in the water — then it's both filled and baptized. This explains how the disciples were all "filled" (Acts 2:4) as well as "baptized" (Acts 1:4-5). A filling always accompanies a baptism, but a baptism doesn't accompany a mere filling.

Now here's the thing: once you're "filled" and "baptized," you have to stay that way. The minute you step out of the "fullness" of the Holy Spirit and start living in self, you're no longer baptized — just like the glass wouldn't be baptized anymore if you pulled it out of the water.

Like that glass, you can get out of the baptism or the fullness of the Spirit and still retain a "measure" or be "filled" with the Spirit like you were before being baptized.

Christ stayed baptized in the Spirit by yielding to God and by praying daily for more virtue and power to bless everyone who came to Him (Mt. 14:23; 17:21; Mk. 1:35; 6:46; Lk. 5:16; 6:12, 16; 9:18, 28-29; 22:32; Jn. 17; Heb. 5:7). The disciples also lived in prayer and received new fillings and anointings from time to time (Acts 4:31; 6:4).

There's no such thing as "once baptized in the Spirit, always baptized in the Spirit" — any more than there's "once saved, always saved," "once in grace, always in grace," "once healed, always healed," "once alive, always alive," "once in sin, always in sin," "once a child of the devil, always a child of the devil," or — picture it this way — "once full of chicken, always full of chicken."

No such principle exists! Common sense alone tells us that certain conditions must be met continuously to maintain any position in life or in God. If it takes complete surrender and yieldedness to God to be wholly possessed with the Spirit, it will take that same consecration daily to maintain a life in all the fullness of God.

If you want to always be in grace and be saved, stay in grace and stay saved. If you want to always be a child of the devil, keep sinning. And if you want to always be full of chicken — well, we all know what you'd have to do! If you ever get baptized in the Spirit, you must constantly maintain that place in God, or you'll lose the spiritual anointing.

## VI. The Purpose of the Baptism in the Spirit

The purpose of the Spirit-baptism is not to save your soul, sanctify you, make you a child of God, or qualify you for the rapture. These ideas are unscriptural, and here's the proof:

> **1.** All the Old Testament saints and the disciples before Pentecost were saved, sanctified, justified, and free from all sin — as we proved in Lesson Eighteen, Point IX and Point I above. Since they were never baptized in the Spirit, the purpose of Spirit-baptism clearly wasn't to give them these blessings.

> **2.** Jesus was always sinless, yet He needed the Spirit-baptism to have power for His mighty works. So in His case, the purpose of Spirit-baptism wasn't to sanctify Him, save His soul, or make Him a child of God.

> **3.** In Point I above, we showed many Scriptures proving that people receive the Spirit in a measure at the new birth — and these blessings are not the Spirit-baptism.

> **4.** The Spirit-baptism was promised only to saved people after they had repented, been baptized in water, were obedient to God, and had asked for it as a child of God (Mt. 3:11; Jn. 1:31-34; 14:12-17, 26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Acts 1:4-8; 2:38-39; 5:32; Lk. 11:7-13).

> **5.** It was given only to saved people. The disciples were saved and sanctified before receiving the baptism in the Spirit at Pentecost, as proven in Lesson Eighteen, Point IX and in Point I above. The Samaritans were saved, sanctified, baptized in water, healed, and had great joy before Peter and John laid hands on them so they could receive the Spirit-baptism (Acts 8:5-20). The disciples of John were saved, sanctified, and baptized in water years before Paul laid hands on them so they could receive the Spirit-baptism (Acts 19:1-7).
>
> The only two recorded cases of people receiving the baptism in the Spirit at the same time they were saved are Paul and the Gentiles (Acts 9:17; 22:16; 10:43-48; 11:14-18; 15:7-11). In both cases, they were "granted repentance unto life" and their hearts were "purified by faith" — or they wouldn't have received the Spirit-baptism. So even here, the purpose of this baptism wasn't to give them those blessings.

> **6.** The baptism in the Spirit is not given to qualify saints for the rapture. Not a single passage mentions this subject in connection with the rapture. Old Testament saints will go in the rapture, and they were never baptized in the Spirit. Multitudes of people throughout all ages will go in the rapture who have never even heard of the baptism in the Spirit.
>
> Here are the actual qualifications to go in the rapture:
>
> - **(1)** Be "in Christ" (1 Thess. 4:16). Being "in Christ" means you must be a "new creature" (2 Cor. 5:17; Eph. 4:22-24).
>
> - **(2)** Be "Christ's at His coming" (1 Cor. 15:23). Being "Christ's" means the flesh with its affections and lusts have been crucified (Gal. 5:24).
>
> - **(3)** Be "blessed and holy" (Rev. 20:4-6; Heb. 12:14).
>
> - **(4)** Be "good" (Jn. 5:28-29; Gal. 5:16-24; 2 Cor. 7:1).
>
> - **(5)** Be in "the body of Christ," the Church which He is coming for (Eph. 5:25-27; 1 Cor. 13; Eph. 1:22-23; Col. 1:18, 24).
>
> - **(6)** Be "pure" (1 Jn. 3:1-3; Mt. 5:8; Eph. 5:27).
>
> - **(7)** Be walking "in the light" (1 Jn. 1:7; 2:6-11; 3:9-10).
>
> - **(8)** Be "worthy" (Lk. 21:34-36).
>
> The sole purpose of the Spirit-baptism is to clothe you with power from on High to do the same works that Christ did. Christ plainly taught that such an enduement of power was necessary to confirm the Word of God (Mk. 16:20; Heb. 2:3-4), to continue the things "that Jesus began both to do and teach" (Acts 1:1-2; Mt. 28:20), to make people successful witnesses (Acts 1:8), and to prove that believers are genuine and divinely sent to represent God (Mk. 16:15-20; Jn. 14:12; Acts 1:8).
>
> Christ reminded people that if they wouldn't believe in Him for what He said, they should believe in Him because of His works (Jn. 5:20, 36; 9:4; 10:25, 32, 37-39; 14:11; 15:24). It was by such works that people would recognize the Messiah (Isa. 11:1-2; 42:1-7; 61:1; Mt. 11:2-6). It was by the good works of the disciples that God would be glorified (Mt. 5:16; 2 Tim. 2:21; Titus 3:1; Heb. 13:21).
>
> Paul used his power to do miracles as proof that he was sent by God (2 Cor. 6:4-7; 10:3-11; 12:12; 13:10; Gal. 3:3-5; 1 Thess. 1:5; Heb. 2:3-4); to defeat and punish those who opposed the gospel and didn't live right (Acts 13:6-11; 1 Cor. 4:18-21; 5:1-5); to make people obedient (Rom. 15:18-19, 29; 1 Cor. 4:18-21); and to establish people's faith in God (Rom. 1:11; 1 Cor. 2:1-5).
>
> Here's the bottom line: if you're not able to do the same things Christ and the apostles did, it stands to reason you don't have the same enduement of power they had. The same enduement of power today would accomplish the same things it did in the early Church.
>
> Think about how this principle worked in Scripture: the same portion of the Spirit that Moses had, when divided among the seventy elders, accomplished the same work it had through Moses before its division (Numbers 11:16-25). The "double portion" of Elijah's Spirit caused Elisha to do exactly twice as much and perform miracles twice as great. Elijah did sixteen miracles; Elisha did thirty-two. Elijah shut the heavens for 3½ years; Elisha shut them for seven years — proving he had a double portion of the Spirit that rested on Elijah. Read all of 1 Ki. 18:1–2 Ki. 13:21 for a record of these miracles. If the same portion of Samson's Spirit came upon anyone else, it would accomplish similar results.
>
> The law of nature and the law of mechanics prove that two persons or things of equal power can do exactly the same things. It's also true that the stronger of the two will be able to do more than the weaker one, and each one can do exactly according to the degree of power they possess.
>
> To whatever extent you're endued with power from on High, you'll be able to work. Christ received the Spirit "without measure" and was unlimited in His power (Jn. 3:34). The Old Testament saints were able to do works according to their power. The disciples did works according to the power given them. The same principle holds true today.
>
> You're promised unlimited power, and it can be attained in this life if you meet the conditions (Lk. 24:49; Jn. 14:12; Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24). We'll prove this from Scripture in Point VIII below.
>
> So we conclude: the real purpose of the Spirit-baptism is for the enduement of power for service — not to save or sanctify you, and not to qualify you for the rapture.

## VII. Importance of the Spirit-Baptism

If the chief purpose of the Spirit-baptism is to give you power to accomplish what we've described above, then this is the all-important experience for believers today who want to be successful in gospel work.

Jesus commanded His disciples to teach all nations throughout this age "to observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you" (Mt. 28:20). That being baptized in the Spirit was a definite command to them is clear from Lk. 24:49; Acts 1:4-5. If it was a command for them, it's still commanded for us, because we're still in this age.

This enduement of power for service is promised to every believer in the simplest language possible (Mt. 3:11; Lk. 11:13; 24:49; Jn. 1:31-34; 7:37-39; 14:12-17, 26; 15:26; 16:13-15; Mk. 16:15-20; Acts 1:4-8; 2:16-21, 38-39; 5:32; 19:17). In fact, it's one of the two things Christ died for (Gal. 3:14).

Jesus made it absolutely clear to the disciples that they had to be baptized in the Spirit before going out to evangelize the world (Lk. 24:40; Jn. 14:12, 17; 16:7-15; Acts 1:4-8). Only one hundred and twenty of many hundreds of followers (1 Cor. 15:6) felt it was necessary to obey (Acts 1:4-5; 12-15), and these are the only ones who received the enduement of power at Pentecost (Acts 2:1-33).

For you to receive this power today is even more needful! There are infinitely more demon-possessed people, more sick and diseased people, more false doctrines, more false teachers, and more divisions among Christians than at any other time in history.

The Word of God needs confirmation today just as before to convince people of the truth. This is the greatest need of the modern Church. If God could get people to believe in these things again and "tarry until" as they did of old, we would again see mighty works of power and a new type of Christianity.

Let no one complain about lacking power to win souls, heal the sick, and do the works of Christ. Instead, let everyone quit their laziness and unbelief and seek God again "till he come and rain righteousness" on us (Hos. 10:12). Then God will show His mighty works again (

lessons.title: The Kingdom of Heaven and the Kingdom of God
lessons.content:
# The Kingdom of Heaven and the Kingdom of God

Let's dive into one of the most important—and often confusing—topics in Scripture: understanding the difference between the kingdom of Heaven and the kingdom of God. This study will help you see how these two terms relate to each other and why it matters for your faith.

## I. Definitions of the Kingdom of Heaven and Kingdom of God

The Greek word for "kingdom" is *basileia*, meaning "a realm" or "a region governed by a king." Think about it this way: in recent centuries, some countries that were once kingdoms became republics when they no longer had a king. Without a king, there's no kingdom.

The country itself stays the same—same people, same cities, same mountains and rivers—but it's no longer a kingdom. You simply cannot have a kingdom without a king.

### 1. THE KINGDOM OF HEAVEN

Here are all the passages in the Bible where "the kingdom of Heaven" appears: Mt. 3:2; 4:17; 5:3, 10, 19, 20; 7:21; 8:11; 10:7; 11:11-12; 13:11, 24, 31, 33, 44, 45, 47, 52; 16:19; 18:1, 3, 4, 23; 19:12, 14, 23; 20:1; 22:2; 23:13-14; 25:1.

Notice something interesting? Every single one of these references appears in Matthew's Gospel. That's because Matthew presents Christ as Jehovah's King. Mark shows Christ as Jehovah's Servant, Luke presents Him as Jehovah's Man, and John reveals Him as Jehovah's Anointed One—God manifested in the flesh.

The word "Heaven" in all these passages is actually plural in the Greek. The phrase "kingdom of Heaven" literally means "the kingdom from the heavens." It's a dispensational term referring to the Messiah's kingdom on Earth—not originating from this world. The sovereignty comes from Heaven because that's where the King is from (Jn. 18:36).

This is why Jesus was born and what His ministry was all about from the very beginning (Mt. 3:2; 4:17).

The Gospels cover four main subjects:
- **First**, the proclamation of the kingdom (306 verses: Mt. 4:12–7:29; Mk. 1:14-20; Lk. 4:14–5:11; Jn. 1:35–4:54)
- **Second**, the proclamation of the king (964 verses: Mt. 8:1–16:20; Mk. 1:21–8:30; Lk. 5:12–9:21; Jn. 5:1–6:71)
- **Third**, the rejection of the king (901 verses: Mt. 16:21–20:34; Mk. 8:31–10:52; Lk. 9:22–18:43; Jn. 7:1–11:53)
- **Fourth**, the rejection of the kingdom (782 verses: Mt. 21:1–26:35; Mk. 11:1–14:25; Lk. 19:1–22:38; Jn. 11:54–17:26)

Not only was the kingdom rejected, but people even rejected hearing any more about it (Mt. 22:2-7; Acts 1:6-7; 3:19-26). After that, the earthly realization of the kingdom was postponed. It's on hold until the King returns from glory (Acts 3:20; Rev. 19:11-21).

The parables of the kingdom of Heaven we'll explore reveal this postponement and what happens during this time when both the King and His kingdom are rejected. Regardless of where you land on other doctrines, we can all agree that the literal kingdom hasn't been set up yet—and won't be until Christ's second coming.

Satan is still the ruler of this world system and will continue to be until Christ returns to eject him from Earth and restore dominion to man (Rev. 19:11-21; 20:1-10; Zech. 14:1-5; Mt. 24:29-31; 25:31-46; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:7-12; Jude 14; Isa. 63:1-5; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:9-14, 18, 27; Lk. 1:32).

Right now, the kingdom of Heaven isn't the literal reign of Heaven over Earth. Instead, it's the "sphere of profession"—the professing Christian world. This becomes crystal clear in each parable we'll study below. During this Dispensation of Grace, this sphere of profession covers what we call Christendom, taking in both the good and the bad—anyone who claims to be a child of the future kingdom (Mt. 13).

In the next dispensation, the Millennium, everything changes. The kingdom of Heaven stops being just the sphere of profession and becomes the real, literal kingdom of the Son of Man—the very kingdom that was rejected at the beginning of this age. Christ will rule with a rod of iron, putting all enemies under His feet, whether they genuinely profess to be sons of the kingdom or not (1 Cor. 15:24-28).

The kingdom of Heaven is what the prophets foretold—designed to re-establish the kingdom of God over the rebellious part of the universe so God can be all-in-all, just as He was in the beginning.

### 2. THE KINGDOM OF GOD

Here are the references for "the kingdom of God": Mt. 6:33; 12:28; 19:24; 21:31, 43; Mk. 1:14, 15; 4:11, 22, 30; 9:1, 47; 10:14, 15, 23, 24, 25; 12:34; 14:25; 15:43; Lk. 4:43; 6:20; 7:28; 8:1, 10; 9:2, 11, 27, 60, 62; 10:9, 11; 11:20; 12:31; 13:18, 20, 28, 29; 14:15; 16:16; 17:20, 21; 18:16, 17, 24, 25, 29; 19:11; 21:31; 22:31; 22:16, 18; 23:51; Jn. 3:3, 5; Acts 1:3; 8:12; 14:22; 19:8; 20:25; 28:23, 31; Rom. 14:17; 1 Cor. 6:20; 6:9, 10; 15:24, 50; Gal. 5:21; 1 Cor. 4:11; 2 Thess. 1:5; Rev. 12:10.

The "kingdom of God" refers to God's sovereignty over the entire universe. It includes and embraces the kingdom of Heaven along with every other realm in existence. It's moral and universal, existing from the beginning with no end in sight.

Here's a key insight: the kingdom of God existed even before Earth was created. When God laid the foundations of the Earth, angels and other spirit-beings were already in His kingdom (Job 38:4-7). The kingdom of Heaven couldn't have existed then because there was no Earth for the kingdom from the heavens to rule over.

The term "kingdom of God" appears 72 times in Scripture; "kingdom of Heaven" appears 33 times. (See also Mt. 13:43; 16:28; Col. 1:13; 2 Pet. 1:11; Rev. 11:15.)

## II. The Usage of "the Kingdom of Heaven" and "the Kingdom of God"

There's been quite a bit of controversy over whether these two kingdoms are different or the same. A study here will help clear things up. The controversy exists because both expressions sometimes appear in parallel passages. For example, compare Mt. 11:11 with Lk. 7:28, and Mt. 13 with Mk. 8; Lk. 8 and 13.

Here's how we can understand these parallel usages: The kingdom of Heaven is a more specific term than the kingdom of God. Think of it like this: the kingdom of Heaven is the earthly sphere within the universal kingdom of God. It's similar to how a state exists within a nation—the state (kingdom of Heaven) shares many things in common with the nation (kingdom of God), but the nation is the larger reality.

Because of this relationship, almost everything you can say about the kingdom of Heaven you can also say about the kingdom of God—since the kingdom of Heaven is contained within the kingdom of God. However, some things said about the kingdom of God in this age could never be said about the kingdom of Heaven.

When the two terms appear in parallel passages, they're referring to this present age. That's because during this age, the kingdom of Heaven has shifted from being a literal kingdom to being the sphere of profession—all because the King who will rule the earthly kingdom was rejected. When He's finally established in the next age, things will be different.

## III. General Contrasts Between the Two Terms

## IV. Entrance into the Two Kingdoms

Let's clarify the technical difference in how you enter these two kingdoms.

The kingdom of God on Earth right now is primarily spiritual. In one sense, it includes only those who willingly submit to God's will. Of course, like any kingdom, it also includes rebels—but God's purpose in establishing the kingdom of Heaven and sending His Son with an "expeditionary force" from Heaven was to put down that rebellion in this earthly part of the universal kingdom.

Picture it this way: imagine a king whose territory includes some areas in active rebellion. Until those rebels are either reconciled or subdued, they can't really be considered loyal subjects—even though they're technically within the kingdom's borders.

Once the Messiah puts down all rebellion and every enemy is destroyed, the kingdom of Heaven will be absorbed back into the kingdom of God. God will become supreme over all, just as He was before rebellion started in the universe. The Son will become subject to the Father but continues to reign with the Father forever and ever (Isa. 9:6, 7; Dan. 7:13, 14; Lk. 1:32-35; Rev. 11:15; 1 Cor. 15:24-28).

Here's a crucial distinction: in this age, both professed sons and true sons of God are in the kingdom of Heaven. And all of them, along with the rest of the universe, are technically in the universal kingdom of God. But God only recognizes the *true* sons as being genuinely part of His kingdom.

This is why you must be born again to become a willing subject of God and truly belong to His Kingdom (Jn. 3:1-8; Rom. 8:1-13). You don't have to be born again to be recognized as part of the kingdom of Heaven in this age—since it's merely the sphere of profession (Mt. 13). But you *must* be born again to be a true subject of God and share in the kingdom of Heaven in the next age (Mt. 5:3, 5, 10; 18:3).

To wrap up this section: the kingdom of Heaven is what God gives to His Son and the glorified saints. It's the kingdom promised to David that will become universal over all earthly kingdoms. Jesus called it "my kingdom" (Lk. 22:50; Jn. 18:36). The disciples recognized it as such (Mt. 20:21; Lk. 23:42). It's what God has prepared for the righteous since the foundation of the world (Mt. 25:34).

This kingdom was first announced by John the Baptist (Mt. 3:2-3), then by Christ and the apostles (Mt. 4:17; 10:7) as being "at hand." But it was rejected and is now on hold until the King returns. Then it will be established as a separate kingdom from Heaven with the purpose of re-establishing the kingdom of God on Earth forever.

---

# The Kingdom of Heaven Parables

Understanding parables is one of the most important skills you can develop for studying the Bible. Getting them right matters tremendously because so many false teachings come from misinterpreting them.

Here's a problem we need to address: the average person in Christendom thinks parables don't really mean anything specific. Thousands of people dismiss clear biblical truth by saying, "Oh, that's just a parable"—as if parables cancel out truth rather than teach it! Let's fix that misunderstanding right now.

## I. The Definition of Parable

A parable comes from the Greek word *parobollo*, which means "to throw beside for comparison." It's a story that compares some moral, spiritual, or literal truth with a human experience or natural fact. Parables use real-life situations to illustrate truth in a way that's different from just stating it directly. Sometimes parables also serve to conceal truth from uninterested listeners while revealing it to those who genuinely want to learn.

Here's a helpful tip: the most common word for "parable" today would be "illustration." If you substitute "illustration" every time you see "parable" in your Bible, a lot of confusion will clear up immediately.

You understand how illustrations work, right? But the enemy has made the word "parable" seem so mystical that many people think parables have some supernatural, magical meaning beyond human understanding. Some actually believe that because "parable" appears in the Bible, it must mean something completely different from what it means anywhere else.

This is one of the devil's greatest tricks to sidetrack people from truth. He gets us to think that words in the Bible automatically have different meanings than those same words outside the Bible. We need to wake up to this fallacy if we want simple, correct knowledge of Scripture.

Words in the Bible mean exactly what they mean in other books. We shouldn't spiritualize, reinterpret, or change their plain, common meanings just because they appear in Scripture. Why would anyone start changing word meanings when they see them in the Bible? Why spiritualize and make symbolic what's expressed in plain human language when they'd never do that with the same expressions elsewhere?

This foolish practice alone should alert us that demonic spirits are real and actively working to make the Bible seem so mystical and confusing that people give up trying to understand it. If the word "parable" intimidates you, just substitute "illustration" and use plain common sense. A parable is simply an example or comparison that helps explain truth.

All New Testament parables are straightforward illustrations—understand them that way. The ones that need interpretation are interpreted for us. These interpretations give us the right foundation for understanding all the others. In fact, every parable is either interpreted for us, or the illustrated points are so clearly stated within the parable itself that there's no room for misunderstanding—if we're honest enough to accept what the Bible actually says.

The Old Testament parables are prophetic sayings of plain truth that explain themselves. In Num. 23:7-12, 16-26; 24:1-10, 15-25, we have several parables that are clear prophecy and blessing concerning Israel. Even the pagan king Balak understood them! They're so clear that no interpretation is needed.

In Job 27:1 and 29:1, Job's complaints are called parables—again, clear in themselves. In Ps. 49:4, a song for the sons of Korah is called a parable. Ps. 78:2 prophesies that the Messiah would open His mouth to speak parables—truths that had been hidden since ancient times.

The phrase "dark sayings" in that passage doesn't mean parables are hard to understand. It means they *were* hidden previously and *are now* made clear, as Mt. 13:35 explains. Things kept secret from the world's foundation naturally couldn't be understood until revealed. But once they're revealed, they're no longer "dark"—they're clearly explained by the greatest Teacher who ever lived.

A few parables appear in the prophets. In Ezek. 17:1-10, the prophet calls his message a riddle and parable. The king of Babylon is compared to a great eagle taking the best branches of Lebanon's cedars—referring to Nebuchadnezzar capturing Israel's best people. The king of Egypt is compared to another eagle whom Israel trusted for help, but God says it won't work. This parable is interpreted in Ezek. 17:11-24, so there's no room for misunderstanding.

Ezek. 24 contains another prophetic parable, also explained in the same chapter. In most Old Testament passages, "parable" could have been translated "proverb" since it comes from the Hebrew *mashal*, meaning "a pithy saying," usually metaphorical—"an adage," "byword," or "proverb." But they're all explained and just as clear as New Testament parables. The idea of riddle or proverb is evident in Prov. 26:7, 9; Ezek. 20:49; Mic. 2:4; Hab. 2:6.

Certain New Testament passages carry this same sense, as shown in Mt. 15:11-20; Mk. 3:23-27; 7:14-21; Lk. 5:26-39; 6:39. These are all entirely clear—either self-explanatory or explained by context. The parables we'll study below mainly concern the course of this present age and Christian living—and they're frequently misused to teach false doctrines.

## II. The Nature of Parables

Parables are extended similes that stay within the realm of reality. The stories relate true facts and experiences from human life and nature, making them genuine illustrations and comparisons of the ideas being conveyed.

Here's a crucial distinction: the parables themselves are *not* the truths being communicated—they're merely *illustrations* of those truths. The parable is one thing; the truth it illustrates is something entirely different. Once a parable has done its job of clarifying the truth, it has no further use in teaching doctrine—as we'll see in Point IV below.

## III. The Purpose of Parables

> 1. To present moral, spiritual, and eternal truth in an attractive form.

> 2. To capture and stimulate the interest of hearers (Mt. 13:10-17, 35; 2 Sam. 12:1-14).

> 3. To deliver correction without creating resentment (2 Sam. 12:1-14; Lk. 7:36-50).

> 4. To convey truth to sincere listeners while concealing it from others as a test of their hearts (Mt. 13:10-17, 34-35).

## IV. Rules Governing the Interpretation of Parables

> 1. Note when and why the parable was given.

> 2. Identify the main point being conveyed or illustrated.

> 3. Always distinguish between the illustration and the point being illustrated.

> 4. Consider the historical background of the parable.

> 5. Examine the illustration itself and make sure you understand the meaning of its words.

> 6. Guard against fanciful interpretations.

> 7. Don't spiritualize the details of illustrations.

> 8. Don't base doctrines on the details of illustrations. Those details are necessary to construct the story that illustrates the intended truth. Take the story as a literal illustration. Once it clarifies the truth, the illustration has served its purpose and carries no further meaning—no more than details of illustrations outside the Bible would.

> 9. The point made by a biblical illustration is always clear within the parable itself and in parallel passages, being evident:
>
> (1) At the beginning (Lk. 18:9; 11:11).
>
> (2) At the close (Mt. 25:13; Lk. 16:9).
>
> (3) In both places (Mt. 18:21-35; 19:27–20:16).
>
> (4) In parallel passages (Lk. 15:3-7 with Mt. 18:11-14).
>
> (5) Through the general scope (Lk. 13:6-9; 15:11-32).
>
> (6) Through the doctrine illustrated. (Kingdom of Heaven parables should be understood as illustrating different aspects of truth about the kingdom of Heaven, while others illustrate individual experience, etc.)

> 10. Understand parables in light of their context. Any interpretation inconsistent with the illustrated truth must be rejected.

## V. Summary of the Kingdom of Heaven Parables

The following kingdom of Heaven parables reveal the course of the Dispensation of Grace discussed in Lesson Nineteen. Each parable highlights a distinct aspect of truth about this age—always clear within the parable itself. Many of these parables have been wrongly interpreted, as we'll see. Here are the parables and the central truths they illustrate:

> 1. **The Sower**—Classes of hearers in the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 13:3-8, 18-23).

> 2. **The Tares and Wheat**—Mixture of good and bad in the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 13:24-30, 36-43).

> 3. **The Mustard Seed**—Abnormal growth of the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 13:31-32).

> 4. **The Leaven**—Truth about the kingdom of Heaven being corrupted (Mt. 13:33).

> 5. **The Hidden Treasure**—Israel in the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 13:44).

> 6. **The Pearl of Great Price**—The Church in the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 13:45-46).

> 7. **The Dragnet**—Separation of good and bad in the kingdom of Heaven at the end of this age (Mt. 13:47-50).

> 8. **The Scribe**—Mixture of old and new truths by members of the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 13:52).

> 9. **The Unmerciful Servant**—Mercy and forgiveness between members of the kingdom of Heaven (Mt. 18:21-35).

> 10. **The Laborers in the Vineyard**—Selfishness rebuked; humility in service required of kingdom members (Mt. 19:27–20:16).

> 11. **The Householder**—Transfer of the kingdom of God from Israel to the Gentiles (Mt. 21:23-46).

> 12. **The Marriage Feast**—Individual preparation for eternity and the necessity of humility (Mt. 22:1-14).

> 13. **The Pounds**—Postponement of the kingdom of God (Lk. 19:11-27).

> 14. **The Fig Tree**—Nearness of the second coming (Mt. 24:32-33).

> 15. **The Goodman of the House**—Readiness for the second coming (Mt. 24:43-44; Lk. 12:39-40).

> 16. **The Faithful and Evil Servants**—Faithfulness in view of the second coming (Mt. 24:45-51; Lk. 12:42-48).

> 17. **The Ten Virgins**—Watchfulness in view of the second coming (Mt. 25:13).

> 18. **The Talents**—Diligence in view of the second coming (Mt. 25:14-30).

## VI. The Kingdom of Heaven Parables Interpreted

### 1. The Parable of the Sower (Mt. 13:3-8, 18-23)

Both the parable of the sower and the parable of the tares and wheat are explained by our Lord Himself. He gives us the true foundation for interpreting parables. Remember: keep the illustration separate from the truth being illustrated. Do this consistently with each parable, and you'll be protected from the many errors others have fallen into.

> **(1)** The kingdom of Heaven is "like."

> **(2)** The sower illustrates the Son of Man (Mt. 13:24, 37).

> **(3)** The seed illustrates the Word of the kingdom (Mt. 13:19).

> **(4)** The four kinds of ground illustrate four classes of people who hear the Word of the kingdom (Mt. 13:19-23). These four classes are determined by the attitude people take toward the preached Word. This does *not* divide humanity into four equal parts, nor does it teach that only one-fourth will be saved, as some claim.
>
> **A.** The seed sown on wayside ground, eaten by birds, illustrates people who hear the Word but refuse to obey due to indifference and lack of understanding. The devil immediately snatches away the Word sown in their hearts before it can take root and bear fruit (2 Cor. 4:4).
>
> **B.** The seed sown on stony ground, which sprang up quickly but had no depth, illustrates people who receive the Word with joy but are shallow in their experience. The seed can't go deep enough to take root and withstand trouble. When persecution comes, they stumble and reject the Word as quickly as they received it (Mt. 18:6; 2 Tim. 3:13).
>
> **C.** The seed sown among thorns, which got choked and became unfruitful, illustrates people who hear the Word but let it get choked by worldly loves, life's anxieties, and the deceitfulness of riches (Rom. 12:1-3; 2 Tim. 4:10; Jas. 4:1-7; 1 Jn. 2:15-17).
>
> **D.** The seed sown on good ground, producing fruit, illustrates people who hear the Word and let it work freely, producing fruit—some hundredfold, some sixtyfold, some thirtyfold. This group makes an effort to understand the Word and remove from their lives the obstacles symbolized by hard, stony, and thorny ground. The varying amounts of fruit don't represent degrees of Christians but degrees of fruit-bearing, which depends on soil quality and the care of cultivation (Jn. 15:1-16). There's only one kind of Christian—the "hundred percent" kind represented by wheat in the next parable. While the ground produces varying amounts of fruit, the wheat itself cannot be part wheat and part tare (Mt. 6:24; 7:13-29; Heb. 12:14; 1 Jn. 3:8-10).

### 2. The Parable of the Tares and Wheat (Mt. 13:24-30, 36-43)

> **(1)** The kingdom of Heaven is "like" (Mt. 13:24).

> **(2)** The sower illustrates the Son of Man (Mt. 13:24, 37).
