> **(3)** The "good seed" illustrates the true children of the kingdom of Heaven. The tares illustrate those who *profess* to be children of the kingdom but are actually sons of the devil, who sowed them among the wheat (Mt. 13:25, 38-39).

The wheat being planted first shows God's original plan—only good should be in the kingdom, and everyone professing to be a child of God should actually be one. But an enemy sowed tares among the wheat, creating a mixture of good and bad in the same field. This gives the kingdom of Heaven a new dimension: it becomes "the sphere of profession," "Christendom," or "the kingdom of Heaven in mystery."

The word "tares" (Greek *zizania*, found only in Mt. 13:25, 36) isn't the same as the naturalists' darnel but what's known today in Palestine as *zewan*. While growing, it looks like wheat. But when mature, the ears are long and the grain is black. Each grain must be removed before grinding wheat, or the bread becomes bitter and poisonous. Tares reveal their true color as they ripen.

Verse 26 shows that tares couldn't be detected until fruit began appearing (Mt. 7:15-20). The enemy "went his way," confident the seed would grow according to its nature. Shouldn't we have the same confidence about good seed?

> **(4)** The field illustrates the world of humanity (Mt. 13:38).

> **(5)** Both tares and wheat grow side by side in the same field—according to the householder's will—until harvest (Mt. 13:37-40). Both saved and unsaved who profess to be kingdom children will mingle together in this world until the age ends. For a real Christian to escape sinners and hypocrites, they'd have to leave the world entirely (1 Cor. 5:9-11).

> **(6)** The harvest illustrates the separation of the wicked from the righteous at the end of this age, when the Messiah comes to reign (Mt. 13:39-43; 25:31-46; 1 Thess. 5:1-11; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Jude 14; Zech. 14:1-21). It does *not* illustrate the rapture of the Church before His coming to rule this world.

> **(7)** The reapers illustrate angels who will separate the wicked from the good when Christ comes (Mt. 13:39-43; 24:31, 51; 25:31-46; 2 Thess. 1:7-10).

The expression "the end of the world" means "the end of the age." At that time, the kingdom of Heaven stops being merely the sphere of profession and becomes the literal kingdom of Heaven—the Messiah's reign over all the kingdoms of this world (Rev. 11:15; Dan. 7:13-14, 18).

> **(8)** "Then shall the righteous shine forth in the kingdom of their Father" (Mt. 19:28; 25:31-46; Rev. 5:10; 20:1-10).

### 3. The Parable of the Mustard Seed (Mt. 13:31, 32; Mk. 4:31)

The popular interpretation says the Church starts small and grows large enough to shelter all nations. But this *cannot* be the true meaning.

> **(1)** The kingdom of Heaven (not the Church) is "like" (Mt. 13:31).

> **(2)** The kingdom of Heaven, like a mustard seed, has a tiny beginning but grows rapidly to an abnormal size—becoming a tree that shelters things it was never intended to shelter.

The mustard seed is mentioned in Mt. 13:31; 17:20; Mk. 4:31; Lk. 13:19; 17:6. In all these passages, the seed's minuteness is emphasized, while the first three also mention the large size of the plant it produces. Several varieties of mustard have notably tiny seeds but grow under favorable conditions into tall herbs—ten to twelve feet high in just a few months. This rapid growth is always striking. Among the rabbis, "a grain of mustard" was a common expression for anything minute, which explains our Lord's phrase "faith as a grain of mustard seed" (Mt. 17:20; Lk. 17:6).

> **(3)** The "birds" are the same ones that devoured seeds by the wayside in the parable of the sower. They represent demonic powers headed by the devil. This parable illustrates the rapid, abnormal growth of the kingdom of Heaven—from an insignificant beginning to a great presence on Earth. It went from being poor, little-known, and separate from the world to becoming a great worldly institution that serves as a dwelling place for wicked people and demonic powers (Mt. 13:4, 19, 32; Rev. 17:1-18; 18:2).

This parable reinforces the truth from the tares and wheat parable: God's plan was to have only wheat in the field and only good people in the kingdom of Heaven. But an enemy sowed tares and transformed the kingdom's true nature into something abnormal—a mixture of good and bad.

### 4. The Parable of Leaven (Mt. 13:33; Lk. 13:20-21)

The popular interpretation says the Church (the woman) takes the gospel (leaven) and introduces it into the world (three measures of meal) until the whole world is converted. This *cannot* be the true meaning, as we'll see.

> **(1)** The kingdom of Heaven (not the Church) is "like" (Mt. 13:33).

> **(2)** Leaven (sour dough) is *always* used negatively in Scripture, while meal is used positively. Therefore, the common interpretation—leaven converting the world—is the exact *opposite* of the true meaning: leaven corrupting the meal.

Let's look at how leaven is used in Scripture:

In the **Old Testament**, leaven portrays the sinner's evil nature (Exodus 12:8, 15-20; 34:25; Lev. 2:11; 6:17; 10:12). These last three passages forbid leaven in certain sacrifices. Only one sacrifice permitted it (Lev. 7:12-13), and there it signifies that although the believer has made peace with God through another's work (Christ), human traits and selfishness remain and must be continually purged through growth in grace.

In the **New Testament**, leaven is used four ways:
- **First**, the leaven of the Pharisees—external religion,

lessons.title: The Old and New Covenants
lessons.content:
# The Old and New Covenants

## I. Biblical Proof That the Old Covenant Was Entirely Abolished

### 1. The Whole Law Was Done Away in Christ

Here's something that might surprise you: the entire law of God and Moses—including the Ten Commandments and all the civil and ceremonial laws built on them—was abolished in Christ on the cross. The New Covenant has completely taken the place of the old one.

Paul makes this crystal clear in 2 Cor. 3:6-15: "Who hath made us able ministers of the new testament . . . if the ministration of death, written and engraven in stones (the Ten Commandments); was glorious, so that the children of Israel could not steadfastly behold the face of Moses for the glory of his countenance which was to be done away. For if the ministration of condemnation be glory, much more doth the ministration of righteousness exceed in glory. For even that (the Old Covenant) which was made glorious had no glory in this respect, by reason of the glory that excelleth. For if that (the Old Covenant) which is done away was glorious, much more that (the New Covenant) which remaineth is glorious . . . not as Moses, which put a veil over his face, that the children of Israel could not steadfastly look to the end of that (the Old Covenant) which is abolished: But their minds were blinded: for until this day remaineth the same vail untaken away in the reading of the old testament; which is done away in Christ" (see also Col. 2:14-17; Eph. 2:15).

Now, here's something important to notice: the word "vail" in verse 14 isn't actually in the original Greek. So it's not just the veil that's "done away"—it's the Old Covenant itself that's done away in Christ on the cross.

You might think you need a scholar to explain this passage, but honestly, it's pretty straightforward for any reader. The Old Covenant has been "done away" and "abolished" entirely, and the New Covenant has taken its place. Remember, the Ten Commandments were the only part of the law written and engraved on stone tablets, so they were done away with on the cross (Exod. 24:12; 31:18; 32:15; 34:1-4, 27-35; Deut. 4:13; 5:22; 10:4).

Some folks argue that only the *glory* of the Old Covenant was done away. But think about it—what good is a covenant that's lost its glory? Why would we cling to it when we have a better, more glorious New Covenant that's still in force?

Paul's logic is this: the Old Covenant was given in glory, so if its glory is done away, the covenant itself is also done away. When you leave out the second "glory" in verse 7 and the second "vail" in verse 14 (which aren't in the original Greek), you see plainly that it's "the old testament; which is done away in Christ." And notice in verse 15 that the Old Covenant is called "Moses"—proving that Moses gave the Ten Commandments.

Let me show you the contrasts Paul draws between the Old and New Covenants in 2 Cor. 3:

- The first is "old"; the second is "new"
- The first brought "death"; the second brings "life"
- The first was "glorious"; the second is "much more glorious"
- The first brought "condemnation"; the second brings "righteousness" that frees from condemnation (Rom. 8:1-4)
- The first was of "the letter"; the second is of "the Spirit"
- The first had "no glory" compared to the second "by reason of the glory that excelleth"
- The first was "done away" and "abolished"; the second "remaineth"
- The first came by "Moses" and was "done away in Christ"
- The first required a veil to hide the glory because it was "the ministration of death," but the exceeding glory of the second can be looked upon with "open face" because it is "the spirit that giveth life"
- The first brought blindness and bondage; the second brings enlightenment, liberty, and transforms character by the Spirit of the Lord

Think of it like this: a mere change in how the Old Covenant was administered, or a failure by either party to keep it, or even where it was written—none of that changes one covenant into another or affects its glory. Some may argue and try to get around this plain Scripture that says three times the Old Covenant was "done away" and once that it was "abolished" in Christ.

It's similar to a contract or a will: if you just change how a contract is administered, that doesn't change the contract itself. The contract or will itself would have to be changed—or a new one would have to take the place of the old one—for it to be "done away" and "abolished" as Scripture says of the Old Covenant.

Why any group, if they had the slightest honesty and faith that the Bible is God's Word, would twist Scripture to fit some human theory is hard to understand. It would be much easier to accept God's plain words and, if human theories don't harmonize with them, throw those theories away and stick with God and His Word on all questions.

### 2. Christ Fulfilled the Law Before Abolishing It

Christ came to "fulfill" the law before He "abolished" it on the cross (Mt. 5:17, 18). The Greek word for "fulfill" is *plero*, meaning "to satisfy," "execute," "finish," "end," "make complete," and "cause to expire."

This same word is used for many prophecies that came to an end when they were fulfilled (Mt. 1:22; 2:15, 17, 23; 4:14; 8:17; 12:17; 13:35; 26:54-56; 27:9, 35; etc.). That the law also ceased to be in force when it was "fulfilled" is clear from Mt. 11:13; Lk. 16:16; 24:27, 44; Gal. 3:19-25.

The law was only a shadow of things to come, and when those realities arrived, the mere type, picture, or shadow was no longer needed (Col. 2:14-17; Heb. 4:1-11; 8:1-6; 9:1-10; 10:1-18).

### 3. Grace and Truth Replaced the Law

"The law was given by Moses, but grace and truth came by Jesus Christ" (Jn. 1:17). This not only proves that the law was not made known before Moses, but that it was replaced by the New Covenant of grace and truth when Christ came.

### 4. The Apostles Did Not Require Law Keeping

The early church faced this very question head-on. In Acts 15:5-29, we read: "But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them [Gentile Christians], and to command them to keep the law of Moses. And the apostles and elders came together for to consider of this matter. And when there had been much disputing, Peter rose up, and said . . . Now therefore why tempt ye God, to put a yoke upon the neck of the disciples, which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear . . . Simeon hath declared how God at the first did visit the Gentiles, to take out of them a people for his name. And to this agree the words of the prophets; as it is written, after this [the Church Age] I will return, and will build again the tabernacle of David, which is fallen down, and I will build the ruins thereof, and I will set it up . . . Wherefore my sentence is, that we trouble not them, which from among the Gentiles are turned to God: But that we write unto them, that they abstain from fornication, and from things strangled, and from blood. For Moses of old time hath in every city them that preach him [his law, the Old Covenant], being read in the synagogues every sabbath. Then pleased it the apostles and elders and the whole Church to send men . . . they wrote letters by them after this manner; The apostles and elders and brethren send greeting unto the brethren which are of the Gentiles . . . Forasmuch as we have heard, that certain which went out from us [Jews in the early Church who have been under the law from birth] have troubled you with words, subverting your souls, saying, ye must be circumcised, and keep the law: to whom we gave no such commandment . . . For it seemed good to the Holy Ghost, and to us, to lay upon you no greater burden than these necessary things; that ye abstain from meats offered to idols, and from blood, and from things strangled, and from fornication [all fleshly lusts as in Gal. 5:19-21]; from which if ye keep yourselves, ye shall do well."

The apostles did away with all law keeping and circumcision. They stated in no uncertain terms that Gentiles were not to keep the Law of Moses, which included the old Jewish sabbath. This is the Christian position throughout this age. However, if this decision had been made by some modern teachers, it might have been law and sabbath-keeping as the sum total of salvation!

Included in this apostolic decree was the very sign of law-keeping—circumcision—which was abolished. Interestingly, even those who insist on keeping the Jewish sabbath don't practice circumcision, proving their inconsistency in requiring people to keep a law they themselves don't fully keep (Exod. 12:44-48; Lev. 12:3; Jn. 7:22-23).

Circumcision was required as a sign of the covenant God made with Abraham (Gen. 17:9-14). The New Covenant doesn't require it (Rom. 4:11; 1 Cor. 7:18; Gal. 2:3; 5:1-5). This change proves that the New Covenant is *not* the same as the one made with Abraham, as some teach today. If it were the same covenant, circumcision would still be required.

### 5. Freedom Regarding Days and Meats

Paul writes about meats and sabbath days: "One man esteemeth one day above another: another esteemeth every day alike. Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind. He that regardeth the day, regardeth it unto the Lord; and he that regardeth not the day, to the Lord he doth not regard it . . . But why dost thou judge thy brother? or why dost thou set at nought thy brother? for we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ . . . So then every one of us shall give account of himself to God. Let us not therefore judge one another any more" (Rom. 14:1-13; see also Gal. 4:9-11; Col. 2:14-17).

If the Law of Moses, including the fourth commandment, were still in force under the New Covenant, Paul never would have written these statements. Each Christian can do as they please concerning the sabbath and shouldn't be judged by their brother "any more."

### 6. Christian Experiences Don't Come by the Law

Paul asks pointedly: "Received ye the Spirit by the works of the law, or by the hearing of faith? . . . the law is not of faith: but, the man that doeth them [things of the law] shall live in them . . . how turn ye again to the weak and beggarly elements, whereunto ye desire to be in bondage?" (Gal. 1:6-9; 2:15-21; 3:1-12, 19-25; 4:1-3, 19-31; 5:1-9, 11-21).

He continues: "Ye observe days, and months, and times, and years [that the law required]. I am afraid of you, lest I have bestowed labour upon you in vain" (Gal. 3:1-12; 4:9-11).

Going back to observe sabbaths according to the law is described here as returning to bondage. If someone does this, "he is a debtor to do the whole law" (Gal. 5:3).

### 7. The Law Was a Temporary Schoolmaster

The law "was added because of transgressions, till the seed [Christ] should come to whom the promise was made . . . But before faith came [that Christ brought in the gospel and the New Covenant, Heb. 12:1, 2], we were kept under the law, shut up unto that faith which should afterwards be revealed . . . Wherefore the law was our schoolmaster to bring us to Christ that we might be justified by faith. But after that faith is come, we are no longer under a schoolmaster" (Gal. 3:19-25).

Picture it this way: not being "under" the law is like graduating from school—you're no longer under your teacher's authority. The law is not in force and has no claim or authority over someone under the New Covenant. You're now obligated to keep New Covenant laws and commandments, which include new laws that were never part of the Old Covenant, as well as those old laws that God chose to bring into the New Covenant.

### 8. The Law Has Been "Cast Out"

Paul uses a powerful illustration: "Tell me, ye that desire to be under the law, do ye not hear the law. For it is written, that Abraham had two sons . . . he who was of the bondwoman was born after the flesh; but he of the freewoman was by promise. which things are an allegory: for these are the two covenants; the one from mount Sinai, which gendereth to bondage, which is Agar (Hagar) . . . and answereth to Jerusalem which now is, and is in bondage with her children. But Jerusalem which is above is free, which is the mother of us all . . . Now we, brethren, as Isaac was, are the children of promise. But as then he that was born after the flesh persecuted him that is born after the Spirit, even so it is now. Nevertheless what saith the Scripture? Cast out the bondwoman [the first or Old Covenant from Mount Sinai] and her son [those under the law]: for the son of the bondwoman shall not be heir with the son of the freewoman. So then, brethren, we are not children of the bondwoman [the old "ten commandment" covenant and its many laws], but of the free [the New Covenant]. Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free, and be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage [the Old Covenant] . . . For I testify again to every man that is circumcised [no man had any part in the Old Covenant unless he was circumcised], that he is a debtor to do the whole law" (Gal. 4:21-31; 5:1-5, 18).

Those who advocate law-keeping today rarely mention this passage—and for good reason! There's simply no way to explain away such clear language: the Old Covenant "from Sinai" has been "cast out" along with all its sons who are under bondage and death.

The Old Covenant that brought death has no more relationship to people under the New Covenant than Hagar and Ishmael had with Sarah and Isaac. Abraham fathered both Ishmael and Isaac, but Ishmael had no part in Isaac's inheritance (Gen. 21:9-21). God was the author of both covenants, but one was designed to bring death and guilt of sin, while the other was designed to bring life and freedom from sin. You can't keep both, for reasons we'll explore further below.

### 9. The Law Was Shadows—Now Abolished

The law was a covenant of types and shadows, and it was abolished when the realities of those shadows appeared.

"Having abolished in his flesh the enmity [the law of bondage and death], even the law of commandments contained in ordinances . . . blotting out [making void] the handwriting of ordinances that was against us, which was contrary to us, and took it out of the way, nailing it to his cross . . . let no man therefore judge you in meat, or in drink, or in respect of an holyday, or of the new moon, or of the sabbath days: which are a shadow of things to come; but the body [or reality of which those things were mere shadows] is of Christ" (Eph. 2:15; Col. 2:14-17).

Now, followers of Moses today naturally try to explain away these passages that say the old law or covenant has been "abolished." But the more they attempt this impossible job, the more ridiculous their position becomes. It's like trying to sink a battleship with a blow gun—it simply can't be done!

In trying to explain away these passages, they say "the law of commandments" refers only to the ceremonial law, and "the sabbath days" of Col. 2:14-17 were the ceremonial sabbaths, not the weekly sabbath.

Here's the inconsistency: wherever else "commandments" and "sabbath days" appear in Scripture, they argue those refer to the Ten Commandments and the weekly sabbath! This only magnifies how inconsistent their doctrine is.

Regardless of what Old Covenant disciples say about these passages, Paul certainly wasn't one of them—he never once required people to keep any certain day, especially the old fourth commandment sabbath. The Greek word for "sabbath" is plural, and "days" isn't in the original language, so it should read "sabbaths." It's the same Greek word used for weekly sabbaths in 59 other places, so it must include the weekly sabbath as well as any other sabbath of the Old Covenant.

The weekly sabbath, along with the other sabbaths, is part of the ordinances God gave to Moses. The weekly sabbath was actually the first of eight feasts of the Lord in Lev. 23: "Concerning the feasts of the Lord, which ye shall proclaim to be holy convocations, even these are my feasts. Six days shall work be done: but on the seventh day is the sabbath of rest, an holy convocation; ye shall do no work therein: it is the sabbath of the Lord in all your dwellings." After commanding this weekly "feast" and "holy convocation," the Lord next commanded the seasonal "feasts" and "holy convocations," to be kept "in their seasons" (Lev. 23:2-4). So we have a "weekly feast" and "seasonal feasts," all meant to be "holy convocations" and times of rest and holy worship.

In Lev. 23:38, after giving the seven seasonal feasts (which came after the weekly feast), the Lord said, "These are the feasts of the Lord . . . Besides the sabbaths of the Lord," referring to the weekly sabbaths. This proves that "sabbaths" includes the weekly sabbath as much as any other sabbath. See also Exod. 31:13-17; Ezek. 20:12, 20, or any place where "sabbaths" appears—you'll have to acknowledge that the weekly sabbath is included in "sabbaths" in Col. 2:14-17.

All these "feasts" were to be observed "for ever" (Lev. 23:14, 31, 41), so the theory that the weekly sabbath is the only eternal feast is unscriptural. All eight feasts were part of one eternal law that would have continued eternally if man could have kept his part of the contract. Since man couldn't keep it, and there was no provision in it to make a person righteous and capable of keeping it, it had to be set aside for the New Covenant—which doesn't have faults like the old one.

When the old law was "abolished," the weekly sabbath (the sign of the Old Covenant commemorating deliverance from Egypt) was done away, along with all other sabbaths and parts of the old law. Interestingly, eternal terms are used profusely in the so-called ceremonial part of the Law of Moses, but not once in connection with the Ten Commandments of Exod. 20:1-17 or Deut. 5:1-21. So if anything, it can be argued more strongly that the ceremonial part was eternal and the Ten Commandments were temporary—if we're using eternal terms as proof!

The Greek word for "ordinances" is *dogma*, meaning "a civil or religious law" (Lk. 2:1; Acts 16:4; 17:7; Eph. 2:15; Col. 2:14). Since the Ten Commandments were part of God's law to Israel, and the whole law has been abolished, there's no basis to argue that "the law of commandments" in Eph. 2:15 refers to all laws *except* the Ten Commandments. If those commandments were the foundation of the whole law (as everyone agrees), then they must be included in the law of commandments abolished in Christ on the cross.

As we've already seen, it's specifically the Ten Commandments that are singled out as having been "done away" and "abolished." And since the weekly sabbath is part of the ceremonial law, when people admit that "sabbaths" in Col. 2:14-17 refers to ceremonial sabbaths, they're telling the truth—but this doesn't mean the weekly sabbath is excluded from all those old law ceremonial sabbaths.

### 10. The Old Law Was Imperfect and Had to Be Changed

Christ came "after the order of Melchisedec" and not after the Levitical priesthood, which couldn't bring perfection—and under which "the people received the law . . . For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change [abolishment] also of the law . . . For there is verily a disannulling of the commandment going before for the weakness and unprofitableness thereof. For the law made nothing perfect, but the bringing in of a better hope did . . . By so much was Jesus made a surety of a better testament. . . . For the law maketh men priests which have infirmity; but the word of the oath, which is since the law, maketh the Son, who is consecrated for evermore. . . . But now hath he obtained a more excellent ministry [than Old Covenant priests], by how much also he is the mediator of a better covenant, which is established upon better promises. For if that first covenant [from Sinai, Gal. 4:21-31] had been faultless, then should no place have been sought for the second. But finding fault with them, he saith . . . a new covenant, he hath made the first old [obsolete]. Now that [Old Covenant] which decayeth [is obsolete, discarded] and waxeth old is ready to vanish away [to be abrogated]. Then verily the first covenant had also ordinances of divine service, and a worldly sanctuary . . . Which was a figure for the time then present. . . . Which stood only in meats and drinks, and divers washings, and carnal [human] ordinances, imposed on them until the time of the reformation [setting things right of the new order] . . . for this cause he is the mediator of the new covenant, that by means of death, for the redemption of the transgressions that were under the first testament, they which are called might receive the promise of eternal inheritance. For where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator. For a testament is of force after men are dead: otherwise, it is of no strength at all while the testator liveth [the New Covenant was not in force until Christ died]. Whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood. For when Moses had spoken every precept [including the Ten Commandments] to all the people according to all the law, he took the blood of calves and of goats . . . and sprinkled both the book and all the people, Saying, this is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you . . . For the law having a shadow of good things to come, and not the very image of the things can never . . . make the comers thereunto perfect . . . Then said he, Lo, I come to do thy will, O God. He taketh away [abolishes] the first [Old Covenant], that he may establish the second [New Covenant]" (Heb. 7:11-12, 22, 28; 8:6-13; 9:1, 9, 10, 15-22; 10:1-18).

The word "establish" here means to enact the laws of the New Covenant, while the same word in Rom. 3:31 means the righteousness of the Old Covenant is upheld in the New Covenant by fulfilling that righteousness in people—not by keeping the old law, but by faith in Christ provided through the New Covenant (Rom. 8:1-4; Gal. 2:15-20; 3:1-14; 5:1-26).

These many passages from Hebrews and the other points above make it clear: the Old Covenant with the Ten Commandments and all the laws given to Moses was abolished, abrogated, and annulled.

## II. Contrasts Between the Old and New Covenants

The contrasts between the Old and New Covenants prove they were two distinct covenants made for different purposes and to be in force at different times:

> 1. One is called "the first covenant"; the other "the second covenant" (Heb. 8:7; 9:1-18; 10:1-9).

> 2. The first is called "the old covenant"; the second is called "the new covenant" (Mt. 26:28; 2 Cor. 3; Heb. 8:13).

> 3. The first covenant was given by Moses; the second by Jesus Christ (Jn. 1:17; Gal. 3:19; Heb. 9:15; Mt. 26:28).

> 4. One is "the law of Moses"; the other "the law of Christ" (Acts 13:39; Gal. 6:2).

> 5. One is "the law of sin"; the other is "the law of righteousness" (Rom. 7:7-25; 8:1-4; 9:31).

> 6. One is the law of "the flesh"; the other is "the law of the Spirit" (Rom. 7:5, 6; 8:1-4; Gal. 5:16-26).

> 7. One is "not of faith"; the other is "the law of faith" (Gal. 3:12; Rom. 3:27).

> 8. One is the "yoke of bondage"; the other is "the law of liberty" (Gal. 5:1; Jas. 1:18-25).

> 9. One is brought to an end by Christ (Rom. 10:4; 2 Cor. 3:14; Heb. 10:9); the other is established by Christ (Heb. 8:6; 9:15; 10:9; 2 Cor. 3:6-18; Mt. 26:28).

> 10. One brought death; the other brought life (2 Cor. 3:6-18; Rom. 8:1-4; Gal. 3:21; Heb. 9:15; 10:1-18).

> 11. One makes guilty (Rom. 3:19-20); the other justifies or makes not guilty (Acts 13:39; Rom. 3:21-31; 5:1-11).

> 12. One is "a shadow of things to come"; the other is the reality (Col. 2:14-17; Heb. 10:1-18).

> 13. One is "fulfilled" or completed; the other is still in force (Mt. 5:17, 18; 2 Cor. 3:6-18; Heb. 10:9).

> 14. One demanded righteousness; the other gave righteousness (Lk. 10:28; Rom. 8:1-4; Gal. 3:1-29; 5:1-26).

> 15. One made nothing perfect; the other made perfect (Heb. 7:19).

> 16. One was glorious; the other was more glorious (2 Cor. 3).

> 17. One was powerless to save from sin; the other saves to the uttermost (Heb. 7:11-28; 8:7-13; 9:9-28; 10:1-18).

> 18. One had many sacrifices; the other had only one (Heb. 9:9-14; 10:14; Rom. 6:6-13).

> 19. One had a changeable priesthood; the other did not (Heb. 7:23-28; 4:14-16; Rom. 8:34).

> 20. One remembered sins; the other forgets sin (Heb. 10:3; 8:12; etc.).

> 21. One had a representative and a seasonal access to God (Heb. 9:7-10); the other had a personal and daily access to God (Heb. 4:14-16; 7:25; 10:19, 20; Eph. 2:18).

> 22. One had a sinful ministry (Heb. 5:1-4); the other had a sinless ministry (Heb. 7:26-28; 2 Cor. 3; 1 Jn. 3:9).

> 23. One was given under the Levitical priesthood (Heb. 7:11, 12); the other under the present Melchisedec priesthood (Heb. 6:20; 7:11-21, 24).

> 24. One had an earthly tabernacle service of animal sacrifices (Heb. 9:1-10); the other had a heavenly tabernacle service of spiritual salvation (Heb. 4:14-16; 7:25; 8:1-5; 9:11-15).

> 25. One had a sinful mediator (Gal. 3:19); the other had a sinless mediator (1 Tim. 2:5; Heb. 7:26; 1 Pet. 2:22).

> 26. One had no eternal inheritance (Rom. 4:13); the other had an eternal inheritance (Rom. 8:17; Heb. 9:15; 1 Pet. 1).

> 27. One was ratified by animal blood (Exod. 29:1-8; Heb. 9:16-22); the other by the blood of Christ (Mt. 26:28).

> 28. One was a law of works; the other a law of grace (Jn. 1:17; Rom. 3:24-31; Gal. 3:10-12; Eph. 2:8, 9).

> 29. One brought wrath (Rom. 4:15); the other brought salvation from wrath (Rom. 5:9; Gal. 3:13, 14).

> 30. One could not redeem; the other could (Gal. 3:10-14; Rom. 8:1-4; Eph. 1:7; Col. 1:14; Heb. 7:25).

> 31. One could not satisfy God's demands; the other one did (Gal. 2:21; Heb. 7:22; 8:6; 10:5-18).

> 32. One made no provision for doing miracles; the other one provided for them (Gal. 3:1-5; 1 Jn. 5:8; Jn. 14:12; Lk. 24:49).

> 33. Prophecy foretold the abolishing of one and the establishing of the other (Isa. 51:4; Jer. 31:33; Acts 3:22; Heb. 8:7-13; 10:4-18; Rom. 11:25-29).

> 34. One had a fleshly sign of obedience (Acts 7:8; Rom. 2:25); the other did not (Rom. 4:11; 1 Cor. 7:18).

> 35. One was too weak to overcome sin; the other gave victory over sin (Rom. 6:1-23; 8:1-4; Eph. 2:8, 9; 1 Jn. 5:1-18).

> 36. One was made to be changed (Heb. 7:11-22; 8:5, 6; 9:9, 10; 10:1-18; Gal. 3:19-24); the other was made to be unchanged (Heb. 7:22; 8:6; 10:9; 13:20).

## III. The Old and New Covenants Illustrated

The scriptures we've studied prove that Christians are not under the law in any sense. No Christian is obligated to keep the Ten Commandments—not one of them—because they're part of the Old Covenant that was "abolished."

However, those commandments and laws of the Old Covenant that were brought into the New Covenant, you're

lessons.title: The Biblical Doctrine of Salvation
lessons.content:
# 33. THE BIBLICAL DOCTRINE OF SALVATION

## THE BIBLICAL DOCTRINE OF SALVATION

The word "salvation" is the all-inclusive word of the gospel. It gathers into itself every redemptive act and process you can imagine. You'll find this word 163 times throughout Scripture—118 times in the Old Testament and 43 times in the New Testament.

Seven different Hebrew and Greek words get translated as "salvation," appearing 388 times total. These same words are also translated using 23 other English words that mean "salvation" or some aspect of it. Here's the full list: "deliverance," "deliver," "delivered," "deliverers," "save," "saved," "savest," "saveth," "saving," "health," "help," "welfare," "safety," "victory," "safe," "Savior," "Saviors," "defend," "avenged," "avenging," "rescue," "preserved," and "preservest."

## I. "Salvation" the All-Inclusive Gospel Word

Salvation is the great, all-encompassing word of the gospel. It gathers together all the redemptive acts and processes—everything expressed in the doctrines we'll explore below.

When you look at these words and the Scriptures that follow, you'll see that salvation teaches full deliverance from dangers, troubles, sin, disease, and poverty. Think of it like an umbrella that covers absolutely everything that threatens your wellbeing—nothing is left out in the rain.

Salvation includes deliverance from every phase of sin, including inbred sin (or the old man) and the devil himself, as we'll see in Lessons Thirty-three and Thirty-four. It wouldn't be true deliverance from all sin if you were still bound by sin and Satan in any form. The following is a summary of the different words that express the different phases of salvation:

1. **REPENTANCE**—A change of mind, a new mind about God (Mt. 3:2; 4:17; Lk. 13:1-5; 24:47; Acts 2:38; 3:19; 10:43; 2 Cor. 7:9, 10; 1 Jn. 1:9).

2. **CONVERSION**—A change of direction, a new walk with God (Ps. 19:7; 51:13; Mt. 18:3; Lk. 22:32; Acts 3:19; Jas. 5:19).

3. **REGENERATION**—A change of nature, a new nature before God (Jn. 3:3-5; Rom. 3:24, 25; 2 Cor. 5:17; Titus 3:5; 2 Pet. 1:4-14).

4. **ADOPTION**—A change of family, a new relationship with God (Rom. 8:14-23; Gal. 4:5; Eph. 1:4; 3:15; Jn. 1:12; 3:16; 1 Jn. 3:8-10; 5:1-18).

5. **WASHING**—A change of morals, a new creature before God (1 Cor. 6:9-11; Eph. 4:24; 5:26; Titus 3:5; Rev. 1:5; 7:14; Jn. 15:3; Isa. 1:16-18).

6. **REDEMPTION**—A change of owners, a new master in God (Rom. 3:24, 25; 6:16-23; 1 Cor 1:30; 3:16; 6:19-20; Gal. 3:13; Eph. 1:7; Col. 1:14, 22; 1 Pet. 1:18-23).

7. **PROPITIATION**—A change of clothing, a new covering of sins before God (Rom. 3:24, 25; 4:7; 1 Jn. 2:1, 2; Ps. 32:1; 85:2; 1 Pet. 4:8).

8. **GRACE**—A change of favor, a new standing before God (Jn. 1:16, 17; Rom. 5:1, 2; Eph. 2:1-9; Titus 2:11-14; 2 Pet. 3:18).

9. **IMPUTATION**—A change of accounts, a new debt to God (Rom. 1:14; 4:1-11, 22-24; Gal. 3:6-14; Jas. 2:23; 2 Cor. 5:17-19).

10. **FORGIVENESS**—A change of judgment, a new pardon by God (Ps. 103:3; Eph. 1:7; Col. 2:13; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; Lk. 24:47; Acts 10:43).

11. **DELIVERANCE**—A change of bondage, a new liberty before God (Lk. 4:18; Gal. 5:1, 13; Heb. 10:19; 2 Cor. 3:6-18; Jas. 1:18-25).

12. **FAITH**—A change of confidence, a new trust in God (Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; Rom. 1:16-17; 4:1-25; 10:9-17; Heb. 11; 12:1, 2; 2 Pet. 1:5).

13. **BLOOD**—A change in life, a new life from God (Lev. 17:11; Mt. 26:28; Rom. 5:1-11; 6:1-23; 8:1-13; Eph. 1:7; 1 Jn. 1:7; Rev. 1:5; 5:8-10).

14. **FORBEARANCE**—A change of attitude, a new patience in God (Eph. 4:2; 2 Tim. 2:24; Rom. 3:24, 25; 5:1-4; 15:4, 5; Col. 3:13; Jas. 1:3, 4).

15. **RECONCILIATION**—A change of friendship, a new union with God (Rom. 5:10; Eph. 2:14-16; Col. 1:20, 21; 2 Cor. 5:17-20; Heb. 2:17; 1 Cor. 6:17).

16. **SUBSTITUTION**—A change of victims, a new sacrifice to God (Gen. 22:8, 13; Isa. 53; Heb. 10:5-18; 1 Cor. 5:7; Rom. 12:1, 2; Heb. 13:15).

17. **REPRESENTATION**—A change of agency, a new representative before God (Rom. 8:26, 27, 34; 1 Tim. 2:5; Heb. 2:18; 4:14-16; 5:5-10; 7:24-28; 9:1-28).

18. **NEW BIRTH AND SONSHIP**—A change of parents, a new Father in God (Mt. 6:8, 9; Jn. 1:12; 3:1-18; Gal. 4:5, 6; Heb. 12:7; 1 Jn. 3:1-3; 5:1-5, 10-18).

19. **HEIRSHIP**—A change of fortune, a new inheritance in God (Rom. 8:17; Gal. 3:29; Heb. 1:4; 9:15; Jas. 2:5; 1 Pet. 1:4; Eph. 1:11-18).

20. **PRAYER**—A change of communication, a new approach to God (Mt. 6:9; 7:7-11; 21:22; Mk. 11:22-24; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26; Heb. 10:19-23; 11:6; 1 Jn. 3:22; 5:14-16).

21. **ELECTION**—A change of office, a new position in God (Mt. 20:16; Eph. 1:4; Rom. 8:33; 1 Pet. 1:2; 1 Thess. 1:4; 2 Pet. 1:10; Jn. 15:16).

22. **RIGHTEOUSNESS**—A change of acts, a new obedience to God (Rom. 4:1-25; 6:11-22; 8:3; Mt. 6:33; 1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7, 10; 1 Cor. 1:30; Eph. 4:24).

23. **CRUCIFIXION**—A change of death, a new subjection to God (Rom. 6:3-22; 8:13; Gal. 2:20; 5:24; 6:14; 2 Cor. 5:17; Col. 3:5-10).

24. **RESTITUTION**—A change of dealings, a new honesty before God (Exod. 22:1-12; Lev. 6:4, 5; 24:21; Prov. 6:31; Lk. 3:8; 19:8).

25. **RETRIBUTION**—A change of vengeance, a new attitude before God (Mt. 5:39; 7:12; Lk. 6:27-46; 9:51-55; Rom. 12:17-21; Heb. 10:30; 1 Cor. 6).

26. **RANSOM**—A change from slavery, a new release by God (Job 33:24; Mt. 20:28; 1 Tim. 2:6; Gal. 5:1; 6:1; Rom. 6:3-6, 11-23; 8:1-13).

27. **MORTIFICATION**—A change of practice, a new conduct before God (Rom. 8:13; Col. 3:1-17; Mk. 11:24; Mt. 5:6; 1 Jn. 4:19).

28. **CONSECRATION**—A change of devotion, a new offering to God (Rom. 12:1; 2 Cor. 8:5; Acts 6:4; 1 Tim. 4:13-15; Heb. 13:15).

29. **ASSURANCE**—A change of security, a New Covenant with God (Col. 2:2; 1 Thess. 1:5; Heb. 6:11; 10:19-23; 2 Tim. 3:14; 2 Pet. 1:4-14).

30. **HUMILITY**—A change of spirit, a new temperament in God (Mt. 18:1-4; Acts 20:19; Col. 3:5-17; 1 Pet. 5:5, 6; Jas. 4:6-11).

31. **LOVE**—A change of affection, a new devotion to God (Deut. 6:5; Mt. 22:37; Rom. 5:5-8; Jn. 13:34, 35; 1 Cor. 13; 1 Jn. 3:11-18; 4:7-21; Gal. 5:21, 22).

32. **JOY**—A change in emotion, a new delight in God (Neh. 8:10; Ps. 16:11; 51:12; Lk. 19:37; 24:52; Jn. 15:11; 1 Pet. 1:8, 9; Gal. 5:21, 22).

33. **PEACE**—A change in mind, a new tranquillity in God (Acts 10:36; Rom. 5:1; 14:17; 15:13; Eph. 2:14-18; Col. 1:20; Gal. 5:21, 22).

34. **LONGSUFFERING**—A change in forbearance, a new endurance before God (Gal. 5:21, 22; Mk. 4:17; 2 Tim. 2:3; 4:5; Jas. 5:11; Rom. 5:1-8).

35. **GENTLENESS**—A change in manner, a new behavior before God (Gal. 5:21, 22; 1 Thess. 2:7; 2 Tim. 2:24; Titus 3:2; Jas. 3:17).

36. **GOODNESS**—A change in virtue, a new holiness in God (Gal. 5:21, 22; Rom. 15:14; Eph. 5:9; Phil. 4:8; 2 Pet. 1:3-14).

37. **MEEKNESS**—A change of disposition, a new humility before God (Gal. 5:21, 22; Num. 12:3; Ps. 76:9; 149:4; Eph. 4:2; Col. 3:12; 1 Tim. 6:11).

38. **TEMPERANCE**—A change to moderation, a new sobriety before God (Gal. 5:21, 22; Acts 24:25; 2 Pet. 1:3-14; 1 Cor. 9:25-27; Titus 1:8; 2:2).

39. **REVELATION**—A change of knowledge, a new doctrine from God (Mt. 11:25; 13:35; 16:17; Deut. 29:29; Rom. 1:17; 1 Cor. 2:10; Eph. 1:17; 3:1-11).

40. **CONVICTION**—A change in the sense of guilt, a new awakening before God (Jn. 8:9; 16:7-12; Acts 2:37; 18:28; 1 Cor. 14:24; Jas. 2:9).

41. **REMISSION**—A change of sentence, a new justification by God (Mt. 26:28; Lk. 24:47; Acts 2:38; 10:43; Heb. 9:22).

42. **HEALING**—A change in health, a new health in God (Ps. 91; 103:3; Mt. 8:17; Isa. 53; Rom. 8:11; 1 Pet. 2:24; 2 Jn. 2; Jas. 5:14-16).

43. **SOUNDNESS**—A change of condition, a new perfection in God (Prov. 8:14; 14:30; Ps. 91; Col. 2:10; 2 Tim. 1:7; 1 Tim. 1:10; Titus 1:9, 13; 2:1-8).

44. **CREATION AND TRANSFORMATION**—A change of likeness, a new image before God (2 Cor. 3:18; 5:17; Rom. 12:1, 2; 6:3-22; 8:1-13; 2 Pet. 1:3-14; Col. 3:1-17; Gal. 5:16-26).

45. **SANCTIFICATION**—A change of service, a new separation unto God (Jn. 10:36; 1 Cor. 6:11; 2 Tim. 2:21; Exod. 13; 30:39; 40:10, 11; Lev. 27:14-27; Jer. 1:5).

46. **JUSTIFICATION**—A change of state, a new righteousness in God (Rom. 3:20-30; 5:1, 9; 1 Cor. 6:11; Gal. 3:6-14; Acts 13:38, 39).

47. **PRESERVATION**—A change of assurance, a new security in God (Ps. 37:28; 145:20; 2 Tim. 4:18; Phil. 1:6; 1 Thess. 5:23; Jude 20-24; 1 Pet. 1:5).

48. **PROVIDENCE**—A change of supply, a new provision in God (Mt. 6:33; Phil 4:18; Mk. 11:22-24; Jn. 15:7, 16; Ps. 84:11).

49. **BAPTISM INTO CHRIST**—A change of elements, a new burial in God (Eph. 2:1-9; Rom. 6:3-6; 1 Cor. 12:13; Gal. 3:27; 5:24; Col. 2:11-17).

50. **RESURRECTION**—A change in substance, a new body given by God (1 Cor. 15:21-58; Phil. 3:20, 21; 1 Thess. 4:13-17; 1 Jn. 3:1-3; Jn. 5:28, 29).

51. **PREDESTINATION**—A change of decree, a new destiny in God (Rom. 8:28-30; Eph. 1:5, 11; 3:10, 11; 1 Pet. 1:2-13; 2 Pet. 1:4-14; 2 Cor. 3:18).

52. **FOREKNOWLEDGE**—A change in revelation, a new knowledge of God (Rom. 8:29; 11:2; Acts 2:23; 1 Pet. 1:2; Eph. 1:1-17; Mt. 11:25; Deut. 29:29).

53. **FOREORDINATION**—A change in appointment, a new future in God (Rom. 3:25; Jn. 15:16; 1 Thess. 5:9; 1 Pet. 1:20; Eph. 1:10; 2:7; 3:10, 11).

54. **POWER**—A change in authority, a new power in God (Lk. 24:49; Jn. 14:12-14; Mk. 16:15-20; Acts 1:8; 1 Cor. 12-14; Eph. 1:19; 3:19, 20; Heb. 2:3, 4; Mk. 11:22-24).

55. **GLORIFICATION**—A change of realm, a new dominion with God (Rom. 8:17, 30; Acts 3:13; Phil. 3:21; 1 Cor. 15:21-58; Rev. 5:10; 20:1-7).

56. **DESTINATION**—A change of dwelling, a new home with God (Jn. 14:1-3; Heb. 11:10, 13-16; 13:14; Rev. 3:12; 21:1–22:5).

57. **HOLINESS**—A change in living, a new conformity to God (Rom. 6:16-23; 8:1-13; 12:1, 2; 11:16; 1 Cor. 3:16-17; 6:19, 20; 2 Cor. 5:17; 7:1; Heb. 12:14; Rev. 20:4-6).

58. **LIFE**—a change of existence, a new eternal life with God (Jn. 3:16-20, 36; 5:24; 17:2, 3; 1 Jn. 2:24, 25; 5:11, 12; 1 Thess. 4:13-17).

59. **MERCY**—A change of heart, a new disposition in God (Mt. 5:7; Rom. 12:8-19; Jas. 2:13; Lk. 6:36; Phil. 2:1; Col. 3:12).

60. **INSPIRATION**—A change of thoughts, a new influence from God (1 Cor. 2:9-14; 2 Tim. 3:15-17; Jn. 14:16, 17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15; 1 Jn. 2:27).

Now here's something really important to understand: not one of these phases of salvation should be thought of as a separate work from the salvation they're part of. You couldn't possibly receive salvation without having every single one of these blessings started in you that has to do with sin.

They all have both an initial, instantaneous aspect and a progressive aspect until they're completed in the eternal future.

Although salvation and all its various phases that deal with sin are received in their initial stages by faith when you truly repent and believe the gospel, the different phases continue operating in you from the moment of salvation until you receive and enter into the final stage of salvation at the rapture and resurrection (1 Pet. 1:5-9, 13; Rom. 13:11; Heb. 1:14; Phil. 3:20, 21; 1 Thess. 4:13-17; 5:23; 1 Cor. 15:51-58; 1 Jn. 3:1-3).

Salvation, in its general sense, even includes the redemption of all creation at the end of the Millennium (Rom. 8:17-25; Heb. 1:10-12; 12:25-28; 2 Pet. 3:10-13; Rev. 21-22; Isa. 66:22-24). If salvation takes in all creation affected by the Fall, then it's easy to see how it encompasses every phase of deliverance for mankind in the process of redemption from beginning to completion.

## II. Righteousness of God (Rom. 3:21-23)

The word "righteousness" (Greek, *dikaiosune*) occurs 92 times in the New Testament and 36 times in Romans alone. The Greek word *dikaioma* means "a righteous ordinance, a decree of acquittal," and is translated "righteousness" (Rom. 2:26; 5:18; 8:4; Rev. 19:8); "ordinance" (Lk. 1:6; Heb. 9:1, 10); "judgment" (Rom. 1:32; Rev. 15:4); and "justification" (Rom. 5:16).

Righteousness in the Bible means:

> 1. The perfection of the divine nature, whereby God is most just and holy in Himself, and in all His dealings with His creatures (Jn. 17:25).

> 2. The clemency, mercy, and goodness of God (Deut. 2:25; Ps. 51:14).

> 3. The faithfulness of God to His promises (Ps. 31:1; Isa. 42:6; 1 Cor. 10:12-13).

> 4. "The righteousness of Christ" is the active and passive obedience of Christ, whereby He perfectly fulfilled the law, and propitiated the justice of God so that man could obey God and receive the righteousness which is by faith, and their persons be accepted of God and received to eternal glory (Dan. 9:24; Jer. 23:6; 1 Cor. 1:30).

> 5. Uprightness and justice in dealing between men (Lk. 1:75).

> 6. Holiness of life and conversation (1 Cor. 15:34).

> 7. "The righteousness of God" is that righteousness which has been fully met and maintained in the atoning work of Christ on the cross, by which God is now able to save sinners and still be righteous in doing so (Rom. 1:17; 3:5, 21-22; 10:3). Under the law God required righteousness from man, but the law, instead of giving it to many, only demonstrated that he was destitute of it and needed God's righteousness, which He now freely gives under Grace.

The following points are different phases of the righteousness of God and help us understand it:

> (1) His righteousness is revealed in the gospel (Rom. 1:17).

> (2) It is not commended by unrighteousness (Rom. 3:5).

> (3) It is manifested wholly apart from the law (Rom. 3:21).

> (4) It is witnessed by the law and the prophets (Rom. 3:21).

> (5) It is imputed by faith apart from works (Rom. 3:22; 4:3, 22).

> (6) It is for all who believe (Rom. 3:22).

> (7) It is freely given to us through redemption (Rom. 3:24-26).

> (8) It is the righteousness of faith because it is apprehended and applied by faith in Christ and His work (Rom. 4:5, 11, 13; 9:30; 10:6).

> (9) It is the righteousness of faith because all the righteousness of God, which He required in order to redeem man, was fully met by Christ and now God is righteous in justifying all who believe (Rom. 5:18).

> (10) It is the medium of grace unto eternal life by Christ (Rom. 5:21).

> (11) It is the controlling factor in the lives of saints (Rom. 6:13, 16, 18-20).

> (12) It brings eternal life to all who believe (Rom. 8:10).

> (13) It is the righteousness which God required under law but gives under grace (Rom. 8:4; 9:31-32; 10:5-6).

> (14) Conditions for receiving righteousness (Rom. 3:22; 4:1-5, 11; 6:13-19; 8:1-10; 10:10). You become righteous in God by faith in Christ.

Other than the righteousness of God, there is self-righteousness which prides itself in being righteous through self-effort (Rom. 10:3; Phil. 3:3-9; Isa. 64:6; Lk. 18:9-14; Mt. 23).

## III. Atonement or At-one-ment (Lev. 17:11)

The words "atonement" and "blood" are used 523 times in the Bible. The following points reveal the blessings of the blood of Jesus, which come through faith:

> 1. Remission of sins (Rom. 3:24, 25; Heb. 9:22; Eph. 1:7; 1 Jn. 1:7).

> 2. Salvation from wrath (Exod. 12:13, 23; Rom. 5:9-11; 1 Thess. 5:9-10).

> 3. Deliverance from Hell (Zech. 9:11; Rom. 5:1-11; Rev. 5:8-10).

> 4. Sanctification (Heb. 2:11; 10:14, 29; 13:12).

> 5. Justification (Rom. 3:24-30; 5:9-11; Acts 13:38-39; Gal. 2:16-17; Titus 3:4-7).

> 6. Citizenship (Eph. 2:12, 13; Phil. 3:20-21; Gal. 3:27-29).

> 7. Peace with God (Rom. 5:1; Col. 1:20).

> 8. Purging of the conscience (Heb. 9:14).

> 9. Redemption (Rom. 3:24, 25; Eph. 1:7; Heb. 9:15; 1 Pet. 1:18-23; Rev. 5:8-10).

> 10. High priestly benefits (Heb. 4:14-16; 9:21; 10:1-18).

> 11. Access to God (Heb. 10:19; Eph. 2:14-18).

> 12. Power to become the elect of God (1 Pet. 1:2).

> 13. Washing from sins (Rev. 1:5; 7:14; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; Eph. 1:7).

> 14. Atonement for the soul (Lev. 17:11; Rom. 5:11).

> 15. Life eternal, abundant life (Rom. 5:1-11; Col. 1:20).

> 16. A New Covenant (Mt. 26:28).

> 17. The new birth (1 Pet. 1:18-23).

> 18. Righteousness (Rev. 7:14; 19:8).

> 19. Overcoming power (Rev. 12:11).

> 20. Membership in the body of Christ (Acts 20:28).

> 21. Salvation (Ps. 51; Acts 4:12; Eph. 1:7).

### Many Scriptures Proving Unlimited Atonement

The Bible does not teach a limited atonement. What we mean by this is that salvation is provided for all human beings alike, and on the same basis. This is clear from many statements in the Bible itself:

1. God told Cain that if he did well and would offer the right sacrifice, he also would be accepted, the same as his brother, Abel (Gen. 4:7). There was no difference between these two or any others when it came to God's requirements for atonement.

2. The sprinkling of blood was required of every Israelite in Egypt who wanted protection—and this was on a voluntary basis (Exod. 12).

3. Every man in Israel, without exception, was under contract with God to offer blood atonement, and this also was on a voluntary basis—from Moses to Christ (Exod. 24:5-8; Lev. 1:2-4; Heb. 9:22). The same was true of any Gentile who became a Jewish proselyte (Exod. 12:49; Lev. 24:22; Num. 15:15-16, 29).

4. Atonement was made for every person and for every sin, disease, and corruption among men, under the law (Lev. 12:6-8; 14:53-57). Proper atonement was always required of any and every man and woman, Jew or Gentile, who chose to follow God. Not once was any person of the human race turned down by God or told that atonement was not for him. In the thirty-nine books of the Old Testament there is not a single statement that even hints of a limited atonement or of an atonement for a special few of the human race, instead of all men.

5. In prophecy and in promise, atonement has been referred to as being for all men alike, Jews and Gentiles (Isa. 1:16-20; 11:10; 42:1, 6; 49:6, 22; 60:3-16; 61:9; 62:2; Mal. 1:11).

6. The God who accused the whole race of going astray from Him said that He had laid on Christ the Messiah "the iniquity of us all" (Isa. 53:6).

7. This applies to all men alike: "Thou shalt call his name Jesus; for he shall save his people from their sins" (Mt. 1:21). It's clear from God's promises that "whosoever

lessons.title: The Truth About Sanctification and Justification
lessons.content:
# The Truth About Sanctification and Justification

## I. Scriptural Usage and Definition of Sanctification

Let's be honest — sanctification is one of those Bible topics that gets people tangled up! The Christian world is pretty divided on it, and that's exactly why every thoughtful believer should dig into Scripture with an open mind. So here's my invitation to you: set aside any preconceptions you might have and let's honestly examine what the Bible actually says. What follows comes from looking at over 25,000 separate statements in Scripture.

### 1. The Fact and Usage of the Doctrine of Sanctification

Sanctification is definitely taught in Scripture — there's no question about that. The word "sanctification" itself only appears five times, but "sanctified" shows up 62 times, "sanctify" appears 70 times, and "sanctifieth" is used four times. That's 141 occurrences total — 110 in the Old Testament and 31 in the New Testament.

Here's something interesting: this doctrine is mentioned about four times more often in the Old Testament than in the New. The same pattern holds for related words like "holy," "holiness," "clean," and "pure."

### 2. The Different Material or Inanimate Things Sanctified

Here's where things get really interesting. When you look at everything the Bible calls "sanctified," you quickly realize this can't just be about removing sin from people. Think about it — how do you remove sin from a building or a piece of bread?

Scripture tells us these things were sanctified:
- "The seventh day" (Gen. 2:3)
- "The tabernacle and its furniture" (Exod. 29:43–30:29; Lev. 8:10-15)
- "The priest's garments" (Lev. 8:30)
- "Houses, fields and other property" (Lev. 27:9-29)
- "Bread" (1 Sam. 21:5)
- "The temple of Solomon and its furniture" (2 Chron. 7:16-29; 29:17-19)
- "The gates of Jerusalem" (Neh. 3:1)
- "Mt. Sinai" (Exod. 19:23)
- "The offerings" (Exod. 29:27)
- "God's name" (Isa. 29:23; Ezek. 36:23)
- "Daily food" (1 Tim. 4:5)
- "Herod's temple" (Mt. 23:17)

In every one of these passages, the idea isn't about removing some "old man" or dealing with sin. It's about setting something apart for sacred purposes — taking it from ordinary use and dedicating it to God.

### 3. Examples of Persons Sanctified

The different people who were sanctified in both Testaments make the same point even clearer. Sanctification wasn't primarily about extracting an "old man" or sin from someone. It was about "setting apart to a sacred purpose or work," "hallowing," "holding as sacred," and "consecrating to accomplish the divine will." If cleansing from sin was necessary for any person being sanctified, that was just a secondary part of the main purpose.

Look at this list:
- "Israel as a nation" (Exod. 19:14-15)
- "Individuals and families" (1 Sam. 16:5)
- "Heathen soldiers" (Isa. 13:3, 17)
- "Prophets" (Jer. 1:5)
- "Priests" (Lev. 8:30)
- "The first-born of Israel" (Exod. 13:2; Num. 8:17; Deut. 15:19)
- "Unsaved companions" (1 Cor. 7:14)
- "Christ and the disciples" (Jn. 10:36; 17:17-19)
- "God the Father" (Lev. 10:3; Num. 20:13; Isa. 5:16; Ezek. 20:41; 28:22, 25; 36:23; 38:16; 39:27)

Now think about this: those heathen soldiers were never cleansed from sin as far as we know — they were simply set apart as God's instrument for disciplining Babylon. Jeremiah was sanctified before he was even born in sin. Both Christ and God the Father were sanctified, yet neither of them had any sin in them!

This shows us clearly that people can be sanctified without sin being part of the picture at all. John the Baptist was sanctified before birth because he was filled with the Spirit from that moment (Lk. 1:15). And here's an important principle: one must be sanctified before being filled with the Spirit (Jn. 14:17).

### 4. Various Ways Sanctification is Used in Scripture

The different ways sanctification is used throughout both Testaments prove that the meaning isn't about cleansing from sin or removing the "old man" from someone.

**Sanctification is Associated With God the Father**

> (1) It is used of God in setting apart inanimate things from a profane or secular to a sacred and divine use, as seen in Point 3, above.

> (2) It is used of God in setting apart men to execute His vengeance upon Babylon, and this was done without any reference to cleansing from sin (Isa. 13:3, 17).

> (3) It is used of God in setting apart men from a sinful life to a life of holiness and divine service. (See Point II, below.)

> (4) It is used of God to manifest His glory and vindicate Himself (Ezek. 36:23).

> (5) It is used of God making food holy for natural use (1 Tim. 4:4, 5; Rom. 14:1-23; Col. 2:14-17).

> (6) It is used of God in consecrating His Son to fulfill a mission in the world and to die for all men in order to redeem them (Jn. 10:36).

> (7) It is used of God in manifesting His will concerning sin (1 Thess. 4:3, 4).

> (8) It is used of God in manifesting His choice (2 Thess. 2:13; 1 Pet. 1:2).

> (9) It is used of God as an agent in sanctification (Jude 1).

> (10) It is used of God as the source of sanctification (Jn. 10:36; 17:17-19; 1 Thess. 5:23; 1 Cor. 1:30).

**Sanctification is Associated With Jesus Christ**

> (1) Christ sanctified Himself (Jn. 17:17-19).

> (2) Christ, and not some experience, is our sanctification (1 Cor. 1:30).

> (3) Christ sanctifies the Church with the Word (Eph. 5:25, 26).

> (4) Christ sanctifies sinners by His own blood (Heb. 9:13-15; 10:10-14; 13:12).

> (5) Christ was sanctified by God (Jn. 10:36).

**Sanctification is Associated With the Holy Spirit**

> (1) He sanctifies sinners by the blood (1 Pet. 1:2, 18-23; Heb. 10:29).

> (2) He sanctifies sinners by the Word (Jn. 3:5; 15:3; 1 Pet. 1:23; Jas. 1:18-25; 2 Thess. 2:13).

> (3) He sanctifies men by the name of Jesus (1 Cor. 6:9-11).

> (4) He sanctifies by His own power (Rom. 8:1-13; Jn. 3:5).

**Sanctification is Associated With Man**

> (1) Man can sanctify himself; that is, set himself apart from sin to God (Lev. 11:44; 20:7, 8; 2 Cor. 7:1; Jas. 4:8-10).

> (2) Man can set himself apart to see God's works (Exod. 19:9-22; Josh. 3:5).

> (3) Man can sanctify God and His name; that is, hold them in reverence and as sacred (Isa. 29:23; Ezek. 36:23; Mt. 6:9; 22:37; 1 Pet. 3:15).

> (4) Man can keep himself sanctified by divine means (1 Thess. 4:3, 4).

### 5. The Two-Fold Meaning of Sanctification

The Hebrew and Greek words for "sanctification" carry rich meaning: "to make or pronounce clean or holy, morally, physically, and ceremonially," "to consecrate, dedicate, hallow," "to purify," "to set apart from a profane or secular use to a sacred or holy use," "to separate from carnal and natural to spiritual purposes," and "to venerate or reverence."

> (1) Separation from an evil, profane or secular purpose to a sacred purpose; to make or to be holy, reverent, sacred, separated, pure, hallowed; to prepare for spiritual and sacred use, to cleanse from sin and uncleanness and make free from manifestations and uses of the natural and carnal life; to make holy anything consecrated to God; the state of being holy and consecrated to God; and the devotion of a person or thing to a particular, spiritual exercise and use.

> (2) Separation, dedication or consecration unto God. This includes any person or material thing that can be given entirely over to God for sacred and spiritual use, anything set apart for service unto God. Thus, the two-fold meaning of sanctification in one statement is any person or thing separated from a profane or secular use, and consecrated unto God to be used wholly for divine and spiritual use, whether sin is involved or not.

### 6. The Threefold Moral Sanctification of Man

> (1) **Past or instantaneous sanctification.** This refers to the initial, definite act of making a person holy. Both God and man have parts in this act. Man must first consecrate or sanctify himself to God, and then God makes him holy by the various means of the gospel, as we shall see.

> (2) **Present or progressive sanctification.** This refers to the state of being sanctified, of growth to maturity, and of conformity to the image of Christ in all the principles of holiness that have been imparted in the initial act of making holy. The work of sanctification by God or man could not end with one act of being made holy, for if the person is made holy and does not continue in this state, he could not be considered as sanctified. There must be the state of holiness as well as the act of making holy. If there is a definite act of making holy, there must also be a definite process to keep holy.

> Think of it like breeding prize-winning birds — if you neglect them after developing them to a fine breed, they'll revert back to their original state. Or picture a garden: you can plant it and pull every weed, but neglect it and it'll become a desolate mess again. It's the same with our spiritual lives. If you neglect keeping holy a man who has been made holy and given the principles of holiness, he'll slide back into a sinful life. After being sanctified, we must keep ourselves sanctified — hence the need for a daily process to maintain what has been established. Both God and man have roles in this, as we'll see.

> (3) **Future or complete sanctification.** This refers to the final act and process of being made holy forever in body, soul and spirit, and preserved blameless forever. It's plainly evident that no one made holy here is made absolutely and eternally holy by one act. There must be the process of keeping holy until one is made "whole" in body, soul, and spirit at the rapture, the resurrection, and entrance into the eternal state.

---

These six points make clear the meaning, time, place, extent, process, standing, purpose, and use of a person or thing sanctified or separated from a profane to a sacred use. Every scripture in both Testaments fits perfectly with these facts.

So here's our conclusion: sanctification cannot be limited to dealing with sin in a person. A holy person can sanctify himself more and more, and anything he possesses can also be sanctified to God for His use. The main idea of sanctification is setting apart any person or thing from profane and secular use to spiritual and holy use — and sin may or may not be involved.

If the person to be sanctified is sinful, then and only then would sin be involved. But when we're talking about houses, fields, material things, a holy person already saved from sin, or Christ and God themselves — there's no sin involved and no "old man" taken out. When God the Father is spoken of as being sanctified, it simply means "to reverence." When Christ is sanctified, it means "to set apart for God's service."

## II. When Were Men in the Old Testament Sanctified?

1. **When they were separated from the womb:** "Sanctify unto me all the firstborn . . . both man and beast: it is mine . . . thou shalt set apart [sanctify] unto the Lord all that openeth the matrix" (Exod. 13:2, 12).

2. **When they were chosen before birth:** "Before I formed thee in the belly I knew thee; and before thou camest forth out of the womb I sanctified thee, and I ordained thee to be a prophet unto the nations" (Jer. 1:5).

3. **When they prepared to worship God:** "Go unto the people, and sanctify them today and tomorrow . . . be ready against the third day for the third day the Lord will come down in the midst of the people" (Exod. 19:10, 11, 14, 15, 22).

4. **When they were set apart for ministry** (they were sanctified before, as in Point 3 above): "Thou shalt anoint them [Aaron and sons by Moses], and consecrate them, and sanctify them, that they may minister unto me in the priests' office" (Exod. 28:41).

5. **When they ate the atonement flesh and bread:** "Aaron and his sons shall eat those things wherewith the atonement was made, to consecrate and to sanctify them" (thus they were sanctified daily, Exod. 29:32, 33).

6. **When they were "anointed" and had "the blood" applied:** "Moses took the anointing oil, and of the blood which was upon the altar, and sprinkled it upon Aaron . . . and upon his sons . . . and sanctified them" (Lev. 8:30; Heb. 9:21, 22). Notice: there was never a time gap between two applications of the blood upon any person or thing being sanctified.

7. **When they were made holy:** "Sanctify yourselves, and ye shall be holy; for I am holy" (Lev. 11:44; 20:7, 8). This proves that Old Testament saints were morally sanctified and holy like God (See Lesson Eighteen, Point IX).

8. **When they were to see miracles:** "Sanctify yourselves: for tomorrow the Lord will do wonders among you" (Josh. 3:5).

9. **When they had sinned:** "Sanctify yourselves against tomorrow . . . there is an accursed thing in the midst of thee" (Josh. 7:13). Sanctification had to happen every time men sinned, so there's no such thing as "once sanctified, always sanctified."

10. **When a new priest was consecrated:** "The men . . . sanctified Eleazar his son to keep the ark of the Lord" (1 Sam. 7:1).

11. **When sacrifices were offered:** "I am come to sacrifice to the Lord . . . sanctify yourselves and come with me to the sacrifice. And he [Samuel] sanctified Jesse and his sons, and called them to the sacrifice" (1 Sam. 16:2, 5).

12. **When the ark was brought back to Jerusalem:** "The priests and the Levites sanctified themselves to bring up the ark of the Lord" (1 Chron. 15:12-15).

13. **When revival was needed:** "And they gathered their brethren and sanctified themselves" (2 Chron. 29:5, 15-19, 34; 30:3, 15-17, 24; 31:18; 35:6).

14. **When there was fear of sin:** "Job sent and sanctified them. . . . for Job said, It may be that my sons have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts. Thus did Job continually" (Job 1:5).

It's crystal clear from all these statements that sanctification isn't just one or two acts — it's a constant separation from sinful and profane uses to holy uses.

## III. When Does Sanctification Begin According to the New Testament?

Sanctification began in God's plan in eternity past (Eph. 1:4). But in each person's actual experience, if sanctification means separation from a life of sin to a life of holiness, then it naturally begins — from our viewpoint — when we turn to God to be made holy, and from God's viewpoint when He makes us holy. You have to be made holy before you can continue in holiness throughout your life. Sanctification begins with these experiences:

1. **When you get salvation:** "God hath from the beginning chosen you to salvation through sanctification of the Spirit and belief of the truth" (2 Thess. 2:13). What could be clearer about when you're sanctified? You can't be saved or get salvation except "through sanctification of the Spirit" — and this happens when you believe the truth. Zacharias, speaking by the Holy Ghost, said people would serve God "in holiness and righteousness" from the time they're "redeemed," get "salvation," have "remission of sins," and are "saved" (Lk. 1:67-77).

The word "salvation" is the all-inclusive word of the gospel, as we've seen in Lesson Thirty-three, Point I, especially the last three paragraphs after Point 60. Sanctification is part of salvation and should never be understood as a separate work of grace apart from it. Christ is our salvation (Lk. 2:30) and sanctification (1 Cor. 1:30), and you can't have Christ without having both. You'd have just as much right to take any other phase of salvation and make it a separate work of grace from salvation as you would to do this with sanctification.

2. **When you receive Christ who is your sanctification** (1 Cor. 1:30; Jn. 1:12; 1 Jn. 2:29; 3:5-10; 5:1-5, 18). Sanctification comes from receiving a person more than getting an experience separate from that person.

3. **When you are born-again.** "Every one that doeth righteousness is born of him . . . he that doeth righteousness is righteous, even as he is righteous . . . Whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin . . . he cannot sin, because he is born of God . . . everyone that loveth is born of God, and knoweth God . . . Whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God . . . Whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world . . . he sinneth not; but he that is begotten of God keepeth himself, and that wicked one [the old man, the devil] toucheth him not" (1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7-10; 4:7, 17; 5:1-5, 18).

If these benefits start at the new birth, they couldn't begin months or years afterward. If you had to wait until some indefinite time after the new birth to start acting in righteousness, cease from sin, love, know God, overcome the world, and keep yourself from that wicked one (so the old man can't touch you), then you'd be a sinner until that point after being born-again — and the new birth would mean nothing to you.

Peter also taught that everyone born-again is "holy": "Be ye holy; for I am holy. . . . Forasmuch as ye know ye were not redeemed with corruptible things . . . but with the precious blood of Christ . . . Seeing ye have purified your souls in obeying the truth . . . see that ye love one another with a pure heart fervently: Being born again . . . by the word of God" (1 Pet. 1:15-23). You're made holy, redeemed, purified, and love with a pure heart when born-again, or this Scripture isn't true. Heart-purity comes at the new birth and redemption, so it couldn't come later. We're born-again by the blood and by the Word of God — and that's exactly how we're sanctified (Eph. 5:26; Heb. 13:12). Sanctification and the new birth happen at the same time by the same means.

Some holiness groups believe people are made holy at the new birth, yet they constantly contradict themselves by saying people aren't made holy until they're sanctified sometime after the new birth. If the Scriptures above are true and you're made holy in the new birth (to the extent that you don't commit sin), then you're holy to the fullest extent of living free from the old man, the devil — and this is all that ever needs to be done. This is all God required of Adam before he fell.

The reason the born-again person doesn't commit sin is because he's not "of the devil" (1 Jn. 3:8), and he "keepeth himself, and that wicked one toucheth him not." Therefore, he couldn't have an old man (1 Jn. 5:18). We've seen in Lesson Twenty-three that the old man is the spirit and nature of the devil in people, so if the "born-again one" is free from the devil to the extent that he's not touched by him, then he's as free from the old man as he'll ever be. All he has to do is stay free from the old man as Paul commanded in Col. 2:6-7 and walk in the light as John taught in 1 Jn. 1:7; 2:1, 3-6, 9-11, 29; 3:3-10, 20-24; 4:17; 5:1-5, 18.

Every person in Christ is a new creature (2 Cor. 5:17), holy and free from sin — and they must walk in the Spirit to remain free (Rom. 6:14-23; 8:1-13; Gal. 5:16-26; Col. 3:5-17). If they stay free (which they may do at all times), they never yield to the old man any more than anyone else who claims freedom from sin through other experiences.

John Wesley himself, the founder of "the second-work theory," taught that sanctification begins at the new birth. Here's what he wrote in Page 109, in "Sermons," Vol. 1 of The Works of Rev. John Wesley:

> "We allow that the state of the justified person is inexpressibly great and glorious. He is born again . . . he is a child of God, a member of Christ, an heir of the kingdom . . . peace keepeth his heart and mind in Christ Jesus. His very body is the temple of the Holy Ghost . . . He is created anew in Christ Jesus; he is washed, he is sanctified. His heart is purified by faith. He is cleansed from all corruption that is in the world; the love of God is shed abroad in his heart by the Holy Ghost which is given him. As long as he walketh in love (which he may always do), he worships God in spirit and in truth. He keepeth the commandments of God, and doeth those things that are pleasing in his sight; so exercising himself as to have a conscience void of offence towards God and towards man; and he has power both over outward and inward sin, even from the moment he is justified."

What could be clearer about when sanctification begins? Any other idea is entirely out of harmony with the Bible. Whatever Wesley taught after outlining these early convictions, he never came any closer to biblical truth than in these statements.

We're not arguing that sanctification ends at the new birth, but we must be honest with God and His Word and teach that it begins there. People will constantly need deeper works of grace as they conform to truth and walk in the light. This is just as true for those who claim a special second work of grace as for those who don't. To limit sanctification to one, two, or even three works of grace misses the mark and shows ignorance of the whole truth — it limits a person to a shallow, stationary experience in God.

After all, even those who limit sanctification this way are constantly deepening in grace and power if they're normal Christians. Limiting the constant work of sanctification to some human theory is simply an error of the mortal mind. The Christian development actually happening in these people's lives is a constant rebuke and testimony against such a theory.

4. **When you become God's "elect":** "Elect . . . through sanctification of the Spirit [the new birth by the Spirit], unto obedience and sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ . . . which according to his abundant mercy hath begotten us again unto a lively hope" (1 Pet. 1:2-4; Eph. 1:7; Rom. 8:33; Col. 3:12).

5. **When you are made "righteous, even as he is righteous"** — at the new birth (1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-5, 18; 2 Cor. 5:17; Eph. 4:24; Gal. 5:24; Rom. 8).

6. **When you "are created in righteousness and true holiness"** — when you get in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17; Eph. 4:24; Gal. 5:24).

7. **When you "put off" the old man and "put on" the new man**, "which after God is created in righteousness and true holiness" (Eph. 2:8-10; 4:22-24; Rom. 6:3-23; 8:1-16; 2 Cor. 5:17; Gal. 5:24).

8. **When you become "dead to sin"** and are "raised up from the dead" to start walking "in newness of life" (Rom. 6:2-22; Eph. 2:1-10). This happens when you're "saved" and "created in Christ" (Eph. 4:22-24; 2 Cor. 5:17-18).

9. **When you are "baptized into Jesus Christ"** and into his body (Rom. 6:2-7; 1 Cor. 12:13; Gal. 3:27-29). This happens when we become the "children of God by faith in Jesus Christ" (Gal. 3:26-29. See the Three Baptisms on the chart, in the Age of Grace).

10. **When you cease to live longer in sin** (Rom. 6:2-7; 8:1-13). This happens at the new birth (1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-5, 18; 2 Cor. 5:1).

11. **When you truly reckon the "old man" dead and the "new man" alive** through Jesus Christ (Rom. 6:6-22). This happens when you're created in Christ (Eph. 4:22-24; 2 Cor. 5:17; 1 Jn. 5:1-5, 18).

12. **When you refuse to let "sin reign in the mortal body"** (Rom. 6:12-22). You do this at the new birth (1 Jn. 3:5-10; 5:1-5, 18; Eph. 4:24; 2 Cor. 5:17).

13. **When you quit yielding your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin** — this is at the new birth (Rom. 6:11-22; 1 Jn. 3:8-10; 5:1-18).

14. **When you recognize you're free from one master** (the old man, the devil) and are a servant to righteousness and the new master — God (Rom. 6:14-22). Paul here states that acts of obedience indicate what master is being served (Rom. 6:16), that a change in acts of obedience indicates a change in masters (Rom. 6:17-18), that the one served indicates the nature of obedience (Rom. 6:19-20), and that the nature of obedience indicates the wages received (Rom. 6:21-23). You can't serve God and Satan at the same time, but you can serve both, one at a time (Mt. 6:24). Hence the old man or the devil is not in a saved person (1 Jn. 5:18).

15. **When you are made free from the law of sin and death** (Rom. 8:1-4, 12-13; Gal. 5:16-26). Paul illustrates freedom from sin by the marriage of a man and woman who are bound to each other until one dies. Only on the death of one is the other free to marry again (Rom. 7:1-6).

It's similar to how a widow becomes legally free to remarry only after her husband dies. By the same token, when you become free from sin by the crucifixion and death of the old man, you're then free to marry Christ and enter the new relationship with God under the New Covenant. To claim that the old man isn't destroyed until sometime after the new birth is to compare both Christ and us to adulterers — which would mean an illegal marriage before the old man is dead.

We're freed from sin at the new birth, so that's when we're sanctified and free to marry Christ. If the old man isn't destroyed at the new birth when you marry Christ, then Christ marries someone still married to the devil, who has the devil in them, who is still unclean and lives in sin. This cannot be, for "the temple of God is holy" and "he that is joined to the Lord is one spirit" (1 Cor. 3:16-17; 6:17-20; Rom. 11:16).

16. **When you receive the spirit of adoption** (Rom. 8:1-17). This is at redemption and forgiveness of sins (Gal. 4:4-7; Eph. 1:7). Every adopted son is holy according to Eph. 1:4-5; Heb. 12:5-15; Rom. 11:16.

17. **When you become an heir to the kingdom of God** (1 Cor. 6:9-11). This is so when you're made righteous according to this Scripture and Rom. 8:1-17; 1 Jn. 2:29; 3:7-10; 5:1-18.

18. **When you turn "from darkness to light, and from the power of Satan unto God, that they might receive forgiveness of sins, and an inheritance among them that are sanctified by faith that is in me"** (Acts 26:18). Paul here is telling Agrippa what Christ said to him. If Christ can be depended upon as knowing what He says, then it's clear that people are sanctified by faith in Christ at the time they turn to God, have their sins remitted, and become heirs of God. You'd have just as much right to argue that "forgiveness of sins" is a separate experience from the new birth occurring sometime after it, as to argue that being "sanctified by faith" is after the new birth, because "forgiveness of sins," "an inheritance" and "sanctified by faith" are all spoken of in this same verse as results of turning to God.

19. **When you are accepted of God** (Rom. 15:16). Do people have to wait months or years after the new birth before being accepted by God?

20. **When you get into Christ** (1 Cor. 1:2). This is when you become a new creature in Christ (2 Cor. 5:17; Eph. 4:24; Gal. 5:24; Rom. 8:1-7).

21. **When you become Christ's:** "they that are Christ's have crucified the flesh with the affections and lusts" (Gal. 5:24). You can't belong to Christ until you've done this. But you no longer have the old man when you've done this (1 Jn. 3:7-10; 5:1-5, 18).

22. **When you are washed from all sin before justification** (1 Cor. 6:9-11). Here Paul gives the true order of these phases of salvation: "but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified, but ye are justified" (For proof of this see Points IV, V and VIII, below).

lessons.title: The Truth About Eternal Security
lessons.content:
# The Truth About Eternal Security

Some teachers today, claiming to honor Christ's finished work on the cross, lead people to believe that once you're in God's grace, you'll always stay there—no matter how much you sin afterward. These teachings go by various names, and many people believe them because they sound reasonable. Sometimes they're even championed by well-known Bible scholars with national or international reputations.

Sadly, for too many people, this teaching of "eternal security" actually creates *eternal insecurity*. It lulls them into a "sinning-saint" religion that will bring real disappointment on Judgment Day. Some accept these arguments simply because they don't know the full scope of what Scripture teaches on the subject. Others, being willfully blind, embrace it because they want an excuse to keep sinning.

Now, let me be clear: there *is* a true sense in which every believer can have "everlasting life" and be "eternally secure." These blessings are absolutely real—whenever God's conditions are met. That's what we'll explore in this lesson:

> I. What is the Question Involved in the Teaching of Unconditional Security?

> II. Is Man a Free Moral Agent?

> III. What Is Eternal Life?

> IV. Can Eternal Life Be Lost?

> V. Examples in the Bible of Eternal Life Being Lost.

> VI. Is Eternal Life an Unconditional Possession Now?

> VII. What Are the Conditions for Keeping Eternal Life?

> VIII. Real Bible Assurance and True Eternal Security.

## I. What is the Question Involved in the Teaching of Unconditional Security?

The main question in this whole debate is this: Who bears the responsibility for bringing about this coveted state of eternal security? And we might add: Under what conditions, if any, can it be obtained? Are there "any strings attached," as we commonly say?
