Could it be completely unconditional—or might we find somewhere in the Bible that conditions belong to this doctrine just as much as the blessings do?

The Bible *does* teach conditional eternal security. This is the only kind of security a sensible person can expect and a just God can offer. But to teach *unconditional* eternal security—and to maintain that position in the face of overwhelming scriptural evidence to the contrary—is to make God the most unjust tyrant in the universe.

To hold this view, you have to manufacture and maintain many other false doctrines to prop up the sinking-sand foundation of the main false doctrine. You have to twist, contort, misinterpret, misapply, take from, and add to many Scriptures to support such teaching.

Every Bible doctrine should be measured by plain facts of Scripture and by true principles and examples from life—not by misinterpreting a few texts while ignoring everything else Scripture says on the subject. It's vital to get God's complete thought on a topic as expressed across *all* Scripture.

The devil has always used Scripture to cloak his teachings. He can twist it until whatever he teaches *appears* quite scriptural. Just because a theory has a few passages that seem to support it doesn't prove it's true. Any doctrine that contradicts the true principles of life—or even *one* plain Scripture rightly understood—is bound to be wrong.

Teachers of unconditional security, like teachers of other false doctrines, tend to explain away all Scriptures that don't fit their theory. Their usual approach is to claim that contradicting passages apply only to Jews, not Christians—or to some past age, not ours—and to quickly dismiss other Scriptures written to Christians as irrelevant to the question.

Meanwhile, passages written to both Jews and Gentiles that *can* be twisted to fit their theory get emphasized beyond all true meaning, under the guise of "just accepting what the Bible says."

God designed the Bible so that no single passage contains all the truth on any subject, and no passage contradicts another (Isa. 28:9-13). All Scriptures on a topic must be gathered and harmonized with each other—and with all Scriptures on other subjects—before you can fully understand the whole truth.

No "Scripture is of any private interpretation" (2 Pet. 1:19). That means no Scripture should be isolated from all others and treated as the complete truth on a subject while ignoring everything else written about it. Two or three plain passages are required to establish any single truth, and if there are more than that, they must all testify to the same thing without contradicting each other or any other passages on any other subject (Num. 35:30; Deut. 17:6; 19:15; Mt. 18:16; 2 Cor. 13:1).

We're told to "rightly divide the word of truth" (2 Tim. 2:15) and to compare "spiritual things with spiritual" (1 Cor. 2:13). When you do this with the very few passages used to teach unconditional security, you'll find they're in perfect harmony with the many *hundreds* of passages that plainly teach conditional security.

On the other hand, those hundreds of passages simply cannot be harmonized with the wrong interpretations placed on those few verses. Teaching unconditional security leaves an irreparable gap between two sets of Scriptures, but teaching conditional security brings perfect harmony to all of them.

If every Christian would be willing to believe *all* Scriptures on this subject—and be just as eager to defend all truth—there would be no division over the question of true Bible security.

The truth is this: the Bible teaches a true, just, and lasting security for all believers who put forth an honest effort to remain secure in Christ. Any person can have true biblical security if they'll meet a few simple conditions God has clearly stated—conditions anyone can meet through the means of grace provided in the gospel.

## II. Is Man a Free Moral Agent?

This is the first question we need to settle. If man is *not* a free moral agent, then God can be held entirely responsible for all sin, rebellion, sickness, and all the effects of sin—as well as for the damnation of men and angels. That's what unconditional security teachers end up implying.

One of these teachers says, "No man is an absolutely free moral agent, for he is either led away by the devil against his will or he is a servant of Christ. I do not simply choose to keep myself in a place where I am secure. God has chosen me and I am secure in His choice."

Another says, "Ownership means lordship. That which is owned has no right of will contrary to the will of the owner... Adam was the only true free moral agent. When he sinned he lost free moral agency. No man ever wills to be born in the human race, and equally powerless is he to will to separate himself from the human race... Yet it is argued that man can separate himself from God... It was not his own will to be born-again, but the will of God (Jn. 1:13)... Man has as little to do with that as he had to do with his physical birth. As it is impossible for man to separate himself from the human race, so it is equally impossible for him, by a free act, to separate himself from God's kingdom. There is no such thing as free moral agency in the kingdom of God... To say that a man can will to go away from God and be lost is to make the sovereign grace of God subject to the will of man."

Let me unpack what these statements really mean. They're saying:

- Man has no power to choose to serve God
- Man has no responsibility in serving God or Satan, or in being saved or lost
- It's entirely up to God to choose each person—or not
- Those God chooses are secure by His choice alone, with man having nothing to do with it
- God is a respecter of persons because He doesn't choose everyone equally
- God is responsible for the sins and damnation of the lost, as well as the security of the few He supposedly "delights in choosing"
- Some people can never be saved because God hasn't chosen them
- God is responsible for all the sins, sufferings, and curses in the universe

But think about Adam. If he was responsible for his fall from grace, if he *chose* to fall from his former state, if grace and divine nature didn't automatically keep him saved and holy, and if God left it up to his free will in the beginning—then it's the same with all people today.

There's no passage proving that man is *not* a free moral agent all the days of his life, whether saved or unsaved.

Man is absolutely a free moral agent and serves the devil or God as he freely chooses. If someone wants to turn to God from the devil, he's always free to do so. If he wants to turn from God to the devil, he's always free to do that too. Neither master can force obedience if the person doesn't freely choose to give it. Until death, everyone has the free choice of whom to serve.

Man is ethically obligated by his moral nature to serve righteousness, live holy, and consecrate himself to the same end God is consecrated to—the highest good of all. But man isn't *forced* to live holy. He's free to consecrate himself to the end the devil pursues—a life of self-gratification. He's free to choose either end and to use the means of attaining whatever end he chooses. No one forces him to choose either path.

Think of it like a GPS navigation system. The GPS can tell you the best route, warn you of dangers, and recalculate when you make a wrong turn—but it can't grab the steering wheel. *You* decide whether to follow it or ignore it. That's what free moral agency is like.

A saved person has the same power of choice he had before salvation. His *desires* are changed in salvation, but he can permit the old desires to take hold again. The flesh can gain control over him once more, and he can choose to live in his old sins again.

If he chooses to sin again, he incurs the same death penalty and will be damned as surely as if he'd never been saved (Ezek. 18:4; Rom. 6:14-23; 8:1-13; Gal. 6:7-8; Jas. 5:19-20).

Now, in matters outside the moral realm where man has no choice—like not choosing to be born—that has nothing to do with free moral agency. That faculty kicks in after birth. As he grows up, he's free to choose his own destiny by the life he lives in serving God or Satan. It's only when someone *becomes* a free moral agent that he's held responsible.

To say a person has no willpower when he chooses to go back into sin and becomes overpowered by it—that's false. This doesn't prove he's not a free moral agent. It actually *proves* he is one, because he couldn't have chosen to sin again without free will.

When someone yields to sin, Satan can make him a captive and dominate his life to varying degrees, depending on how much the person submits. A person can completely submit to demons and become their total slave. But at any moment, he can turn to God by his own free will and defeat those same spirit-rebels with God's help.

If he had no power to choose to live right and turn to God, *then* we might argue he's not a free agent in the moral realm. It's similar to how winning a war doesn't prove the losing side had no willpower—the Axis powers chose to make war and chose to surrender, which proves they had will.

If a person can resist sin *at all*, it proves willpower. If he's exercising will *in the least degree* concerning moral things, it proves he's a free moral agent.

Being a servant of Christ doesn't eliminate willpower—it *demonstrates* it! The person couldn't have turned from Satan and sin without exercising that power. To believe otherwise would make God an unjust tyrant holding slaves in worse bondage than Satan ever did.

It's actually Satan who tries to enslave people and control their choices. God always gives them freedom to serve Him. When they voluntarily turn to God, He delivers them from Satan and sin—but He still permits perfect freedom of action regarding whether they'll continue serving Him or go back into sin. Yes, people are called "servants" of God, but it's a voluntary service. There's nothing arbitrary about it on God's part.

True, we have no freedom to choose whether to come into existence, and no freedom to choose to become nothing again—we're eternal creatures and can't will ourselves out of existence. But this doesn't disprove free moral agency. We're always free to choose the highest good for ourselves and others, or to choose selfish ends contrary to that highest good.

If we admit that actions have certain effects, we should also admit these are free moral actions. If a person had no free actions concerning moral living and God alone controlled everything, then *God* would be responsible for all evil and its effects. God would be responsible for sin and couldn't justly judge anyone. He'd also be responsible for death—the penalty for sin—and couldn't rightly execute the sentence of broken law.

Adam never lost the power of choice in the moral realm as long as he remained sane—and neither does anyone else. To lose the power to *be* what we were before the Fall doesn't take away our power of *choice*. That's simply the result of the Fall, of a wrong choice.

We're still free to choose God and righteousness and return to our original state where the effects of the moral Fall are erased. We can be redeemed from those effects and do right again from free choice. Being overpowered by sin was a willing action to begin with—otherwise Adam never would have fallen, unless we want to blame God for it.

Since the Fall, it's been a willing cooperation with Satan toward the same end: self-gratification. No one has to serve sin and Satan even one day if they don't choose to.

If a person never wills to be saved, he never will be saved. This is why some are saved and some aren't. It's God's will that all be saved and come to the knowledge of the truth (1 Tim. 2:4; 2 Pet. 3:9). If God's will were automatically done, everyone would be saved. But God will not and cannot save anyone until that person chooses to be saved and calls upon Him.

God's plan is "whosoever will," and God won't operate contrary to His own Word. However, you're not saved by your own choice alone—there's no saving power in a mere choice. And salvation can't be given by God without your choice (Mk. 16:15; Jn. 3:15-20; 4:13-22; 14:6; Rom. 1:16; Heb. 7:25; 1 Jn. 1:9; 5:1; Rev. 22:17).

Neither can you continue to be saved apart from your free choice (Isa. 59:2; Rom. 8:1-13; 2 Tim. 2:12; 1 Cor. 15:2; 1 Thess. 3:8; Heb. 10:26-29; 2 Pet. 2:20-21; etc.).

This power of choice to get saved and stay saved doesn't make human willpower more sovereign than God's power or grace. It simply means that God, as an intelligent being, made His plan—which includes free wills for people, granting them sovereignty over their own wills regarding whether they want to be saved or stay saved by grace.

God won't break His own law and save or keep people saved contrary to their choice. If this is God's plan, then for God to arbitrarily force people to be saved or stay saved against their will would be completely unjust—it would make God break His own laws.

To teach that people have nothing to do with the new birth and that it's entirely up to God's choice is to make God false according to His own Word. You must make a choice in your spiritual birth because you *exist* with power to make a free choice. In natural birth, you weren't in existence with power to decide whether you wanted to be born.

God has told us that anyone who is a respecter of persons is a sinner (Jas. 2:9). Therefore, if it were left up to God alone to save some and damn others as He sees fit, He would be a sinner Himself. But God is no respecter of persons (Rom. 2:6-16). If a person can't exercise willpower in God's kingdom, and can't sin after being saved, then many Scriptures are false—as we'll see in Point IV below.

You are a subject of moral law and moral government. You're under moral obligation to obey the moral Governor of all free wills according to prescribed law. You're subject to penalties for disobedience and rewards for obedience.

If you have no free moral choice to render obedience or disobedience and meet your own moral obligation, your very existence is an act of injustice on the Creator's part. That you have intellect, reason, sensibility, and powers of knowledge and choice cannot be doubted as long as you're sane.

The freedom of human will in moral action has been denied in theory by those who believe in unconditional security, but it has never been disproved and never will be. The very people who deny free will actually admit it in their arguments while trying to disprove it! The fact that they *freely choose* to deny such a plain fact as free action proves they have it and are exercising it.

The fact that others who are more logical choose *not* to believe their empty arguments—and are honest enough to admit plain everyday facts—also proves that all people have free wills to do as they please.

No one can deny the fact of free action in daily life without appearing insane. In your own daily life, you *know* you have freedom to do right or wrong, to be saved or lost according to God's law. By the necessity of your own nature, you know you're a free agent in the moral realm.

You may try to argue yourself out of this fact, but you might as well try to argue yourself out of your very existence. Both truths remain: you *are*, and you *are free* to choose your own destiny and moral actions at every stage of life until eternity.

You not only have the power of choice, but you have a natural created faculty—your conscience—that tells you what choice you *should* make concerning every moral action. To deny the will is to deny the conscience. By doing this, you can soon make yourself believe you're not responsible for sin and its effects, even though you may sin every day. But it takes more than a false doctrine of unconditional security to make you immune from sin and its effects.

Having will and conscience makes you subject to the moral obligation to keep moral law and obey moral government. Every normal person recognizes these powers in themselves and in everyone else. This assumption is irresistible and universal and cannot logically be questioned—and wouldn't be, unless it's for some selfish purpose to prove a point or excuse living in sin contrary to Scripture.

Regardless of why it's questioned, human free will remains an established fact of our constitutional makeup. The affirmation by millions of people of sound mind that they're responsible for their acts among others and before God shows beyond contradiction that everyone regards themselves—and others—as subjects of moral obligation.

Moral obligation, made possible by free will and conscience, extends to the ultimate acts of the will concerning moral law. You're free to choose obedience to moral law, although you may be overpowered by sin and Satan in some acts because you chose not to serve God and live spiritually alive enough to resist the effects of a moral Fall.

Nevertheless, in these matters you're always free to reject Satan and turn to God to be free from the effects and responsibility of these sinful acts—or you're free to continue submitting to such bondage by your own choice. You have a Helper in God and deliverance in salvation if you choose it. But as long as you choose to remain in rebellion against God, you have no claim on such help.

You're absolutely free to choose the end God is consecrated to—the highest good of all. Or you're free to choose the end Satan and all rebels are consecrated to—self-gratification contrary to the highest good of the universe.

You can choose your own end and the means of securing it. You can relinquish your choice at any moment. You can refuse known necessary conditions and means to an end, or accept them. You can choose holiness as an end along with the means of attaining it, or choose sin and the means to secure that end.

You have absolute preference and choice in all matters pertaining to moral law and obligation. You can choose the means to obey moral law and meet moral obligations, or reject them. No choice can be made without the mind having a reason for it, and in this sense free will extends to the ultimate intention of the mind.

Such freedom of will and conscience is recognized in young children who justify themselves in their actions. Grown people also exercise such freedom and make excuses to justify many acts. In some actions, people are honest enough to admit wrong, while in others they're justified by right and voluntary decisions.

Courts of justice have always assumed that people are free to choose, and they judge on the basis of ultimate intention. Only those considered mentally incompetent are regarded as not responsible for their acts—proving the universal belief in free moral agency as a first truth of reason, as well as of revelation.

The Bible throughout recognizes freedom of will and conscience. Moral obligation extends to everything about us over which the will has direct or indirect control. Our muscles, intellect, senses, and appetites are all controlled by the will. If you can't directly do as you will concerning moral law, you can choose the means of grace that will enable you to obey. Hence, you're a free moral agent in the highest sense of the term.

Moral action is voluntary action, and moral obligation respects the ultimate intention only. Involuntary acts aren't moral actions. Choices concerning the well-being of God and the universe constitute moral obligation, and this is what God demands in His moral law. He can't excuse breaking this law because He's provided all necessary means to obey it, if you're willing to use them.

This is why you're just as responsible for your sins after being saved as before. God requires both in the law and in the gospel that free moral agents choose the same end He's consecrated to. All obligation consists of choosing that end. Virtue is consecration to this end; vice is consecration to the wrong end—self-gratification.

So-called "sovereign grace" and the will of God aren't the basis of moral obligation and free action. Moral law governs voluntary action only. It's your moral obligation to will the best good for all regardless of God's will or grace, because it's the law of your creative makeup.

Whether God commanded it or not, you would by nature be obligated to will, choose, and secure the highest good for yourself and others. God's will merely reveals the rule of action and choice—it never *forces* the choice. This must be your own free choice.

The will of God as expressed in His law is the rule for all free wills. This law defines the rule of moral action, and the reason to conform to it is the highest good of the universe. If God commanded something your reason told you was unwise and not for the good of all, it would be impossible for you to feel obligated to obey Him. Thus reason doesn't regard His law as the foundation of moral obligation, but only as proof that what He commands is wise, best for all, and commanded for that reason.

Moral law makes known what's right and wrong to free wills, but the whole thing is a farce if you have no choice in whether to do right or wrong. Any theory about moral action that destroys human free moral agency is completely illogical, impractical, unnatural, and unscriptural—and must be rejected.

All this fantasy of self-indulgence with dreams of Heaven and irresponsibility here and hereafter is a religion of Satan designed to damn souls, and it should be treated as such. To teach it means making God the one committing sins in both saints and sinners, and the one responsible for some being lost and others being saved.

The Bible never teaches that God saves and keeps anyone contrary to their will. Any action for which you're responsible through exercising your own created willpower—should you make the wrong choice—doesn't make God responsible. It doesn't lessen God's power, will, purpose, or change His attitude or plan to help you, should you change to the right choice and accept God's way and help.

God is limited in blessing you should you rebel against Him. If He weren't, He'd be obligated to force everyone to be saved alike. If anyone is lost, it only proves failure on that person's part, not God's.

It's God's will that everyone be saved, but who *will* be saved depends on the individual meeting the conditions of salvation and of staying saved (1 Tim. 2:4; 2 Pet. 3:9; Jn. 3:15-20, 36; Rev. 22:17). A saved person no longer belongs to God if they rebel and live and die in sin, as we'll see in Point IV below.

The following statements prove that both saints and sinners have the power of free choice, and that God holds them responsible for wrong use of this faculty:

- "Choose you this day whom ye shall serve" (Josh. 24:15)
- "did not choose the fear of the Lord" (Prov. 1:29-33)
- "I have chosen the way of truth" (Ps. 119:30, 173)
- "They have chosen their own ways" (Isa. 66:3)
- "he will hold to the one, and despise the other" (Mt. 6:24)
- "I will follow thee" (Mt. 8:19)
- "If any man will come after me" (Mt. 16:24, 25)
- "Whosoever will do the will of God" (Mk. 3:35; 16:16)
- "ye shall not come to me, that ye might have life" (Jn. 5:40)
- "If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine" (Jn. 7:17; 9:31)
- "If any man serve me, him will my Father honour" (Jn. 12:26)
- "hath power over his own will" (1 Cor. 7:37)
- "If I do this thing willingly" (1 Cor. 9:17)
- "not by constraint, but willingly" (1 Pet. 5:2)
- "whosoever will" (Rev. 22:17)

These and literally hundreds of similar passages prove free moral agency. The words "choice," "will," and similar terms are used over 4,000 times in Scripture. Other words expressing power of choice in all phases of life are used thousands of times. To deny free moral agency for all people—saved or unsaved—is the rankest ignorance and willful blindness to plain facts.

## III. What is Eternal Life?

Eternal life is not the physical life of the body. We have physical life only as long as the soul and spirit remain in the body. Death in Scripture means *separation* from the purpose for which a creature was created.

Physical death is the separation of the inner person from the body. When this happens, you lose physical life (Jas. 2:26).

Eternal life is not the natural immortality or consciousness of the soul and spirit. Both saved and unsaved exist forever and are fully conscious after physical death (Lk. 16:19-31; Mt. 10:28; 17:3; Lk. 20:38; 23:43; Phil. 1:21-23; 2 Cor. 5:8; Rev. 6:9-11; Heb. 12:23; 1 Pet. 3:4; Jn. 11:24-25; 14:19).

The soul exists and is conscious even though it's "dead" (Eph. 2:1-10; Mt. 8:22; 1 Tim. 5:6). Spiritual death is the separation of the inner person from God because of sin (Isa. 59:2; Eph. 2).

So eternal life can't be the natural eternal life or consciousness of the inner person either in or out of the body. Eternal life can't be the eternal existence or consciousness of souls in eternal Hell, because only the righteous have eternal life by abiding in Christ (Jn. 3:15-18, 36; 5:24; 15:1-7; 1 Jn. 2:24, 25; 5:11).

Eternal death is eternal *separation* from God (Mt. 10:28; 25:41, 46; Rev. 2:11; 14:9-11; 20:11-15; 21:8; Isa. 66:22-24). Eternal life is the opposite—eternal *union* with God (Jn. 17:3; 1 Jn. 2:24-25; 5:11-12; 1 Cor. 6:17).

It was Adam in his sinless spiritual state who was told, "in the day that thou eatest thereof thou shalt surely die" (Gen. 2:17; 3:1-19). The Greek word for "life" is *zoe*, used thirty times with "eternal" and fourteen times with "everlasting."

One writer tries to prove that believers receive *zoe* life and that this isn't the kind of life Adam had. But 1 Cor. 15:45 states that Adam had *zoe* ("living") soul.

Others argue that Adam's life was natural and forfeitable, not spiritual and eternal like what believers have today. But the word translated "living" *is* zoe, used of Adam in that passage. It's also translated "living" in connection with God:

- "the living [zoe] God" (Mt. 16:16; 22:32; 26:63)
- "living [zoe] water... springing up into everlasting life" (Jn. 4:10, 14)
- "living [zoe] bread" that gives eternal life (Jn. 6:51, 54)
- "living [zoe] waters" referring to the Holy Spirit (Jn. 7:37-39)
- "living [zoe] way" that leads to life (Heb. 10:20)

Do God and all these things have "natural and forfeitable" life? If so, then that's what Adam had.

Eternal life was given to Adam on condition that he remain in union with God and not sin. God said if he sinned, he would die—be separated from God and lose that life (Gen. 2:17). Sin was the only thing that could and did take his eternal life away (Rom. 5:12-21; 1 Cor. 15:21, 22).

Since Adam could have lived forever if he hadn't sinned, he had eternal life. If he had eternal life, he lost it by sin. If he could lose it by sin, then all others who regain it can also lose it again by sin.

What kind of life did he lose the day he sinned, if not eternal life? According to Lesson Nine, Point VIII, 4, the penalty for sin was eternal death. So he had to lose the opposite of eternal death—eternal life.

It couldn't have been merely natural life he lost that day, because he lived 248,652 literal days after that (Gen. 5:5). If Adam's body didn't die the day he sinned, then it must have been his soul that died and was cut off from living forever.

Adam's soul lost eternal life the day he sinned. Since it couldn't be natural life he lost, it must be spiritual and eternal life, canceled by eternal death.

Adam's soul didn't lose its own life or consciousness, or it would have stopped functioning. The soul is the life of the body—the body can't function without it. Adam certainly didn't lose physical life since his body kept functioning for 930 years. Therefore, it must have been spiritual and eternal life that he lost.

A person's natural life exists of itself, separate from God. Sinners continue to exist separate from God (Isa. 59:2; Eph. 2). Spiritual and eternal life can't be given to anyone until they're resurrected from sins and united to God's Spirit (1 Cor. 6:17; 12:12, 23; Rom. 8:9; Eph. 2:1-9). This proves that spiritual and eternal life are something apart from natural life.

Think of it like electricity in a house. The house exists whether it's connected to the power grid or not—but it only has *electricity* when it's connected. If the power is cut off, the house still stands, but the lights are dead. Eternal life is like that connection to God's power; sin cuts off that connection.

If eternal life were a *part* of you, it couldn't be lost without losing yourself. But as long as something isn't part of you, it *can* be lost. The fact that eternal life is God's life—given to you as something separate from yourself—proves it can be lost. Since it's only *in God* that you have eternal life, to be separated from Him is to lose that life.

Since only sin separates from God (Isa. 59:2) and causes death (Gen. 2:17; Ezek. 18:4), only sin can cause you to lose this life (Rom. 8:12, 13).

Eternal life is the opposite of eternal death. Since eternal death is eternal separation from God (as proven in Lesson Nine, Point VIII, 4), eternal life is eternal union with God by the cancellation of the eternal death penalty.

It's God's life, and it's never *your* life if you're not dwelling in God. It's yours only when you get into Christ, and only as long as you abide in Him (1 Jn. 2:24; 5:11, 12). It's therefore something separate from you, imparted only when you're saved and as long as you're saved.

Christ is our life, and we have it as long as we have Him (Col. 2:6, 7; 3:4; Jn. 15:1-7). We're free from the death penalty as long as we live holy and free from sin. When we go back into sin, we incur the death penalty, we die again, and we lose the eternal life that's only in Christ. This will be proven by many Scriptures in Point IV below.

If this separation from sin is only temporary, the life with God will be only temporary. But

lessons.title: Fifteen Great Covenants of Scripture
lessons.content:
# Fifteen Great Covenants of Scripture and British-Israelism

A covenant is simply an agreement made between two or more people. Throughout Scripture, we find fifteen major covenants — contracts made between God and humanity that reveal how these parties relate to one another. Even when certain conditions aren't spelled out directly, they're understood between the parties involved.

Let's walk through each of these fifteen covenants together.

## I. The Solaric Covenant (Gen. 1:14-18)

This first covenant was made between God and humanity, promising eternal seasons of fruitfulness and the continuation of the natural world. These blessings would last as long as the solar system itself endures.

Think of it like a landlord promising a tenant that the building's utilities will keep running as long as the building stands. God connected this promise to Noah, David, and others as we see in Gen. 8:22; Jer. 31:35-37; 33:19-26; Ps. 89:34-37.

## II. The Edenic Covenant (Gen. 1:26–3:24)

God made this covenant with Adam and Eve before the Fall, and it was conditional upon their remaining faithful to Him (Gen. 2:17). The terms of this agreement required humanity to:

> 1. Replenish the Earth (Gen. 1:28).

> 2. Subdue the Earth and rule over God's creation (Gen. 1:28; Ps. 8).

> 3. Work the ground and care for the Garden (Gen. 2:15).

> 4. Stay away from the Tree of Knowledge (Gen. 2:17).

> 5. Understand that disobedience meant death (Gen. 2:17).

## III. The Adamic Covenant (Gen. 3:14-19)

This agreement came after the Fall but before Adam and Eve were expelled from the Garden. It marked the beginning of what we call the Age of Conscience. The covenant had two main parts:

> 1. The five-fold curse:

> (1) On the serpent (Gen. 3:14, 15; Isa. 65:25).

> (2) On Satan (Gen. 3:15; Jn. 12:31; Col. 2:14-17; Heb. 2:14-18).

> (3) On the woman (Gen. 3:16; 1 Cor. 11:3; 14:34; 1 Tim. 2:11-15).

> (4) On the man (Gen. 3:17-19; Ps. 90:9, 10; Rom. 5:12-21).

> (5) On the ground itself (Gen. 3:17-19; Rom. 8:18-23).

> 2. The Promise (Gen. 3:15). This included complete redemption and the eventual removal of the curse (Rev. 22:3; Rom. 8:18-23).

## IV. The Cainic Covenant (Gen. 4:11-15)

God made this contract with Cain, promising that he would be protected. Anyone who found and killed him would face sevenfold vengeance.

## V. The Noahic Covenant (Gen. 8:20–9:29)

After the flood, God established this covenant with Noah and even the animals of the field. It ushered in the Age of Human Government. Here are the terms:

> 1. God would never again curse the ground and living creatures "while the earth remaineth" (Gen. 8:22; 9:12, 16).

> 2. Humanity should replenish the Earth forever (Gen. 9:1, 12, 16).

> 3. Humanity should rule the Earth (Gen. 9:2, 3).

> 4. Animals could be eaten, but not their blood (Gen. 9:3, 4).

> 5. Capital punishment was established for murderers (Gen. 9:5, 6; Num. 35).

> 6. The rainbow would serve as the sign of this covenant (Gen. 9:12-17).

> 7. This covenant would last forever (Gen. 9:12, 16).

## VI. The Abrahamic Covenant (Gen. 12:1-13)

When God saw that dealing with the whole human race at once wasn't working after the confusion of tongues, He made this covenant with Abraham. It marked the beginning of the Age of Promise (Gen. 13:14-18; 15:1-21; 17:4-8; 22:15-18; 26:1-5; 28:3, 10-15). This covenant had two parts:

> 1. The sevenfold promise:

> (1) "I will make of thee a great nation" (Gen. 12:1-3; 13:16; 17:18-20; 24:34, 35; Gal. 3).

> (2) "Make thy name great" (Gen. 12:1-3; Exod. 2:24, 25; 6:3-8).

> (3) "Thou shalt be a blessing" (Gen. 12:1-3; Gal. 3:13, 14).

> (4) "I will bless them that bless thee" (Gen. 12:1-3; Mt. 25:31-46).

> (5) "I will bless thee" (Gen. 13:14-18; 15:18-21; Gal. 3).

> (6) "I will curse them that curse thee" (Zech. 14; Mt. 25:31-46).

> (7) "In thee shall all the nations of the world be blessed" (Deut. 28:8-14; Isa. 60:3-5; 66:18-21; Gal. 3:16; Jn. 8:56-58).

> 2. The sign of the covenant: circumcision (Gen. 17:1-21; Isa. 24:5).

## VII. The Hagaric Covenant (Gen. 16:7-14)

God made this covenant with Hagar and her son Ishmael, extending for many generations. It contained three parts:

> 1. Commands:

> (1) Return and submit to Sarah (Gen. 16:9).

> (2) Name her son Ishmael, meaning "God shall hear" (Gen. 16:11).

> 2. Promises:

> (1) Multiply her descendants beyond counting (Gen. 16:10; 17:20).

> (2) Blessings on Ishmael to become a great nation (Gen. 17:20; 21:17-18).

> (3) Ishmael would father twelve princes, just like Jacob would (Gen. 17:20; 25:12-18).

> 3. Prophetic revelation:

> (1) Ishmael would be a wild man (Gen. 16:12).

> (2) His hand would be against every man (Gen. 16:12).

> (3) Every man's hand would be against him (Gen. 16:12).

> (4) He would dwell in the presence of his brethren (Gen. 16:12).

## VIII. The Sarahic Covenant (Gen. 17:15-19; 18:9-15)

God made this covenant with Sarah, promising blessings to her and her offspring, Isaac, for many generations. The covenant had two parts:

> 1. Commands:

> (1) Change her name from Sarai to Sarah, meaning "princess" (Gen. 17:15).

> (2) Name her son Isaac (Gen. 17:19).

> 2. Promises:

> (1) Bless Sarah with a son (Gen. 17:16-19; 19:10-15).

> (2) Make her a mother of nations (Gen. 17:16).

> (3) Make her a mother of many kings (Gen. 17:16).

> (4) Continue the Abrahamic Covenant with Isaac (Gen. 17:19).

> (5) Continue the Abrahamic Covenant with Isaac's descendants forever (Gen. 17:19).

## IX. The Healing Covenant (Exod. 15:26; 23:25)

God made this covenant with Israel and anyone who chose to come under Moses' covenant. It had two parts:

> 1. Commands:

> (1) Diligently listen to God's voice (Exod. 15:26; Lev. 26:14-15).

> (2) Do what is right in His sight (Exod. 15:26).

> (3) Pay attention to His commandments (Exod. 15:26).

> (4) Keep all His statutes (Exod. 15:26; Lev. 26:3; 14-15).

> (5) Serve the Lord (Exod. 23:25).

> 2. Promises:

> (1) "I will put none of the diseases upon thee" (Exod. 15:26).

> (2) "I am the Lord that healeth thee" (Exod. 15:26).

> (3) "I will take away sickness from the midst of thee" (Exod. 23:25).

> (4) This covenant became part of the New Covenant, for Christ "took our infirmities and bare our sicknesses" (Isa. 53; Mt. 8:17; 1 Pet. 2:24).

## X. The Palestinian Covenant (Deut. 27:1–30:20)

God made this covenant with Israel through Moses, and it was conditional upon Israel's obedience (Deut. 11:8-32; 27:1–30:20; Lev. 26:1-46). It has seven parts:

> 1. Dispersion for disobedience (Deut. 30:1; 28:63-68).

> 2. Repentance while scattered among the nations (Deut. 30:2; Zech. 12:10-14).

> 3. The return of the Lord (Deut. 30:3; Acts 15:14-17; Zech. 14).

> 4. Restoration to the land (Deut. 30:5; Isa. 11:1-12; Mt. 24:31; Ezek. 37).

> 5. National conversion (Deut. 30:6; Rom. 11:26, 27; Zech. 12:10-13; Isa. 66).

> 6. Judgment on Israel's oppressors (Deut. 30:7; Mt. 25:31-46; Zech. 14).

> 7. National prosperity (Deut. 30:9-10; Rom. 11).

## XI. The Covenant of Levi (Num. 25:10-14)

God gave this covenant through Moses to Phinehas, the son of Levi, who was zealous for the Lord and executed judgment upon rebels (Num. 25:1-9). It contained two great promises:

> 1. A covenant of peace and blessing to Levi's house (Num. 25:12).

> 2. An everlasting priesthood (Num. 25:13).

## XII. The Covenant of Salt (Lev. 2:13; Num. 18:19)

God made this covenant with Israel concerning the sacrifices they were to offer forever. You can see how salt is used symbolically in Mk. 9:49, 50; Col. 4:6.

Here's something interesting: In Palestine and the surrounding regions, salt was used when making covenants. If two people — even the worst of enemies — ate food with salt together, they became friends. It's similar to how we might seal a business deal with a handshake today.

The Arab expression is "There is salt between us" or "He has eaten of my salt," meaning they've shared hospitality that creates a lasting bond. Since covenants were typically confirmed at sacrificial meals and salt was always present, this covenant of salt represented the everlasting friendship between God and His people (Num. 18:19).

## XIII. The Davidic Covenant (2 Sam. 7:1-17)

God made this covenant with David and his descendants through Nathan the prophet. Like the other covenants, it was conditioned upon obedience. This everlasting covenant contains seven blessings:

> 1. A Davidic house forever (2 Sam. 7:13-16; Ps. 89:20-37; Lk. 1:32-35).

> 2. A Davidic throne forever (2 Sam. 7:12-16; Isa. 9:6, 7; Lk. 1:32-35).

> 3. A Davidic kingdom forever (2 Sam. 7:12-16; Isa. 9:6, 7; Lk. 1:32-35).

> 4. A secure land for Israel forever (2 Sam. 7:10; Gen. 17).

> 5. Freedom from affliction by other nations forever (2 Sam. 7:10; Deut. 28:1–30:10).

> 6. The fatherly care of God forever (2 Sam. 7:14; 2 Cor. 6:15-18).

> 7. An eternal covenant (2 Sam. 7:10-16; Isa. 9:6, 7; Lk. 1:32, 33).

## XIV. and XV. The Old and New Covenants

The Old and New Covenants, along with the relationship between law and grace, were covered in Lesson Thirty-two.

## XVI. Examining British-Israelism

Now let's turn our attention to this subject with a sincere desire to help you see what the Bible actually teaches about the Anglo-Saxon theory. Here's what this teaching claims:

> 1. The ten tribes of Israel from the Northern Kingdom lost their identity for centuries, and the British and Anglo-Saxons are actually those lost tribes.

> 2. The king of England is the true descendant and legal heir to David's throne over the ten lost tribes — but not over the Jews.

> 3. All the promises made to Israel are being or will be fulfilled in the English-speaking peoples, not in the Jews.

> 4. There's a vital distinction between Israel and the Jews, and each has a different destiny.

> 5. The terms "Israel," "Judah," "Jews," "house of Israel," and "house of Judah" are never used interchangeably in Scripture.

> 6. Because Anglo-Saxons are so great and powerful, they must be the ten lost tribes of the house of Israel.

> 7. The Israel mentioned in Isaiah, Jeremiah, all the prophets, the Gospels, Acts, and the Epistles had no tribe of Judah in it.

> 8. The Jews descended only from the tribe of Judah.

> 9. Jesus Christ and none of the apostles were Jewish.

### The Following Points Prove Anglo-Saxonism to Be Unscriptural:

> 1. Judah was one of the twelve sons of Abraham through Isaac and Jacob. He was of the same stock and race as all Israelites (Gen. 29:28-35; 30:1-24).

> 2. Judah was chosen as the ruling tribe among the twelve (Gen. 49:8-10; 2 Sam. 7; 1 Ki. 12:16-19; Mic. 5:1, 2; etc.).

> 3. Balaam, who was hired by Balak to curse the twelve tribes of Israel as they came out of Egypt, said of all Israel, "The people shall dwell alone, and shall not be reckoned among the nations" (Num. 23:7-12). Think about this: Anglo-Saxons throughout history have never dwelled alone or separate from other nations. They've always been counted among the nations of the world.

> 4. Israel outside the Promised Land was never supposed to receive the covenant blessings (Gen. 28:15; Deut. 5:16; 11:9; 25:19; 28:8, 11; 31:13; etc.). The tribes of Israel are scattered among the nations today because of a curse (Lev. 26:32-39; Deut. 28:63-65; Jer. 4:23-27). Prophecy tells us that this same promised land will be given back to them in the Millennium, when all twelve tribes will become one nation again in that land (Isa. 11:10-15; Ezek. 20:33-35; 37:1-28; 43:7; 47:13–48:35; Mt. 24:31; Rom. 11:25-29; Rev. 7:1-7). If Anglo-Saxons were Israel, this would require the people of England, America, Ireland, Scotland, Sweden, and other countries to leave their lands and all move back to Palestine!

> 5. Israel outside the land was to be "few in number" (Deut. 4:26-27; 28:63-68). Weren't these promises spoken to all twelve tribes? This has been and continues to be fulfilled with the Jews, so why wouldn't the same apply to the ten tribes who also disobeyed and were cursed? If the Jews fulfill this prophecy and Anglo-Saxons don't, then which group is actually the Israel upon whom this curse was pronounced?

> 6. The penalty for being uncircumcised was being "cut off" (Gen. 17:10–34:15, 24; Exod. 12:48). Every uncircumcised man was to be excluded from the covenants and promises. Circumcision was a physical sign of an everlasting covenant. So here's the question: If Anglo-Saxons are enjoying the benefits of the Old Covenant, why aren't they circumcised? If the penalty was being "cut off," then uncircumcised Anglo-Saxons have been cut off — no matter how much they claim to be the rightful heirs. And if they're under the New Covenant and don't need circumcision, they can't simultaneously be under the Old Covenant receiving its blessings. Under the New Covenant, they have no more privileges than any other nation, since all people from all nations share equally in the New Covenant blessings (Jn. 3:3-5, 14-18; 3:36; 5:24; Acts 10:11-24, 34, 35; 11:5-18; 13:44-52; 15:7-11; Rom. 1:16; 2:9-11; 10:9-13; 1 Cor. 12:13; Gal. 3:28; Col. 3:11).

> 7. The godly people from the ten tribes stayed loyal to David when the kingdom divided (1 Ki. 12:17-23; 2 Chron. 11:5-17; 10:17; 15:9; 16:1-6; 30:1-11; etc.). The ungodly were cut off and lost their place in the covenant fulfillment (2 Ki. 17:7-23) until they repent at Christ's second coming (Rom. 11:25-29; Acts 15:13-18; Mt. 23:37-39).

> 8. The ten tribes continually returned to Judah and the worship of the true God, becoming part of the Southern Kingdom. That's why they could be called Jews just as much as the Benjamites and others (1 Chron. 9:3; 2 Chron. 10:17; 11:3-17; 15:9; 16:1; 19:8; 23:2; 29:24; 30:1-25; 31:6; 35:17, 18). These passages prove that Israelites from all ten tribes returned to Judah when Jeroboam set up idols, and all of these from the twelve tribes were called Jews. Remember, the ten tribes left the Northern Kingdom "in abundance" and settled in Judah's cities. They weren't taken captive to Assyria with the Northern Kingdom but were later taken to Babylon with the Southern Kingdom. So we have all twelve tribes still represented in the kingdom of Judah.

> 9. The captives returning from Babylon are called "all Israel" (Ezra 2:2, 59, 70; 3:1; 6:21; 7:10-13; 8:25; 9:1; 10:1-5, 10, 25; Neh. 2:10; 7:7, 61, 73; 8:17; 10:33; 11:3; 12:47). This shows that the Jews after the captivities still represented "all Israel."

> 10. "All Israel" was permitted to return from Babylon because they were taken captive together, as these passages explain (Ezra 1:3-5; 1 Chron. 9:1-3; Ezra 2:2, 59, 70; 3:1; 6:16, 17, 21; 7:7-10).

> 11. The Southern Kingdom after the ten tribes' captivity is called "Israel" (2 Chron. 31:1-6, 8, 20; 34:6, 7, 9, 33; 35:3, 17, 18, 25).

> 12. Tribes other than Judah were called "Jews" during the captivities (Esther 2:5; 3:4-6; 5:13; 6:10; 8:7-17; 9:1-32; 10:1-3). Were these multitudes of Jews in all 120 provinces of Persia from the tribe of Judah only? Of course not!

> 13. People from other nations became "Jews" during the captivities (Esther 8:17). The word "Jews" doesn't refer only to nationality or the tribe of Judah, as British-Israelism teaches. If other nations could become Jews, why couldn't other tribes of Israel be called Jews, even though they weren't born into the tribe of Judah?

> 14. Men from tribes other than Judah are called Jews. Jesus was a Jew from the tribe of Judah (Jn. 4:9; Rev. 5:6; Heb. 7:14). The apostles were Jews (Acts 10:28; Gal. 2:11-15). Paul was a Benjamite and was called a Jew (Acts 21:39; 22:3; Phil. 3). Men from all the tribes of Israel, as well as Gentile converts, are called Jews in Acts 2:5. This proves that "Jew" doesn't mean only those born into the tribe of Judah. It also proves that Christ and the apostles were Jews.

> 15. In the Palestinian Covenant, God said it would apply to "all the men of Israel" (Deut. 29:9-12). It also included Gentiles living within their borders. This proves that Judah is just as much God's chosen people as the ten tribes. All the curses applied to the ten tribes as well as the two tribes (Deut. 29:18-30). This directly contradicts Anglo-Israel teaching, which claims the curses were fulfilled only with the Jews. All twelve tribes were to be scattered among the nations the same way, and all were to remember the broken covenant while among the nations and return together. "Then the Lord thy God will turn thy captivity, and have compassion on thee, and will return thee from all nations, whither the Lord thy God hath scattered thee . . . bring thee into the land which thy fathers possessed . . . and multiply thee above thy fathers" (Deut. 30:1-20; Isa. 11:10-12; Jer. 31-32; Ezek. 37).

> 16. The word "Israel" is used 160 times for all twelve tribes before 1 Ki. 12, when the kingdom divided. This was the "Israel" that David ruled and that was promised an everlasting kingdom. Judah has just as much right to this fulfillment as the ten tribes — in fact, more, since Judah was the ruling tribe and would always be (Gen. 49:8-10).

> 17. The following terms are used for the same captives from Babylon who represented all Israel: "fathers of Judah and Benjamin" (Ezra 1:5); "fathers of Israel" (Ezra 4:3; 8:29); "elders of the Jews" (Ezra 5:5; 6:7, 8, 14); and "children of Israel" (Ezra 6:21; 7:7, 10, 13, 28; 9:1; 10:5). After the captivities, tribal distinctions broke down because there weren't enough people from each tribe to return and possess all twelve original plots of land. They kept their tribal identity by race but not so much by location. In Ezra alone, the Bible refers to the returned captives as "Jews" eight times, "Israel" forty times, and "Judah" fifteen times. In Nehemiah, "Jews" appears ten times, "Israel" twenty-two times, and "Judah" twenty-eight times.

> 18. Anglo-Saxonism teaches that the Israel of the prophets and New Testament had no tribe of Judah in it. The following points from Isaiah to Malachi prove that the terms "house of Israel," "house of David," "house of Judah," "house of Jacob," "Jews," "Judah," and "Israel" are used interchangeably throughout. If this is true even once, the entire foundation of the Anglo-Saxon theory crumbles.

> In the prophetic books, the terms "Jews" and "Judah" appear 528 times, while "Israel" appears only 487 times. The phrase "God of Israel" is used about 104 times, and in nearly every case it refers to Judah. In many of these 487 uses of "Israel," the word actually refers to Judah, as we'll see.

> Nearly all the prophets from Isaiah to Malachi were written to Judah and Jerusalem. This is true of Isaiah (1:1; 2:1; 3:1); Jeremiah (1:1-3, 15-19); Lamentations (1:3-8, 17); Ezekiel (1:1-2; 4:1); Joel (2:1, 23; 3:1); Zephaniah (1:1, 12; 2:7); Haggai (1:1; 2:2); Zechariah (1:1, 12-21; 2:2); and Malachi (1:1; 2:11; 3:4). Daniel was a prophet of Judah living in Babylonian captivity, and his prophecies primarily concern his own people in the latter days (1:1; 2:25-28; 7:13-14, 18-27; 8:23-25; 9:2, 20-27; 11:40-45; 12:1-13). Hosea and Amos were prophets in the Northern Kingdom of Israel, Micah was a prophet in Judah at the same period, and all three prophesied concerning the whole house of Israel, which was then divided into two kingdoms (Hos. 1:1-11; 2:1; 3:4-5; 4:15; 6:4; 11:12; Amos 1:1-2; 3:1; 6:1; 9:11-15; Mic. 1:1, 5-16; 3:8-12; 5:1-2). Obadiah, Nahum, and Jonah were Jewish prophets to surrounding heathen nations. Obadiah prophesied about Edom and Judah. Jonah and Nahum prophesied about Nineveh's fall. Habakkuk was a prophet in Judah who foretold the Jewish captivities and how God would do a great spiritual work among the heathen during the Jews' dispersion (Hab. 1:5; Acts 13:41).

> 19. In Isaiah, Judah is called "Israel" about 93 times (1:1, 3; 4:2-3; etc.). Zion, or the Jews, are called God's chosen people (Isa. 51:16; 65:9). They will be blessed forever (Isa. 11:10-12; 65:18-19; 66:7-24; Ezek. 37; Rom. 11:25-29). The kingdom of Judah and David was to be cut off because of sin until the Messiah comes, when it will be restored forever (Isa. 9:6-7; Jer. 21:24-30; 32:37-44; 35:7-26; 60:4-5; Ezek. 11:17-35; Rom. 11:25-29; Lk. 1:32-33; Rev. 11:15; 19:11-21; Zech. 14).

> 20. Jeremiah was written to Judah 133 years after the ten tribes were taken captive. He refers to the Jews in Jerusalem before and during the Babylonian exile as Israel 120 times (Jer. 1:1-3; 2:2-4; 4:1-3; 5:1, 15, 20; 6:1-9; 10:1, 22; 18:6, 11-16; 19:1-13; 29:22-23; etc.). Judah is called Israel because it was the only kingdom of Israel in existence at that time. Both the children of Israel and the children of Judah are referred to as being one nation of Jews who would be punished by Babylon, proving that the Jews represented all Israel (Jer. 32:26-36). After the captivity, Jeremiah said all Israel would be gathered again at Jerusalem (Jer. 31:31-40; 32:37-44; 33:14-26). So the Jews who returned from Babylon were from all Israel. Those returning today are also from all Israel (Ezek. 36:16-38; 37:1-28; Rom. 11:25-29; Acts 15:13-18; Heb. 8:10-13). Jeremiah speaks of Israelites from all tribes under Zedekiah as "Jews" and "Hebrews" (Jer. 34:8-15). The term "Hebrews" is used for all Israel before the kingdom divided about 25 times (Gen. 40:15; Exod. 1:15-19; 1 Sam. 4:6-9; etc.) and about 15 times for the same people after the Babylonian captivity. Jews and Hebrews are the same people in the New Testament (Lk. 23:38; Acts 6:1; 2 Cor. 11:22; Phil. 3:5). Both Israel and Judah are described as being oppressed at the same time in Babylon, so they must be the same people (Jer. 50:17-20). Both were to return from Babylon together (Jer. 50:33-34; 51:5-6). The captives who returned under Ezra and Nehemiah weren't the only ones who came back, because Israel was a great nation in 70 A.D. when Jerusalem was destroyed. At that time, in Jerusalem alone, one million Jews were killed and 97,000 taken captive by the Romans.

> 21. In Ezekiel, the Jews under Zedekiah are called the children of Israel, a rebellious nation, and the House of Israel about 82 times (Ezek. 2:3; 3:1-17; 4:13-17; 5:4-10; 6:2-11; 7:2-27; 8:1-18; 9:4-9; 11:1-16; 12:6-28; 13:2-9; 14:1-21; 17:2-24; 18:2-32; 19:1-14; 20:1-44; 21:1-32; 22:1-22; 33:1-20; 34:1-10; etc.). In Ezek. 37, we're told that the two nations of Israel will become one nation again. Since modern Jews are the only ones fulfilling this prophecy by returning to Palestine, they must be the Israel referred to. Not one Anglo-Saxon is fulfilling this and many other Scriptures by going back to Palestine.

> In Ezek. 40–48, we find nine chapters predicting in detail the final restoration of the Jews, or all Israel, to the promised land. The borders run from Ha

lessons.title: Where Are the Dead?
lessons.content:
# Where Are the Dead?

## Part IV: God's Future Dealings with Man (Lessons 37–52)

In this lesson, we're going to explore what the Bible teaches about death and the afterlife. We'll walk through six major topics together: Physical Death, Spiritual Death, Immortality, the Intermediate State, the Five Departments in the Underworld of Departed Spirits, and Eternal Death.

## I. Spiritual Death (Eph. 2:1-10)

### 1. What is spiritual death?

Simply put, spiritual death is separation from God because of sin. It's not about your heart stopping—it's about being cut off from the Source of true life.

"But your iniquities have separated between you and your God, and your sins have hid his face from you, that he will not hear" (Isa. 59:2).

"And you hath he quickened [made alive, resurrected] who were dead in trespasses and sins . . . even when we were dead in sins, hath quickened us together with Christ, (by grace ye are saved;) And hath raised us up together, and made us sit together in heavenly places in Christ Jesus" (Eph. 2:1-10).

Think of it like this: imagine a branch that's been snapped off a tree. The branch might still look green for a while, but it's no longer receiving life from the trunk. That's what happened to Adam when he sinned—he was cut off from his life source.

When God warned Adam that he would die "the day" he sinned, this couldn't mean physical death since Adam lived another 930 years. It had to mean spiritual and eternal death—being severed from God by sin.

Adam's soul lost its true life that day. He didn't lose his natural, biological life, or his soul would have stopped functioning entirely. You see, natural life can exist apart from God—sinners keep on existing, after all. But spiritual and eternal life can only come when a person is resurrected from death in trespasses and sins and reunited with God (1 Cor. 6:17; Rom. 8:9-16; Eph. 2). For a more detailed study of spiritual death, see Lesson Thirty-five, Point V, 18.

### 2. Resurrection from spiritual death

In Eph. 2, Paul tells us about being "quickened" and "raised up" from death in trespasses and sins. The Greek word for "quickened" is *zoopoieo*, which means "to resurrect," "make alive," or "quicken."

This same word is translated "made alive" in 1 Cor. 15:22, where it refers to the physical resurrection of all people. It's also translated "give life" in 2 Cor. 3:6 and Gal. 3:21, and "quicken" in Jn. 5:21; 6:63; Rom. 4:16; 8:11; 1 Cor. 15:36, 45; 1 Tim. 6:13; and 1 Pet. 3:18.

Here's a sobering truth: sinners are considered spiritually dead even while they're physically alive (Eph. 2:1-10; Col. 2:12-13; 1 Tim. 5:6; Jude 12). When they're saved from sin and reunited with God, they experience a spiritual resurrection—they're raised up to walk in newness of life.

## II. The Doctrine of Immortality

Let's look at the biblical words that describe eternal existence—the never-dying condition of both soul and body. These words are used of God and of human beings.

### 1. Immortal

This word comes from the Greek *aphthartos*, meaning "undecaying," "not corruptible," or "immortal." The word "immortal" appears only once in Scripture, and there it refers to God (1 Tim. 1:17).

But here's what's fascinating: this very same Greek word is translated "not corruptible" when Peter talks about "the hidden man of the heart" and the "spirit" of a person in 1 Pet. 3:4. This proves beyond doubt that your inner self is immortal (see also Ps. 22:26).

The word is also translated "incorruptible" when referring to the eternal inheritance and the crown that believers will receive (1 Cor. 9:25; 1 Pet. 1:4). It's used for the Word of God (1 Pet. 1:23) and for the resurrected bodies of saints (1 Cor. 15:52).

So what can we conclude? God, the Word of God, the soul and spirit of man, the future crown, the inheritance of saints, and the resurrected bodies of believers are all immortal and incorruptible.

Now, some might argue that the soul and spirit aren't immortal. But if Peter said the hidden man of the heart—the spirit—is "not corruptible," that settles it for honest hearts. We'd have just as much right to argue that God isn't immortal as to argue that human souls and spirits aren't immortal. The same Greek word is used for both, and if it means "immortal" in one case, it means "immortal" in the other.

The Greek word literally means "undecaying in essence or continuance"—not corruptible, immortal, incorruptible. Whatever this word describes is immortal.

### 2. Immortality

This word translates two Greek words that carry the same meaning as the word in Point 1:

> (1) *Aphtharsia* means "incorruptibility," "unending existence," or "immortality." It's translated "incorruption" when referring to the resurrected bodies of saints in 1 Cor. 15:42, 50, 53, 54. It's translated "immortality" for the life saints receive through the gospel (Rom. 2:7; 2 Tim. 1:10), "sincerity" for the uncorrupt love believers have for Christ (Eph. 6:24), and "incorruptness" for the immortal life saints demonstrate before others (Titus 2:7).

> (2) *Athanasia* means "deathlessness" or "immortality." It's used of God being immortal in body and of the bodies of saints in the resurrection (1 Cor. 15:53-54; 1 Tim. 6:16). Twice this word describes man's future immortal body (1 Cor. 15:53-54), and once it says God is "the only" immortal one (1 Tim. 6:16). This doesn't mean He's the only being with an immortal body—it means He's the Source and Giver of all such life to others. Angels have immortal bodies. Christ and the saints who were resurrected with Him received immortal bodies (Mt. 27:52-53; 1 Cor. 15:20-23; Phil. 3:20-21; Rev. 1:18). All people will be raised from the dead to live conscious lives forever—both the righteous and the wicked—as we'll see in Point IV below.

### 3. Incorruption

This word comes from the Greek *aphtharsia* in Point 2, (1) above. It's used four times of the resurrected bodies of people (1 Cor. 15:42, 50, 53, 54).

### 4. Incorruptible

This word comes from the Greek *aphthartos* in Point 1 above. It's used of the resurrected bodies of believers (1 Cor. 15:52), an incorruptible crown (1 Cor. 9:25), an incorruptible inheritance (1 Pet. 1:4), and the incorruptible Word of God (1 Pet. 1:23).

## III. Biblical Proof of the Immortality of the Soul and Spirit

The Bible makes it crystal clear that your inner self—made up of soul and spirit—is immortal right now. It will continue in a state of consciousness between death and resurrection, and forever after the resurrection. Let's look at the evidence.

### 1. Plain statements in Scripture prove the immortality of the soul

Peter said that the inner man—the spirit—is not corruptible: "But let it be the hidden man of the heart, in that which is not corruptible, even the ornament of a quiet spirit, which is in the sight of God of great price" (1 Pet. 3:4).

In 1 Pet. 4:6, the apostle states that all the dead "live according to God in the spirit." This proves that everyone's inner self continues in consciousness just like God does. We have to believe this or else believe that God is unconscious! If people after death continue to live "according to God" in their spirits, then they continue to be conscious like God is conscious.

David said of Christ, "For thou will not leave my soul in hell neither wilt thou suffer thine Holy One to see corruption" (Ps. 16:9-10). We know that Christ's soul and spirit were alive and conscious while His body was in the grave. How do we know? Because He went and preached to the spirits in prison during that time, as we saw in Lesson Eleven, Point VII, 6, (9).

This is also clear from the fact that Christ's soul went "into the lower parts" and "into the heart of the Earth" (Mt. 12:40; Eph. 4:7-11), while His body was placed in a grave on the surface of the Earth (Mt. 27:57-66). His grave certainly wasn't in the center and lower parts of the Earth!

David also said in Ps. 22:26 that the hearts of the saved "shall live forever." The word "heart" here refers to feelings, will, and mind—the inner person.

The apostles made many statements proving that people continue to live after death: "Who died for us, that, whether we wake [live in the body] or sleep [die or leave the body, Jas. 2:26], we shall live together with him" (1 Thess. 5:10). This plainly says that saints who die physically will live together with Christ.

This fits perfectly with 2 Cor. 5:6-9: "Therefore we are always confident, knowing that, whilst we are at home in the body, we are absent from the Lord . . . We are confident, I say, and willing rather to be absent from the body, and to be present with the Lord. Wherefore we labour, that whether present or absent, we may be accepted of him."

This proves that the inner person leaves the body at physical death and goes to be with the Lord. He's not the God of the dead (the physical body, Jas. 2:26) but of the living (Mt. 22:23-33).

This same truth shines through in Phil. 1:21-23: "For to me to live is Christ, and to die is gain. But if I live in the flesh, this is the fruit of my labour: yet what I shall choose I wot not. For I am in a strait betwixt two, having a desire to depart, and to be with Christ; which is far better: Nevertheless to abide in the flesh is more needful for you."

Now stop and think about this with me. What gain would there be in physical death if the soul and spirit were dormant, lifeless, and unconscious in the grave? How could the soul remain in the body in the grave and yet "depart" from the body to be with Christ? Is Christ in the grave? Is the grave where saints go to be with Him at death?

How could being in the grave be "far better" if the soul isn't conscious? How could total extinction of consciousness after death be "far better" than staying alive to win souls and help believers live right? How could your inner self be "absent from the body" at death if it stayed in the body and both went into the grave together?

Paul speaks of the outward man "perishing" while the inward man is "renewed day by day." He says things that are seen (including the body) are "temporal," but things not seen (including soul and spirit) are "eternal" (2 Cor. 4:16-18). This clearly shows the difference between outer and inner, temporary and eternal—the ever-increasing law of death for the body until the soul separates at physical death, and the ever-increasing life of the soul which is eternal.

Paul refers to "a life after this one" in 1 Tim. 4:8. This surely speaks of life after the resurrection, since he makes clear elsewhere that the inner person leaves the body and goes to be with Christ. He taught that those who continue in Christ until death have eternal life because they don't give up this life for sin (1 Cor. 15:2; Rom. 8:12-13; 1 Thess. 3:8; Gal. 6:7-8). He warns that we must fight the good fight of faith and lay hold on eternal life to be saved (1 Tim. 6:12; Titus 3:7).

If eternal life is possible, then there's no period when life ceases between death and resurrection—except for the body. The truly saved person continues to have eternal life after death, or many Scriptures would be untrue (Jn. 3:16, 36; 5:24; 6:39-58; 10:27-29; 11:25-26; 14:19).

John said "he that doeth the will of God abideth forever" (1 Jn. 2:17). Others confirm the same thing, as we'll see below.

### 2. Only the body lies and sleeps in the dust until the resurrection

Physical death is the separation of the inner person from the outer person (Jas. 2:26). The body is then described as returning to dust and as being "asleep" (Gen. 3:19; Mt. 9:24; Jn. 11:11; 1 Cor. 11:30; 15:6, 18, 20, 51; 1 Thess. 4:13-17).

The spirit of a righteous person goes to Heaven (2 Cor. 5:6-9; Phil. 1:21-23; Heb. 12:23; Rev. 6:9-11). The spirit of a wicked person goes to Hell (Lk. 16:19-31; Isa. 14:9-15; Prov. 15:24; Ezek. 26:20; 31:14-18; 32:18-27).

### 3. Promises that the path of the just shines brighter until the perfect day prove the immortality of the soul

Prov. 4:18 and Dan. 12:2-3 describe increasing glory into eternity. If there were any period of extinction of life, these passages couldn't be true—there would be no constant glory into eternity.

### 4. Jesus said God was not the God of the dead but of the living

"For all live unto him" (Lk. 20:38). Not everyone lives physically unto Him, so this must mean all live in their spirits unto Him, as stated in 1 Pet. 3:4 and 4:6.

Jesus said of Himself, "I am the resurrection, and the life: he that believeth in me, though he were dead, yet shall he live: and whosoever liveth and believeth in me shall never die" (Jn. 11:25-26).

This can't mean believers never die physically—"it is appointed unto men once to die and after this the judgment" (Heb. 9:27). The promise of "never dying" must refer to the life of the soul, proving the soul is immortal.

Jesus also taught that the soul cannot be killed by man like the body can (Mt. 10:28). If the soul can't be killed with the body, then the soul is immortal.

### 5. The existence of demons or disembodied spirits proves life after death

Demons are the spirits of the pre-Adamite races or of persons other than Adam's race. They're loose and cannot fully manifest themselves except by possessing the bodies of people or animals, as we saw in Lesson Six.

They're not the souls and spirits of human beings—those are confined to either Heaven or Hell at death. But since demons exist and continue alive after leaving their own bodies, this proves that human souls and spirits can also continue in consciousness after death.

### 6. Souls are conscious after death, proving their immortality

We've already seen many statements about continued life after death in Point 1 above. Scripture also gives us concrete examples of souls being conscious after death. Here are a few:

> (1) In Mt. 17:3, Moses appears with Christ—wearing clothes, talking with Christ, recognized by the disciples as the lawgiver. He couldn't have been in his earthly body; that had died and been decomposing for about 1,700 years (Deut. 34). He couldn't have had a glorified resurrected body yet either, since Christ hadn't yet become "the first-fruits" of all who had died (1 Cor. 15:23; Acts 3:26; Col. 1:18). Therefore, Moses had to have his spiritual body, as we explained in Lesson Four, Point II, 1, and the last paragraph in Point II, 7.

> (2) The story of the rich man and Lazarus proves consciousness of souls after death. Both were fully conscious but in two separate places and conditions (Lk. 16:19-31).
>
> Some teach this is a parable, but it's never stated to be one. We'd have just as much right to call the statement about marriage and divorce in Lk. 16:18 a parable! As we saw in Lesson Thirty-one, every New Testament parable has a particular truth it illustrates. If no special point is stated, it's not a parable but a historical record.
>
> In Lk. 16:19-31, there's no particular point being illustrated—this must be a real event showing the true condition of life after death through the actual experiences of two men from very different stations in life.
>
> One man begged for a living but was righteous enough to be saved. He went to a place of comfort to await the resurrection. The other man begged in the next life because he'd been ungodly and unmerciful. He died in his sins and went to Hell—a place of torment—to await the resurrection, judgment, and eternal Hell.
>
> Even if Jesus used this true event as a parable, it would still record the actual experiences of two men who lived, died, and went to two different places. Jesus didn't use lies to illustrate truths like some do today. Since no points are illustrated by this historical record, we shouldn't try to prove it's a parable.
>
> Jesus said "There was"—and if these men didn't live and these things didn't happen, then He told a lie. Since He knew no sin and no guile was found in His mouth, we have every right to believe He was relating true experiences.
>
> This story plainly reveals consciousness after death. The poor beggar was in comfort with water and other blessings. The rich man was in torment in literal fire. He had a tongue, eyes, speech, compassion, feelings, memory—all the faculties proving he had his soul (feelings) and spirit (that which knows) in Hell. Lazarus also had his own form and soul-and-spirit realities that caused the rich man to recognize him as the one who had lain at his gate full of sores.
>
> Nothing in Scripture is clearer than the reality of full consciousness after death—some in comfort, others in torment. As we explained in Lesson Four, Point II, 7, the soul after leaving the body is just as real as the outer person. It can wear clothes, feel, think, speak, remember, act, and be seen just as it did while in the body.

> (3) Christ was conscious during the three days His body was in the grave, as we proved in Point I above. The penitent thief was also conscious and went with Christ into Paradise in the heart of the Earth (Lk. 23:43). Stephen prayed for Christ to receive his spirit when he died—Christ didn't receive Stephen's body, because the disciples buried it (Acts 7:59; 8:2).

> (4) The spirits of just men are described as being in Heaven (Heb. 12:23). John saw the souls of the first tribulation martyrs under the altar in Heaven (Rev. 6:9-11). They had been given robes to wear and were fully conscious, as the passage makes clear. They were told to rest a little longer until the other tribulation saints are killed as they were—then vengeance would come on those who had killed them on Earth.

These examples should be enough to prove that souls and spirits, after leaving their bodies, are fully conscious and immortal.

### 7. The nature of souls proves them to be immortal

Souls are real spiritual bodies, as we proved in Lesson Four, Point II, 1 and 7. Souls and spirits are the spiritual parts of human beings, and as such they're not subject to decay like the body is. They're as spiritual as God, angels, and demons—and just as eternal. (See Lesson Six for a study of spirits.)

### 8. The intermediate state proves the immortality of the inner person

The places where souls go between death and resurrection demonstrate this truth, as we'll see in Point V below.

### 9. The doctrine of eternal life in Scripture proves the immortality of the soul

Eternal life is knowing God and Jesus Christ (Jn. 17:3).

Think of it like this: Scientists say that perfect correspondence with your environment would be perfect life. If there were no environmental changes except those you could adapt to, and if you never failed in adapting to them, you'd have eternal knowledge—and therefore eternal life.

Jesus said something similar: eternal life is knowing and experiencing God and His Son. It's entirely possible for every person to know and experience God and Christ, to have perfect correspondence with them, and to have absolute power to adapt spiritually to the eternal life of God in Christ. This is true immortality—true eternal life.

Right now, your soul and spirit are being transformed into Christ's image "from glory to glory" and "from faith to faith" by the Holy Spirit (2 Cor. 3:18; Rom. 1:16-17).

Christianity and the Bible claim there are certain people who possess eternal life. Everyone who believes in Christ and is made a new creature by the new birth has eternal life (Jn. 3:16, 36; 5:24; 6:39-58; 10:27-29; 11:25-26; 14:19; 1 Jn. 2:24-25; 1 Tim. 6:12). They've passed from death to life (1 Jn. 3:14-15). This life is in Jesus Christ (1 Jn. 5:11-13).

This Christian understanding of eternal life actually harmonizes with science. Both the Bible and science reveal why life is high or low, long or short. Both define the conditions under which life never dies, and explain why things live and why they die. The condition for perpetual, perfect life is perfect correspondence with the eternal God. Such conditions don't exist in the natural world—only in the spiritual, because that which is spiritual is eternal (2 Cor. 4:18).

It's well known that there are degrees of life—some lives correspond more fully with their environment than others. An amoeba, a tiny blob of matter, can only relate to the smallest area of environment. An insect, with its more complex structure, corresponds with a wider area—it has "more life" because it's a higher creature.

Fish, birds, reptiles, and mammals have more and longer lives than insects because they're higher animals with wider correspondence to creation. And human beings? We're the most varied in our makeup—capable of relating to all parts of creation and even knowing the Creator Himself. We can be in perfect harmony with God eternally.

We're the highest type of creature and life under God. We're capable of perfect, eternal correspondence with our eternal Creator. The law of life varies with the degree of correspondence or relationship—from the smallest microbe to the highest living thing. Life becomes fuller, richer, more sensitive, and more responsive as it rises from lower to higher forms, depending on relationship to God and His creation.

When creatures are removed from their native environment, they die. A fish out of water dies. A bird fallen into water will soon die because it's not a water animal. Put the fish back in water and it revives; pull the bird out and it lives again.

Higher forms of life can maintain life longer than lower forms because they have greater powers of adaptation and mastery over their environment. Yet there are always higher powers beyond their control—and when overcome by these, they must die.

Humanity was given dominion over all creation. Had we remained masters of everything, we would have lived forever. But Satan, evil spirits, and sin gained mastery over us, and we had to die. Christ came to restore complete mastery over these powers and to annul death so we can live forever again.

Where there's perfect correspondence with the eternal God, there's everlasting life. Eternal correspondence and relationship with the eternal God brings eternal life. When you pass beyond the finite and material—where your life no longer depends on these things—you enter the infinite, spiritual, and eternal. Because of this correspondence with the spiritual and eternal, you can live forever. Such a life possesses the elements of eternity, provided this spiritual life is maintained and not broken by sin.

To know and experience God eternally is eternal life. To know Him only temporarily is temporary life. If anything cuts life off from God, life ceases and death results. To know God is to conform to Him; to conform to Him is perfect environment; and perfect, uninterrupted environment is eternal life.

Eternal life isn't merely prolonged existence or indefinite continuance of being—the wicked have that. It's knowing and experiencing God and sharing in His eternal, infinite environment. This life is only in God's Son, so those outside Christ don't possess eternal life even though they exist forever (1 Jn. 5:11-12).

Everlasting life has nothing to do with everlasting existence. True life consists of true and perfect environment and blessing—what we were created for. When we fail in our created purpose, we're not truly living; it's not normal.

We were created to live in perfect harmony with God in perfect environment—and we would have, if sin hadn't separated us from that purpose. In Christ, we're restored to this state and may continue forever—or we may commit sin and once again be cut off from this perfect environment, losing eternal life.

Since eternal life consists in knowing God, the more you know Him, the more you truly live. That's why knowledge is stressed in so many Scriptures as essential to higher spiritual living.

When humanity knew only good, we weren't sinful. When we got to know evil, we became sinners (Gen. 2:17). We must know the truth before it can set us free from sin, sickness, and poverty (Jn. 8:32; Rom. 6:14-23). Now we know in part and have life in part, but when we know as we are known, we'll have perfect, full, complete life (1 Cor. 13:9-12).

Those who know God keep His commandments (1 Jn. 2:3-5). Believers are commanded to increase in the knowledge of God to have increased life (Phil. 1:9; Col. 1:9-10; 3:10; 2 Pet. 1:1-8; 3:18). No one can live in or enjoy a realm they know nothing about. In the coming age, "the knowledge of the Lord will cover the Earth as the waters cover the sea" (Isa. 11:9).

According to our knowledge, we can conform to God's will. Of all Earth's creatures, only humans are capable of full consciousness with God and knowledge of His will and life—hence we're the only creatures who attain real, lasting life in Christ. It's the spiritual life that's eternal, because it knows God and lives eternally with Him.

Christ came to give life—real, literal, spiritual, eternal life. You have this life when you have Christ, and you lose it when you lose Christ by sin, as proven in Lesson Thirty-five, Point IV.

Here's the truth: if you're going to inherit eternal life, you must cultivate correspondence with the Eternal. If you stop doing this, you revert to death (Rom. 8:12-13; Jas. 5:19-20; Ezek. 18:4). When someone rejects the knowledge of God and returns to sin against that knowledge, they become ignorant of God again (Rom. 1:18-32). You must walk in the light to stay in the light and have your path grow brighter—turn your back on the light and you go back into darkness (1 Jn. 1:7). Eternal life demands eternal environment; it cannot be maintained otherwise.

### 10. Many other proofs

We'll see additional evidence for the soul's immortality in other arguments below about the state of the dead and the future immortality of the body.

## IV. Biblical Proof of the Future Immortality of the Body

The following arguments prove the future immortality of the body. The inner person is already immortal, as we've seen. But the body is mortal—it will return to dust until the resurrection, when it will be raised to deathlessness and continue forever in either comfort and bliss or in torment.

### 1. The doctrine of bodily immortality has never been disproved

We have more facts and statements proving the future immortality of the body than we have proving what life is, what odor is, or what ether is. Many life problems can't be answered nearly as definitely as the immortality of humanity. It's a law of logic that an assumed, believable faith that hasn't been discredited or disproved has a right to exist.

For example: if I believe in God and immortality and you don't, which has a better right to exist—my faith or your unbelief? You can't prove there's no God or no immortality, but I can prove both. Faith in God and immortality is native to the soul. It has a right to be and grow in human conscience because it's true. It comes from God, from His Word, and from nature—so it must be real.

Belief in bodily immortality has as much right to exist as the little flower, the insect, or anything else that exists.
