Immortality grows in human hearts and is firmly embedded in people of all nations in all lands. This couldn't be so if it had no right to be. Nature has a right to exist because it was created to exist—and since belief in immortality exists in all moral creatures, and since there are thousands of actual demonstrations of it in nature, it must be true.

The soul's longing for continued bodily life proves there's a future life beckoning it. The soul's yearning for bodily immortality proves it will be immortal—otherwise there would be no constant yearning for it. No faculty of the soul can long for something that doesn't exist or isn't possible to have. This passion for longer and future life was placed in us by God—it's a foretaste, a prophecy of what will satisfy us in due time. Nature makes nothing in vain, and God creates nothing without purpose.

### 2. Universal human belief proves we will receive immortality of the body

If such belief is natural to us and part of our creative makeup, it must be true—or God wouldn't have made it part of us. Even the ungodly expect continued existence forever and look forward with dread to judgment and future life.

Relics in ancient graves prove people expected a future life. Indians expected a happy hunting ground and buried arrowheads and vessels with the dead. The Greeks put a silver obolus in the dead person's mouth to pay passage to the next life. Egyptians furnished corpses with the Book of the Dead, the prayer to offer, and a chart of the journey to the unseen world.

The Gauls loaned money expecting repayment in the next world. The Norsemen buried horses and armor for the dead to ride triumphantly in the next world. Judaism had its Paradise. Christianity and the Bible plainly teach a future life for both the righteous and the wicked.

The Hindu expects countless incarnations until reaching the highest form of life. Other peoples in all lands and ages have shown tokens of faith in future life—this isn't a recent or local idea in human hearts.

### 3. Testimonies of the dying—saved and unsaved—prove life after death

Thousands of testimonies at the moment of death demonstrate faith in eternal existence. The righteous have been shown glories of the world to come; the wicked have seen Hell and experienced the torment of the damned in their dying hour.

**Testimonies of Dying Infidels and Wicked Men**

> (1) **Sir Francis Newport** was trained in early life to understand great Gospel truths. In early manhood, his family hoped he would become an ornament and blessing to his family and nation. The result was far otherwise. He fell into company that corrupted his principles and morals, became an avowed infidel, and his life of dissipation brought on an incurable disease.
>
> When he felt death approaching, he exclaimed: "Whence this war in my heart? What argument is there now to assist me against matters of fact? Do I assert that there is no Hell, when I feel one in my bosom? Am I certain that there is no after retribution when I feel present judgment? . . . Wretch that I am, whither shall I flee from this breast?"
>
> An infidel companion tried to dispel his thoughts. He replied: "That there is a God I know, because I continually feel the effects of His wrath; that there is a Hell I am equally certain, having received an earnest of my inheritance there already in my breast; that there is a natural conscience I now feel with horror and amazement, being continually upbraided by it with my impieties, and all my iniquities, and all my sins brought to my remembrance . . . O that I was to lie upon the fire that never

lessons.title: The Seven Judgments of Scripture
lessons.content:
# The Seven Judgments of Scripture

When you think about judgment in the Bible, you might picture one massive courtroom scene where everyone—good and bad—stands together before God. But that's not what Scripture actually teaches.

The Bible reveals that different groups will face judgment at different times and places. Angels and various categories of people will each have their own separate judgment. In all, there are seven distinct judgments revealed in Scripture.

Let's walk through each one, looking at who's being judged, when and where it happens, what it's based on, and what the outcome is.

## I. The Judgment of the Believer's Sin (Jn. 12:31-32)

> 1. **Subject:** The believer as to sin (Rom. 6:10; Heb. 9:25-28).

> 2. **Time:** On the cross (Jn. 3:14; 1 Pet. 2:24; Jn. 17:4).

> 3. **Place:** Calvary (Lk. 23:33; Jn. 19:17-24).

> 4. **Basis:** Christ's perfect work (Jn. 3:16; 5:24; Rom. 6:8).

> 5. **Result:** Death to Christ, justification to believers (Rom. 4:1-25; 5:1-11, 15-21; 8:1-16). This judgment deals with the believer as a sinner.

Here's the incredible news: if you're a believer, the judgment for your sin has already happened! It took place two thousand years ago at Calvary when Jesus hung on the cross.

Think of it like someone paying off your entire debt before you even knew you owed it. Christ took the full penalty that your sin deserved. The result? Death came to Him, but justification—being declared righteous—came to you.

This judgment deals with you as a sinner, addressing the fundamental problem of your guilt before God.

## II. The Believer's Judgment of Himself (1 Cor. 11:31, 32)

> 1. **Subjects:** Believers walking in the light (1 Jn. 1:7).

> 2. **Time:** During this life (Eph. 4:22-32; Col. 3:5-17).

> 3. **Place:** In his own life (Rom. 8:1-16; Heb. 12:5-11).

> 4. **Basis:** Obedience to God and His Word (Jas. 1:22-25).

> 5. **Result:** Chastisement or approval from God (Heb. 12:5-11). This judgment deals with the believer as a son during his earthly pilgrimage.

This one is happening right now, every day of your life. As a believer, you're called to examine yourself—to walk in the light and honestly evaluate your thoughts, words, and actions against God's Word.

It's similar to how a good athlete reviews their own performance after every game. You don't wait for the coach to point out every mistake; you look at the film yourself and make corrections.

When you judge yourself honestly and deal with sin, God doesn't have to discipline you. But when you refuse to examine yourself, your loving Father will step in with correction. This judgment deals with you as a child of God during your journey through this life.

## III. The Judgment of the Believer's Works (Rom. 14:10; 2 Cor. 5:10)

> 1. **Subjects:** Believers (Gal. 6:8; Eph. 6:8; Col. 3:24; Rom. 14; 2 Cor. 5:10).

> 2. **Time:** Between the rapture and the second advent (Lk. 14:14).

> 3. **Place:** In Heaven (1 Cor. 9:24-27; Rom. 14:10; 2 Cor. 5:10).

> 4. **Basis:** Works, both good and bad (1 Cor. 3:11-15; 2 Cor. 5:10; Rom. 14; Col. 3; Mt. 16:27; Rom. 2:6; 2 Tim. 4:14; Rev. 2:23; 22:12). Believers will be judged concerning:

> (1) Doctrines (Rom. 2:14-16; Rom. 14).

> (2) Conduct to others (Mt. 18; Rom. 14).

> (3) Carnal traits (Col. 3; Rom. 1-2; 8:1-13; 14:1-23).

> (4) Words (Mt. 12:32-37; Rom. 14).

> (5) Things that affect others: slander, quarrels, idle words, foolishness, joking, debts, broken promises, wrong dealings, etc. (Rom. 1:29-32; 1 Cor. 6:9-11; Gal. 5:19-21; Col. 3; Eph. 4:1-32; 5:1-33; Rom. 12:1-21; 14:1-23).

> (6) Things that affect themselves: neglected opportunities, talents wasted, loose living, lack of spirituality, etc. (Rom. 2:14-16; Heb. 2:1-4; Gal. 5:1-26; 6:1-10; Col. 3).

> (7) Things that affect God: refusal to walk in the light, disobedience, rejection, failure to cooperate and yield to the Spirit, etc. (1 Cor. 12; Rom. 12; Eph. 4:1-32; 5:1-33).

> 5. **Result:** Reward or loss of reward, but never the loss of the soul for those wrong doings providing they have been forgiven (1 Cor. 3:11-15). This judgment deals with the believer as a servant (Rom. 14; 2 Cor. 5:10, 11; 1 Cor. 3:11-15).

This is the judgment seat of Christ, and it's coming for every believer. After the rapture but before Christ returns to earth, you'll stand before Him in heaven to have your life's work evaluated.

Here's what's crucial to understand: this isn't about whether you get into heaven. That's already settled! This is about rewards—it deals with you as a servant.

Your works will be tested like metal going through fire. What was done with pure motives and in obedience to God will survive and bring reward. What was done from selfishness, pride, or wrong motives will burn away—you'll lose the reward, but not your soul.

> 6. **Illustration:** Analysis of the zeal of a candidate for a crown in glory.

The following analysis was taken from the experience of a prominent Christian worker who was successful above the ordinary minister. His church was crowded, souls were being saved and everything was going along fine.

One day he went home after a successful Sunday at church and fell asleep in a parlor chair. An angel appeared to him and asked him, "How is your zeal?" He was proud of his zeal and answered, "Fine!"

The angel then asked him for his zeal and he pulled it out of his bosom and gave it to him. The angel put it in a crucible and put a fire under it until it melted. He then poured it out on the hearth and it formed strata. He then broke it in pieces and put each piece on scales and weighed it and gave the following analysis to the minister:

**TOTAL WEIGHT 100 POUNDS.** Of this analysis there proves to be:

- (1) Bigotry — 11 parts
- (2) Personal ambition — 22 parts
- (3) Love of praise — 19 parts
- (4) Pride of denomination — 15 parts
- (5) Love of authority — 12 parts
- (6) Pride of talents — 14 parts
- (7) Love of God — 4 parts
- (8) Love of man — 3 parts

This Christian worker said his anger arose and he began to deny the truth of this analysis, but the angel walked toward the door, stopped and looked back with pity in his eyes and said, "May God save you," and walked out the door.

When the minister awoke the conviction seized his heart and he got down and prayed more to be saved from himself than he had ever prayed to be saved from Hell. He prayed until the refining fire of the Spirit purged him of self and thereafter he was a greater success than ever.

This refining process is no doubt the greatest need of the modern church (Rom. 6; 12; Gal. 5; Col. 3; Eph. 4 and 5).

What a sobering illustration! Can you imagine your zeal being weighed and finding that only 7% of it was actually love for God and people? This story reminds us that outward success doesn't equal inward purity—and God sees everything.

## IV. The Judgment of Israel (Ezek. 20:33-44)

> 1. **Subjects:** The living Jews (Zech. 12:10-13).

> 2. **Time:** During the Tribulation (Jer. 30:3-11; Mt. 24:15-22; Dan. 9:27).

> 3. **Place:** Palestine, Edom and Moab (Zech. 12:4-13; 13:1; 14:1-21; Dan.11:40-45; Isa.16:1-5; Ezek. 20:33-44; Hos. 2:14-17; Mt. 24:15-31; Rev. 12).

> 4. **Basis:** Obedience to God and His Word (Jas. 1:22-25).

> 5. **Result:** Conversion of Israel as a nation at the second coming of Christ (Rom. 11:25-29; Zech. 12:10–13:1; Ezek. 22:19-22; Jer. 30:3-11; Isa. 66:7, 8; Mt. 23:37-39).

God has a special plan for His chosen people Israel. During the Tribulation period, the Jewish people who are alive will go through an intense time of judgment in the land of Palestine and surrounding regions.

But here's the beautiful outcome: this judgment leads to the national conversion of Israel! When Christ returns, they will look upon Him whom they pierced and finally recognize their Messiah. What looks like devastating judgment becomes the pathway to restoration.

## V. The Judgment of the Nations (Mt. 25:31-46)

> 1. **Subjects:** Gentile nations (Mt. 25:32).

> 2. **Time:** At the revelation of Christ before the Millennium (Mt. 25:31-46).

> 3. **Place:** Palestine (Zech.14; Mt. 25:31; Rev. 19:11-21).

> 4. **Basis:** Their treatment of Christ's brethren—the Jews (Mt. 25:31-46).

> 5. **Result:** Some saved to go into the Millennium and some destroyed (Mt. 25:34, 41, 46; Zech. 14; Mt. 13:41-50; 24:51; Isa. 53; Joel 3).

When Christ returns to earth, He will gather all the Gentile nations before Him for judgment. The basis is fascinating: how did they treat "the least of these my brethren"—the Jewish people—especially during the Tribulation?

Picture it this way: during the darkest time in human history, some will risk their lives to help persecuted Jews. Others will join in the persecution. Christ will separate them like a shepherd separates sheep from goats.

Those who showed compassion will enter the Millennial Kingdom. Those who didn't will face destruction.

Now, some people confuse this judgment with the Great White Throne Judgment we'll discuss in Point VII. But they're completely different events. Here's a comparison:

| Judgment of the Nations | Great White Throne Judgment |
|---|---|
| 1. Living nations (Mt. 25:31-46). | 1. Wicked dead resurrected (Rev. 20:11-15). |
| 2. Before the Millennium (Mt. 25:41-46). | 2. After the Millennium (Rev. 20:1-15). |
| 3. Christ the judge (Mt. 25:31). | 3. God the judge (Rev. 20:11-15). |
| 4. On Earth (Mt. 25:31-46; Zech. 14). | 4. In Heaven (Rev. 20:11-15). |
| 5. Two classes (Mt. 25:31-46). | 5. One class (Rev. 20:11-15). |
| 6. Some saved (Mt. 25:34; Zech. 14:16). | 6. None saved (Rev. 20:4-15). |
| 7. Some destroyed (Mt. 25:41, 46). | 7. All destroyed (Rev. 20:4-15). |
| 8. No resurrection (Mt. 25:32; Zech. 14). | 8. A resurrection (Rev. 20:4-15). |
| 9. No books opened (Mt. 25:31-46). | 9. Books opened (Rev. 20:11-15). |
| 10. Basis: sins, especially one sin—persecution of Israel (Mt. 25:31-46). | 10. Basis: sins of all the ages recorded in the books (Rev. 20:11-15). |
| 11. One generation (Mt. 25:31-46). | 11. All generations (Rev. 20:11-15; Acts 17:31). |
| 12. Gentiles only (Mt. 25:32). | 12. Jews and Gentiles (Rev. 20:11-15; Acts 17:31). |
| 13. Angels help in this judgment (Mt. 13:42-50; 24:31; 25:31). | 13. No angels mentioned at this judgment. |

As you can see, these are two distinct events separated by a thousand years!

## VI. The Judgment of Angels (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7)

> 1. **Subjects:** Fallen angels (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7).

> 2. **Time:** After the Millennium (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7; Rev. 20:7-15).

> 3. **Place:** In Heaven (Rev. 20:12).

> 4. **Basis:** Sins (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6-7).

> 5. **Result:** Eternal damnation in the Lake of Fire (Rev. 20:10; Mt. 25:41).

You might not think about it often, but angels face judgment too! The fallen angels—those who rebelled with Satan—are being held for their day in court. After the Millennium, they'll stand before God's throne.

The outcome is certain: eternal damnation in the Lake of Fire. Satan and demons will likely be judged at this same time (Eph. 6:12; Mk. 8:28; Lk. 8:31).

## VII. The Great White Throne Judgment (Rev. 20:11-15)

"And I saw a great white throne, and him that sat on it, from whose face the earth and the heaven fled away; and there was found no place for them. And I saw the dead, small and great, stand before God; and the books were opened: and another book was opened, which is the book of life: and the dead were judged out of those things which were written in the books, according to their works. And the sea gave up the dead which were in it; and death and hell delivered up the dead which were in them: and they were judged every man according to their works. And death and hell were cast into the Lake of Fire. This is the second death. And whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the Lake of Fire" (Rev. 20:11-15).

This is the final judgment—the most solemn event in all of Scripture. It happens after the Millennium and after Satan's final revolt. John sees a great white throne occupied by God, and the scene is so overwhelming that Earth and Heaven flee from His face.

The Greek word for "face" is *prosopon*, meaning the countenance, appearance, or outward form. It appears nine times in Revelation and shows that God has a real body and visible appearance (Rev. 4:7; 6:16; 7:11; 9:7; 10:1; 11:16; 12:14; 22:4). This isn't symbolic language—God truly has a face.

Now, you might notice that Christ isn't specifically mentioned here. Does that mean He's absent? Not at all. Both the Father and the Son will participate in this final judgment. Let's look at the various aspects:

### 1. The Judges

> (1) God the Father is spoken of as the judge of all men (Heb. 12:23-24; 13:4; Rev. 6:10; Acts 17:30, 31; Rom. 2:12-16).

> (2) God the Son is also spoken of as the judge of the world (Jn. 5:19-27; Acts 10:42; 17:30, 31; 2 Tim. 4:8; Rev.19:11).

> (3) Both the Father and the Son will be judges (Acts 17:31; Rom. 2:16).

> (4) God the Father will judge by His Son (Acts 17:31; Rom. 2:16).

> (5) God the Father will decree; the Son will execute (Jn. 5:22-27; Rom.1:32; 2:5).

> (6) The saints will have part in this judgment (1 Cor. 6:2-3; Rev. 3:21).

Here's something amazing: you, as a believer, will actually participate in this judgment! Scripture tells us that the saints will judge angels and the world alongside Christ.

### 2. The Subjects Judged

> (1) Wicked men of the whole human race except the beast, false prophet, the goat nations, tares, etc., will be the subjects judged (Acts 17:31; Rom.3:6; Rev. 20:11-15). That those judged at the judgment of the nations will not be judged at the final judgment seems clear from Mt. 13:30, 39-43, 49, 50; 24:51; 25:30, 41, 46; Rev. 14:9-11; 19:20, 21; 20:10. These already will have had sentence pronounced upon them a thousand years before the final judgment, so it would not be necessary to judge them again. The ones judged at this judgment will be the wicked dead of all ages, whereas those judged at the judgment of the nations will be the living nations on Earth at the return of Christ to set up His eternal Kingdom.

> (2) The angels "that sinned" and "are now bound" in Tartarus will also be loosed from their long confinement in chains and be judged at this judgment (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6, 7).

### 3. The Time of the Judgment

The time of this judgment will be after the Millennium and after the doom of Satan in the Lake of Fire is realized (Rev. 20:7-15). It is called "the day of judgment" and so there must be a definite time set for it (Mt. 10:15; 11:24; 12:36; Acts 17:31; 2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6, 7).

### 4. The Place of the Judgment

The judgment is to be before the great white throne of God, which will still be in Heaven, for it is not to come down to the Earth until after the renovation of the Earth by fire and after the New Heaven and the New Earth are completed (Rev. 21:1-5). This seems to be proven further by 2 Pet. 3:7 where it states that the renovation of the Earth takes place during the final judgment. It seems to be pictured also in Rev. 20:11 at the same time of the final judgment.

### 5. The Purpose of the Judgment

> (1) To give every man a fair trial before his condemnation and punishment (Acts 17:31).

> (2) To judge the "secrets of men" (Rom. 2:16).

> (3) To judge all idle words (Mt. 12:36).

> (4) To judge all the works, thoughts, actions and sins of man (1 Tim. 5:24; 1 Pet. 1:17; Rev. 20:12, 13; 22:12; Rom. 2:5-6).

### 6. The Basis of Judgment

> (1) The law of conscience (Rom. 2:12-16; Heb. 10:27).

> (2) The law of memory (Lk. 16:25).

> (3) The Law of Moses (Rom. 2:12-16; Rev. 20:11-15).

> (4) The law of character (Heb. 3:8-10, 15; Eph. 4:19).

> (5) The gospel (Rom. 2:12-16; Jn. 12:47, 48; Rev. 20:11-15).

> (6) All acts and words (Mt. 12:36; Lk. 12:2-9; Jn. 3:18).

> (7) The Book of Life (Rev. 20:11-15).

Here's something profound: a person who goes through this judgment will have no excuse—no legitimate complaint about the sentence. In a sense, each person becomes their own judge.

Why? Because their own conscience will testify against them. Their own memory will recall every sin. The law they knew will condemn them. Their own hardened character will witness to their choices. And most importantly, the absence of their name in the Book of Life will seal their fate.

The "books" mentioned in Rev. 20:12 aren't records kept by some recording angel. They contain God's Word—the standard by which every person will be judged. The Book of Life is mentioned throughout Scripture (Exod. 32:32-33; Ps. 69:25-29; Dan. 12:1; Lk. 10:20; Phil. 4:3; Rev. 3:5; 13:8; 17:8; 20:12, 15; 21:27; 22:19) and contains the names of everyone who will receive eternal life. This book alone is sufficient to condemn—it will show even those who claimed to serve Christ (Mt. 7:21-23) that their names were never written in it.

Scripture doesn't specifically tell us what basis the angels in Tartarus will be judged on, but they probably know. What's clear is that "the dead are judged out of those things which are written in the books" and "every man" will be judged according to his works.

The dead mentioned here don't include angels—they're the occupants of "death and hell." This proves that Hades (the present, temporary place of the wicked dead) is different from the Lake of Fire (eternal Hell). Think of it like the difference between a county jail and a federal prison. The sinner waits in Hades before being formally sentenced to the Lake of Fire—already guilty, already suffering (Lk. 16:19-31), but awaiting final judgment.

### 7. The Nature of the Judgment

It will be one of justice and righteousness to every man (Ps. 9:8; Mt.7:2; 2 Tim. 1:5; 1 Pet. 2:23). This won't be some invisible, ongoing spiritual process. It will be a visible, actual trial happening at a specific time and place (Heb. 9:27; 2 Pet. 2:4-9; 3:7-10; Rev. 20:11-15).

### 8. The Result of the Judgment

> (1) If a person's name is not written in the Book of Life, he will be cast into the Lake of Fire. Hades is the present abiding place for the souls of the wicked dead. It never means the grave where the body of man goes. It is a place of consciousness, where men are in torment until the resurrection, as we have seen in Lesson Thirty-seven. All human beings in Hades and in death, the angels in Tartarus, the demons in the Abyss and every rebel against God will be cast into the Lake of Fire to be punished forever and forever (Rev. 2:11; 14:9-11; 20:10-15; 21:8; Mk. 9:42-48; Mt. 18:9; 24:51; 25:41, 46; Isa. 66:22-24).

> (2) The degrees of punishment will be the result of this judgment, as degrees of reward will be the result of the judgment of the saints at the Judgment Seat of Christ (Mt. 7:2; 10:15; 11:22-24; 12:41-45; 23:12-14; Mk. 6:11; Lk. 10:14; 11:31, 32; Rev. 20:11-15).

Hell, as far as the torment of fire is concerned, will be alike for all the lost—just as Heaven's bliss and comfort will be alike for all the redeemed. But the degrees of punishment will come through the torment of conscience. The inward anguish over sins committed will eat more deeply into a person's being as the ages roll on into eternity.

This is the exact opposite of rewards for the saints, which will increase in glory and splendor throughout all eternity.

### 9. The Length of the Judgment

The judgment passed upon each individual will be eternal. The same words used to describe God's eternity are used to describe the eternity of Hell—if one is eternal, so is the other (Isa. 66:22-24; Mt. 5:22, 29, 30; 10:28; 13:42, 50; 18:9; 23:15, 33; 24:51; 25:30, 41-46; Mk. 9:42-48; Lk. 12:5; Heb. 6:2; 10:26-31; Rev. 14:9-11; 19:20; 20:10-15; 21:8). See Lesson Nine, Point VIII, 4 and Lesson Thirty-seven for arguments why man's punishment must be eternal.

---

## Questions on Lesson Thirty-eight

- Do the Scriptures teach a general resurrection and judgment?

- Name the seven judgments and give a brief study of each.

- Discuss the judgment of the believer's sin.

- Discuss the believer's judgment of himself.

- Discuss the judgment of the believer's works.

- Give an illustration of the result of a believer's judgment of his works.

- Discuss the judgment of Israel.

- Discuss the judgment of the nations.

- Give a number of contrasts between the judgment of the nations and the Great White Throne Judgment.

- Discuss the judgment of the angels.

- Discuss fully the Great White Throne Judgment.

- Prove from Scripture that God has a real face like other persons.

- How can God the Father and the Son both be judges?

- Who else will help in this judgment? Prove.

- Who will be judged at this judgment? Prove.

- Prove from Scripture the time and place of this judgment.

- Discuss the purpose and basis of this judgment.

- State the nature of this judgment.

- Explain fully the result and length of this judgment.

- Prove from Scripture that there will be degrees of punishment for the wicked.

lessons.title: The Book of Daniel
lessons.content:
# The Book of Daniel

Before we dive into this study, let's first lay out 110 prophetic future wonders stretching from 1949 into eternity. We'll also cover some fundamental principles for interpreting prophecy that will help you in all your future studies.

Now, we're not claiming to be infallible ourselves. But we do claim that every simple statement and event in prophecy that God has given us is absolutely infallible.

We claim—for God and by His authority—that the Bible gives predictions of things to come that are 100 percent correct. Not 80 percent correct, as some modern prophets of current events suggest. The following 110 future prophetic wonders are plainly stated, and we'll deal with them in their proper order of fulfillment as nearly as possible.

## 110 Prophetic Wonders From 1949 Into Eternity

1. **Israel firmly established as a nation in Palestine.** This is now in process of fulfillment, as we've already explained in Lesson Eighteen, Points V and VI.

2. **The revision of the Roman Empire into ten separate kingdoms ruled by ten kings**—the result of the first tri-continent war (Dan. 7:7, 8, 19-25). This will mean the defeat of Communism and Russia. In the formation of these ten kingdoms, Russia must give up her control of Austria, Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria, Albania, and all parts of the old Roman Empire. So we can say with definite authority that Russia and Communism will never rule the world.

3. **The rapture of the Church—the end of the Church Age** (1 Thess. 4:13-17; 5:9; 2 Thess. 2:7, 8; 1 Cor. 15:23, 51-58; Phil. 3:20, 21; Rev. 4:1; etc.). It's not clear whether the rapture will come before or after the formation of the ten kingdoms inside the old Roman Empire. The rapture could take place today without any prophecy being fulfilled and without any sign that the end of the age is coming to pass. Both the revision of Rome and the rapture could happen at any time. When each will be fulfilled, no one knows yet. The rapture will take place in a moment, whereas it will take longer for Rome to be revised following the war that causes the ten kingdoms to form.

4. **A world revival or spiritual awakening** (Acts 2:16-21; Jas. 5:7; Rev. 7:9-17; 12:12, 17; 15:2-4). There may be a great spiritual awakening before the rapture, but this isn't definite—nothing needs to happen before the rapture for it to be fulfilled. If there's no religious revival before the rapture, we know from these passages there will be one after the rapture, during the future tribulation days when God shows wonders in Heaven and on Earth, right up to the great and dreadful day of the Lord itself (Acts 2:16-21). We hope and pray there will be a revival in our day before the rapture, but to definitely teach that it must happen is to teach that the rapture cannot come any day before such a revival—and no one has authority to say this. True believers are always ready for the rapture, so a religious revival doesn't have to come to get the Church ready, as some men teach. If we don't have definite Scripture to prove an event will happen, we're not going to make such a prediction.

5. **The rise of the Antichrist** (Dan. 7:7-8, 19-25; 8:8-9, 20-25; 9:27; 11:35-45; 2 Thess. 2:1-12; Rev. 6:1-2). By this we mean the Antichrist coming out of one of the ten kingdoms at the beginning of Daniel's Seventieth Week (Dan. 9:27). This Week, as well as the rise of the Antichrist, must take place after the rapture and after the ten kingdoms are formed (Dan. 7:23-24; 2 Thess. 2:7-8).

6. **The seven-years covenant between Antichrist and the new nation of Israel** (Dan. 9:27). These seven years of the covenant will be the last seven years of this Age of Grace in which we now live. This seven years will be the Tribulation period, as we'll see in Lesson Forty.

7. **The building of the Jewish temple at Jerusalem** (Dan. 9:27; Mt. 24:15; Rev. 11:1-2). We'll discuss this event in Lesson Forty-One. Whether the temple will be built before the seven-years covenant is not clear, but it must be built by the middle of the Week. That's when the Antichrist breaks his seven-years covenant with Israel and does away with their worship in the temple. He then sets himself up in the temple, declaring himself to be God (Dan. 9:27; 11:45; Mt. 24:15; 2 Thess. 2:3-4; Rev. 11:1-2; 13:1-18).

8. **The Revival of the Grecian Empire—the second tri-continent war** (Dan. 7:23-24; 8:8-9, 23-24; 11:35-45; Rev. 13:1-7; 17:8-17). After the ten kingdoms form inside the old Roman Empire from the first tri-continent war, and after these ten kingdoms (the seventh kingdom of Rev. 17:9-12) exist "a short space," the Antichrist comes out of one of these ten kingdoms. He overthrows three others of the ten and forms the eighth and last kingdom, symbolized by the leopard of Rev. 13 and 17. It will take Antichrist the first 3½ years of his seven-years covenant with Israel to overthrow these three kingdoms and get power over the other six, forming the eighth kingdom which will rule for the last 3½ years of this age. We'll explain this more fully later in our study of Daniel.

9. **The rise of Syria to be a world power** (Dan. 8:8-9, 20-25; 11:35-45). We'll see in our study of Daniel below that Antichrist will come from Syria and will make it a world power.

10. **Military alliance between Russia, Germany, and many other countries of Europe and Asia outside of the Roman Empire territory** (Dan. 11:44; Ezek. 38; 39). While the Antichrist rises to power over the ten kingdoms of the old Roman Empire, the countries to the north and east of these ten kingdoms will be preparing for a death struggle with Antichrist and the ten kingdoms.

11. **The fall of Russia, Germany, etc.—the third tri-continent war** (Dan. 11:44; Ezek. 38; 39). In the middle of Daniel's Seventieth Week, when the Antichrist comes to power over the ten kingdoms, war will be declared against him by the countries to the north and east of the old Roman Empire. This war will continue through most of the last 3½ years of the Week. Antichrist will conquer Russia and her allies in the north and east, and he'll then become the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal (Ezek. 38; 39). He'll then lead these newly conquered countries, together with the ten kingdoms and many other nations who cooperate with him through the ministry of the three unclean spirits of Rev. 16:13-16, and make war against Jerusalem to totally destroy the Jews and stop Christ from setting up His kingdom (Ezek. 38; 39; Zech. 14; Joel 3; Rev. 19:11-21). This will result in the second defeat of Russia and Communism at the end of this age. After this, Russia will be defeated by Jesus Christ at Armageddon, for she'll be one of the major powers under the Antichrist in that battle. We can say with scriptural authority that Russia will be defeated in wars three times between now and the Millennium: once in the formation of the ten kingdoms, once by the Antichrist, and once by Jesus Christ at His second advent.

12. **The Judgment Seat of Christ—the judgment of the raptured saints in Heaven during Daniel's Seventieth Week** (Rom. 14:10-11; 2 Cor. 5:9-10; 1 Cor. 3:11-15). (See Lesson Thirty-Eight, Point III).

13. **The heavenly tabernacle—scenes in Heaven after the rapture** (Rev. 4:1–5:14). In this passage we have different scenes in Heaven that will happen just as John saw them centuries ago. We'll discuss these in Lesson Forty-One.

14. **The first seal—the rise of the Antichrist** (Rev. 6:1-2). This event begins the fulfillment of the prophecies of Rev. 6:1–19:21, which are to be fulfilled during Daniel's Seventieth Week. The following points reveal the order of events as to the time of fulfillment from the beginning of the Week to its end. We'll discuss these events in future lessons and in my book *Revelation Expounded*.

15. **The second seal—war** (Rev. 6:3-4). These last two events are the same as those in Points 5 and 8 above.

16. **The third seal—famine** (Rev. 6:5-6). This will be the natural result of the wars caused by the Antichrist's rise to power over the ten kingdoms.

17. **The fourth seal—death and Hell** (Rev. 6:7-8). This will be the natural result of the wars, famines, and pestilences of the previous seals.

18. **The fifth seal—the lesser tribulation martyrs** (Rev. 6:9-11). These will be the first martyrs of the Tribulation period after the rapture of the Church.

19. **The sixth seal—the wrath of God begins** (Rev. 6:12-17). God's wrath begins at this point in the first 3½ years of the Week and continues through the Week, ending when the seven vials are poured out and Christ comes at the end of the Tribulation (Mt. 24:29-31; Rev. 19:11-21).

20. **The sealing of the 144,000 Jews** (Rev. 7:1-8). They're sealed for their own protection from the trumpet judgments which follow immediately up to the middle of the Week (Rev. 7:1-3; 9:4).

21. **The vision of the great multitude of tribulation saints** (Rev. 7:9-17). The martyrs of the fifth seal and all their brethren throughout the whole tribulation period who are killed are included in this company (Rev. 14:13; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

22. **The seventh seal—silence in Heaven for half an hour** (Rev. 8:1).

23. **The seven trumpet angels and the priestly angel** (Rev. 8:2-6).

24. **The first trumpet—hail, fire, and blood** (Rev. 8:7).

25. **The second trumpet—one-third of the sea turned to blood** (Rev. 8:8-9).

26. **The third trumpet—drinking waters poisoned** (Rev. 8:10-11).

27. **The fourth trumpet—one-third of the planets affected** (Rev. 8:13).

28. **The messenger angel—three woes upon men announced** (Rev. 8:13).

29. **The fifth trumpet—the first woe, men tormented five months** (Rev. 9:1-12).

30. **The sixth trumpet—the second woe, one-third of men killed** (Rev. 9:13-21).

31. **The mighty angel—announcement of no delay in completion of the mystery of God and John's further commission and receiving of the rest of the Revelation** (Rev. 10:1-11).

32. **Seven-years covenant between Antichrist and Israel broken** (Dan. 9:27; Mt. 24:15; Rev. 11:1-2). In the order of future prophetic wonders, this brings us down to the middle of Daniel's Seventieth Week when Antichrist breaks his covenant with Israel and enters Palestine (Dan. 9:27; 11:40-45). The following events come immediately thereafter.

33. **Jerusalem falls and the Jewish temple will be seized as the capital building of the Antichrist** (Rev. 11:1-2; Dan. 9:27; 11:40-45; 2 Thess. 2:3-4; Mt. 24:15).

34. **The two witnesses will appear on Earth** (Rev. 11:3-13). They'll protect Israel, turn her to God, and withstand the Antichrist during the last 3½ years of this age.

35. **The great tribulation begins** (Dan. 9:27; 12:1-7; Mt. 24:15-31; Rev. 7:9-17; 12:1-17; 13:1-18; 14:9-11; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

36. **The seventh trumpet—the third woe or the casting out of Satan** (Rev. 11:15-19; 12:7-12).

37. **The 144,000 Jews are translated** (Rev. 12:5; 14:1-5; Dan. 12:1; Isa. 66:7-8). These Jews will be protected through the trumpet judgments until the middle of the Week. They'll be caught up to Heaven as the manchild in Rev. 12:5, and they'll be in Heaven in Rev. 14:1-5, as we'll prove in Lesson Forty-Five.

38. **War in Heaven** (Rev. 12:7-12). Satan and his angels will be cast down to the Earth to be among men for the last 3½ years, causing the third woe of Rev. 8:13; 11:15-19; 12:12.

39. **Israel flees to the countries of Edom and Moab for the last 3½ years** (Rev. 12:6, 13-17; Isa. 16:1-5; Ezek. 20:33-39; Dan. 11:40-45; Hos. 2:14-16; Mt. 24:15-21). These Scriptures name the very place to which Israel will flee for protection, where the Arabs will protect Israel from the Antichrist. We'll discuss this fully in Lesson Forty-Five.

40. **The Earth opens and swallows the armies of the Antichrist who follow fleeing Israel** (Rev. 12:13-16).

41. **The remnant of Israel is protected from the Antichrist** (Rev. 12:17). This protection will come through the two witnesses (Rev. 11:3-13) and the new wars of the north and east (Dan. 11:44). Antichrist will be too busy in his new wars to concentrate on destroying Israel. After he conquers the countries of the north and east, he'll seek to destroy Israel, but the Lord Jesus Christ appears in Heaven and delivers them when they're about to be destroyed (Zech. 14; Joel 3; Rev. 19:11-21).

42. **The rise of the beast out of the sea** (Rev. 13:1-10). This symbolizes the Antichrist being given power over the ten kingdoms of the old Roman Empire to continue for the last 3½ years (Rev. 13:1-7, 18; 17:8-17).

43. **The rise of the beast out of the Earth** (Rev. 13:11-18). This represents the rise of another man, the false prophet, who will exercise the power of the Antichrist who precedes him (Rev. 16:13-16; 19:20; 20:10).

44. **The setting up of the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel the prophet** (Rev. 13:11-18; Dan. 9:27; 12:7; Mt. 24:15). This simply means that the Antichrist will do away with Jewish worship in their temple and establish his own worship. He'll be worshipped as God in the temple (Dan. 11:45; 12:7; Rev. 13:11-18; 2 Thess. 2:3-4).

45. **The making of a living image of the Antichrist** (Rev. 13:11-18).

46. **The increase of satanic powers** (Rev. 13:1-18; Mt. 24:24; 2 Thess. 2:8-12).

47. **The branding of the beast worshippers** (Rev. 13:16-18; 14:9-11; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

48. **The destruction of Mystical Babylon** (Rev. 17:16-18. See Lesson Forty-One).

49. **The Jewish choir in Heaven—the 144,000 Jews in Heaven** (Rev. 14:1-5).

50. **The first messenger angel—preaching the everlasting gospel** (Rev. 14:6-7).

51. **The second messenger angel—the Fall of Literal Babylon announced** (Rev. 14:8; 16:17-21; 18:1-24; Isa. 13-14; Jer. 50-51).

52. **The third messenger angel—the doom of beast worshippers** (Rev. 14:9-11).

53. **The announcement—the blessed dead, those killed by Antichrist** (Rev. 14:13).

54. **The harvest and the vintage—Armageddon symbolized** (Rev. 14:14-20).

55. **The seven angels, the sea of glass, and the temple in Heaven** (Rev. 15:1–16:1).

56. **The first vial—boils upon men** (Rev. 16:2).

57. **The second vial—the sea to blood** (Rev. 16:3).

58. **The third vial—rivers to blood** (Rev. 16:4-7).

59. **The fourth vial—the great heat that scorches men** (Rev. 16:8-9).

60. **The fifth vial—the dense darkness upon the kingdom of the beast** (Rev. 16:10-11).

61. **The sixth vial—the river Euphrates dried up** (Rev. 16:12).

62. **The three unclean spirits—the gathering of nations to Armageddon** (Rev. 16:13-16).

63. **The seventh vial—a great earthquake and hail** (Rev. 16:17-21).

64. **The destruction of Literal Babylon** (Rev. 16:17-21; 18:1-24).

65. **Translation of the Tribulation saints** (Rev. 7:9-17; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

66. **The defeat, death, and translation of the two witnesses** (Rev. 11:7-13).

67. **Worship in Heaven by all the redeemed and holy angels** (Rev. 19:1-6).

68. **The marriage of the Lamb and the Marriage Supper** (Rev. 19:7-10).

69. **The first coming of God the Father to the Earth** (Dan. 7:9-14; Titus 2:13; Zech. 14:5). God the Father will come to the Earth at the second coming of Jesus Christ. He will give the Earth kingdoms to the Son to rule and to put down all rebellion (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Dan. 7:13-14).

70. **The second advent of Jesus Christ to the Earth** (Rev. 19:11-21; Jude 14; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Mt. 24:29-31; Zech. 14; Isa. 63:1-7; Dan. 7:13-14).

71. **The Mount of Olives splits into two parts.** It will then pass away, making a very great valley and raising the Dead Sea (Zech. 14:1-5; Ezek. 47).

72. **The Battle of Armageddon—the defeat of Antichrist** (Rev. 19:11-21; Joel 3; Zech. 14; Ezek. 38; 39; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Mal. 4:1-4).

73. **Judgment of the beast and the false prophet** (Rev. 19:20; 20:10).

74. **Expulsion of Satan from the Earth** (Rev. 20:1-7).

75. **Salvation of a nation in a day** (Isa. 66:7-8; Rom. 11:25-28).

76. **The end of "the times of the Gentiles"** (Lk. 21:24; Rom. 11:25-29; Zech. 14).

77. **The judgment of the living nations at the second coming of Christ** (Mt. 25:31-46).

78. **The final and complete regathering of Israel as an eternal nation under the Messiah** (Isa. 11:10-12; Jer. 31; 32; Mt. 24:29-31; Rom. 11:26-29; Lk. 1:32-33).

79. **Jerusalem rebuilt as the world capital of the kingdom of Heaven** (Ezek. 48; Isa. 2:1-4; Mic. 4; Zech. 14:1-21).

80. **The Millennial and eternal temple built** (Ezek. 43:7; Zech. 6:12-15).

81. **Millennial and eternal worship established** (Isa. 2:1-4; Ezek. 40-48; Zech. 14).

82. **The establishment of the kingdom of Heaven** (Dan. 12:7-13; Zech. 14; Rev. 20:1-10; Isa. 2:1-4; Mic. 4; Ezek. 43:7; Mt. 25:31-46).

83. **Restoration of normal light as before the Fall** (Isa. 24:21-23; 25:7; 30:26; 60:18-22). The daylight will be increased seven-fold, and the night will be as light as our present day.

84. **Restoration of fertility to barren lands** (Isa. 35; 55:12-13; Ezek. 36:8-12).

85. **The nature of the animals changed** (Isa. 11:6-8; 65:20-25).

86. **The whole Earth evangelized** (Isa. 2:2-4; 11:9; Zech. 8:22-23).

87. **Great outpouring of the Holy Spirit** (Joel 2:28-32; Isa. 32:5; Ezek. 36:25-27).

88. **Universal physical healing** (Isa. 32:1-5; 33:24; 35:3-6).

89. **New form of government—a theocracy** (Isa. 9:6-7; Dan. 7:13-14; Zech. 14; Mt. 25:31-46; Lk. 1:32-33; Rev. 1:5-6; 5:10; 11:15; 20:1-10).

90. **New eternal laws** (Isa. 2:2-4; Mic. 4:2; Ezek. 40:1–48:35).

91. **Proper division of the Earth** (Ezek. 47:13-23; 48:1-35; Isa. 60:21).

92. **The Millennial river created and the Dead Sea healed** (Ezek. 47; Zech. 14:8).

93. **New and universal religion** (Mal. 1:11; Zech. 14:16-21; Isa. 2:1-4; Joel 2:28-32; Jer. 31:31-36; Ezek. 40:1–48:35).

94. **Universal peace** (Isa. 2:1-4; 9:6-7; Mic. 4:3-4).

95. **Universal prosperity** (Isa. 2:1-4; 65:24; Mic. 4:1-5).

96. **Human life lengthened** (Isa. 65:20-25; Zech. 8:4; Lk. 1:33).

97. **The glory of God continually manifest** (Isa. 4:4-6; Ezek. 43:1-7).

98. **New financial system for the universal government and for world religion** (Gen. 14:20; Mal. 3:7-12; Prov. 3:9-10; Heb. 6:20; 7:1-11; 1 Cor. 9).

99. **Universal justice for all** (Isa. 9:6-7; 11:3-5; 57:15; 65:20; 66:1-2).

100. **The loosing of Satan and the last rebellion** (Rev. 20:7-10).

101. **The second resurrection and the Great White Throne Judgment** (Rev. 20:11-15).

102. **The renovation of the atmospheric heavens and of the Earth** (2 Pet. 3:10-13).

103. **The new Heaven and the New Earth** (Isa. 65:17; 66:22-24; 2 Pet. 3:10-13; Rev. 21-22). These will be the old ones renovated and changed, not annihilated (Heb. 1:10-12; 12:25-28; Rom. 8:21-23; Eccl. 1:4; Ps. 104:5).

104. **The second coming of God the Father to the Earth** (Rev. 21:1-7). The New Jerusalem, God's capital city of the universe, and the Bride, the Lamb's wife, will be moved from Heaven to the New Earth to be among men forever (Rev. 21:1-27; 22:1-5).

105. **Eternal generations of natural peoples** (Gen. 8:22; 9:12; 2 Sam. 7; Isa. 9:6-7; 51:8; 59:21; Ezek. 37:24-28; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14, 18; Lk. 1:31-35; Deut. 7:9; 1 Chron. 16:15; Ps. 105:8; Jer. 31:35-36; 32:38-40; Rev. 11:15).

106. **No more curse** (Rev. 21:1-8; 22:1-7). Everything on Earth will be as it would have been if man had not fallen. There will be on Earth perfect eternal natural conditions, peoples, animals, planting and harvesting, marriages, childbirth, worship, natural life, covenants with God, laws, government, feasts, pleasures, food, raiment, and innumerable eternal natural blessings, as we'll see in Lesson Fifty-One.

107. **The river of life for all peoples** (Rev. 22:1-5).

108. **Trees of life for all peoples** (Rev. 21:1-5).

109. **An eternal kingdom—the kingdom of God firmly established forever** (Heb. 12:25-28; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14, 18; Isa. 9:6-7; Lk. 1:32-35; Rev. 11:15; 22:4-6).

110. **God all-in-all as before rebellion started in the universe by Satan and Adam** (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Rev. 21:1-8; 22:1-7; Isa. 66:22-24).

## How to Interpret Prophecy

Before we take up a study of prophecy, let's first learn how to interpret it. Thousands of preachers and Bible teachers constantly say that prophecy is hard to understand. And it's true—it can be hard if you're trying to harmonize all the foolish speculations and interpretations men have come up with on the subject.

But thank God, it's not hard to understand if you follow a few common-sense rules. Think of it like learning to read a map: once you know the basic symbols and rules, the whole thing becomes clear.

### Rule 1: Give Words Their Normal Meaning

Give the same meaning to the words of prophecy that you'd give to words of history. In other words, give the same meaning to words in the Bible that you'd give to the same words outside the Bible.

There's a common theory that just because a word is found in prophecy or in the Bible, it automatically has some mystical meaning and can't be taken literally. This theory is entirely wrong. For example, the word "year" is often taken to mean a day, and a day to mean a year, just because it's prophecy. This is entirely wrong, as we've seen in Lesson Fifteen, Point VIII.

### Rule 2: Don't Change the Literal to the Symbolic

Don't change the literal meaning to a spiritual or symbolic meaning. One modern writer in his book of lectures on Revelation is a fair example of this trend—changing words and statements from their literal meaning to whatever suits one's fancy.

He interprets the "earthquake" of the sixth seal (Rev. 6:12-17) as the breaking up of society instead of a literal earthquake. He says the sun darkened represents Christ rejected and God dethroned. The moon turned to blood? That's the destruction of derived authority. Stars falling? The downfall and apostasy of religious leaders in "the ecclesiastical heavens" (whatever those are). And the heavens departing as a scroll means all organized Christianity will be destroyed.

This same writer says the trumpet judgments aren't literal either. The grass of the first trumpet (Rev. 8:7) means the common people, and the trees mean the dignity of man. So instead of grass and one-third of trees being literally burned as it plainly states, all common men and one-third of man's dignity will be burned.

Instead of a third of the sea turning to blood, a third of sea creatures dying, and a third of ships being destroyed in the second trumpet (Rev. 8:8, 9), he says the burning mountain means spiritual Babylon cast into the sea of nations and destroyed by the people.

Instead of drinking waters being made bitter by a star falling from Heaven causing many deaths in the third trumpet (Rev. 8:10, 11), he says the star means the pope of Rome or some religious dignitary. But how could the pope fall from Heaven into the waters and poison them? How will he ever get to Heaven to fall from Heaven in the Tribulation? How could he poison the drinking waters if he did fall in them?

The darkening of a third of the sun, moon, and stars in the fourth trumpet (Rev. 8:12), he says, means spiritual darkness instead of the literal darkening of part of the Earth.

This writer explains the fifth trumpet (Rev. 9:1-12) like this: The star that falls from Heaven with the key to the Abyss is the pope or the apostate religious leader of the third trumpet. (This would make the pope fall from Heaven twice—once under the third trumpet and once again under the fifth trumpet.) The key is the system that opens the Abyss. The smoke of the pit is the blotting out of true light in man's spiritual sky by demon powers when false religions are dominant after the Holy Spirit is taken out of the world. The locusts aren't literal but symbolize false religions spreading like locusts. The torment of their stings is the torment these religions will bring. The faces of men these creatures have signify intelligence and reason (but since they're not real creatures, how are they to exercise these faculties?). Their hair like women represents an unholy life, and the iron breastplates mean the conscience is destroyed. The grass and trees aren't symbolical in this trumpet as they were in the first. The five months these creatures torment men aren't literal, and he doesn't explain what they mean. So nothing in Revelation is literal if we believe this method!

The sixth trumpet (Rev. 9:13-21), he says, isn't literal either—the 200 million creatures refer to Asiatic hordes that overran Europe and Palestine through many centuries. This writer says the two witnesses aren't two men but symbolize the witnessing Jewish remnant. He also says the manchild is Christ; the woman is Israel; the sun is the glory of the New Testament; the moon the glory of the Old Testament; the twelve stars are the twelve tribes; the 1,260 days of Rev. 12:6 mean the first part of Daniel's Seventieth Week

lessons.title: Daniel's Seventieth Week and the Tribulation
lessons.content:
# Daniel's Seventieth Week and the Tribulation

## I. The Vision of the Seventy Weeks (Dan. 9:24-27)

When Daniel received this vision, the phrase "seventy weeks" literally means "seventy sevens" of years. If days were meant, Scripture would say so directly, as it does in Dan. 10:3.

Daniel's prayer that prompted this vision wasn't about days — it was about years (Dan. 9:2). We also know from Scripture that the last week (Dan. 9:27) divides into two parts of 3½ years each (Dan. 7:25; 12:7; Rev. 11:2, 3; 12:5, 14; 13:5).

So the whole period of "seventy sevens" equals 490 years. These years are marked off from all other years and concern only "thy people [Israel] and thy holy city [Jerusalem]" — the very things Daniel was praying for (Dan. 6:10; 9:1-23).

Six prophetic events will take place during these 490 years, all relating to Israel and Jerusalem:

**1. "To finish the transgression."** The Hebrew word "pasha" means "to revolt," "rebel," or "sin against lawful authority." It's often translated "transgression" (Ps. 51:13; Isa. 43:27; etc.). This refers to Israel's rebellion against God, and this prophecy foretells when that rebellion will finally reach its culmination.

The law was added because of transgression until the Seed should come, serving as a schoolmaster to lead Israel to Christ (Gal. 3:17-25). But Israel failed to receive the Messiah and was broken off in unbelief from God's favor as a nation. She won't be fully received again until Christ's second coming, when He will turn ungodliness from Jacob and cause a nation to be born at once (Rom. 11:25-29; Isa. 66:7-10; Ezek. 36:24-30).

**2. "To make an end of sins."** If you could gather all of Israel's sins into concrete matter, they would fill the whole earth. She has been in rebellion against God from her beginning and will continue until this prophecy is fulfilled at Christ's return.

This "end of sins" won't happen until after the Tribulation. But from that time forward, Israel will obey God forever (Ezek. 36:24-30; 37:24-27; 43:7; Zech. 4:1-21).

**3. "To make reconciliation [atonement] for iniquity."** The Hebrew word "avon" means "perverseness," "to be crooked," or "wrung out of course" (1 Sam. 20:30; 2 Sam. 19:19). Atonement was made on the cross for the whole world, but Israel as a nation hasn't yet appropriated its benefits. They won't until Christ returns (Zech. 13:1-7; Rom. 11:25-27).

**4. "To bring in everlasting righteousness."** When the transgression has been finished, the end of sins made, and Israel fully realizes the benefits of atonement, then everlasting righteousness will be ushered in (Isa. 9:6, 7; 12:1-6; Dan. 7:13, 14, 18, 27; Mt. 25:31-46; Ezek. 43:7; Rom. 11:25-29).

**5. "To seal up the vision and prophecy."** This means prophecies will end because they'll all be fulfilled regarding Israel and Jerusalem. The word "prophecy" should actually be translated "prophet." There will be no need for inspired men to rebuke Israel and lead them toward righteousness "for all shall know the Lord from the least unto the greatest" (Jer. 31:31-40; Isa. 11:9).

**6. "To anoint the most Holy."** This refers to cleansing the Holy of Holies, the temple, and Jerusalem from the abomination of desolation and Gentile sacrilege. It also involves establishing and anointing the Millennial temple described in Ezek. 40-43; Zech. 6:12, 13.

The "most Holy" is never used for a person, nor would Jews ever associate this term with their Messiah. The Messiah is actually distinguished from this term in this passage by His own title. This vision needs no interpretation beyond what the angel gave Daniel — we simply need to understand that explanation, connect it with other Scriptures on the same subject, and determine when it will be fulfilled.

### The 490 Years Are Divided Into Three Periods:

**1. THE FIRST PERIOD** consisted of seven sevens, or forty-nine years. During this time, the Holy City, its street, and wall were to be built "even in troublous times" (Dan. 9:25). This tells us the 490 years began with "the commandment to restore and build Jerusalem unto the Messiah."

There were three decrees for restoring the city. The first came during Cyrus's first year as king of Persia (Ezra 1:1-4; 3:8; Isa. 44:28; 45:1-4; 46:11). Cyrus reigned nine years, then his son Cambyses reigned seven years. During Cambyses's reign, work on the temple and city stopped (Ezra 4:1-24).

Darius I reigned thirty-five years. In his second year, he reconfirmed Cyrus's decree and work resumed. The temple was finished in his sixth year, but the city still wasn't fully restored — even though fifty-seven years had passed since Cyrus's first decree (Ezra 6:1-15).

Xerxes reigned twenty-one years (Dan. 11:1-3), and the city still wasn't completed. Then Artaxerxes reigned, and in his twentieth year, he gave the third decree to Nehemiah to restore "Jerusalem unto the Messiah" (Neh. 2:1–6:19; Dan. 9:25, 26).

Nehemiah rebuilt the walls in just fifty-two days after reaching Jerusalem, but this wasn't the full restoration. That took the complete seven sevens — forty-nine years after the third decree, which was given around 452 B.C.

**2. THE SECOND PERIOD** consists of sixty-two sevens, or 434 years. It began immediately after the first period and continued without interruption until the Messiah was "cut off" — crucified (Dan. 9:26). This phrase "cut off" comes from the Hebrew "karath," meaning "to cut off in death" (Gen. 9:11; Deut. 20:20; Jer. 11:19; Ps. 37:9).

These forty-nine years plus 434 years equal 483 years from the third decree to the Messiah's crucifixion. That accounts for sixty-nine of the seventy sevens, leaving one final seven-year period concerning Israel and Jerusalem to be fulfilled after the crucifixion.

**3. THE THIRD PERIOD** will be one seven-year period, better known as Daniel's Seventieth Week. The Messiah's crucifixion ended the sixty-ninth week, and God ceased dealing with Israel as a nation.

They were broken off in unbelief, and their city was destroyed — just as this same vision foretold (Dan. 9:26) and as Jesus predicted (Mt. 21:43; 23:37-39; 24:2; Lk. 21:20-24). See also Acts 13:45-49 and Rom. 11.

This Seventieth Week will be fulfilled when Israel, partially gathered, exists as a nation possessing Jerusalem. That Jerusalem will be in their hands again is proven by the fact that it will be given to the Gentiles in the middle of the Week (Rev. 11:1, 2).

Not one of those six events has been fulfilled yet. They must happen in the future.

The Seventieth Week will be the last seven years of this dispensation and will run parallel to the seven-year covenant between Antichrist and Israel (Dan. 9:27). This is when all the events of Rev. 6:1–19:21 will be fulfilled, and the entire tribulation will run its course.

Daniel didn't receive the details of what would happen during this Week — but John did. This Week of years will begin after the Church is raptured, and at Christ's second advent, all six prophetic events concerning Israel will be fulfilled.

The present Church Age fits between the sixty-ninth and seventieth weeks — during the time of Israel's rejection. It will be completed before the Seventieth Week begins, which primarily concerns Israel, just as the first sixty-nine weeks did.

## II. The Tribulation (Rev. 6:1–19:21)

### 1. The Time and Length of the Tribulation

The Tribulation will begin affecting Israel before the Seventieth Week officially starts. How long before? We can't be certain.

But when Antichrist rises at the Week's beginning, Israel will already be undergoing persecution from the whore and the ten kings of Revised Rome. These kings are dominated by the whore until the middle of the Week.

Antichrist will emerge from one of those ten kingdoms (see Lesson Thirty-nine) and make a seven-year covenant with Israel, promising to protect them as they continue establishing their nation (Dan. 9:27).

The Jews won't accept Catholicism when it dominates the old world nations again and starts murdering heretics as it did in the past. Because they won't submit, widespread persecution will break out — "they shall be hated of all nations" during "the beginning of sorrows" while Antichrist tries to conquer these nations (Mt. 24:4-12).

Antichrist will need Jewish moral and financial support to help him rise over these nations, so he'll make an alliance with them for seven years. Think of it like a political deal where both sides need something from the other — but one side has hidden, sinister motives.

Therefore, the Tribulation spans the entire Seventieth Week (Dan. 9:27). It will end at Christ's second advent (Mt. 24:29-31).

### 2. The Divisions of the Tribulation

**(1) THE FIRST DIVISION** covers the first 3½ years of the Seventieth Week. It's called "the lesser tribulation" because it's not as severe as the last 3½ years. Why? Because Antichrist is protecting Israel during this time.

Israel's persecution in this period comes from a completely different source than in the second half. During these earlier years, she'll be persecuted by the whore and the ten kings, as mentioned above.

This division includes the fulfillment of Rev. 6:1–9:21. The judgments of the sixth seal and first six trumpets occur in this period, proving there is real tribulation during this time.

**(2) THE LAST DIVISION** covers the final 3½ years of the Week. It's called "the great tribulation" because persecution against Israel will be far more severe than the first 3½ years.

Here's what happens: Antichrist, who protected Israel in the first half, breaks his covenant with her at the midpoint and becomes her most bitter enemy. It's similar to someone who pretends to be your closest ally, then suddenly turns and tries to destroy you — except this betrayal will be on a national scale.

He'll try to annihilate Israel, which triggers the judgments of the seven vials in the last 3½ years. This part of the Tribulation includes the fulfillment of Rev. 10:1–19:21.

Jesus, Daniel, Jeremiah, and many others describe this time of Israel's trouble as worse than anything that has ever been on Earth or ever will be (Dan. 12:1; Jer. 30:4-11; Mt. 24:21, 22; Rev. 11:1, 2; 12:14-17; 13:5-7).

### 3. The Purpose of the Tribulation

> (1) To purify Israel and bring them back to a place where God can fulfill the everlasting covenants made with their fathers (Isa. 2:6; 3:26; 16:1-5; 24:1-25; 26:20, 21; Ezek. 20:33, 34; 22:17-22; Rom. 11:25-29).

> (2) To purify Israel of all rebels (Ezek. 20:33, 34; 22:17-22; Zech. 13:8, 9; Mal. 3:3, 4).

> (3) To plead with and bring Israel into the bond of the New Covenant (Ezek. 20:33, 34; 36:24-28; Jer. 30:3-11; Zech. 12:10–13:9; Mal. 4:3, 4).

> (4) To judge Israel and punish them for their rejection of the Messiah and make them willing to accept Him when He comes the second time (Ezek. 20:33, 34; Zech. 12:10–13:9; 14:1-15; Mt. 24:15-31).

> (5) To judge the nations for their persecution of Israel (Isa. 63:1-5; Joel 3; Rev. 6:1–19:21).

> (6) To bring Israel to complete repentance (Zech. 12:10–13:9; Rom. 11:26-29; Mt. 23:39).

> (7) To fulfill the prophecies of Dan. 9:24-27; Rev. 6:1–19:21; Mt. 24:15, 29, etc.

> (8) To cause Israel to flee into the wilderness of Edom and Moab and to be so persecuted by the nations that Israel will have to turn to God for help (Isa. 16:1-5; Ezek. 20:33-35; Dan. 11:40–12:7; Hos. 2:14-17; Mt. 24:15-31; Rev. 12).

### 4. The Character of the Tribulation

You can understand the Tribulation's character when you consider that God's wrath will be poured out on mankind for wickedness and corruption exceeding even the days of Noah and Lot (Gen. 6; Mt. 24:37-39; Lk. 17:22-37; 2 Tim. 3:1-12).

People will reject the truth until God turns them over to the "strong delusion" of the Antichrist. He'll cause them to believe a lie and be damned (2 Thess. 2:8-12; 2 Pet. 3:1-9).

Even after God pours out His judgments, they'll still defy Him (Rev. 9:20, 21; 6:2-11; 17:1-18; 18:1-24). Words can't adequately describe the utter rebellion and wickedness of humanity during this final struggle between God and the devil over possession of the Earth (Rev. 11:15; 12:7-12; 19:11-21; 20:1-3).

### 5. Will the Tribulation Be Worldwide?

The old theory that the Tribulation will be worldwide isn't stated in even one Scripture. On the contrary, the Bible is clear that Antichrist won't reign over the whole world — only over the ten kingdoms formed inside the old Roman Empire (see Lessons Forty-six and Forty-seven).

Most trumpet and vial judgments are stated as affecting only a third or fourth part of the Earth (Rev. 8:7-12), falling specifically upon "the men which had the mark of the beast" and "upon the seat [throne] of the beast: and his kingdom" (Rev. 16:2, 10, 12).

However, nothing limits the extent of the demon-locusts or the devil's wrath when he's cast out (Rev. 9:1-11; 12:7-12). The sixth trumpet kills only a third part of men (Rev. 9:12-21).

When we talk about the Tribulation, we mean the troubles God's people — especially the Jewish people — will endure. Daniel's Seventieth Week concerns only Israel and Jerusalem. The last half of the Week will be "the time of Jacob's trouble," and these troubles primarily concern Israel, as Point 3 above shows.

The Tribulation won't be worldwide, even if it covers all lands where Christians live after the rapture. Unknown tribes in the interior of heathen lands won't even know about the Tribulation caused by Antichrist, as we'll see in Lesson Forty-six.

---

## Questions on Lesson Forty

- Explain fully the vision of the seventy weeks.

- What does the term "weeks" really mean? Prove.

- What six main events will be fulfilled during the seventy weeks?

- Have these events been fulfilled yet? When will they be?

- How would you divide the seventy weeks into three periods?

- When did the seventy weeks begin?

- What was done concerning Israel during the first period?

- What event marked the end of the second period?

- What event will end the third period?

- What takes place between the Sixty-ninth and the Seventieth Week?

- Divide Daniel's Seventieth Week as to its relation with the Antichrist?

- When is the time of the Tribulation and what will be its length?

- How many divisions of the Tribulation are there?

- What makes these divisions of the Tribulation and what are they called?

- What is the purpose of the Tribulation?

- What will be the character of the Tribulation?

- Will the Tribulation be worldwide? Prove.

- What part of the book of Revelation will be fulfilled in the Tribulation?

lessons.title: A Gist of the Book of Revelation
lessons.content:
# A Gist of the Book of Revelation

---

Without the book of Revelation, the Bible would feel incomplete. Think of it like a magnificent symphony missing its final movement — thousands of vital questions would remain unanswered. Let's walk through this remarkable book together so you can see how it fits into God's grand plan for humanity.

## I. Literalness of the Book

The book of Revelation contains both literal and figurative language. Here's a helpful guideline: take statements literally wherever possible.

In other words, when you read something in Revelation, understand it to mean exactly what it says — unless doing so would be highly improbable, violate basic principles of language, contradict spiritually sound reasoning, or conflict with other Scriptures on the same topic. Only when a passage truly won't work as a literal statement should you look for a symbolic interpretation.

This approach is the only sound way to read Revelation. Why? Because the book calls itself a revelation — something meant to be understood, not a mystery wrapped in confusion. To spiritualize everything is to deny what the book claims to be.

Every scene and truth in Revelation is explained within the book itself. Before you go hunting through other parts of the Bible for answers, first discover what Revelation itself says about its own truths. Earlier prophecies from other books will certainly shed light on many passages and help you dig deeper, but Revelation stands in perfect harmony with all preceding prophecies — it's the logical and beautiful completion of them all.

## II. The Key to the Interpretation

"Write the things which thou hast seen, and the things which are, and the things which shall be hereafter" (Rev. 1:19).

> 1. PART I. "The things which thou hast seen"; that is, Christ in the midst of the seven candlesticks (Rev. 1:12-18, 20), as seen by John before he began to write.

> 2. PART II. "The things which are"; that is, the things concerning the churches then existent and those which should exist throughout the Church Age to the rapture. This division takes in only Rev. 2-3.

> 3. PART III. "The things which shall be hereafter"; that is, the things which shall come to pass after the rapture of the Church. This division includes all of the events of Rev. 4-22. One has only to believe this threefold natural division as given by Christ to understand the book fully, especially as to the time of the fulfillment of the things of each division. The moment these divisions are forgotten and the reader begins to disarrange them and insert certain things into the one or the other that are not part of the division, he will become confused as to the divine order of these "things" which are so clearly given in consecutive order, and he will miss the true intent of the "things" written therein. That we refrain from confusing these "things" is absolutely imperative if a true understanding of them is to be gained.

Here's something important to understand: everything in Rev. 4–22 must take place after the churches, and therefore after the rapture. Rev. 4:1 confirms this clearly.

After John recorded the vision of Christ in Rev. 1 (completing Part I) and everything Christ told him to write to the churches in Rev. 2-3 (completing Part II), he was told in Rev. 4:1 that he would now see "things which must be hereafter" — that is, after the things of the churches. So if everything from Rev. 4:1 onward must be after the churches, then all the events of Rev. 4-22 must be after the churches.

If they must be after the churches, they cannot happen during the time of the churches. And if they cannot happen during the time of the churches, then the Church is no longer on Earth during the fulfillment of these events. Think of it like chapters in a book — you can't read chapter three while you're still in chapter two.

Revelation 4:1 literally reads in the Greek, "After these things [the things concerning the churches of the previous division] I saw, and behold a door opened in heaven, and the first voice [of Rev. 1:10] which I heard was as a trumpet speaking with me, saying, Come up hither, and I will show to thee what things must take place after these things," that is, after the churches which he wrote about in the previous chapters.

These three divisions don't overlap or run simultaneously. One division must be completely fulfilled before the next begins.

So if you're fair with the text and recognize that every event from Rev. 4:1 through the end of the book must be fulfilled after the rapture — and if you don't pull any of these events back and place them among the churches as happening before the rapture — everything in the book will automatically clear up.

Fully 95 percent of the false theories about Revelation exist because interpreters have failed to recognize this natural threefold division. Many teachers emphasize these divisions at the start of their study, but when it comes to actually keeping all the events of Rev. 4-22 after the churches, they fail to follow through.

They'll place some events from the seals, trumpets, and vials back in the Church Age, explaining them as fulfilled before the rapture. They'll interpret the woman and the man child in connection with the Church of this age, when Jesus told John these were part of the "things" that must come after the churches. They'll explain the dragon, the beast, the false prophet, or other events of Rev. 4-22 as being fulfilled alongside the churches, when the truth is these are part of what must come after the churches.

Watch for this pattern, and you'll be sensible enough to place the events of Rev. 4-22 where they belong — after the churches, and therefore after the rapture.

## III. The Division of the Book By Classes (1 Cor. 10:32)

> 1. Rev. 1–3 deals primarily with the Church on Earth.

> 2. Rev. 4–5 pictures the Church and the Old Testament saints with God in Heaven after the rapture, represented by the twenty-four elders.

> 3. Rev. 6–19 deals primarily with Israel under the last oppression by the Gentiles in fulfillment of Daniel's Seventieth Week after the rapture of the Church.

> 4. Rev. 20–22 refers to all three classes, the Church, the Jews, and the Gentiles. The earthly Jews will be the head of all earthly Gentiles and Christ with the Church will reign over both forever and ever.

## IV. The Consecutive Order of Revelation

The book follows a succession of consecutive events from beginning to end — it's not unsystematic or confused. The events should be understood in the order God gave them, not rearranged according to our own finite ideas.

It's almost universally recognized that Rev. 1-5 and Rev. 19-22 form a consecutive story. We can therefore logically conclude that the events of Rev. 6-18 are also consecutive.

If Revelation's events aren't meant to be taken consecutively, on what grounds would we attempt to fix another arrangement? Where would we find any other authorized standard than what's plainly evident in the book itself?

Why would we hopelessly confuse a plain prophecy given by the Lord concerning "things which must shortly come to pass" and change the order of events from what He gave? Surely He set forth the events in their proper order of fulfillment so He wouldn't confuse His servants.

## V. The Outline of the Book of Revelation

INTRODUCTORY REMARKS (Rev. 1:1-11, 19).

> 1. The Introduction (Rev. 1:1-3).

> 2. The Salutation—From God the Father, Jesus Christ, and the Holy Spirit (Rev. 1:4-5a).

> 3. The Exaltation—"Unto him that loved us . . . washed us . . . made us kings" (Rev. 1:5b-6).

> 4. The Chief Theme of Revelation—"Behold he cometh with clouds" (Rev. 1:7).

> 5. The Eternity of the Son of God—"I am Alpha and Omega" (Rev. 1:8).

> 6. The Prophet—John and His Commission to Write (Rev. 1:9-11, 19).

> 7. The Key to the Interpretation (Rev. 1:19).

I. "THE THINGS WHICH THOU HAST SEEN" (Rev. 1:12-18, 20).

> 1. The Vision of Christ (Rev. 1:12-18).

> 2. The Symbols of the Vision Interpreted (Rev. 1:20).

II. "THE THINGS WHICH ARE" (Rev. 2:1–3:22).

> 1. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Ephesus (Rev. 2:1-7).

> 2. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Smyrna (Rev. 2:8-11).

> 3. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Pergamos (Rev. 2:12-17).

> 4. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Thyatira (Rev. 2:18-29).

> 5. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Sardis (Rev. 3:1-6).

> 6. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Philadelphia (Rev. 3:7-13).

> 7. The Things Concerning the Local Church at Laodicea (Rev. 3:14-22).

III. "THE THINGS WHICH SHALL BE HEREAFTER" (Rev. 4:1–22:5).

> 1. The Heavenly Tabernacle—the raptured saints with God (Rev. 4:1–5:14).

> (1) The Heavenly Door (Rev. 4:1).

> (2) The Heavenly Throne (Rev. 4:2, 3, 5).

> (3) The Heavenly Elders (Rev. 4:4).

> (4) The Heavenly Sea of Glass (Rev. 4:6b-8).

> (5) The Heavenly Living Creatures (Rev. 4:6b-8).

> (6) The Heavenly Worship because of Creation (Rev. 4:9-11).

> (7) The Heavenly Book (Rev. 5:1-4).

> (8) The Heavenly Lamb (Rev. 5:5-7).

> (9) The Heavenly Worship because of Worthiness to the Lamb (Rev. 5:8-14).

> 2. Daniel's Seventieth Week (Rev. 6:1–19:21; Dan. 9:27).

> (1) The First Six Seals (Rev. 6:1-17). (Parenthetical, Rev. 7:1-17.)

> (2) The Seventh Seal and First Six Trumpets (Rev. 8:1–9:21). (Parenthetical, Rev. 8:2-6, 13; Rev. 10:1–11:13.)

> (3) The Seventh Trumpet (Rev. 11:14–13:18). (Parenthetical, Rev. 14:1-20.)

> (4) The First Six Vials (Rev. 15:1–16:12). (Parenthetical, Rev. 15:2-4; Rev. 16:13-16.)

> (5) The Seventh Vial (Rev. 16:17–18:24). (Parenthetical, Rev. 17:1-18.)

> (6) The Marriage of the Lamb, the Second Advent, and Armageddon (Rev. 19:1-21). (Parenthetical, Rev. 19:1-10.)

> 3. The Thousand Years and After (Rev. 20:1-15).

> (1) The Expulsion of Satan from the Earth (Rev. 20:1-3).

> (2) The Millennial Reign of Christ and His Saints (Rev. 20:4-6).

> (3) Satan's Post-Millennial Career and Doom (Rev. 20:7-10).

> (4) The Final Judgment (Rev. 20:11-15).

> 4. The Eternal Perfect State—the Ages of the Ages (Rev. 21:1–22:5).

> (1) The New Heaven (Rev. 21:1).

> (2) The New Earth (Rev. 21:1).

> (3) The New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:2, 9-21).

> (4) The New Peoples (Rev. 21:3).

> (5) The New Conditions (Rev. 21:4-8).

> (6) The New Temple (Rev. 21:22).

> (7) The New Light (Rev. 21:23-27).

> (8) The New Paradise (Rev. 22:1-5).

THE CONCLUSION (Rev. 22:6-21).

> 1. The Confirmation of Revelation (Rev. 22:6-7).

> 2. The Mistake in the Object of Worship (Rev. 22:8-9).

> 3. The Last Instructions (Rev. 22:10-19).

> 4. The Last Promise and Last Prayer (Rev. 22:20-21).

## VI. Part I. "The Things Which Thou Hast Seen" (Rev. 1:12-20)

This section concerns John's vision of Christ on the island of Patmos. You'll see Christ's body, head, eyes, feet, voice, right hand, mouth, and countenance described in vivid detail.

John also records the powerful effect this vision had on him, along with the interpretation of the first symbols he saw.

## VII. Part II. "The Things Which Are" (Rev. 2:1–3:22)

The messages to the seven churches appear immediately after the vision of Christ standing in the midst of the churches. Chapters 2 and 3 contain only "the things which are" — that is, the things concerning the Church on Earth down to the rapture.

### The Threefold Application of the Letters

**1. A LOCAL APPLICATION** of the letters to the churches in John's day. These seven epistles portray actual conditions in seven local churches in Asia. This is clear from the messages themselves and from Rev. 1:4, 11, 20.

**2. A PROPHETIC APPLICATION** of the letters to churches throughout this dispensation until the rapture. They reveal the spiritual conditions found in local churches and individuals throughout the Church Age. This makes sense because the book is prophecy, and these letters are as applicable to believers throughout the Church Age as other New Testament epistles and Scriptures (2 Tim. 3:16, 17).

**3. AN INDIVIDUAL APPLICATION** of the letters, so that individuals may be warned by the failures revealed, profit from the warning, and find encouragement from the promises to the overcomer.

Now, here's something important to understand: the so-called "dispensational application" of these church letters — the idea that they portray seven church periods or phases of church history — is really based on human theory alone. You won't find Scripture to prove this theory in any aspect.

Similarity to certain phases of church history proves nothing. You can find similarities between the history of the Church and Israel, and there's no end to similarities you could find between almost any two events you choose to compare. It's similar to finding shapes in clouds — you can see what you're looking for, but that doesn't mean it's actually there.

Any conclusions based on human theory alone are not dependable and can be misleading and unscriptural. Much confusion and many false teachings have grown from this method of interpretation. If there truly was such an application of these letters, surely we'd have two or three passages to support each letter and its application to some definite period in history.

Some schools say we're in the Philadelphian or Laodicean periods, that the rapture takes place in one of these two periods, that the Church Age ends with Philadelphia, that Laodicea deals with things after the rapture, that the Ephesian period ended with the apostles, and many other unscriptural theories.

The truth is, we still have the kind of conditions described in Ephesus and all seven churches in local churches today. These conditions existed in John's day and will continue as long as the Church is on Earth. We can see Scriptures that prove local, prophetic, and individual applications of the letters, but anything said about a dispensational application of seven distinct periods must rest on human theory and causes endless differences in interpretation — so it's best to set this application aside entirely.

With the seven letters to the seven churches in Asia (Rev. 2:1–3:22), we end everything in Revelation concerning the Church on Earth. From Rev. 4:1 onward, the Revelation deals with prophecies that will happen after the rapture.

We won't attempt to discuss each application of the letters or explore all their details here. The applications are clear — they apply to anyone in any local church throughout this dispensation where these conditions exist.

To overcome such conditions and be rewarded is the message to every believer in any local church in this entire age. All these conditions have existed more or less in local churches since John's time and will always exist as long as churches remain on Earth. The duty of each person reading these letters is clear, so further comment isn't necessary here. This saves space for discussing more important truths in the book. (See Point II in the outline under Point V, above, for a list of the seven churches.)

## VIII. Part III. "The Things Which Shall Be Hereafter" (Rev. 4:1–22:5)

### 1. THE HEAVENLY TABERNACLE (Rev. 4:1–5:14)

In chapters four and five, we're given scenes in Heaven. We see almost a complete picture of the heavenly tabernacle — the pattern from which the earthly tabernacle was made (Heb. 8:1-5; 9:1-10, 22-24; 10:1). See and compare the illustrations of the earthly and heavenly tabernacles on your chart. Here are nine key truths:

> (1) **The heavenly door** (Rev. 4:1). This is a literal opening to Heaven, as is plainly evident here and in Rev. 11:19; 14:15-18; 15:5-8; 16:1, 17.

> (2) **The heavenly throne** (Rev. 4:2-5).
