This is the throne seen throughout the book where God the Father sits (Rev. 1:4; 3:21; 4:10; 5:1, 7, 11-13; 6:16; 7:9-17; 8:3; 12:5; 14:1-5; 16:17; 19:4-5; 21:5; 22:1-3). The throne is white (Rev. 20:11-15). It's a real throne because God as a real person sits on it (Dan. 7:9-14; Isa. 6; Heb. 8:1–12:1-2).

> (3) **The heavenly elders** (Rev. 4:4-11; 5:8-10; 7:13; 11:16-19; 14:3; 19:4). These elders have thrones placed around God's throne — so if one throne is literal, the others must be too. The elders are redeemed persons, as the word "elders" is never used in Scripture of angels, only of human beings. Their white garments are used in Revelation only of the righteousness of saints (Rev. 3:5, 18; 6:11; 7:9, 13; 15:6; 19:9, 14). (See Revelation Expounded for a detailed study of all the truths of Rev. 4-5.)

> (4) **The heavenly sea of glass** (Rev. 4:6). This is another part of the heavenly tabernacle or capital building of God. It's located before God's throne, and on it the redeemed and other heavenly creatures gather to worship on certain occasions (Rev. 7:9-17; 15:2-4).

> (5) **The heavenly living creatures** (Rev. 4:6-8). These are four angelic beings that call attention to God's holiness. They appear in Rev. 4:4-8; 5:8-10; 15:7. (See Lesson Six for a study of spirit-beings.)

> (6) **The heavenly worship** (Rev. 4:9-11; 5:8-14). How worship is done is made plain in these passages, so no additional comment is necessary.

> (7) **The heavenly book** (Rev. 5:1-4; 10:1-11). This book contains the events of Revelation as recorded by John. It's not a book of redemption or of title deeds to the Earth, as some teach — there's no reference to such books anywhere in Scripture. The seals that bind the book clearly contain certain events (Rev. 6:1–8:1). The things "written within" are revelations that John wrote in his book, as is clear in Rev. 10:1-11 where the angel gives the book to John to reveal its contents. The things in the book are "concerning many peoples and nations and tongues and kings" which John wrote about after the seals were broken.

> (8) **The heavenly Lamb** (Rev. 5:5-7). This symbolizes Christ, who is the root of David in this passage and in Gen. 49:10; Mic. 5:1-2; 2 Sam. 7:8-17; Ps. 89:35-37; Isa. 9:6-7; 11:1-9; Jer. 23:5-6. He is the Lamb of God (Isa. 53; Jn. 1:29; 1 Pet. 18-23).

---

### The First One-Half of Daniel's Seventieth Week or the Tribulation (Rev. 6:1–9:21)

### 2. THE FIRST SIX SEALS (Rev. 6:1-17)

The seven seals and the first six trumpets take place in succession during the first 3½ years of the Tribulation Week — from the beginning to the middle of the Week. The seventh trumpet blows in the middle of the Week, as we'll see.

> (1) **The first seal** (Rev. 6:1-2). The white horse rider represents the rise of the Antichrist at the beginning of Daniel's Seventieth Week. He'll make a seven-year covenant with Israel and go forth conquering and to conquer other nations, as we've studied in Lessons Thirty-nine and Forty.

> This rider couldn't be a symbol of the United States, as some teach, because this is a revelation showing a man being crowned and then warring during events that must take place after the churches. There's no clear reference in prophecy concerning America, as we've seen in Supplement Thirteen, Question 7.

> Neither could this be a symbol of Christ, for He's the one opening the seals and revealing events that happen on Earth after the rapture. Christ doesn't come to conquer until after the Tribulation (Mt. 24:29-31; Rev. 19:11-21). This seal reveals the conquests of Antichrist, fulfilling Dan. 7:8-9, 23-25; 8:8-10, 20-23; 35-45.

> (2) **The second seal** (Rev. 6:3-4). This is a clear picture of war following the rise of an ambitious conqueror, the Antichrist (Dan. 7:24; 11:40-45).

> (3) **The third seal** (Rev. 6:5-6). This symbolizes famine, which always follows wars. Bread by measure signifies scarcity of food (Ezek. 4:10-17).

> (4) **The fourth seal** (Rev. 6:7-8). Here death and Hell are symbolized by riders on horses. Death and Hell are always turned loose on Earth during wars and famines, so this is the natural result of the first three seals (Mt. 24:6-7). The word "pale" means "green" in the Greek, as translated in Rev. 8:7; 9:4; Mk. 6:39. This is why the grave line on the chart is colored green from the Garden of Eden to the Great White Throne Judgment.

> (5) **The fifth seal** (Rev. 6:9-11). This is a picture of the first martyrs of the Tribulation. They weren't ready to go in the rapture or they would have been translated before the Tribulation. They get saved after the rapture and are those killed between the rapture and the fifth seal. They're told to rest until the remaining tribulation saints are killed, and then vengeance will be taken on those who killed them.

> (6) **The sixth seal** (Rev. 6:12-17). Beginning with this seal in the first part of the Week, God's wrath begins. The first five seals show the wrath of man, but with the sixth seal God begins pouring out His judgments because of the persecution of His people on Earth. Seven great events happen under this seal: an earthquake, darkening of the sun, turning the moon to blood, falling of meteors, rolling back of the heavens, moving of mountains and islands, and the beginning of God's wrath.

---

### (Parenthetical: the Two Companies of Redeemed, Rev. 7:1-17)

Revelation 7 is the first parenthetical passage of the book. Picture it this way: the main story pauses to zoom in on important details happening alongside the main events. These events aren't the contents of the seals — they're inserted between the sixth and seventh seals to explain other things happening alongside the main order of events after the sixth seal.

This revelation of two companies of the redeemed breaks the order of the seal events to reveal that both Jews and Gentiles will be saved during the Tribulation.

> (1) **The sealing of 144,000 Jews** (Rev. 7:1-8). That they're all Jews and not Gentiles is clear from the fact that they're from the tribes of Israel. They get saved after the rapture, for if they had been saved at the time of the rapture they wouldn't have been left on Earth (1 Thess. 4:16-17; 1 Cor. 15:23).

> They're sealed for the purpose of going through the trumpet judgments — from when they begin after the seals are finished in the first part of the Week to the middle of the Week. The first four trumpet angels were told not to hurt the earth, trees, grass, and seas until the 144,000 were sealed. Special direction is given to the fifth and sixth trumpet angels to hurt only those who were not sealed (Rev. 9:4). The 144,000 will be caught up to Heaven under the seventh trumpet, as we'll prove in Lesson Forty-five.

> The seal is the name of the Father written on their foreheads — it doesn't mean keeping Saturday as the sabbath (Rev. 7:3; 14:1). The promise of being written upon is given to all believers as a reward in the next life (Rev. 3:12; 22:4), but these will be written upon in this life to protect them from the trumpet judgments.

> (2) **The tribulation saints** (Rev. 7:9-17). This company won't escape the Tribulation because they weren't ready to go in the rapture when it happened. They get saved after the rapture (Acts 2:16-21) and become martyrs for Christ (Rev. 6:9-11; 7:9-17; 15:2-4; 20:4-6). The majority will be slain by Antichrist (Rev. 13:7-18; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

---

### 3. THE SEVENTH SEAL AND THE FIRST SIX TRUMPETS (Rev. 8:1–9:21)

> (1) **The seventh seal** (Rev. 8:1). This simply means silence in Heaven for half an hour, just as it plainly states — no interpretation necessary.

---

### (Parenthetical: Seven Trumpet Angels and the Priestly Angel, Rev. 8:2-6)

Revelation 8:2-6 is parenthetical because it explains the work of the priestly angel and the preparation of the seven trumpet judgments. This passage is fulfilled before the seven trumpets begin.

After Christ's ministry at the altar, the seven angels prepare to sound their trumpets. The judgments are as literal as stated and are simple to understand if you believe what's written about what happens when each trumpet sounds. They're as literal as the plagues in Egypt and serve the same purpose — to protect Israel during the Tribulation of the first 3½ years.

> (2) **The first trumpet** — hail, fire and blood burning up grass and trees (Rev. 8:7).

> (3) **The second trumpet** — one-third of the sea changing to blood (Rev. 8:8-9).

> (4) **The third trumpet** — one-third of the rivers poisoned (Rev. 8:10-11).

> (5) **The fourth trumpet** — one-third of the planets darkened (Rev. 8:12).

### (Parenthetical: Three Woes Announced, Rev. 8:13)

> (6) **The fifth trumpet — the first woe** (Rev. 9:1-12). Demon creatures will be loosed from the pit to torment men for five months. Men will desire to die but cannot until this period is over. The language of this passage should be understood as literally as any other descriptive matter in human literature.

> (7) **The sixth trumpet — the second woe** (Rev. 9:13-21). Two hundred million demon horsemen will be liberated from the same abyss to slay one-third of all men at a certain hour. This passage is also to be taken as literally as it reads — no special interpretation necessary.

---

### 4. THE MIDDLE OF THE TRIBULATION WEEK (Rev. 10:1–13:18)

### (Parenthetical: the Angel, Temple of God, and the Two Witnesses, Rev. 10:1–11:13)

Between the sixth and seventh trumpets, this parenthetical passage explains certain events not revealed as the contents of the trumpets and vials, but which will happen during the same time.

#### (1) The Mighty Angel (Rev. 10:1-11)

This angel is Christ, who has broken the seals on the little book of Rev. 5 and now holds it open to swear that delay in fulfilling the mystery of God will be no longer. Christ now gives John the little book to eat, signifying the reception of the rest of the Revelation to record for people.

Compare this with Ezek. 2:3–3:14; Jer. 15:16; 20:7-9; Ps. 119:103. See also Point VIII, 1, (7), above, and my book, Revelation Expounded. The expression "time no longer" means "delay no longer" and refers to the casting out of Satan under the seventh trumpet, as we'll see below.

#### (2) The Temple of God (Rev. 11:1-2)

This temple in Rev. 11:1-2 isn't Herod's temple — that one was destroyed in 70 A.D., about twenty-five years before Revelation was written. It also can't be the Millennial temple described in Ezek. 40-48, because that one won't be built until Christ comes to Earth (Zech. 6:12, 13).

This temple is one that will be rebuilt by the Jews before Daniel's Seventieth Week. It will be destroyed at the end of the Tribulation, either by the earthquake under the seventh vial (Rev. 16:18, 19), or by the armies of Antichrist when taking Jerusalem (Zech. 14:1-5).

Both Testaments are clear that there will be such a temple where sacrifices will be offered for at least 3½ years. Then this temple will be made desolate for 3½ years and will be polluted by the Antichrist and the Gentiles (Dan. 9:27; 12:7-12; Mt. 24:15; 2 Thess. 2:2-4).

This treading down of Jerusalem by the Gentiles for forty-two months proves that "the times of the Gentiles" won't end until Christ returns at the end of the forty-two months, as we've already proven in Lesson Fifteen.

#### (3) The Two Witnesses (Rev. 11:3-13)

Let's first determine what Rev. 11:3-13 says about the two witnesses before examining other Scriptures. Any theory about their identity must harmonize with the statements about them in Rev. 11.

The two witnesses are two men — not two covenants, two dispensations, or anything symbolic. This is clear from the plain description in Rev. 11:3-13 and elsewhere. Here's what we learn:

> 1. They are my [Christ's] two witnesses (Rev. 11:3).

> 2. They will be given power by Christ in the future (Rev. 11:3). This excludes the argument that they must be two men who have already exercised this power in past history.

> 3. They will prophesy the last 3½ years. All passages referring to forty-two months, 1,260 days, and 3½ years in Daniel and Revelation always refer to the last half of

lessons.title: Heaven and the Resurrections
lessons.content:
# Heaven and the Resurrections

## The Kinds of Resurrection

There are two main kinds of resurrection: spiritual and material.

**Spiritual resurrection** is what happens when your spirit is brought to life after being dead in sin. This kind of resurrection renews you in the image and likeness of God (1 Cor. 11:7; Eph. 4:21-24; Col. 3:10; Jas. 3:9). You can see this taught in Eph. 2:1-6; 5:14; Rom. 6:11.

Here's the crucial thing: spiritual resurrection only happens in this life. Once physical death comes, any chance for spiritual resurrection is gone (Eccl. 11:1; Heb. 9:27; Rev. 22:11).

**Material resurrection** is the resurrection of the body—and that's what we'll focus on for the rest of this study.

## The Kinds of Material Resurrection

There are two kinds of physical resurrection. The righteous will rise to life *before* the Millennium, and the wicked will rise to damnation *after* the Millennium (Dan. 12:3; Jn. 5:28-29; Rev. 20:4-6, 11-15).

Think of it like two separate court dates on a divine calendar—there will be 1,000 years between these two resurrections (Rev. 20:4-6).

### 1. The Fact of a Resurrection

The following Bible characters taught it: Job (19:25-27); Isaiah (26:14-19); Daniel (12:3); David (Ps. 16; 17:15; Acts 2:31); Old Testament saints (Heb. 11:35); New Testament saints (Mt. 28; Mk. 16; Lk. 24; Jn. 20-21; Acts 2; 4; etc.); Jesus (Mt. 16:21; 22:23-31; Lk. 14:14; 20:27-36; 24:13-35); Matthew (28:1-20); Mark (16:1-18); Luke (24:1-49); John (19:21; Rev. 20:4-6); Mary (Jn. 11:24); Herod (Mt. 14:2); Peter (1 Pet. 1:3; 3:21); Paul (Acts 17:18, 32; 23:6; 24:15, 21; Rom. 1:4; 6:5; 8:34; 1 Cor. 15; Phil. 3:10-11; Heb. 6:2; 11:35).

### 2. The Resurrections Foretold

Scripture predicted these resurrections in many places (Isa. 26:14-19; Dan. 12:3; Ps. 16:10; Mt. 12:41-42; 20:19; 27:63; 1 Thess. 4:14-17; 1 Cor. 15; Rev. 20:4-6; Jn. 5:28-29).

### 3. Examples of Resurrection—Temporary and Permanent

> **(1) Temporary**—raised to mortality to die again: "a widow's son" (1 Ki. 17:17-24); "the Shunammite's son" (2 Ki. 4:18-37); "a Moabite invader" (2 Ki. 13:20-21); "Jonah" (2:1-10; Mt. 12:40); "Jairus' daughter" (Mt. 9:18-26); "a widow's son of Nain" (Lk. 7:11-18); "Lazarus" (Jn. 11); "Tabitha" (Acts 9:36-43); "Eutychus" (Acts 20:6-12).

> **(2) Permanent**—raised to immortality to live forever in their bodies. Christ "the firstfruits" was the first of all permanent resurrections (1 Cor. 15:1-23), then there were many Old Testament saints (Mt. 27:53).

### 4. The Order of the Resurrection

Here's the sequence laid out in 1 Cor. 15:20-34:

Christ "the firstfruits" was the guarantee of the resurrection of all people (1 Cor. 15:20-23). The "many" Old Testament saints arose immediately after Christ did (Mt. 27:53).

Next in order will be those who are "in Christ" and "are Christ's" at the rapture (1 Cor. 15:23, 51-58; 1 Thess. 4:14-17). After that will be the 144,000 Jews as the manchild of Rev. 12:5.

Then comes the "great multitude" of tribulation saints and the two witnesses at the end of the Tribulation—they'll be there in time for the Marriage Supper and will return with Christ to establish His kingdom (Rev. 6:9-11; 7:9-17; 11:2-13; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

All these groups—because they are "holy," "blessed," and "just"—will be part of the first resurrection and will help complete it (Rev. 20:4-6). The wicked dead won't be raised until 1,000 years later (Rev. 20:4-15).

### 5. The Method of the Resurrection

Paul illustrates the method of resurrection by pointing to the death and resurrection of a grain of wheat or some other grain (1 Cor. 15:35-50). The process grain goes through is something we can all understand, so if you apply this process to the resurrection, it becomes clear.

Here are the main points:

> **(1)** The fact that there is death and resurrection (1 Cor. 15:1-21, 36).

> **(2)** Death must come before resurrection (Jn. 12:24; 1 Cor. 15:36).

> **(3)** What is sown is not the body that will be reproduced (1 Cor. 15:37).

> **(4)** In the resurrection, a body is produced just like the one sown in the ground. If the original body (if perfect) and the reproduced body were placed side by side, you couldn't detect one particle of difference between them (1 Cor. 13:12; 15:38; Lk. 24:39; Acts 1:11).

> **(5)** A grain of corn won't reproduce as a grain of wheat. All flesh is not the same flesh, so in the resurrection you'll be raised with your own flesh—not that of fish or birds or anything else (1 Cor. 15:38-39). You'll be resurrected with your own size, shape, bodily appearance, and features. You won't look unlike you did in your natural body (1 Cor. 13:12).

> **(6)** People will be raised to share more or less glory, or more or less damnation, according to what they've done in life. It's similar to how some grains that are poisonous and useless are reproduced only to be destroyed, while good grains are reproduced for good purposes (1 Cor. 15:40-50).

> **(7)** A grain of wheat may be marred, shriveled, and parts of its body scattered here and there. Yet in reproduction, it will be reproduced as a perfect grain (under normal conditions)—regardless of where the particles were or what shape it was in at germination. The only thing necessary is that the life germ be in the grain. So in the resurrection, each body will be reproduced as a perfect body (1 Cor. 15:35-58).

> **(8)** The only difference between grain and resurrected human bodies will be in the nature of existence. People will be raised to immortality—some to everlasting life and some to everlasting shame and contempt (Dan. 12:3; Jn. 5:28-29; Mt. 10:28). Both the righteous and the wicked will live again in their resurrected bodies, but only the righteous will enter into various glories and have different rewards and responsibilities of rulership in God's eternal affairs (1 Cor. 3:11-15; 15:21-23, 39-50; Rom. 14; 2 Cor. 5:10; Rev. 20:4-6). See Lesson Thirty-seven for a study of the immortality of the soul and body.

### The First Resurrection

This is the resurrection of all the just, the blessed, and holy from Adam to the Millennium (Rev. 20:4-6; Dan. 12:3; Jn. 5:28-29). It covers the period from Christ's resurrection to the resurrection of the Tribulation saints and the two witnesses, including all the groups listed under Point 4 above.

Several raptures are included in the first resurrection. All who have part in this resurrection will have some role in ruling with Christ—not just a select few, as many teach (Rev. 20:4-6).

This resurrection may be called the "out resurrection"—that is, a resurrection *out of* or *from among* the dead (Phil. 3:11-14; 1 Thess. 4:14-17; 1 Cor. 15:51-58; Lk. 14:14; 20:35-36; Heb. 11:35; Rev. 20:4-6). Picture it this way: just as Christ came out from among the dead as "the firstfruits," so at the rapture, the "dead in Christ" will come forth from among the wicked dead. Even the Tribulation saints will come out from the wicked dead (Rev. 20:4-6). The wicked dead don't live again until the thousand years are finished (Rev. 20:4-6).

The Greek expression *ek nekron*, "out of the dead," is used 49 times and teaches a select resurrection from the dead—the righteous selected from among the wicked. But it doesn't teach a select resurrection of some righteous from among the righteous (Mt. 17:9; Mk. 9:10; Lk. 24:46; Acts 3:15; 4:10; 10:41; 13:30; 17:31; Rom. 1:4; 4:24; 6:4-9; 8:11; 1 Cor. 15:12, 20-23; etc.).

The theory that only a few of the righteous will be selected from among the righteous is a mere human invention based on a few Old Testament stories that were never intended as types at all. It's not even worthy of consideration given the lack of one plain Scripture on the subject.

All the dead and living "in Christ" will be in the first resurrection—not just a few. All those who are outside of Christ will be raised together after the Millennium to be judged and cast into the Lake of Fire (Rev. 20:4-6, 11-15).

The "first resurrection" may also be called "the resurrection of the just," "resurrection of life," "a better resurrection," and the "resurrection of [from among] the dead" (Lk. 14:14; Jn. 5:28-29; 11:25; Heb. 11:35; Phil. 3:10-15; Rev. 20:4-6).

### The Second Resurrection

This is the resurrection of all the wicked from Adam to the end of the Millennium (Dan. 12:3; Jn. 5:28-29; Rev. 20:4-6). It will take place after the Millennium and will also include those wicked who die during the Millennium (Rev. 20:4-6, 11-15).

They will be raised with immortal bodies to be tormented in Hell forever (Mt. 10:28; Jn. 5:28-29; 12:24; Dan. 12:3; Acts 24:15; 1 Cor. 15:21, 34-50; Rev. 14:9-12; 19:20; 20:4-6, 11-15).

The theory that only the righteous will be raised to immortality is false and isn't founded on one Scripture. The method of resurrection for the just and unjust is plainly taught and illustrated by a grain of wheat, as explained above.

So what makes the difference between the two resurrections? Doesn't a bad or poisonous seed reproduce in the same way that a good seed does? Don't both have to go through the same process?

The resurrection of people follows this same process. The only difference will be in the glory of one person over another. However, saved and unsaved will both be immortal, as Scripture plainly teaches. Not one statement was ever made to the contrary.

## The Truth About Heaven

We won't take the time and space for a lengthy study about Heaven, but the following points will give you the right biblical understanding of the subject.

### 1. Heaven Is a Created Place

God created Heaven (Gen. 1:1; Isa. 42:5; 45:18; Col. 1:16-18; Neh. 9:6; Ps. 102:25; Prov. 8:27).

### 2. It Is a Real Planet Like the Earth

Heaven is as real as the Earth (Gen. 1:1; 11:8-16). In this last passage, it's called a "better country" than the Earth.

Heaven, therefore, is not an invisible nothing or a cloud floating around in space where saints sit and play a harp forever.

### 3. There Are Three Heavens

Besides the planet called Heaven where God dwells (Gen. 21:17; 22:11, 15; 2 Cor. 12:1-3; Deut. 26:15; 1 Ki. 8:30, 38, 43, 49), there are two other spheres called Heaven.

Paul speaks of three heavens (2 Cor. 12:1-4). Here the third Heaven is called Paradise where God lives.

The other two heavens are: the clouds (Gen. 1:8; 7:23; 8:2; Job 38:9, 34; Isa. 14:14; Jer. 51:16) and the starry space between the Earth and the planet Heaven (Gen. 1:15-20; 22:17; Isa. 14:12-14; Ps. 8:3).

### 4. Heaven Is the Capital of the Universe

God has His capital city—the New Jerusalem—His capitol building (the Heavenly Temple or Tabernacle), and His throne in the Temple in Heaven. John describes this city in Rev. 21-22, as we'll discuss more fully in Lessons Fifty-one and Fifty-two.

This capital city will continue to be in Heaven until the end of the Millennium, and then it will be moved from the planet Heaven to the planet Earth (Rev. 21:2, 9-21).

Many Scriptures speak of God's throne being in Heaven (Ps. 103:19; Rev. 4:1-2). That God has a real temple in Heaven is clear from Rev. 4-5; 11:19; 14:17; 16:17.

### 5. God Owns and Rules in the Heaven of Heavens

God owns and rules the highest Heaven (Gen. 14:19, 22; Ps. 11:4; 89:11; Lk. 10:21; 11:2). He also owns the Earth and all things in the universe, but at present this planet is in rebellion against Him.

This is why Jesus will be sent from Heaven with the armies of Heaven to seize this rebellious part of the universe and restore God's absolute rule on Earth as it was before the rebellion started (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Rev. 19:11-21; 20:1-10; Zech. 14; Jude 14; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Joel 3; Mt. 24:29-31; 25:31-46).

### 6. The Location of the Planet Heaven Is in the Northern Part of the Universe

In Isa. 14:12-14, we're told that Lucifer led a rebellion into Heaven "into the sides of the north." In Ps. 75:6-7, we're told that promotion comes not from the south, east, or west, but from the Lord—and therefore from the north.

### 7. Heaven Is Inhabited

Heaven is full of inhabitants (1 Ki. 22:19; 2 Chron. 18:18; Mt. 18:10; 22:30; 24:36; Rev. 12:12; 13:6). Armies and hosts of Heaven are mentioned a number of times in these passages and in Dan. 4:35; Lk. 2:13; Rev. 19:14.

These inhabitants include common angels, archangels, seraphim, cherubim, and other spirit-beings, as listed in Lesson Six. Heavenly bodies are mentioned in 1 Cor. 15:48-49. Rejoicing in Heaven by its inhabitants is referred to in Ps. 96:11; 148:1, 4; Isa. 44:23; Rev. 12:12.

### 8. Paul Speaks of "The Invisible Things" in Heaven as Being Like the Visible Things on Earth

That is, the things in Heaven are just like things on Earth (Rom. 1:20). The word "things" is used in many passages referring to realities in Heaven (Phil. 2:10; Col. 1:16-20; Heb. 8:12; 9:23).

We know from Scripture that in Heaven there are:

- "cities" (Rev. 21)
- "mansions" (Jn. 14:1-3)
- "trees" (Rev. 22:1-3)
- "rivers" (Rev. 22:1-3)
- "fountains of water" (Rev. 7:17)
- "food" (Exod. 16:4; Ps. 78:25; 105:40; Jn. 6:31-51; Lk. 22:16, 18, 30; Rev. 2:7, 17; 19:1-10; 22:1-3)
- "animals" (2 Ki. 2:11-12; 6:13-17; Zech. 1:8-11; 6:1-8; Rev. 19:11-14, 21; Rom. 1:20)
- "furniture" (Isa. 6:1; Dan. 7:9; Heb. 8:5; 9:23; Rev. 4:2, 4; 6:17)
- "altars" (Isa. 6:6; 6:9-11; 8:5)
- "tongs" (Isa. 6:6)
- "fire" and "coals" (Isa. 6:6; Rev. 8:5)
- "censer" and "incense" (Rev. 8:4-6)
- "smoke" (Rev. 8:4; 15:8)
- "musical instruments" (Rev. 5:8; 14:1-5; 15:2-4)
- "clothes" (Dan. 7:9; Rev. 1:13; 6:9-11)
- "stones" (Rev. 2:17)
- "books" (Rev. 3:5; 5:1-7; 10:1-11; 20:11-15)
- "vials" or "bowls" (Rev. 5:8; 8:3; 15:7; 16:1)
- "crowns" (Rev. 4:4; 14:14; 19:12)
- "stones" (Josh. 10:11)
- "treasures" (Mt. 6:20; 19:21)
- "rainbows," "thunderings," "lightnings," "clouds" (Rev. 3-8; 10:1-7)
- "lamps" (Rev. 4:5)
- "a sea of glass" (Rev. 4:6; 15:2-4)
- "singing" (Rev. 5:9; 14:1-5; 15:2-4)
- "worship" (Rev. 4:9-11; 5:8-14; 7:9-17; 15:2-4; 19:1-10)
- "palm leaves," so there must be palm trees (Rev. 7:9)
- "a temple" (Rev. 7:15; 11:19; 14:17; 16:17)
- "silence," so there must be noise at other times (Rev. 8:1)
- "trumpets" (Rev. 8:2-6; Mt. 24:29-31; 1 Thess. 4:16)
- "hail" (Rev. 8:7; 16:17-21; Ezek. 38:17-21)
- "mountains" (Zech. 6:1; 8:8; 14:1; Heb. 12:23)
- "keys" and "chains" (Rev. 9:1; 20:3; Jude 6-7)
- "measuring sticks" (Rev. 11:1; 21:15)
- "olive trees" and "candlesticks" (Rev. 11:4; Zech. 4)
- "the ark of the testament" (Rev. 11:19)
- "doors" and "posts" (Rev. 4:1; 11:19; Isa. 6:4)
- "girdles" (Rev. 1:13; 15:6)
- "banquets" (Rev. 19:7-10; Lk. 22:30)
- "walls," "gates," "pearls," "diamonds," and other precious stones, as well as "gold" in abundance (Rev. 4:3, 4; 8:3; 15:7; 21:12-21)
- "streets" (Rev. 21:21; 22:1-3)
- "fruits" (Rev. 22:1-3)
- and "innumerable things" (Rom. 1:20; 1 Cor. 2:9; 2 Cor. 12:4)

If the invisible things in the heavenlies are clearly seen by the things on Earth, as Paul taught in Rom. 1:20, then we have a right to believe there are in Heaven the same kind of things we have on Earth. We have no right to make an exception of any good thing that God created and intended for people to have before the Fall.

Several natural men have gone to Heaven and have come back or will later come back. They are "Enoch" (Gen. 5:22; Heb. 11:5); "Elijah" (2 Ki. 2); "Paul" (2 Cor. 12:1-4); and "John" (Rev. 4:1).

The first two of these men are still in Heaven living a natural life and will come back as the two witnesses of Zech. 4:11-14; Rev. 11:3-13. Enoch has been in Heaven over 5,142 years and Elijah has been there over 3,500 years—and they're still eating, drinking, and enjoying life on the planet Heaven.

If we understand statements like these as literal, then the doctrine of the heavenly world will no longer be a mystery.

---

## Questions on Lesson Forty-two

- What two main kinds of resurrections are there? Define both.

- How many kinds of material resurrection are there? Name them.

- Prove from Scripture the fact of a resurrection.

- Give some Scriptures predicting the resurrection of the dead.

- Give some examples of temporary and permanent resurrections.

- Discuss the order of the resurrections.

- Explain and illustrate the method of the resurrections.

- Who will be included in the first resurrection?

- How long does the first resurrection continue?

- How many raptures are there in the first resurrection? Prove.

- What is meant by the "out resurrection" of the dead?

- Does the Bible teach a resurrection of some righteous from the righteous dead?

- Discuss the second resurrection.

- Prove that Heaven is a created planet.

- How many heavens are there? Prove.

- Where is God's capital located? Will it ever be moved? When?

- Who owns and rules Heaven? The Earth?

- Where is Heaven located? Is it inhabited? Prove.

- Name as many things in Heaven as you can.

- Can natural men go to Heaven? Prove.

lessons.title: The Rapture of the Church
lessons.content:
# 43. The Rapture of the Church

## The Rapture of the Church

When we talk about the rapture of the Church, we're talking about something breathtaking — the moment when all true believers in Christ are caught up to meet the Lord in the air. This isn't some mysterious, hidden teaching. It's simply and clearly predicted in 1 Thess. 4:13-17; 1 Cor. 15:23, 51-58; Phil. 3:20, 21; Jn. 14:1-3; Lk. 21:34-36; Col. 3:4 and several other passages we'll look at below.

Even a single reading of these passages will prove that there *will* be such an event — the Lord descending from Heaven to take, in a moment of time, all the dead and living in Christ out of this world. Honestly, it's hard to see how anyone could fail to understand this if they'll just be honest and believe what's plainly written.

For example, look at 1 Thess. 4:16, 17: "For the Lord Himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first: . . . Then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air: and so shall we ever be with the Lord."

With language that clear, you'd have to deliberately deny what's written — or simply believe it will happen exactly as stated. There's really no room for misunderstanding here. If someone doesn't believe in the rapture, it's because they don't believe the Bible.

Now, the rapture is called "the coming of the Lord," but it's never called the second coming of Christ. At the rapture, Christ doesn't appear visibly to people on Earth — He comes in the air *above* the Earth to catch up the dead and living saints, who rise together to meet Him there.

There are many different ideas about these two comings, and that's caused a lot of confusion. So many Scriptures have been misapplied to one coming or the other that you can see why people get tangled up. Here's the key distinction: The rapture is purely a New Testament doctrine and was first revealed to Paul as a special revelation (1 Cor. 15:51-58). The second coming, however, isn't just a New Testament teaching — it's one of the chief themes of the Old Testament, as we'll see in Lesson Forty-nine.

The Old Testament prophets never saw the New Testament Church, much less the rapture of the Church. So we shouldn't call the rapture "the second coming" or "second advent" of Christ — He doesn't come to the Earth at that time. And we shouldn't call it "one stage or phase" of the second coming, either. At the rapture, the Lord doesn't remain in the air during the tribulation and then complete a "second stage" by coming to Earth. No — the rapture is a distinct coming, not to the Earth, but in the air, where Christ meets the saints and takes them back to Heaven to present them blameless before God (Jn. 14:1-3; 1 Thess. 3:13; 4:16, 17).

Here's where ministers sometimes confuse people. They'll say that many signs must be seen and many prophecies fulfilled before the second coming of Christ — and then, in the same breath, say He might come tonight or at any moment. How can both be true? If they wouldn't mix up the rapture with the second advent, the contradiction would disappear. The rapture can happen at any moment; the second advent cannot happen until certain events take place.

Think of it like this: imagine you're expecting two different guests. One could knock on your door at any second — no warning needed. The other has told you they won't arrive until after certain things happen first. If you mix up which guest is which, you'll be thoroughly confused about what to expect and when!

These two comings simply cannot be mixed if we want to understand either one clearly. The Scriptures that apply to one don't apply to the other. Not one passage under the second advent in Lesson Forty-nine refers to the rapture, and not one passage we'll list below refers to the second coming. There's not a single Bible passage that treats both events as if they were one.

They are two distinct comings, separated by several years — not two "phases" or "stages" of the same coming. The rapture takes place several years before Christ's literal advent to the Earth, because the saints come back *with* Him at that time. The saints are in Heaven before God — not in the air — from the time of the rapture until they return with Christ to reign as kings and priests (Jude 14; Rev. 19:14; Zech. 14:5).

This seems clear from several facts: the saints are judged, given their rewards, and partake of the Marriage Supper in Heaven, not in the air (Rev. 19:1-10; 2 Cor. 5:9-10). Christ departs from Heaven at His second advent to Earth — not from the air (Rev. 19:11-21; 2 Thess. 1:7-10). The rapture must happen first — Christ must come *for* His saints (1 Thess. 4:16-17) before He can come back to Earth *with* them (Jude 14; Zech. 14:5).

At the rapture, the Lord comes from Heaven as far as the air, and the saints are caught up to meet Him there. At this event, the Lord isn't being raptured — the saints are. At the second coming, the saints aren't raptured and neither is Christ; both come back to Earth together.

The rapture takes place *before* the Tribulation; the second coming takes place *after* the Tribulation. The rapture may occur any moment, but the second coming cannot occur until all the signs in Lesson Forty-four come to pass and until certain prophecies are fulfilled.

We've separated these two comings because they *should* be separated — for the sake of clarity and because Scripture always keeps them distinct. The following points will help you distinguish the rapture from the second coming and give you a solid, scriptural understanding of this New Testament revelation.

## I. The Fact and Manner of the Rapture

You don't need to be in the dark about this subject — it's fully and clearly revealed in many New Testament passages. Let's look at the fact and manner of the rapture as Scripture reveals it:

"Watch ye therefore, and pray always, that ye may be accounted worthy to escape all these things that shall come to pass, and to stand before the Son of man" (Lk. 21:34-36).

"And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also" (Jn. 14:1-3).

"But every man in his own order: Christ the firstfruits; afterward they that are Christ's at his coming . . . Behold, I shew you a mystery; We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed . . . In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump: for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed . . . For this corruptible must put on incorruption, and this mortal must put on immortality . . . So when this corruptible shall have put on incorruption, and this mortal shall have put on immortality, then shall be brought to pass the saying that is written, Death is swallowed up in victory . . . O death, where is thy sting? O grave, where is thy victory? The sting of death is sin; and the strength of sin is the law. But thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, forasmuch as ye know that your labour is not in vain in the Lord" (1 Cor. 15:23, 51-58).

"That he might present it to himself a glorious Church, not having spot, or wrinkle, or any such thing; but that it should be holy and without blemish" (Eph. 5:27).

"For our conversation is in heaven; from whence also we look for the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ: Who shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto his glorious body, according to the working whereby he is able even to subdue all things unto himself" (Phil. 3:20, 21).

"But I would not have you to be ignorant, brethren, concerning them which are asleep, that ye sorrow not, even as others which have no hope. For if we believe that Jesus died and rose again, even so them also which sleep in Jesus will God bring with him. For this we say unto you by the word of the Lord, that we which are alive and remain unto the coming of the Lord shall not prevent [precede] them which are asleep. For the Lord himself shall descend from heaven with a shout, with the voice of the archangel, and with the trump of God: and the dead in Christ shall rise first: Then we which are alive and remain shall be caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air: and so shall we ever be with the Lord" (1 Thess. 4:13-17).

"Now we beseech you, brethren, by the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, and by our gathering together unto him . . . For the mystery of iniquity doth already work: only he who now letteth will let, until he be taken out of the way. And then shall that Wicked be revealed whom the Lord shall consume with the spirit of his mouth, and shall destroy with the brightness of his coming" (2 Thess. 2:1, 7, 8).

See also these other passages on the rapture: 2 Cor. 5:1-8; Phil. 3:11; 1 Thess. 2:19; 3:13; 5:9, 23; Col. 3:4; Jas. 5:7, 8; 1 Jn. 2:28; 3:2; 1 Pet. 5:4.

Not one of these passages refers to the second advent. We won't use any verse in Mt. 24 and 25 in connection with the rapture — there's not one verse in those chapters that refers to the rapture, as we'll see in Lesson Forty-four. We're dealing only with passages that refer to the rapture and that can be consistently explained only in that context.

There are two Greek words used in most of these rapture passages:

**1. Parousia** means "personal coming or appearance" and is used of both the rapture and the revelation of Christ. At the rapture, Christ appears personally in the air to meet the saints; at the second advent, He appears personally to mankind on the Earth with His saints. This word *parousia* is generally translated "coming" — so both the rapture and the revelation are called "the coming of the Lord," but they are two different comings for two different purposes.

The word is used of the rapture in 1 Cor. 15:20-23; 1 Thess. 2:19; 3:13; 4:15; 5:23; 2 Thess. 2:1; Jas. 5:7, 8; 1 Jn. 2:28. All these passages clearly refer to the rapture, though 1 Thess. 3:13; 5:23; 2 Thess. 2:1 are used by some in connection with the second advent. But it's clear that 1 Thess. 3:13; 5:23 refer to when the Father pronounces the saints "blameless" before His throne in Heaven — *after* Christ has met the saints in the air, taken them to Heaven, and presented them "before God." So these refer only to the rapture.

"The coming of our Lord Jesus Christ with all his saints" in these passages refers only to His coming back to Heaven with the saints at the rapture — not at the second advent (1 Jn. 3:1-3; Phil. 3:21; 1 Thess. 3:13; 5:23; Col. 3:4).

The last reference (2 Thess. 2:1) also refers to the rapture, as proven by the Greek word *episunagoge*, which means a complete collection or gathering of all the living and dead in Christ from all parts of the Earth, out of all denominations and dispensations, to meet Christ in the air. We are gathered "unto him" at the rapture, not at the second coming (Lk. 21:34-36; Jn. 14:1-5; 1 Cor. 15:51-58; 2 Cor. 5:1-8; 1 Thess. 4:13-18; 2 Thess. 2:6-8).

In Phil. 3:11, the rapture is called "the resurrection of the dead" — or literally, "the out-resurrection"; that is, the resurrection *from out among* the dead. The term "resurrection of the dead" is frequently used in the New Testament and includes the resurrection of both the just and the unjust (Jn. 5:29; Acts 24:19). But "out-resurrection" is used only in Phil. 3:11 and implies the resurrection of *some* — the just from the unjust (Rev. 20:4-6). The dead "rise first," then the living who are saved are "caught up together with them in the clouds, to meet the Lord in the air" (1 Thess. 4:13-18). If you keep these thoughts in mind, you can easily harmonize all the passages above — and the ones below — about the rapture.

**2. Phaneros** means "to shine," "be apparent," "manifest," or "be seen," and is used in 1 Jn. 2:28; 3:2; 1 Pet. 5:4; Col. 3:4. The English translation is "appear" — meaning Christ is to appear to the saints in the air at the rapture. He won't appear to the world until His second coming (Heb. 9:28).

The rapture will include Old Testament saints and Church saints — everyone saved within the scope of redemption from Adam until the rapture, as explained above (1 Thess. 4:13-18; 2 Thess. 2:1). We're not saying the scope of redemption ends with the rapture — it's eternal, as we'll see in Lessons Forty-nine through Fifty-two.

The "trump of God" (1 Thess. 4:16) is not the same as the seventh trumpet of Rev. 11:15; 13:18. Here's how they differ:

- One occurs at the rapture of the Church and the Old Testament saints (1 Thess. 4:16); the other is at the rapture of the manchild (Rev. 11:15–12:5).
- One is the trumpet of God (1 Thess. 4:16); the other is the trumpet of the seventh trumpet angel (Rev. 11:15).
- One heralds a single event that takes place "in the twinkling of an eye" (1 Cor. 15:51-58); the other heralds many events lasting days (Rev. 10:7).
- One is a trumpet of blessing (1 Thess. 4:16-17); the other is a trumpet of "woe" (Rev. 8:13; 11:15; 12:12).
- One is before the beginning of the Seventieth Week (2 Thess. 2:7-8; Dan. 9:27; Rev. 4:1); the other is in the middle of the Week (Rev. 11:15–12:6).
- One is before the saints, represented by the twenty-four elders, are caught up in Rev. 4:1, as proven in Point VII below; the other is after the elders are already in Heaven (Rev. 11:15–12:5).
- One is before the seven seals and first six trumpets (Rev. 6:1–9:21); the other is after them (Rev. 11:15).

So there's no need to confuse this trumpet and rapture with the seventh trumpet and the rapture of the manchild.

## II. The Purpose of the Rapture

The purpose of the rapture is to resurrect the just from the dead and take all the saints out of the world *before* the Tribulation — so that God can fulfill in them the purpose for which He saved them.

Jesus told His disciples that some would escape the terrible things coming on the Earth in the last days. He said, "Pray that ye may be accounted worthy to escape all these things [of Mt. 24, 25; Lk. 21:1-19, 25-28] that shall come to pass, and to stand before the Son of man" (Lk. 21:34-36). This passage is practically the same in essence as Jn. 14:1-3.

These two passages (Lk. 21:34-36; Jn. 14:1-3) are the only ones in the Gospels that are clear concerning the rapture. Jesus didn't reveal this mystery in full — that was given to Paul many years later in 1 Cor. 15:51. The disciples had no idea *how* they were to escape unless they thought Christ would deliver them by His power. The "how" wasn't revealed or even mentioned before Paul explained it.

Now, in the light of the mystery revealed, we can see that the rapture is what Christ had in mind when He spoke of some being worthy to escape. The Thessalonians were taught to expect the living to be taken out of the world, but some were confused about whether dead believers would share in the rapture. So Paul explained in his first epistle that both the living and the dead would be caught up to meet Christ in the air (1 Thess. 4:13-18). In this passage, the purpose is expressed: "so shall we ever be with the Lord." The rapture enables the saints to escape the Tribulation days and serve God in all eternity in whatever capacity He chooses.

This rapture before the beginning of the Week is the first in a series of raptures during the first resurrection. Besides this one, there will be:

- The rapture of the manchild in the middle of the Week (Rev. 7:1-3; 12:5; 14:1-5)
- The rapture of the great multitude of tribulation saints (Rev. 6:9-11; 7:9-17; 15:2-4; 20:4-6)
- The rapture of the two witnesses at the end of the Week (Rev. 11:3-13)

The teaching of more than one rapture is not only stated in these passages, but is necessary to make clear what Paul meant when he said, "every man in his own order" (1 Cor. 15:20-23). The Greek word for "order" is *tagma*, and it occurs only here in the New Testament. It's used in the Septuagint (the Greek version of the Old Testament) of a body of soldiers and a whole army (Num. 2:2; 2 Sam. 23:13). It means "a company or body of individuals."

For every man to be raptured "in his own order" or company, there must be different companies of redeemed people, saved and raptured at different periods. This is proven by the following facts:

> 1. **The Old Testament saints.** John the Baptist completed this company (Mt. 11:1-12; Lk. 16:16; Jn. 3:29). In these passages, both Jesus and John recognized it as separate from the Church saints. (See also Acts 7:38; Heb. 11:1-4; 12:1.)

> 2. **The Church saints.** Passages like Mt. 16:18; 1 Cor. 12:27, 28; Eph. 1:20-23; 2:14-22; 4:12-16; 5:21-32; Col. 1:18, 24 prove that the Church of the New Testament did not begin before Christ's ministry on Earth — He is the head and founder of it.

> 3. **The 144,000 Jews** are identified as a separate company from any others in Rev. 7:1-8; 9:4; 14:1-5. (See also Isa. 66:7-8; Dan. 12:1.)

> 4. **The great multitude of tribulation saints** are proven to be a distinct company in Rev. 6:9-11; 7:9-17; 14:13; 15:2-4; 20:4-6.

### The Purpose of the Rapture May be Summed up as Follows:

> 1. To receive the saints to Himself (Jn. 14:1-3; Eph. 5:27; 2 Thess. 2:1).

> 2. To resurrect the dead "in Christ" from among the wicked dead (1 Cor. 15:21-23, 51-58; 1 Thess. 4:13-17; Phil. 3:11, 20, 21; Rev. 20:4-6).

> 3. To take the saints to Heaven where they will receive judgment for works done in the body, receive their rewards, and partake of the Marriage Supper (Jn. 14:1-3; Col. 3:4; 1 Thess. 3:13; 2 Cor. 5:10; Rev. 19:1-11).

> 4. To change the bodies of saints from mortality to immortality (1 Cor. 15:21-23, 51-58; Phil. 3:20, 21; 2 Cor. 5:1-8).

> 5. To present the saints before God the Father to be forever with Him (1 Thess. 3:13; 4:13-17; Eph. 5:27; 2 Thess. 2:1; Jn. 14:1-3).

> 6. To make the saints "whole" in body, soul, and spirit (1 Thess. 5:23; 2 Thess. 3:13). The Greek word translated "unto" in 1 Thess. 5:23 should have been translated "at" to make the passage clear, just as it is translated in 1 Thess. 2:19; 3:13.

> 7. To receive the fruit of the early and latter rain (Jas. 5:7).

> 8. To cause the saints to escape the Tribulation and "all these things," and stand before the Son of man (Lk. 21:34-36; 2 Thess. 2:7, 8; Rev. 4:1; 1 Thess. 5:9).

> 9. To remove the hinderer of lawlessness (2 Thess. 2:1-8).

> 10. To permit the revelation of the Antichrist (2 Thess. 2:1-8).

## III. Qualifications For Partakers in the Rapture

What does it take to be part of the rapture? The qualifications are revealed in the passages we've looked at. The one and only necessary requirement — whether you're dead or alive — is to be "in Christ" (1 Thess. 4:16, 17; 2 Cor. 5:17; 1 Cor. 15:23; Gal. 5:24).

These qualifications are expressed in nine ways in Scripture. You must:

> 1. Be "Christ's" (1 Cor. 15:23; Gal. 5:24).

> 2. Be "in Christ" (1 Thess. 4:16, 17; 2 Cor. 5:17).

> 3. Be "blessed and holy" (Rev. 20:4-6; Heb. 12:14).

> 4. "Have done good" (Jn. 5:28, 29).

> 5. Be in "the way, the truth, and the life" (Jn. 14:1-6).

> 6. Be "worthy" (Lk. 21:34-36).

> 7. Be in "the Church" or "body of Christ" (Eph. 5:27; 1 Cor. 12:13). The body of Christ and the Church are the same (Eph. 1:22, 23; Col. 1:18, 24).

> 8. Purify "himself, even as he is pure" (1 Jn. 1:7; 3:2, 3; 2 Cor. 7:1; Gal. 5:16-26; Heb. 12:14).

> 9. Be "without spot or wrinkle . . . and without blemish" (Eph. 5:27).

If you've met these scriptural qualifications, what more could you do? This means someone going up in the rapture is walking "in the light as he is in the light" (1 Jn. 1:7; 2:6, 9-11; 3:8-10; 5:4, 18).

Being "in Christ" means you're a "new creature: old things are passed away; behold, all things are become new. And all things are of God who hath reconciled us to himself by Jesus Christ" (2 Cor. 5:17, 18). To belong to Christ means you "have crucified the flesh (Gal. 5:19-21) with the affections and lusts" (Gal. 5:24). This means you're good, holy, blessed, and free from the sin business. What better qualifications could God require?

Notice that these nine points come from Scriptures that specifically deal with the rapture. If there were other qualifications — more definite or more important — wouldn't they have been stated in those passages instead of these?

We conclude that it's not receiving other experiences (however scriptural they may be) that qualifies you for the rapture. It's maintaining a holy walk "in Christ" at the time of the rapture — or at the time of death, as the case may be.

Why should some be protected while others go through the Tribulation and be martyred? This is easily answered. At the rapture, everyone in Christ is taken up — there are no real Christians left at that moment. Those who are martyred in the Tribulation are those who refused to walk in the light and live "in Christ." They weren't ready to go at the time of the rapture; otherwise, they would have gone along with everyone else in Christ (1 Thess. 4:16-17; 1 Cor. 15:23).

They are saved *after* the rapture (Acts 2:16-21), having realized by then their mistake of not heeding the many warnings to be ready. They'll become determined to be faithful even unto death — which will be their only prospect of having a part in the first resurrection.

Those who aren't martyred but are taken through the Tribulation and are on Earth at Christ's second coming will be permitted to enter the Millennium as an earthly people. They'll be part of the subjects of Christ's kingdom over whom the raptured saints will reign forever (Mt. 13:41-43; 25:34, 46; Isa. 2:1-4; 9:6-7; Lk. 1:32-35; Rev. 5:10; 11:15; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14).

## IV. The Time of the Rapture

The time of the rapture — like the second advent — isn't stated as to the exact day or hour. But we do know it will take place *before* the Tribulation and the revelation of the Antichrist, as we'll prove in Point VII below.

## V. The Signs of the Rapture

Here's something important: we can say, with full scriptural backing, that there are *no* signs of the rapture the way there are signs of the second advent. None of the signs and prophecies listed in Lesson Forty-nine as happening before Christ's second coming ever refer to the rapture.

There never was a sign that had to come to pass, nor a prophecy to be fulfilled, before the rapture. The rapture could have happened at any time in the past, and it can happen at any moment now or in the future — without any sign or prophecy needing fulfillment first. If certain prophecies had to be fulfilled beforehand, we'd be looking for those events instead of looking for the rapture.

It's possible some of the signs and prophecies might come to pass before the rapture, but that remains to be seen. We do know, by certain indications, that some prophecies are beginning to be fulfilled — showing us the second coming is very near. And if the second coming is near, the rapture is nearer, since it will take place at least seven years before the revelation (as we'll see below).

The rapture can take place at any time. This is proven by the fact that the early Christians were looking for it in their day (Phil. 3:20-21; Titus 2:13). They never said certain events had to happen first.

So all the modern theories are wrong that teach certain signs must come to pass, that certain prophecies must be fulfilled, that the Antichrist must first come, that the Tribulation must come first and be completed, or that a revival must first come to get the Church ready. All of that is completely out of harmony with the doctrine of the rapture as taught in Scripture.

## VI. The Lord's Comings Are Practical Doctrines

The two comings of the Lord — the rapture and the second advent — are some of the most practical and fruitful doctrines in all of Scripture. They're real "incentives to holiness" (1 Jn. 3:1-3), "profitable in exhorting to watchfulness and faithfulness" (Mt. 24:25; 1 Jn. 2:28), "patience" (Jas. 5:7, 8), "death to self" (Col. 3:3-5), "endurance" (1 Pet. 1:7; 4:13), "holy conversation and godliness" (2 Pet. 3:11-13), "soberness and righteousness" (Titus 2:11-13), "consolation" (1 Thess. 4:13-18), "blamelessness" (1 Cor. 1:4-8), "pastoral diligence and purity" (1 Pet. 5:1-4), "sincerity" (Phil. 1:9, 10), "mildness" (Phil. 4:5, 6), and "hope" (1 Cor. 15).

For the saints, the rapture will be the beginning of eternal and ever-increasing joy and glory. For Israel and all righteous earthly nations, the second coming will be the beginning of eternal and ever-increasing blessing and favor.

## VII. Reasons for the Rapture of the Church in Rev. 4:1

We believe that the Church and the Old Testament saints will be raptured in fulfillment of Rev. 4:1 — that this is foreshadowed by John's bodily ascension to Heaven, and that those raptured are represented in Heaven by the twenty-four elders throughout the remainder of Revelation. Here's the proof:

### 1. A Marked Change in God's Attitude

There's a dramatic shift in God's attitude toward humanity — from mercy (Rev. 1-3) to judgment (Rev. 6-19). Revelation 4-5 pictures the raptured Church and Old Testament saints with God in Heaven *before* the Tribulation.

We know that from the Church's beginning until its rapture is a period of extended mercy without judgments from Heaven. But from the rapture until the second advent, judgments are predominant. The seals and first six trumpets (Rev. 6:1–9:21) take place in the first 3½ years of the Week (the "lesser tribulation"). The sixth seal and all seven trumpets reveal terrible judgments on Earth from Heaven, as do the seven vials during the last 3½ years (the "great tribulation").

The evil designs of the whore and the ten kings of Revised Rome, before the Antichrist gains control and destroys the whore (Rev. 17:12-17), cause the lesser tribulation, which calls forth the judgments in the sixth and seventh trumpets. The judgments themselves don't *make* the Tribulation — they're sent *because of* it.

Scarcely anyone believes the Church will undergo the Tribulation. Some who think the Church isn't caught up until the middle of the Week try to place the seals and trumpets in the last 3½ years — to match their view that the Church isn't here during that time.

It's true the Church won't undergo the seals and trumpet judgments. But taking them out of the first 3½ years and placing them in the last 3½ years is out of harmony with the plain, consecutive order of Revelation. These judgments happen in the order given — from the beginning to the

lessons.title: Exposition of Matthew 24 and 25
lessons.content:
# Exposition of Matthew 24 and 25

## The Ten Virgins

Let's dive into what I believe is one of the most illuminating sections of Scripture for understanding the Age of Grace, Christ's second advent, and the end of the age. What we're about to explore will help correct some common misunderstandings about the rapture and shed light on how Rev. 6–19 fits into Daniel's Seventieth Week.

Here's the interesting thing — these chapters in Mt. 24–25 are actually among the simplest to understand, yet they're also some of the most misunderstood passages in the entire Bible! They present a series of events in consecutive order, just as they're given.

The whole discourse began when the disciples proudly showed Jesus the magnificent stones of Herod's temple. His response must have stunned them: "There shall not be left one stone upon another that shall not be thrown down." This shocking statement prompted three specific questions from the disciples.

## I. "Tell us when shall these things be?"

This first question (Mt. 24:3) refers directly to Jesus' statement about Jerusalem's destruction — something that was fulfilled in 70 A.D. by the Romans (Dan. 9:26; Lk. 21:20-24).

You have to understand why the disciples were so shaken by Jesus' words. The Jews had every right to be proud of their temple! It was made of snowy marble and gold, glittering brilliantly in the sunlight against the half-encircling green background of Mount Olivet.

In all his travels, no Jew had seen a city to match Jerusalem. Neither Antioch in Asia nor even Rome herself could compete with its architectural splendor. There simply has never been — in ancient or modern times — a sacred building equal to this temple, whether for location or magnificence.

The temple occupied an area of about nineteen acres and was considered one of the wonders of the world. Josephus tells us there were stones in the temple about 25 cubits (roughly 52 feet) in length, 8 cubits (about 16 feet) in height, and about 12 cubits (approximately 25 feet) in breadth.

The disciples were pointing out the nine gates overlaid with gold and silver, the one gate of solid Corinthian brass, the towering porches, the alternating blocks of red and white marble, and the vast clusters of golden grapes — each cluster as large as a man — hanging over the golden doors.

Yet Jesus declared that not one stone would be left upon another in the future destruction. Think about how improbable this must have seemed at the time! The world was at peace, the Jewish nation was subject to Rome and under its protection.

But within forty years, the prophecy was fulfilled to the letter. After a three-year siege by Vespasian and his son Titus, Jerusalem fell and the temple was destroyed in August, 70 A.D.

## II. "What shall be the sign of thy coming?"

This second question (Mt. 24:3) is crucial to understand correctly. It does *not* concern the rapture of the Church to meet Christ in the air (1 Thess. 4:13-17). Instead, it's asking about the second coming of Christ to Earth with the saints — the event that happens *after* the rapture when He returns to establish His kingdom.

The disciples knew nothing about the rapture at this point, as we've seen in previous lessons. But they had often heard about Christ's literal advent to Earth (Mt. 18:1; 19:29 etc.).

The signs of the second advent are as follows:

> 1. False messiahs before the middle of the Seventieth Week of Daniel, or setting up of the abomination of desolation (Mt. 24:4, 15; Mk. 13:5, 6; Lk. 21:8).

> 2. Wars and rumors of wars (Mt. 24:6; Mk. 13:7; Lk. 21:9).

> 3. Nations against nations (Mt. 24:7; Mk. 13:8; Lk. 21:10; Rev. 6:1-4).

> 4. Famines (Mt. 24:7; Mk. 13:8; Lk. 21:11; Rev. 6:5-6).

> 5. Pestilences (Mt. 24:7; Mk. 13:8; Lk. 21:11; Rev. 6:7-8).

> 6. Earthquakes (Mt. 24:7; Mk. 13:8; Lk. 21:11; Rev. 6:12-17; 11:13; 16:17-21).

> 7. Persecution of the Jews by all nations (Mt. 24:9; Mk. 13:9-11; Lk. 21:12; Joel 3; Zech. 14; Rev. 12:13-17).

> 8. Many offences and betrayals (Mt. 24:10; Mk. 13:12-13; Lk. 21:16; 2 Tim. 3).

> 9. False prophets before the middle of the Seventieth Week (Mt. 24:11, 15; 1 Tim. 4; 2 Tim. 3:1-8; 4:1-4).

> 10. Iniquity abounding (Mt. 24:12; 1 Tim. 4; 2 Tim. 3:1-8; 4:1-4; Rev. 9:20-21; 13:1-18; 16:2, 6, 9, 11; 18:2).

> 11. Love waxing cold (Mt. 24:12; 2 Tim. 3; Rev. 6:9-11; 7:14).

> 12. The gospel of the kingdom preached into all the world as a witness unto all nations, not to each person of all nations (Mt. 24:13-14).

> 13. The abomination of desolation set up (Mt. 24:15; Dan. 9:27; 12:7-11; Rev. 13:14-18; 2 Thess. 2:3-4).

> 14. Flight of Israel into the wilderness (Mt. 24:16-20; Mk. 13:14-18; Isa. 16:1-5; 26:20-21; 63:1-5; Ezek. 20:33-44; Dan. 11:35-45; Hos. 2:14-23; Ps. 60:6-12; Rev. 12:6-17).

> 15. The great tribulation days (Mt. 24:21-22, 29-31; Mk. 13:19-20; Dan. 12:1; Rev. 12:13-17; 13:1-18; 16:1–19:21).

> 16. False messiahs after the middle of the Seventieth Week (Mt. 24:23-26; Mk. 13:21-22; 1 Tim. 4; 2 Tim. 3:1-8; 4:1-4; 2 Thess. 2; Rev. 13:1-10).

> 17. False prophets after the middle of the Seventieth Week (Mt. 24:23-26; Mk. 13:22; 1 Tim. 4; 2 Tim. 3:1-8; 4:1-4; Rev. 13:11-18).

> 18. Conditions as in the days of Noah and Lot repeated (Mt. 24:37-39; Lk. 17:22-37; 2 Tim. 3; 2 Pet. 3; Rev. 9:20-21).

> 19. Fearful sights and distress on Earth (Mt. 24:4-31; Lk. 21:11, 25-28; Acts 2:16-21; 2 Tim. 3; Rev. 6:1–19:21).

> 20. Great signs in the heavens and on Earth (Mt. 24:4-31; Lk. 21:11, 25-28; Acts 2:16-21; Rev. 6:12-17; 8:7–9:21; 11:1-13; 12:13-17; 13:1-18; 14:1-11; 16:1-21; 18:1-24).

All these signs — along with many others — must take place before Christ's second coming to Earth, which is exactly what the disciples were asking about. This proves the coming they inquired about was not the rapture, but the second advent. So we need to understand Mt. 24-25 in light of Christ's literal coming to Earth, not the rapture of the Church.

The first twelve signs will be fulfilled before the abomination is set up in Mt. 24:15. After listing the first six signs, Jesus said, "All these signs are the beginning of sorrows." The word "sorrows" here means "birth pangs" (Greek, *odin*), as used in 1 Thess. 5:3; Gal. 4:19, 27; Rev. 12:2.

Think of it like a woman going into labor — these "birth pangs" refer to Israel's agonies under the future ten kings of Revised Rome and under the whore during the first 3½ years of the Seventieth Week. These pangs will continue throughout the rest of the Week until the whole nation is delivered by Christ at His coming (Zech. 12:10; 14:1-21; Mt. 23:37-39; 24:29-31; Rev. 19:11-21).

Israel's travail will actually begin *before* the Seventieth Week, for Antichrist will make a covenant with her to protect her for seven years — the entire Seventieth Week (Dan. 9:27).

### Three Important Questions Answered

**1. How do we know that Israel is the one primarily dealt with in Mt. 24–25, and not the Church?**

> (1) Jesus is speaking to Jews and answering a Jewish question concerning their Messiah and His coming to deliver them from Gentile oppression (Mt. 24:34; Lk. 12:32; 22:29; Acts 1:6; Joel 3; Zech. 14).

> (2) The deceptions by false messiahs primarily concern Israel (Mt. 24:5, 23-26; 1 Jn. 2:18).

> (3) The seventh sign about Jewish persecution by Gentiles proves Mt. 24–25 concerns Israel. This sign is connected to the "birth pangs" by the word "then," showing the fulfillment happens at the same time as the sorrows.

> (4) The seventh through twelfth signs are connected to the "birth pangs" by connecting words, showing all the first twelve signs will be fulfilled between these sorrows and the end of the Week. The first six signs mark the beginning of sorrows, so all twelve concern Israel during her travail-time at the end of the age.

> (5) The fulfillment of all these signs happens during the last generation at the end of the age. Those who undergo these sufferings are blessed only if they endure to the end (Mt. 24:13-14). How could some endure to the end if they weren't living at that time? Who besides the Jews will undergo such sufferings at the end of the age? The tribulation saints couldn't be meant, because they aren't in Judea to flee like these afflicted people (Mt. 24:15, 16).

> (6) "The gospel of the kingdom" is purely Jewish — it's the good news that the kingdom of Heaven is at hand and David's kingdom will soon be re-established because the King of the Jews is returning. This will be preached during the Tribulation "as a witness unto all nations," and then the end will come (Mt. 24:13, 14).

> (7) The abomination of desolation concerns only Israel (Mt. 24:15; Dan. 9:24-27; 12:7-13; 2 Thess. 2:3-4; Rev. 11:1-2).

> (8) The Jews fleeing from Judea when the abomination is set up in the middle of the Week proves Israel is being dealt with (Mt. 24:15-22; Rev. 12:13-17).

> (9) The "sabbath day" — which would limit orthodox Jews' flight from Antichrist to about a mile — proves a Jewish connection (Mt. 24:15-22; Rev. 12:13-17).

> (10) The great tribulation from the setting up of the abomination primarily concerns Israel (Mt. 24:15-22; Dan. 9:27; Jer. 30; Rev. 12:6, 14; 13:1-18).

> (11) The "elect" in Mt. 24:21-26 whom Christ will fully regather when He comes after the Tribulation are Jews, as proven in Mt. 24:31; Isa. 11:10-15; Ezek. 37.

> (12) Christ's coming referred to in this question (Mt. 24:3) is to deliver Israel and fulfill all prophecies of her restoration (Mt. 24:29-31; 25:31-46; Joel 3; Zech. 14).

> (13) The judgment of nations at Christ's coming is based on how they treated Israel — His "brethren" — at the end of the age (Mt. 25:31-46).

> (14) All the parables in Mt. 24–25 illustrate the end of the age in connection with Christ's literal advent, proving they refer primarily to Israel.

> (15) In none of these passages could we insert the Church, because it's raptured "before all these things" (Lk. 21:34-36). This promise in Luke was given to believers *after* Jesus fully answered the disciples' questions, so it's separate from those answers. It shows some will escape "all these things" mentioned in the answers. Even if you believe the Church is raptured in the middle of the Week, you couldn't insert the Church in Mt. 24:15–25:46, because the middle of the Week is mentioned in Mt. 24:15 and everything afterward concerns events *after* that midpoint. This is proven by connecting words like "then" and "for then" (Mt. 24:16, 21, 23, 29-31, 40, 45; 25:1; etc.).

> If the Church or Gentile Christians were being dealt with in Mt. 24–25, wouldn't you expect at least *some* plain reference to them, as there is with Israel in these fifteen points?

**2. Are all the signs of Mt. 24-25 to be fulfilled in one particular generation, or throughout this whole age?**

That they're fulfilled in one generation at the end of the age is clear:

> (1) This has already been proven in the fifteen points above.

> (2) The parable of the fig tree (discussed below) proves this.

> (3) It's definitely stated that "all these things" happen in one particular generation. "This generation shall not pass, till all these things be fulfilled" (Mt. 24:34). The phrase "this generation" appears sixteen times in the New Testament, and in every case it refers to a particular generation — not to a race of people (Mt. 11:16; 12:41-42; 23:36; 24:34; Mk. 8:12; 13:30; Lk. 7:31; 11:30, 31, 32, 50, 51; 17:25; 21:32). Compare "evil" and "adulterous" generation (Mt. 12:39, 45; 16:4; Mk. 8:38; Lk. 11:29), "faithless and perverse" generation (Mt. 17:17; Mk. 9:19; Lk. 9:41), and "untoward generation" (Acts 2:40). The word "generation" couldn't refer to a race passing away, since all people are eternal and will continue in the New Earth forever.

> (4) "The days of Noah" refer to one generation only (Mt. 24:37-39; Gen. 7:1).

> (5) Jesus plainly promised some will escape "all these things" — which couldn't be true if they were fulfilled throughout the age (Lk. 21:34-36).

> (6) Some will "endure to the end" of the age — which couldn't be true unless those who endure are living at the end (Mt. 24:13).

> (7) The abomination of desolation will be set up in the middle of the Week (Mt. 24:15; Dan. 9:27). Everything after Mt. 24:15 will be fulfilled after this. All the signs before verse 15 could easily be fulfilled in one generation.

**3. If these signs are to be fulfilled during the last generation, what signs today show we're near the second advent and can look for the rapture before His literal coming?**

Many signs in other Scriptures are being fulfilled today. Many prophecies prove we're in the last days and near Christ's second coming — but we're much nearer the rapture, which will itself be a sign of the second advent.

The signs in Mt. 24-25 refer to a definite period just before the age ends and *after* the rapture — namely, Daniel's Seventieth Week.

Here's a summary of the answer about signs of Christ's coming: In Mt. 24:4-14 we have signs before the abomination is set up (Mt. 24:15). These, along with the signs of Mt. 24:15-26 (from the abomination to the second coming), will continue until Christ comes as pictured in Mt. 24:27-31. At that coming, the third question is answered (see Point III below).

Here are signs *outside* Mt. 24-25 that prove the nearness of the second advent:

> (1) **Travel and increase of knowledge** (Dan. 12:4). Under this heading you could list countless inventions proving we're living near Jesus Christ's return. While no specific invention is mentioned in Scripture, all of them can be included under this prophecy about increased knowledge.

> (2) **The cry of peace and safety** (1 Thess. 5:1-9). This will be the unbelieving world deceived by Antichrist into thinking that when he conquers Jerusalem, there will be world peace — but instead, sudden destruction from Heaven will come when Christ returns (Zech. 14; Joel 3; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Jude 14; Rev. 19:11-21).

> (3) **Great deceptions and delusions** (2 Thess. 2:1-12; 1 Tim. 4:1-8; Rev. 13).

> (4) **An abundance of false doctrines of demons** (1 Tim. 4:1-8; 2 Tim. 3:1-8; 4:1-4).

> (5) **Many other signs** listed in Scripture: general lying, seared consciences, forbidding to marry, commanding to abstain from meats (1 Tim. 4:1-8), perilous times, lovers of self, covetous, boasters, proud, blasphemers, disobedient to parents, unthankful, unholy, without natural affection, trucebreakers, false accusers, incontinent (unrestrained in passions), fierce, despisers of good people, traitors, heady, highminded, pleasure-lovers more than God-lovers, having a form of godliness but denying its power, lustful, leading captive silly women, ever learning but never coming to truth, corrupt minds, reprobate concerning truth, destitute of faith, rejecting sound doctrine, heaping up teachers with itching ears, turning from truth to fables (2 Tim. 3:1-8; 4:1-4).

> (6) **Increased labor troubles** (Jas. 5:1-8).

> (7) **Scoffers mocking the doctrine of the Lord's coming** (2 Pet. 3:1-4). Anyone living today can see by all these signs — and many others — that we're in the last days before Christ's return. All other signs will be fulfilled after the rapture.

> (8) **The second gathering of Israel.** The first regathering was after the Babylonian captivity (Ezra, Nehemiah). The nation scattered again in 70 A.D. (Lk. 21:20-24). Isaiah spoke of regathering "the second time" (Isa. 11:11-12; 14:1; 27:12; 43:5; 60:9; 66:20); many other prophets predicted this regathering (Jer. 30:10; 31:8; 32:37; 33:7; 46:27; 50:4; Ezek. 16:53; 20:33-40; 34:11; 36:24; 37:11-19; 39:25; Hos. 2:14; 3:5; Amos 9:13; Mic. 4:6; 5:7; 7:11; Zeph. 2:6; 3:18; 10:6-9; Mal. 3:18; Mt. 24:31).

This second regathering has been happening since 1948 when Israel became a nation again. It will continue only partially until Christ's second advent; then all Israel will be gathered from all lands to be an eternal nation under their Messiah. Angels will bring about the final regathering (Mt. 24:31).

This is really the first and greatest sign we can be definite about. There are many general signs, of course, as listed above. Some might argue we've had wars, earthquakes, famines, pestilences, persecutions, false prophets, and the rest in all past generations. So these alone might be questioned as definite signs.

But the regathering of Israel cannot be disputed as a sign of His soon return to reign on Earth!

## III. "And of the end of the age?"

This "end of the age" is the same "end" mentioned in Mt. 13:37-42, 49-50; 22:13; 24:3; 25:31-46. Both the end of the age and Christ's literal advent were familiar to the disciples, but they knew nothing about the rapture at that time. So they couldn't have been asking about rapture signs, and Christ wouldn't have answered a different question than what they asked.

This third question — about what happens at the age's end — is fully answered in Mt. 24:27–25:46; Mk. 13:24-37; Lk. 21:25-33.

After answering the second question about signs of His coming (Mt. 24:4-26), Christ explains the manner of His coming (Mt. 24:27) and what will happen when He comes (Mt. 24:28-46). Here are the events at His literal coming to Earth:

> 1. Eagles will gather to the carcasses of men slain at Armageddon (Mt. 24:28; Lk. 17:34-37; Rev. 19:17, 21; Ezek. 39:17-21. Compare Job 39:27-30).

> 2. The sun shall be darkened (Mt. 24:29).

> 3. The moon shall not give her light (Mt. 24:29).

> 4. Stars shall fall from Heaven (Mt. 24:29) — referring to a great meteoric shower, not falling planets.

> 5. The powers of Heaven shall be shaken (Mt. 24:29).

> 6. Then shall appear the sign of the Son of man in Heaven (Mt. 24:30).

> 7. Then all Earth's tribes shall mourn (Mt. 24:30).

> 8. Then they shall see the Son of man coming in clouds with power and great glory (Mt. 24:30; 2 Thess. 1:7; Jude 14).

> 9. Then He shall send His angels with a great trumpet sound to gather His elect from Earth's four quarters (Mt. 24:31; Isa. 11:10-15).

> 10. The ungodly will be destroyed (Mt. 24:32-42; 2 Thess. 1:7; Jude 14).

> 11. Unfaithful servants will be judged and punished in eternal Hell (Mt. 24:43-51; 25:14-30; Mt. 13:30, 40-43).

> 12. The nations will be judged (Mt. 25:31-46).

So you can see the Lord fully answered in Mt. 24:27–25:46 the third question about what will happen at the age's end.

## IV. Prophetic Date-Setting (Mt. 24:36–25:46)

These verses definitely state and illustrate that no one will know the day or hour of Christ's second coming to Earth. All we may know is "the times and seasons" that prove the second advent is near (1 Thess. 5:1-3).

To keep His disciples from speculating about the exact timing, Christ compared the days of Noah to the days just before His coming. He stated that people before the flood "knew not until the flood came and took them all away; so shall also the coming of the Son of man be" (Mt. 24:37-39; 1 Thess. 5:1-3; Jude 14).

Certain religious groups have spent much time setting definite dates for Christ's second coming, the rapture, and other prophetic events. These dates are all based on the fallacy that a day in prophecy means a year and vice versa. This has led some to predict the second advent at dates now in the past.

William Miller set one date for October 22, 1843, based on the 2,300 days of Dan. 8:13. His followers put on special ascension robes expecting to be caught up to meet the Lord. Nothing happened.

Instead of giving up, Miller explained he'd miscalculated by a year and reset the date for October 22, 1844. The excitement was even greater the second time. One Advent message declared, "There is no possibility of a mistake this time."

But the second date passed too, and the results were heartbreaking. Many followers drifted into other groups or abandoned faith entirely. Those remaining tried to explain — Elder White even set the date up another year, and still Christ didn't come.

If all Miller's followers had been as honest as he was, the movement might have ended. But Mrs. Ellen G. White proclaimed that God "was in the proclamation of 1843 and His hand hid a mistake in some of the figures." This made it seem God had blundered along with Miller, but it was satisfactorily explained to the faithful. Christ was supposedly examining sins in the heavenly sanctuary.

In 1849, Mrs. White claimed to see the exact hour Christ would come and said His heavenly work was "nearly finished." Later it was revealed He would continue there until the seventh thousand years from Adam. But according to calculations, we're already over 143 years into the seventh thousand years since Adam. Christ supposedly entered the heavenly sanctuary in 1844, but Scripture says He entered there over 1,900 years ago (Heb. 8:1-2; Acts 1:11; 2:35; Rom. 8:34; Eph. 1:19-23; Col. 3:1; Heb. 1:3; 12:2; 1 Pet. 3:22; Rev. 3:21).

Miller himself later wrote: "We expected the second coming of Christ at the time and now to contend that we were not mistaken is dishonest. I have no confidence in any of the new theories that grew out of the movement."

Adventists explain the 2,300 days of Dan. 8:14 as 2,300 years from 457 B.C. through 1844 A.D. They explain the 1,260 days of Rev. 11:1-3; 12:6 as 1,260 years from 538 A.D. to 1798 A.D. But we've already seen that the 1,260 days are literal days relating to the future Tribulation when Antichrist reigns. The beast of Revelation is a man (Rev. 13:18), not the Catholic Church, and will be destroyed at Christ's second coming (Rev. 19:11-21; Dan. 7:11; 2 Thess. 2:7, 8).

The vision of Dan. 8 was to be fulfilled mainly at the time of the end (Dan. 8:17, 23-26). The Grecian Empire wasn't even formed by 457 B.C., much less its four kingdoms. The little horn was to come "in the latter time of their kingdom." The four divisions of Greece didn't exist until about 323-301 B.C. Since the little horn hasn't yet come, the 2,300 days are still future.

The 2,300 days are literally 2,300 evenings and mornings — 1,150 evenings and 1,150 mornings — referring to the morning and evening sacrifices in Jerusalem's temple. So the 2,300 days are only three years, two months, and ten days (using a 360-day year). This is clear because the prophecy concerns "the evening and the morning" (Dan. 8:26) and "the daily sacrifice" (Dan. 8:11-13), offered every morning and evening (Num. 28:3-4; 1 Chron. 16:40).

The 2,300 days refer to sacrifices that will be stopped in the future Jewish temple by Antichrist when he breaks his seven-year covenant with Israel and sets himself up as God in their temple (Dan. 9:27; Mt. 24:15-31; 2 Thess. 2:1-8; Rev. 13:1-18).

The end of these three years, two months, and ten days falls 110 days short of the 1,260-day great tribulation in Daniel's Seventieth Week's last half. This harmonizes perfectly with prophecy that Antichrist loses Jerusalem's control in those final days while warring against northern countries (Dan. 11:44).

It's similar to a military campaign where the general is off fighting distant battles and loses grip on home territory. When Antichrist returns from conquering the north and east, he must retake Jerusalem from the Jews who've come out of hiding in Edom and Moab. They evidently reclaim the city and cleanse the temple of beast worship during those 110 days. Antichrist conquers half of Jerusalem before Christ suddenly appears to defeat the nations and establish His kingdom (Zech. 14:1-21; Rev. 19:11-21).

Pastor Russell was another date-setter. According to him, the Millennium began in 1872 A.D. and "the times of the Gentiles" ended in 1914. Michael Baxter was another — according to him, the seals, trumpets, and vials of Revelation were fulfilled from 1907 to 1931 A.D.

This date-setting practice has been followed by many Bible students, all proven wrong. It's time for people to use better judgment than to set definite dates for specific events.
