**Setting dates is unscriptural.** No one can know when these events will happen until Antichrist comes and makes his seven-year covenant with Israel. Then, and only then, can we know the timeline.

When Antichrist comes, everyone will know only seven years remain until the second advent. They can then know Rev. 6-19 will be fulfilled consecutively from the beginning to the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week, ending with Christ's second advent and the Millennium's beginning.

## V. Rapture or Destruction—Which? (Mt. 24:40-42)

These verses are some of the most misunderstood in Scripture because many interpret them as referring to the rapture instead of the second advent. But it's clear from context that the rapture isn't referenced at all in Mt. 24-25.

So regardless of how much these verses *sound* like the rapture, they don't refer to it. They refer to Christ's literal coming to destroy the ungodly — similar to the flood — as made clear by the word "then."

"Then [at the coming of Christ with the saints to end wickedness as did the flood] shall two be in the field; the one shall be taken and the other left . . . Watch therefore; for ye know not what hour your Lord doth come."

Why take these verses out of their proper context — Christ's coming *with* the saints — and make them refer to His coming *for* the saints? We have plenty of Scriptures proving the rapture without using this one. So why base a doctrine on a passage that doesn't concern it?

If these verses refer to Christ's literal coming, what do they mean? Where are the people who are "taken"?

The answer is in Lk. 17:34-37 — a parallel passage showing both refer to Christ's coming to Earth, not the rapture. Luke's verses conclude a discourse about "the day

lessons.title: Sun-Clothed Woman, Manchild, Dragon, Beast, and False Prophet
lessons.content:
# Sun-Clothed Woman, Manchild, Dragon, Beast, and False Prophet

**(Revelation 12–13)**

## I. The Sun-clothed Woman (Rev. 12:1-6, 13-17)

In these passages, you'll find a complete picture of who this woman is — her identity, her motherhood, the persecution she faces, her flight, and her protection. Let's walk through these subjects together, connecting them with other scriptures as we go.

Now, John saw the symbol of the sun-clothed woman in Heaven, but what she *represents* is actually on Earth. Here's how we know:

> 1. The manchild will be caught up to Heaven at its deliverance — and that couldn't happen if the woman giving birth were already in Heaven (Rev. 12:5).

> 2. The woman will flee into the wilderness, which is on Earth (Rev. 12:6).

> 3. When the dragon is cast down to the Earth, he will persecute the woman (Rev. 12:13).

> 4. The Earth will help the woman by swallowing the flood cast out of the dragon's mouth to devour her. This couldn't be said of her if she were in Heaven (Rev. 12:15, 16).

Here's something important to remember: the woman is a *symbol*, and we need to treat her as such. We have to clearly distinguish between her and what she symbolizes.

Think of it like a flag representing a nation — the cloth isn't the country itself, but it stands for something much bigger. There's always one central truth conveyed by a symbol, and we shouldn't stress the minor details. When details *are* given attention, they must harmonize with the main truth. This eliminates speculation about unexplained parts of the symbol.

We'll see shortly why this woman could not symbolize the Virgin Mary, the Church, or Christendom when we examine the four theories about the manchild in Point II below.

We firmly believe this woman symbolizes **National Israel**. By this term, we mean all the Israelites who will be in Palestine during the Seventieth Week and who will form the nation at that time (Joel 3; Zech. 9:10-16; 12:4-14; 13:8, 9; 14:1-21; Mt. 24:15-26; Dan. 9:27). The Jews in all other lands will remain where they are today and won't be gathered back to their land until the second advent (Isa. 11:11, 12; Ezek. 37:1-28; Mt. 24:31).

Here's why the woman symbolizes National Israel:

> 1. Israel is often mentioned in the Old Testament as a married woman (Isa. 54:1-6; Jer. 3:1-14). The entire book of Hosea shows the marriage of Israel and God, Israel's backsliding in playing the harlot and leaving God, her future humiliation and brokenness in the wilderness, and her return to her husband (Hos. 2:14-23; Rom. 11; Acts 15:13-18).

> 2. The Bible recognizes only three classes of people in the world today: the Church, the Jews, and the Gentiles (1 Cor. 10:32). At the time this woman travails — in the middle of the Week — the Church will have been removed from the Earth, leaving only the last two classes, as proven in Lessons Forty-three and Forty-four.

> 3. If the sun, moon, and twelve stars mean anything, they can only symbolize the same as seen in Joseph's dream in Gen. 37:9-11 — namely, the twelve tribal heads and their parents.

> 4. Israel is the only one dealt with in Scripture that could fulfill what's said about the woman. Let's look at this more closely:

### (1) The Travail of the Woman (Rev. 12:2-5)

In this passage, "travail" is used figuratively, since the woman is a symbol and therefore can't travail literally. Israel's travails have been numerous throughout her history — clearly revealed in her bondages and deliverances, beginning with Israel in Egypt and continuing through the period of the judges and kings, as listed in Lesson Seventeen, Point II.

These travails have continued more or less throughout history and will finally culminate in the worst period of travail she has ever known (Dan. 12:1; Mt. 24:15-26). Passages like Exod. 18:8; Jer. 4:31; 6:24; 13:21; 22:22, 24; and Mic. 4:9, 10 record some of Israel's past travails.

There is to be a future continued travail of Israel in the Tribulation Week with a two-fold result:

> **A.** The deliverance of the manchild in the middle of the Week (Rev. 12:2-5; Jer. 30:6-9; Dan. 12:1; Isa. 66:7, 8). This will result from the terrible anguish and sorrow of Israel in the middle of the Week, because the Antichrist — with whom she has made a seven-years covenant as a guarantee of her protection from the great whore and the ten kings of Revised Rome — will have broken this covenant and determined to completely destroy her. At this time of travail, the manchild is to be delivered because that company of Jews symbolized by the manchild is not to go through the great tribulation, which will immediately follow.

> **B.** The deliverance of the woman herself at the end of the Week when the Antichrist, along with many nations, tries to destroy Israel (Isa. 66:7, 8; Mic. 5:3; Zech. 12:10–14:21; Joel 3:21). The result of this travail will be the conversion of Israel as a nation in one day when Christ comes to defeat the Antichrist, exalt Israel, and set up His kingdom (Rom. 11:26, 27; Rev. 19:11–20:6; 2 Thess. 1:7-10).

### (2) The Persecution, Flight, and Protection of the Woman (Rev. 12:6, 13-16)

The result of the war in Heaven will be the casting out of Satan and his angels to the Earth. "When the dragon saw [actually realized] that he was cast unto the earth, he persecuted the woman, which brought forth the manchild."

The dragon's first move will be to destroy the manchild "as soon as it was born" (Rev. 12:4). Having failed in this because of the catching up of the manchild, he will then turn on the woman who brought forth the manchild and will bitterly persecute her by causing the Antichrist to break his covenant with Israel.

The dragon will give his "power, throne, and great authority" to the Antichrist (Rev. 13:1, 2), and together they will make war on Israel. This persecution of Israel by the Antichrist is often referred to by the prophets (Isa. 10:20-27; 14:1-27; Jer. 30:3-9; Dan. 7:21-27; 8:23-25; 9:27; 11:40-45; Mic. 5:3-15, etc.).

The Jews will realize that the only way of escape is to flee into some country that is not under the control of the Antichrist (Mt. 24:15-24; Dan. 11:40-45). This persecution will therefore cause Israel to flee into the wilderness.

She will be aided by God in her flight, symbolized by the "two wings of an eagle," which will be given her "that she might fly into the wilderness into her place where she is nourished for a time, and times, and half a time from the face of the serpent" (Rev. 12:14. Compare Exod. 19:4; Deut. 32:11, 12).

When the dragon learns that the woman is fleeing, he will cast out of his mouth water "as a flood" to swallow the woman, but the Earth will help her and open its mouth and swallow the flood, thus permitting the woman to escape the dragon.

Now, a symbolic dragon cannot cast out a literal flood, nor can a symbolic flood be swallowed up by a literal Earth. The dragon is symbolic of Satan (Rev. 12:9), the woman of Israel, and the flood of the armies of the dragon and of the Antichrist, who will pursue after the fleeing Israelites to destroy them.

The Earth will open its mouth and swallow these armies as it did the company in Num. 16:29-35. Armies are often symbolized by floods (Jer. 46:7, 8; 47:2, 3; Dan. 9:26, 27; 11:21, 26).

There are many other prophecies in the Bible concerning the persecution, flight, and protection of the woman during the last 3½ years of the Week. Let's consider them briefly:

> **A. In Isaiah.** "Send ye the lamb to the ruler of the land from Sela to the wilderness, unto the mount of the daughter of Zion . . . For it shall be, as a wandering bird cast out of the nest, so the daughters of Moab shall be at the fords of Arnon . . . hide the outcasts; bewray not him that wandereth . . . Let mine outcasts dwell with thee, Moab; be thou a covert to them from the face of the spoiler: for the extortioner is at an end, the spoiler ceaseth, the oppressors are consumed out of the land . . . And in mercy shall the throne be established; and he shall sit upon it in truth in the tabernacle of David, judging, and seeking judgment, and hasting righteousness" (Isa. 16:1-5).

> This is the first prophecy of importance concerning the flight of the woman. It names the very place to which she will flee — the rock-hewn city of Sela, or Petra, as the Romans called it. It's located in Mount Seir, near Mount Hor, in the land of Edom and Moab.

> This prophecy states that the daughters of Moab shall be at the fords of Arnon — a little stream at the north border of Moab — to welcome the Israelites as they flee into this wilderness city and country.

> The city Petra is located in Edom proper, but the prophet speaks of Moab as also being Israel's protector at that time. This country forms part of the great wilderness mentioned so often in Scripture where Israel wandered for forty years.

> Petra was a commercial center in the days of Solomon and was taken by Amaziah, king of Judah (2 Ki. 14:7; 2 Chron. 25:11, 12). It's mentioned several times as "the rock" because Sela means "rock" or "stronghold" (Judg. 1:36; Isa. 42:11; Jer. 48:28; 49:16; Obad. 3). In the first and last reference, the Revised Version translates it "Sela." It's also called "Bozrah" (Amos 1:12; Isa. 34:5, 6; 63:1-5; Jer. 48:24; 49:13, 22). The ancients gave it the name "Bozrah of the rock."

> In 105 A.D. the Romans conquered this country and called it Arabia-Petra. When the Roman Empire's dominion waned, this territory fell back into the hands of the Arabians and was completely lost to the civilized world in the seventh century. Burckhardt rediscovered it in 1812.

> The city of Petra lies in a large valley and is reached only by one narrow passageway, so narrow at places that two people cannot ride abreast. The sides at the top of this passage are so close as to shut out the sunlight.

> The mountains forming a great protecting wall on all sides are from 200 to 1,000 feet high. The sides of this canyon are lined with temples, houses, and tombs, all hewn out of rock with great skill — they look as fresh as if they had been cut yesterday. The temple alone has accommodation for 3,000 people! Tradition says that Paul visited this city when he went into Arabia (Gal. 1:17). (See National Geographic magazine of Feb. 1935 for interesting pictures of this rock-hewn city.)

> Isaiah 26:20, 21 also shows that this will be the place prepared by God to feed Israel for the 1,260 days: "Come, my people Israel, enter thou into thy chambers and shut thy doors about thee: hide thyself as it were for a moment [3½ years] until the indignation [great tribulation] be overpast." The setting of this passage is positively identified as being fulfilled in the last days, for the next verse states, "The Lord cometh out of his place to punish the inhabitants of the earth for their iniquity."

> Isaiah again predicts that Israel will be hiding in Edom when Christ comes: "Who is this that cometh from Edom, with dyed garments from Bozrah? This that is glorious in his apparel, travelling in the greatness of his strength? I that speak in righteousness, mighty to save. . . Wherefore art thou red in thine apparel, and thy garments like him that treadeth in the winefat? . . . I have trodden the winepress alone; and of the people there was none with me: for I will tread them in mine anger, and trample them in my fury; and their blood shall be sprinkled upon my garments, and I will stain all my raiment . . . For the day of vengeance is in mine heart, and the year of my redeemed is come . . . And I looked, and there was none to help; and I wondered that there was none to uphold: therefore mine own arm brought salvation unto me; and my fury, it upheld me . . . And I will tread down the people in mine anger, and make them drunk in mine fury, and I will bring down their strength to the earth" (Isa. 63:1-6).

> Here is a vivid picture of Christ coming to Earth to destroy the nations at Armageddon, which answers the questions asked in the first part of this passage. In His coming to Earth, Christ will pass over Petra, descending from the east (Mt. 24:27), and the Jewish fugitives hiding in Moab and Edom will see Him coming in clouds of Heaven with power and great glory (Mt. 24:15-31).

> Then the words of Isa. 42:11-13 will be fulfilled: "Let the wilderness and the cities lift up their voice . . . let the inhabitants of the rock [Sela] sing, let them shout in the top of the mountains . . . The Lord shall go forth as a mighty man . . . He shall cry like a man of war . . . He shall prevail against His enemies." These passages, with Joel 3; Zech. 9:1–14:21; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Rev. 14:14-20; 19:11-21, picture Armageddon when Christ delivers Israel from the Antichrist.

> **B. In the Psalms.** "Moab is my washpot; over Edom will I cast out my shoe: Philistia triumph thou because of me . . . Who will bring me into the strong city? Who will lead me into Edom? . . . Wilt not thou, O God, which hadst cast us off? And thou, O God, which didst not go out with our armies? . . . Give us help from trouble: for vain is the help of man . . . Through God we shall do valiantly: for He it is that shall tread down our enemies" (Ps. 60:8-12).

> This predicts the preservation of these countries from the Antichrist and God's protection of Israel in her flight from Judea into the wilderness when Antichrist breaks his covenant with her and seizes Palestine (Mt. 24:15-21; Dan. 11:40-45).

> **C. In Ezekiel.** "As I live, saith the Lord God, surely with a mighty hand, and with a stretched out arm, and with fury poured out, will I rule over you: . . . And I will bring you out from the people, and will gather you out of the countries wherein ye are scattered, with a mighty hand, and with a stretched out arm, and with fury poured out . . . And I will bring you into the wilderness of the people, and there will I plead with you face to face. Like as I pleaded with your fathers in the wilderness of the land of Egypt, so will I plead with you, saith the Lord God . . . And I will cause you to pass under the rod, and I will bring you into the bond of the covenant: . . . And I will purge out from among you the rebels, and them that transgress against me: I will bring them forth out of the country where they sojourn, and they shall not enter into the land of Israel: and ye shall know that I am the Lord" (Ezek. 20:33-38).

> This passage records another prophecy that God will bring Israel back from the nations in the last days and then bring her "into the wilderness of the people, and there I will plead with you face to face. Like as I pleaded with your fathers in the wilderness of the land of Egypt."

> God will send the two witnesses with power to do miracles and to bring Israel back to God, literally fulfilling this passage in pleading with Israel like God pleaded with them through the first two prophets — Moses and Aaron — in their first wilderness experience (Rev. 11:3-13; Mal. 4:4-5). At the time of Israel's flight, the two witnesses will appear and do miracles in protecting them from the devil and Antichrist, and they will lead Israel into Petra and bring them back to their God.

> Thus, when Christ appears over Edom in His descent to the Earth, Israel will look upon Him whom they have pierced and will say, "Blessed is he that cometh in the name of the Lord" (Mt. 23:37-39; Rev. 1:7).

> **D. In Daniel.** "And at the time of the end shall the king of the south [Egypt] push at him [Syria] and the king of the north [Syria] shall come against him like a whirlwind with chariots, and with horsemen, and with many ships; and he shall enter into the countries, and shall overflow and pass over . . . He shall enter also into the glorious land, and many [not all] countries shall be overthrown: but these shall escape out of his hand, even Edom, and Moab, and the chiefs of the children of Ammon . . . He shall stretch forth his hand also upon the countries: and the land of Egypt shall not escape" (Dan. 11:36-45).

> This reveals that at the time of Syria's conquests under the Antichrist, "many countries shall be overthrown: but these shall escape out of his hand, even Edom and Moab, and chief of the children of Ammon."

> If Edom and Moab are to escape his hand, this seems to be the only nearby place where the Israelites in Judea can flee during the great tribulation. God will reserve these countries from Antichrist to use them as a place of refuge for His people, showing the "place prepared of God, that they [these countries] should feed her there" for 3½ years. This shows that the feeding is not necessarily supernatural — the inhabitants of the wilderness will no doubt feed Israel.

> **E. In Hosea.** "Therefore, behold, I will allure her, and bring her into the wilderness, and speak comfortably unto her . . . And I will give her vineyards from thence, and the valley of Achor for a door of hope: and she shall sing there, as in the days of her youth, and as in the day when she came up out of the land of Egypt . . . And it shall be at that day, saith the Lord, that thou shalt call me Ishi; and shalt call me no more Baal" (Hos. 2:14-23).

> This speaks of the wilderness to which Israel will flee for protection from the Antichrist, at which time God redeems Israel and marries her forever.

> **F. In Matthew.** "When ye therefore shall see the abomination of desolation, spoken of by Daniel the prophet, stand in the holy place, (whoso readeth, let him understand:). . . Then let them which be in Judea flee into the mountains: . . . let him which is on the housetop not come down to take any thing out of his house: . . . Neither let him which is in the field return back to take his clothes . . . And woe unto them that are with child, and to them that give suck in those days! . . . But pray ye that your flight be not in the winter, neither on the sabbath day: . . . For then shall be great tribulation, such as was not since the beginning of the world to this time no, nor ever shall be . . . and except those days should be shortened, there should no flesh be saved: but for the elect's sake those days shall be shortened" (Mt. 24:15-22).

> Jesus predicted that at the time the abomination of desolation is set up — when the Antichrist breaks his seven-years covenant with Israel, does away with their daily sacrifices to God in their temple, and sets himself and his image in the Holy of Holies to be worshipped during the last half of the Week — the Jews "in Judea" will flee into the mountains and "there shall be great tribulation, such as was not since the beginning of the world to this time, no, nor ever shall be" (Mt. 24:15-22; Dan. 9:27; 2 Thess. 2:1-3; Rev. 13:1-18).

So we have many passages speaking of the persecution, flight, and protection of the woman in the wilderness. They all refer to Israel — not to the Church, Christendom, or the Gentiles, as is clear in each passage.

Where in Scripture do we find even *one* passage that speaks of the Church or any tribe or nation other than Israel going into the wilderness? We don't. Therefore, we conclude that National Israel is the one symbolized by the sun-clothed woman of Rev. 12.

---

## II. The Manchild—Various Interpretations

Let's briefly discuss the various interpretations of the manchild of Rev. 12:5. This is another symbol and must be treated as such. Here are four theories of the manchild and the reasons why they could not possibly be what the symbol means.

### 1. The Manchild as Christ

Some believe the manchild is Christ because He came from Israel according to the flesh (Rom. 9:4, 5), because He will rule the nations with a rod of iron (Rev. 19:15; Ps. 2), because He was translated to God (Eph. 1:20), and because He is called a man (2 Tim. 2:5).

This argument is too general and proves nothing, for *every* Jew came from Israel according to the flesh. On this basis, any son of Israel could be taken as the manchild!

The expression "rod of iron" means authority over the nations, and in this sense *every* raptured saint will have such authority with Christ. Scripture proves this for all classes of redeemed people:

> (1) The Old Testament saints will reign with Christ (Ps. 149:6-9; Dan. 7:18, 27; Mt. 9:11, 12; Jer. 30:9; Ezek. 34:24; 37:24, 25; Hos. 3:5).

> (2) The Church saints will reign with Christ (Mt. 19:28; 20:20-28; Rom. 8:17; 1 Cor. 4:8; 6:2; 2 Tim. 2:12; Rev. 1:5, 6; 2:26, 27).

> (3) The 144,000 Jews will reign with Christ (Rev. 7:1-8; 12:5; 14:1-5; Ps. 149:6-9; Dan. 7:18, 27).

> (4) The tribulation saints and all who have part in the first resurrection will reign with Christ (Rev. 20:4-6).

Since all saints of all ages will reign with Christ, we conclude that the phrase "rod of iron" in Rev. 12:5 doesn't prove that Christ is the manchild any more than it could prove that any one of the above-mentioned four companies of the redeemed is the manchild.

All these saints will not have the same *degree* of rule, nor will it depend on being in a certain company. All will be given authority in proportion to their degree of faithfulness in service here on Earth, determined at the judgment seat of Christ (Lk. 19:11-27; Mt. 16:27; Rom. 14:10; 1 Cor. 3:11-16; 2 Cor. 5:10, 11).

The translation of Christ is no definite proof that Christ is the manchild either. The angel is not showing John the historical ascension of Christ, which he saw with his own eyes about sixty years previously, but a *prophecy* that will be fulfilled in the middle of the Week, as proven in Lesson Forty-one, Point II. All raptured saints will be caught up to God and His throne (1 Thess. 2:19; 3:13; 4:13-18; 5:23; Rev. 3:21; 4:4; 7:9-17; 14:1-5; 19:1-10).

That Christ is called a man doesn't prove He is the manchild, for others are also called men in Scripture. The Church is called a man (Eph. 2:15; 4:13). So all these arguments are too general and cannot be used as proof that Christ — or any other individual or group — is symbolized by the manchild.

This theory destroys the plain consecutive order of the book of Revelation, disregards its natural divisions, and inserts historical events in this plain prophecy. If this theory were true, it would require the fulfillment of all of Rev. 1:1–12:4 *before* the ascension of Christ.

Surely it has been proven incontrovertibly that we are dealing with things in the middle of the Week and that all of Rev. 4-22 takes place after the Church Age. The plain statements concerning the woman and manchild could not possibly be harmonized with the events in the lives of Christ and the Virgin Mary.

Therefore, we conclude that this theory is out of harmony with the intended thought in the "prophecy" and has no part in the "things which must shortly come to pass."

### 2. The Manchild as the True Church

> **(1)** Some believe the Church is the manchild, and professing Christians are the woman. They teach that the Church will be caught up out of the mass of professing Christians who will be left here to go into the wilderness, because the majority in Christendom won't be in the Church and therefore won't be ready to go up in the rapture.

> This argument is no proof that the Church is symbolized by the manchild and the mass of professing Christians. There is no statement or indication to this effect in Scripture. Neither does such an argument prove that the Church is caught up in the middle of the Week. We have already proven in Lessons Forty-three and Forty-four that the Church will be raptured before the Week in Rev. 4:1. This being true, the Church cannot be caught up in the middle of the Week as the manchild will be. This theory, like the one about Christ, is out of harmony with plain revelation concerning the woman and manchild.

> **(2)** It is further believed that the sun clothing the woman means the righteousness of Christ, that the moon symbolizes the Jewish ordinances superseded by Christ's teachings, and that the twelve stars in her crown represent the twelve apostles.

> This too is without scriptural foundation and actually contradicts their own arguments in Point 2, (1) above. The woman cannot be the mass of professing Christians who are not ready to meet Christ or go up in the rapture *and still* be clothed with the righteousness of Christ.

> If professing Christians were clothed with the righteousness of Christ, they would *be* Christ's, and if they were His, they would go in the rapture along with all other Christians (1 Cor. 15:20-23; 1 Thess. 4:16). Instead of clothing professing Christians with His righteousness, Christ condemns such to eternal damnation (Mt. 23; Rev. 3:14-17; Gal. 5:19-21; 6:7-8; Rom. 8:12-13).

> On the same grounds, the twelve stars cannot represent the twelve apostles, for they are the foundation of the true Church and are not in the crowd of backslidden professing Christians (Eph. 2:19-22). Such a theory would have us believe that Christ and the apostles sanctioned hypocrisy and mere profession of the gospel — but they never did.

> God will never protect a body of professing Christians who trample His Word under their feet any more than He will protect others who admit they are sinners. It is absurd to believe He would do this while allowing godly tribulation saints to suffer martyrdom without such protection and care.

> However, in the case of Israel, God has sworn to protect her in the same manner that He is to protect this woman. God, to fulfill His covenants with their fathers, is obligated to protect Israel and bring her back to Himself. The primary purpose of the Tribulation is to accomplish this.

> **(3)** It is argued that Christendom will travail in the middle of the Week because of the persecution of Christians by the Antichrist. This argument cannot be used of Christians, for in no passage do we find any statement that Christendom will travail in the middle of the Week or at any other time.

> If the woman symbolizes the mass of professing Christians, she does not have real salvation, or she would be raptured. People must have real salvation if they undergo any serious persecution. This need not be the case with Israel, for the Jews are to be persecuted in fulfillment of prophecy. They cannot escape persecution by denying some faith as will be possible with Christians during the Seventieth Week — they will be persecuted because of *nationality* regardless of their religion.

> **(4)** Some think the manchild is the Church because the seventh trumpet (Rev. 11:14–13:18), according to their belief, is the same as the "last trump" blown at the rapture of the Church (1 Cor. 15:51-58; 1 Thess. 4:13-18).

> This association of the seventh trumpet with the "trump of God" does not prove the Church is the manchild. There are many points of contrast between these trumpets as has been proven in Lesson Forty-three, Point I, 2. Even after this seventh — or so-called "last trump" — takes place, there will be others sounded in the Tribulation (Isa. 27:12, 13; Zeph. 1:16-18; Zech. 9:14, 15; Mt. 24:31).

> In conclusion, it is clear that the Church cannot be symbolized by the manchild, nor Christendom by the woman.

### 3. The Bride Part of the Church as the Manchild

This theory teaches that the manchild represents the bride part of the Church, and that the bride of Christ is not the Church but merely a select company of believers — known as full overcomers — who are outside of the Church. This theory resolves itself into two questions:

> **(1) Are the body of Christ and the Church the same?** According to this theory, they are not. However, three definite passages prove they *are* the same (Eph. 1:22, 23; Col. 1:18, 24). These passages state that the body of Christ and the Church are the same. There is no plain passage teaching a difference between the Church and a bride part of the Church. No Scripture ever mentions a difference between the body of Christ and the Church.

> **(2) Are there two classes of people in the Church — partial overcomers as the Church and full overcomers as the bride part of the Church?** This theory teaches there are two classes and attempts to create within the Church two groups of believers — those who partially live in sin and those who do not, those who partially overcome and those who fully overcome.

> This class of interpreters uses the seven promises made to the overcomer in Rev. 2 and 3, claiming that full overcomers form the bride who will receive rewards and reign with Christ, while partial overcomers are to be finally saved but have no part in Christ's reign.

> Let's examine the New Testament books to determine whether there are two groups in the Church or not.

> **A.** Paul, in writing to the Corinthians, leaves no doubt as to whether they form part of the body of Christ, saying, "For as the body is one [not two parts] and hath many members, and all the members of the one body, being many, are one body . . . by one Spirit are we all baptized into one body."

> If the body is not one, then we might say that the Spirit is not one. If we can misconstrue such plain language and divide the one body, we can also divide the one Spirit on the same grounds, for the same terms are used of both.

> Paul continues, "Now ye are

lessons.title: The Beasts Out of the Sea and Earth
lessons.content:
# The Beasts Out of the Sea and Earth

**(Rev. 13:1-18)**

Revelation 13 is a prophecy about two men who will fulfill everything predicted here during the last 3½ years of this age. Let's dig into what Scripture reveals about both of them.

## I. The Beast Out of the Sea (Rev. 13:1-10, 18)

First, let's examine what this passage tells us about the beast out of the sea. This is symbolic language, and we need to treat it that way.

The sea represents peoples (Dan. 7:2, 3; Rev. 17:1, 15). The beast in Revelation points to the rise of a kingdom — and more specifically, to the Antichrist, the earthly head of that kingdom (Rev. 13:18). It also symbolizes a supernatural spirit out of the Abyss, as we'll see later.

Throughout Scripture, beasts symbolize kingdoms and kings (Dan. 2:38, 39; 7:2-7 with 7:17, 23), as well as the supernatural powers controlling those kingdoms. This passage covers the personal Antichrist — his power, where that power comes from, his worship, characteristics, mouth, titles, wars, exaltation, reign, length of reign, and more. Let's walk through these briefly:

### 1. Who is the Antichrist?

At the present time (1949), this question simply cannot be answered. It will remain unanswerable until the Antichrist personally makes the covenant with Israel for seven years (Dan. 9:27).

Many people today, as always, speculate about whether the pope, Stalin, or others might be the Antichrist. This kind of speculation has done tremendous harm to the study of prophecy — many thinking people have become disgusted by it. Plenty of folks have turned their backs on prophetic Scripture because of unfounded guesses about Hitler, Mussolini, or other men who never became the Antichrist.

Here's the bottom line: no man now prominent in world affairs could possibly be the Antichrist. The points below make this clear.

### 2. From where does he come?

This question is fully answered in our study of Daniel (see Lesson Thirty-nine, Point IV, 2).

### 3. When is he to be revealed?

When will the Antichrist step into prominence in world affairs? Scripture answers this clearly:

> **(1)** In Dan. 7:24, we have definite proof that the Antichrist cannot be revealed or prominent until after ten kingdoms are formed inside the Roman Empire. According to this verse, those ten kingdoms must first exist for some time as the seventh kingdom, or Revised Rome.
>
> The Antichrist will arise and gain control of all ten kingdoms during the first 3½ years of the Week. By the middle of the Week, he'll be seen as the beast of Rev. 13 coming up out of the sea of humanity — already possessing the seven heads and ten horns he conquered before the Week's midpoint.
>
> His emergence "out of the sea" at mid-Week simply represents the recognition of his power by the ten kingdoms and their acceptance of him, granted by the ten kings and the dragon (Rev. 13:2-4; 17:12-17). This verse also teaches that because he rises from within the ten kingdoms, he comes out of obscurity and his rise to power will be rapid. Daniel saw the "little horn" rising so suddenly among the ten that he was bewildered (Dan. 7:7-8, 19-24). So no one can identify the Antichrist until after those ten kingdoms are formed.

> **(2)** The Antichrist also cannot be revealed until after the rapture, as proven in 2 Thess. 2:6-8 (see Lesson Forty-three, Point VII, 9).

### 4. How long is he to reign?

He'll reign over one of the ten kingdoms of Revised Rome at the start of the Week. During the last 3½ years, he'll gain power over all ten kingdoms (Rev. 13:5; 7:25; 12:7).

It's in those final 3½ years that he'll exalt himself above every god and be worshipped by many of his subjects (Rev. 13:14-18; Dan. 8:25; 11:36-45; 2 Thess. 2:4).

### 5. Where is he to reign?

During part of the last 3½ years, the Antichrist will reign in Jerusalem "in the glorious holy mountain" where the temple will be rebuilt (Dan. 11:45). He'll sit "in the temple of God, showing himself that he is God" (2 Thess. 2:4).

This temple is where the abomination of desolation will be placed (Dan. 9:27; 12:7-13; Mt. 24:15-22; Rev. 11:1, 2; 13:12-18). Until then, Babylon — not Rome — will be his place of reign (as we saw in Lesson Thirty-nine, IV, 2, (6)).

The fact that there will be ten separate kingdoms with ten separate capitals and ten separate kings during the first 3½ years shows that up to mid-Week, the Antichrist doesn't have one central capital. The ten kingdoms won't be under him yet. Rome will be just one of those ten capitals, with her king ruling only over Italy and her possessions — not over all of Revised Rome.

Only when the Antichrist becomes head of the ten kingdoms at mid-Week will he establish one central throne in Jerusalem for the entire newly formed empire. Even then, the kings will continue as kings under him (Rev. 17:9-17).

### 6. The power of the Antichrist

The Antichrist's power will come from Satan, the spirit of the Abyss, and the ten kings. God has already predicted this power, and it will be given to him at the proper time.

Think of it like a CEO who receives authority from multiple sources — a board of directors, investors, and executive teams. In the Antichrist's case, God will permit Satan and his agents to give their power to the beast and inspire his evil designs (Dan. 8:24; 2 Thess. 2:8-12; Rev. 13:1, 2). God will put it into the hearts of the ten kings to give him their power for the purpose of destroying Mystical Babylon (Rev. 17:12-17). The satanic prince out of the Abyss (Rev. 11:7; 17:8) will be the executor of Satan's power to the beast, inspiring and backing the Antichrist in all his diabolical activities (more on this in the next two lessons).

Satan will give the Antichrist the world-kingdoms he once offered Christ. The Antichrist will accept what Christ refused. But the Antichrist will still have to fight to possess them — just as Christ would have had to do and will yet do.

The Antichrist will succeed in this world conquest by conquering the Revised Roman Empire by mid-Week, and all the northern and eastern countries of Asia and Europe by the Week's end. He'll also gain cooperation from many other nations through the ministry of three unclean spirits, who will help him against the Jews and Christ at the second advent.

After his defeat at Armageddon by Christ, the Antichrist will be cast into the Lake of Fire. The kingdom of God will succeed his kingdom and extend throughout all the Earth.

Here's a summary of the beast's power:

> **(1)** To blaspheme God (Dan. 7:8, 11, 20, 25; 11:36; Rev. 13:5, 6)
>
> **(2)** To overcome saints (Rev. 7:9-17; 14:13; 15:2-4)
>
> **(3)** To overcome the Jews (Dan. 7:21; 12:7; Rev. 13:7, 15)
>
> **(4)** To conquer many nations (Dan. 7:8, 20-24; 11:36-45; Ezek. 38; 39) and rule them as he wills (Rev. 13:7)
>
> **(5)** To destroy Mystery Babylon (Rev. 17:12-17)
>
> **(6)** To overcome and kill the two witnesses (Rev. 11:7)
>
> **(7)** To change times and laws (Dan. 7:25)
>
> **(8)** To understand mysteries (Dan. 8:23)
>
> **(9)** To protect the Jews as long as he desires, and also to succeed in destroying them for a period (Dan. 9:27; 2 Thess. 2:4; Rev. 11:1-2)
>
> **(10)** To work signs and wonders (Dan. 8:24; 2 Thess. 2:8-12; Rev. 13:1-18; 19:20)
>
> **(11)** To cause craft to prosper (Dan. 8:25)
>
> **(12)** To control money and riches in his own realm (Dan. 11:38-43)
>
> **(13)** To cause great deceptions (2 Thess. 2:10-12; Jn. 5:43; Dan. 8:25; Rev. 13:1-18)
>
> **(14)** To do according to his own will (Dan. 11:36)
>
> **(15)** To control religion and worship (Dan. 11:36; 2 Thess. 2:4; Rev. 13:1-18; 14:9-11; 16:21)
>
> **(16)** To control the lives of all men in his realm (Rev. 13:12-18)
>
> **(17)** To control kings as he wills (Rev. 17:12-17)
>
> **(18)** To make all other nations fear him (Rev. 13:4)
>
> **(19)** To fight against Christ (Rev. 19:11-21; Dan. 8:25)
>
> **(20)** To reign forty-two months (Rev. 13:5)

### 7. The Titles of the Antichrist

> **(1) "Antichrist."** This is the most common title we use when speaking of him, since he'll be the great opponent of Christ at the end of the age. The word appears only four times in the Bible (1 Jn. 2:18, 22; 4:3; 2 Jn. 7), but our studies show he's the one who bears that title more than any other and is expressly stated to come according to these passages.

> **(2) "The Assyrian"** (Isa. 10:20-27; 30:18-33; 31:4–32:20; Mic. 5:3-15). The prophecies in these passages were recorded against the Assyrian king in the prophets' days, but a careful study reveals they have a latter-day fulfillment in the future Assyrian king who will oppress Israel just before her final restoration. (The Assyrian territory will be part of the Antichrist's kingdom, so in that sense he is the king of Assyria.)
>
> The first passage (Isa. 10:20-27) refers to the "remnant" of Rev. 12:17: "In that day the remnant of Israel . . . shall no more again stay [Hebrew, 'look for support'] upon him [Antichrist] that smote them; but shall stay upon the Lord. . . . O my people that dwellest in Zion, be not afraid of the Assyrian: he shall smite thee with a rod . . . yet a little while [1,260 days, Rev. 12:6, 14-17; 13:5], and the indignation [Hebrew, 'God's anger and wrath,' the day of vengeance in the Tribulation, as in Isa. 26:20; Dan. 8:19; 11:36] shall cease, and mine anger in their destruction . . . in that day his burden shall be taken away from off thy shoulder . . . the yoke shall be destroyed because of the anointing." The Hebrew root for "anointing" is *shawman*, "to shine," and no doubt refers to the brightness of Christ's coming in 2 Thess. 2:8, 9.
>
> The second passage (Isa. 30:18-33) clearly refers to Israel's final restoration under the Messiah: "Therefore will he [the Lord] be exalted . . . the people shall dwell in Zion at Jerusalem: thou shalt weep no more . . . in the day that the Lord bindeth the breach of his people . . . the name of the Lord cometh from far, burning with his anger . . . to sift the nations . . . with the flame of a burning fire, with scattering, and tempest, and hailstones. For through the voice of the Lord shall the Assyrian be beaten down." (See also Ezek. 38:17-21; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:8-12.)
>
> The third passage (Isa. 31:4–32:20) speaks of the same truth: "Like as the lion roaring on his prey . . . shall the Lord of hosts come down to fight for mount Zion . . . As birds flying so will the Lord of hosts defend Jerusalem; defending also he will deliver it; and passing over he will preserve it . . . For in that day every man shall cast away his idols . . . then shall the Assyrian fall with the sword, not of a mighty man [but by Christ, 2 Thess. 2:8, 9] . . . a king shall reign in righteousness . . . And my people shall dwell in a peaceable habitation, and in sure dwellings, and in quiet resting places."
>
> The last passage (Mic. 5:3-15) definitely speaks of Israel being given up "until the time that she which travaileth hath brought forth [until Israel has brought forth the manchild, as we saw in the previous lesson]: then the remnant of his brethren shall return unto the children of Israel. And he [Christ, verses 1, 2] shall stand and feed in the strength of the Lord . . . now shall he be great unto the ends of the earth. And this man shall be the peace, when the Assyrian [Antichrist] shall come into our land: and shall tread in our palaces . . . thus shall he deliver us from the Assyrian . . . I will execute vengeance in anger and fury upon the heathen, such as they have not heard." (See also Dan. 9:27; 11:40-45; 2 Thess. 2:3-4; Rev. 13.)

> **(3) "The king of Babylon"** (Isa. 14:4). This passage is in a prophecy of Babylon that had partial fulfillment in Babylon's overthrow by the Medes and Persians (Isa. 13:17). The complete fulfillment will come in the last days under the Antichrist, as proven by the mention of "the day of the Lord" and Israel's restoration, which will occur when Christ comes to Earth during the Antichrist's reign (Isa. 13:6-16, 19-22; 14:1-8, 18-27). All these prophecies have never been fulfilled as stated here. The Antichrist will be the king of Babylon because he'll be the king of Assyria, which will include Babylon (see Lesson Forty-one on Babylon).

> **(4) "The spoiler" and "the extortioner"** (Isa. 16:1-5). That these terms refer to the Antichrist is clear from studying this passage in Lesson Forty-five, Point I.

> **(5) "Gog, the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal"** (Ezek. 38; 39). These two chapters will be fulfilled at Armageddon (see Lesson Forty-nine).

> **(6) The "little horn"** (Dan. 7:8, 24; 8:9, 23).

> **(7) "A king of fierce countenance"** (Dan. 8:23).

> **(8) "The prince that shall come"** (Dan. 9:26, 27). This title refers to the same man as the "little horn" coming from the ten kingdoms of Revised Rome who will make the seven-year covenant with Israel and then break it mid-Week, causing the abomination of desolation in the Jewish temple at Jerusalem (see Lesson Forty).

> **(9) "The king of the north"** (Dan. 11:36-45). This is the king of the Syrian division of the old Grecian Empire (see our study of Daniel in Lesson Thirty-nine). He's called the king of the north because he'll come from the northern division of old Greece — that is, north of Palestine.
>
> Many Bible teachers say the Antichrist will come from Russia and use this term to prove it. But if "the king of the north" refers to Russia, then what countries are there north of Russia that could fight against Russia, as required in Dan. 11:44? There are none! So this title applies to the future king of Syria — the northern division of the four divisions of the old Grecian Empire from which the Antichrist must come (Dan. 8:8, 9, 20-25).

> **(10) "The man of sin"** (2 Thess. 2:1-12).

> **(11) "The son of perdition"** (2 Thess. 2:1-12).

> **(12) "The wicked" and "that wicked"** (Isa. 11:4; 2 Thess. 2:1-12).

These last four titles picture the Antichrist in his role as the most sinful and wicked man of his time — perhaps of all time. He'll literally murder multitudes who won't conform to his every desire (Rev. 7:9-17; 13:16-18; 15:1-3; 20:4-6). For this wickedness, he's "the son of perdition" because he's destined for perdition — destruction and eternal Hell.

Now, some teach that the Antichrist is the "mystery of iniquity," or Satan manifest in the flesh just as Jesus was the mystery of godliness (God manifest in the flesh). They claim the Antichrist will be "the son of perdition" — a literal son of Satan by a woman, just as Jesus was "the Son of God" by a woman. They argue the Antichrist is Christ's opposite in every detail.

But this is not taught in Scripture.

The Antichrist is a mysterious figure, and he'll accomplish astonishing things. But not one statement in Scripture about him is truly mysterious or teaches that he'll be supernatural, an immortal man from the Abyss, an incarnation of the devil, or a natural son of the devil. We'll see this clearly in the next two lessons.

The phrase "mystery of iniquity" literally means "the invisible spirit of lawlessness" or "the evil spirit forces that cause man to sin" (Jn. 8:44; 14:30; Eph. 2:1-3; 1 Jn. 3:8; Eph. 6:10-18; 2 Cor. 4:3, 4).

Some who teach that the Antichrist is the mystery of iniquity also claim he's the beast now bound in the Abyss who will come out again as the Antichrist. They teach this spirit is Judas who will be reincarnated, with their main argument being that both Judas and the Antichrist are called "the son of perdition" (Jn. 17:12; 2 Thess. 2:14).

But as we'll see in the next two lessons, no human being has ever gone into the Abyss, nor will one ever go there — so Judas couldn't be in the pit to come out. The expression "the son of perdition" literally means "the son of destruction" because both Judas and the Antichrist are destined for destruction — not because they're natural sons of Satan.

They couldn't be sons of Satan and sons of their earthly fathers at the same time. And they couldn't be natural sons of Satan and incarnations of him too, as some teach.

In the Greek, it reads "the son of the destruction" just as it reads "the man of the sin." This last phrase doesn't limit the Antichrist to being the only man of sin, and the former phrase doesn't limit him to being the only son of destruction.

It's similar to how the Hebrews and Greeks called anyone subject to a particular trait the "son" or "child" of that trait. We see this throughout Scripture:

- "sons of Belial" (1 Sam. 1:16; 2:12; 25:17, 25; 1 Ki. 21:10)
- "child of the devil" (Acts 13:10)
- "children of the wicked one" (Mt. 13:38)
- "children of the devil" (1 Jn. 3:10)
- "children of wisdom" (Lk. 7:35)
- "children of the world" (Lk. 16:8)
- "children of light" (Lk. 16:8; Jn. 12:36)
- "children of disobedience" (Eph. 2:1-3; 5:6-8; Col. 3:6)

Anyone destined for a particular fate was called the child of that destiny:

- "children of the kingdom" (Mt. 8:12)
- "children of wrath" (Eph. 2:1-3)
- "children of the resurrection" (Lk. 20:36)

Given this common usage, it's only natural to call both Judas and the Antichrist "the son of perdition" or destruction — both are destined for destruction in Hell because of their sin.

The word "perdition" appears only eight times and comes from the Greek *apoleia*, meaning "ruin, loss, destruction, perdition, and perish." It's never used as a name for the devil. So calling Judas and the Antichrist "sons of the devil by a woman" is simply not biblical.

Scripture never states that Judas ever was or will be, nor that the future Antichrist ever will be, a direct and literal child of the devil by a woman. Such a statement is as far from truth as the devil himself!

Try substituting "devil" for "perdition" everywhere else it's found, and see if it makes sense (Phil. 1:28; 1 Tim. 6:9; Heb. 10:39; 2 Pet. 3:7; Rev. 17:8, 11). The Greek word *apoleia* is translated "destruction" (Mt. 7:13; Rom. 9:22; Phil. 3:19; 2 Pet. 2:1; 3:16), "damnation" (2 Pet. 2:3), "die" (Acts 25:16), "perish" (Acts 8:20), and other ways — but never as "devil."

We can conclude that "son of perdition" does not mean "son of the devil."

All other statements about the Antichrist — "coming in his own name" (Jn. 5:43), "exalting himself" (2 Thess. 2:4), "being worshipped" (Rev. 13:8), "being cast into Hell" (Rev. 19:20), "doing his own will" (Dan. 11:36), "destroying men" (Dan. 8:24), "being wicked" (2 Thess. 2:3-8), and other facts — don't prove he's some super-mysterious being.

All these statements can be understood in connection with any natural, mortal man, as we'll see. If the Antichrist is the mystery of lawlessness, then he's been here all along and can't come from the pit — Paul said this mystery was already at work in his day (2 Thess. 2:7).

Too many people spend their lives trying to make the Bible a mystery instead of seeking to understand the simple book it really is. All such hidden interpretations must be rejected for the sake of simple truth.

The devil is never going to have a natural son by a woman. Genesis 3:15 certainly doesn't teach this. The seed of the serpent should be understood as the natural offspring of snakes and the spiritual children of the devil (Mt. 13:38; 1 Jn. 3:8-10; Jn. 8:44).

Some take this last passage to mean the Antichrist will be a natural seed of the devil: "Ye are of your father the devil . . . When he speaketh a lie, he speaketh of his own; for he is a liar, and the father of it." They claim "it" refers to one particular son of the devil — the Antichrist.

But this same passage speaks of all those men as being "of your father the devil." Plus, the "lie" refers to an actual lie, not a natural son of the devil by a woman!

If telling a lie can be turned into a natural son of the devil here, then we could make natural sons of Satan in Acts 5:3, Rom. 1:25, Ps. 78:36, and everywhere else in Scripture where lies were spoken. If the "lie" here means a natural son the devil will have by a woman, then he'll "speak" this son into existence — which means he couldn't be a natural son by a woman or by incarnation, as some teach.

If Satan could do this, he'd be speaking enough sons into existence to fill the Earth for a better chance to defeat God. Such teaching is plain foolishness.

Some also argue that Judas was the only one ever called a "devil," proving he was the devil incarnate, the mystery of iniquity, and a son of perdition (Jn. 6:70, 71; 17:12). They point to the definite article "the" making Judas "the devil."

But the definite article isn't in the Greek at all — it reads "a devil." The Greek word for "devil" is *diabolos*, meaning "adversary" or "slanderer." It's used of other people who are called "false accusers" (2 Tim. 3:3; Titus 2:3) and "slanderers" (1 Tim. 3:11).

Since the word is used of other people, Judas clearly isn't the only one it applies to. The word never implies incarnation. If it did, then these other men who are slanderers (devils) were also incarnations of the devil — which destroys the whole theory.

The devil never incarnates himself in the Antichrist any more than he did in Judas. The dragon is always seen as a separate person outside the beast.

If Judas were the devil incarnate, then the devil was the son of Simon, a human being — so he couldn't have been created by God and couldn't have existed until Christ's time (Jn. 6:70, 71).

According to this reasoning, it was the devil who:

- Betrayed Jesus (Mt. 10:4)
- Jesus chose as one of His own trusted disciples and planned to give him a throne in the eternal kingdom (Mt. 10:1-8; Lk. 22:28-30)
- Received power from the Holy Spirit through Jesus to cast out himself and his demons and destroy his own work (Mt. 6:7-13)
- Followed Jesus and had fellowship with Him for over three years (Ps. 41:9; 55:12, 13)
- Had a place in the bishopric and fell from it by transgression (Acts 1:15-25; 109:8)
- Lost his name out of the Book of Life (Acts 1:20 with Ps. 69:25-28)
- Entered into himself to betray Jesus (Lk. 22:3)
- Carried the bag and was the trusted treasurer of the apostolic band (Jn. 13:29)
- Repented himself for betraying Jesus (Mt. 27:3-10)
- Hung himself (Mt. 27:5)
- Had his bowels gush out and was buried in a potter's field (Acts 1:17-20)

Who could believe these things happened to the devil?

If Judas were the devil incarnate, then it was Judas who:

- Was a created being and angel that ruled Earth before Adam and invaded Heaven and was cast out (Isa. 14:12-14; Ezek. 28:11-17; Lk. 10:18)
- Opposed Israel and smote Job with boils and has present access to Heaven (2 Chron. 21:1; Job 1:6–2:7)
- Stood at his own right hand to betray Jesus and became childless ever afterward (Ps. 109:20)
- Tempted Christ (Mt. 4:1-11)
- Used Peter as a tool (Mt. 16:23)
- Caused all the sickness in men (Acts 10:38; Lk. 13:16)
- Was still alive and worked against early believers after committing suicide (Acts 5:3; 26:18)
- Does all the things the devil does throughout Scripture

Check the words "Satan" and "devil" in a concordance and see all the things Judas (supposedly) did to oppose God from creation until now — if he's the devil.

If the Antichrist were the devil incarnate, we'd have to conclude:

- That the devil hasn't come yet (1 Jn. 2:18; Jn. 5:43)
- That he won't come until after ten kingdoms form inside the Roman Empire (Dan. 7:24) and after the Church is raptured (2 Thess. 2:7, 8)
- That he'll only continue forty-two months when he does come (Rev. 13:5)
- That the devil is a "man" (Rev. 13:18)
- That this man is in Heaven now accusing the saints and will be cast out of Heaven mid-Week (Rev. 12:7-17; 13:1-8)
- That the dragon isn't a separate person outside the beast as he's pictured in all relevant passages (Rev. 13:2-4; 16:13-16; 19:20; 20:10)
- That the devil will be "slain" by Christ at His second advent (Dan. 7:11; Isa. 11:4; 2 Thess. 2:8, 9)
- That he'll be put in two different places during the Millennium — the beast in the Lake of Fire and the dragon in the Abyss (Rev. 19:20; 20:1-3)
- That the devil is still in the Lake of Fire while he's loosed at the Millennium's end and will be cast back where he already is and has been since Armageddon (Rev. 19:20; 20:1-10)
- That the devil has died once and will die twice more if we believe these theories

We'd also have to believe that Judas became his own father when he incarnated himself in a woman and was born, that he died and will incarnate again in a woman in the latter days as a second incarnation of the devil — or of himself.

How could anyone become nothing but a seed in a woman twice and grow from nothing but a seed to a full-grown man twice? How could the devil as an immortal angel be a man and die three times? How could he be the devil outside both Judas and the Antichrist while being an incarnation of the devil in both?

How could the devil enter into Judas if he were Judas? How could he give his power to the Antichrist and be a dragon outside him while still being the Antichrist? How could he be the beast in the Lake of Fire and still be himself in the pit?

These and other ridiculous theories we'd have to believe if we accepted what some teach about the Antichrist being both Judas and the devil. These teachings aren't the truth about the devil, Judas, or the Antichrist — or about the head wounded to death, as we'll see in the next two lessons.

> **(13) "The beast"** (Dan. 7:11; Rev. 13:1-18; 14:9-11; 15:2, 3; 16:2, 10; 17:1-18; 19:19-21; 20:2-4, 10). We'll explain this beast in the next two lessons.

### 8. His Person

All our studies prove that the Antichrist will be a real person — not a religious system or the successive head of some system like the pope. He's yet to come and will literally fulfill all the prophecies concerning himself.

His character and characteristics are clearly implied in the points above. He'll be a man possessing the talent and leadership of all previously gifted conquerors and leaders. Beyond these natural gifts, he'll possess miraculous power

lessons.title: The Beast with Seven Heads and Ten Horns
lessons.content:
# The Beast with Seven Heads and Ten Horns

**(Rev. 17:1, 3, 7-17)**

The symbol of the beast shows up repeatedly throughout Revelation — from chapters eleven through twenty — but we get the fullest explanation in chapters thirteen and seventeen. When you piece together all these descriptions, you get a complete picture of the beast with its seven heads and ten horns.

The Greek word *therion*, translated "beast," appears thirty-seven times in Revelation in connection with this symbol. It refers to a wild, untamed, dangerous animal. Think of it like this: if you were choosing an image to represent the hostile powers that will stand against Christ in the last days, a ferocious predator would be the perfect choice.

## I. Sixty Fundamental and Prophetic Facts

Revelation gives us sixty historical and prophetic facts about the beast. These serve as our foundation — without them, we can't truly understand the future beast with its seven heads and ten horns.

Any interpretation of this subject must line up with these facts, or it's built on shaky ground. The historical details help us grasp the prophetic ones, showing how the last Gentile kingdom will be reconstructed during "the times of the Gentiles" under the Antichrist's direction.

Here's the key purpose of this vision: it's not meant to teach us history for history's sake. It's meant to help us identify the eighth and final kingdom that will form after the rapture.

We shouldn't interpret anything in Rev. 4-22 as already fulfilled in past history — all these events are still future, coming after the rapture, as we established in Lesson Forty-one. But here's what's perfectly legitimate: prophecy can point to a future event while also giving us a look back at what led up to it.

So to help us properly identify the eighth kingdom of the future, Scripture mentions the seven kingdoms that precede it. This shows us that the final kingdom will reign over all the peoples and territories of the ones that came before.

Here are the sixty factual statements about the beast found in Rev. 11:7–20:10:

> 1. The beast is to come out of the bottomless pit (Rev. 11:7; 17:8).

> 2. He will make war on the two witnesses during the last part of the Week (Rev. 11:7).

> 3. He will finally overcome and kill the two witnesses (Rev. 11:7).

> 4. He will come from the sea of humanity (Rev. 13:1; 17:1, 3, 11, 15).

> 5. He has seven heads and names of blasphemy written on them (Rev. 13:1; 17:3, 7-11).

> 6. He has ten horns and on them ten crowns (Rev. 13:1. Compare Rev. 12:3; 17:12-17; Dan. 7:7, 8, 19-25).

> 7. He is like a leopard, a symbol of Greece (Rev. 13:2; 17:3. Compare Dan. 7:6).

> 8. He has the feet of a bear, a symbol of Medo-Persia (Rev. 13:2. Compare Dan. 7:5).

> 9. He has the mouth of a lion, a symbol of Babylon (Rev. 13:2. Compare Dan. 7:4).

> 10. The dragon gives him his power, his seat (throne), and great authority (Rev. 13:2. Compare Dan. 8:24; 2 Thess. 2:7-12).

> 11. One of the beast's heads was wounded to death and it will be healed (Rev. 13:3, 12, 14).

> 12. All the world will wonder after him (Rev. 13:3; 17:8).

> 13. Many will worship the dragon, which will give power to the beast. Also they will worship the beast, saying, "Who is like unto the beast? Who is able to make war with him?" (Rev. 13:4, 8, 16-18; 14:9-11; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

> 14. The beast will be given a mouth to speak great things and blasphemies (Rev. 13:5. Compare this passage with Dan. 7:8, 11, 20; 11:36).

> 15. He will be given power for forty-two months (Rev. 13:5).

> 16. He will blaspheme God, His name, His tabernacle, and those that dwell in Heaven (Rev. 13:6. Compare this passage with Dan. 7:8, 11, 20; 11:36).

> 17. He will make war on the saints (Rev. 13:7. Compare this passage with Dan. 7:19-22).

> 18. He will overcome the saints and kill them (Rev. 13:7; 14:9-11; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

> 19. He will be given power over all the people of the ten kingdoms (Rev. 13:1, 7; 17:12-17. Compare these passages with Dan. 7:23, 24).

> 20. Saints are commanded to have patience and faith and not to repay him evil for evil (Rev. 13:10).

> 21. He will have a co-worker and religious leader in the false prophet who will exercise his power and cause men to worship him (Rev. 13:11-18; 16:13-16; 19:20; 20:10).

> 22. Miracles will be done in his sight to get the world to worship him (Rev. 13:12-14).

> 23. An image will be made of him to be worshiped (Rev. 13:14, 15).

> 24. His image will be given life to speak and cause men to be killed if they refuse to worship the beast and his image (Rev. 13:15).

> 25. He will have a name, which at present is not known (Rev. 13:17, 18; 15:2).

> 26. He will have a mark, which at present is not known (Rev. 13:17; 14:9; 15:2; 16:2; 20:4-6).

> 27. His number, not his mark or his name, is 666 (Rev. 13:16-18; 15:2).

> 28. These three things — his mark, his name, or the number of his name — will be forced upon men who must take either one of three brands in the right hand or forehead, or they will be boycotted and killed (Rev. 13:16-18; 14:9-11; 15:2-4; 20:4-6).

> 29. The beast is a man (Rev. 13:18; 16:13-16; 19:19-21; 20:10. Compare these passages with Dan. 7:7, 8, 19-27; 8:9, 20-25; 9:26, 27; 11:36-45; Isa. 11:4; 2 Thess. 2:1-12; Jn. 5:43).

> 30. Men will be warned by literal angels flying in mid-heaven not to take his mark, his name, or his number, or they will suffer eternal Hell (Rev. 14:9-11).

> 31. His worship will be so apostate in nature that all who worship him or take any one of his three brands will be damned in Hell forever (Rev. 14:9-11).

> 32. Many will win the victory over his worship and brands (Rev. 15:2-4).

> 33. His followers will partake of the vial plagues (Rev. 16:2, 10).

> 34. He has a throne and a kingdom (Rev. 16:10).

> 35. He will be in league with, and be possessed by demon powers, to gather the nations at Armageddon who are not under him (Rev. 16:13-16).

> 36. He carries the woman or great whore (Rev. 17:3, 7).

> 37. He "was" or had been on the Earth before John's day (Rev. 17:8).

> 38. He "is not." That is, he was not on the Earth, but was in the bottomless pit during John's day (Rev. 17:8).

> 39. He "shall ascend out of the bottomless pit" and live on Earth again to revive the Grecian Empire and rule for forty-two months (Rev. 17:8).

> 40. He will then "go into perdition," or be destroyed at the second coming (Rev. 17:8).

> 41. "The seven heads" are seven mountains on which the woman sitteth. And "there are seven kings" or kingdoms (Rev. 17:9).

> 42. "Five are fallen." That is, the first five of these seven kingdoms had passed away before John saw the Revelation (Rev. 17:10). They are Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, Medo-Persia, and Greece.

> 43. "One is." That is, the sixth of these seven kingdoms, or the old Roman Empire, was in existence in John's day (Rev. 17:10).

> 44. "The other is not yet come; and when he cometh he must continue a short space." That is, the seventh of these seven kingdoms which will be made up of the ten kingdoms inside the old Roman Empire has not yet come. When such ten kingdoms are formed they will continue a short time before the "little horn" will arise from among them to get power over them (Rev. 17:10. Compare this passage with Dan. 7:7, 8, 19-27).

> 45. "The beast that was, and is not, even he is the eighth." That is, when Antichrist comes out of the ten kingdoms he will get power over them by the middle of the Week and he will form the eighth kingdom, which continues forty-two months (Rev. 17:11; 13:5).

> 46. He "is of the seven and goeth into perdition." That is, he is one of the seven, not necessarily the seventh (Rev. 17:11). He must be one of the first five that had fallen before John, either of Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, Medo-Persia, or Greece, for the beast "was," or had been before the sixth that was in existence in John's day, and he becomes the eighth kingdom following the seventh, which was to come between the sixth and the eighth. It is the Grecian Empire revived as the eighth kingdom that will fight against Christ at Armageddon and be destroyed (Zech. 9:13; Dan. 8:23-25; Rev. 19:19-21).

> 47. The ten horns "are ten kings" inside the Roman Empire (Rev. 17:12. Compare this passage with Dan. 7:23, 24).

> 48. They "have received no kingdom as yet" (Rev. 17:12. Compare this passage with Dan. 7:23, 24).

> 49. They "receive power as kings one hour (Greek, *hora*, 'hour' or 'period') with the beast" (Rev. 17:12. Compare this passage with Rev. 13:5).

> 50. "These have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast" (Rev. 17:13, 16, 17. See Lesson Forty-seven, Point III).

> 51. "These shall make war on the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them" at the second advent, so the ten kings must all be in existence at that time (Rev. 17:14; 19:19-21. Compare these passages with Dan. 2:44; 7:23-24; 8:20-25; 11:45–12:7).

> 52. "These shall hate the whore, and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire" (Rev. 17:16).

> 53. "For God hath put in their hearts to fulfill his will, and to agree, and give their kingdom unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled" (Rev. 17:17).

> 54. The beast is the commander-in-chief of all armies of the kings of the Earth at Armageddon (Rev. 19:19. Compare this passage with Ezek. 38; 39).

> 55. He mobilizes the armies of the nations to fight against Christ and the armies of Heaven at the second coming (Rev. 19:19. Compare this passage with Zech. 14:1-5; Rev. 16:13-16; Ezek. 38; 39).

> 56. The beast will be slain by Christ at His coming, thus proving him to be a mortal man at that time (Rev. 19:20. See also Dan. 7:11; Isa. 11:4; 2 Thess. 2:8, 9).

> 57. He, and the false prophet, will both be cast bodily into the Lake of Fire (Rev. 19:20).

> 58. The beast's armies will be destroyed by Christ and His armies (Rev. 19:20, 21; 17:14. Compare these passages with Ezek. 38:17-21; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Joel 3).

> 59. Those whom he kills for not worshipping him will be resurrected in the first resurrection and will reign with Christ (Rev. 20:4-6).

> 60. The beast and the false prophet will be in the Lake of Fire for 1,000 years before the devil is cast into that lake, and all will "be tormented day and night for ever and ever" (Rev. 20:10; 14:9-11; Isa. 66:22-24; Mt. 25:41, 46).

Now here's what all these statements make clear: the beast isn't just one thing — it's a symbol representing several different realities. The heads, horns, feet, body, mouth, the wounding of one head — each of these symbolizes different truths.

The beast itself represents something entirely different from the seven heads and ten horns that sit on it. If we fail to make this distinction, we'll end up with a confused understanding.

Here's the key: what the seven heads and ten horns represent will already have come to pass when the beast appears. They're already on the beast when he rises. They show us the succession of seven world kingdoms throughout Israel's history before the eighth kingdom arrives.

## II. The Beast Symbolizes Three Things

> 1. **A human being** — the Antichrist, or beast out of the sea, which we covered in Lesson Forty-six. Facts like Points 4, 10, 12-35, and 49-60 above prove that a human being is part of this symbol. He's explicitly called "a man," so we can't understand the beast without recognizing this. We studied the human aspect in the previous lesson.

> 2. **A supernatural being** — the beast out of the Abyss, which we'll explore in the following sections. Points 1-3, 35, and 37-40 prove that a satanic prince is also part of this symbol.

> 3. **An empire** — the eighth kingdom, which immediately follows the seven heads or kingdoms. Points 5-9, 11, 36-38, and 41-46 prove that a kingdom is also symbolized by the beast.

Here's where some interpreters have gone wrong. Some see only a kingdom and miss the human or supernatural person. Others see only a human being. Still others recognize an earthly king and kingdom but miss the satanic powers controlling it.

All these errors have led people to ignore some of the plain facts we just listed. But when you see all three things in the one symbol — and distinguish which passages clearly refer to which — everything becomes clear.

The key is to take each passage and apply it to whichever aspect makes the reading most sensible. Some passages apply to all three things; others apply to just one or two.

So the beast out of the Abyss, the beast out of the sea, and the beast as the eighth kingdom are all different things, but all three are present in the one symbol. We've already studied the beast out of the sea (the human aspect), so now let's turn to the beast out of the Abyss — the satanic aspect of the symbol.

## III. The Beast Out of the Abyss (Rev. 11:7; 17:8)

There have been many theories about who or what this beast from the Abyss might be. Some believe it's a human spirit currently confined there who will come out and exist again as the Antichrist. Others think it represents the revival of a kingdom that once existed and will exist again.

But Scripture suggests something more than a mere kingdom revival. Think about it: how could a kingdom be confined to the Abyss for centuries, come out again as a kingdom, and then go into destruction a second time? That would require all the original subjects of that kingdom to live again on Earth — and Scripture doesn't teach that (Heb. 9:27).

Only a person can be described as "coming out of the Abyss." If that's true, then the beast's identity must be either a human or a supernatural spirit who once existed on Earth, is currently confined in the Abyss, and will exist again on Earth in the last days.

You can find about as many candidates for the future Antichrist as there are interpreters! Among them are Nimrod, various Egyptian, Assyrian, and Babylonian kings, Alexander the Great, Antiochus Epiphanes, Julius Caesar, Nero, Judas Iscariot, Mohammed, various popes of Rome, Napoleon, and more.

Here's what this school of interpreters believes: when this human spirit (whoever he is) gets reincarnated as the Antichrist, he'll grow to maturity. In the middle of the Week, an assassin will kill him. His body will lie in state for three days, then be raised from the dead. At that point, Satan will incarnate himself in this man, who becomes an immortal being. They claim this will be Satan's imitation of Christ's resurrection and proves that Antichrist won't be an ordinary mortal during the last 3½ years — which would explain why he gets cast "alive" into the Lake of Fire.

However, here's the problem: none of this can be proven from Scripture. We can't prove that any of these historical figures will be the beast out of the Abyss, nor can we prove that any of them will be the Antichrist.

Neither the beast out of the Abyss nor the Antichrist will be a reincarnated human being — or any other kind of reincarnated being. Let me show you why the beast of the Abyss and the Antichrist are actually two different persons. If we can establish this, then all the reincarnation arguments collapse, and there's no basis for believing the Antichrist will be assassinated and resurrected as an immortal being.

Here's the evidence:

> 1. We've already established from the sixty scriptural statements that the beast symbol represents three things: first, a human being (the beast out of the sea of humanity); second, a supernatural being (the beast out of the Abyss); third, an empire (the eighth kingdom composed of many peoples, Rev. 17:1, 3, 7, 9-11, 15). According to Scripture, then, the beast out of the Abyss and the Antichrist are not the same person.

> 2. The Antichrist will be an ordinary mortal throughout his life until the Battle of Armageddon, when Christ will kill him at His coming. Look at these passages: "He [Christ] shall smite the earth with the rod of His mouth, and with the breath of His lips shall He slay the wicked" (Isa. 11:4). "I beheld even until the beast was slain, and his body destroyed, and given to the burning flame" (Dan. 7:11; Rev. 19:20). "He [Antichrist] shall be broken [slain, as in Dan. 8:8, 22; 11:4, 22] without hand" (Dan. 8:25). "And then shall that wicked [Antichrist] be revealed, whom the Lord shall consume [Greek, *analisko*, 'to slay, remove'] with the spirit of his mouth, and shall destroy [Greek, *katargeo*, 'to be useless, destroy'] with the brightness of his coming" (2 Thess. 2:8). Antichrist must be mortal to fulfill these statements. He's expressly called "a man" in Rev. 13:14-18 and 2 Thess. 2:3, 4. He can't be mortal and also come out of the Abyss without being reincarnated or resurrected to immortality.

> 3. Reincarnation isn't taught in Scripture. You certainly can't build such a doctrine on the mere fact that a spirit comes out of the Abyss. To be reincarnated, you'd have to go through rebirth and growth to maturity all over again. This would make Antichrist a baby at least twice! And if he's also killed by an assassin in the middle of the Week and then slain by Christ, he'd have to die at least three times. This contradicts Scripture: "it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment" (Heb. 9:27). This verse doesn't teach that after death there can be reincarnation and another death. The only exceptions to dying once will be those translated without seeing death at the rapture and at the catching up of the manchild (1 Cor. 15:51-58; 1 Thess. 4:16-17; Rev. 12:5). The Bible does teach resurrection after death, at which time each person will be raised full-grown and have an immortal body that cannot die (1 Cor. 15:20-23, 34-51; Jn. 5:28, 29). There's simply no room for a doctrine where a mature human spirit becomes a baby again, is born in flesh, grows to maturity a second time, and dies again. And if Antichrist became a baby and grew to maturity in a second life, what would be the purpose? Any baby could grow up and fulfill the same things. If this one person can be reincarnated, why not others? And if we accept reincarnation, we shouldn't criticize Hindus who believe the same thing! The logical conclusion is that Antichrist is simply a man born on Earth in the last days for the first time, and he won't be the beast out of the Abyss — who, as we'll see, is a supernatural spirit.

> 4. Scripture makes clear that the Abyss is not where departed human spirits go — therefore, a human spirit cannot come out of it. The Abyss is the prison for demon spirits (Lk. 8:31; Rev. 9:1-21). It's also where certain satanic princes are currently confined (Rev. 9:11; 11:7; 17:8). We don't know how many other angels are there. It will be Satan's prison for a thousand years (Rev. 20:1-10). Based on these Scriptures, the Abyss is reserved for Satan and his demon and angelic followers — never for human spirits. Human spirits went into Paradise and Hell before Christ's resurrection (Lk. 16:19-31; Ps. 16:10; Lk. 23:43; Eph. 4:7-11; Mt. 12:40; Rev. 20:11-15). When Christ rose, He led all righteous souls out of Paradise under the Earth and took them to Heaven with Him. So now when a Christian dies, they go to be "with Christ" until the first resurrection (Eph. 4:7-11; Heb. 2:14; Phil. 1:21; 2 Cor. 5:8; Rev. 6:9-11). Wicked human spirits still go to Hell as before — Judas included. No human spirit is in the Abyss to come out as the beast, so we must identify the beast of the Abyss as a supernatural spirit. This proves the human Antichrist is not the same as the beast of the Abyss. (See the Chart and Lesson Thirty-seven for more on the Abyss and the place of the dead.)

> 5. There's no Scripture proving that Antichrist will be different from any other natural man regarding his being, birth, or death. He'll simply be used by satanic powers in the last days. Neither God nor Satan needs to bring back a dead man to fulfill prophecy. If some historical man could fulfill the requirements, why couldn't a future man do so just as well? Reincarnation has never been necessary for supernatural powers to operate in the world, and it won't be in the future either. The only requirement for receiving God's or Satan's power is complete submission to the operator's will.

> 6. The idea that Antichrist will be assassinated in the middle of the Week and rise from the dead as an immortal, Satan-incarnate being is unscriptural. The wounding of one of the heads and its healing doesn't prove this. Here's the crucial detail some interpreters have missed: it was one of the seven heads on the beast that was wounded to death — not the beast itself. The angel explained to John that the seven heads were seven kingdoms, and all of them precede the beast itself, which is the eighth kingdom ruled by Antichrist and the satanic prince of the Abyss (Rev. 17:9-11). So the Antichrist, king of the eighth kingdom, won't be assassinated at all, and therefore can't be resurrected in the middle of the Week. And Antichrist will never be an incarnation of Satan — we see the dragon (Satan) as a separate being from Antichrist throughout the entire period he's supposedly incarnated in him (2 Thess. 2:8, 9; Rev. 13:1-4; 16:13-16; 20:10). Satan can't be incarnate in Antichrist while also existing as a separate being outside of him.

## IV. Identification of the Beast Out of the Abyss

We firmly believe the beast out of the Abyss represents a mighty supernatural satanic prince — not a human spirit. We don't believe this spirit will be incarnated in any human body to become the Antichrist. Instead, he'll be the ruling prince under Satan's guidance, dominating the Antichrist and exalting him as the earthly king over the eighth and last kingdom.

Here's what we believe about this being's history and future:

- He ruled one of the first kingdoms that had fallen before John's day (represented by the first five heads on the beast)
- When his kingdom fell, he was cast into the Abyss and confined there during the sixth kingdom (the Roman Empire in John's day)
- He'll remain confined until the formation of the ten kingdoms (the seventh kingdom)
- He'll be released from the Abyss during the seventh, cause Antichrist's rise from the ten kingdoms, and revive the Grecian kingdom he ruled before
- This kingdom will become the eighth and last, headed by Antichrist, who will fight Christ at Armageddon

This is what the revealing angel meant when he said the beast "was [had existed on Earth before John], and is not [on the Earth in John's day], and shall ascend out of the bottomless pit [in the last days to revive the kingdom he ruled before being confined] and go into perdition" (Rev. 17:8).
