This angelic spirit will control Antichrist through demons, making him the embodiment of wickedness and the manifestation of satanic power. The angel is represented by the beast out of the Abyss, which will cause Antichrist's rise. Antichrist in turn is symbolized by the beast out of the sea of humanity, reviving and forming the eighth kingdom. That kingdom is also symbolized by the beast. All three things make up the powers represented by the one symbol, as we've seen.

It's the beast out of the Abyss that will make war on the two witnesses through Antichrist. Here we see the cruel operations of satanic powers through Antichrist and his kingdom — a combination of human and supernatural forces. But only when the witnesses' testimony is finished will God remove His protecting hand from them.

It's similar to how kingdoms have always worked throughout history: supernatural powers control the kingdoms of this world, and earthly events are really the result of battles won or lost between the good and evil spirits of God and Satan.

The devil controls the kingdoms of this world. He offered them to Christ, who didn't deny Satan's claim (Lk. 4:5, 6). Throughout this present age, God intervenes in Satan's plans to make His prophetic Word possible — while Satan and evil forces constantly try to make it fail. That's why there are battles in the heavenlies.

This battle between Satan and God can be traced throughout the Bible from Genesis to Revelation. God is working to bring humanity back to where we were before the Fall. To do that, He must continually counteract Satan's powers, as the enemy tries every conceivable way to hinder God's plan.

In the Old Testament, the battle was mainly about the coming of the woman's seed and God's purpose for Israel. Satan's great weapon was inspiring the world's kingdoms to destroy Israel so Christ would never come to pronounce doom on him. (See Lesson Eleven, Point VII, 6, (9), F and G.)

From Israel's beginning in Gen. 12 through the Old Testament, five great kingdoms served Satan's purpose. But God turned Satan's efforts around and used these same nations to discipline Israel when she needed it. When one nation overstepped its role in God's plan, He overthrew it through another — and that required dealing with Satan in the heavenlies.

These five kingdoms were Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, Medo-Persia, and Greece with its divisions — the five that had fallen before John. The sixth was the old Roman Empire in John's day, which scattered Israel to the four winds. The seventh will be that same empire in the form of ten kingdoms, which will also persecute Israel. The beast will establish and rule the eighth, immediately following the seventh, becoming the greatest and bitterest persecutor of Israel of all eight kingdoms. It will try to carry out Satan's original plan to destroy Israel and thwart God's eternal purpose.

In every crisis in Israel's history with these kingdoms, God has intervened at just the right moment. That's how Israel has been preserved in the past, and how she'll be preserved in the future as God promised.

Besides using kingdoms to oppress Israel, Satan has also caused Israel to sin so that God Himself has cursed them at times. But during those times, God has used the very tool Satan devised to destroy Israel as the means to chasten them and bring them to repentance. This will happen again in Israel's final repentance during the Tribulation.

You can see these patterns throughout Israel's history in the Pentateuch and historical books, especially in the prophets, where God's servants try to bring Israel to repentance by warning of foreign enemies.

The Bible gives us numerous passages showing satanic powers operating over different kingdoms in attempts to thwart God's purpose for Israel. We also see God's spirit forces opposing these satanic powers to carry out His plan (Isa. 14:4-21; 24:21-23; 25:7; Ezek. 28:11-17; Dan. 2:19-23, 28, 31-45; 4:25-37; 5:18-31; 7:1-28; 8:1-26; 9:24-27; 10:1-12, 13; Joel 3; Zech. 14; Lk. 4:5, 6; Jn. 12:31; 2 Cor. 4:4; Eph. 2:2; 6:11-17; 2 Thess. 2; Rev. 11:15; 13:1-18; etc.).

These references clearly illustrate this truth, and the beast out of the Abyss is plainly identified in Daniel alongside Revelation. In Daniel, we see empires rise and fall — each used to carry out God's purpose in disciplining or delivering Israel — from Daniel's time to the Messiah's coming.

When one kingdom fails to carry out God's purpose, He chooses another, as illustrated in 2 Ki. 23:29–24:10. In Dan. 2, 7, 8, 9, and 10-12, we see six world kingdoms that oppressed Israel:

- Babylon as the head of gold and the lion
- Medo-Persia as the silver, the bear, and the ram
- Greece as the brass, the leopard, and the he-goat
- Rome as the iron and the terrible beast
- Revised Rome as the ten toes and ten horns
- Revived Grecia as the kingdom of the little horn out of the ten horns of Dan. 7, out of the four horns of Dan. 8, and as the king of the north (Syria) in Dan. 11, as we studied in Lesson Thirty-nine

Now here's the key passage: Dan. 10:1–11:1 is the main Scripture in the entire Bible concerning the supernatural princes under Satan who rule different kingdoms. It tells us how God overrules these rulers to fulfill prophecy.

Picture it this way: just as earthly kings have their territories, so do these spiritual princes. And just as earthly wars determine who rules which land, spiritual warfare determines which supernatural power controls which kingdom.

lessons.title: The Ten Horns and the Beast Itself
lessons.content:
# THE TEN HORNS AND THE BEAST ITSELF

Let's dig into one of the most detailed prophecies in Scripture — the vision of the ten horns and the beast itself. This is where Daniel and Revelation come together to paint a vivid picture of what's coming in the last days.

> "The fourth beast shall be the fourth kingdom upon the earth [from Daniel's time to Christ's coming] which shall be diverse from all kingdoms, and shall devour the whole earth . . . And the ten horns out of this kingdom are ten kings that shall arise: and another shall rise after them; and he shall be diverse from the first [ten] and shall subdue three kings [of the ten, which are the other three divisions of Greece besides Syria, the one Antichrist shall come from]" (Dan. 7:23-24).

> "And the ten horns which thou sawest are ten kings, which have received no kingdom as yet; but receive power as kings one hour [period] with the beast. These have one mind, and shall give their power and strength unto the beast. These shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them: for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings: and they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful. And he said unto me, The waters which thou sawest where the whore sitteth, are peoples, and multitudes, and nations, and tongues. And the ten horns which thou sawest upon the beast, these shall hate the whore, and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire. For God hath put in their hearts to fulfill his will, and to agree, and to give their kingdom unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled" (Rev. 17:12-17. See also Dan. 2:40-45; 7:7-27; 8:8, 9, 17-25; 9:26, 27; 11:40-85; 12:1-13; Rev. 12:3; 13:1-3; 17:8-11; 19:11-21).

Now let's break this down so you can clearly see how the beast is a kingdom made up of ten kingdoms — and exactly how far Antichrist's reign will extend.

## I. The Ten Horns — the Seventh Kingdom or Revised Rome

### 1. IS ROME TO BE REVIVED?

If so, what form will it take? Here's the key insight: Scripture never predicts a single Revived Roman Empire ruled by one man like the old Roman emperors. Instead, we'll see ten separate governments formed inside that territory, functioning as the seventh head on the beast. You might call it the "Revised" Roman Empire rather than "Revived."

Think of it like this: instead of one company taking over, imagine a corporate merger where ten independent businesses form a loose federation — each keeps its own leadership, but they're all connected.

Here's the evidence that Rome will exist as ten kingdoms in the last days — kingdoms that will still be around when both Christ and Antichrist appear:

> (1) In Dan. 2:40-45, we get our first picture of Rome — symbolized by two legs of iron and ten toes made of iron and clay. Rome has never existed as separate kingdoms (symbolized by those ten toes), so for prophecy to be fulfilled, it must take that form eventually.
>
> Rome has existed as one kingdom (the body where the legs join) and as two divisions (the two iron legs, representing the eastern and western empire). But those ten toes are still future. Dan. 2:44-45 tells us exactly when: "in the day that the God of Heaven sets up a kingdom." That hasn't happened yet — it won't until Jesus returns to reign forever (Isa. 9:6, 7; Dan. 7:13-14; Rev. 11:15).
>
> So the Roman Empire will reappear in a new form of ten kingdoms at Christ's second coming, succeeding old Rome just like Rome succeeded the first five world powers. Those ten toes on two feet represent ten kingdoms from both the eastern and western divisions — five from each. The phrase "the kingdom" appears twice in Dan. 2:40-45, showing these ten will have some kind of loose federation.

> (2) Daniel 7:7-27 confirms that ten kingdoms will emerge from Rome, all existing at the same time. But here's the additional detail: "another little horn" (called "the beast" in Dan. 7:11) will arise "after them" and overthrow three of the ten.
>
> This little horn continues "until the Ancient of days" comes — when judgment goes to the saints and they possess the kingdom. This points to Christ's return to destroy Antichrist and the ten kings and establish His reign. Both the ten kings and the eleventh (the beast) will exist when Christ returns.
>
> The ten will hand their kingdoms to the eleventh (the beast), and together they'll destroy Mystery Babylon (religion) in the middle of the Week. Then 3½ years later, they'll fight Christ at Armageddon (Rev. 17:12-17). This beast of Dan. 7:11 will be slain just like the one in Rev. 19:20.
>
> So Rome will be revised and exist as ten separate kingdoms. Some have thought these ten kings rule in succession like the seven heads, but Scripture never says that about them. Here's the proof that Daniel's ten toes and ten horns are the same as Revelation's ten horns and ten kings — all existing at Christ's second coming and destroyed at that time:

> A. The number matches in both Daniel and Revelation — ten toes and ten horns (Dan. 2:40-45; 7:7-27; Rev. 12:3; 13:1; 17:12).

> B. All ten will exist together and fight Christ when He returns to Earth (Dan. 2:34, 35, 44, 45; 7:8-14, 21-27; Rev. 17:14; 19:17-21).

> C. All ten come after the "fourth kingdom" (Rome) and revise that kingdom (Dan. 2:4-45; 7:7, 8, 23, 24; Rev. 17:12-14).

> D. All ten are connected with the same beast at the same time (Dan. 7:8, 11, 21-27; Rev. 17:8-17; 19:11-21).

> E. The beast or "little horn" is an individual just like each of the ten horns, and he rises "after them" in both Daniel and Revelation — so these accounts must be identical (Dan. 7:8-24; Rev. 17:12-17).

> F. All ten become subject to the same beast (Dan. 7:23, 24; Rev. 13:1-10; 17:12-17).

> G. All ten exist when God sets up His kingdom, which immediately follows theirs (Dan. 2:4-45; 7:7-27; Rev. 17:12-17; 19:11-21).

If these ten were meant to rule in succession, Daniel would have seen them rise one at a time like the four beasts. Instead, he saw them all at once on the feet of the image and on the head of the terrible beast (Dan. 2:31-45; 7:7, 8). The "little horn" couldn't rise "among" the ten if only one existed at a time. And he couldn't overthrow three of them unless they all existed together.

The "little horn" of Dan. 7:7-11, 23-27 exists at the same time as the ten kingdoms and continues the same length of time as "the beast" of Rev. 13:1-8; 17:8-17; 19:11-21.

> H. The "fourth kingdom" of Dan. 2 and 7 is the same as the sixth head of Rev. 17. The ten horns in both passages emerge from and succeed old Rome — so they must be the same.
>
> The seventh head of Rev. 17 and the ten horns are identical. Daniel's ten horns were to be the next kingdom after Rome, formed inside Roman territory. It's the only kingdom mentioned as still future (besides the "little horn" and his empire composed of the ten). The only future kingdom in Rev. 17 after John's day (besides the beast) was the seventh head — existing for "a short space" before giving its kingdom to the beast.
>
> These must be the same: the ten kings in both books exist at the same time, form the next kingdom after Rome, become subject to the beast, help form the eighth kingdom, and fight under him against Christ.

> I. The ten kings in Daniel and Revelation reign both before and after the beast or "little horn." Their reign changes with his rise. Before he appears, they control their own kingdoms. After he comes, they submit to his supreme will. They control the seventh kingdom; he controls the eighth.
>
> So we conclude: the ten horns in both books are the same. Rome will be revised and exist as ten kingdoms before Antichrist rises. Then Antichrist will emerge from these ten kingdoms and over 3½ years subdue all ten kings. He'll form the eighth kingdom from all ten, possibly adding other parts of the old Grecian Empire that weren't under Rome.
>
> Antichrist will come from Syria and overthrow the three other Grecian divisions — making up four of the ten kingdoms of Revised Rome. This revives Greece and brings both Greek and Roman territory under him as the eighth kingdom. Revised Rome will stay as ten kingdoms throughout its short existence, while Revived Greece will include those same ten plus others it conquers during its brief time.

### 2. WHEN IS ROME TO BE REVISED?

The ten kingdoms will form in the near future. We don't know exactly when. But Rome must be revised first and exist as ten kingdoms before Antichrist can arise — he's supposed to come up "after them" and "among them" (Dan. 7:8, 24).

### 3. HOW LONG IS REVISED ROME TO EXIST?

Revised Rome (the seventh head) will exist only a "short space." We can't know exactly how long before Daniel's Seventieth Week arrives. It seems that soon after Rome is revised, Antichrist will arise and start conquering some of the ten kingdoms.

This war continues during the first part of the Week until Antichrist gains full power over the ten, forming his kingdom — the Revived Grecian Empire. The "short space" of Rev. 17:10 matches the "short time" of Rev. 12:12 — about 3½ years (Rev. 12:6, 14).

These ten kings continue this short period before Antichrist rises from one of them to gain power over all ten. He'll overthrow three of them with help from the kingdom he emerges from (Dan. 7:8, 20). Then the other six will surrender without further war (Rev. 17:12-17).

So if the ten kings last about 3½ years before Antichrist rises, and he's here for only seven years (Dan. 9:27), there will be only about 10½ years from when the ten kingdoms form until Christ's return.

We don't know how long before the ten kingdoms start forming. But if war broke out in Europe, Asia, and Africa today, the ten kingdoms could be fully established in just a few short years. This age could end in ten to fifteen years if such a war started immediately.

What we do know: we're in the closing years of this age because Israel is being rebuilt. The prophet said, "When the Lord shall build up Zion, he shall appear in his glory" (Ps. 102:16).

### 4. HOW IS ROME TO BE REVISED?

The ten kingdoms will form through wars and agreements. We should expect them to form only from old Roman territory (Dan. 7:19-27).

Here's a helpful overview of these empire territories:

- **Egypt** (first head): smallest territory, ruling from Egypt to Syria
- **Assyria** (second head): ruled from Egypt to Armenia, part of Asia Minor, all of Babylon, Media, and Persia
- **Babylon** (third head): ruled Egypt and all Syrian countries west of the Tigris
- **Medo-Persia** (fourth head): largest so far — from the Indus River east to parts of Thrace and Macedonia west, from the Caspian and Black Seas north to Egypt and part of North Africa
- **Greece** (fifth head): conquered all Medo-Persian territory plus Greece, Macedonia, and Thrace — the largest of the first five
- **Rome** (sixth head): controlled all Greek territory except lands east of the Caspian Sea and Persian Gulf, and added all countries west to the Atlantic — from northern Africa south to the Rhine and Danube rivers north (including Dacia and England)

It's from this Roman territory that we expect ten kingdoms to emerge as the seventh head on the beast. The eighth kingdom (Antichrist's) will succeed these ten and include not only all this territory but also the far eastern parts of Greek territory and whatever these ten kingdoms control outside the old Roman Empire.

## II. The Beast Itself — the Eighth Kingdom

As we saw in the previous lesson, the seven heads and ten horns on the beast are entirely different from the beast itself. The beast is the eighth in a succession of world powers — from Israel's beginning as a nation in Egypt to Christ's second advent, when Israel will be delivered from the Gentiles and "the times of the Gentiles" will end.

The beast (as the eighth kingdom) will be ruled by two figures: the personal, visible, mortal human being called Antichrist, and the personal, invisible, immortal angel — the prince of Grecia.

This prince of Grecia will be released from the pit during the existence of the ten kingdoms and will cause the human Antichrist to rise to power over the original four Grecian divisions. This revives the Grecian Empire that didn't exist in John's day, had existed before his day, and will exist as the eighth kingdom when Christ returns.

This Revived Grecian Empire will swallow the entire ten kingdoms of Revised Rome during the first half of the Week. By the end of the last half of the Week, it will consume countries north and east of the ten kingdoms. But before Antichrist can complete his world conquest, Christ defeats him at Jerusalem (Zech. 14).

So Antichrist's kingdom will be the greatest of all eight Gentile world powers — but like all before him, he'll fall short of total world conquest.

### Will Russia, Antichrist, or Christ Rule the Whole World?

Communism will never rule the world as a whole — and neither will any other "ism." No earthly man will become a worldwide dictator before Christ comes to reign.

Antichrist will come closer than anyone, but before he even conquers all of Asia, Europe, and Africa, he'll "come to an end and none shall help him" (Dan. 11:45; Zech. 14).

Jesus Christ will be the world's first literal worldwide ruler. He will bring universal peace and prosperity — not Antichrist. But before that universal peace arrives, certain wars must occur to fulfill prophecy.

### There Must Be at Least Three More European Wars Before the Second Advent

> 1. **The first war** will form the ten kingdoms. This war is necessary because at least twenty-two or twenty-three states currently exist inside old Roman territory — they must be reduced to ten to fulfill Dan. 7:24.

> 2. **The second war** occurs after the ten are formed. Then — and only then — Antichrist arises from the ten. In his rise to power, he'll overthrow three of them (Dan. 7:8, 24).

> 3. **The third war** happens during the last half of the Week, between the ten kingdoms under Antichrist and countries "of the east and of the north" of those kingdoms, fulfilling Dan. 11:44. (See Supplement Two, Question 1, and Lesson Thirty-nine for more details.)

### No World Peace and Prosperity Under Antichrist

The old idea of Antichrist as someone who miraculously brings world peace is unscriptural. He's a man of war from the moment he appears until he's destroyed at Armageddon (Dan. 7:7-8, 23-24; 8:20-25; 11:40-45; Rev. 6:1-2).

The only peace he'll bring is a seven-year covenant with the Jews. Then he'll break it halfway through and try to destroy them. But he wages war among the Gentiles the entire time until his death at Armageddon (Dan. 7:11; 8:25; 9:27; Rev. 19:11-21; 2 Thess. 2:8; Ezek. 38; 39; Zech. 14; Joel 3).

## III. The Extent of Antichrist's Reign (Rev. 13:5-18)

People often ask: "Will Antichrist have power over all kindreds, tongues, and nations? Will everyone on Earth worship him?"

Generally, yes — but it depends on what we mean by "all." If "all" means every single individual on the planet today, then no. But if "all" refers to what God has in mind — the ten kingdoms of old Rome in the last days — then yes, "all" includes everyone affected by Antichrist's decree within his empire.

Here's the Scriptural proof that Antichrist will not rule America or become a worldwide dictator (as many prophecy students teach):

### 1. The word "all" in Rev. 13 is a figure of speech

It's called "synecdoche" — where a part stands for the whole, or the whole for a part. Scripture uses this constantly:

> (1) "I, even I, do bring a flood of waters upon the earth, to destroy all flesh, wherein is the breath of life, from under heaven; and every thing that is in the earth shall die" (Gen. 6:17). If we took this as literally as some take Rev. 13, Noah's family and all the ark animals would have died — but they didn't.

> (2) "And they utterly destroyed all that was in the city, both men and women" — referring to Jericho (Josh. 6:21-25). But Rahab and her family were spared.

> (3) "David and all the house of Israel played before the Lord" and "brought up the ark" (2 Sam. 6:5, 15). But not every Israelite played instruments — many didn't know how, many were too young, many weren't even there.

> (4) "Six months did Joab remain there [out of his own country] with all Israel, until he had cut off every male in Edom" (1 Ki. 11:16, 17). "All Israel" here means part of the army.

> (5) "So when all Israel saw that the king hearkened not unto them . . . see to thine own house, David. So Israel departed unto their tents. But as for the children of Israel which dwelt in the cities of Judah, Rehoboam reigned over them" (1 Ki. 12:16-19). Here "all Israel" means only those who rebelled. (See similar examples in 1 Chron. 10:6; 2 Chron. 10:1, 3, 16; 11:16, 17; 12:1; 13:15; 16:6; Ezra 10:5; Ezek. 21:4.)

> (6) Nebuchadnezzar was made ruler over "all men" — but ancient Babylon ruled only part of the Earth (Dan. 2:37, 38; 4:1, 11, 12, 20). He never reigned over Greece, Rome, and many other lands. Dan. 2:39 says Greece ruled "over all the earth" — but Greece never ruled Italy, Spain, and many other countries. Dan. 7:23 says Rome ruled the "whole earth" — but we know that didn't include many tribes and nations.

> (7) Mt. 3:5, 6 says "all Judea" and "all the region round about Jordan" came to John for baptism — but the Pharisees, Sadducees, and many others never were baptized. Many women, children, and sick people never even saw John.

> (8) Lk. 2:1-3 says Caesar Augustus decreed that "all the world should be taxed." Any Roman law didn't affect countries outside the Empire — so "all" here applies only to those under Augustus.

> (9) Paul said Roman believers' faith was "spoken of throughout the whole world" (Rom. 1:8). He meant many in the Empire knew about them — not that everyone on Earth had heard of Christianity. The same applies to Col. 1:23 and Rom. 10:18.

> (10) Acts 11:28 mentions a drought "throughout all the world" in Claudius Caesar's time. That drought didn't cover every continent and island.

Think of it like when someone says, "The whole town came to the game last night." We understand they mean a lot of people showed up — not literally every single resident.

Hundreds of examples show "all" used figuratively. So we don't have to believe Rev. 13 means Antichrist will literally reign over all the Earth and kill everyone who refuses his mark.

The writers above weren't false teachers — they used language the way all people naturally do. We should understand them the same way we understand someone today saying "everyone was there" about a big crowd.

If U.S. lawmakers made a law requiring "all kindreds, tongues, and nations" to register on a certain day, we'd understand it applies only to those under U.S. jurisdiction — not the same ethnic groups living under other governments worldwide. Antichrist's laws work the same way.

We must understand how "all" is used in each specific Scripture. If it means all without exception, there will be no limits in the passage or elsewhere. If the passage (or related passages) makes clear it means a part of something, we need to recognize that "all" doesn't include everyone on Earth.

When Paul said God wills that "all men come to the knowledge of the truth" (1 Tim. 2:4), we know he means all without exception. But when we read that "all" were baptized by John — while the same passage shows many weren't — we take it as a figurative expression meaning "a great many."

Both Testaments use universal terms for certain kingdoms and kings' power. Yet these terms show only part of the Earth was ruled by those empires. Logically, Antichrist's kingdom could likewise be limited to part of the world.

If multiple Scriptures limit his power to certain territory — telling us exactly what's under him and what isn't — then we must limit the "all" of Rev. 13 to his actual domain.

### 2. Revelation 13 itself limits Antichrist's kingdom

His realm consists of ten kingdoms that will form inside old Roman territory:

"The ten horns which thou sawest are ten kings [formed inside old Rome just before Christ's return, Dan. 7:23, 24], which have received no kingdom as yet; but receive power as kings one hour [Greek: hora, meaning 'a period of time' as in Jn. 16:2, 4, 25; 1 Jn. 2:18; Rev. 14:15; Rom. 13:11] with the beast. These [ten only] . . . shall give their power and strength unto the beast. These shall make war with the Lamb [at Christ's return, Rev. 19:11-21], and the Lamb shall overcome them . . . And the ten horns which thou sawest upon the beast, these shall hate the whore [of Rev. 17:1-7, sitting on the peoples that make up the beast — the eighth kingdom], and shall make her desolate and naked, and shall eat her flesh, and burn her with fire. For God hath put in their hearts to fulfill his will, and to agree, and to give their kingdom [the ten kingdoms] unto the beast, until the words of God shall be fulfilled" (Rev. 17:8-18).

If the beast has only "ten horns" representing ten kings over ten kingdoms, that's the extent of Antichrist's reign. The "all kindreds, tongues, and nations" he rules refers to those within the ten kingdoms — not the same ethnic groups under other governments elsewhere.

"All that worship him" means many in the ten kingdoms. "All that receive his mark" (Rev. 13:16-18) means many in those same ten kingdoms. He'll make a law requiring everyone in his empire to worship him and take his mark or die — but that doesn't mean this law is literally enforced everywhere, as we'll see.

### 3. Daniel 7:7, 8, 17-27 limits Antichrist's kingdom to the ten kingdoms

In Dan. 7:7-8, this is symbolized by a beast with ten horns plus another little horn emerging from the ten and uprooting three. All eleven horns come from the same beast's head.

Daniel explains: "The ten horns out of this kingdom [the fourth — old Rome] are ten kings that shall arise: and another [the eleventh, the little horn] shall rise after them; and he shall be diverse from the first, and he shall subdue three kings. And he shall speak great words against the most High (Rev. 13:5, 6), and shall wear out the saints of the most High (Dan. 7:21, 22; Rev. 13:7), and think to change times and laws: and they shall be given into his hand until a time and times and the dividing of time [3½ years, 1,260 days, Dan. 12:7; Rev. 11:2; 12:6, 14; 13:5]. But the judgment [of Dan. 7:9-14; Mt. 25:31-46] shall sit, and they shall take away his dominion, to consume and to destroy it unto the end [2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:7-12; Rev. 19:11-21; Zech. 14]. And the kingdom under the whole heaven, shall be given to the saints of the most High, whose kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and all dominions shall serve and obey him" (Dan. 7:21-27; 2:44, 45; 8:25; 11:45; Zech. 14; Ezek. 38; 39; Joel 2, 3; Lk. 1:32-35; Mt. 25:31-46; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:7-12; Jude 14; Rev. 19:11–22:5).

Only "ten kings" give their power to the beast and continue as kings "with the beast" for forty-two months. Daniel 7 shows exactly where these ten kingdoms will be located — "out of this kingdom" (old Rome).

So when Antichrist makes his law requiring all to worship him and take his mark, his authority extends over ten kingdoms within old Roman territory.

America and all countries outside old Rome won't be affected by this law because they're not within the ten kingdoms. If God said the ten kingdoms are "out of this kingdom" (the old Roman Empire), that settles it for those who believe God knows what He's talking about.

If God said only ten horns would be on the beast and told us exactly where the ten kingdoms will be, it's unwise to paste fifty-five extra horns on the beast and extend the Roman Empire over the whole world. Why change Scripture? Prophecy will be fulfilled exactly as God said. No Scripture ever changes the ten horns to sixty-five.

### 4. Daniel 11:40-45 plainly states limits to Antichrist's power

Antichrist will break his seven-year covenant with the Jews (Dan. 9:27), enter Palestine in the middle of the seven years, and gain power over the ten kingdoms. He'll decree that all must worship him.

But notice: "many countries [not all] shall be overthrown: but these shall escape out of his hand, even Edom, and Moab, and the chief of the children of Ammon."

If these border countries right next to Palestine (where Antichrist has his capital during the last 3½ years — Dan. 11:45; Rev. 11:1, 2) escape him, it's certainly reasonable that countries across vast oceans — never part of old Rome — will also escape him.

### 5. Israel in Judea will flee from Antichrist

When he breaks his covenant with them halfway through Daniel's Seventieth Week (Dan. 9:27), they'll flee into Edom and Moab — a "place prepared of God" where they're protected from Antichrist during the very time when supposedly "all men" are under him. (See Lesson Forty-five.)

### 6. Daniel 11:44, 45 tells of other nations not under Antichrist

After Antichrist conquers many countries and seizes Palestine, "tidings out of the east and out of the north shall trouble him: therefore he shall go forth with fury to destroy, and utterly to make away many."

Countries north and east of the ten kingdoms will wage war against Antichrist. They won't be under him. They won't take his mark. (See Lesson Thirty-nine.)

### 7. Zechariah shows many people won't take the mark — and won't be killed

He describes a battle between Jews at Jerusalem and many nations under Antichrist the very day Christ returns with heaven's armies to deliver Israel and establish His kingdom (Zech. 14:1-5, 14).

Then Zechariah says: "And it shall come to pass, that every one that is left of all the nations which came against Jerusalem shall even go up from year to year to worship the King, the Lord of hosts, and to keep the feast of tabernacles. And it shall be, that whoso will not come up of all the families of the earth unto Jerusalem to worship the King, the Lord of hosts, even upon them shall be no rain. And if the family of Egypt go not up, and come not, that they have no rain . . . This shall be the punishment of Egypt, and the punishment of all nations that come not up to keep the feast of tabernacles. In that day shall there be upon the bells of the horses, holiness unto the Lord . . . Yea, every pot in Jerusalem and in Judah shall be holiness unto the Lord of hosts" (Zech. 14:6-21).

This proves that even within Antichrist's ten kingdoms, not everyone takes the mark or gets killed.

Dan. 11:40-45 says "the land of Egypt shall not escape" Antichrist. So if Egypt is under him, yet some Egyptians survive to enter the Millennium (as Zech. 14:16-21 proves), then they haven't taken the mark.

Rev. 14:9-12 declares that everyone who takes the mark will be eternally damned when Christ comes. The fact that survivors remain and will worship at Jerusalem during the Millennium proves they didn't take the mark — and they escaped death at Antichrist's hands.

### 8. The reality: Antichrist's law will be unenforceable

Yes, Antichrist will decree that everyone in his ten kingdoms must take a mark and worship him or die. But there's a war with countries north and east that keeps him so occupied he can't possibly enforce such a law.

It would be nearly impossible to strictly enforce this across such vast territory in only 3½ years. There will be ways to avoid this law — just as with any law humans have made. Corrupt local officials, family connections, money — all will create loopholes. Plus, people in mountains, deserts, and rural areas will be simply unreachable.

And in all lands outside his ten kingdoms? They won't be affected at all. Even if Antichrist ruled the whole world (which he doesn't), enforcing such a law everywhere in forty-two months would be impossible.

Whole tribes in Tibet, China, Africa, Australia, South America, and Mexico will never even hear about Antichrist during his entire reign — let alone be conquered and forced to change their religion.

Think of it this way: countless people today still haven't heard of Jesus Christ, World War I or II, or other major world events. The same will be true during Antichrist's time. He'll reign only over old Roman territory — northern Africa, southern Europe, and western Asia — organized as ten kingdoms.

Scripture teaches that even in the Millennium, multitudes will not have heard of God through Jesus Christ until Jewish missionaries go out from Jerusalem to tell them Christ reigns there (Isa. 2:2-4; 40:9; 52:7; 61:6; 66:18-21; Zech. 8:23; 14:16-21).

If many people haven't heard of Jesus Christ in over 1,900 years of the Age of Grace, many certainly won't hear of Antichrist in only 3½ years.

### 9. "Sheep nations" prove not everyone cooperates with Antichrist

If all nations cooperated in destroying Israel during the Tribulation, there wouldn't be any "sheep" nations to enter the Millennium, as Christ teaches in Mt. 25:31-46:

"When the Son of man shall come in his glory, and all the holy angels with him, then shall he sit upon the throne of his glory: and before him shall be gathered all nations: and he shall separate them one from another, as a shepherd divideth his sheep from the goats: and he shall set the sheep on his right hand, but the goats on his left. Then shall the King say unto them on his right hand, Come, ye blessed of my Father, inherit the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world . . . Then shall he say also unto them on his left hand, Depart from me, ye cursed, into everlasting fire, prepared for the devil and his angels"

lessons.title: The Marriage Supper, Second Advent, and Armageddon
lessons.content:
# The Marriage Supper, Second Advent, and Armageddon

## I. The Marriage Supper of the Lamb (Rev. 19:1-10)

This passage in Rev. 19:1-10 is what we call parenthetical — meaning it's inserted here in the sequence of Revelation, but it actually describes events happening in Heaven just *before* Christ returns to Earth. Let me explain why this matters.

While the text appears after the vial judgments, the Marriage Supper will actually take place in Heaven when all the Tribulation saints and the two witnesses are raptured at the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week. Christ's return happens at the seventh vial or shortly after, but the Marriage Supper occurs before He comes back. So Rev. 19:1-10 explains what's happening in Heaven right before Christ descends to Earth with His saints.

What's clear in this passage is that the multitudes in Heaven are praising God because He has already judged the "great whore," and the smoke from Literal Babylon's destruction is rising up at that very moment.

### The Identity of the "Great Whore"

Now, this "great whore" cannot be the same as the woman in Rev. 17. Here's why: that woman will have been destroyed 3½ years before this passage is fulfilled, so her smoke wouldn't still be ascending at this time.

This judgment comes directly from God Himself, who avenges the blood of His servants at her hand. The rejoicing we see here is actually in obedience to the command of Rev. 18:20, which calls "heaven, and ye saints, and ye apostles, and ye prophets" to rejoice over her judgment.

Her smoke ascends to God "for ever and ever" — a phrase used fourteen times in Revelation. This matches the eternal desolations of Literal Babylon described in Isa. 13:19-22; Jer. 50:13, 23, 39, 40; 51:26, 37, 62. This may actually be the one place where the eternal lake of fire will be visible to those living on the New Earth after the Millennium (Isa. 66:22-24; Rev. 14:9-11).

### Worship in Heaven

Next in this passage, we see the elders and living creatures worshipping God. This is the last time they appear in Revelation.

Then a voice from the throne calls out, "Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great." John then hears what sounds like a great multitude — like the roar of many waters and powerful thunder — saying, "Alleluia: for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth." After this, the angel shows John the Marriage Supper of the Lamb.

### The Bride Made Ready

The statement in Rev. 19:7, "His wife hath made herself ready," shows us that certain preparations are necessary. Think of it like a bride spending months preparing for her wedding day — there's intentional effort involved. This seems clear from her white robe, which represents the righteousness of the saints (Rev. 19:8).

We can't fully describe the wedding supper itself because Scripture doesn't give us those details. What we do know is that it will be a real banquet — as real as any ever held on Earth.

The Greek word for "marriage" actually means "marriage feast," proving this supper will be just as literal and real as any we know here. Compare Rev. 19:1-10 with Mt. 22:2; 25:10.

Don't let the scale of such a wedding supper trouble you — "with God all things are possible." If we can imagine thousands eating at a banquet here on Earth, we can understand on the same basis how innumerable companies will do so in Heaven. That food will be eaten in Heaven is clear from Lk. 22:16, 18, 30; Rev. 2:7, 17; 22:1-2; Ps. 78:25; Exod. 24:11.

### John's Mistake

When John witnessed these things, he fell down to worship the angel as he would worship God. But he was immediately stopped and told that the angel was also a redeemed person — a fellow-servant among the brethren the prophets, and those who have the testimony of Jesus, which is the spirit of prophecy.

## II. The Second Coming of Christ (Rev. 19:11-21)

> "And I saw heaven opened, and behold a white horse; and he that sat upon him was called Faithful and True, and in righteousness he doth judge and make war. His eyes were as a flame of fire, and on his head were many crowns; and he had a name written, that no man knew, but he himself. And he was clothed with a vesture dipped in blood: and his name is called The Word of God. And the armies which were in heaven followed him upon white horses, clothed in fine linen white and clean. And out of his mouth goeth a sharp sword, that with it he should smite the nations: and he shall rule them with a rod of iron: and he treadeth the winepress of the fierceness and wrath of Almighty God. And he hath on his vesture and on his thigh a name written, KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS" (Rev. 19:11-16).

The second coming of Christ is the central theme of Revelation. Let's walk through the key facts from Rev. 19:11-21:

> 1. He is coming from Heaven (Rev. 19:11, 14. Compare with Mt. 24:29-31; 2 Thess. 1:7 and Dan. 7:13, 14).

> 2. He is coming on a white horse (Rev. 19:11).

> 3. His appearance will be similar to the first vision in Revelation (Rev. 1:12-18). Five of the eight characteristics of Christ dealt with in Chapter Three of my book *Revelation Expounded* are listed here again (Rev. 19:12, 13).

> 4. His titles and names are "Faithful," "True," "The Word of God," and "KING OF KINGS AND LORD OF LORDS," plus a name that no one knows except Himself (Rev. 19:11-16).

> 5. He is coming with authority to make war upon the nations and judge them (Isa. 11:4; 49:2), to tread them in the fierceness of the winepress of God's wrath (Isa. 63:3; Rev. 14:17-20; 19:15), and to rule (shepherd) them with a rod of iron (Ps. 2:9. Compare with Rev. 2:27; 12:5; Ps. 149:7-9).

> 6. He will command the armies of Heaven, who follow Him on white horses, clothed in linen, white and clean — the righteousness of the saints (Rev. 9:8, 14).

### A Glorious Picture

These six points summarize Christ's glorious return with His saints and angels to defeat the dragon, the beast, and the false prophet with their armies, deliver Israel, and establish righteousness on Earth.

Picture this: there will never be a more glorious event or more beautiful sight than the armies of Heaven, clothed in spotless white, in perfect order and rank, following Christ on white horses, accompanied by heavenly angels — all ready to take vengeance on God's enemies. (For spirit horses in Heaven, see Lesson Six.)

### 1. The Fact of the Second Coming of Christ

> (1) Old Testament prophets who testified to the second coming include: "Enoch" (Gen. 5:21-24; Jude 14, 15); "Jacob" (Gen. 49:10); "Balaam" (Num. 24:7, 17-19); "Isaiah" (59:20; 63:1-5); "Jeremiah" (23:5, 6; 25:30-33); "Ezekiel" (34:23-29; 37:17-29; 43:7); "Daniel" (2:44, 45; 7:13, 14); "Hosea" (2:18-23; 3:4, 5); "Joel" (2:28-3:21); "Amos" (5:15-21); "Micah" (1:3, 4; 2:12, 13; 4:1–5:7); "Nahum" (1:5, 6); "Habakkuk" (2:13, 14); "Zephaniah" (1:14-18; 3:8, 9); "Haggai" (2:6, 7, 21-23; Heb. 12:25-29); "Zechariah" (2:10-13; 3:8; 6:12, 13; 8:3-23; 12:4-14; 13:1-9; 14:1-21); and "Malachi" (3:1–4:6).

> (2) New Testament prophets who testified include: "Jesus" (Mt. 16:27; 24:1-25:46; Lk. 17:22-37; 21:1-33); "Peter" (Acts 3:21; 2 Pet. 1:16; 3:3-9); "Paul" (Rom. 11:26, 27; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:1-8; Heb. 9:28); "John" (Rev. 1:7; 19:11-21); and "Jude" (Jude 14, 15).

> (3) The Lord's Supper declares the second coming (Lk. 22:19; 1 Cor. 11:26).

> (4) Angels declare it (Acts 1:10, 11. Compare Lk. 1:26-35; 2:8-18).

> (5) The Old Testament contains as many, if not more, references to Christ's second coming as to His first. The 260 chapters of the New Testament refer to it about 300 times. Every time you pray the Lord's Prayer, you're praying for His coming. Nearly every book in both Testaments directly or indirectly predicts His return to Earth.

### 2. Some False Theories of His Second Coming

Let's address some incorrect views people have held:

> (1) **That the Day of Pentecost was the second coming.** This doesn't hold up because the entire New Testament was written *after* Pentecost, and all the writers still spoke of a *future* coming of Christ. Jesus Himself said the Holy Spirit would be "another Comforter" — not a second coming of Himself (Jn. 14:16, 17, 26; 15:26, 27; 16:7-16).

> (2) **That the second coming happens at a sinner's conversion.** This can't be right because at conversion there's no literal coming of the sinner to Jesus or of Jesus to the sinner. Conversion is a spiritual work in the heart where the sinner repents.

> (3) **That death is the second coming.** This is absurd when you think about it — approximately three people die every two seconds; 90 every minute; 5,400 hourly; 129,600 daily; 907,200 weekly; and 47,304,000 annually.

> (4) **That His coming occurred at Jerusalem's destruction in 70 A.D.** This view is false because when Jesus comes, He will *restore* Jerusalem, not destroy it (Zech. 14:1-21; Lk. 21:24).

> (5) **That His coming is the preaching of the gospel.** This is certainly incorrect because gospel preaching has spanned many centuries, while His coming will happen suddenly (Mt. 24:27-51; 2 Thess. 1:7-10).

> (6) **That prophecies of His coming should be spiritualized with no literal return.** This would practically destroy God's Word and render it meaningless — just as the Jews did when they spiritualized prophecies of Christ's first coming, which were literally fulfilled (Lk. 1:31). If that verse was literally fulfilled in His first coming, then Lk. 1:32, 33 must be literally fulfilled in His second coming. It's a serious distortion of Scripture to make literal words mean only a spiritual coming. You'll see this clearly when you read through the passages in Point 1 above.

### 3. The Time of His Second Coming

Just because some people have foolishly tried and failed to set exact dates for Christ's return doesn't change the fact that He *will* come. Jesus Himself warned us against such date-setting (Mt. 24:32-51; 25:13).

While we can't know the exact day or hour, we *can* know that His coming is **pre-millennial**, not post-millennial. Christ is coming *before* the 1,000-year reign, not after it (Rev. 20:1-7).

**Postmillennialism is dangerous** because it substitutes human effort for God's work. This theory claims that mankind ushers in the Millennium through their own efforts, securing happiness without God's grace. It teaches that the organized Church will prosper until the whole world is converted — but in reality, the Church isn't as close to this as it was in apostolic times.

This view also claims Christ couldn't or wouldn't return while the world is sinful, and that once converted, humanity will invite Christ back to reign. This simply isn't what Scripture teaches. The purpose of this dispensation isn't world conversion — not everyone will believe. It's about calling out a people to serve as kings and priests with Christ during the Millennium and forever (Rev. 1:5, 6; 5:9, 10; 20:4; 22:5). The passages in Point 1 don't picture a converted world at Christ's coming.

### 4. Reasons for a Pre-Millennial Second Coming

> (1) The Antichrist, whose coming everyone agrees is pre-millennial, is destroyed by the brightness of Christ's coming (Rev. 19:11-21; 2 Thess. 2:8). This fixes Christ's coming as pre-millennial.

> (2) Christ comes "immediately after the Tribulation" (Mt. 24:27-31). The Tribulation occurs just before the Millennium (Mt. 24:15-31; Zech. 14).

> (3) When Christ comes, He separates the tares from the wheat. Since the Millennium follows this separation, He must come before the Millennium (Mt. 13:40-43).

> (4) Conditions like those in Lot's and Noah's days will exist when Christ comes (Mt. 24:37-51; Lk. 17:22-37; 2 Tim. 3; 4:1-3; 2 Pet. 3:2-5). These passages don't fit the post-millennial theory.

> (5) The Millennial kingdom will be a literal kingdom, not simply Church exaltation or a converted world continuing (Rev. 5:1, 10; 11:15; 20:1-10; Dan. 2:44, 45; 7:13, 14; Zech. 14).

> (6) The resurrections prove a pre-millennial coming — the righteous dead are raised before the Millennium to live and reign with Christ during it. The wicked dead aren't raised until after (Rev. 20:1-15).

> (7) When Christ comes, Satan is bound. Since Satan is bound during the Millennium, there can be no Millennium until Christ comes (Rev. 20:1-7).

> (8) Israel will be restored and regathered when Christ comes (Mt. 24:31), and since they're restored during the Millennium, Christ must come first (Ezek. 36:24-28; Isa. 11:1-16).

> (9) See Lesson Fifty, Point III, 5, for additional reasons.

So the timing of His return to Earth is immediately after Daniel's Seventieth Week and at the beginning of the Millennium (Rev. 19:11-21; Zech. 14; Mt. 24:27-31). It occurs at the start of "the day of the Lord," as all passages using this expression make clear.

The day of the Lord never refers to the rapture — it doesn't begin until after that event. Paul doesn't correct the early Christians for expecting the rapture, but he *does* rebuke them for expecting "the day of the Lord" at any moment (1 Thess. 5:1-11; 2 Thess. 2:1-12).

### 5. Signs of the Second Coming of Christ

Many events and prophecies must still be fulfilled before Christ returns to Earth. We've listed many of these signs in Lesson Forty-four, Point II.

From the signs now unfolding, we know we're only a few years from Christ's literal return. And if *that* event is near, how much nearer is the rapture, which occurs at least seven years before the second advent? We should expect the rapture at any time, but not the second advent until all its signs have come to pass.

### 6. The Manner of His Second Coming

Four Greek words explain how Christ will come. Let's look at each one with its key passages:

> (1) **Parousia** means "personal coming," "immediate presence," "arrival," "advent," or "return." It's used in Mt. 24:3, 27, 37, 39; 2 Thess. 2:8; 2 Pet. 3:4 — translated "coming" in each case, referring to Christ's personal appearance on Earth.

> (2) **Phaneros** means "to shine," "be apparent," "appear publicly," "be manifest," and "be seen." It's used in this connection only in Mt. 24:30.

> (3) **Erchomai** means "to go" or "to come." It's used generally for the second coming in Mt. 24:30, 42, 43, 48; 25:13, 19, 27, 31; Jn. 21:23; Acts 1:11; 1 Thess. 5:2; Jude 14; Rev. 1:7; etc. It's translated "come," "cometh," and "coming."

> (4) **Epiphaneia** means "advent," "appearing," "brightness," "to give light," or "become visible." It appears in 1 Tim. 6:14; 2 Tim. 4:1, 8; Titus 2:13; Heb. 9:28, translated "appear" and "appearing."

These passages reveal the following about how Christ will return:

> (1) He's coming "as the lightning cometh out of the east, and shineth even unto the west" (Mt. 24:27). This refers to the direction He'll come to Mount Olivet in His descent — the same place He departed (Isa. 63:1-5; Zech. 14:1-5).

> (2) He's coming as destruction to the ungodly, like the flood did (Mt. 24:38-51; 25:31-46; 1 Thess. 5:2; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:8; Jude 14, 15; Zech. 14).

> (3) He's coming visibly in the same way He left (Acts 1:11; Rev. 1:7).

> (4) He's coming with mighty brightness and fire (2 Thess. 1:7-10; 2:8; Ezek. 38:17-21; Mal. 4:1-6).

> (5) He's coming in vengeance to punish His enemies (Rev. 14:14-20; 19:11-21; Jude 14; 2 Thess. 1:7-10).

> (6) He's coming with power and great glory (Mt. 24:27-31).

> (7) He's coming with all His saints and angels (Rev. 19:11-21; Zech. 14:5; 2 Thess. 1:7-10; Jude 14, 15; Mt. 24:31).

> (8) He's coming with clouds (Rev. 1:7; Dan. 7:13, 14; Mt. 24:27-30; Rev. 19:11-21).

> (9) He's coming as Judge and King (Rev. 19:11-21; Isa. 11; Zech. 14; Mt. 25:31-46).

> (10) He's coming "as a thief in the night" (1 Thess. 5:2-4; 2 Pet. 3:10; Rev. 16:15).

These are the only passages where this phrase is used of the second advent and the day of the Lord. It's never used once for the rapture. The only other occurrence is a warning of judgment to the church at Sardis. Think of it like a homeowner who doesn't know when a thief might come — that's how sudden and unexpected Christ's return will be, as Mt. 24:36, 39, 42-51; 25:13 clearly shows.

## III. The Battle of Armageddon or the Supper of the Great God

The last part of Rev. 19 describes the Battle of Armageddon, also called "the supper of the great God." It's a mistake to think Armageddon is just another war between nations like traditional warfare.

When the last two world wars broke out, some newspapers printed headlines like "Armageddon!" or "Is it Armageddon?" Such speculation shows a failure to understand our times and what prophecy actually says. To answer any question about Armageddon, we must consult the Bible — it's exclusively a Bible theme. Armageddon follows the fulfillment of everything in Revelation up to chapter 19.

### 1. The Place Where Armageddon Will Be Fought

The word "Armageddon" appears only once in Scripture. It's a place name where the greatest battle of all time will be fought. The battle itself, under different terms, is mentioned many times.

This battleground is where the three unclean spirits gather the nations for battle (Rev. 16:13-16). Armageddon is also called "the valley of Jehoshaphat" in other passages, identifying it as the area extending from Mount Carmel southeast to Jerusalem (Joel 3).

"Armageddon" comes from two Hebrew words: *har*, meaning "a mountain or range of hills, hill country," and *Megiddo*, meaning "rendezvous." Combined (Har-Megiddo), they refer to the Hill of Megiddo on the south side of the valley of Megiddo or Esdraelon, southeast of Mount Carmel (2 Chron. 35:22; Zech. 12:11).

"Megiddo" was the capital of a portion of Canaan that fell to Joshua (Josh. 12:21; 17:11; Judg. 1:27). It sits at the entrance to a pass across the Carmel mountain range, on the main highway between Asia and Africa — the key position between the Euphrates and the Nile. It's been a battlefield throughout many ages. Thothmes III, founder of the old Egyptian empire, said, "Megiddo is worth a thousand cities."

This will likely be Antichrist's headquarters when he comes from the north after conquering Russia and countries north and east of the old Roman Empire. Rev. 16:13-16 says, "And he gathered them together into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon."

There he'll await Christ's return from Heaven, expecting Him to set foot on the Mount of Olives and deliver Israel when half of Jerusalem is taken (Zech. 14:1-5). The devil "knoweth that he hath but a short time" (Rev. 12:12), and it will be common knowledge to Antichrist and others that Christ is expected at the end of the 1,260 days of Rev. 11:1-3; 12:6, 14; 13:5.

By some means, Antichrist will have lost control of Jerusalem toward the end of the Week. While he's been directing armies against northern and eastern countries (Dan. 11:44), the Jews will regain control of the city. The two witnesses will help the remnant of Israel recapture Jerusalem through their miraculous powers. At any rate, Zech. 14 makes clear that Antichrist returns from the north to gather nations against the Jews and Jerusalem by the time Christ returns.

Ezek. 38 and 39 also show that after Antichrist conquers Russia, Germany, and other northern and eastern countries, he returns with armies from these newly conquered lands — along with the ten kingdoms and cooperating nations — to destroy Israel and stop Christ from establishing His kingdom.

These Ezekiel chapters are often interpreted as proving Antichrist will come from Russia because he's "the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal" who leads Russia and Germany south against Palestine. But Dan. 11:44 proves Antichrist first *conquers* these northern and eastern countries before becoming their chief prince and leading them against the Jews.

### 2. The Time Armageddon Will Be Fought

> (1) When Israel is safe in the wilderness and "Sheba, and Dedan, and the merchants of Tarshish, with all the young lions thereof" — identified as Edom, Moab, chief of Ammon's children, including Arabian chiefs (Dan. 11:41; Rev. 12:6, 14-17) — ask, "Art thou come to take a spoil? . . . to carry away silver and gold, to take away cattle and goods, to take a great spoil?" (Ezek. 38:1-16).

> (2) When Antichrist has completed conquering Russia, Germany, and other countries north and east of his ten-kingdom empire (Dan. 11:44; Ezek. 38:1-16).

> (3) At Christ's second coming to deliver the Jews and Jerusalem from Antichrist's armies (Joel 3; Zech. 14; Isa. 63:1-6; Jude 14; 2 Thess. 1:7-10).

> (4) Immediately after the Tribulation (Mt. 24:29-31; 25:31-46).

> (5) After the Marriage Supper of the Lamb (Rev. 19:1-21).

> (6) When Satan is bound for 1,000 years (Rev. 19:11; 20:3).

> (7) When Jerusalem is surrounded by nations' armies and half the city is taken (Zech. 14:1-15; Rev. 14:14-21; 16:13-16).

> (8) Just before the Millennium (Rev. 19:11–20:3).

> (9) At this age's end (Mt. 13:40-43; 25:31-46).

> (10) When God establishes His earthly kingdom (Dan. 2:44; 7:13, 14, 18-27).

> (11) When the first resurrection is completed (Rev. 19:11–20:6).

> (12) At the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week (Dan. 9:27; Rev. 13:5; 19:11-21).

> (13) Forty-two months after Antichrist gains power over the ten kingdoms (Rev. 13:5; 19:11-21).

> (14) When people think universal peace has arrived because Antichrist has conquered much of the world (1 Thess. 5:1-3).

> (15) At the beginning of the day of the Lord (2 Thess. 2:1-12; 1 Thess. 5:1-3; Rev. 19:11-21; 20:1-3).

### 3. The Combatants at Armageddon

This won't be an ordinary battle between earthly nations. It's a battle between Heaven's armies under Christ and Earth's armies under the dragon, the beast, and false prophet.

**On Christ's side:** earthly Israel (Zech. 14:1-15), God's angels (Mt. 25:31-45; 2 Thess. 1:7-10), and resurrected saints of all ages (Zech. 14:1-5; Jude 14; Rev. 19:11-21).

**On Antichrist's side:** the devil with his angels and demons (Rev. 12:7-12; 16:13-16; Rev. 20:1-3), the ten kings (Rev. 17:14-17; Dan. 2:44; 7:19-27), countries north and east of the ten kingdoms recently conquered by Antichrist (Dan. 11:44; Ezek. 38; 39; Rev. 16:12), and many other nations cooperating through the three unclean frogs' ministry (Rev. 16:13-16; Zech. 14:1-5, 16; Ezek. 38; 39).

### 4. The Purpose of Armageddon

**God's purposes:** To deliver Israel from total destruction by Antichrist and his allied nations (Zech. 14; Isa. 63:1-10). To punish these nations for persecuting the Jews (Mt. 25:31-46). To establish an earthly kingdom with Christ as head (Dan. 7:13, 14; Lk. 1:32). To rid Earth of rebellion and restore God's dominion as before the Fall (1 Cor. 15:24-29; Eph. 1:10). To give mankind one more dispensational test before destroying every rebel (Eph. 1:10; Rev. 20:1-10). To establish the eternal perfect state (Rev. 21:1–22:5; 2 Pet. 3:10-13).

**Man's and Satan's purposes:** To stop God's plan of taking over earthly governments and to avert their own impending doom if defeated (Rev. 12:12; 19:19-21; 20:1-10; Zech. 14:1-5).

### 5. The Length of the Battle of Armageddon

According to Zech. 14:1-14, the battle lasts only one day: "The Lord my God shall come, and all the saints with thee. And it shall come to pass in that day, that the light shall not be clear, nor dark: but it shall be one day which shall be known to the Lord, not day, nor night: but it shall come to pass, that at evening time it shall be light."

It's similar to a decisive military strike — the outcome isn't in doubt, and it's over swiftly.

### 6. The Results of Armageddon

> (1) Total defeat of Earth's armies and Satan's spirit forces (Isa. 24:21-23; 25:7; 63:1-6; Rev. 19:19–20:3; Ezek. 38; 39).

> (2) All vast armies destroyed except "the sixth part" (Ezek. 39:2; Rev. 19:19-21; Zech. 14:1-15; Joel 3).

> (3) These armies will provide carcasses for fowls to eat for up to seven months (Ezek. 39:4-24; Rev. 19:17-21; Mt. 24:27, 28, 40-42; Lk. 17:34-37; Job 39:27-30).

> (4) The beast and false prophet cast into the Lake of Fire (Rev. 19:20; Dan. 7:11; 8:25; 11:45; 2 Thess. 2:8, 9).

> (5) The devil and his angels cast into the bottomless pit (Rev. 20:1-7).

> (6) Blood flowing up to horses' bridles (Rev. 14:14-20; Ezek. 39:17-24).

> (7) Israel delivered and vindicated, God's eternal kingdom established (Mt. 25:31-46; Dan. 2:44; 7:18, 23-27; Rev. 11:15; 20:1-10; 21:2–22:5).

### 7. Ways Antichrist's Armies Will Be Destroyed

> (1) By the brightness of Christ's coming (2 Thess. 2:8).

> (2) By angels (Mt. 24:27-31; 2 Thess. 1:7-10).

> (3) By the saints (Zech. 14:5; Jude 14; Rev. 17:14; 19:11-21).

> (4) By hail and rain from Heaven (Ezek. 38:22; Rev. 16:21).

> (5) By the Jews (Zech. 12:1–13:1; 14:14).

> (6) By beast's armies slaying each other (Ezek. 38:21;

lessons.title: The Dispensation of Divine Government
lessons.content:
## THE DISPENSATION OF DIVINE GOVERNMENT

### THE MILLENNIUM
(Rev 19:11–20:15)

### THE AGE TO COME (Gen. 19:11-15)

THE AGE TO COME is that stretch of time from Christ's second advent all the way to the New Heaven and New Earth. It contains just one dispensation—the Millennium. This will be the final age before the eternal perfect state, when God will once again be all-in-all, just as He was before rebellion ever entered the universe.

In Rev. 20:1-15, we get a panoramic view of what's coming: Satan's expulsion from Earth, Christ's thousand-year reign with His saints, Satan's brief post-millennial career and ultimate doom, and the final judgment of all.

### I. The Expulsion of Satan From the Earth

"And I saw an angel come down from heaven, having the key of the bottomless pit and a great chain in his hand. And he laid hold on the dragon, that old serpent, which is the devil, and Satan, and bound him a thousand years. And cast him into the bottomless pit, and shut him up, and set a seal upon him, that he should deceive the nations no more, till the thousand years should be fulfilled: and after that he must be loosed a little season" (Rev. 20:1-3).

This passage picks up right where Rev. 19:11-21 left off—after the Battle of Armageddon—and shows us the dragon being confined to the Abyss. Once Armageddon is over, an angel will descend from Heaven carrying the key to the Abyss. This angel is explained under the fifth trumpet in Lesson Forty-one, and the Abyss itself is covered in Lesson Thirty-seven.

Now, you might wonder: how can an angel or spirit be bound by a literal chain and thrown into a material place? It only makes sense when you remember that angels have bodies and can be localized and confined to physical locations, as we saw in Lesson Six. Think of it like this: even though angels are spiritual beings, they still have form and substance—they're not just abstract ideas floating around.

If this weren't true, how could the demons currently bound in the Abyss be released under the fifth and sixth trumpets? How could angels be confined to Tartarus in chains (2 Pet. 2:4; Jude 6, 7)? And how will all wicked men, demons, fallen angels, and rebellious creatures of every kind be confined in the Lake of Fire forever? (Mt. 25:41; Rev. 14:9-11; 19:20; 20:10-15).

This angel mentioned in Rev. 20:1-3 will lay hold of Satan, overpower him through actual combat, bind him with a great chain, and cast him into the Abyss for a thousand years. Then the angel will "set a seal upon him"—literally sealing the Abyss over Satan to keep him there so he cannot deceive the nations until the Millennium is finished.

So we see that Satan is a literal person, his doom is literal, he will be bound by a literal angel with a literal chain, cast into a literal Abyss, and sealed with a literal seal for the duration of the Millennium.

In Rev. 12, we learned that the dragon and his angels will be cast out of Heaven to Earth in the middle of the Week. They'll remain there until the Battle of Armageddon, when they'll fight against Christ and His angels and saints. Scripture doesn't explicitly state whether Satan's angels will be cast into the Abyss with him. However, Isa. 24:21-22 and 25:7 make it clear they will be cast into the pit with Satan and will be loosed with him "after many days" to help him deceive the nations once more (Rev. 20:1-10).

### II. The Millennial Reign of Christ and His Saints

"And I saw thrones and they sat upon them, and judgment was given unto them: and I saw the souls of them that were beheaded for the witness of Jesus, and for the word of God, and which had not worshipped the beast, neither his image, neither had received his mark upon their foreheads, or in their hands; and they lived and reigned with Christ a thousand years. But the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished. This is the first resurrection. Blessed and holy is he that hath part in the first resurrection: on such the second death hath no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign with him a thousand years" (Rev. 20:4-6).

This passage reveals that the Tribulation martyrs will also share in Christ's reign during the Millennium and forever. Even if no other passage in the Bible taught the doctrine of the Millennium, this one alone would be sufficient. The word "millennium" simply means "one thousand years"—a term repeated six times in just the first seven verses of Rev. 20.

Before we explore what other Scriptures say about the Millennium, let's note what Rev. 20 itself tells us:

> 1. Satan must be bound before the Millennium can begin (Rev. 20:1-10). This clearly implies that all the events of Rev. 4-19 must be fulfilled first. Therefore, the thousand years cannot arrive until these things come to pass.

> 2. Satan will be bound during the Millennium (Rev. 20:3).

> 3. After Satan's binding, John saw "thrones" and their occupants (Rev. 20:4). The ones sitting on these thrones will be the Tribulation martyrs. They will reign as kings and priests with Christ, just as all other redeemed heavenly peoples will.

> 4. In Rev. 20:5, John shows that the Tribulation martyrs have a part in the first resurrection, which occurs before the Millennium and includes all the different companies of the redeemed, saved, and resurrected from Adam to the binding of Satan. This verse also implies that the Tribulation saints will be the last redeemed company to be resurrected and translated. This first resurrection ends with the rapture of this company and of the two witnesses. All other passages on resurrections in Scripture—except a few on the rapture of the Church—speak of the first and second resurrections as being one event occurring at the same time. However, Rev. 20:5 and 1 Cor. 15:20-23, 51-58; Phil. 3:10-14; 1 Thess. 4:13-17; 2 Thess. 2:1, 6-8; 2 Cor. 5:1-6; Eph. 5:26, 27; Heb. 11:35; 1 Jn. 3:1-3 speak of a resurrection from out of the dead—the dead and living saints up to the rapture, plus only those who get saved and die in the Tribulation. In Rev. 20:11-15, the wicked dead—who have no part in the first resurrection and won't be resurrected until after the thousand years—are pictured standing before the great white throne to be judged. This will be the second resurrection, including all the wicked dead from Adam to the end of the Millennium.

> 5. Next, a blessing is pronounced upon all who have part in the first resurrection, for "on such the second death [the Lake of Fire, Rev. 2:11; 19:20; 20:10-15; 21:8] hath no power, but they shall be priests of God and of Christ, and shall reign one thousand years" (Rev. 20:6).

> 6. "When the thousand years are expired, Satan shall be loosed out of his prison. And shall go out to deceive the nations which are in the four quarters of the earth, Gog and Magog, to gather them" against God (Rev. 20:7-10). Then fire will come down from Heaven to devour his armies, and Satan himself will be cast into the Lake of Fire—where the beast and false prophet are and have been for the entire thousand years (Rev. 19:20; 20:7-10).

> 7. Then the wicked dead will be resurrected, and the final judgment will take place. After that comes the renovation of the heavens and Earth, resulting in a New Heaven and a New Earth, as pictured in Rev. 21-22.

Now, with these basic facts in mind, we can better understand the many other Scriptures that speak of the Messiah's reign without distinguishing between His first thousand years and His reign forever. Beyond what Rev. 20 tells us, here's a brief study of the subject.

### III. General Truths About the Millennium

**1. Definition of the Millennium**

This dispensation is called Divine Government because God Himself—along with the Son and Holy Spirit—will establish a divine government on Earth over all nations forever. The first thousand years of God's reign is called the Millennium, which simply means "one thousand years." It comes from *mille* (thousand) and *annum* (year).

Scriptural terms for this age include:

> (1) "The thousand years" reign of Christ (Rev. 20:1-10).

> (2) "The dispensation of the fulness of times" (Eph. 1:10).

> (3) "The day of the Lord" (Isa. 2:12; 13:6, 9; 34:8; Ezek. 30:3; Amos 5:18; Joel 2:1; Zeph. 1:7, 8, 18; 2:2-3; Zech. 14:1-21; Mal. 4; 1 Thess. 5:2; 2 Thess. 2:1-8; 2 Pet. 3:10).

> (4) "That day" (Isa. 2:11; 4:1-6; 19:21; 24:21; 26:1; Ezek. 39:22; 48:35; Hos. 2:18; Joel 3:18; Zech. 12:8-11; 13:1; 14:1-9; Mal. 3:17).

> (5) "The world [age] to come" (Mt. 12:32; Mk. 10:30; Lk. 20:35; Eph. 1:21; 2:7-14).

> (6) "The kingdom of Christ and of God" (Mk. 14:25; Lk. 10:11; 22:14-18).

> (7) "The kingdom of heaven" (Mt. 3:2; 4:17; 5:3, 10, 19, 20; 7:21; 8:11; 10:7; 13:43; 18:1-4; Lk. 19:12-15).

> (8) "The kingdom of God" (Mk. 14:25; Lk. 10:11; 22:14-18).

> (9) "The regeneration" (Mt. 19:28; Eph. 1:10).

> (10) "The times of the restitution [restoration] of all things" (Acts 3:20-21).

> (11) "The consolation of Israel" (Lk. 2:25).

> (12) "The redemption of Jerusalem" (Lk. 2:38).

**2. Length of the Millennium (Rev. 20:1-10)**

This dispensation will last 1,000 years (Rev. 20:1-10). The expression "thousand years" appears six times in just this one passage. Although the length is mentioned only here, the age is referenced throughout Scripture, as we'll see.

It will begin with the binding of Satan at Christ's return to Earth to reestablish David's throne and set up God's kingdom (Mt. 24:29-31; Rev. 19:11–20:7). It will end with the loosing of Satan, the last rebellion, the renovation of Earth by fire, and the Great White Throne Judgment (Rev. 20:11-15; 2 Pet. 3:8-13).

One thing we must emphasize: these 1,000 years are only the *first* thousand years of Christ's reign on Earth. He will continue to reign over the same kingdom eternally. The first thousand years serve the express purpose of ridding Earth of all rebellion, destroying all enemies, abolishing death and sin, and restoring this rebellious part of God's universal kingdom to its rightful place over all other creations—just as it was before sin entered through Lucifer and Adam.

In this Millennial Age, God will gather together all things in Christ, both the things in heaven and on Earth (1 Cor. 15:24-28; Eph. 1:10; Rev. 22:4-5).

**3. The Favorable Beginning**

In this age, humanity will have a more favorable beginning than in any other dispensation. Besides enjoying many wonderful benefits from previous ages, people will have the God of Heaven as their ruler and experience all the privileges that such rulership brings. The following points about divine government and the blessed conditions on Earth during the Millennium will clearly reveal just how favorable this dispensation will be.

**4. The Kingdom Foretold**

God's covenants with Abraham and David guaranteed Israel an everlasting earthly kingdom and established them as the channel of blessings to all families of the Earth. It seemed these covenants and promises might fail when Israel divided into two kingdoms around 1009 B.C.

After this split, God raised up many prophets who continued emphasizing that He would still fulfill His promises to Israel—though He would have to use different means than originally planned, given their continued rebellion against His righteous requirements.

Sixteen of these prophets left writings concerning the coming King and kingdom. Notice in the following passages that because of Israel's rebellion, they would go into captivity and be scattered among the nations. They would remain "many days" without a king, a sacrifice, or knowledge of the true God. Afterward, they would be gathered back to their own land but brought very low through Gentile oppression. Then they would be delivered from the nations by the Messiah, who would come from glory to set up the kingdom (Isa. 1:2-9, 25-28; 2:2-5; 9:6-7; 11:2-16; 27:12-13; 32:1-5, 15-19; 34:1-17; 63:1-6; Jer. 33:17-26; Ezek. 24:11-30; 36:16-38; 37:1-28; Dan. 7:12-27; 8:16-27; 9:24-27; 11:36–12:13; Hos. 2:14-23; 3:4-5; Joel 2:28–3:21; Mic. 4:1-13; 5:1-15; Zech. 8:1–14:21; Mal. 3:1–4:6; Lk. 1:30, 35; 21:20-24; Acts 15:13-17; Rom. 11:25-27; Rev. 1:5; 5:10; 11:15; 20:1-4).

Men looked for this kingdom through the ages (Heb. 11; 2 Sam. 7; Isa. 9:6-7; Mt. 4; Mk. 15:43; Heb. 12:25-28; Acts 3:19-21).

**5. When Will Christ's Kingdom Be Set Up?**

> (1) At the return of the King from glory (Mt. 25:31-46; Isa. 9:6-7; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14; 8:18-22; Zech. 14; 1 Thess. 1:7; Jude 14; Rev. 17:14; 19:11–20:7).

> (2) After the Church is raptured (1 Cor. 15:51-58; 1 Thess. 4:13-17), for the Church comes back to Earth with Christ to help Him set up the kingdom and reign over the nations (Zech. 14:1-5; Jude 14; Rev. 1:4-5; 5:10; 17:14; 19:11-21). It is not until after the days of the Church that Christ comes to rebuild the house of David (Acts 15:13-18; Isa. 9:6-7; 11:11; Hos. 3:4-5; Lk. 1:32-35). The Church is raptured before the Antichrist comes (2 Thess. 2:7-8), and Antichrist makes his appearance before Christ comes (2 Thess. 2:1-6), so Christ's kingdom cannot be established until these events take place.

> (3) After the future tribulation, for Christ does not come to Earth with the saints until then (Mt. 24:15-31; Zech. 14:1-21; Dan. 12:1-13; Rev. 19:11-21).

> (4) After the great apostasy and the revelation of the Antichrist, for Antichrist is destroyed at Christ's coming to Earth, so he must be here when Christ comes (2 Thess. 2:1-12; Rev. 19:11-21; Dan. 7:18-27; 8:16-27; 9:27; 11:36–12:13).

> (5) At the time the Antichrist is destroyed and Satan is bound for 1,000 years (Rev. 5:10; 20:1-7). During the Church Age and the future tribulation period, Satan is loose (1 Pet. 5:8; Rev. 12:12-17; 13:1-8; 20:1-7). The devil is still loose, so we are still in the Church Age and will remain so until Christ comes to bind him.

> (6) After the first resurrection, for the saints reign with Christ on Earth for 1,000 years (Rev. 5:9-10; 20:1-6). Therefore, the saints must first be resurrected before they can reign with Him. This present time is the period of suffering for Christ, not the period of reigning with Him (Rom. 8:18; 1 Cor. 15:20-58; 2 Cor. 5:6; Phil. 1:23; 3:20-21; Col. 1:24; 2 Tim. 2:12; 3:12). The 1,000 years fall between the resurrection of the righteous and that of the wicked (Rev. 20:4-6, 11-15).

> (7) At the time Ezekiel's temple is built (Ezek. 40:1–43:7). Christ's reign will be established in Jerusalem in Ezekiel's temple (Ezek. 43:7), so the kingdom cannot be set up until then. Christ will build this temple when He comes, but the kingdom naturally cannot be established in the temple until it exists (Isa. 9:6-7; 52:1-8; 62:6-14; Ezek. 36:24-36; 41:1; 43:7; Zech. 6:12-13; 14:1-21; Lk. 1:32-35; Acts 15:13-18).

> (8) After Israel is gathered back from all countries (Ezek. 20:33-36; 36:17-38; 37:1-28; Hos. 3:4-5; Dan. 9:27).

> (9) When the Jews are delivered from the armies of the nations and become a blessing to all the families of the Earth (Gen. 12:1-3; Ps. 2:6-8; Isa. 9:6-7; 25:6-9; Zech. 9:9-11; 14:1-21; Acts 15:13-18; Lk. 1:32-35).

> (10) In the days of the ten kings of Revised Rome and Revived Grecia (Dan. 2:40-45; 7:18-28; Rev. 17:8-18).

> (11) After all of Rev. 4-19 is fulfilled, for Christ comes in Rev. 19-20 to set up the kingdom.

According to all these facts, the kingdom is still future and will follow all these events. Christ Himself taught in the following passages that the kingdom would follow His second advent (Mt. 16:21-27; 19:28; 20:20-23; 23:37-39; 24:3-31; 25:31-46; 26:29, 64; Acts 1:6-7; 3:19-21; Jn. 14:3; Lk. 9:26; 19:11-27; Rev. 5:9-10; 11:15; 20:1-7).

The apostles taught that the kingdom was a future event following Christ's second coming (Acts 1:7-11; 1 Pet. 1:7; 5:4; 2 Pet. 1:16; 3:3-4; Jas. 5:7; 1 Jn. 2:28; Jude 14; 1 Tim. 6:14-15).

**6. Will It Be a Literal Earthly Kingdom?**

The kingdom will be just as earthly and literal as any other kingdom that has ever existed on Earth. It will be the ninth in the following sequence of kingdoms mentioned in Daniel and Revelation: Egypt, Assyria, Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, Rome, Revised Rome, Revived Greece, and the kingdom of Heaven (Isa. 9:6-7; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14, 17-27; Zech. 14; Rev. 17:8-18).

Since all the preceding kingdoms have been literal, the kingdom of Heaven must also be literal. The following studies will further demonstrate that this kingdom will be a literal kingdom on Earth.

The Seventh Day Adventists and others teach that Earth will be desolate during the 1,000 years and that this Earth is the bottomless pit where the devil will be bound. There is not one word of truth in this theory. The bottomless pit is a department in the underworld for the confinement of certain demon and angelic spirits. That the Earth will not be desolate during this period is proven by the Scriptures in the points below.

**7. What Will Be the Form of Government?**

The government in the Millennium will not be monarchic, democratic, or autocratic, but theocratic—that is, God reigning through:

> (1) Jesus Christ, His only begotten Son (2 Sam. 7; Ps. 2; 89:35-37; Isa. 2:2-4; 4:2-3; 9:6-7; 11:1-15; 16:5; 24:23; 32:1-4; 40:9-10; 42:1-4; 52:7; Jer. 23:5-8; Ezek. 43:7; Dan. 2:44-45; 7:13-14; Mic. 4; 5:1-7; Zech. 6:12-13; 14:1-21; Mt. 25:31-46; Lk. 1:32-35; Rev. 11:15; 20:1-10).

> (2) David, the king of Israel (Jer. 30:9; Ezek. 34:24; 37:24-28; Hos. 3:4-5).

> (3) The Apostles and all saints from Adam to the Millennium—that is, all who have part in the first resurrection (Ps. 149:5-9; Dan. 7:18-27; Mt. 19:28; 1 Cor. 4:8; 6:2; Eph. 2:7; 2 Tim. 2:12; Heb. 11; Rom. 8:17; 2 Thess. 1:4-7; Rev. 1:6; 2:26-27; 5:9-10; 11:18; 12:5; 20:4-6; 22:5). All saints will be judged and rewarded according to the deeds done in the body and will be given places of rulership according to their degree of rewards—not according to which company of the redeemed they belong to, or which age they were redeemed in. This is the only true and just basis for giving rewards.

> Think of it like this: If some New Testament saint were automatically rewarded and given a greater place in the kingdom than some Old Testament saint simply because he was redeemed in a different age, that would be unjust. But if all are rewarded "according to their works" regardless of when they were redeemed, then God cannot be accused of injustice or of being a respecter of persons. It's up to every person to make good in their own age and prove true to their particular test.

> From the passages in Points (2) and (3), we learn that David will have a greater rulership than any one of the apostles. He is to be king over all Israel under Christ, while the apostles will each have only one tribe.

> Some Old Testament saints did more for God and had more power than the average New Testament saint. Take an ordinary professed follower of Christ today and compare his life and works with Enoch, Abraham, Moses, Elijah, or Elisha. Should the New Testament person receive a greater degree of reward and responsibility in the eternal kingdom than these men just because he's saved in a different age? That wouldn't be just, and it contradicts the doctrine of personal reward according to a person's works, as proven in Prov. 24:12; Ps. 62:12; Jer. 31:16; Rom. 14:12; 2 Cor. 5:10-11; 1 Cor. 3:11-15; Jas. 2:21-25; Rev. 14:12; 20:12-15; 22:12.

> Certain classes of people will not have part in reigning with Christ (Mt. 18:1-13; Lk. 9:62; 14:27; Jn. 3:3-5; Rom. 8:9; 1 Cor. 6:9-10; Gal. 5:19-21; Eph. 5:5; 2 Tim. 2:12; Heb. 12:14; 1 Jn. 3:10; Rev. 20:15).

> (4) Earthly judges and kings will be restored, but as subordinates to the heavenly rulers (Isa. 1:25-28; Ezek. 43:7-12; Rev. 21:24).

**8. Where Will the Seat of Government Be?**
