Jerusalem, rebuilt and restored to a greater glory than ever before, will be the seat of government, the world capital, and the center of worship forever (1 Chron. 23:25; 2 Chron. 33:4-7; Ps. 48:8; Isa. 2:2-4; 11:11–12:6; Jer. 17:25; Ezek. 34:1-35; 43:7; Joel 3:17, 20; Mic. 4:7; Zech. 8:3-23; 14:1-21; 15:1-18).

**9. What Will Be the Territorial Extent of the Kingdom?**

It will be worldwide and will forever "increase" in population and blessings (Isa. 9:6-7; 11:9; Ps. 72:8; 97:9; Dan. 7:13-14; Mic. 4:1-3; Zech. 9:10; 14:1-21; Rev. 11:15).

**10. Who Will and Will Not Be the Subjects of the Kingdom?**

Various classes of people will be excluded from the kingdom (Mt. 5:20; 13:49, 50; 24:45-51; 25:25-28, 31-46; Rev. 14:9-12). Some people from all nations currently on Earth will continue as natural people in the kingdom forever. "All people, nations, and languages should serve him: his dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away, and his kingdom that which shall not be destroyed" (Dan. 7:13-14, 18, 22-27; Isa. 9:6-7; Zech. 14:1-21; Lk. 1:32-35; Rev. 11:15).

After the Battle of Armageddon and the judgment of the nations at Christ's return, many will be left from all nations. These survivors will go up from year to year to worship the Lord of hosts and keep the Feast of Tabernacles. These people and their children will populate the Earth during the Millennium (Isa. 2:1-2; Zech. 14:16-21; Mt. 25:31-46; Rev. 11:15; 20:1-10).

The Russellists teach that Christ's atonement was for Adam only—that one man could only atone for one man, that all the sons of Adam must remain dead until the Millennium when they'll be resurrected and given a second chance, and that all who won't accept God during this second probation will be annihilated. This is one of the most erroneous and unscriptural teachings ever set forth.

It denies the very heart of the gospel, for Jesus died for every person, as proven in Mt. 1:21; 26:29; Lk. 24:47; Jn. 1:12-16; 3:5, 16-19; 6:37-40; 14:6; Acts 2:38; 4:12; Rom. 1:16; 5:6-11, 15-21; 6:23; 8:1-4, 32; 2 Cor. 5:14-21; Eph. 1:7; Col. 1:14, 20-22; Heb. 1:3; 2:9-10; 9:11-28; 10:4-22, 29; 1 Pet. 1:18-23; 1 Jn. 1:7.

What is it in all these passages that makes God just in forgiving everyone today who repents and believes the gospel? It is the one sacrifice of Christ for all people of all times—not for Adam alone. If one man could only atone for one man's sins, then to be redeemed, every person ever born would need God to die for them individually. God would then have to do what He did in Christ a countless number of times. Such a thing is absurd.

If people are going to have another chance in the Millennium, the basis of their reconciliation must be the same as it is now. Thus, their teaching that Christ died for one man only is contradicted by their teaching that all will have a second chance during the Millennium. Otherwise, how are they to be saved then unless Christ dies separately for each individual, as they teach in the doctrine of the atonement?

The Bible teaches that all the righteous dead will be resurrected before the Millennium and the wicked dead after that period (Rev. 20:4-15). So how could the wicked be resurrected and given a second chance during the Millennium if they're not to rise until after the Millennium?

Nowhere does the Bible teach that people have a second chance. On the contrary, it says, "it is appointed unto men once to die, but after this the judgment" (Heb. 9:27). The Bible does not say, "after this a second chance, and then annihilation if they fail again." The Bible does not teach annihilation for anyone, as is abundantly proven in Lesson Thirty-nine. During their natural lifetime, people have hundreds of chances to get right with God, and if they fail to do so, they will be cut off "without remedy" (Prov. 29:1).

So it's clear that the subjects of the Millennial Kingdom will not be the wicked dead raised to life after the Millennium, but people from the nations who will be on Earth at Christ's coming with the saints to set up the kingdom (Isa. 2:1-4; 11:11; 66:17-21; Zech. 14:16).

The Adventists, instead of believing there will be inhabitants on Earth in the Millennium, teach that Earth will be desolate of all human, animal, and plant life during this period. They teach the devil will be bound on Earth at this time, but this is disproved by the facts of Rev. 20:1-15, as listed in Point II above.

**11. Will There Be Laws for the Subjects of the Kingdom?**

There will be laws in this kingdom for the same purpose as in any other kingdom. There are laws even in Heaven and throughout the universe. Adam was given laws to keep before he fell. People will have laws to keep in all eternity after mankind

lessons.title: The New Heaven and the New Earth
lessons.content:
# The New Heaven and the New Earth

**(Rev. 21:1–22:5)**

## The Earth's Third and Eternal Sinless Career

Let's explore together one of the most breathtaking visions in all of Scripture — the eternal state that God has planned for His creation. We're talking about the Age of Ages, an endless succession of ages stretching into forever. This is when the Earth enters its third perfect state, a sinless career under the leadership of redeemed rulers across countless generations.

---

## I. The Dispensation of the Redeemed

Throughout our studies, we've traced God's seven dispensational periods — His different ways of dealing with fallen humanity. And what stands out in every single one is God's incredible patience and mercy. He's "not willing that any should perish, but that all should come to repentance" (2 Pet. 3:9).

Here's what God has been doing: He's been testing humanity under every possible condition before the final restoration of all things. Why? To demonstrate that He's completely just in requiring all free wills to submit to Him — because that's the only path to eternal security and true happiness.

Think of it like a parent who lets a stubborn child try things their own way, knowing they'll eventually learn that Mom and Dad were right all along. God has given humanity plenty of time to learn that we're failures apart from His grace and help.

After the Millennium, God will usher in this new eternal perfect state for the benefit of all generations to come. By that point, it will be the right time in His plan to destroy all rebels, sin, and death — and to remove the curse entirely. His plan is to take everyone who has willingly submitted to Him, everyone whose will has been purged of any possibility of rebellion through grace, and restore them to that original purpose: replenishing the Earth and subduing it, just like before the Fall.

So you see, the fall of humanity didn't derail God's eternal plan — it just delayed it. By the end of the Millennium, God will have used the Fall and all His dispensational dealings to actually advance and perfect His plan. Countless people will have been redeemed and glorified as immortal beings to help administer the affairs of the universe and rule over coming generations.

Here's the beautiful part: humanity will no longer be on probation. The testing will be complete. Those who've proven themselves faithful will be ready for an eternal state where they'll continue living faithfully to God forever.

We can call this age "The Dispensation of the Redeemed" because Earth's government will be administered by the redeemed — and it will succeed forever. No more dispensational failures. Both the redeemed and natural humanity will have been purged of any possibility of failure.

---

## II. The New Heaven and the New Earth (Rev. 21:1–22:5)

**(End of the Earth's Second Sinful Career — the Earth Made Perfect the Third Time)**

The New Heaven and New Earth are the result of the renovation by fire described in 2 Pet. 3:10-13. When the old sinful order on Earth is destroyed, that renovation will mark the last time in all eternity that any part of the universe will be marred by sin and rebellion.

Now, we can't know everything about the eternal perfect state. But there are dozens of Scriptures that speak about eternal things! If we're willing to believe what's plainly written, we can know quite a bit about what's coming. But if we reject what's clearly stated, we'll remain in ignorance by our own choice.

If we believe the Bible is God's Word, then we can certainly take Him at His Word when He speaks in passage after passage about eternal conditions on this Earth. The studies that follow will help you gain a fuller understanding of our eternal future. We'll focus on eternal events not covered in our previous lessons on the Millennium and the renovation of the Earth. It would be helpful to review those lessons before diving in.

---

## III. New Lands (Rev. 21:1)

> "And I saw a new heaven and a new earth: for the first heaven and the first earth were passed away; and there was no more sea."

Does this mean there will be no water on Earth after the renovation? Not at all! Many other passages make this clear. This verse is describing the great change that will happen to the large oceans — the ones covering about three-fourths of the Earth today.

Here's what will happen: those massive oceans will be evaporated by the "fervent heat" during the Earth's renovation. The waters that fell from the heavens and came up from the deep to cover the Earth during the chaos of Gen. 1:2 will return to the heavens and the deep places within the Earth. The Earth will be renewed to its original state — as it was before Lucifer fell, before the first rebellion.

But there will still be an abundance of rivers, lakes, and smaller seas on Earth forever. Let me show you the evidence:

> 1. Seas and islands are specifically mentioned in the eternal reign of the Messiah (Ps. 72:8-10, 17; 97:1-6; Isa. 42:4, 10-12; 66:19-21; Zech. 9:10; Ezek. 47:1-23).

> 2. Rivers are mentioned too (Ezek. 47:1-12, 18-20; Zech. 9:10; 14:8; Rev. 22:1-3).

> 3. Some seas were originally created with the Earth to be eternal — they'll last as long as Israel, the sun, moon, and Earth itself (Jer. 5:22; 31:35, 36; Ps. 146:6; Prov. 8:29; Acts 4:24; 14:15; Rev. 14:7).

> 4. Abundant water will be necessary to fulfill prophecies about fruitful seasons and waters springing up in the desert (Gen. 8:22; Isa. 35; Amos 5:8; 9:6; Job 38:4-16, 22-30; Ps. 104:5-14, 24-28).

Picture it this way: when those enormous oceans evaporate, millions of square miles of new land will be reclaimed and made habitable for the generations of natural people yet to come. It's like gaining entire continents of living space!

---

## IV. The New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:2–22:4)

The next thing John saw in his vision was absolutely stunning: the Holy City, New Jerusalem, coming down from God out of Heaven to take its eternal place on the New Earth. Let's walk through everything Scripture tells us about this incredible city — its names, age, source, preparation, location, measurements, appearance, materials, walls, foundations, buildings, lighting, water system, traffic patterns, restrictions, food, inhabitants, and rulers.

### 1. The Names of the City (Rev. 3:12; 21:2; 9-10; Jn. 14:1-3; Heb. 12:22-23)

The city goes by many names, and each one reveals something significant about the capital of the universe:

> **(1) The New Jerusalem** (Rev. 3:12; 21:2). The word "New" simply means the city is kept fresh, clean, pure, and new in appearance by God's very presence.

> **(2) The Holy City** (Rev. 21:2; 22:19). "Holy" tells us it's a pure, clean city consecrated to God's holiness and to all the social systems of the universe.

> **(3) The Bride, the Lamb's Wife** (Rev. 21:2, 19-20). It's called this because the city will be the eternal home of all the redeemed, where they'll live in eternal union with God, Christ, the Holy Spirit, and faithful angels. The redeemed inhabitants have been purchased by Christ's blood. They become part of that city through His redemption. All the redeemed are united to Christ and are spoken of as being married to Him even now, as we'll see in our next lesson.

> **(4) The Tabernacle of God** (Rev. 13:6; 15:5; 21:3). This name fits because it's where God's throne is located — it's where He personally lives and reigns.

> **(5) The Great City** (Rev. 21:10). This name is earned! It's the largest, most magnificent, most wonderful city in all creation. It's great because it's the universal capital of the Great God.

> **(6) The Holy Jerusalem** (Rev. 21:10). This emphasizes again the absolute purity and holiness of God's city.

> **(7) The Heavenly Jerusalem** (Heb. 12:22-23). This name highlights that the city is in Heaven — it's a heavenly city, not an earthly one. There's a heavenly Jerusalem and an earthly Jerusalem. The earthly Jerusalem will be Christ's headquarters for His reign on Earth and the seat of world government. But the heavenly Jerusalem is the capital of the entire universe. Paul speaks of "the Jerusalem that is above" as being free and "the mother of us all" — meaning we're all born again by God who lives and reigns in the Heavenly Jerusalem (Gal. 4:19-31).

> **(8) The City of the Living God** (Heb. 12:22-23). This expresses the truth that the New Jerusalem belongs to God, the Living God, who owns it along with all other parts of His vast creation.

> **(9) My Father's House** (Jn. 14:1-3). Jesus Himself calls the New Jerusalem "my Father's house." He speaks of it as containing many mansions. And it's not just mansions — there are streets, fountains of water, rivers, furniture, and all the material things that make the city a real place for real people to live, just as much as any city on Earth.

Now, some Bible students argue there's no real city with mansions in Heaven. They claim the saints themselves are the mansions Jesus mentioned. They can't seem to accept a real material city with streets, homes, fountains, and everything else that makes for real life.

But the Bible is crystal clear on this! If people don't want to believe what it says, they'll simply have to remain in ignorance. As Paul puts it, "If any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant" (1 Cor. 14:38).

As we saw in an earlier lesson, Heaven is a real place, a real country, a real planet, and a material land just like Earth. Nearly all of Rev. 21-22 pictures the reality of Heaven and the New Jerusalem with many concrete details.

I once knew a brilliant young preacher who seemed destined to make a mark for God. He was rising high in religious circles. But he had one fault: he became too worldly-wise to believe the simple, literal statements of Scripture. He started reasoning that the Bible was purely spiritual, heavenly, and symbolic.

Once, in a discussion about the New Jerusalem and the mansions in Heaven, he insisted that the mansions were the saints and the city was just symbolic of the Church. There was no way to reason with him because we had no common ground. When one person believes the simplicity of the Bible and another rejects taking it literally, agreement becomes impossible.

This young man went east and achieved great success in church circles, largely because most church members have been taught that the more mysterious and spiritualized the Bible becomes, the more receptive they should be.

One day I read in a religious paper that this young man had died. His wife described how, on his deathbed, he saw the most beautiful city he was about to enter. He described it in detail. I said to my wife, "It's too bad that men have to die before they get sense enough to believe the Bible." This minister became a believer in the Bible's literalness in his dying hours — when he could have believed it his whole life and been a far greater blessing to others.

Think about how ridiculous it is to argue that the saints are the mansions Jesus spoke about. Let's read Jn. 14:1-3 the way these teachers would have us understand it:

> "In my Father's house [the Father Himself] are many mansions [saints]: if it were not so, I would have told you [mansions]. I go to prepare a place [saints] for you [mansions]. And if I go and prepare a place [saints] for you [mansions], I will come again, and receive you [mansions] unto myself; that where I am, there ye [mansions] may be also."

Does that make any sense? Absolutely not!

It would be equally foolish to make the New Jerusalem a symbol of the Church. Imagine reading everything in Rev. 21-22 as symbolizing the Church. What could walls, gates, foundations, streets, a river, trees of life, and all the city's details possibly symbolize? You might as well throw the Bible away and give up any hope of understanding it if you're going to eliminate the plain, literal, sensible meaning of what's clearly written.

A God who couldn't make Himself clear in human language so we can believe what He says would be no God at all. You can't hold that distorted view of God and the Bible while claiming to have a clear understanding of truth.

On the other hand, how wonderfully clear everything in Scripture becomes when we understand it the same way we would any other book! The description of the New Jerusalem is far too detailed to spiritualize away. So let's be sensible and take the Bible as it was meant to be read.

If God says there's a real city in Heaven, let's believe He knows what He's talking about. He should know whether there's a city or not — He made all things and He lives in Heaven! If we can't believe Him on this, we can't believe Him on anything. Rejecting anything God says makes Him out to be a liar and unreliable on every other question too.

### 2. The Source and Origin of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 3:12; 21:2, 10; Jn. 14:1-3; Gal. 4:26; Heb. 9:11; 11:10-16; 12:22)

These passages reveal that God is the Architect, Builder, and Owner of the Holy City. Right now it's in Heaven, but after the Millennium it will be moved to Earth. The city is prepared as the home for the redeemed of all ages.

The city had its beginning in God's mind sometime in the dateless past. When it was originally created, we don't know — speculation is pointless. What we do know is that it's a real city, built by God, and it will always be His capital city.

### 3. The Preparation of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:2; Jn. 14:1-3; Heb. 11:10-16; 13:14)

These passages tell us that the saints will have a part in the Holy City and that such a place is now being prepared for them. This fact alone proves that the saints are not the mansions or the city! Jesus promised He was going away to prepare a place for the saints in His Father's house.

Paul stated that all the Old Testament saints and present-day Church saints were not the city — they were looking forward to becoming part of it (Heb. 11:8-16; 13:14). This is definite proof. You can't look for a city to become part of it while simultaneously being that city.

### 4. The Eternal Location of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 3:12; 21:2, 9-10, 24-26; 22:1-5)

These passages tell us the Holy City will be transplanted from Heaven to Earth after the Millennium to dwell among men forever. Its eternal location will be right here on Earth.

### 5. The Outward Appearance of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:11)

Let me paint you a picture of this breathtaking city:

- Adorned as a bride prepared for her husband
- Radiant with the glory of God
- Like a precious stone, clear as crystal
- Surrounded by a jasper wall about 300 feet high
- Twelve massive pearl gates inscribed with the names of Israel's twelve tribes
- Twelve angels stationed at each of the great gates, which never close day or night
- Square in shape with three gates on each side
- Twelve foundations bearing the names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb
- 1,500 miles square and 1,500 miles high
- Foundations adorned with every kind of precious stone
- Streets of gold like transparent glass
- Rivers, trees, fountains of water
- Illuminated by the glory of God Himself
- The city itself made of pure gold, like clear glass

Here's something important to understand: the city is not a cube. A wall around a cube that's already 1,500 miles high on all sides would serve no purpose! From all the statements in Scripture, it's clear the city is a series of mountain peaks — beginning as low foothills inside the walls and rising higher and higher until the tallest peak reaches 1,500 miles high.

On that highest peak sits the heavenly tabernacle, the capital building where God rules as the Supreme Moral Governor of the Universe. It was after the patterns of this tabernacle that Moses built his earthly version (Heb. 8:5; 9:23). Scattered across the mountains and through the valleys are the many mansions of Jn. 14:1-3. Living fountains spring up throughout the city, and rivers of water, clear as crystal, flow through it.

The floor of the throne room itself is like a sea of glass, clear as crystal, and it's immense — large enough to contain the countless multitude of tribulation saints described in Rev. 7:9-17; 15:2-4. There's also a massive banquet hall where the Marriage Supper of the Lamb and other grand feasts will be held throughout eternity.

These facts prove that Heaven is a real planet and the New Jerusalem is a real city. We can't fully grasp the greatness and grandeur of Heaven and the universe's capital city, but Scripture gives us enough to understand this: there will be a real life in the most perfect environment possible for all who love God and follow His plan in this life.

### 6. The Walls, Gates, and Foundations of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:12-14; Heb. 11:10-26)

The walls stand 144 cubits high — about 300 feet if we take a cubit as 25 inches. The gates are solid pearls, and the foundations are adorned with the most precious stones in all creation.

The city must rest on the ground — there would be no need for foundations if it were floating in the air. The gates stand open day and night so people from the nations on Earth can come and go (Rev. 21:23-27).

### 7. The Measurements of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:15-17)

The city measures 1,500 miles square and 1,500 miles high, with a 300-foot wall all around it. It's foursquare at the base and rises like a series of mountain peaks.

The New Jerusalem site in Heaven is called Mount Zion in Heb. 12:22-23 and Rev. 14:1. This likely refers to the highest mountain peak where the capitol building stands.

### 8. The Materials of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:10-21)

The building materials are gold and precious stones of every kind.

### 9. The Age of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:2)

It's called "new" (the Greek word *kainos* — "new in freshness and character," not *neos*, "new in age") because it's kept perpetually fresh and new by God's very presence.

The city's actual age is unknown. It might have been created alongside Heaven and Earth. It has been the location of God's throne ever since Heaven was created (Ps. 11:4; 93:2; 103:19; Isa. 6:1; 14:12-14; 66:1; Rev. 4:2-10; 5:1-13; 7:9-17; 8:3; 22:1-3). It existed when humanity was created — all the saints from Abel onward have looked for it (Heb. 11:8-16; 13:14).

### 10. The Streets of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:21)

The singular "street" in this passage doesn't mean there's only one street — any more than the same wording in Rev. 11:8 means Jerusalem has only one street.

There are at least twelve great boulevards in the city because there are twelve great gates, and people pass through those gates into the city. John is simply describing one street so we can understand what all the rest are like. They're all made of pure gold, transparent and clear as glass.

### 11. The Buildings of the New Jerusalem (Jn. 14:1-3; Rev. 3:12; 7:15; 11:19; 14:15, 17; 15:1-8; 16:1, 17)

John's statement in Rev. 21:22, "I saw no temple therein: for the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple of it," can't possibly mean there's no literal temple in the New Jerusalem — because there clearly is one, as shown in the passages above.

The meaning is this: no material temple or shrine will exist as an object of worship, or a place for pilgrimages, or a building people must enter to worship. Instead, all people will worship God and the Lamb in Spirit and in truth — they themselves are the true sanctuary and objects of worship.

### 12. The Light of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:23, 25; 22:5)

The city's light will surpass the sun, moon, and stars. We've seen that during the Millennium the light of the sun and moon will be increased (Isa. 30:26). So if God's glory outshines even that enhanced light, it will be beyond words!

These passages don't teach that the sun and moon won't shine — just that in this particular city there's no need for them because there's a greater light: God's glory. The passages also don't mean there won't be sun and moon elsewhere on Earth. They will shine forever, and day and night will continue (Gen. 8:22; Ps. 89:2-3, 29, 35, 37; Jer. 31:35-36).

This light will radiate from God's throne on the highest peak — 1,500 miles up — and illuminate the entire city below. It's beyond imagination to picture how dazzling that light will be, shining on a city of transparent gold decked with every kind of precious stone and pearl!

Whether this glory will be the full brightness that God currently dwells in (1 Tim. 6:16) isn't clear, but it will be brighter than the brightest sunlight possible (Rev. 21:23-25).

### 13. The Water of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 22:1)

The city will have abundant water. Twelve great rivers will flow from God's great throne, 1,500 miles high, down through the middle of the twelve great boulevards to the base of the city and out into different parts of the Earth.

Beyond these rivers, there will be countless living fountains and smaller streams throughout the city. Every mansion will likely have its own water system springing up perpetually.

Imagine the beauty of God's glory shining on these living fountains and rivers (Rev. 7:17; 14:7; 22:1)! From the midst of the fountains, gorgeous colors will appear in God's glory like rainbows on Earth.

### 14. The Inhabitants of the New Jerusalem (Jn. 14:1-3; Heb. 11:10-16; 12:22; 13:14; Rev. 3:12; 12:12; 13:5; 21:2-5, 9; 22:3-4)

These passages show us who will live in the city: God, Christ, the Holy Spirit, angels, redeemed glorified saints, seraphim, cherubim, and other heavenly beings. Earthly natural people won't live in the city — they'll simply visit for commerce, as we'll see below.

The city is called "the bride, the Lamb's wife" because it's prepared for the glorified saints and faithful angels as their eternal home.

### 15. The Traffic in the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:24-27)

Natural people from the nations — those who live through the Millennium and don't rebel with Satan at its end — will enter the New Earth to plant, harvest, build, multiply, and replenish the Earth, just as Adam's descendants would have done if they hadn't fallen.

These people will do business in the New Jerusalem, walk in its light, and their kings will bring the glory and honor of the nations into it forever.

### 16. The Food of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 2:7, 17; 19:1-10; 22:2; Ps. 78:25; Lk. 22:15-18, 28-30)

Yes, even glorified saints will eat in the next life! The overcomer is promised "the tree of life," "the hidden manna," "the fruit of the vine," and "the Passover Lamb" in the life to come.

The inhabitants of the New Jerusalem will "eat and drink" at Christ's table in His kingdom and enjoy foods they've never experienced in this world.

Picture this: rows of trees of life stretching 1,500 miles long on both sides of the twelve great rivers running through the twelve great boulevards. The trees of life bear twelve kinds of fruit, and their leaves will preserve natural life for the peoples of the natural nations throughout all the eternal generations to come. Beyond this, there will be abundant fruit from trees throughout the entire Earth (Ezek. 47:1-12).

### 17. Restrictions of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 21:27; 22:14-15)

No dogs, sorcerers, sexually immoral people, murderers, idolaters, liars, or anything defiling will ever enter the city — only those written in the Lamb's Book of Life, those who enter the New Earth from the Millennial Age, and those born throughout eternity.

### 18. Rulers of the New Jerusalem (Rev. 22:3-5)

The Great God, His Son, and the Spirit will rule from this city over the entire universe and all the free moral agents within it.

---

## V. The New Peoples (Rev. 21:3-4, 24-26)

Here's something that surprises many people: there will be natural humans on this Earth forever, and natural life will continue throughout all eternity exactly as God intended when He created it. Let me walk you through the evidence:

**1.** In Isa. 66:22-24, God assures Israel they'll be an earthly people forever:

> "For as the new heavens and the new earth, which I will make, shall remain before me, saith the Lord, so shall your seed and your name remain."

The Hebrew word for "seed" is *Zera*, meaning "seed, fruit, plant, or posterity." It's used 273 times in the Old Testament, and every single time it means natural seed — whether seed sown in the ground or the natural offspring of humanity (Isa. 59:21).

**2.** The phrase "all flesh" in Isa. 66:22-24 further proves natural people will continue in the New Earth, coming to worship God during new moons and sabbaths. The Hebrew word for "flesh" is *basar*, meaning "flesh," "skin," "nakedness," or "body." It's used 252 times and always refers to natural flesh or animal life.

**3.** In Rev. 21:3-4, 24-26, the men and nations mentioned are natural peoples. God's tabernacle comes down to dwell among men, and God wipes away all tears. This couldn't refer to glorified saints — God has already been dwelling among them for 1,000 years, and He wiped away their tears 1,000 years before these Scriptures are fulfilled!

**4.** The Earth was created to be inhabited by natural beings, and natural humans were promised the Earth as an eternal inheritance from the very beginning (Exod. 32:13; Ps. 25:13; 37:9-11, 22, 29, 34; 69:36; 82:8; Isa. 60:21; Mt. 5:5; 25:34).

These promises were spoken to earthly people and will be fulfilled when earthly people inherit the Earth after the Millennium. Glorified saints will reign as kings and priests under Christ over these earthly peoples forever (Dan. 7:13-14, 18-27; Isa. 9:6-7; Lk. 1:32-35; 1 Cor. 6:2; Rev. 5:9-10; 11:15; 20:4-10).

People living on Earth during the Millennium won't be glorified or enter the same privileges as those who participate in the first resurrection and were glorified before the Millennium. If those earthly people were glorified, who would be the subjects of Christ's eternal kingdom? Who would marry and produce the natural generations on the eternal Earth?

**5.** God created humanity to live forever in natural bodies. This is clear from the fact that humans would have lived naturally forever if they hadn't sinned. The penalty for sin was death — physical, spiritual, and eternal. But humanity's fall didn't cause God to change His original plan.

God can't suffer defeat in the end, so He turned humanity's Fall into a blessing. During this period, He's been gathering a heavenly people to reign over the natural people who will be redeemed from the Fall and all its effects after the Millennium.

Think of it like this: the Fall simply delayed God's original purpose, but because of the delay, God gained more than He would have otherwise. Now God will have a heavenly, resurrected, glorified people who will reign over coming generations of natural people. If the Fall had never happened, all humans would have continued as natural people forever, and there would be no class of glorified saints from the human race to help God administer the universe.

After the Millennium, natural humanity will be fully redeemed and will live on the New Earth just as Adam did before the Fall, carrying out God's original purpose of filling the Earth with natural people (Gen. 1:27-31; 9:12; Isa. 45:18).

**6.** The Bible plainly declares there will be eternal generations of natural peoples. Here's just a sampling of the many passages:

> Gen. 9:12; 13:15; 17:7-8, 19; Exod. 3:15; 12:14, 42; 27:21; 30:8, 21; 31:16; 40:15; Lev. 3:17; 6:18; 10:9; 17:7; 23:14, 21, 31, 41; 24:3; 25:30; Num. 10:8; 15:15; 18:23; Deut. 5:29; 12:28; 28:46; 29:29; 2 Sam. 7:24-29; 1 Chron. 23:25; Ps. 12:7; 45:17; 72:5; 79:13; 89:4; 100:5; 102:12, 24; 106:31; 119:90; 135:13; 145:13; 146:10; Isa. 9:6-7; 51:8; 59:21; Jer. 31:35-36; 32:38-40; Ezek. 37:24-28; Lam. 5:19; Dan. 2:44-45; 4:3, 34; 7:13-18; Joel 3:20; Lk. 1:32-35, 55; Rev. 5:10; 11:15.

Let me quote a few of these remarkable promises, all spoken by God Himself:

> "And God said, This is the token of the covenant which I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations" (Gen. 9:12).

> "For all the land which thou seest, to thee will I give it, and to thy seed for ever... And I will establish my covenant between me and thee and thy seed after thee in their generations for an everlasting covenant, to be a God unto thee, and to thy seed after thee... And I will give unto thee, and to thy seed after thee, the land wherein thou art a stranger, all the land of Canaan, for an everlasting possession; and I will be their God" (Gen. 13:15; 17:7-8).

> "Observe and hear all these words which I command thee, that it may go well with thee, and with thy children after thee for ever, when thou doest that which is good and right in the sight of the Lord thy God" (Deut. 12:28).

> "For thou hast confirmed to thyself thy people Israel to be a people unto thee for ever: and thou, Lord, art become their God... And now, O Lord God, the word that thou hast spoken concerning thy servant, and concerning his house, establish it for ever, and do as thou hast said... Therefore now let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may continue for ever before thee: for thou, O Lord God, hast spoken it: and with thy blessing let the house of thy servant be blessed for ever" (2 Sam. 7:24, 25, 29).

> "For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given: and the government shall be upon his shoulders: and his name shall be called Wonderful, Counsellor, The mighty God, The everlasting Father, The Prince of Peace... Of the increase of his government and peace there shall be no end, upon the throne of David, and upon his kingdom, to order it, and to establish it with judgment and with justice from henceforth even for

lessons.title: The Bride of Christ
lessons.content:
## THE BRIDE OF CHRIST (Rev. 21:2, 9)

Few Bible subjects have been more misunderstood than this one. The confusion usually comes from building entire doctrines on parables or Old Testament stories rather than on clear, direct Scripture.

Some people argue that the exact term "the bride of Christ" never appears in the Bible, so we shouldn't use it — especially for the Church. But here's the thing: Scripture *does* use "the bride, the Lamb's wife" in Rev. 21:1-9. And Christ is clearly the Lamb, as shown in Jn. 1:29; 1 Pet. 1:19; Rev. 5:5-7; 14:5; 21:22-23; 22:3.

So if Christ is the Lamb, and the Lamb has a bride, then talking about "the bride of Christ" is perfectly scriptural. In this lesson, we'll discover who that bride really is.

Let's work through this topic under five headings:

### I. Is the Bride of Christ Israel?

Those who teach that Israel is the bride of Christ usually rely on so-called "types" drawn from Old Testament historical events. A favorite example is the story of Isaac and Rebekah. They try to show how the characters in this story represent God the Father, the Son, the Holy Spirit, and the bride.

But here's the problem: there's not one passage of Scripture that actually confirms this interpretation. All genuine biblical types are either plainly stated in Scripture or backed up by clear passages on the same subject. Think of it like this: you can't just declare something a "type" without scriptural proof — otherwise, anyone could make any story mean whatever they want.

If this historical event were truly a type proving Israel is the bride, we'd need two or three clear passages teaching that directly. Without those, we're just building on sand. One person might use Isaac and Rebekah to argue Israel is the bride; another might use the same story to argue the Church is the bride. Both arguments would be equally unfounded.

**What Other Scriptural Basis is There for Such Teaching?**

1. Some argue that Israel is the only "married wife" of God, pointing to Old Testament scriptures about God's marriage to Israel. The conclusion? If Israel — not the Church — is married to God, then Israel must be the "wife" in Rev. 19:1-10 and 21:1-9.

It's true that Israel was married to God — we established that in Lesson Eighteen, Point VIII. But this doesn't prove Israel is the "wife" of Revelation 19 and 21. Israel is also married to Christ, as we've seen. And the Church is *also* currently married to Christ, as we'll prove shortly.

Simply being married to God doesn't identify anyone as the specific "wife" in those passages.

2. Others argue that the Greek word for "wife" in Revelation is *gune*, meaning "a married woman" — therefore, Israel must be that wife. But *gune* doesn't only mean a married woman. It can mean *any* woman — married, single, or widowed. You can see this in Lk. 10:38; 22:5-6; Jn. 20:11-15; 1 Tim. 5:2, 14; Lk. 4:26; Jn. 19:26; Rev. 17.

If *gune* can refer to an unmarried woman, the argument that it *must* mean Israel because Israel was married to God simply doesn't hold up.

3. The parables of the marriage feast (Mt. 22) and the ten virgins (Mt. 25) are also used to argue Israel is the bride, since these parables were spoken to Jews about Jewish matters. But as we saw in Lesson Forty-four, this doesn't prove anything about the bride's identity.

Revelation 19 and 21 clearly show the bride as a *heavenly* people, not an earthly people like the Jews. It's simply inconceivable that the New Jerusalem and its inhabitants — called "the bride, the Lamb's wife" — could be Israel.

Think about it: when did Israel get to Heaven so they could come back to Earth after the Millennium as occupants of the New Jerusalem? Where in Scripture was Israel ever promised the New Jerusalem as their eternal dwelling?

All saints of all ages are promised such a city — glorified Jews *and* Gentiles will dwell there (Jn. 14:1-3; Heb. 11:8-16; 13:14; Rev. 3:12). But earthly Israel was never promised this city. Israel will have *earthly* Jerusalem as her city forever (Ezek. 48).

Even if earthly Israel did dwell in this city alongside glorified saints, it would be unscriptural to call earthly Israel the bride while excluding the glorified saints. What would be the difference if they both live in the same city called the bride?

Actually, Israel and other earthly peoples in the New Earth don't dwell *in* the city — they simply traffic in it (Rev. 21:3, 24-27). Can't we understand that a city can come down from Heaven to be among earthly people without those people actually living inside it?

**Conclusion:** There's no warrant for claiming the bride of Christ is made up of Israel only. The bride will include glorified Jews — and Gentiles too, as we'll see below.

### II. Is the Bride of Christ Only a Part of the Church?

This theory suggests the bride consists of a select number of New Testament Church members — often called "full overcomers." People who hold this view use terms like "the bride part of the Church," "full overcomers," "partial overcomers," or "a select part of the Church."

Notice something important: *none of these terms appear in Scripture.*

This theory really comes down to questions we fully discussed in Lesson Forty-five, Point II, 3. There we proved that the bride is *not* made up of only a few special people in the Church, so we won't repeat that discussion here.

### III. Is the Bride of Christ Only the New Testament Church?

Many believe the bride will consist only of New Testament saints. But this can't be true, as we saw in Lesson Forty-five, Point II. Hebrews 11:8-16, 40 makes it clear: all Old Testament saints from Abel onward were promised the *same city* as New Testament saints.

So all saints of all ages must be part of the bride of Christ (Rev. 21:9).

### IV. Is the New Testament Church Now Married to Christ?

The common belief goes like this: Israel was married to God throughout the Old Testament, but the Church is *not* currently married to God — that won't happen until the future Marriage of the Lamb (Rev. 19:1-10).

But this is inconsistent. If Israel was married to God because of their covenant relationship, then on the exact same basis, the New Testament Church is *also* married to God right now. In fact, anyone God made a covenant with in any age could be considered God's wife by covenant relationship (see Lesson Thirty-six, "The Fifteen Great Covenants of Scripture").

The New Testament definitely states that the present Church is married — or united — to God now, just as Israel was in Old Testament times.

**Here are the New Testament proofs:**

1. Jesus called Himself "the bridegroom" of the disciples during His earthly ministry (Mt. 9:15; Mk. 2:19, 20). These disciples were the *first* members of the present Church (Eph. 2:19-22). What did Jesus mean if He wasn't their bridegroom?

Certainly He knew what to call Himself. If He was the bridegroom of the first members of this called-out body, He's also the bridegroom of the last members.

The Greek word for "bridegroom" is *numphios*, meaning "a young married man" or "a newly married man." Every passage using this word confirms this (Mt. 9:15; 25:1-10; Mk. 2:19-20; Lk. 5:34-35; Jn. 2:9; 3:29; Rev. 18:23).

2. John called Christ the bridegroom (Jn. 3:29). The bride of Christ will be composed of people in the new creation in Christ. Old Testament saints weren't legally made part of the new creation until *after* Christ, because He is the head of the new creation and the "firstborn" of every creature (Rom. 8:28-29; Col. 1:15-18; Heb. 12:23).

Their sins weren't legally atoned for and couldn't be blotted out until the New Testament was made (Rom. 3:25; Heb. 9:15).

John belonged to the old order — a forerunner and herald of the Head of the new creation. His salvation was on the same basis as Old Testament saints: faith in the blood that *would be* shed. The disciples belonged to the new order (Mt. 11:11-12; Lk. 16:16) because Christ had already become the "firstborn" of every creature.

All Old Testament saints were made part of the new creation after Christ made atonement for them. Christ had power on Earth to forgive sins, but no one before Him ever had that power (Mt. 9:6; Mk. 2:5-12; Lk. 5). As members of the new creation after Christ, Old Testament saints will become members of the future bride just as much as New Testament saints (Heb. 11:8-16, 40).

Some believe John couldn't be a member of the bride because he called himself a "friend" of the bridegroom. But Jn. 3:29 doesn't say he couldn't become a member. John simply recognized he belonged to the old order and wasn't yet legally part of the new creation when he made that statement.

If John couldn't be in the bride because he was a "friend" of the bridegroom, then neither could New Testament saints — because they're also called friends in the same sense (Lk. 12:4; Jn. 11:11; Jn. 15:13-15).

This theory fails for another reason: Abraham is called a friend of God (2 Chron. 20:7; Isa. 41:8; Jas. 2:23) *and* the father of all who believe (Rom. 4:11-16; Gal. 3:7-9). If his children in faith are going to be in the bride, Abraham must be too (Heb. 11:8-16, 40).

3. Hebrews 11:40 clearly states that Old Testament saints won't be made perfect *without* New Testament saints — implying both will be made perfect together. This teaches that both classes will finally be united in one great heavenly family as members together of the bride of Christ.

4. Old Testament saints were all united to God by the terms of the New Covenant (Rom. 3:25; Heb. 9:15) and saved by the same blood of the New Testament that saves Church saints. What would make them different?

Living in a different age certainly wouldn't create a difference. If Israel was married to God because of their covenant, why wouldn't the Church be married to God because of *our* covenant with Christ?

Aren't we now in covenant relationship with God just as Israel was? If so, we're married to God just as much as Israel was. That we're in such covenant relationship now is clear from Mt. 26:28; Lk. 22:20; 1 Cor. 11:17-30; 2 Cor. 3:1-18; Heb. 7:22; 8:1–10:38; 12:24.

5. Paul tells Roman Jews that if they would become dead to the law through the body of Christ and be made alive under the New Testament's terms, they would be married to Jesus Christ (Rom. 7:1-6). This was a *present* union with Christ, not future.

6. After His ascension, Jesus in Rev. 22:16-17 recognizes that believers on Earth were *already* His Bride. How else could the bride say, "Come . . . take of the water of life freely"?

This bride was on Earth, inviting anyone to come and be saved. If this doesn't refer to believers in every generation, who does it refer to?

The Greek word for "bride" here is *numphe*, meaning "a young married woman" or "a newly married woman." This establishes beyond doubt that present believers are already married to God under the New Covenant, just as Israel was under the Old Covenant.

7. Paul uses the marriage relationship between a man and woman to teach the relationship between Christ and His Church (1 Cor. 11:2; 12:12-28; Eph. 4:12-16; 5:21-33). Paul clearly knew what he was doing.

8. The New Testament speaks of believers now being "joined" to the Lord (1 Cor. 6:16-17). Old Testament people were also spoken of as being "joined" to God (Isa. 56:6; Jer. 50:5). If they were married to God, why aren't New Testament saints?

More scriptural arguments could be added, but these sufficiently prove that New Testament saints are *presently* married to God.

**Now let's examine the passages used to teach they're *not* yet married:**

1. The first is 2 Cor. 11:1, 2. Paul tells the Corinthians he had espoused them to one husband, wanting to present them as a chaste virgin to Christ. But instead of referring to the whole bride of Christ, this refers to Paul's *own* converts.

He was jealous over his converts and wanted them to remain true to Christ so he could present them faultless before God. This is the same desire expressed in 1 Thess. 2:19: "What is our hope, or joy, or crown of rejoicing? Are not even ye [Paul's own converts] in the presence of our Lord Jesus Christ at His coming?"

Paul wasn't claiming responsibility to present the *whole* body of Christ to the Lord. In Eph. 5, he taught that *Christ* presents the Church to Himself.

The Greek word "espoused" here is *harmodzo*, meaning to join in *actual* marriage relationship. Instead of teaching believers aren't yet married, it teaches they *are* joined to Christ in marriage relationship — something Paul pictures many times.

2. The second scripture used is Rev. 19:1-10, describing the future "marriage of the Lamb" in Heaven after the rapture. The argument goes: if the Lamb will be married in the future, He's not married now.

If the Church is currently married to Christ yet John pictures a future marriage, something seems wrong. Some try to explain this marriage as referring to Israel, but that's unscriptural as we've seen.

We don't have to accept this theory to harmonize the facts that believers are *now* married to Christ *and* will eat a Marriage Supper in the future. Notice in Rev. 19:1-10 that whoever is referred to is called "his wife" — she's *already* His before the Marriage Supper. This perfectly harmonizes with what we've said about Christ's relationship with all believers of every age who will be glorified and made a heavenly people.

Let me quote from *The International Standard Bible Encyclopedia* to illustrate how believers could be married to God now yet still be united to Him in final ceremonies at the Marriage Supper:

> "Betrothal with the ancient Hebrews was of a more formal and far more binding nature than the engagement with us. Indeed, it was esteemed a part of the transaction of marriage, and that the most binding part. Among the Arabs today it is the only legal ceremony connected with marriage. Genesis 24:58-60 seems to preserve for us an example of an ancient formula and blessing for such an occasion. Its central feature was the dowry (mohr), which was paid to the parents, not to the bride. It may take the form of service (Gen. 29; 1 Sam. 18:25). It is customary in Syria today when the projected marriage is approved by both families, and all financial preliminaries have been settled, to have this ceremony of betrothal. It consists in the acceptance before witnesses of the terms of the marriage contracted for. Then God's blessing is solemnly asked on the union thus provided for, but to take place probably only after some months, or perhaps some years. The betrothal affected, all danger of any further financial fencing and bluffing now being at an end, happiness and harmony may preside over all the arrangements for the marriage day. Among the Jews the betrothal was so far regarded as binding that, if marriage should not take place, owing to the absconding of the bridegroom or the breach of contract on his part, the young woman could not be married to another man until she was liberated by due process and paper of divorce. A similar custom prevails in China and Japan, and in cases becomes very oppressive. The marriage may have been intended by the parents from the infancy of the parties, but this formality of betrothal is not entered on until the marriage is considered reasonably certain and measurably near. A prolonged interval between the betrothal and marriage was deemed undesirable on many accounts, though often an interval was needed that the groom might render the stipulated service or pay the price—say a year or two, or, as in the case if Jacob, it might be seven years. The betrothed parties were legally in the position of a married couple and unfaithfulness was adultery (Deut. 22:23; Mt. 1:19)."

For the marriage ceremony, see Lesson Forty-four. After the ceremony, the marriage supper followed. There was no formal religious ceremony like our modern weddings. The entrance into the chamber consummated the marriage, and the marriage supper immediately followed.

The Marriage Supper of the Lamb is simply the consummation of the union between God and all heavenly, glorified saints of all ages. It couldn't simply refer to the first and final union of the believer with Christ.

These are the only passages that could teach such a theory, except for a few Old Testament historical events that aren't worth consideration given the many plain passages on this subject.

### V. What is the Bride of Christ?

Here's what's absolutely certain, stated in the clearest way possible: **the Holy City, the New Jerusalem, is the bride, the Lamb's wife.**

This is clearly stated in Rev. 21:2, 9-10: "And I John saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down from God out of heaven, prepared as a bride adorned for her husband . . . And there came unto me one of the seven angels which had the seven vials full of the last plagues, and he talked with me, saying, Come hither, I will shew thee the bride, the Lamb's wife. And he carried me away in the spirit to a great and high mountain, and shewed me that great city, the holy Jerusalem, descending out of heaven from God."

What could be clearer?

This plain passage should settle the question for everyone seeking truth. No one could honestly read this and still claim the bride is something else.

The bride of Christ is not the Church as a whole. It's not only a part of the Church. It's not Israel as a whole. It's not only a part of Israel. It's not the Gentiles as a whole. It's not only part of the Gentiles. It's not any particular nation, denomination, race, or color. It's not the many things people have taught through the ages.

**It is the Holy City — the New Jerusalem — and it cannot be anything else.**

If the angel said the bride is the New Jerusalem, then he either lied or told the truth. If he told the truth, then the bride is a city — not some nation or Church as many Bible teachers claim today.

Let's be reasonable and believe the bride is a city, just as the angel told John.

**You might ask: how could a city be the bride?** The Bible writers answer this plainly.

In Heb. 11:8-16, we have definite proof that Abel, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, and all the Old Testament faith heroes looked for a city as their eternal home. Hebrews 13:14 shows that present Church saints are also looking for the same city. Revelation 19:7-10 shows that the *saints* are the wife of Christ. And Rev. 21:9-10 shows that the *New Jerusalem* is the bride of Christ.

If the saints are the wife and the city is the bride, we must conclude that the city *with all its buildings and inhabitants* will make up the final bride of Christ. All the redeemed of all ages — from Abel to the last tribulation saint — who will have part in the first resurrection will live in the New Jerusalem, which is called "the bride, the Lamb's wife."

Think of it like this: you could ask anyone living in Atlanta if *they* are Atlanta, GA — they'd have to say no. Point to any church building in Atlanta and ask if *that* is Atlanta — again, no. The same would be true for any citizen, building, or single part of Atlanta. For anyone to claim they *are* Atlanta would be to lose common sense.

It's the same with the New Jerusalem. It's unscriptural to call the present Church "the bride," because the bride is the New Jerusalem. It's unscriptural to call any particular group of redeemed from any period "the bride," because the bride is a real city — not just some company of redeemed people from a particular time.

However, it *is* scriptural to say about any redeemed person that they're now married to Christ, that they're a citizen of Heaven, and that they are or will be *part of* the bride when they become a citizen of the Holy City.

It's scriptural to say the Old Testament saints as a company are and will always be *a part of* the bride. It's perfectly scriptural to say that present Church saints are married to Christ and will also be *a part of* the bride. We can say the same about all future tribulation saints, the 144,000 Jews of the Tribulation, or any individual or company of any period — they'll be just as much a part of the bride as all other redeemed people living in the New Jerusalem forever.

Even now, Christians are citizens of Heaven, destined to have a part in the bride — the New Jerusalem — when they die and are taken to that city to live forever. Naturally, until a person or group actually gets to the city, they can't be called *the* bride, because they're not yet actually part of the New Jerusalem.

It's similar to how anyone who moves to a city doesn't become a citizen or part of that city until they settle there. So the saints won't actually become part of the New Jerusalem until they move to the city and become part of it.

For centuries, Bible students have limited the word "bride" to the Church or some other company of redeemed. But this isn't accurate. The city itself is the bride — and this includes *all* its inhabitants, not just a few.

It's not proper to call the present Church *the* bride. But it *is* proper to call it *a part of* the bride. When we understand this, we won't limit the bride to a special select few of any age. The bride includes all redeemed saints of every age who have part in the first resurrection and who will become inhabitants of the New Jerusalem.

**You might also ask:** how could a city with material buildings be called a bride and be "clothed in fine linen, which is the righteousness of the saints" (Rev. 19:7-10)?

This becomes clear when we recognize that "the bride" also refers to the city's *inhabitants*. It's similar to reading in the newspaper that "Atlanta, GA gave a banquet and a big welcome" to some visiting dignitary. Could the *buildings* of Atlanta welcome anyone or host a banquet? Of course not. This is simply how we say the city officials and prominent citizens hosted the banquet and welcomed the visitor.

Obviously, the buildings of the New Jerusalem can't eat a marriage supper or be clothed in fine linen. This simply means the *inhabitants* will eat the supper and be clothed in linen, as Rev. 19:7-10 teaches.

What God wants us to understand in Rev. 19:7-10; 21:2, 9-11; 22:17 and similar passages is that there's a real material city with buildings *and* inhabitants — just like any earthly city. It doesn't make sense to ignore the fact that there's a real city in Heaven. And it doesn't make sense to ignore that the city has inhabitants. There can be no city without buildings and inhabitants. It takes both to make a city.

With these facts in mind, we're not robbing any Christian of the hope of being part of the bride of Christ. But when present Church saints teach they *are* the bride of Christ, they're wrong — they'll only be *a part* of the bride.

When they teach Old Testament saints aren't part of the bride, they're wrong — they're also part of the bride. When they make any particular group the *exclusive* bride of Christ, they're wrong.

**To be perfectly scriptural, we must conclude:**

- Old Testament saints will be part of the New Jerusalem, which is the bride, the Lamb's wife
- New Testament saints will be part of the bride
- Every redeemed person of every age who goes to live in the New Jerusalem will be part of the bride of Christ, just as much as all other redeemed people

This truth will correct the following errors in Christianity:

- That the bride consists of a special few of the redeemed
- That only a few will reign with Christ
- That only a few will be the wife while the rest are merely guests at the Marriage Supper
- That you have to be a super-saint to be in the bride
- That only God knows which redeemed people will be in the bride and which won't
- That some will be rewarded and exalted higher because they lived in a particular period
- That you must belong to a certain denomination to be in the bride
- That present Church saints will be exalted higher than Old Testament saints
- That those least in this age will be greater than the greatest of any other age
- That you must live in this age and receive certain spiritual experiences only available now to be in the bride

These and countless other false doctrines are taught by various church groups today. All such unscriptural teachings are automatically disproven when we grasp the true conception of the bride of Christ.

**We conclude, therefore, that the bride is the real material city in Heaven called the New Jerusalem — together with its inhabitants, who have been redeemed from the human race in all ages from Adam to the Millennium.**

---

## Questions on Lesson Fifty-two

- Is the term "bride of Christ" used in Scripture?

- Is such a term unscriptural to use? Explain and prove from Scripture.

- Name the five main points in Lesson Fifty-two.

- Should we use any so-called type or parable to prove who the bride is?

- Is it necessary to use some historical story such as Isaac and Rebekah, Joseph and his bride, Moses and his wife, or some other story to prove what the bride is?

- Give one plain Scripture that clearly states what the bride is.

- Is the bride of Christ the whole or a part of Israel?

- What basis of proof do men use to prove that Israel is the bride?

- Is Israel the only people mentioned in Scripture as being married to God?

- What other proof in Scripture do men use to prove that Israel is God's wife.

- Does this prove that Israel is the wife of Rev. 19:7-10?

- Give Scripture to prove that "the bride, the Lamb's wife" is not Israel.

- Will earthly Israel ever live in the New Jerusalem? Prove.

- Who will go to live in the New Jerusalem? Prove.

- What city will be the earthly capital city of Israel?

- Is the bride of Christ only a part of the New Testament Church?

- Prove from Scripture that the body of Christ and the Church are the same.

- Prove from Scripture that there is only one class of people in the Church.

- Is the bride of Christ only the whole New Testament Church?

- Is the Church now married to Christ? Prove.

- On what basis is the Church now married to Christ?

- Give some Scriptures to prove that Christians are now married to Christ.

- Prove from Scripture that both the Old and New Testament saints are members of the future and complete bride of Christ.

- How were the Old Testament saints made a part of the bride of Christ?

- Explain the two main Scriptures that are used to teach that Christians are not presently married to Christ.

- How could Christians be presently married to Christ and yet have the Marriage Supper when they get to Heaven?

- Illustrate how the saints can be now married to Christ and yet have the Marriage Supper when they get to Heaven.

- How binding were ancient marriages?

- Was there always a period of time between the betrothal and the actual coming together in marriage as man and wife?

- When were people considered married, at the betrothal or at the marriage supper?

- How are we to understand the Marriage Supper of Rev. 19:7-10?

- What is the bride of Christ? Prove.

- Who will be in the bride of Christ? Prove.

- Is the bride of Christ a city or a Church?

- Illustrate how no specific person or building in a city could be that city.

- Is it proper to speak of any particular person as the bride of Christ? Why?

- Is it proper to speak of any particular nation as the bride of Christ? Why?

- Is it proper to call the Church the bride of Christ? Why?

- How could a city be the bride of Christ? Explain and illustrate.

- Will all Christians of every age be in the bride of Christ? Prove.

- Name a number of fallacies that this teaching will automatically disprove.

supplements.title: Supplement 1
supplements.content:
# Supplement One

**For Lessons One and Two**

There are some foundational biblical teachings about your personal, immediate needs that we've placed in this special supplement section. We're doing this so we can focus specifically on what matters most to *you* right now—whatever your situation might be.

Think of these supplements as a heart-to-heart conversation between us. We want to help you understand what you can expect from God as you work through these lessons. Our goal is to help you start receiving benefits—physically, materially, morally, and spiritually—from the very first lesson.

We want to show you what's available from God and exactly how to receive it. But here's an important tip: make sure you read and study both lessons before diving into their supplement.

If you follow the instructions in the lessons and supplements carefully—the way you'd follow a doctor's orders—you'll see results from the start. In Lessons One and Two, we've introduced you to the course and demonstrated that the Bible is God's eternal Word. It wasn't given to entertain you—it was given to help you in every area of life.

## I. God's Promises

In Lessons Ten, Twelve, Sixteen, and other lessons and supplements, you'll find many promises that you should take literally. Read them as if a trusted friend made those same promises directly to you.

Start acting on them. Believe them exactly as they're written, without questioning. They don't need interpretation—they mean what they say.

Don't attach conditions beyond what's stated. Simply meet the conditions given, and you'll receive the benefits. The moment you start wondering if these promises are really true for you today, you're being attacked by the devil and demonic powers trying to get you to doubt God's Word. This is exactly what caused the fall in the beginning. Getting victory over doubt is the very first thing you must conquer if you want to be redeemed from the fall and receive what God has promised.

Here's the bottom line: you cannot doubt God's Word—essentially calling Him a liar—and still receive what you need from Him. God won't make a special exception for you or anyone else. He requires faith in His Word before He obligates Himself to meet your needs and fulfill His promises.

This is the first lesson you must learn, and you must learn it well. It's absolutely imperative!

You can already see from the promises that it's God's will for you to have everything in life you want. God loves you and wants you to be happy, healthy, wealthy, and successful beyond anything you've ever dreamed.

Believe God! Adopt the right attitude toward Him and trust that He means what He says. Start cooperating with Him in asking and receiving life's blessings.

God won't force these things on you—it has to be a willing, dependent attitude on your part. He won't give you these blessings unilaterally, no matter how much He desires to. His Word makes clear what you must do and what He promises to do when you do your part. He cannot break His own laws, and He will not fail to meet His obligations to you.

## II. The Reality of the Living God

Faith in God and His promises presupposes that God exists and that He's a God of love, justice, and truth. We'll take this as given until we prove what the Bible says about God in later lessons.

You can and should believe in God if you want the benefits He's promised. We're assuming you believe in God and that you believe the Bible is His Word. We must start on this biblical and fundamental foundation.

As you receive benefits according to the promises, you'll have the best proof that God exists.

## III. The Reality of Satan, Fallen Angels, and Demons

The Bible doesn't just teach about God, Jesus Christ, and the Holy Spirit—it also teaches about enemies to both God and humanity: Satan, fallen angels, and demonic spirits of all kinds.

We'll prove this thoroughly in Lesson Six, but here are some passages that demonstrate these beings exist:

> 1. SATAN (1 Chr. 21:1; Job 1:6-12; 2:1-7; Ezek. 28:11-17; Zech. 3:1; Mt. 4:1-11; 1 Pet. 5:8-9; Rev. 12:7-12; 20:1-10; Eph. 2:1-3; 2 Cor. 4:4; Jn. 12:31). 2. FALLEN ANGELS (Mt. 24:41; Eph. 1:21; 3:10; 6:10-17; Rev. 12:7-12).

> 3. DEMONS (Mt. 4:23-24; 10:1-7; 12:22-32; Mk. 5; 1 Tim. 4:1; 1 Jn. 4:1-6).

## IV. The Source of Sin, Sickness, and Disease

The Bible teaches that Satan and demons are the source of sin, sickness, and disease. They propagate sin and disease and control the law of sin and death in humanity (Mt. 4:23-24; 10:1-7; Lk. 10:1-20; 13:16; Jn. 10:10; Acts 10:38; Rom. 7:7-25; 8:1-4; 1 Jn. 3:8; Heb. 2:14-15).

The Bible also teaches that God sent Jesus into the world to save people from all sin (Mt. 1:21; Eph. 1:7; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; Rev. 1:5); to heal people of all diseases and deliver them from demons (Mt. 8:16-17; Acts 10:38; 1 Pet. 2:24; 1 Jn. 3:8); and to answer all prayers for anything covered by God's promises (Mt. 7:7-11; 18:19-20; 17:20; 21:21-22; Mk. 11:22-24; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7; 16:23-26).

## V. Two Sources of Relief from Diseases

### 1. Satan and Demons Can Remove Diseases They Cause Under Certain Circumstances

They'll keep people in defeat and sickness as long as possible. Only God can deliver from their power—unless they choose to release their hold on their own.

Jesus taught in Mt. 12:43-45 that demons can leave a person and then return if they want to. The fact that they leave when rebuked by those with authority over them proves they can release anyone they've bound (Mt. 12:22-32; Lk. 10:18-20; Acts 19:11-17). It makes sense that if they can put diseases on people, they can also remove them when it benefits their purposes.

When would it benefit them? When they can deceive people with false doctrines and get them to accept false religions that deny the essential gospel truths that save souls.

Jesus and others predicted that in the last days, these demonic powers will become increasingly active and will "shew great signs and wonders; insomuch that, if it were possible, they shall deceive the very elect" (Mt. 24:24; 2 Thess. 2:8-12; 1 Tim. 4:1-8; 1 Jn. 4:1-6; Rev. 9:20; 13:1-18; 16:13-16; 19:20).

Some false religions today are getting people healed and bringing them certain benefits, but their deceived followers never discover the true source of these manifestations. Just because people receive some benefits, they become convinced the source must be God—but that's not always true, as these scriptures show.

Jesus said that in the judgment "many will say to me . . . Lord, Lord, have we not prophesied in thy name? and in thy name have cast out devils? and in thy name done many wonderful works? And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity" (Mt. 7:21-23).

These religions, without exception, deny the blood of Christ, the new birth, the bodily resurrection of Jesus, and all the gospel essentials that save souls eternally. The devil backs up such teachings by removing some sicknesses and helping followers in other ways, convincing them they've found the true religion. But they've abandoned Christianity and the Bible and will face damnation for rejecting Jesus Christ and the cross (Acts 4:12).

### 2. God Is the True Source of Help—Not the Devil and Demons

We come to you with the true message of the Bible. We guarantee on the authority of God's Word that you will receive healing, health, prosperity, answers to prayer, and anything else God promises—if you faithfully follow His instructions.

And you won't have to deny God, Christ, or anything in Scripture to receive these benefits. You can know God in reality and enjoy His fullness as you walk in truth and conform to His will. Your eternal soul will be saved, and you'll have eternal life instead of the eternal damnation that followers of false religions will endure.

Let's be crystal clear: there are two sources of help. You can get some help from demons if you deny the truth. But if you let the true God be your source, you'll get everything you want in this life and the next.

## VI. How to Pray

Maybe the Bible and prayer are unfamiliar to you, and religion seems like a puzzle. Let's start with some basic advice for beginners in Christianity. Even if you're more advanced, these foundational principles will help you too.

**First**, realize that you're a sinner and that Jesus Christ died to save you from your sins (Col. 1:20; 1 Pet. 2:24). If you understand this much, you're not far from the kingdom of God.

**Second**, begin praying directly to God—the one you've offended by your sins. Confess your sins and talk to Him just as you would to any person you've wronged (Lk. 18:9-14; 1 Jn. 1:9).

God loves you! He's not going to hurt you unless you reject Him to the end and He has to cast you into hell because you chose to go there instead of heaven (Ps. 9:17; Mt. 24:41, 46). Understand that He loves you and gave Jesus to save you (Jn. 3:16). He wants to help you with every problem in life, so take Him as your partner from now on.

Read Rom. 10:9-10 and 1 Jn. 1:7-9, then do exactly what these passages instruct. You want to be saved, don't you? Wouldn't you love to know that all your sins are forgiven and that Jesus is your personal Savior?

Then do as you're told. Forget all religions and human theories—focus on one objective: getting right with God. No church can save you. Jesus is the Savior, and He alone can forgive your sins.

If you've never prayed and don't know how, simply repeat this prayer with deep humility and genuine sorrow for your sin:

**PRAYER FOR FORGIVENESS:**

"O God, be merciful to me a sinner! Forgive me for all my sins and blot out my transgressions. Cleanse me from all unrighteousness and make me clean by the precious blood of Jesus Christ and by the power of the Holy Spirit. I will forsake sin and consecrate my life to your service and to the good of others and I will walk in the light of your Word as I learn it. I believe that you forgive me according to your Word. In Jesus' name I ask it. Amen."

**Third**, believe that it's done—that you are now a child of God. You cannot possibly pray a simple, heartfelt prayer like this without God hearing and immediately answering. His oath guarantees He will hear and forgive you (Isa. 55:7; 1 Jn. 1:9).

**Fourth**, start reading the Bible and praying when you wake up, before bed, and as often as possible throughout the day. Don't make a show of it—find a quiet place, whether at work or anywhere else, and talk to God as you would talk to a friend. Forsake sin and walk in the light of truth as you come to know it (1 Jn. 1:7).

You don't need a certain place or posture to pray. Just learn to talk and commune with God at home, at work, or while doing anything. It doesn't always have to be out loud.

Don't think you'll be heard because you use many words. Don't pray to be seen by others. These are foundational lessons of the Christian life (Mt. 6:5-15). There are times, however, when you should unite in prayer with others, as taught in Mt. 18:18-20; Acts 1:14; 2:42-47; 4:24.

If you're a beginner, it's wise to concentrate on what you can truly have faith for. As God answers those prayers, begin asking for more. But here's the key: never ask for something you're not willing to believe God for and persist in asking until you receive a complete answer.

You will face obstacles in prayer. People and demons will try to discourage you and make you doubt God—but don't give in! Believe despite all opposition. Fight for your answer based on God's promises, the name of Jesus, and the power of the Holy Spirit living in you as God's child (Rom. 8:9-16, 26-27, 34; Jude 20-24).

You and God together can work wonders. It's a personal matter—you can receive from God anything you want, regardless of what anyone says. One person plus God equals a majority, and "all things are possible to him that believeth" (Mk. 9:23–11:22-24). The only thing that can defeat you is lack of faith—so always believe God without doubting (Heb. 11:6; Jas. 1:5-9).

## VII. What to Pray for—the Abundance of Life

God invites you to ask and receive anything you want—not just what you need, but what you *want* that's covered by His promises.

I know this might startle you. It goes against how many Christians think today. But it's true! Look at the promises you've read. Read them again. See for yourself.

Forget all limitations. Forget the old prayer "if it be Thy will"—it's already God's will for you to "ask what ye will, and it shall be done unto you" (Jn. 15:7; 16:23-26; Mk. 11:22-24).

Why cast doubt on God by asking if it's His will when He's already made His will clear? What God has promised in His Word *is* His will—so ask and receive. You only need to seek God's will for things not specifically covered by His promises.

Do you understand what this means? All your needs—financial, business, social, religious, physical, moral, spiritual, any problem of any kind—God promises that if you ask, you shall receive (Mt. 7:7-11; 17:20; 21:21-22).

What more could you want? Could God make it any clearer? Does He need to personally rebuke your unbelief before you wake up to the abundance He's promised?

It doesn't matter what your needs are. Don't limit God or His promises. Be bold and aggressive in asking and receiving. If you limit the promises, you limit your faith. If you limit your faith, you limit what God can give, because He gives according to your faith.

You'll receive exactly what you have faith for. The principle is: "According to your faith be it unto you" (Mt. 8:13; 9:29; Mk. 11:22-24; Jas. 1:5-9; Heb. 11:6).

The promises are unlimited. God is unlimited. The believer is unlimited if they exercise unlimited faith (Mk. 9:23; 11:22-24; Mt. 7:7-11; 18:15-19; 21:21-22; Jn. 14:12-15; 15:7, 16; 16:23-26).

## VIII. Practice the Presence of God

Start cultivating an awareness of God's presence in your life. If you've yielded to Him, He's with you. He sees you and cares about every detail of your life. He's your partner now—don't ignore Him in any activity.

Think of it like being in a business partnership with someone who's always watching out for your best interests. You'd naturally keep them informed and consider their input in every decision.

Do nothing you wouldn't do if He were physically present. Say nothing you wouldn't want Him to hear. Go nowhere you'd have any doubt He would go.

Follow these simple principles, and your life will be happy and victorious. God will prosper you in everything as you ask in faith, without wavering (Jas. 1:5-9; Heb. 11:6).

## IX. How to Overcome Sin and Bad Habits

Once you've confessed your sins to God (which you've done by now if you've been following along faithfully), you have no reason to worry about past sins. They've all been forgiven and cleansed by Christ's blood. They're blotted out—God doesn't remember them against you anymore (Acts 3:19; Eph. 1:7; 1 Jn. 1:7-9; Rev. 1:5).

Since God doesn't remember them, don't burden your mind with them either. Just forsake sin and live for God from here on (Prov. 28:13).

When an old habit or sin tempts you, resist it with a definite, vigorous refusal. Don't argue with the devil or try to get as close to your old sins as possible without actually committing them. Get as far away as you can!

Focus your mind on God and His goodness to you. If temptation lingers and feels strong, start reading the Bible and go to God in prayer. Draw closer to Him. Love God with genuine affection. Thank Him for saving you from those evils, and the power of temptation will break (Jas. 4:6-10; 1 Cor. 10:12-14).

Don't yield to temptation—it gets harder to break each time. Win a decisive victory over your old life once and for all. Walk in the Spirit and in the light of the truths you're learning, and living victoriously over sin will become natural (Gal. 5:16-26; 1 Jn. 1:7; Rom. 8:1-3).

If you do yield to temptation, go immediately to God for forgiveness. He will forgive and give power to overcome (2 Cor. 5:17-18; 1 Jn. 1:9).

Christ will change your life and make you happy, free, and victorious—that's His part. Your part is to confess and forsake sin.

God doesn't hold grudges. He forgives and helps every time we need Him. He's not ready to banish you to hell the moment you do something wrong. He's a kind, loving Heavenly Father who forgives us even when we can't seem to forgive ourselves.

All complications about God's dealings with us come from our side. Just confess what's necessary, resist temptation, and God will give you power to overcome.

God wants you to face the world, overcome temptation, and become a living example that His power can keep anyone who submits to Him. He won't eliminate your temper or desires or remove you from tempting situations. But He will keep you from yielding and make you victorious over everything contrary to His Word.

Remember: thoughts and feelings aren't sin. No sin is committed until sin is "finished" (Jas. 1:13-16). Temptations are legitimate warnings that the devil is attacking, but don't mistake temptation itself for sin.

## X. Healing and Health

If you're sick, include your request for healing in your prayer for forgiveness. God wants to heal your body, and He will if you ask in faith when you seek forgiveness.

Healing and forgiveness go together in the gospel, as you can see in Ps. 103:1-3; Mt. 8:16-17; 9:1-8; 13:15; Jas. 5:14-16; 1 Pet. 2:24.

You can receive healing just as easily as forgiveness. Both come on the same basis—faith in what Christ accomplished for you at Calvary. Ask, then believe you are healed and forgiven, and God will do both.
